External ritual without internal reality is not true worship. (2) 'In truth' — worship must be based on true knowledge of God as he has revealed himself, not on ignorance (v.22) or falsehood. The Samaritans worshipped in sincerity but not in full truth; the Jews had the truth but often lacked spiritual reality. True worship requires both. (3) 'The Father seeketh' — the most remarkable statement: God is a seeking God. He seeks worshippers — not because he needs worship but because he desires relationship. This connects to the seeking shepherd (Luke 15:4), the seeking woman (Luke 15:8), and the seeking father (Luke 15:20).

verses.kjv_text: God is a Spirit: and they that worship him must worship him in spirit and in truth.
verses.original_text: πνεῦμα ὁ θεός καὶ τοὺς προσκυνοῦντας αὐτὸν ἐν πνεύματι καὶ ἀληθείᾳ δεῖ προσκυνεῖν
verses.transliteration: pneuma ho theos kai tous proskynountas auton en pneumati kai alētheia dei proskynein
verses.study_note: 'God is a Spirit' (pneuma ho theos — Spirit [is] God; the predicate 'Spirit' comes first for emphasis). This is one of four great 'God is' statements in John's writings: 'God is Spirit' (4:24), 'God is light' (1 John 1:5), 'God is love' (1 John 4:8, 16). God's nature as Spirit means he is not confined to any location, cannot be contained in any building, and is not limited to any form. 'Must' (dei — it is necessary, divinely required). The nature of God determines the nature of worship: because God is Spirit, worship must be spiritual; because God is truth, worship must be truthful.
verses.doctrinal_note: The declaration 'God is Spirit' is foundational for all theology and worship. (1) God is not material — he has no body, no physical form, no spatial limitation (though he may manifest himself in physical ways, as in the Incarnation). (2) God is omnipresent — he cannot be confined to Gerizim or Jerusalem. (3) Worship that matches God's nature must be 'in spirit' (spiritual, not merely physical) and 'in truth' (genuine, informed by revelation). This does not eliminate physical expressions of worship but insists that they be animated by the Spirit and grounded in truth.

verses.kjv_text: The woman saith unto him, I know that Messias cometh, which is called Christ: when he is come, he will tell us all things.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ ἡ γυνή Οἶδα ὅτι Μεσσίας ἔρχεται ὁ λεγόμενος Χριστός ὅταν ἔλθῃ ἐκεῖνος ἀναγγελεῖ ἡμῖν πάντα
verses.transliteration: legei autō hē gynē Oida hoti Messias erchetai ho legomenos Christos hotan elthē ekeinos anangelei hēmin panta
verses.study_note: 'Messias' (Messias — the Aramaic/Hebrew form, transliterated into Greek; John translates for his readers: 'which is called Christ,' Christos, the Anointed One). The Samaritan expectation was of the Taheb — the 'Returning One' or 'Restorer' based on Deuteronomy 18:15-18 (a prophet like Moses). 'He will tell us all things' (anangelei hēmin panta — he will announce, declare everything to us). The woman recognises that the kind of comprehensive knowledge Jesus has demonstrated is a messianic attribute. She is on the threshold of faith.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus saith unto her, I that speak unto thee am he.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῇ ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ λαλῶν σοι
verses.transliteration: legei autē ho Iēsous Egō eimi ho lalōn soi
verses.study_note: 'I that speak unto thee am he' (egō eimi ho lalōn soi — I am, the one speaking to you). 'Egō eimi' (I am) — the first explicit messianic self-identification in John. This is the formula that echoes the divine name (Exodus 3:14: 'I AM THAT I AM') and will recur throughout the Gospel (6:35; 8:12, 24, 28, 58; 10:7, 11; 11:25; 14:6; 15:1; 18:5-6). Remarkably, Jesus makes this disclosure not to a Jewish leader or his own disciples but to a Samaritan woman of questionable reputation — the gospel is for all.
verses.doctrinal_note: Jesus' self-revelation to the Samaritan woman is theologically remarkable: (1) He reveals his messiahship to a Samaritan before doing so openly in Jerusalem. (2) The formula 'egō eimi' carries overtones of the divine name — 'I AM' (Exodus 3:14). (3) He reveals himself to the most unlikely person — a woman, a Samaritan, an outcast — demonstrating that grace transcends all human boundaries. (4) In the Synoptics, Jesus commands secrecy about his messiahship (the 'messianic secret'); in John, he reveals it freely to this woman, suggesting that Samaria is a safer context for this disclosure than Jerusalem, where political messianism could be explosive.

verses.kjv_text: And upon this came his disciples, and marvelled that he talked with the woman: yet no man said, What seekest thou? or, Why talkest thou with her?
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐπὶ τούτῳ ἦλθον οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐθαύμασαν ὅτι μετὰ γυναικὸς ἐλάλει οὐδεὶς μέντοι εἶπεν Τί ζητεῖς ἢ Τί λαλεῖς μετ᾽ αὐτῆς
verses.transliteration: kai epi toutō ēlthon hoi mathētai autou kai ethaumasan hoti meta gynaikos elalei oudeis mentoi eipen Ti zēteis ē Ti laleis met autēs
verses.study_note: 'Marvelled' (ethaumasan — were astonished, amazed). The disciples' surprise reveals the cultural norms Jesus was breaking: a rabbi speaking with a woman in public was considered improper. The Talmud warned: 'One should not talk with a woman on the street, not even with his own wife' (Berakhot 43b). Yet they dare not question him — their growing awareness of his authority restrains them.

verses.kjv_text: The woman then left her waterpot, and went her way into the city, and saith to the men,
verses.original_text: ἀφῆκεν οὖν τὴν ὑδρίαν αὐτῆς ἡ γυνὴ καὶ ἀπῆλθεν εἰς τὴν πόλιν καὶ λέγει τοῖς ἀνθρώποις
verses.transliteration: aphēken oun tēn hydrian autēs hē gynē kai apēlthen eis tēn polin kai legei tois anthrōpois
verses.study_note: 'Left her waterpot' (aphēken tēn hydrian — she abandoned her water jar). This small detail is significant: (1) she forgot her original errand in her excitement, (2) she has found 'living water' and no longer needs the jar, (3) she left it for Jesus to drink — an act of hospitality. 'Went into the city' — the woman who came alone at noon to avoid people now goes to the people. Encounter with Jesus transforms shame into testimony.

verses.kjv_text: Come, see a man, which told me all things that ever I did: is not this the Christ?
verses.original_text: Δεῦτε ἴδετε ἄνθρωπον ὃς εἶπέν μοι πάντα ὅσα ἐποίησα μήτι οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ Χριστός
verses.transliteration: Deute idete anthrōpon hos eipen moi panta hosa epoiēsa mēti houtos estin ho Christos
verses.study_note: 'Come, see' (deute idete — come, look; cf. 1:39, 46: 'Come and see' — the invitation that creates disciples). 'Told me all things that ever I did' — an overstatement (he told her about her husbands), but her point is that he knew her completely. 'Is not this the Christ?' (mēti houtos estin ho Christos — the particle mēti expects a tentative negative but hopes for a positive: 'This couldn't be the Christ, could it?'). She does not dogmatically proclaim but invitingly questions — the most effective evangelism often begins with a question rather than a declaration.

verses.kjv_text: Then they went out of the city, and came unto him.
verses.original_text: ἐξῆλθον ἐκ τῆς πόλεως καὶ ἤρχοντο πρὸς αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: exēlthon ek tēs poleōs kai ērchonto pros auton
verses.study_note: The imperfect tense (ērchonto — they were coming) suggests a continuous stream of Samaritans heading toward Jesus. One woman's testimony sets in motion a communal response — the harvest that Jesus will describe in vv.35-38.

verses.kjv_text: In the mean while his disciples prayed him, saying, Master, eat.
verses.original_text: ἐν δὲ τῷ μεταξὺ ἠρώτων αὐτὸν οἱ μαθηταὶ λέγοντες Ῥαββί φάγε
verses.transliteration: en de tō metaxy ērōtōn auton hoi mathētai legontes Rhabbi phage
verses.study_note: While the Samaritans are on their way, the disciples urge Jesus to eat. This sets up another Johannine misunderstanding: the disciples think of physical food; Jesus speaks of spiritual nourishment.

verses.kjv_text: But he said unto them, I have meat to eat that ye know not of.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς Ἐγὼ βρῶσιν ἔχω φαγεῖν ἣν ὑμεῖς οὐκ οἴδατε
verses.transliteration: ho de eipen autois Egō brōsin echō phagein hēn hymeis ouk oidate
verses.study_note: 'I have meat to eat that ye know not of' (brōsin echō phagein — I have food to eat). Another level-shift from physical to spiritual, like 'living water' (v.10) and 'born again' (3:3). Jesus' deepest satisfaction comes not from food but from doing the Father's will. The disciples' bewilderment (v.33) parallels the woman's confusion about living water.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore said the disciples one to another, Hath any man brought him ought to eat?
verses.original_text: ἔλεγον οὖν οἱ μαθηταὶ πρὸς ἀλλήλους Μή τις ἤνεγκεν αὐτῷ φαγεῖν
verses.transliteration: elegon oun hoi mathētai pros allēlous Mē tis ēnenken autō phagein
verses.study_note: The disciples' literal misunderstanding ('Did someone bring him food?') follows the pattern of Nicodemus (3:4), the Samaritan woman (4:11, 15), and later audiences (6:52). Physical thinking must give way to spiritual perception.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus saith unto them, My meat is to do the will of him that sent me, and to finish his work.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ἐμὸν βρῶμά ἐστιν ἵνα ποιῶ τὸ θέλημα τοῦ πέμψαντός με καὶ τελειώσω αὐτοῦ τὸ ἔργον
verses.transliteration: legei autois ho Iēsous Emon brōma estin hina poiō to thelēma tou pempsantos me kai teleiōsō autou to ergon
verses.study_note: 'My meat' (emon brōma — my food, my nourishment). 'To do the will of him that sent me' (poiō to thelēma tou pempsantos me — to do the will of the one who sent me). 'To finish his work' (teleiōsō autou to ergon — to complete, bring to perfection his work). Jesus' life is entirely oriented toward the Father's mission. 'Finish' (teleiōsō — to bring to completion, perfection) anticipates the cross: 'It is finished' (tetelestai, 19:30). The conversation with the Samaritan woman was not a detour but a direct expression of the Father's will — this is the 'work' that nourishes Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: Say not ye, There are yet four months, and then cometh harvest? behold, I say unto you, Lift up your eyes, and look on the fields; for they are white already to harvest.
verses.original_text: οὐχ ὑμεῖς λέγετε ὅτι Ἔτι τετράμηνός ἐστιν καὶ ὁ θερισμὸς ἔρχεται ἰδοὺ λέγω ὑμῖν ἐπάρατε τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς ὑμῶν καὶ θεάσασθε τὰς χώρας ὅτι λευκαί εἰσιν πρὸς θερισμὸν ἤδη
verses.transliteration: ouch hymeis legete hoti Eti tetramēnos estin kai ho therismos erchetai idou legō hymin eparate tous ophthalmous hymōn kai theasasthe tas chōras hoti leukai eisin pros therismon ēdē
verses.study_note: 'Four months, and then cometh harvest' — possibly a proverb about patience, or a literal time reference (if it is December, harvest would come in April). Jesus shatters the conventional timeline: 'Look on the fields' — the Samaritans streaming from the city toward him are the 'white' harvest. 'White already' (leukai ēdē — already white, ready). The spiritual harvest does not follow the calendar of nature; it is ready now. 'Lift up your eyes' — the disciples are looking at food; Jesus tells them to look at people.

verses.kjv_text: And he that reapeth receiveth wages, and gathereth fruit unto life eternal: that both he that soweth and he that reapeth may rejoice together.
verses.original_text: ὁ θερίζων μισθὸν λαμβάνει καὶ συνάγει καρπὸν εἰς ζωὴν αἰώνιον ἵνα ὁ σπείρων ὁμοῦ χαίρῃ καὶ ὁ θερίζων
verses.transliteration: ho therizōn misthon lambanei kai synagei karpon eis zōēn aiōnion hina ho speirōn homou chairē kai ho therizōn
verses.study_note: 'Receiveth wages' (misthon lambanei — receives reward). 'Gathereth fruit unto life eternal' (synagei karpon eis zōēn aiōnion — collects fruit for eternal life). 'That both he that soweth and he that reapeth may rejoice together' — the sower (the OT prophets, John the Baptist, Jesus himself) and the reaper (the disciples, later the church) share in the joy of the harvest. No competition between labourers — all share the reward and rejoicing.

verses.kjv_text: And herein is that saying true, One soweth, and another reapeth.
verses.original_text: ἐν γὰρ τούτῳ ὁ λόγος ἐστὶν ὁ ἀληθινός ὅτι Ἄλλος ἐστὶν ὁ σπείρων καὶ ἄλλος ὁ θερίζων
verses.transliteration: en gar toutō ho logos estin ho alēthinos hoti Allos estin ho speirōn kai allos ho therizōn
verses.study_note: The proverb 'One soweth, and another reapeth' usually carried a negative sense (you labour and someone else benefits). Jesus redeems it: in God's economy, sowing and reaping are complementary, not competitive. The sower does not lose when another reaps — both share the joy (v.36).

verses.kjv_text: I sent you to reap that whereon ye bestowed no labour: other men laboured, and ye are entered into their labours.
verses.original_text: ἐγὼ ἀπέστειλα ὑμᾶς θερίζειν ὃ οὐχ ὑμεῖς κεκοπιάκατε ἄλλοι κεκοπιάκασιν καὶ ὑμεῖς εἰς τὸν κόπον αὐτῶν εἰσεληλύθατε
verses.transliteration: egō apesteila hymas therizein ho ouch hymeis kekopiakate alloi kekopiaksin kai hymeis eis ton kopon autōn eiselēlythate
verses.study_note: 'Other men laboured' (alloi kekopiaksin — others have toiled). 'Ye are entered into their labours' — the disciples inherit the work of Moses, the prophets, John the Baptist, and Jesus himself. The harvest they will reap in Samaria (and later worldwide) was prepared by centuries of divine sowing. The verb kopiō (to labour, toil) is the same used of Jesus' weariness in v.6 — he is the ultimate sower who labours so that others may reap.

verses.kjv_text: And many of the Samaritans of that city believed on him for the saying of the woman, which testified, He told me all that ever I did.
verses.original_text: ἐκ δὲ τῆς πόλεως ἐκείνης πολλοὶ ἐπίστευσαν εἰς αὐτὸν τῶν Σαμαρειτῶν διὰ τὸν λόγον τῆς γυναικὸς μαρτυρούσης ὅτι Εἶπέν μοι πάντα ἃ ἐποίησα
verses.transliteration: ek de tēs poleōs ekeinēs polloi episteusan eis auton tōn Samareitōn dia ton logon tēs gynaikos martyrousēs hoti Eipen moi panta ha epoiēsa
verses.study_note: 'Believed on him' (episteusan eis auton — believed into him). 'For the saying of the woman' (dia ton logon tēs gynaikos — because of the word/testimony of the woman). 'Which testified' (martyrousēs — bearing witness; the same verb used of John the Baptist's testimony, 1:7, 15). The woman becomes the first evangelist in John's Gospel — and she evangelises Samaritans, the despised outsiders. Her testimony is effective because it is personal and authentic: 'He told me all that ever I did.'

verses.kjv_text: So when the Samaritans were come unto him, they besought him that he would tarry with them: and he abode there two days.
verses.original_text: ὡς οὖν ἦλθον πρὸς αὐτὸν οἱ Σαμαρεῖται ἠρώτων αὐτὸν μεῖναι παρ᾽ αὐτοῖς καὶ ἔμεινεν ἐκεῖ δύο ἡμέρας
verses.transliteration: hōs oun ēlthon pros auton hoi Samareitai ērōtōn auton meinai par autois kai emeinen ekei duo hēmeras
verses.study_note: 'Besought him' (ērōtōn — kept asking, imperfect: persistent request). 'Tarry with them' (meinai par autois — remain, abide with them). 'Two days' — Jesus, a Jew, stays with Samaritans for two days, eating and teaching. This two-day stay produces a deeper faith than signs alone could produce (v.42). The verb 'abode' (emeinen — remained) is a key Johannine word (15:4-10: 'Abide in me').

verses.kjv_text: And many more believed because of his own word;
verses.original_text: καὶ πολλῷ πλείους ἐπίστευσαν διὰ τὸν λόγον αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai pollō pleious episteusan dia ton logon autou
verses.study_note: 'Many more' (pollō pleious — far more, considerably more). 'Because of his own word' (dia ton logon autou — because of his word). The progression: first they believed because of the woman's testimony (v.39), then 'many more' because of Jesus' own word. Second-hand testimony brings them to Jesus; direct encounter with Jesus deepens and multiplies faith. The pattern is paradigmatic for evangelism: human testimony leads to personal encounter.

verses.kjv_text: And said unto the woman, Now we believe, not because of thy saying: for we have heard him ourselves, and know that this is indeed the Christ, the Saviour of the world.
verses.original_text: τῇ τε γυναικὶ ἔλεγον ὅτι Οὐκέτι διὰ τὴν σὴν λαλιὰν πιστεύομεν αὐτοὶ γὰρ ἀκηκόαμεν καὶ οἴδαμεν ὅτι οὗτός ἐστιν ἀληθῶς ὁ σωτὴρ τοῦ κόσμου ὁ Χριστός
verses.transliteration: tē te gynaiki elegon hoti Ouketi dia tēn sēn lalian pisteuomen autoi gar akēkoamen kai oidamen hoti houtos estin alēthōs ho sōtēr tou kosmou ho Christos
verses.study_note: 'The Saviour of the world' (ho sōtēr tou kosmou) — this title occurs only here and in 1 John 4:14 in the entire Bible. Samaritans — despised outsiders — are the first to confess Jesus not merely as Messiah but as the Saviour of the whole world. 'We have heard him ourselves' (autoi akēkoamen — we ourselves have heard; perfect tense: we have heard and continue to know). 'We know' (oidamen — we know with certainty). Their faith has moved from secondhand (the woman's testimony) to firsthand (Jesus' own word) — from 'Is not this the Christ?' (v.29) to 'This is indeed the Christ, the Saviour of the world.'
verses.doctrinal_note: The Samaritan confession — 'the Saviour of the world' — is theologically climactic. (1) The title transcends Jewish messianism: Jesus is not merely Israel's Messiah but the world's Saviour. (2) The Samaritans, whom the Jews despised, recognise what Jerusalem's leaders will reject. (3) The title 'Saviour of the world' was used by Roman emperors (soter tou kosmou was applied to Augustus and others); these Samaritans confess that the true Saviour is not Caesar but Jesus. (4) The progression from 1:29 ('Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world') through 3:16 ('God so loved the world') to 4:42 ('the Saviour of the world') establishes the universal scope of Jesus' mission.

verses.kjv_text: Now after two days he departed thence, and went into Galilee.
verses.original_text: μετὰ δὲ τὰς δύο ἡμέρας ἐξῆλθεν ἐκεῖθεν καὶ ἀπῆλθεν εἰς τὴν Γαλιλαίαν
verses.transliteration: meta de tas duo hēmeras exēlthen ekeithen kai apēlthen eis tēn Galilaian
verses.study_note: After the two days in Samaria, Jesus continues to Galilee. The narrative transitions from the Samaritan harvest to the Galilean response, and from the conversation about worship to the second sign at Cana.

verses.kjv_text: For Jesus himself testified, that a prophet hath no honour in his own country.
verses.original_text: αὐτὸς γὰρ Ἰησοῦς ἐμαρτύρησεν ὅτι προφήτης ἐν τῇ ἰδίᾳ πατρίδι τιμὴν οὐκ ἔχει
verses.transliteration: autos gar Iēsous emartyrēsen hoti prophētēs en tē idia patridi timēn ouk echei
verses.study_note: 'A prophet hath no honour in his own country' (prophētēs en tē idia patridi timēn ouk echei). In the Synoptics, 'his own country' is Nazareth (Mark 6:1-6; Luke 4:24). In John's context, 'his own country' may refer to Judaea (where he was rejected) or Galilee (where the reception will be superficial, based on signs rather than faith). The Samaritans — outsiders — received him with genuine faith; his own people will require signs.

verses.kjv_text: Then when he was come into Galilee, the Galilaeans received him, having seen all the things that he did at Jerusalem at the feast: for they also went unto the feast.
verses.original_text: ὅτε οὖν ἦλθεν εἰς τὴν Γαλιλαίαν ἐδέξαντο αὐτὸν οἱ Γαλιλαῖοι πάντα ἑωρακότες ἃ ἐποίησεν ἐν Ἱεροσολύμοις ἐν τῇ ἑορτῇ καὶ αὐτοὶ γὰρ ἦλθον εἰς τὴν ἑορτήν
verses.transliteration: hote oun ēlthen eis tēn Galilaian edexanto auton hoi Galilaioi panta heōrakotes ha epoiēsen en Hierosolymois en tē heortē kai autoi gar ēlthon eis tēn heortēn
verses.study_note: 'Received him' (edexanto — welcomed him). The Galileans welcome Jesus, but their reception is based on having seen his miracles at Jerusalem (cf. 2:23). This is sign-based faith — genuine but shallow. The contrast with the Samaritans is deliberate: the Samaritans believed because of Jesus' word (vv.41-42); the Galileans are impressed by what they saw. Jesus will address this directly in v.48.

verses.kjv_text: So Jesus came again into Cana of Galilee, where he made the water wine. And there was a certain nobleman, whose son was sick at Capernaum.
verses.original_text: ἦλθεν οὖν ὁ Ἰησοῦς πάλιν εἰς τὴν Κανᾶ τῆς Γαλιλαίας ὅπου ἐποίησεν τὸ ὕδωρ οἶνον καὶ ἦν τις βασιλικὸς οὗ ὁ υἱὸς ἠσθένει ἐν Καπερναούμ
verses.transliteration: ēlthen oun ho Iēsous palin eis tēn Kana tēs Galilaias hopou epoiēsen to hydōr oinon kai ēn tis basilikos hou ho huios ēsthenei en Kapernaoum
verses.study_note: 'Where he made the water wine' — John connects the second sign back to the first (2:1-11). 'A certain nobleman' (basilikos — a royal official, someone connected to the court, likely of Herod Antipas). 'Whose son was sick' (hou ho huios ēsthenei — whose son was ailing, weak). Capernaum is about 20 miles from Cana. The official's desperation drives him to make the journey.

verses.kjv_text: When he heard that Jesus was come out of Judaea into Galilee, he went unto him, and besought him that he would come down, and heal his son: for he was at the point of death.
verses.original_text: οὗτος ἀκούσας ὅτι Ἰησοῦς ἥκει ἐκ τῆς Ἰουδαίας εἰς τὴν Γαλιλαίαν ἀπῆλθεν πρὸς αὐτὸν καὶ ἠρώτα αὐτὸν ἵνα καταβῇ καὶ ἰάσηται αὐτοῦ τὸν υἱόν ἤμελλεν γὰρ ἀποθνήσκειν
verses.transliteration: houtos akousas hoti Iēsous hēkei ek tēs Ioudaias eis tēn Galilaian apēlthen pros auton kai ērōta auton hina katabē kai iasētai autou ton huion ēmellen gar apothnēskein
verses.study_note: 'Besought him' (ērōta — kept asking, imperfect: persistent request). 'Come down' (katabē — descend; Cana is in the hills, Capernaum on the lake shore). 'At the point of death' (ēmellen apothnēskein — was about to die). The nobleman's faith has a critical limitation: he believes Jesus must be physically present to heal. Jesus will challenge and deepen this faith.

verses.kjv_text: Then said Jesus unto him, Except ye see signs and wonders, ye will not believe.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν οὖν ὁ Ἰησοῦς πρὸς αὐτόν Ἐὰν μὴ σημεῖα καὶ τέρατα ἴδητε οὐ μὴ πιστεύσητε
verses.transliteration: eipen oun ho Iēsous pros auton Ean mē sēmeia kai terata idēte ou mē pisteusēte
verses.study_note: 'Except ye see signs and wonders' (ean mē sēmeia kai terata idēte — unless you [plural] see signs and wonders). The plural 'ye' addresses not just the nobleman but the Galileans generally (cf. v.45). 'Signs and wonders' (sēmeia kai terata) — John usually says 'signs' alone; adding 'wonders' echoes the Exodus language (Exodus 7:3; Deuteronomy 6:22) and implies a faith that demands spectacular evidence. Jesus challenges the Galileans' dependence on miracles — the Samaritans believed without them (v.41).

verses.kjv_text: The nobleman saith unto him, Sir, come down ere my child die.
verses.original_text: λέγει πρὸς αὐτὸν ὁ βασιλικός Κύριε κατάβηθι πρὶν ἀποθανεῖν τὸ παιδίον μου
verses.transliteration: legei pros auton ho basilikos Kyrie katabēthi prin apothanein to paidion mou
verses.study_note: 'Sir, come down ere my child die' (Kyrie katabēthi — Lord, come down). The nobleman does not argue theology; he pleads as a father. 'My child' (to paidion mou — my little child; the diminutive conveys tenderness). His faith is imperfect (he still thinks Jesus must be present) but genuine (he persists despite Jesus' rebuke). Desperation can be a pathway to deeper faith.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus saith unto him, Go thy way; thy son liveth. And the man believed the word that Jesus had spoken unto him, and he went his way.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς Πορεύου ὁ υἱός σου ζῇ καὶ ἐπίστευσεν ὁ ἄνθρωπος τῷ λόγῳ ᾧ εἶπεν αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ ἐπορεύετο
verses.transliteration: legei autō ho Iēsous Poreuou ho huios sou zē kai episteusen ho anthrōpos tō logō hō eipen autō ho Iēsous kai eporeueto
verses.study_note: 'Go thy way; thy son liveth' (poreuou ho huios sou zē — go, your son lives; present tense: is living right now). 'The man believed the word' (episteusen tō logō — believed the word; dative: trusted in the word). This is the critical moment: the nobleman believed Jesus' word without seeing the result. He must walk 20 miles back to Capernaum with nothing but Jesus' word to sustain him. His faith has been elevated from sign-based ('come down and heal') to word-based ('he believed the word'). This is the faith Jesus commends: trusting his word without sight (cf. 20:29).
verses.doctrinal_note: The nobleman's faith journey models the progression John seeks for all readers: (1) He hears about Jesus (v.47) — report-based interest. (2) He comes to Jesus (v.47) — action on incomplete faith. (3) He asks for a miracle (v.47) — sign-dependent faith. (4) He believes Jesus' word (v.50) — word-based faith. (5) His faith is confirmed (v.53) — experience validates faith. (6) His whole house believes (v.53) — faith becomes communal. The key transition is step 4: believing the word without seeing the sign. This is the faith John's Gospel seeks to produce: 'blessed are they that have not seen, and yet have believed' (20:29).

verses.kjv_text: And as he was now going down, his servants met him, and told him, saying, Thy son liveth.
verses.original_text: ἤδη δὲ αὐτοῦ καταβαίνοντος οἱ δοῦλοι αὐτοῦ ἀπήντησαν αὐτῷ καὶ ἀπήγγειλαν λέγοντες ὅτι Ὁ παῖς σου ζῇ
verses.transliteration: ēdē de autou katabainontos hoi douloi autou apēntēsan autō kai apēngeilan legontes hoti Ho pais sou zē
verses.study_note: 'Going down' (katabainontos — descending; Cana to Capernaum is downhill). 'Thy son liveth' (ho pais sou zē — your boy lives; the servants use the same words Jesus spoke, zē: lives, present tense). The servants' report confirms Jesus' word. The healing was instantaneous and remote — Jesus did not need to be present. This sign reveals Jesus' authority over space: his word operates at a distance.

verses.kjv_text: Then enquired he of them the hour when he began to amend. And they said unto him, Yesterday at the seventh hour the fever left him.
verses.original_text: ἐπύθετο οὖν παρ᾽ αὐτῶν τὴν ὥραν ἐν ᾗ κομψότερον ἔσχεν καὶ εἶπον αὐτῷ ὅτι Χθὲς ὥραν ἑβδόμην ἀφῆκεν αὐτὸν ὁ πυρετός
verses.transliteration: epytheto oun par autōn tēn hōran en hē kompsoteron eschen kai eipon autō hoti Chthes hōran hebdomēn aphēken auton ho pyretos
verses.study_note: 'The seventh hour' (hōran hebdomēn — 1 PM by Jewish reckoning, or 7 PM by Roman). 'The fever left him' (aphēken auton ho pyretos — the fever released him, let him go). The nobleman inquires about the exact hour — he wants to verify the connection between Jesus' word and the healing. The correlation is exact: the moment Jesus spoke, the boy was healed. This precision eliminates coincidence and confirms divine causation.

verses.kjv_text: So the father knew that it was at the same hour, in the which Jesus said unto him, Thy son liveth: and himself believed, and his whole house.
verses.original_text: ἔγνω οὖν ὁ πατὴρ ὅτι ἐν ἐκείνῃ τῇ ὥρᾳ ἐν ᾗ εἶπεν αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ὁ υἱός σου ζῇ καὶ ἐπίστευσεν αὐτὸς καὶ ἡ οἰκία αὐτοῦ ὅλη
verses.transliteration: egnō oun ho patēr hoti en ekeinē tē hōra en hē eipen autō ho Iēsous Ho huios sou zē kai episteusen autos kai hē oikia autou holē
verses.study_note: 'At the same hour' (en ekeinē tē hōra — at that very hour). 'Himself believed' (episteusen autos — he himself believed). 'And his whole house' (hē oikia autou holē — his entire household). The nobleman's faith deepens: from initial request (v.47) to trusting the word (v.50) to confirmed faith (v.53). 'His whole house' — a household conversion (cf. Acts 10:2; 16:15, 31-34; 18:8). The sign produces not just individual but communal faith.

verses.kjv_text: This is again the second miracle that Jesus did, when he was come out of Judaea into Galilee.
verses.original_text: τοῦτο πάλιν δεύτερον σημεῖον ἐποίησεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἐλθὼν ἐκ τῆς Ἰουδαίας εἰς τὴν Γαλιλαίαν
verses.transliteration: touto palin deuteron sēmeion epoiēsen ho Iēsous elthōn ek tēs Ioudaias eis tēn Galilaian
verses.study_note: 'The second miracle' (deuteron sēmeion — the second sign). John counts Galilean signs: the first was water to wine at Cana (2:11); this is the second, also connected to Cana. Between these two Galilean signs, Jesus performed signs in Jerusalem (2:23; 3:2) which John does not number. The numbering may indicate a 'signs source' that John used, or simply John's literary structure connecting Cana-to-Cana. Both Cana signs involve transformation at a distance: water to wine, death to life.

meta.overview: John 5 marks a decisive turning point in the Gospel: the first major confrontation between Jesus and the Jewish authorities. At the pool of Bethesda in Jerusalem, Jesus heals a man who has been infirm for thirty-eight years: 'Rise, take up thy bed, and walk' (v.8). The healing occurs on the Sabbath, provoking immediate conflict: 'It is the sabbath day: it is not lawful for thee to carry thy bed' (v.10). When Jesus is identified as the healer, the Jews persecute him (v.16). Jesus responds with a statement that escalates the conflict dramatically: 'My Father worketh hitherto, and I work' (v.17). The Jews understand perfectly — 'he said also that God was his Father, making himself equal with God' (v.18). What follows (vv.19-47) is the most extensive christological discourse in John: Jesus claims equality with the Father in work (v.19), love (v.20), resurrection power (v.21), judgment (v.22), and honour (v.23). 'As the Father raiseth up the dead, and quickeneth them; even so the Son quickeneth whom he will' (v.21). 'The Father judgeth no man, but hath committed all judgment unto the Son' (v.22). 'All men should honour the Son, even as they honour the Father' (v.23). 'He that heareth my word, and believeth on him that sent me, hath everlasting life, and shall not come into condemnation; but is passed from death unto life' (v.24). Jesus announces two resurrections: a present spiritual resurrection — 'The hour is coming, and now is, when the dead shall hear the voice of the Son of God: and they that hear shall live' (v.25) — and a future bodily resurrection — 'all that are in the graves shall hear his voice, And shall come forth; they that have done good, unto the resurrection of life; and they that have done evil, unto the resurrection of damnation' (vv.28-29). Jesus then presents four witnesses to his identity: John the Baptist (vv.33-35), Jesus' own works (v.36), the Father himself (vv.37-38), and the Scriptures (vv.39-40). He closes with a devastating indictment: 'Do not think that I will accuse you to the Father: there is one that accuseth you, even Moses, in whom ye trust. For had ye believed Moses, ye would have believed me: for he wrote of me' (vv.45-46).
meta.theme: The Son's Divine Authority — Equal with the Father in Work, Judgment, and Life
meta.keyWordMeaning: Authority, power, right, jurisdiction — from exesti (it is lawful, it is permitted). Exousia in John 5 refers to the delegated yet inherent authority the Son possesses from the Father. The Father has given the Son authority to give life (v.21), to execute judgment (v.22, 27), and to have life in himself (v.26). This is not mere permission but the bestowal of divine prerogatives upon One who is qualified to exercise them because he is himself God (1:1). The authority is delegated in the economy of redemption (the Father sends, the Son acts), yet inherent in nature (the Son does 'whatever the Father does,' v.19).
outline: The Pool of Bethesda: Thirty-Eight Years of Infirmity (vv.1-9): After this there was a feast of the Jews and Jesus went up to Jerusalem. Now there is at Jerusalem by the sheep market a pool called Bethesda having five porches. In these lay a great multitude of impotent folk blind halt withered waiting for the moving of the water. A certain man was there which had an infirmity thirty and eight years. Jesus saith unto him — Wilt thou be made whole? The impotent man answered — Sir I have no man when the water is troubled to put me into the pool. Jesus saith — Rise take up thy bed and walk. Immediately the man was made whole and took up his bed and walked — and on the same day was the sabbath

The Sabbath Controversy (vv.10-18): The Jews said unto him that was cured — It is the sabbath day it is not lawful for thee to carry thy bed. He answered — He that made me whole the same said unto me Take up thy bed and walk. They asked — What man is that? He that was healed wist not who it was for Jesus had conveyed himself away. Afterward Jesus findeth him in the temple — Behold thou art made whole sin no more lest a worse thing come unto thee. The man departed and told the Jews that it was Jesus. Therefore did the Jews persecute Jesus because he had done these things on the sabbath. Jesus answered — My Father worketh hitherto and I work. Therefore the Jews sought the more to kill him because he said God was his Father making himself equal with God

The Son Does What the Father Does (vv.19-23): Then answered Jesus — The Son can do nothing of himself but what he seeth the Father do — for what things soever he doeth these also doeth the Son likewise. The Father loveth the Son and sheweth him all things that himself doeth. Greater works than these shall he shew you. As the Father raiseth up the dead and quickeneth them even so the Son quickeneth whom he will. The Father judgeth no man but hath committed all judgment unto the Son — that all men should honour the Son even as they honour the Father

From Death to Life (vv.24-30): Verily verily I say unto you He that heareth my word and believeth on him that sent me hath everlasting life and shall not come into condemnation but is passed from death unto life. The hour is coming and now is when the dead shall hear the voice of the Son of God and they that hear shall live. As the Father hath life in himself so hath he given to the Son to have life in himself — and hath given him authority to execute judgment because he is the Son of man. Marvel not at this — the hour is coming in the which all that are in the graves shall hear his voice and shall come forth — they that have done good unto the resurrection of life and they that have done evil unto the resurrection of damnation. I can of mine own self do nothing — as I hear I judge and my judgment is just because I seek not mine own will but the will of the Father which hath sent me

Four Witnesses to the Son (vv.31-40): If I bear witness of myself my witness is not true. There is another that beareth witness of me. Ye sent unto John and he bare witness unto the truth. I receive not testimony from man — but these things I say that ye might be saved. He was a burning and a shining light and ye were willing for a season to rejoice in his light. But I have greater witness than that of John — the works which the Father hath given me to finish the same works that I do bear witness of me. And the Father himself which hath sent me hath borne witness of me. Ye have neither heard his voice at any time nor seen his shape. Ye have not his word abiding in you. Search the scriptures for in them ye think ye have eternal life — and they are they which testify of me. And ye will not come to me that ye might have life

Moses Accuses You (vv.41-47): I receive not honour from men. But I know you that ye have not the love of God in you. I am come in my Father's name and ye receive me not — if another shall come in his own name him ye will receive. How can ye believe which receive honour one of another and seek not the honour that cometh from God only? Do not think that I will accuse you to the Father — there is one that accuseth you even Moses in whom ye trust. For had ye believed Moses ye would have believed me for he wrote of me. But if ye believe not his writings how shall ye believe my words?

verses.kjv_text: After this there was a feast of the Jews; and Jesus went up to Jerusalem.
verses.original_text: μετὰ ταῦτα ἦν ἑορτὴ τῶν Ἰουδαίων καὶ ἀνέβη ὁ Ἰησοῦς εἰς Ἱεροσόλυμα
verses.transliteration: meta tauta ēn heortē tōn Ioudaiōn kai anebē ho Iēsous eis Hierosolyma
verses.study_note: 'A feast of the Jews' (heortē tōn Ioudaiōn — some manuscripts have 'the feast,' possibly Passover or Pentecost; the identity is debated). John identifies Jewish festivals for his readers. 'Went up' (anebē — ascended) — the standard expression for going to Jerusalem, which sits on elevated ground. This is Jesus' second visit to Jerusalem in John (after 2:13).

verses.kjv_text: Now there is at Jerusalem by the sheep market a pool, which is called in the Hebrew tongue Bethesda, having five porches.
verses.original_text: ἔστιν δὲ ἐν τοῖς Ἱεροσολύμοις ἐπὶ τῇ προβατικῇ κολυμβήθρα ἡ ἐπιλεγομένη Ἑβραϊστὶ Βηθεσδά πέντε στοὰς ἔχουσα
verses.transliteration: estin de en tois Hierosolymois epi tē probatikē kolymbēthra hē epilegomenē Hebraisti Bēthesda pente stoas echousa
verses.study_note: 'Bethesda' (Bēthesda — 'House of Mercy' or 'House of the Outpouring'). 'Having five porches' (pente stoas echousa — five covered colonnades). Archaeologists discovered the pool in the 19th century near the Church of St. Anne — a trapezoid-shaped double pool with a central dividing colonnade, totaling five covered areas. The discovery confirmed John's topographical accuracy and his detailed knowledge of pre-70 AD Jerusalem. 'By the sheep market' (epi tē probatikē — near the Sheep Gate, cf. Nehemiah 3:1).

verses.kjv_text: In these lay a great multitude of impotent folk, of blind, halt, and withered, waiting for the moving of the water.
verses.original_text: ἐν ταύταις κατέκειτο πλῆθος πολὺ τῶν ἀσθενούντων τυφλῶν χωλῶν ξηρῶν ἐκδεχομένων τὴν τοῦ ὕδατος κίνησιν
verses.transliteration: en tautais katekeito plēthos poly tōn asthenountōn typhlōn chōlōn xērōn ekdechomenōn tēn tou hydatos kinēsin
verses.study_note: 'A great multitude of impotent folk' (plēthos poly tōn asthenountōn — a large crowd of the sick, weak, powerless). 'Blind, halt, and withered' (typhlōn chōlōn xērōn — blind, lame, withered/paralysed). 'Waiting for the moving of the water' — the pool was fed by an intermittent spring; popular belief attributed healing power to the stirring of the water. The scene is one of desperate, helpless humanity — a fitting context for divine intervention.

verses.kjv_text: For an angel went down at a certain season into the pool, and troubled the water: whosoever then first after the troubling of the water stepped in was made whole of whatsoever disease he had.
verses.original_text: ἄγγελος γὰρ κατὰ καιρὸν κατέβαινεν ἐν τῇ κολυμβήθρᾳ καὶ ἐτάρασσεν τὸ ὕδωρ ὁ οὖν πρῶτος ἐμβὰς μετὰ τὴν ταραχὴν τοῦ ὕδατος ὑγιὴς ἐγίνετο ᾧ δήποτε κατείχετο νοσήματι
verses.transliteration: angelos gar kata kairon katebainen en tē kolymbēthra kai etarassen to hydōr ho oun prōtos embas meta tēn tarachēn tou hydatos hygiēs egineto hō dēpote kateicheto nosēmati
verses.study_note: This verse is absent from the earliest and best Greek manuscripts (P66, P75, Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) and is likely a later scribal addition explaining the popular belief about the pool. Whether angelic or natural (an intermittent spring), the point of the narrative is that the man had no hope from this source — he needed someone greater. The textual note does not affect the story: Jesus heals where all other remedies have failed.

verses.kjv_text: And a certain man was there, which had an infirmity thirty and eight years.
verses.original_text: ἦν δέ τις ἄνθρωπος ἐκεῖ τριάκοντα καὶ ὀκτὼ ἔτη ἔχων ἐν τῇ ἀσθενείᾳ αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: ēn de tis anthrōpos ekei triakonta kai oktō etē echōn en tē astheneia autou
verses.study_note: 'Thirty and eight years' (triakonta kai oktō etē — 38 years in his infirmity). The duration emphasises the hopelessness of his condition — nearly four decades of suffering, longer than Israel's wilderness wandering (Deuteronomy 2:14: 38 years from Kadesh-barnea to crossing the Zered). The parallel may be intentional: as Israel wandered 38 years due to unbelief before entering the promised land, this man has waited 38 years before encountering the one who can make him whole.

verses.kjv_text: When Jesus saw him lie, and knew that he had been now a long time in that case, he saith unto him, Wilt thou be made whole?
verses.original_text: τοῦτον ἰδὼν ὁ Ἰησοῦς κατακείμενον καὶ γνοὺς ὅτι πολὺν ἤδη χρόνον ἔχει λέγει αὐτῷ Θέλεις ὑγιὴς γενέσθαι
verses.transliteration: touton idōn ho Iēsous katakeimenon kai gnous hoti polyn ēdē chronon echei legei autō Theleis hygiēs genesthai
verses.study_note: 'Knew' (gnous — having known, perceived; Jesus' supernatural knowledge again, cf. 2:24-25; 4:18). 'Wilt thou be made whole?' (theleis hygiēs genesthai — do you want to become well?). The question seems obvious, but it is pastorally profound: (1) It awakens desire — the man's hope may have died after 38 years. (2) It invites personal engagement — Jesus does not heal impersonally but relationally. (3) It probes whether the man wants healing on Jesus' terms, not his own. The man does not answer 'yes' but explains his helplessness (v.7).

verses.kjv_text: The impotent man answered him, Sir, I have no man, when the water is troubled, to put me into the pool: but while I am coming, another steppeth down before me.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη αὐτῷ ὁ ἀσθενῶν Κύριε ἄνθρωπον οὐκ ἔχω ἵνα ὅταν ταραχθῇ τὸ ὕδωρ βάλῃ με εἰς τὴν κολυμβήθραν ἐν ᾧ δὲ ἔρχομαι ἐγώ ἄλλος πρὸ ἐμοῦ καταβαίνει
verses.transliteration: apekrithē autō ho asthenōn Kyrie anthrōpon ouk echō hina hotan tarachthē to hydōr balē me eis tēn kolymbēthran en hō de erchomai egō allos pro emou katabainei
verses.study_note: 'I have no man' (anthrōpon ouk echō — I have no person, no one to help me). The pathos is intense: friendless, helpless, always too slow. His answer reveals his hopelessness — he has given up on the pool but has nothing else. He does not yet know he is speaking to the One who needs no pool, no angel, no human helper. 'While I am coming, another steppeth down before me' — the competitive, first-come system excludes the weakest. Jesus does not work this way; he goes to the one with no helper.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus saith unto him, Rise, take up thy bed, and walk.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ἔγειρε ἆρον τὸν κράβαττόν σου καὶ περιπάτει
verses.transliteration: legei autō ho Iēsous Egeire aron ton krabatton sou kai peripatei
verses.study_note: 'Rise' (egeire — get up, arise; the same verb used for resurrection, egeirō). 'Take up thy bed' (aron ton krabatton sou — pick up your mat/pallet; krabattos is a poor man's sleeping mat). 'Walk' (peripatei — walk around, keep walking; present imperative: continuous action). Three commands requiring obedience in faith: the man must act before he can see the result. He must attempt to rise before he discovers he is able. Jesus' word carries creative power — the command itself enables the obedience it demands.

verses.kjv_text: And immediately the man was made whole, and took up his bed, and walked: and on the same day was the sabbath.
verses.original_text: καὶ εὐθέως ἐγένετο ὑγιὴς ὁ ἄνθρωπος καὶ ἦρεν τὸν κράβαττον αὐτοῦ καὶ περιεπάτει ἦν δὲ σάββατον ἐν ἐκείνῃ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ
verses.transliteration: kai eutheōs egeneto hygiēs ho anthrōpos kai ēren ton krabatton autou kai periepatei ēn de sabbaton en ekeinē tē hēmera
verses.study_note: 'Immediately' (eutheōs — at once, instantly). 'Was made whole' (egeneto hygiēs — became well, was healed completely). 'On the same day was the sabbath' — John saves this crucial detail for last, creating dramatic tension. The healing on the Sabbath is not accidental but deliberate — Jesus chose this day. In the Synoptics, Jesus performs multiple Sabbath healings (Mark 3:1-6; Luke 13:10-17; 14:1-6). Here in John, the Sabbath controversy leads to the most extensive discourse on Jesus' divine authority.

verses.kjv_text: The Jews therefore said unto him that was cured, It is the sabbath day: it is not lawful for thee to carry thy bed.
verses.original_text: ἔλεγον οὖν οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι τῷ τεθεραπευμένῳ Σάββατόν ἐστιν οὐκ ἔξεστίν σοι ἆραι τὸν κράβαττον
verses.transliteration: elegon oun hoi Ioudaioi tō tetherapeumenō Sabbaton estin ouk exestin soi arai ton krabatton
verses.study_note: 'It is not lawful' (ouk exestin — it is not permitted). The Mishnah (Shabbat 7:2) lists 39 categories of work prohibited on the Sabbath; carrying objects from one domain to another was among them. The Jews see the bed-carrying but not the miracle — they focus on the letter of the law while missing the compassion of God. Jeremiah 17:21-22 prohibited carrying burdens on the Sabbath, but this prohibition was about commercial activity, not a healed man carrying his mat.

verses.kjv_text: He answered them, He that made me whole, the same said unto me, Take up thy bed, and walk.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη αὐτοῖς Ὁ ποιήσας με ὑγιῆ ἐκεῖνος μοι εἶπεν Ἆρον τὸν κράβαττόν σου καὶ περιπάτει
verses.transliteration: apekrithē autois Ho poiēsas me hygiē ekeinos moi eipen Aron ton krabatton sou kai peripatei
verses.study_note: The healed man's logic is simple and sound: the one who had the power to heal after 38 years surely has the authority to command bed-carrying on the Sabbath. The authority to heal implies authority over the Sabbath. The man does not yet know Jesus' name (v.13) but recognises the authority behind the command.

verses.kjv_text: Then asked they him, What man is that which said unto thee, Take up thy bed, and walk?
verses.original_text: ἠρώτησαν οὖν αὐτόν Τίς ἐστιν ὁ ἄνθρωπος ὁ εἰπών σοι Ἆρον τὸν κράβαττόν σου καὶ περιπάτει
verses.transliteration: ērōtēsan oun auton Tis estin ho anthrōpos ho eipōn soi Aron ton krabatton sou kai peripatei
verses.study_note: The Jews ask not 'Who healed you?' but 'Who told you to carry your bed?' — they are more interested in identifying the Sabbath-breaker than celebrating the miracle. Their question reveals misplaced priorities: legal compliance over human compassion. This pattern recurs throughout John (cf. 9:13-16, 24).

verses.kjv_text: And he that was healed wist not who it was: for Jesus had conveyed himself away, a multitude being in that place.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ ἰαθεὶς οὐκ ᾔδει τίς ἐστιν ὁ γὰρ Ἰησοῦς ἐξένευσεν ὄχλου ὄντος ἐν τῷ τόπῳ
verses.transliteration: ho de iatheis ouk ēdei tis estin ho gar Iēsous exeneusen ochlou ontos en tō topō
verses.study_note: 'Wist not' (ouk ēdei — did not know). 'Conveyed himself away' (exeneusen — slipped away, withdrew quietly; from ekneuō, to turn aside). Jesus healed anonymously and departed — he was not seeking publicity. The man received healing before knowing who healed him, illustrating that grace precedes knowledge. Jesus will later find the man (v.14) and complete the encounter.

verses.kjv_text: Afterward Jesus findeth him in the temple, and said unto him, Behold, thou art made whole: sin no more, lest a worse thing come unto thee.
verses.original_text: μετὰ ταῦτα εὑρίσκει αὐτὸν ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἐν τῷ ἱερῷ καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ Ἴδε ὑγιὴς γέγονας μηκέτι ἁμάρτανε ἵνα μὴ χεῖρόν σοί τι γένηται
verses.transliteration: meta tauta heuriskei auton ho Iēsous en tō hierō kai eipen autō Ide hygiēs gegonas mēketi hamartane hina mē cheiron soi ti genētai
verses.study_note: 'Findeth him' (heuriskei — finds, seeks out). 'In the temple' — the man has gone to worship, suggesting gratitude. 'Sin no more' (mēketi hamartane — stop sinning, cease sinning; present imperative with mēketi: do not continue in sin). 'Lest a worse thing come unto thee' — Jesus implies a connection between this man's condition and sin (not universal: cf. 9:3 where Jesus denies such a connection). The 'worse thing' is not more illness but spiritual judgment. Physical healing is temporary; spiritual transformation is what truly matters.

verses.kjv_text: The man departed, and told the Jews that it was Jesus, which had made him whole.
verses.original_text: ἀπῆλθεν ὁ ἄνθρωπος καὶ ἀνήγγειλεν τοῖς Ἰουδαίοις ὅτι Ἰησοῦς ἐστιν ὁ ποιήσας αὐτὸν ὑγιῆ
verses.transliteration: apēlthen ho anthrōpos kai anēngeilen tois Ioudaiois hoti Iēsous estin ho poiēsas auton hygiē
verses.study_note: The man reports to the Jewish authorities that it was Jesus. His motive is debated: informing (neutral), testifying (positive), or betraying (negative). The text does not condemn him, and 'made him whole' (poiēsas auton hygiē) focuses on the healing, not the Sabbath-breaking. The result, however, is that Jesus is now identified and targeted.

verses.kjv_text: And therefore did the Jews persecute Jesus, and sought to slay him, because he had done these things on the sabbath day.
verses.original_text: καὶ διὰ τοῦτο ἐδίωκον οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι τὸν Ἰησοῦν καὶ ἐζήτουν αὐτὸν ἀποκτεῖναι ὅτι ταῦτα ἐποίει ἐν σαββάτῳ
verses.transliteration: kai dia touto ediōkon hoi Ioudaioi ton Iēsoun kai ezētoun auton apokteinai hoti tauta epoiei en sabbatō
verses.study_note: 'Persecuted' (ediōkon — pursued, harassed, persecuted). 'Sought to slay him' (ezētoun auton apokteinai — were seeking to kill him). The persecution is not for the healing but for doing it on the Sabbath. The imperfect tenses (ediōkon, ezētoun) indicate ongoing, sustained hostility. This is the first mention of a plot to kill Jesus in John (cf. 7:1, 19, 25; 8:37, 40; 11:53).

verses.kjv_text: But Jesus answered them, My Father worketh hitherto, and I work.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ Ἰησοῦς ἀπεκρίνατο αὐτοῖς Ὁ πατήρ μου ἕως ἄρτι ἐργάζεται κἀγὼ ἐργάζομαι
verses.transliteration: ho de Iēsous apekrinato autois Ho patēr mou heōs arti ergazetai kagō ergazomai
verses.study_note: 'My Father worketh hitherto' (ho patēr mou heōs arti ergazetai — my Father is working until now). 'And I work' (kagō ergazomai — and I am working). The rabbis acknowledged that God continues to work on the Sabbath (sustaining creation, giving life, judging the dead — activities that cannot cease). Jesus claims the same prerogative: as the Father works continuously, so does the Son. 'My Father' (ho patēr mou — not 'our Father' but 'my Father') claims a unique filial relationship. The implications are staggering: Jesus claims to operate on the same level as God.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse is the trigger for the entire discourse of vv.19-47. The Jewish leaders correctly understand Jesus' claim: (1) He calls God 'my Father' in a unique, exclusive sense. (2) He claims the right to work as God works — even on the Sabbath. (3) He is 'making himself equal with God' (v.18). Their conclusion is theologically accurate: Jesus is claiming deity. Their error is in rejecting the claim rather than accepting it. The discourse that follows is Jesus' sustained defense of this equality — not as an independent rival to God, but as the Son who does exactly what the Father does.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore the Jews sought the more to kill him, because he not only had broken the sabbath, but said also that God was his Father, making himself equal with God.
verses.original_text: διὰ τοῦτο οὖν μᾶλλον ἐζήτουν αὐτὸν οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι ἀποκτεῖναι ὅτι οὐ μόνον ἔλυεν τὸ σάββατον ἀλλὰ καὶ πατέρα ἴδιον ἔλεγεν τὸν θεόν ἴσον ἑαυτὸν ποιῶν τῷ θεῷ
verses.transliteration: dia touto oun mallon ezētoun auton hoi Ioudaioi apokteinai hoti ou monon elyen to sabbaton alla kai patera idion elegen ton theon ison heauton poiōn tō theō
verses.study_note: 'Making himself equal with God' (ison heauton poiōn tō theō — making himself equal/identical to God). Isos means 'equal in quality, quantity, and nature.' The Jews understand Jesus correctly: (1) He broke the Sabbath (or rather, claimed divine authority over it). (2) He called God 'his own Father' (patera idion — his own, personal Father; idion emphasises the unique, exclusive nature of the claim). (3) He made himself equal with God. These are not misunderstandings but accurate perceptions. Jesus does not correct them — instead, he amplifies the claims in vv.19-47.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Jewish charge that Jesus was 'making himself equal with God' is confirmed, not denied, by the discourse that follows. Jesus does not say 'You misunderstand me — I am not equal with God.' Instead, he explains the nature of his equality: it is not independent autonomy (v.19: 'The Son can do nothing of himself') but perfect correspondence with the Father ('what things soever he doeth, these also doeth the Son likewise'). The Son is equal in nature but subordinate in role: the Father sends, the Son obeys; the Father gives, the Son receives; yet what the Father does, the Son does equally. This is the pattern later formulated as the doctrine of the Trinity.

verses.kjv_text: Then answered Jesus and said unto them, Verily, verily, I say unto you, The Son can do nothing of himself, but what he seeth the Father do: for what things soever he doeth, these also doeth the Son likewise.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίνατο οὖν ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς Ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν οὐ δύναται ὁ υἱὸς ποιεῖν ἀφ᾽ ἑαυτοῦ οὐδέν ἐὰν μή τι βλέπῃ τὸν πατέρα ποιοῦντα ἃ γὰρ ἂν ἐκεῖνος ποιῇ ταῦτα καὶ ὁ υἱὸς ὁμοίως ποιεῖ
verses.transliteration: apekrinato oun ho Iēsous kai eipen autois Amēn amēn legō hymin ou dynatai ho huios poiein aph heautou ouden ean mē ti blepē ton patera poiounta ha gar an ekeinos poiē tauta kai ho huios homoiōs poiei
verses.study_note: 'The Son can do nothing of himself' (ou dynatai ho huios poiein aph heautou ouden — the Son is not able to do anything from himself). This is not a confession of weakness but of perfect unity: the Son does not act independently because he and the Father always act in concert. 'What he seeth the Father do' (blepē ton patera poiounta — sees the Father doing). 'These also doeth the Son likewise' (tauta kai ho huios homoiōs poiei — these same things the Son also does in the same way). The Son's works are not different from or inferior to the Father's — they are the same works done in the same manner. This is equality expressed through relational harmony.

verses.kjv_text: For the Father loveth the Son, and sheweth him all things that himself doeth: and he will shew him greater works than these, that ye may marvel.
verses.original_text: ὁ γὰρ πατὴρ φιλεῖ τὸν υἱὸν καὶ πάντα δείκνυσιν αὐτῷ ἃ αὐτὸς ποιεῖ καὶ μείζονα τούτων δείξει αὐτῷ ἔργα ἵνα ὑμεῖς θαυμάζητε
verses.transliteration: ho gar patēr philei ton huion kai panta deiknusin autō ha autos poiei kai meizona toutōn deixei autō erga hina hymeis thaumazēte
verses.study_note: 'The Father loveth the Son' (ho patēr philei ton huion — uses phileō, denoting warm affection, intimate love, as between close friends or family; cf. 3:35 which uses agapaō). 'Sheweth him all things' (panta deiknusin autō — shows, reveals everything to him). The Father holds nothing back from the Son — complete transparency, complete trust. 'Greater works' (meizona erga) — the healing at Bethesda was impressive, but greater works are coming: raising the dead (vv.21, 25-29) and executing final judgment (v.22).

verses.kjv_text: For as the Father raiseth up the dead, and quickeneth them; even so the Son quickeneth whom he will.
verses.original_text: ὥσπερ γὰρ ὁ πατὴρ ἐγείρει τοὺς νεκροὺς καὶ ζωοποιεῖ οὕτως καὶ ὁ υἱὸς οὓς θέλει ζωοποιεῖ
verses.transliteration: hōsper gar ho patēr egeirei tous nekrous kai zōopoiei houtōs kai ho huios hous thelei zōopoiei
verses.study_note: 'As the Father raiseth up the dead and quickeneth them' (hōsper ho patēr egeirei tous nekrous kai zōopoiei — just as the Father raises the dead and gives them life). 'Even so the Son quickeneth whom he will' (houtōs kai ho huios hous thelei zōopoiei — so also the Son gives life to whomever he wills). Giving life to the dead is exclusively a divine prerogative in the OT (Deuteronomy 32:39; 1 Samuel 2:6; 2 Kings 5:7). Jesus claims this power as his own — and exercises it sovereignly ('whom he will'). This is not delegated permission but shared divine authority.
verses.doctrinal_note: The claim to raise the dead and give life is one of the most extraordinary in all of Scripture. In the OT, giving life is God's exclusive prerogative: 'I kill, and I make alive' (Deuteronomy 32:39). 'The LORD killeth, and maketh alive: he bringeth down to the grave, and bringeth up' (1 Samuel 2:6). When Naaman came for healing, the king of Israel said, 'Am I God, to kill and to make alive?' (2 Kings 5:7). Jesus claims precisely this power — and not as a mere instrument but sovereignly: 'whom he will' (hous thelei). The Son's will in giving life is identical to the Father's will.

verses.kjv_text: For the Father judgeth no man, but hath committed all judgment unto the Son:
verses.original_text: οὐδὲ γὰρ ὁ πατὴρ κρίνει οὐδένα ἀλλὰ τὴν κρίσιν πᾶσαν δέδωκεν τῷ υἱῷ
verses.transliteration: oude gar ho patēr krinei oudena alla tēn krisin pasan dedōken tō huiō
verses.study_note: 'Hath committed all judgment unto the Son' (tēn krisin pasan dedōken tō huiō — has given all judgment to the Son; perfect tense: a completed, permanent transfer). Judgment, like giving life (v.21), is a divine prerogative. The Father has entrusted the entire judicial function to the Son. This fulfils Daniel 7:13-14, where 'one like the Son of man' receives authority and dominion. The transfer is complete — 'all judgment' (pasan), without exception.

verses.kjv_text: That all men should honour the Son, even as they honour the Father. He that honoureth not the Son honoureth not the Father which hath sent him.
verses.original_text: ἵνα πάντες τιμῶσιν τὸν υἱὸν καθὼς τιμῶσιν τὸν πατέρα ὁ μὴ τιμῶν τὸν υἱὸν οὐ τιμᾷ τὸν πατέρα τὸν πέμψαντα αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: hina pantes timōsin ton huion kathōs timōsin ton patera ho mē timōn ton huion ou tima ton patera ton pempsanta auton
verses.study_note: 'Honour the Son, even as they honour the Father' (timōsin ton huion kathōs timōsin ton patera — the same honour, the same reverence, the same worship). 'Even as' (kathōs — in the same manner, to the same degree). This is a demand for equal honour — the kind of honour due to God. 'He that honoureth not the Son honoureth not the Father' — to reject the Son is to reject the Father who sent him. There is no path to the Father that bypasses the Son (cf. 14:6). No one can claim to worship God while dishonouring Jesus.
verses.doctrinal_note: John 5:23 is one of the clearest statements of Christ's deity in the NT. The demand for equal honour with the Father would be blasphemous if Jesus were merely human. If Jesus is a created being — even the highest — to demand the same honour as the Creator would be idolatry. Yet Jesus makes exactly this demand, and the Father endorses it by giving all judgment to the Son (v.22). The logic is clear: equal work (v.19), equal life-giving power (v.21), equal judicial authority (v.22), therefore equal honour (v.23). To deny the Son's deity is to deny the Father who sent him.

verses.kjv_text: Verily, verily, I say unto you, He that heareth my word, and believeth on him that sent me, hath everlasting life, and shall not come into condemnation; but is passed from death unto life.
verses.original_text: ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι ὁ τὸν λόγον μου ἀκούων καὶ πιστεύων τῷ πέμψαντί με ἔχει ζωὴν αἰώνιον καὶ εἰς κρίσιν οὐκ ἔρχεται ἀλλὰ μεταβέβηκεν ἐκ τοῦ θανάτου εἰς τὴν ζωήν
verses.transliteration: amēn amēn legō hymin hoti ho ton logon mou akouōn kai pisteuōn tō pempsanti me echei zōēn aiōnion kai eis krisin ouk erchetai alla metabebēken ek tou thanatou eis tēn zōēn
verses.study_note: 'Heareth my word' (ton logon mou akouōn — present participle: the one hearing, listening to my word). 'Believeth on him that sent me' (pisteuōn tō pempsanti me — believing the one who sent me; faith in the Son is faith in the Father). 'Hath everlasting life' (echei zōēn aiōnion — present tense: possesses eternal life now). 'Shall not come into condemnation' (eis krisin ouk erchetai — does not come into judgment). 'Is passed from death unto life' (metabebēken ek tou thanatou eis tēn zōēn — has crossed over, transferred; perfect tense: a completed transition with permanent results). Three assurances: (1) present possession of eternal life, (2) no future condemnation, (3) the transition from death to life is already accomplished.
verses.doctrinal_note: John 5:24 is one of the most comprehensive salvation statements in Scripture. It contains: (1) The condition — hearing Christ's word and believing the Father who sent him. (2) The present result — 'hath' (echei, present tense) everlasting life. Eternal life is not a future reward but a present possession. (3) The judicial result — 'shall not come into condemnation' — the believer will never face judgment for sin. (4) The ontological result — 'is passed from death unto life' (metabebēken — perfect tense: has permanently crossed over). The believer has already made the decisive transition from the realm of death to the realm of life. This parallels 3:18 and anticipates Romans 8:1.

verses.kjv_text: Verily, verily, I say unto you, The hour is coming, and now is, when the dead shall hear the voice of the Son of God: and they that hear shall live.
verses.original_text: ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι ἔρχεται ὥρα καὶ νῦν ἐστιν ὅτε οἱ νεκροὶ ἀκούσονται τῆς φωνῆς τοῦ υἱοῦ τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ οἱ ἀκούσαντες ζήσονται
verses.transliteration: amēn amēn legō hymin hoti erchetai hōra kai nyn estin hote hoi nekroi akousontai tēs phōnēs tou huiou tou theou kai hoi akousantes zēsontai
verses.study_note: 'The hour is coming, and now is' (erchetai hōra kai nyn estin — the 'already but not yet' of Jesus' ministry). 'The dead shall hear the voice of the Son of God' (hoi nekroi akousontai tēs phōnēs tou huiou tou theou). The 'dead' here are the spiritually dead (as distinct from the physically dead in vv.28-29). 'They that hear shall live' — hearing here means responsive, faith-filled hearing that results in spiritual life. The voice of the Son of God has life-giving power: as God spoke creation into existence (Genesis 1:3), so the Son speaks spiritual life into dead souls.

verses.kjv_text: For as the Father hath life in himself; so hath he given to the Son to have life in himself;
verses.original_text: ὥσπερ γὰρ ὁ πατὴρ ἔχει ζωὴν ἐν ἑαυτῷ οὕτως ἔδωκεν καὶ τῷ υἱῷ ζωὴν ἔχειν ἐν ἑαυτῷ
verses.transliteration: hōsper gar ho patēr echei zōēn en heautō houtōs edōken kai tō huiō zōēn echein en heautō
verses.study_note: 'Life in himself' (zōēn en heautō — self-existent, self-sustaining, underived life). God's life is not received from any external source — it is intrinsic, essential, self-generated. 'Given to the Son to have life in himself' — the Son possesses the same quality of self-existent life. The 'giving' refers to the eternal relationship within the Trinity, not a temporal grant: the Father eternally generates the Son, who eternally possesses the same divine life. No created being has 'life in himself' — all creature life is derived and dependent. The Son's possession of self-existent life is a mark of his full deity.
verses.doctrinal_note: This is one of the most metaphysically profound statements in Scripture. Only God has life 'in himself' — aseity, self-existence. All other life is derived (from God), dependent (on God), and contingent (can cease). The Son possesses the same quality of life as the Father — not derived from creation but inherent in his divine nature. The word 'given' (edōken) does not imply that the Son once lacked this life and then received it; rather, it describes the eternal relationship within the Godhead: the Father eternally communicates his nature to the Son, and the Son eternally possesses it. This is the theological basis for Jesus' claim to give life (v.21) and to execute judgment (v.22, 27).

verses.kjv_text: And hath given him authority to execute judgment also, because he is the Son of man.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐξουσίαν ἔδωκεν αὐτῷ καὶ κρίσιν ποιεῖν ὅτι υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου ἐστίν
verses.transliteration: kai exousian edōken autō kai krisin poiein hoti huios anthrōpou estin
verses.study_note: 'Authority' (exousian — right, jurisdiction, delegated power). 'Because he is the Son of man' (hoti huios anthrōpou estin — because he is Son of man; notably without the article, emphasising the quality of humanity). Jesus executes judgment because he is both divine (v.22: the Father gives all judgment to the Son) and human (v.27: as Son of man, he has experienced human life and can judge with experiential knowledge). The reference is to Daniel 7:13-14, where 'one like the Son of man' receives authority, glory, and an everlasting kingdom. The judge of humanity is himself human — he judges from within, not from without.

verses.kjv_text: Marvel not at this: for the hour is coming, in the which all that are in the graves shall hear his voice,
verses.original_text: μὴ θαυμάζετε τοῦτο ὅτι ἔρχεται ὥρα ἐν ᾗ πάντες οἱ ἐν τοῖς μνημείοις ἀκούσονται τῆς φωνῆς αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: mē thaumazete touto hoti erchetai hōra en hē pantes hoi en tois mnēmeiois akousontai tēs phōnēs autou
verses.study_note: 'All that are in the graves' (pantes hoi en tois mnēmeiois — everyone in the tombs). The shift from v.25 is critical: v.25 speaks of the spiritually dead hearing now ('the hour... now is'); v.28 speaks of the physically dead hearing in the future ('the hour is coming' — without 'and now is'). 'Shall hear his voice' — the same voice that said 'Rise, take up thy bed' (v.8) and that speaks spiritual life (v.25) will one day command universal, bodily resurrection.

verses.kjv_text: And shall come forth; they that have done good, unto the resurrection of life; and they that have done evil, unto the resurrection of damnation.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐκπορεύσονται οἱ τὰ ἀγαθὰ ποιήσαντες εἰς ἀνάστασιν ζωῆς οἱ δὲ τὰ φαῦλα πράξαντες εἰς ἀνάστασιν κρίσεως
verses.transliteration: kai ekporeusontai hoi ta agatha poiēsantes eis anastasin zōēs hoi de ta phaula praxantes eis anastasin kriseōs
verses.study_note: 'Resurrection of life' (anastasin zōēs — the resurrection that leads to life). 'Resurrection of damnation' (anastasin kriseōs — the resurrection that leads to judgment/condemnation). Two universal resurrections leading to two destinies. 'Done good' and 'done evil' describe the fruit of faith and unbelief respectively (cf. 3:20-21): those who 'do truth' come to the light; those who practice evil hate it. Good works are the evidence of faith (not the cause of salvation) — cf. v.24 where faith is the basis of passing from death to life.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse affirms the doctrine of universal bodily resurrection — both the righteous and the wicked will rise. Jesus echoes Daniel 12:2: 'Many of them that sleep in the dust of the earth shall awake, some to everlasting life, and some to shame and everlasting contempt.' The two resurrections correspond to the two responses to the Son throughout John: faith (leading to life) and unbelief (leading to judgment). Works are mentioned as evidence, not meritorious cause — the basis of salvation remains faith in the Son (v.24). The one who believes has already passed from death to life; the resurrection is the physical consummation of what has already been spiritually accomplished.

verses.kjv_text: I can of mine own self do nothing: as I hear, I judge: and my judgment is just; because I seek not mine own will, but the will of the Father which hath sent me.
verses.original_text: οὐ δύναμαι ἐγὼ ποιεῖν ἀπ᾽ ἐμαυτοῦ οὐδέν καθὼς ἀκούω κρίνω καὶ ἡ κρίσις ἡ ἐμὴ δικαία ἐστίν ὅτι οὐ ζητῶ τὸ θέλημα τὸ ἐμὸν ἀλλὰ τὸ θέλημα τοῦ πέμψαντός με
verses.transliteration: ou dynamai egō poiein ap emautou ouden kathōs akouō krinō kai hē krisis hē emē dikaia estin hoti ou zētō to thelēma to emon alla to thelēma tou pempsantos me
verses.study_note: 'I can of mine own self do nothing' (ou dynamai egō poiein ap emautou ouden — echoing v.19). 'As I hear, I judge' — Jesus' judgment is not autonomous but derived from perfect communion with the Father. 'My judgment is just' (hē krisis hē emē dikaia estin — my judgment is righteous, fair). 'I seek not mine own will but the will of the Father' — the guarantee of righteous judgment is the Son's perfect submission to the Father's will. This is not weakness but the highest form of divine operation: Father and Son acting in perfect harmony.

verses.kjv_text: If I bear witness of myself, my witness is not true.
verses.original_text: ἐὰν ἐγὼ μαρτυρῶ περὶ ἐμαυτοῦ ἡ μαρτυρία μου οὐκ ἔστιν ἀληθής
verses.transliteration: ean egō martyrō peri emautou hē martyria mou ouk estin alēthēs
verses.study_note: 'If I bear witness of myself, my witness is not true' — in Jewish law, self-testimony was insufficient (Deuteronomy 19:15: two or three witnesses required). Jesus is not saying his testimony is false but that self-testimony alone would not be legally valid. He will now present four independent witnesses (vv.33-39) to corroborate his claims. In 8:14, Jesus will say 'Though I bear record of myself, yet my record is true' — there, he asserts the inherent truthfulness of his testimony even if the legal convention requires external corroboration.

verses.kjv_text: There is another that beareth witness of me; and I know that the witness which he witnesseth of me is true.
verses.original_text: ἄλλος ἐστὶν ὁ μαρτυρῶν περὶ ἐμοῦ καὶ οἶδα ὅτι ἀληθής ἐστιν ἡ μαρτυρία ἣν μαρτυρεῖ περὶ ἐμοῦ
verses.transliteration: allos estin ho martyrōn peri emou kai oida hoti alēthēs estin hē martyria hēn martyrei peri emou
verses.study_note: 'Another' (allos — another of the same kind). Most interpreters identify this 'another' as the Father (cf. vv.37-38; 8:18), though John the Baptist is named next (v.33). The Father's testimony undergirds all other testimony. 'I know' (oida — perfect knowledge, absolute certainty). Jesus is certain of the Father's testimony about him.

verses.kjv_text: Ye sent unto John, and he bare witness unto the truth.
verses.original_text: ὑμεῖς ἀπεστάλκατε πρὸς Ἰωάννην καὶ μεμαρτύρηκεν τῇ ἀληθείᾳ
verses.transliteration: hymeis apestalkate pros Iōannēn kai memartyrēken tē alētheia
verses.study_note: 'Ye sent unto John' — referring to the delegation of priests and Levites in 1:19-28. 'He bare witness unto the truth' (memartyrēken tē alētheia — has testified to the truth; perfect tense: his testimony stands). John the Baptist is the first witness Jesus cites — Witness #1. His testimony was public, authoritative, and pointed directly to Jesus as the Lamb of God (1:29, 36), the Son of God (1:34).

verses.kjv_text: But I receive not testimony from man: but these things I say, that ye might be saved.
verses.original_text: ἐγὼ δὲ οὐ παρὰ ἀνθρώπου τὴν μαρτυρίαν λαμβάνω ἀλλὰ ταῦτα λέγω ἵνα ὑμεῖς σωθῆτε
verses.transliteration: egō de ou para anthrōpou tēn martyrian lambanō alla tauta legō hina hymeis sōthēte
verses.study_note: 'I receive not testimony from man' — Jesus does not depend on human endorsement for validation. He cites John's testimony not for his own benefit but for theirs: 'that ye might be saved' (hina hymeis sōthēte — so that you might be saved). Even in confrontation, Jesus' purpose is salvation, not condemnation (cf. 3:17).

verses.kjv_text: He was a burning and a shining light: and ye were willing for a season to rejoice in his light.
verses.original_text: ἐκεῖνος ἦν ὁ λύχνος ὁ καιόμενος καὶ φαίνων ὑμεῖς δὲ ἠθελήσατε ἀγαλλιαθῆναι πρὸς ὥραν ἐν τῷ φωτὶ αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: ekeinos ēn ho lychnos ho kaiomenos kai phainōn hymeis de ēthelēsate agalliathēnai pros hōran en tō phōti autou
verses.study_note: 'A burning and a shining light' (ho lychnos ho kaiomenos kai phainōn — the lamp that burns and shines). John is a lychnos (lamp), not the phōs (the Light itself — that is Jesus, 1:4-9; 8:12). A lamp burns fuel — John's ministry cost him everything, eventually his life. 'For a season' (pros hōran — for an hour, temporarily). The Jewish leaders enjoyed John's popularity for a time but did not heed his testimony when it pointed to Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: But I have greater witness than that of John: for the works which the Father hath given me to finish, the same works that I do, bear witness of me, that the Father hath sent me.
verses.original_text: ἐγὼ δὲ ἔχω τὴν μαρτυρίαν μείζω τοῦ Ἰωάννου τὰ γὰρ ἔργα ἃ ἔδωκέν μοι ὁ πατὴρ ἵνα τελειώσω αὐτά αὐτὰ τὰ ἔργα ἃ ἐγὼ ποιῶ μαρτυρεῖ περὶ ἐμοῦ ὅτι ὁ πατὴρ με ἀπέσταλκεν
verses.transliteration: egō de echō tēn martyrian meizō tou Iōannou ta gar erga ha edōken moi ho patēr hina teleiōsō auta auta ta erga ha egō poiō martyrei peri emou hoti ho patēr me apestalken
verses.study_note: 'Greater witness' (tēn martyrian meizō — a testimony greater than John's). Witness #2: Jesus' works (ta erga — the works, the signs). The miracles are not mere displays of power but testimonies that the Father sent the Son. 'Given me to finish' (teleiōsō — to bring to completion; the same word in 4:34 and 19:30). Each work Jesus performs is a word from the Father, a revelation of the Father's will and power operating through the Son.

verses.kjv_text: And the Father himself, which hath sent me, hath borne witness of me. Ye have neither heard his voice at any time, nor seen his shape.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὁ πέμψας με πατὴρ αὐτὸς μεμαρτύρηκεν περὶ ἐμοῦ οὔτε φωνὴν αὐτοῦ ἀκηκόατε πώποτε οὔτε εἶδος αὐτοῦ ἑωράκατε
verses.transliteration: kai ho pempsas me patēr autos memartyrēken peri emou oute phōnēn autou akēkoate pōpote oute eidos autou heōrakate
verses.study_note: Witness #3: The Father himself (ho pempsas me patēr autos — the Father who sent me, he himself). 'Hath borne witness' (memartyrēken — perfect tense: has testified and the testimony stands). The Father's testimony may refer to the voice at Jesus' baptism (Matthew 3:17), or more broadly to the entire OT revelation, or to the inner testimony of the Spirit. 'Neither heard his voice... nor seen his shape' — a devastating indictment: they claim to know God but have never truly heard him or perceived him. If they had, they would recognise his Son.

verses.kjv_text: And ye have not his word abiding in you: for whom he hath sent, him ye believe not.
verses.original_text: καὶ τὸν λόγον αὐτοῦ οὐκ ἔχετε μένοντα ἐν ὑμῖν ὅτι ὃν ἀπέστειλεν ἐκεῖνος τούτῳ ὑμεῖς οὐ πιστεύετε
verses.transliteration: kai ton logon autou ouk echete menonta en hymin hoti hon apesteilen ekeinos toutō hymeis ou pisteuete
verses.study_note: 'Ye have not his word abiding in you' (ton logon autou ouk echete menonta en hymin — his word does not remain/dwell in you). The proof that God's word does not abide in them is their rejection of the one God sent. If God's word truly lived in them, they would recognise God's Son — the living Word (1:1-14). Abiding (menō) is a key Johannine concept (15:4-10): God's word must not just be heard but must take up permanent residence in the heart.

verses.kjv_text: Search the scriptures; for in them ye think ye have eternal life: and they are they which testify of me.
verses.original_text: ἐρευνᾶτε τὰς γραφάς ὅτι ὑμεῖς δοκεῖτε ἐν αὐταῖς ζωὴν αἰώνιον ἔχειν καὶ ἐκεῖναί εἰσιν αἱ μαρτυροῦσαι περὶ ἐμοῦ
verses.transliteration: ereunate tas graphas hoti hymeis dokeite en autais zōēn aiōnion echein kai ekeinai eisin hai martyrousai peri emou
verses.study_note: 'Search the scriptures' (ereunate tas graphas — either imperative 'Search!' or indicative 'You search' — both make sense). Witness #4: The Scriptures themselves. 'Ye think ye have eternal life' in them — the scribes and Pharisees meticulously studied the OT but missed its central message. The Scriptures are not an end in themselves but a testimony pointing to Christ: 'they are they which testify of me' (ekeinai eisin hai martyrousai peri emou). Every page, every prophecy, every type and shadow points to Jesus. To study Scripture without finding Christ is to miss its entire purpose.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse establishes a hermeneutical principle of immense importance: the OT Scriptures are christocentric — they testify about Jesus. The Law (vv.45-46), the Prophets, and the Writings all point to Christ. Jesus himself will demonstrate this after his resurrection: 'beginning at Moses and all the prophets, he expounded unto them in all the scriptures the things concerning himself' (Luke 24:27). To read the Bible without seeing Christ is to read it incorrectly. The Pharisees' error was not insufficient study but misdirected study — they searched the Scriptures diligently but refused to come to the One the Scriptures proclaimed.

verses.kjv_text: And ye will not come to me, that ye might have life.
verses.original_text: καὶ οὐ θέλετε ἐλθεῖν πρός με ἵνα ζωὴν ἔχητε
verses.transliteration: kai ou thelete elthein pros me hina zōēn echēte
verses.study_note: 'Ye will not come to me' (ou thelete elthein pros me — you are not willing to come to me; thelete: you do not will, you refuse). The problem is not inability but unwillingness — not intellectual but volitional. Life (zōēn) is available in Christ; they refuse to come. This is the tragedy of unbelief: the light shines, the testimony is clear, the invitation is open — yet they will not come. 'That ye might have life' — the purpose is generous, the offer is genuine, but the will is resistant.

verses.kjv_text: I receive not honour from men.
verses.original_text: δόξαν παρὰ ἀνθρώπων οὐ λαμβάνω
verses.transliteration: doxan para anthrōpōn ou lambanō
verses.study_note: 'I receive not honour from men' (doxan para anthrōpōn ou lambanō — I do not accept glory from human beings). Jesus does not seek human approval or popularity. His authority comes from the Father, not from human endorsement. This makes his testimony reliable: he has no self-serving motive.

verses.kjv_text: But I know you, that ye have not the love of God in you.
verses.original_text: ἀλλ᾽ ἔγνωκα ὑμᾶς ὅτι τὴν ἀγάπην τοῦ θεοῦ οὐκ ἔχετε ἐν ἑαυτοῖς
verses.transliteration: all egnōka hymas hoti tēn agapēn tou theou ouk echete en heautois
verses.study_note: 'I know you' (egnōka hymas — I have come to know you; perfect tense: thorough, settled knowledge). 'The love of God' (tēn agapēn tou theou) — this can mean 'love for God' (subjective genitive) or 'the love that comes from God' (objective genitive); both are true. They claim to love God but reject God's Son. Their rejection of Jesus reveals the absence of genuine love for God. True love for God always leads to receiving the one God has sent.

verses.kjv_text: I am come in my Father's name, and ye receive me not: if another shall come in his own name, him ye will receive.
verses.original_text: ἐγὼ ἐλήλυθα ἐν τῷ ὀνόματι τοῦ πατρός μου καὶ οὐ λαμβάνετέ με ἐὰν ἄλλος ἔλθῃ ἐν τῷ ὀνόματι τῷ ἰδίῳ ἐκεῖνον λήψεσθε
verses.transliteration: egō elēlytha en tō onomati tou patros mou kai ou lambanete me ean allos elthē en tō onomati tō idiō ekeinon lēpsesthe
verses.study_note: 'In my Father's name' (en tō onomati tou patros mou — with the Father's authority, as the Father's representative). 'If another shall come in his own name' — a warning about false messiahs who come with self-generated authority. History fulfilled this prediction: Bar Kokhba (AD 132-135) was accepted by Rabbi Akiva as the Messiah, leading to disaster. The pattern persists: those who reject Christ become vulnerable to accepting counterfeits.

verses.kjv_text: How can ye believe, which receive honour one of another, and seek not the honour that cometh from God only?
verses.original_text: πῶς δύνασθε ὑμεῖς πιστεῦσαι δόξαν παρὰ ἀλλήλων λαμβάνοντες καὶ τὴν δόξαν τὴν παρὰ τοῦ μόνου θεοῦ οὐ ζητεῖτε
verses.transliteration: pōs dynasthe hymeis pisteusai doxan para allēlōn lambanontes kai tēn doxan tēn para tou monou theou ou zēteite
verses.study_note: 'How can ye believe?' (pōs dynasthe pisteusai — how are you able to believe?). The obstacle to faith is not intellectual but moral: they seek honour from one another (doxan para allēlōn — glory from each other, peer approval, human reputation) rather than the glory that comes from God alone. The craving for human approval makes faith impossible — one cannot simultaneously serve the opinion of man and the truth of God. This diagnosis of unbelief is penetrating: pride, not ignorance, is the root barrier.
