verses.kjv_text: Do not think that I will accuse you to the Father: there is one that accuseth you, even Moses, in whom ye trust.
verses.original_text: μὴ δοκεῖτε ὅτι ἐγὼ κατηγορήσω ὑμῶν πρὸς τὸν πατέρα ἔστιν ὁ κατηγορῶν ὑμῶν Μωϋσῆς εἰς ὃν ὑμεῖς ἠλπίκατε
verses.transliteration: mē dokeite hoti egō katēgorēsō hymōn pros ton patera estin ho katēgorōn hymōn Mōysēs eis hon hymeis ēlpikate
verses.study_note: 'Moses, in whom ye trust' (Mōysēs eis hon hymeis ēlpikate — Moses, in whom you have placed your hope; perfect tense: your settled, ongoing hope). The irony is devastating: Moses, their champion, becomes their accuser. They trust in Moses but have not understood him. Moses testified about Christ (v.46); to reject Christ is to reject Moses. Their own authority figure condemns them.

verses.kjv_text: For had ye believed Moses, ye would have believed me: for he wrote of me.
verses.original_text: εἰ γὰρ ἐπιστεύετε Μωϋσεῖ ἐπιστεύετε ἂν ἐμοί περὶ γὰρ ἐμοῦ ἐκεῖνος ἔγραψεν
verses.transliteration: ei gar episteuete Mōysei episteuete an emoi peri gar emou ekeinos egrapsen
verses.study_note: 'He wrote of me' (peri gar emou ekeinos egrapsen — for concerning me that one wrote). Moses wrote about Christ: (1) the seed of the woman crushing the serpent (Genesis 3:15), (2) the blessing through Abraham's seed (Genesis 12:3; 22:18), (3) the prophet like Moses (Deuteronomy 18:15-18), (4) Shiloh from Judah (Genesis 49:10), (5) the Passover lamb (Exodus 12), (6) the bronze serpent (Numbers 21:8-9, cf. John 3:14), (7) the entire sacrificial system pointing to Christ. The Pentateuch is christological — to believe Moses truly is to believe in the one Moses foretold.

verses.kjv_text: But if ye believe not his writings, how shall ye believe my words?
verses.original_text: εἰ δὲ τοῖς ἐκείνου γράμμασιν οὐ πιστεύετε πῶς τοῖς ἐμοῖς ῥήμασιν πιστεύσετε
verses.transliteration: ei de tois ekeinou grammasin ou pisteuete pōs tois emois rhēmasin pisteusete
verses.study_note: 'His writings' (tois ekeinou grammasin — that one's writings; grammata: written documents, the Pentateuch). 'My words' (tois emois rhēmasin — my spoken words). The argument is a fortiori (from the lesser to the greater): if you do not believe the written words of Moses (which you claim to revere), how will you believe the spoken words of Jesus (whom you are rejecting)? The written word and the living Word testify in harmony; to reject one is to reject both. This closing question leaves the Jewish leaders with no defense — their own Scriptures accuse them.

meta.overview: John 6 is the longest chapter in the Gospel and one of the most theologically dense passages in Scripture. It opens with two great signs — the feeding of the five thousand (vv.1-15) and the walking on the sea (vv.16-21) — and then moves into the Bread of Life discourse (vv.22-59), the most sustained christological teaching in John apart from the Upper Room Discourse. After crossing the Sea of Galilee, Jesus feeds a multitude of five thousand men (plus women and children) with five barley loaves and two small fishes: 'And Jesus took the loaves; and when he had given thanks, he distributed to the disciples, and the disciples to them that were set down; and likewise of the fishes as much as they would' (v.11). Twelve baskets of fragments remain — more than the original supply (v.13). The crowd, recognising a prophetic sign, attempts to make Jesus king by force (v.15), but he withdraws to the mountain alone. That evening, the disciples set out across the sea toward Capernaum. A great wind arises, and Jesus comes to them walking on the water: 'It is I; be not afraid' (v.20). The next day, the crowd finds Jesus in Capernaum and he challenges their motives: 'Ye seek me, not because ye saw the miracles, but because ye did eat of the loaves, and were filled' (v.26). This launches the Bread of Life discourse, in which Jesus makes seven extraordinary claims: (1) 'I am the bread of life' (v.35); (2) 'All that the Father giveth me shall come to me; and him that cometh to me I will in no wise cast out' (v.37); (3) 'This is the Father's will... that every one which seeth the Son, and believeth on him, may have everlasting life: and I will raise him up at the last day' (v.40); (4) 'No man can come to me, except the Father which hath sent me draw him' (v.44); (5) 'I am the living bread which came down from heaven: if any man eat of this bread, he shall live for ever' (v.51); (6) 'Whoso eateth my flesh, and drinketh my blood, hath eternal life' (v.54); (7) 'It is the spirit that quickeneth; the flesh profiteth nothing: the words that I speak unto you, they are spirit, and they are life' (v.63). The discourse provokes sharp division: 'Many therefore of his disciples, when they had heard this, said, This is an hard saying; who can hear it?' (v.60). 'From that time many of his disciples went back, and walked no more with him' (v.66). Jesus turns to the Twelve: 'Will ye also go away?' Peter responds with one of the great confessions: 'Lord, to whom shall we go? thou hast the words of eternal life. And we believe and are sure that thou art that Christ, the Son of the living God' (vv.68-69). The chapter closes with Jesus' sobering prediction: 'Have not I chosen you twelve, and one of you is a devil?' — referring to Judas Iscariot (vv.70-71).
meta.theme: The Bread of Life — Christ the True Bread from Heaven
meta.keyWordMeaning: Bread, loaf, food — the basic staple of life in the ancient world. In John 6, artos becomes the central metaphor for Jesus himself. The feeding miracle uses literal bread (artos, vv.5, 7, 9, 11, 13); the discourse transforms it into spiritual reality: 'I am the bread of life' (v.35), 'the living bread which came down from heaven' (v.51). As physical bread sustains physical life, so Christ — received by faith — sustains eternal life. The manna in the wilderness (Exodus 16) was bread from heaven that sustained Israel temporarily; Jesus is the true bread from heaven that gives life to the world permanently (vv.32-33). The word occurs 21 times in this chapter alone.
outline: The Feeding of the Five Thousand (vv.1-15): After these things Jesus went over the sea of Galilee. A great multitude followed him because they saw his miracles. Jesus went up into a mountain and sat there with his disciples and the passover was nigh. Jesus saith unto Philip Whence shall we buy bread that these may eat? — this he said to prove him for he himself knew what he would do. Philip answered Two hundred pennyworth of bread is not sufficient. Andrew saith There is a lad here which hath five barley loaves and two small fishes but what are they among so many? Jesus said Make the men sit down — about five thousand. Jesus took the loaves and gave thanks and distributed to them that were set down and likewise of the fishes as much as they would. When they were filled he said Gather up the fragments that nothing be lost. They filled twelve baskets. Then those men said This is of a truth that prophet that should come into the world. Jesus perceived they would come and take him by force to make him a king and departed into a mountain himself alone

Jesus Walks on the Sea (vv.16-21): When even was come his disciples went down unto the sea and entered into a ship toward Capernaum. It was now dark and Jesus was not come to them. The sea arose by reason of a great wind. When they had rowed about five and twenty or thirty furlongs they see Jesus walking on the sea and drawing nigh unto the ship and they were afraid. But he saith unto them It is I be not afraid. Then they willingly received him into the ship and immediately the ship was at the land whither they went

The Crowd Seeks Jesus (vv.22-34): The day following the people which stood on the other side of the sea saw that there was none other boat there save that one and that Jesus went not with his disciples. Howbeit there came other boats from Tiberias. When the people therefore saw that Jesus was not there they took shipping and came to Capernaum seeking Jesus. They said Rabbi when camest thou hither? Jesus answered Ye seek me not because ye saw the miracles but because ye did eat of the loaves and were filled. Labour not for the meat which perisheth but for that meat which endureth unto everlasting life which the Son of man shall give unto you. They said What shall we do that we might work the works of God? Jesus answered This is the work of God that ye believe on him whom he hath sent. They said What sign shewest thou then? Our fathers did eat manna in the desert as it is written He gave them bread from heaven to eat. Jesus said Moses gave you not that bread from heaven but my Father giveth you the true bread from heaven. For the bread of God is he which cometh down from heaven and giveth life unto the world. They said Lord evermore give us this bread

The Bread of Life Discourse (vv.35-51): Jesus said I am the bread of life he that cometh to me shall never hunger and he that believeth on me shall never thirst. But I said unto you That ye also have seen me and believe not. All that the Father giveth me shall come to me and him that cometh to me I will in no wise cast out. For I came down from heaven not to do mine own will but the will of him that sent me. And this is the Father's will which hath sent me that of all which he hath given me I should lose nothing but should raise it up again at the last day. And this is the will of him that sent me that every one which seeth the Son and believeth on him may have everlasting life and I will raise him up at the last day. The Jews then murmured because he said I am the bread which came down from heaven. They said Is not this Jesus the son of Joseph? Jesus answered Murmur not among yourselves. No man can come to me except the Father which hath sent me draw him and I will raise him up at the last day. It is written in the prophets And they shall be all taught of God. Every man therefore that hath heard and hath learned of the Father cometh unto me. Not that any man hath seen the Father save he which is of God he hath seen the Father. Verily verily I say unto you He that believeth on me hath everlasting life. I am that bread of life. Your fathers did eat manna in the wilderness and are dead. This is the bread which cometh down from heaven that a man may eat thereof and not die. I am the living bread which came down from heaven if any man eat of this bread he shall live for ever and the bread that I will give is my flesh which I will give for the life of the world

Eating the Flesh and Drinking the Blood (vv.52-59): The Jews therefore strove among themselves saying How can this man give us his flesh to eat? Then Jesus said Verily verily I say unto you Except ye eat the flesh of the Son of man and drink his blood ye have no life in you. Whoso eateth my flesh and drinketh my blood hath eternal life and I will raise him up at the last day. For my flesh is meat indeed and my blood is drink indeed. He that eateth my flesh and drinketh my blood dwelleth in me and I in him. As the living Father hath sent me and I live by the Father so he that eateth me even he shall live by me. This is that bread which came down from heaven not as your fathers did eat manna and are dead he that eateth of this bread shall live for ever. These things said he in the synagogue as he taught in Capernaum

The Hard Saying and Peter's Confession (vv.60-71): Many of his disciples when they had heard this said This is an hard saying who can hear it? When Jesus knew in himself that his disciples murmured he said Doth this offend you? What and if ye shall see the Son of man ascend up where he was before? It is the spirit that quickeneth the flesh profiteth nothing the words that I speak unto you they are spirit and they are life. But there are some of you that believe not — for Jesus knew from the beginning who they were that believed not and who should betray him. And he said Therefore said I unto you that no man can come unto me except it were given unto him of my Father. From that time many of his disciples went back and walked no more with him. Then said Jesus unto the twelve Will ye also go away? Simon Peter answered Lord to whom shall we go thou hast the words of eternal life. And we believe and are sure that thou art that Christ the Son of the living God. Jesus answered Have not I chosen you twelve and one of you is a devil? He spake of Judas Iscariot the son of Simon for he it was that should betray him being one of the twelve

verses.kjv_text: After these things Jesus went over the sea of Galilee, which is the sea of Tiberias.
verses.original_text: μετὰ ταῦτα ἀπῆλθεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς πέραν τῆς θαλάσσης τῆς Γαλιλαίας τῆς Τιβεριάδος
verses.transliteration: meta tauta apēlthen ho Iēsous peran tēs thalassēs tēs Galilaias tēs Tiberiados
verses.study_note: 'After these things' (meta tauta — a general chronological link; considerable time has passed since ch.5). The sea is called by both names: 'Sea of Galilee' (its Jewish name) and 'Sea of Tiberias' (its Roman name, from the city Tiberias built by Herod Antipas in honour of Emperor Tiberius, c. AD 20). John uses the Roman name for his broader audience. The shift from Jerusalem (ch.5) to Galilee is abrupt — John omits much Galilean ministry recorded in the Synoptics.

verses.kjv_text: And a great multitude followed him, because they saw his miracles which he did on them that were diseased.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἠκολούθει αὐτῷ ὄχλος πολύς ὅτι ἑώρων αὐτοῦ τὰ σημεῖα ἃ ἐποίει ἐπὶ τῶν ἀσθενούντων
verses.transliteration: kai ēkolouthei autō ochlos polys hoti heōrōn autou ta sēmeia ha epoiei epi tōn asthenountōn
verses.study_note: 'Followed' (ēkolouthei — imperfect: kept following, a continuous stream). 'Because they saw his miracles' (hoti heōrōn ta sēmeia — because they were seeing his signs). John calls them sēmeia (signs), not merely dynameis (mighty works) — they are pointers to Jesus' identity. But the crowd follows for the spectacular, not for the significance. Their faith is sign-based and shallow (cf. 2:23-25).

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus went up into a mountain, and there he sat with his disciples.
verses.original_text: ἀνῆλθεν δὲ εἰς τὸ ὄρος ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ ἐκεῖ ἐκάθητο μετὰ τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: anēlthen de eis to oros ho Iēsous kai ekei ekathēto meta tōn mathētōn autou
verses.study_note: 'Went up into a mountain' (anēlthen eis to oros — the definite article 'the mountain' suggests a known elevated area on the northeast shore of the Sea of Galilee). 'Sat' (ekathēto — the posture of a teacher in Jewish tradition). The scene evokes Moses on Mount Sinai — a deliberate parallel John develops: Moses gave bread from heaven (manna); Jesus will give the true bread from heaven.

verses.kjv_text: And the passover, a feast of the Jews, was nigh.
verses.original_text: ἦν δὲ ἐγγὺς τὸ πάσχα ἡ ἑορτὴ τῶν Ἰουδαίων
verses.transliteration: ēn de engys to pascha hē heortē tōn Ioudaiōn
verses.study_note: 'The passover' (to pascha) — the second Passover in John (the first in 2:13; the third in 11:55/13:1). This time-note is theologically significant: the feeding miracle occurs near Passover, linking Jesus to the Passover lamb and the manna given during the Exodus. The bread Jesus multiplies prefigures the bread of the Last Supper (which occurs at the third Passover). John does not record the institution of the Lord's Supper, but John 6 provides its theological foundation.

verses.kjv_text: When Jesus then lifted up his eyes, and saw a great company come unto him, he saith unto Philip, Whence shall we buy bread, that these may eat?
verses.original_text: ἐπάρας οὖν ὁ Ἰησοῦς τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς καὶ θεασάμενος ὅτι πολὺς ὄχλος ἔρχεται πρὸς αὐτόν λέγει πρὸς τὸν Φίλιππον Πόθεν ἀγοράσωμεν ἄρτους ἵνα φάγωσιν οὗτοι
verses.transliteration: eparas oun ho Iēsous tous ophthalmous kai theasamenos hoti polys ochlos erchetai pros auton legei pros ton Philippon Pothen agorasōmen artous hina phagōsin houtoi
verses.study_note: 'Whence shall we buy bread?' (pothen agorasōmen artous — where can we purchase loaves?). Jesus asks Philip specifically — Philip was from nearby Bethsaida (1:44) and would know the local resources. The question is a test (v.6): Jesus already knows the answer. He involves his disciples in the miracle, making them witness their own insufficiency before demonstrating his sufficiency.

verses.kjv_text: And this he said to prove him: for he himself knew what he would do.
verses.original_text: τοῦτο δὲ ἔλεγεν πειράζων αὐτόν αὐτὸς γὰρ ᾔδει τί ἔμελλεν ποιεῖν
verses.transliteration: touto de elegen peirazōn auton autos gar ēdei ti emellen poiein
verses.study_note: 'To prove him' (peirazōn auton — testing him; peirazō can mean 'test' or 'tempt' — here it is a test of faith, not a temptation to sin). 'He himself knew' (autos ēdei — Jesus had full knowledge of what he was about to do; cf. 2:24-25; 5:6). The testing is pedagogical: Philip must confront the impossibility of the situation so that the miracle's significance is clear. God often tests faith by presenting impossible circumstances before acting (cf. Exodus 14:10-14).

verses.kjv_text: Philip answered him, Two hundred pennyworth of bread is not sufficient for them, that every one of them may take a little.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη αὐτῷ Φίλιππος Διακοσίων δηναρίων ἄρτοι οὐκ ἀρκοῦσιν αὐτοῖς ἵνα ἕκαστος αὐτῶν βραχύ τι λάβῃ
verses.transliteration: apekrithē autō Philippos Diakosiōn dēnariōn artoi ouk arkousin autois hina hekastos autōn brachy ti labē
verses.study_note: 'Two hundred pennyworth' (diakosiōn dēnariōn — 200 denarii; a denarius was a day's wage for a labourer, so this represents about 8 months' wages). 'Is not sufficient' (ouk arkousin — are not enough). Philip calculates in human terms and concludes the task is impossible. His response is rational but faithless — he has seen Jesus' signs but does not yet anticipate miraculous provision. 'That every one... may take a little' (brachy ti — a small amount) — even the most modest provision is beyond their means.

verses.kjv_text: One of his disciples, Andrew, Simon Peter's brother, saith unto him,
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ εἷς ἐκ τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ Ἀνδρέας ὁ ἀδελφὸς Σίμωνος Πέτρου
verses.transliteration: legei autō heis ek tōn mathētōn autou Andreas ho adelphos Simōnos Petrou
verses.study_note: Andrew is identified as 'Simon Peter's brother' — John consistently introduces Andrew this way (1:40; 12:22). Andrew is the disciple who brings people to Jesus: he brought Peter (1:41-42), he brings the boy with the loaves (here), and he brings the Greeks (12:22). His role is modest but crucial — he connects people with Christ.

verses.kjv_text: There is a lad here, which hath five barley loaves, and two small fishes: but what are they among so many?
verses.original_text: ἔστιν παιδάριον ἓν ὧδε ὃ ἔχει πέντε ἄρτους κριθίνους καὶ δύο ὀψάρια ἀλλὰ ταῦτα τί ἐστιν εἰς τοσούτους
verses.transliteration: estin paidarion hen hōde ho echei pente artous krithinous kai duo opsaria alla tauta ti estin eis tosoutous
verses.study_note: 'A lad' (paidarion — a small boy, a young child; diminutive form). 'Five barley loaves' (pente artous krithinous — barley bread was the food of the poor; cf. 2 Kings 4:42 where Elisha feeds 100 with 20 barley loaves). 'Two small fishes' (duo opsaria — opsarion is a diminutive: small dried or pickled fish, used as a relish with bread). Andrew finds something but sees its inadequacy: 'what are they among so many?' Andrew has more initiative than Philip but the same limited vision. God delights to use the small and insufficient (cf. 1 Corinthians 1:27-28).

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus said, Make the men sit down. Now there was much grass in the place. So the men sat down, in number about five thousand.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ποιήσατε τοὺς ἀνθρώπους ἀναπεσεῖν ἦν δὲ χόρτος πολὺς ἐν τῷ τόπῳ ἀνέπεσον οὖν οἱ ἄνδρες τὸν ἀριθμὸν ὡσεὶ πεντακισχίλιοι
verses.transliteration: eipen de ho Iēsous Poiēsate tous anthrōpous anapesein ēn de chortos polys en tō topō anepesan oun hoi andres ton arithmon hōsei pentakischilioi
verses.study_note: 'Make the men sit down' (poiēsate tous anthrōpous anapesein — have the people recline). 'Much grass' (chortos polys — indicating spring, consistent with Passover in v.4; Mark 6:39 adds 'green grass'). 'About five thousand' (hōsei pentakischilioi) — andres (men, males only); Matthew 14:21 adds 'beside women and children,' so the total could be 15,000-20,000. Jesus commands orderly preparation before the miracle — faith and order go together.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus took the loaves; and when he had given thanks, he distributed to the disciples, and the disciples to them that were set down; and likewise of the fishes as much as they would.
verses.original_text: ἔλαβεν δὲ τοὺς ἄρτους ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ εὐχαριστήσας διέδωκεν τοῖς μαθηταῖς οἱ δὲ μαθηταὶ τοῖς ἀνακειμένοις ὁμοίως καὶ ἐκ τῶν ὀψαρίων ὅσον ἤθελον
verses.transliteration: elaben de tous artous ho Iēsous kai eucharistēsas diedōken tois mathētais hoi de mathētai tois anakeimenois homoiōs kai ek tōn opsariōn hoson ēthelon
verses.study_note: 'Given thanks' (eucharistēsas — having given thanks; the verb eucharistō, from which 'Eucharist' derives). Jesus follows the Jewish custom of blessing God before eating. The distribution goes through the disciples — they are channels of his provision, not the source. 'As much as they would' (hoson ēthelon — as much as they wanted) — not meagre rations but abundant supply. The miracle occurs in Jesus' hands: the bread multiplies as it is given away. The more that is distributed, the more there is to distribute.

verses.kjv_text: When they were filled, he said unto his disciples, Gather up the fragments that remain, that nothing be lost.
verses.original_text: ὡς δὲ ἐνεπλήσθησαν λέγει τοῖς μαθηταῖς αὐτοῦ Συναγάγετε τὰ περισσεύσαντα κλάσματα ἵνα μή τι ἀπόληται
verses.transliteration: hōs de eneplēsthēsan legei tois mathētais autou Synagagete ta perisseusanta klasmata hina mē ti apolētai
verses.study_note: 'Were filled' (eneplēsthēsan — were satisfied, had their fill; from empiplēmi: to fill up completely). 'Fragments' (klasmata — broken pieces; from klaō: to break). 'That nothing be lost' (hina mē ti apolētai — so that nothing may perish/be wasted). The verb apollymi (to lose, destroy, perish) is the same word used in v.39 ('I should lose nothing') and 3:16 ('should not perish'). Jesus wastes nothing — not food, not people. The abundance exceeds the need, but the surplus is still precious.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore they gathered them together, and filled twelve baskets with the fragments of the five barley loaves, which remained over and above unto them that had eaten.
verses.original_text: συνήγαγον οὖν καὶ ἐγέμισαν δώδεκα κοφίνους κλασμάτων ἐκ τῶν πέντε ἄρτων τῶν κριθίνων ἃ ἐπερίσσευσεν τοῖς βεβρωκόσιν
verses.transliteration: synēgagon oun kai egemisan dōdeka kophinous klasmatōn ek tōn pente artōn tōn krithinōn ha eperisseusen tois bebrōkosin
verses.study_note: 'Twelve baskets' (dōdeka kophinous — kophinos is a large wicker basket distinctively Jewish; Juvenal mocked Jews for carrying these). Twelve baskets — one for each apostle, more than the original five loaves. The miracle produces superabundance: the supply exceeds the demand. 'Remained over and above' (eperisseusen — abounded, was left over in excess). This is a sign of divine generosity: God does not merely meet needs but overflows them (cf. Ephesians 3:20: 'exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think').

verses.kjv_text: Then those men, when they had seen the miracle that Jesus did, said, This is of a truth that prophet that should come into the world.
verses.original_text: οἱ οὖν ἄνθρωποι ἰδόντες ὃ ἐποίησεν σημεῖον ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἔλεγον ὅτι Οὗτός ἐστιν ἀληθῶς ὁ προφήτης ὁ ἐρχόμενος εἰς τὸν κόσμον
verses.transliteration: hoi oun anthrōpoi idontes ho epoiēsen sēmeion ho Iēsous elegon hoti Houtos estin alēthōs ho prophētēs ho erchomenos eis ton kosmon
verses.study_note: 'That prophet' (ho prophētēs ho erchomenos — the Prophet, the Coming One). The crowd identifies Jesus as the Prophet foretold by Moses in Deuteronomy 18:15: 'The LORD thy God will raise up unto thee a Prophet like unto me.' The connection is natural: Moses gave manna; this prophet gives miraculous bread. Their identification is partially correct — Jesus is the Prophet — but their understanding is incomplete. They want a political deliverer, not a spiritual saviour.

verses.kjv_text: When Jesus therefore perceived that they would come and take him by force, to make him a king, he departed again into a mountain himself alone.
verses.original_text: Ἰησοῦς οὖν γνοὺς ὅτι μέλλουσιν ἔρχεσθαι καὶ ἁρπάζειν αὐτὸν ἵνα ποιήσωσιν αὐτὸν βασιλέα ἀνεχώρησεν πάλιν εἰς τὸ ὄρος αὐτὸς μόνος
verses.transliteration: Iēsous oun gnous hoti mellousin erchesthai kai harpazein auton hina poiēsōsin auton basilea anechōrēsen palin eis to oros autos monos
verses.study_note: 'Take him by force' (harpazein auton — to seize, snatch, take by violence). 'To make him a king' (poiēsōsin auton basilea — to make him king). The crowd wants a bread-king, a political messiah who provides material abundance. This is the third temptation (Matthew 4:8-10) in a different form: the kingdoms of the world without the cross. Jesus refuses — his kingdom is 'not of this world' (18:36). He withdraws alone (autos monos) to pray (Mark 6:46). The true King will not be crowned by popular acclaim but by a cross.

verses.kjv_text: And when even was now come, his disciples went down unto the sea,
verses.original_text: ὡς δὲ ὀψία ἐγένετο κατέβησαν οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ ἐπὶ τὴν θάλασσαν
verses.transliteration: hōs de opsia egeneto katebēsan hoi mathētai autou epi tēn thalassan
verses.study_note: 'When even was come' (opsia egeneto — when evening came). The disciples go down to the sea without Jesus — he has withdrawn to the mountain. The coming darkness (v.17) is both literal and atmospheric: they are entering a time of difficulty without the visible presence of their Lord.

verses.kjv_text: And entered into a ship, and went over the sea toward Capernaum. And it was now dark, and Jesus was not come to them.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐμβάντες εἰς τὸ πλοῖον ἤρχοντο πέραν τῆς θαλάσσης εἰς Καπερναούμ καὶ σκοτία ἤδη ἐγεγόνει καὶ οὔπω ἐληλύθει πρὸς αὐτοὺς ὁ Ἰησοῦς
verses.transliteration: kai embantes eis to ploion ērchonto peran tēs thalassēs eis Kapernaoum kai skotia ēdē egeganei kai oupō elēlythei pros autous ho Iēsous
verses.study_note: 'It was now dark' (skotia ēdē egeganei — darkness had already come). 'Jesus was not come to them' (oupō elēlythei — had not yet come). John uses darkness symbolically throughout his Gospel (1:5; 3:19; 8:12; 13:30). The disciples are in darkness, on troubled waters, without Jesus — a picture of the believer in trial. But 'not yet' (oupō) implies he will come. He always comes, though not always when expected.

verses.kjv_text: And the sea arose by reason of a great wind that blew.
verses.original_text: ἥ τε θάλασσα ἀνέμου μεγάλου πνέοντος διηγείρετο
verses.transliteration: hē te thalassa anemou megalou pneontos diēgeireto
verses.study_note: 'The sea arose' (hē thalassa diēgeireto — the sea was being stirred up, agitated). 'A great wind' (anemou megalou — a strong wind). The Sea of Galilee lies 700 feet below sea level, surrounded by hills that funnel winds through valleys, creating sudden, violent storms. The disciples are experienced fishermen but this storm is beyond their ability. The narrative sets the stage for Jesus' intervention — human skill is insufficient; divine presence is needed.

verses.kjv_text: So when they had rowed about five and twenty or thirty furlongs, they see Jesus walking on the sea, and drawing nigh unto the ship: and they were afraid.
verses.original_text: ἐληλακότες οὖν ὡς σταδίους εἴκοσι πέντε ἢ τριάκοντα θεωροῦσιν τὸν Ἰησοῦν περιπατοῦντα ἐπὶ τῆς θαλάσσης καὶ ἐγγὺς τοῦ πλοίου γινόμενον καὶ ἐφοβήθησαν
verses.transliteration: elēlakotes oun hōs stadious eikosi pente ē triakonta theōrousin ton Iēsoun peripatounta epi tēs thalassēs kai engys tou ploiou ginomenon kai ephobēthēsan
verses.study_note: 'Five and twenty or thirty furlongs' (stadious eikosi pente ē triakonta — about 3-3.5 miles; a stadion is roughly 600 feet). The sea is about 7 miles wide at this point, so they were roughly in the middle. 'Walking on the sea' (peripatounta epi tēs thalassēs — walking upon the sea). This is a theophanic act: in the OT, walking on the sea is exclusively God's prerogative: 'Which alone spreadeth out the heavens, and treadeth upon the waves of the sea' (Job 9:8). 'They were afraid' (ephobēthēsan — they were terrified). The fear is the natural human response to a divine manifestation.

verses.kjv_text: But he saith unto them, It is I; be not afraid.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ λέγει αὐτοῖς Ἐγώ εἰμι μὴ φοβεῖσθε
verses.transliteration: ho de legei autois Egō eimi mē phobeisthe
verses.study_note: 'It is I' (egō eimi — I AM). While this can simply mean 'It is I' (identifying himself), in John's Gospel egō eimi carries deeper resonance. It echoes the divine name revealed to Moses (Exodus 3:14: 'I AM THAT I AM'). Jesus uses this formula seven times in absolute form in John (4:26; 6:20; 8:24, 28, 58; 13:19; 18:5). Here, in the context of walking on water — an act reserved for God alone — the 'I AM' takes on theophanic significance. 'Be not afraid' (mē phobeisthe — stop being afraid; present imperative with mē: cease your fear). The divine presence that causes fear also removes it.
verses.doctrinal_note: The combination of walking on water and saying 'I AM' constitutes a theophany — a manifestation of divine presence. Job 9:8 says God 'treadeth upon the waves of the sea.' Psalm 77:19: 'Thy way is in the sea, and thy path in the great waters.' Isaiah 43:2: 'When thou passest through the waters, I will be with thee.' Jesus does what only God can do and identifies himself with the divine name. The disciples' fear is the appropriate human response to an encounter with the living God (cf. Isaiah 6:5; Luke 5:8).

verses.kjv_text: Then they willingly received him into the ship: and immediately the ship was at the land whither they went.
verses.original_text: ἤθελον οὖν λαβεῖν αὐτὸν εἰς τὸ πλοῖον καὶ εὐθέως τὸ πλοῖον ἐγένετο ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς εἰς ἣν ὑπῆγον
verses.transliteration: ēthelon oun labein auton eis to ploion kai eutheōs to ploion egeneto epi tēs gēs eis hēn hypēgon
verses.study_note: 'Willingly received him' (ēthelon labein auton — they were willing to take him in; their fear turned to welcome). 'Immediately the ship was at the land' (eutheōs to ploion egeneto epi tēs gēs — at once the boat was at the shore). This appears to be a third miracle: instantaneous arrival at their destination. When Jesus enters the boat, the storm is over and the journey complete. The spiritual lesson is clear: receiving Christ brings immediate peace and arrival at the intended destination.

verses.kjv_text: The day following, when the people which stood on the other side of the sea saw that there was none other boat there, save that one whereinto his disciples were entered, and that Jesus went not with his disciples into the boat, but that his disciples were gone away alone;
verses.original_text: τῇ ἐπαύριον ὁ ὄχλος ὁ ἑστηκὼς πέραν τῆς θαλάσσης ἰδὼν ὅτι πλοιάριον ἄλλο οὐκ ἦν ἐκεῖ εἰ μὴ ἓν ἐκεῖνο εἰς ὃ ἐνέβησαν οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ καὶ ὅτι οὐ συνεισῆλθεν τοῖς μαθηταῖς αὐτοῦ ὁ Ἰησοῦς εἰς τὸ πλοιάριον ἀλλὰ μόνοι οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ ἀπῆλθον
verses.transliteration: tē epaurion ho ochlos ho hestēkōs peran tēs thalassēs idōn hoti ploiarion allo ouk ēn ekei ei mē hen ekeino eis ho enebēsan hoi mathētai autou kai hoti ou syneisēlthen tois mathētais autou ho Iēsous eis to ploiarion alla monoi hoi mathētai autou apēlthon
verses.study_note: The crowd's detective work: they know there was only one boat, the disciples left in it, and Jesus did not go with them. So how did Jesus get to Capernaum? John leaves the puzzle unresolved for the crowd — they never learn about the walking on water. The detail emphasises Jesus' mysterious movements and the crowd's inability to track or control him.

verses.kjv_text: (Howbeit there came other boats from Tiberias nigh unto the place where they did eat bread, after that the Lord had given thanks:)
verses.original_text: ἄλλα δὲ ἦλθεν πλοιάρια ἐκ Τιβεριάδος ἐγγὺς τοῦ τόπου ὅπου ἔφαγον τὸν ἄρτον εὐχαριστήσαντος τοῦ κυρίου
verses.transliteration: alla de ēlthen ploiaria ek Tiberiados engys tou topou hopou ephagon ton arton eucharistēsantos tou kyriou
verses.study_note: A parenthetical note explaining how the crowd eventually crossed the sea. Other boats from Tiberias arrived near the feeding site, providing transport. John notes that the Lord had 'given thanks' (eucharistēsantos tou kyriou) — the same verb as v.11, connecting the feeding miracle to thanksgiving and, for John's readers, to the Eucharist.

verses.kjv_text: When the people therefore saw that Jesus was not there, neither his disciples, they also took shipping, and came to Capernaum, seeking for Jesus.
verses.original_text: ὅτε οὖν εἶδεν ὁ ὄχλος ὅτι Ἰησοῦς οὐκ ἔστιν ἐκεῖ οὐδὲ οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ ἐνέβησαν αὐτοὶ εἰς τὰ πλοῖα καὶ ἦλθον εἰς Καπερναοὺμ ζητοῦντες τὸν Ἰησοῦν
verses.transliteration: hote oun eiden ho ochlos hoti Iēsous ouk estin ekei oude hoi mathētai autou enebēsan autoi eis ta ploia kai ēlthon eis Kapernaoum zētountes ton Iēsoun
verses.study_note: 'Seeking for Jesus' (zētountes ton Iēsoun — searching for Jesus). The crowd pursues Jesus — but for the wrong reasons. They seek the miracle-worker, the bread-provider, the potential political king. They do not yet seek the Bread of Life himself. Their seeking is a shadow of true seeking (cf. Jeremiah 29:13: 'Ye shall seek me, and find me, when ye shall search for me with all your heart').

verses.kjv_text: And when they had found him on the other side of the sea, they said unto him, Rabbi, when camest thou hither?
verses.original_text: καὶ εὑρόντες αὐτὸν πέραν τῆς θαλάσσης εἶπον αὐτῷ Ῥαββί πότε ὧδε γέγονας
verses.transliteration: kai heurontes auton peran tēs thalassēs eipon autō Rhabbi pote hōde gegonas
verses.study_note: 'Rabbi, when camest thou hither?' (pote hōde gegonas — when did you arrive here?). They are puzzled by Jesus' presence in Capernaum since he did not take the boat. Jesus ignores their question entirely — he is not interested in satisfying curiosity about logistics. Instead, he addresses their hearts and motives (v.26).

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered them and said, Verily, verily, I say unto you, Ye seek me, not because ye saw the miracles, but because ye did eat of the loaves, and were filled.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ εἶπεν Ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ζητεῖτέ με οὐχ ὅτι εἴδετε σημεῖα ἀλλ᾽ ὅτι ἐφάγετε ἐκ τῶν ἄρτων καὶ ἐχορτάσθητε
verses.transliteration: apekrithē autois ho Iēsous kai eipen Amēn amēn legō hymin zēteite me ouch hoti eidete sēmeia all hoti ephagete ek tōn artōn kai echortasthēte
verses.study_note: 'Ye seek me, not because ye saw the miracles' — they saw the sign (sēmeion) but did not see its significance. They experienced the miracle but missed the message. 'Because ye did eat of the loaves and were filled' (ephagete ek tōn artōn kai echortasthēte — you ate the bread and were satisfied). Their motivation is appetite, not faith. They want Jesus for what he provides, not for who he is. This is the fundamental distinction between true and false seeking: do we want God's gifts or God himself?

verses.kjv_text: Labour not for the meat which perisheth, but for that meat which endureth unto everlasting life, which the Son of man shall give unto you: for him hath God the Father sealed.
verses.original_text: ἐργάζεσθε μὴ τὴν βρῶσιν τὴν ἀπολλυμένην ἀλλὰ τὴν βρῶσιν τὴν μένουσαν εἰς ζωὴν αἰώνιον ἣν ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ὑμῖν δώσει τοῦτον γὰρ ὁ πατὴρ ἐσφράγισεν ὁ θεός
verses.transliteration: ergazesthe mē tēn brōsin tēn apollymenēn alla tēn brōsin tēn menousan eis zōēn aiōnion hēn ho huios tou anthrōpou hymin dōsei touton gar ho patēr esphragisen ho theos
verses.study_note: 'Labour not' (ergazesthe mē — do not work for, do not toil for; not a prohibition of physical work but a redirection of ultimate effort). 'Meat which perisheth' (brōsin tēn apollymenēn — food that is perishing; present participle: in the process of destruction). 'Meat which endureth' (brōsin tēn menousan — food that remains, abides; menō: the Johannine word for permanence). 'The Son of man shall give' (ho huios tou anthrōpou hymin dōsei — the Son of man will give to you; future tense: pointing to the cross). 'Sealed' (esphragisen — authenticated, certified, set his seal upon; a seal indicates ownership, authority, and approval). The Father has certified the Son as the authorized giver of eternal food.

verses.kjv_text: Then said they unto him, What shall we do, that we might work the works of God?
verses.original_text: εἶπον οὖν πρὸς αὐτόν Τί ποιῶμεν ἵνα ἐργαζώμεθα τὰ ἔργα τοῦ θεοῦ
verses.transliteration: eipon oun pros auton Ti poiōmen hina ergazōmetha ta erga tou theou
verses.study_note: 'What shall we do, that we might work the works of God?' (ti poiōmen hina ergazōmetha ta erga tou theou — what should we do so that we may perform the works of God?). The crowd assumes that obtaining eternal food requires human effort — works, rituals, obedience. Their question reflects the natural human assumption: salvation must be earned. Jesus' answer (v.29) completely overturns this assumption.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered and said unto them, This is the work of God, that ye believe on him whom he hath sent.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς Τοῦτό ἐστιν τὸ ἔργον τοῦ θεοῦ ἵνα πιστεύσητε εἰς ὃν ἀπέστειλεν ἐκεῖνος
verses.transliteration: apekrithē ho Iēsous kai eipen autois Touto estin to ergon tou theou hina pisteusēte eis hon apesteilen ekeinos
verses.study_note: 'This is the work of God' (touto estin to ergon tou theou — this is the work God requires). 'That ye believe on him whom he hath sent' (hina pisteusēte eis hon apesteilen ekeinos — that you believe in the one whom that one sent). The singular 'work' replaces their plural 'works.' All the works of God are summed up in one: faith in Christ. This is not works-based salvation but grace-based receiving. Believing is called a 'work' not because it earns salvation but because it is the one response God requires. Even this 'work' is ultimately God's gift (v.44, 65).
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse is a pivotal statement on the nature of salvation. The crowd asks about works (plural); Jesus responds with one work: believe. The 'work of God' (to ergon tou theou) can be read as (1) the work God requires, or (2) the work God produces. Both readings are true: God requires faith, and God produces faith (v.44: 'No man can come to me, except the Father... draw him'). Faith is simultaneously a human responsibility and a divine gift. This is not contradiction but mystery — the mystery of grace.

verses.kjv_text: They said therefore unto him, What sign shewest thou then, that we may see, and believe thee? what dost thou work?
verses.original_text: εἶπον οὖν αὐτῷ Τί οὖν ποιεῖς σὺ σημεῖον ἵνα ἴδωμεν καὶ πιστεύσωμέν σοι τί ἐργάζῃ
verses.transliteration: eipon oun autō Ti oun poieis sy sēmeion hina idōmen kai pisteusōmen soi ti ergazē
verses.study_note: The irony is staggering: Jesus has just fed five thousand with five loaves, and they ask 'What sign shewest thou?' They have seen the sign but want more — they want a permanent supply of miraculous bread, like the manna. Their demand reveals the insatiability of sign-seeking faith: no miracle is ever enough (cf. 1 Corinthians 1:22: 'the Jews require a sign').

verses.kjv_text: Our fathers did eat manna in the desert; as it is written, He gave them bread from heaven to eat.
verses.original_text: οἱ πατέρες ἡμῶν τὸ μάννα ἔφαγον ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ καθώς ἐστιν γεγραμμένον Ἄρτον ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ἔδωκεν αὐτοῖς φαγεῖν
verses.transliteration: hoi pateres hēmōn to manna ephagon en tē erēmō kathōs estin gegrammenon Arton ek tou ouranou edōken autois phagein
verses.study_note: The crowd appeals to the manna tradition, quoting Psalm 78:24 (or Exodus 16:4, 15; Nehemiah 9:15). Their implicit argument: Moses gave bread daily for 40 years; you gave bread once. Can you match Moses? The comparison reveals their misunderstanding on multiple levels: (1) Moses did not give the manna — God did; (2) the manna sustained physical life temporarily — it did not prevent death; (3) Jesus offers something far greater than manna.

verses.kjv_text: Then Jesus said unto them, Verily, verily, I say unto you, Moses gave you not that bread from heaven; but my Father giveth you the true bread from heaven.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν οὖν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν οὐ Μωϋσῆς δέδωκεν ὑμῖν τὸν ἄρτον ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ἀλλ᾽ ὁ πατήρ μου δίδωσιν ὑμῖν τὸν ἄρτον ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ τὸν ἀληθινόν
verses.transliteration: eipen oun autois ho Iēsous Amēn amēn legō hymin ou Mōysēs dedōken hymin ton arton ek tou ouranou all ho patēr mou didōsin hymin ton arton ek tou ouranou ton alēthinon
verses.study_note: Jesus makes two corrections: (1) 'Moses gave you not' (ou Mōysēs dedōken — not Moses gave; it was God who gave the manna). (2) 'My Father giveth' (ho patēr mou didōsin — present tense: is giving, right now). The true bread (ton alēthinon — the genuine, the real, the ultimate reality of which manna was a shadow) is not the manna but what the manna pointed to. The contrast is between type and antitype: manna was temporary bread from the physical sky; the true bread is eternal life from the Father.

verses.kjv_text: For the bread of God is he which cometh down from heaven, and giveth life unto the world.
verses.original_text: ὁ γὰρ ἄρτος τοῦ θεοῦ ἐστιν ὁ καταβαίνων ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καὶ ζωὴν διδοὺς τῷ κόσμῳ
verses.transliteration: ho gar artos tou theou estin ho katabainōn ek tou ouranou kai zōēn didous tō kosmō
verses.study_note: 'The bread of God' (ho artos tou theou — the bread that belongs to God, that comes from God). 'He which cometh down' (ho katabainōn — the one coming down; present participle: continuously descending). The bread is not a thing but a person — 'he which cometh down.' 'Giveth life unto the world' (zōēn didous tō kosmō — giving life to the world). Manna was given to Israel only; the true bread gives life to the whole world. Manna sustained physical life temporarily; this bread gives eternal life permanently.

verses.kjv_text: Then said they unto him, Lord, evermore give us this bread.
verses.original_text: εἶπον οὖν πρὸς αὐτόν Κύριε πάντοτε δὸς ἡμῖν τὸν ἄρτον τοῦτον
verses.transliteration: eipon oun pros auton Kyrie pantote dos hēmin ton arton touton
verses.study_note: 'Lord, evermore give us this bread' (pantote dos hēmin ton arton touton — always give us this bread). Like the Samaritan woman who asked for water that would eliminate trips to the well (4:15), the crowd wants bread that eliminates hunger. They still think in physical categories. But their request, however misguided, opens the door for Jesus' great revelation in v.35.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus said unto them, I am the bread of life: he that cometh to me shall never hunger; and he that believeth on me shall never thirst.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ἄρτος τῆς ζωῆς ὁ ἐρχόμενος πρός με οὐ μὴ πεινάσῃ καὶ ὁ πιστεύων εἰς ἐμὲ οὐ μὴ διψήσῃ πώποτε
verses.transliteration: eipen de autois ho Iēsous Egō eimi ho artos tēs zōēs ho erchomenos pros me ou mē peinasē kai ho pisteuōn eis eme ou mē dipsēsē pōpote
verses.study_note: 'I am the bread of life' (Egō eimi ho artos tēs zōēs — the first of seven great 'I AM' statements with predicates in John). 'He that cometh to me shall never hunger' (ou mē peinasē — emphatic double negative: shall absolutely never hunger). 'He that believeth on me shall never thirst' (ou mē dipsēsē pōpote — shall never, ever thirst). Coming and believing are parallel actions — to come to Jesus is to believe in him. The promises are absolute: permanent satisfaction of the soul's deepest hunger and thirst. This echoes Isaiah 55:1-2: 'Ho, every one that thirsteth, come ye to the waters... eat ye that which is good.'
verses.doctrinal_note: This is the first of seven 'I AM' (Egō eimi) statements with predicates in John: (1) I am the bread of life (6:35, 48); (2) I am the light of the world (8:12); (3) I am the door (10:7, 9); (4) I am the good shepherd (10:11, 14); (5) I am the resurrection and the life (11:25); (6) I am the way, the truth, and the life (14:6); (7) I am the true vine (15:1, 5). Each 'I AM' reveals an aspect of Christ's sufficiency for human need. 'The bread of life' means the bread that gives life, the bread that is life itself. Jesus does not merely give bread — he is the bread. The satisfaction he offers is total and permanent.

verses.kjv_text: But I said unto you, That ye also have seen me, and believe not.
verses.original_text: ἀλλ᾽ εἶπον ὑμῖν ὅτι καὶ ἑωράκατέ με καὶ οὐ πιστεύετε
verses.transliteration: all eipon hymin hoti kai heōrakate me kai ou pisteuete
verses.study_note: 'Ye have seen me, and believe not' (heōrakate me kai ou pisteuete — you have seen me and you do not believe). Seeing is not believing. The crowd has seen Jesus, seen his miracles, eaten his bread — yet they do not believe. Unbelief is not an intellectual problem but a moral and spiritual one. This sets up the crucial teaching of vv.37-44: why some believe and others do not.

verses.kjv_text: All that the Father giveth me shall come to me; and him that cometh to me I will in no wise cast out.
verses.original_text: πᾶν ὃ δίδωσίν μοι ὁ πατὴρ πρὸς ἐμὲ ἥξει καὶ τὸν ἐρχόμενον πρός με οὐ μὴ ἐκβάλω ἔξω
verses.transliteration: pan ho didōsin moi ho patēr pros eme hēxei kai ton erchomenon pros me ou mē ekbalō exō
verses.study_note: 'All that the Father giveth me shall come to me' (pan ho didōsin moi ho patēr pros eme hēxei — everything the Father gives me will come to me). 'Him that cometh to me I will in no wise cast out' (ton erchomenon pros me ou mē ekbalō exō — the one coming to me I will absolutely never throw out). Two truths held together: (1) Divine sovereignty — the Father gives people to the Son. (2) Human welcome — whoever comes will never be rejected. The neuter 'all' (pan) in the first clause views the elect as a collective gift; the masculine 'him that cometh' (ton erchomenon) views each individual personally. No one who comes to Christ will ever be turned away.
verses.doctrinal_note: John 6:37 is one of the most comprehensive statements of salvation in Scripture. It contains both divine sovereignty and human responsibility in perfect balance. The Father gives (divine election); the person comes (human response); the Son receives (guaranteed acceptance). Three certainties: (1) all whom the Father gives will come — divine sovereignty guarantees it; (2) anyone who comes will not be cast out — the invitation is universal and the welcome unconditional; (3) the Son's will is to keep, not to lose (v.39). This verse destroys both fatalism ('I cannot come') and presumption ('I might be rejected').

verses.kjv_text: For I came down from heaven, not to do mine own will, but the will of him that sent me.
verses.original_text: ὅτι καταβέβηκα ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ οὐχ ἵνα ποιῶ τὸ θέλημα τὸ ἐμὸν ἀλλὰ τὸ θέλημα τοῦ πέμψαντός με
verses.transliteration: hoti katabebēka ek tou ouranou ouch hina poiō to thelēma to emon alla to thelēma tou pempsantos me
verses.study_note: 'I came down from heaven' (katabebēka ek tou ouranou — I have come down from heaven; perfect tense: I came and am still here as the one who came from heaven). This is a clear claim to pre-existence and heavenly origin. 'Not to do mine own will' — Jesus' mission is defined by perfect obedience to the Father's will. The Father's will is specified in vv.39-40: to lose nothing and to raise up everything at the last day.

verses.kjv_text: And this is the Father's will which hath sent me, that of all which he hath given me I should lose nothing, but should raise it up again at the last day.
verses.original_text: τοῦτο δέ ἐστιν τὸ θέλημα τοῦ πέμψαντός με ἵνα πᾶν ὃ δέδωκέν μοι μὴ ἀπολέσω ἐξ αὐτοῦ ἀλλὰ ἀναστήσω αὐτὸ ἐν τῇ ἐσχάτῃ ἡμέρᾳ
verses.transliteration: touto de estin to thelēma tou pempsantos me hina pan ho dedōken moi mē apolesō ex autou alla anastēsō auto en tē eschatē hēmera
verses.study_note: 'I should lose nothing' (mē apolesō ex autou — that I should not lose any of it). 'Raise it up again at the last day' (anastēsō auto en tē eschatē hēmera — I will raise it up on the last day). The Father's will is the eternal security of every believer: none will be lost. The neuter 'all which' (pan ho) again treats believers as a collective gift. 'The last day' (tē eschatē hēmera) — the day of resurrection, the consummation of salvation. Jesus repeats 'raise him up at the last day' four times in this chapter (vv.39, 40, 44, 54), emphasising the certainty of bodily resurrection.
verses.doctrinal_note: The doctrine of the perseverance of the saints is grounded in the Father's will and the Son's obedience. Jesus came to do the Father's will; the Father's will is that none be lost; therefore none will be lost. The security of the believer rests not on the believer's ability to hold on but on Christ's faithfulness to the Father's will. 'I should lose nothing' is not a hope but a commission — the Father has entrusted believers to the Son, and the Son will not fail. Cf. John 10:28-29; 17:12; Romans 8:29-30.

verses.kjv_text: And this is the will of him that sent me, that every one which seeth the Son, and believeth on him, may have everlasting life: and I will raise him up at the last day.
verses.original_text: τοῦτο δέ ἐστιν τὸ θέλημα τοῦ πέμψαντός με ἵνα πᾶς ὁ θεωρῶν τὸν υἱὸν καὶ πιστεύων εἰς αὐτὸν ἔχῃ ζωὴν αἰώνιον καὶ ἀναστήσω αὐτὸν ἐγὼ ἐν τῇ ἐσχάτῃ ἡμέρᾳ
verses.transliteration: touto de estin to thelēma tou pempsantos me hina pas ho theōrōn ton huion kai pisteuōn eis auton echē zōēn aiōnion kai anastēsō auton egō en tē eschatē hēmera
verses.study_note: 'Every one which seeth the Son, and believeth on him' (pas ho theōrōn ton huion kai pisteuōn eis auton — everyone who contemplates the Son and believes in him). 'Seeth' (theōrōn — not merely seeing but contemplating, gazing with understanding; from theōreō: to observe attentively). 'May have everlasting life' (echē zōēn aiōnion — present subjunctive: may possess eternal life now). Verse 39 states the Father's will negatively (lose nothing); v.40 states it positively (give eternal life). The human responsibility is seeing and believing; the divine guarantee is eternal life and resurrection.

verses.kjv_text: The Jews then murmured at him, because he said, I am the bread which came down from heaven.
verses.original_text: ἐγόγγυζον οὖν οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι περὶ αὐτοῦ ὅτι εἶπεν Ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ἄρτος ὁ καταβὰς ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ
verses.transliteration: egongyzon oun hoi Ioudaioi peri autou hoti eipen Egō eimi ho artos ho katabas ek tou ouranou
verses.study_note: 'Murmured' (egongyzon — they were grumbling; the word deliberately echoes Israel's murmuring against Moses in the wilderness, Exodus 16:2, 7-9). The parallel is intentional: as Israel murmured against Moses despite the manna, so the Jews murmur against Jesus despite the feeding miracle. 'I am the bread which came down from heaven' — the claim to heavenly origin is the stumbling block.

verses.kjv_text: And they said, Is not this Jesus, the son of Joseph, whose father and mother we know? how is it then that he saith, I came down from heaven?
verses.original_text: καὶ ἔλεγον Οὐχ οὗτός ἐστιν Ἰησοῦς ὁ υἱὸς Ἰωσήφ οὗ ἡμεῖς οἴδαμεν τὸν πατέρα καὶ τὴν μητέρα πῶς οὖν λέγει οὗτος ὅτι Ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καταβέβηκα
verses.transliteration: kai elegon Ouch houtos estin Iēsous ho huios Iōsēph hou hēmeis oidamen ton patera kai tēn mētera pōs oun legei houtos hoti Ek tou ouranou katabebēka
verses.study_note: 'Is not this Jesus, the son of Joseph?' — they know his earthly family and conclude his heavenly origin is impossible. Their reasoning is logical but limited: they judge by appearances (cf. 7:24). They know Joseph and Mary but do not know the virgin birth. Their familiarity breeds contempt — they cannot see the divine in the ordinary. This objection recurs throughout history: how can God come in human flesh?

verses.kjv_text: Jesus therefore answered and said unto them, Murmur not among yourselves.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη οὖν ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς Μὴ γογγύζετε μετ᾽ ἀλλήλων
verses.transliteration: apekrithē oun ho Iēsous kai eipen autois Mē gongyzete met allēlōn
verses.study_note: 'Murmur not' (mē gongyzete — stop grumbling; present imperative with mē: cease this activity). Jesus addresses not the intellectual objection but the heart attitude. Murmuring reveals a spirit of resistance, not genuine inquiry. Instead of answering their genealogical objection, Jesus goes deeper to the real issue: why they cannot believe (v.44).

verses.kjv_text: No man can come to me, except the Father which hath sent me draw him: and I will raise him up at the last day.
verses.original_text: οὐδεὶς δύναται ἐλθεῖν πρός με ἐὰν μὴ ὁ πατὴρ ὁ πέμψας με ἑλκύσῃ αὐτόν κἀγὼ ἀναστήσω αὐτὸν ἐν τῇ ἐσχάτῃ ἡμέρᾳ
verses.transliteration: oudeis dynatai elthein pros me ean mē ho patēr ho pempsas me helkysē auton kagō anastēsō auton en tē eschatē hēmera
verses.study_note: 'No man can come to me' (oudeis dynatai elthein pros me — no one is able to come to me). 'Except the Father... draw him' (ean mē ho patēr helkysē auton — unless the Father draws him). 'Draw' (helkysē — from helkō: to draw, drag, attract with compelling force; the same word in 12:32: 'I, if I be lifted up from the earth, will draw all men unto me'; and in 21:11: Peter drawing the net full of fish; and in Acts 16:19: dragging Paul and Silas). This is not mere invitation but effectual drawing — a powerful, inward work of the Father that overcomes human resistance and inability. The reason they 'cannot' come is not physical impossibility but spiritual inability: fallen humanity is unable to come to Christ apart from divine enablement.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse, together with v.65, is one of the clearest statements of the necessity of divine grace for salvation. 'No man can come' (oudeis dynatai) — the inability is absolute. 'Except the Father draw him' (ean mē helkysē) — the drawing is necessary, effectual, and gracious. This is not merely offering an opportunity but overcoming resistance. The human will is not violated but renewed; the heart is not forced but freed. Those whom the Father draws do come (v.37: 'All that the Father giveth me shall come'); and those who come are kept (v.39: 'I should lose nothing'). The chain is unbreakable: the Father draws → the person comes → the Son receives → the Son raises up at the last day.

verses.kjv_text: It is written in the prophets, And they shall be all taught of God. Every man therefore that hath heard, and hath learned of the Father, cometh unto me.
verses.original_text: ἔστιν γεγραμμένον ἐν τοῖς προφήταις Καὶ ἔσονται πάντες διδακτοὶ τοῦ θεοῦ πᾶς οὖν ὁ ἀκούσας παρὰ τοῦ πατρὸς καὶ μαθὼν ἔρχεται πρός με
verses.transliteration: estin gegrammenon en tois prophētais Kai esontai pantes didaktoi tou theou pas oun ho akousas para tou patros kai mathōn erchetai pros me
verses.study_note: Jesus quotes Isaiah 54:13: 'All thy children shall be taught of the LORD' (didaktoi tou theou — taught by God). 'Every man that hath heard, and hath learned of the Father' (pas ho akousas para tou patros kai mathōn — everyone who has heard from the Father and has learned). The 'drawing' of v.44 is explained as divine teaching — an inward illumination that results in genuine learning. Those who truly hear and learn from the Father inevitably come to Christ. The Father's teaching is effective: all who are taught actually come.

verses.kjv_text: Not that any man hath seen the Father, save he which is of God, he hath seen the Father.
verses.original_text: οὐχ ὅτι τὸν πατέρα τις ἑώρακεν εἰ μὴ ὁ ὢν παρὰ τοῦ θεοῦ οὗτος ἑώρακεν τὸν πατέρα
verses.transliteration: ouch hoti ton patera tis heōraken ei mē ho ōn para tou theou houtos heōraken ton patera
verses.study_note: A clarification: no one has directly seen the Father (cf. 1:18; 5:37; 1 Timothy 6:16). The 'hearing and learning' of v.45 is not direct vision but spiritual illumination. Only the one 'who is of God' (ho ōn para tou theou — the one who is from God, i.e., Jesus himself) has seen the Father. Jesus is the unique revealer of the Father — all knowledge of God comes through him.

verses.kjv_text: Verily, verily, I say unto you, He that believeth on me hath everlasting life.
verses.original_text: ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ὁ πιστεύων εἰς ἐμὲ ἔχει ζωὴν αἰώνιον
verses.transliteration: amēn amēn legō hymin ho pisteuōn eis eme echei zōēn aiōnion
verses.study_note: 'He that believeth on me hath everlasting life' (ho pisteuōn eis eme echei zōēn aiōnion — the one believing in me possesses eternal life). 'Hath' (echei — present tense: possesses right now, not 'will have' in the future). Eternal life is a present possession, not merely a future promise. The condition is belief; the result is immediate. This repeats and reinforces 3:36 and 5:24.

verses.kjv_text: I am that bread of life.
verses.original_text: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ἄρτος τῆς ζωῆς
verses.transliteration: egō eimi ho artos tēs zōēs
verses.study_note: 'I am that bread of life' (egō eimi ho artos tēs zōēs — I AM the bread of life). The 'I AM' is repeated from v.35, now after the discourse on the Father's drawing (vv.37-46). The statement is more emphatic here because it follows the explanation of divine initiative. Jesus is the bread — not a commodity to be earned but a gift to be received by faith.

verses.kjv_text: Your fathers did eat manna in the wilderness, and are dead.
verses.original_text: οἱ πατέρες ὑμῶν ἔφαγον τὸ μάννα ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ καὶ ἀπέθανον
verses.transliteration: hoi pateres hymōn ephagon to manna en tē erēmō kai apethanon
verses.study_note: 'Your fathers' (hoi pateres hymōn — not 'our fathers' but 'your fathers,' creating distance). 'Did eat manna... and are dead' (ephagon to manna kai apethanon — they ate the manna and they died). The manna kept them alive temporarily, but they still died. The manna was good but not ultimate. It sustained bodies but could not conquer death. The contrast with the bread Jesus offers is absolute: his bread gives life that death cannot touch.

verses.kjv_text: This is the bread which cometh down from heaven, that a man may eat thereof, and not die.
verses.original_text: οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ ἄρτος ὁ ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καταβαίνων ἵνα τις ἐξ αὐτοῦ φάγῃ καὶ μὴ ἀποθάνῃ
verses.transliteration: houtos estin ho artos ho ek tou ouranou katabainōn hina tis ex autou phagē kai mē apothanē
verses.study_note: 'That a man may eat thereof, and not die' (hina tis ex autou phagē kai mē apothanē — so that someone may eat of it and not die). 'Not die' does not mean physical immortality but freedom from eternal death. The one who feeds on Christ will experience physical death but not the 'second death' (Revelation 2:11; 20:6, 14). Death is transformed from a terminus into a passage — 'to be absent from the body... present with the Lord' (2 Corinthians 5:8).

verses.kjv_text: I am the living bread which came down from heaven: if any man eat of this bread, he shall live for ever: and the bread that I will give is my flesh, which I will give for the life of the world.
verses.original_text: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ἄρτος ὁ ζῶν ὁ ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καταβάς ἐάν τις φάγῃ ἐκ τούτου τοῦ ἄρτου ζήσεται εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα καὶ ὁ ἄρτος δὲ ὃν ἐγὼ δώσω ἡ σάρξ μού ἐστιν ὑπὲρ τῆς τοῦ κόσμου ζωῆς
verses.transliteration: egō eimi ho artos ho zōn ho ek tou ouranou katabas ean tis phagē ek toutou tou artou zēsetai eis ton aiōna kai ho artos de hon egō dōsō hē sarx mou estin hyper tēs tou kosmou zōēs
verses.study_note: 'The living bread' (ho artos ho zōn — the bread that is alive; not dead bread like manna, but bread that has life in itself). 'If any man eat of this bread' (ean tis phagē — if anyone eats) — the invitation is universal. 'He shall live for ever' (zēsetai eis ton aiōna — will live into the age, eternally). 'The bread that I will give is my flesh' (ho artos hon egō dōsō hē sarx mou estin) — this is the turning point. Jesus identifies the bread with his own flesh (sarx — physical body), pointing directly to the cross. 'For the life of the world' (hyper tēs tou kosmou zōēs — on behalf of, for the sake of the world's life). The bread is given by dying — the cross is the means by which the bread of life becomes available.
verses.doctrinal_note: Verse 51 bridges the feeding miracle and the cross. The bread Jesus 'will give' (dōsō — future tense) is his flesh — his body offered in sacrificial death. 'For the life of the world' (hyper tēs tou kosmou zōēs) uses the preposition hyper (on behalf of, in the place of), the language of substitutionary atonement. Jesus' death is not an accident but a gift: 'the bread that I will give.' The scope is universal ('the world'), though the application is particular (those who 'eat,' i.e., believe). This verse anticipates both the Last Supper ('This is my body, which is given for you,' Luke 22:19) and the theology of the cross.

verses.kjv_text: The Jews therefore strove among themselves, saying, How can this man give us his flesh to eat?
verses.original_text: ἐμάχοντο οὖν πρὸς ἀλλήλους οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι λέγοντες Πῶς δύναται οὗτος ἡμῖν δοῦναι τὴν σάρκα φαγεῖν
verses.transliteration: emachonto oun pros allēlous hoi Ioudaioi legontes Pōs dynatai houtos hēmin dounai tēn sarka phagein
verses.study_note: 'Strove among themselves' (emachonto pros allēlous — they were fighting, quarreling with one another). 'How can this man give us his flesh to eat?' — they take Jesus literally and find the idea revolting (cf. Nicodemus, 3:4: 'How can a man be born when he is old?' and the Samaritan woman, 4:11: 'Thou hast nothing to draw with'). John's pattern: Jesus speaks in spiritual metaphor; the audience takes it literally; the misunderstanding leads to deeper teaching.

verses.kjv_text: Then Jesus said unto them, Verily, verily, I say unto you, Except ye eat the flesh of the Son of man, and drink his blood, ye have no life in you.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν οὖν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ἐὰν μὴ φάγητε τὴν σάρκα τοῦ υἱοῦ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου καὶ πίητε αὐτοῦ τὸ αἷμα οὐκ ἔχετε ζωὴν ἐν ἑαυτοῖς
verses.transliteration: eipen oun autois ho Iēsous Amēn amēn legō hymin ean mē phagēte tēn sarka tou huiou tou anthrōpou kai piēte autou to haima ouk echete zōēn en heautois
verses.study_note: 'Except ye eat the flesh... and drink his blood' (ean mē phagēte tēn sarka... kai piēte autou to haima — unless you eat the flesh and drink the blood). 'Ye have no life in you' (ouk echete zōēn en heautois — you do not have life in yourselves). Far from softening his language, Jesus intensifies it. 'Eating flesh' and 'drinking blood' would have horrified Jewish listeners — blood consumption was absolutely forbidden (Leviticus 17:10-14). Jesus is using the most vivid possible metaphor for the total personal appropriation of his sacrifice. As food is taken into the body, becoming part of the person, so Christ must be received into one's innermost being by faith.
verses.doctrinal_note: The language of eating flesh and drinking blood is metaphorical, not literal — as v.63 clarifies: 'It is the spirit that quickeneth; the flesh profiteth nothing.' The 'eating' and 'drinking' represent believing (cf. v.35: 'he that cometh to me shall never hunger; he that believeth on me shall never thirst' — coming/believing = eating/drinking). The parallel structure of vv.35, 47, 53-54 confirms this: believing (v.47) = eating and drinking (v.54). Nonetheless, the language anticipates the Lord's Supper, where bread and wine symbolise body and blood. The Eucharist is the visible sign of the invisible spiritual reality taught here: appropriating Christ's sacrificial death by faith.

verses.kjv_text: Whoso eateth my flesh, and drinketh my blood, hath eternal life; and I will raise him up at the last day.
verses.original_text: ὁ τρώγων μου τὴν σάρκα καὶ πίνων μου τὸ αἷμα ἔχει ζωὴν αἰώνιον κἀγὼ ἀναστήσω αὐτὸν τῇ ἐσχάτῃ ἡμέρᾳ
verses.transliteration: ho trōgōn mou tēn sarka kai pinōn mou to haima echei zōēn aiōnion kagō anastēsō auton tē eschatē hēmera
verses.study_note: 'Whoso eateth' (ho trōgōn — from trōgō: to gnaw, to munch, to chew; a more graphic word than phagō, emphasising intimate, personal consumption). 'Hath eternal life' (echei zōēn aiōnion — present tense: possesses eternal life now). 'I will raise him up at the last day' (anastēsō auton tē eschatē hēmera — the fourth and final occurrence of this refrain in ch.6). The one who feeds on Christ by faith possesses eternal life now and will be raised bodily at the last day.

verses.kjv_text: For my flesh is meat indeed, and my blood is drink indeed.
verses.original_text: ἡ γὰρ σάρξ μου ἀληθής ἐστιν βρῶσις καὶ τὸ αἷμά μου ἀληθής ἐστιν πόσις
verses.transliteration: hē gar sarx mou alēthēs estin brōsis kai to haima mou alēthēs estin posis
verses.study_note: 'Meat indeed' (alēthēs brōsis — true food, genuine nourishment; some manuscripts read alēthōs: truly, really). 'Drink indeed' (alēthēs posis — true drink). Jesus' flesh and blood provide what all physical food merely symbolises: genuine, lasting, soul-sustaining nourishment. All earthly food is a shadow of this reality — it sustains temporarily but cannot satisfy ultimately. Only Christ satisfies the deepest hunger of the human soul.

verses.kjv_text: He that eateth my flesh, and drinketh my blood, dwelleth in me, and I in him.
verses.original_text: ὁ τρώγων μου τὴν σάρκα καὶ πίνων μου τὸ αἷμα ἐν ἐμοὶ μένει κἀγὼ ἐν αὐτῷ
verses.transliteration: ho trōgōn mou tēn sarka kai pinōn mou to haima en emoi menei kagō en autō
verses.study_note: 'Dwelleth in me, and I in him' (en emoi menei kagō en autō — abides in me, and I in him). The result of feeding on Christ is mutual indwelling — the believer in Christ and Christ in the believer. This is the deepest expression of spiritual union: not merely external association but internal interpenetration. The language anticipates the vine metaphor (15:4-5: 'Abide in me, and I in you') and the prayer of 17:21 ('that they all may be one; as thou, Father, art in me, and I in thee').

verses.kjv_text: As the living Father hath sent me, and I live by the Father: so he that eateth me, even he shall live by me.
verses.original_text: καθὼς ἀπέστειλέν με ὁ ζῶν πατὴρ κἀγὼ ζῶ διὰ τὸν πατέρα καὶ ὁ τρώγων με κἀκεῖνος ζήσεται δι᾽ ἐμέ
verses.transliteration: kathōs apesteilen me ho zōn patēr kagō zō dia ton patera kai ho trōgōn me kakeinos zēsetai di eme
verses.study_note: 'The living Father' (ho zōn patēr — the Father who lives, who has life in himself; cf. 5:26). 'I live by the Father' (zō dia ton patera — I live through/because of the Father). 'He that eateth me shall live by me' (ho trōgōn me zēsetai di eme — the one who feeds on me will live through/because of me). A chain of life: the Father → the Son → the believer. As the Son's life flows from the Father, so the believer's life flows from the Son. The relationship is analogical, not identical, but the principle is the same: dependent, derived, flowing life.

verses.kjv_text: This is that bread which came down from heaven: not as your fathers did eat manna, and are dead: he that eateth of this bread shall live for ever.
verses.original_text: οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ ἄρτος ὁ ἐξ οὐρανοῦ καταβάς οὐ καθὼς ἔφαγον οἱ πατέρες ὑμῶν τὸ μάννα καὶ ἀπέθανον ὁ τρώγων τοῦτον τὸν ἄρτον ζήσεται εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα
verses.transliteration: houtos estin ho artos ho ex ouranou katabas ou kathōs ephagon hoi pateres hymōn to manna kai apethanon ho trōgōn touton ton arton zēsetai eis ton aiōna
verses.study_note: The discourse reaches its conclusion with a summary contrast: manna → death; Christ → eternal life. The summary gathers the entire argument: (1) This bread came from heaven (v.33). (2) It is superior to manna (vv.31-32, 49). (3) It gives eternal life (vv.35, 47-48). (4) It is Jesus' flesh given for the world (v.51). (5) It must be personally appropriated by faith (vv.53-56). (6) It results in eternal life (vv.40, 47, 54).

verses.kjv_text: These things said he in the synagogue, as he taught in Capernaum.
verses.original_text: ταῦτα εἶπεν ἐν συναγωγῇ διδάσκων ἐν Καπερναούμ
verses.transliteration: tauta eipen en synagōgē didaskōn en Kapernaoum
verses.study_note: 'In the synagogue' (en synagōgē — in a synagogue; without the article, possibly 'in synagogue assembly'). 'In Capernaum' — Jesus' adopted home base in Galilee (cf. Matthew 4:13). The discourse was public, delivered in a formal teaching setting. The synagogue at Capernaum has been excavated — a magnificent limestone structure (the current ruins are 4th-century, built over the 1st-century basalt synagogue where Jesus taught).

verses.kjv_text: Many therefore of his disciples, when they had heard this, said, This is an hard saying; who can hear it?
verses.original_text: πολλοὶ οὖν ἀκούσαντες ἐκ τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ εἶπον Σκληρός ἐστιν οὗτος ὁ λόγος τίς δύναται αὐτοῦ ἀκούειν
verses.transliteration: polloi oun akousantes ek tōn mathētōn autou eipon Sklēros estin houtos ho logos tis dynatai autou akouein
verses.study_note: 'His disciples' (tōn mathētōn autou — not the Twelve, but the broader circle of followers). 'A hard saying' (sklēros logos — harsh, rough, offensive speech; sklēros means hard like a rock, not intellectually difficult but spiritually offensive). 'Who can hear it?' (tis dynatai autou akouein — who is able to listen to this?). The offence is not intellectual confusion but spiritual resistance — they find the teaching unacceptable, not incomprehensible. Jesus demands what human pride resists: total dependence on him for life.

verses.kjv_text: When Jesus knew in himself that his disciples murmured at it, he said unto them, Doth this offend you?
verses.original_text: εἰδὼς δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἐν ἑαυτῷ ὅτι γογγύζουσιν περὶ τούτου οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς Τοῦτο ὑμᾶς σκανδαλίζει
verses.transliteration: eidōs de ho Iēsous en heautō hoti gongyzousin peri toutou hoi mathētai autou eipen autois Touto hymas skandalizei
verses.study_note: 'Knew in himself' (eidōs en heautō — perceiving within himself; Jesus' supernatural awareness). 'Doth this offend you?' (touto hymas skandalizei — does this scandalise you? From skandalizō: to cause to stumble, to be a stumbling-block). Jesus does not soften his teaching in response to offence. He asks whether they are stumbling over his words — and then provides an even greater claim (v.62).

verses.kjv_text: What and if ye shall see the Son of man ascend up where he was before?
verses.original_text: ἐὰν οὖν θεωρ���τε τὸν υἱὸν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἀναβαίνοντα ὅπου ἦν τὸ πρότερον
verses.transliteration: ean oun theōrēte ton huion tou anthrōpou anabainonta hopou ēn to proteron
verses.study_note: 'Ascend up where he was before' (anabainonta hopou ēn to proteron — ascending to where he was formerly). This is a clear claim to pre-existence: before his incarnation, the Son of man was in heaven. 'Where he was before' implies eternal pre-existence with the Father (cf. 1:1; 17:5). The sentence is incomplete — Jesus leaves them to work out the implication: if they are offended by his claim to be bread from heaven, what will they think when they see him ascend back to heaven? The ascension will confirm his heavenly origin.

verses.kjv_text: It is the spirit that quickeneth; the flesh profiteth nothing: the words that I speak unto you, they are spirit, and they are life.
verses.original_text: τὸ πνεῦμά ἐστιν τὸ ζωοποιοῦν ἡ σὰρξ οὐκ ὠφελεῖ οὐδέν τὰ ῥήματα ἃ ἐγὼ λαλῶ ὑμῖν πνεῦμά ἐστιν καὶ ζωή ἐστιν
verses.transliteration: to pneuma estin to zōopoioun hē sarx ouk ōphelei ouden ta rhēmata ha egō lalō hymin pneuma estin kai zōē estin
verses.study_note: 'The spirit quickeneth' (to pneuma estin to zōopoioun — the Spirit is the one who gives life). 'The flesh profiteth nothing' (hē sarx ouk ōphelei ouden — the flesh avails nothing, accomplishes nothing). 'My words... are spirit, and are life' (ta rhēmata... pneuma estin kai zōē estin). This is the interpretive key to the entire discourse: (1) Jesus is not speaking of literal flesh-eating but of spiritual appropriation. (2) The Spirit gives life, not physical consumption. (3) Jesus' words are the vehicle of spiritual life — to 'eat his flesh' is to receive his words by faith. The flesh/spirit contrast is not body vs. soul but human effort vs. divine enablement.
verses.doctrinal_note: Verse 63 is crucial for interpreting the entire Bread of Life discourse. 'The flesh profiteth nothing' does not denigrate the incarnation (the Word became flesh, 1:14) but denies that fleshly understanding or human effort can produce spiritual life. The 'eating' of vv.53-56 is spiritual, not physical. Jesus' words are spirit and life — to hear them with faith is to feed on him. This verse also grounds the later sacramental theology: the bread and wine of the Lord's Supper are spiritually efficacious through the work of the Holy Spirit, not through any magic in the physical elements. The Spirit gives life through the word of Christ received by faith.

verses.kjv_text: But there are some of you that believe not. For Jesus knew from the beginning who they were that believed not, and who should betray him.
verses.original_text: ἀλλ᾽ εἰσὶν ἐξ ὑμῶν τινες οἳ οὐ πιστεύουσιν ᾔδει γὰρ ἐξ ἀρχῆς ὁ Ἰησοῦς τίνες εἰσὶν οἱ μὴ πιστεύοντες καὶ τίς ἐστιν ὁ παραδώσων αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: all eisin ex hymōn tines hoi ou pisteuousin ēdei gar ex archēs ho Iēsous tines eisin hoi mē pisteuontes kai tis estin ho paradōsōn auton
verses.study_note: 'Some of you that believe not' — Jesus knows who believes and who does not. 'From the beginning' (ex archēs — from the start of his ministry, or from eternity). 'Who should betray him' (ho paradōsōn auton — the one who was going to hand him over; a clear reference to Judas). Jesus was never deceived about the true state of those around him (cf. 2:24-25). He chose Judas knowing full well what Judas would do.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Therefore said I unto you, that no man can come unto me, except it were given unto him of my Father.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἔλεγεν Διὰ τοῦτο εἴρηκα ὑμῖν ὅτι οὐδεὶς δύναται ἐλθεῖν πρός με ἐὰν μὴ ᾖ δεδομένον αὐτῷ ἐκ τοῦ πατρός μου
verses.transliteration: kai elegen Dia touto eirēka hymin hoti oudeis dynatai elthein pros me ean mē ē dedomenon autō ek tou patros mou
verses.study_note: 'Except it were given unto him of my Father' (ean mē ē dedomenon autō ek tou patros mou — unless it has been granted to him from my Father; perfect passive participle: a settled gift from the Father). This repeats and reinforces v.44 ('except the Father draw him') but uses different language: 'drawing' becomes 'giving.' The ability to come to Christ is a gift from the Father. This explains the unbelief: they do not believe because it has not been given to them. Human inability does not excuse but explains — the fault lies in the rebellious will, not in God's justice.

verses.kjv_text: From that time many of his disciples went back, and walked no more with him.
verses.original_text: ἐκ τούτου πολλοὶ ἀπῆλθον τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ εἰς τὰ ὀπίσω καὶ οὐκέτι μετ᾽ αὐτοῦ περιεπάτουν
verses.transliteration: ek toutou polloi apēlthon tōn mathētōn autou eis ta opisō kai ouketi met autou periepatoun
verses.study_note: 'Went back' (apēlthon eis ta opisō — went away to the things behind, turned back). 'Walked no more with him' (ouketi met autou periepatoun — they no longer walked about with him). This is one of the saddest verses in the Gospel. The crowd that wanted to make Jesus king (v.15) now abandons him. The teaching about divine sovereignty, spiritual eating, and total dependence on Christ was too much. Jesus does not chase them, does not soften his message, does not call them back. He lets them go — and turns to the Twelve.

verses.kjv_text: Then said Jesus unto the twelve, Will ye also go away?
verses.original_text: εἶπεν οὖν ὁ Ἰησοῦς τοῖς δώδεκα Μὴ καὶ ὑμεῖς θέλετε ὑπάγειν
verses.transliteration: eipen oun ho Iēsous tois dōdeka Mē kai hymeis thelete hypagein
verses.study_note: 'Will ye also go away?' (mē kai hymeis thelete hypagein — you do not also want to go away, do you? The question with mē expects a negative answer but allows for the possibility). Jesus does not coerce faith. He offers the Twelve a genuine choice — they are free to leave. But the question is also a test: having seen and heard everything, where will they go? This is the first time John uses 'the twelve' (tois dōdeka) as a designation for the inner circle.

verses.kjv_text: Then Simon Peter answered him, Lord, to whom shall we go? thou hast the words of eternal life.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη αὐτῷ Σίμων Πέτρος Κύριε πρὸς τίνα ἀπελευσόμεθα ῥήματα ζωῆς αἰωνίου ἔχεις
verses.transliteration: apekrithē autō Simōn Petros Kyrie pros tina apeleusometha rhēmata zōēs aiōniou echeis
verses.study_note: 'To whom shall we go?' (pros tina apeleusometha — to whom shall we go away?). The question implies: there is no alternative. 'Thou hast the words of eternal life' (rhēmata zōēs aiōniou echeis — you have utterances of eternal life). Peter echoes v.63: Jesus' words are spirit and life. Peter does not claim to understand everything — he claims there is nowhere else to go. True faith is not perfect comprehension but tenacious commitment to the one who has the words of life.

verses.kjv_text: And we believe and are sure that thou art that Christ, the Son of the living God.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἡμεῖς πεπιστεύκαμεν καὶ ἐγνώκαμεν ὅτι σὺ εἶ ὁ ἅγιος τοῦ θεοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai hēmeis pepisteukamen kai egnōkamen hoti sy ei ho hagios tou theou
verses.study_note: 'We believe and are sure' (pepisteukamen kai egnōkamen — we have believed and have come to know; both perfect tense: settled, established faith and knowledge). The Greek text reads 'the Holy One of God' (ho hagios tou theou), while the KJV follows later manuscripts reading 'the Christ, the Son of the living God.' Both readings affirm Jesus' unique divine identity. 'The Holy One of God' is a messianic title (cf. Mark 1:24; Luke 4:34; Psalm 16:10). Peter's confession here parallels the Synoptic confession at Caesarea Philippi (Matthew 16:16).
verses.doctrinal_note: Peter's confession is the positive counterpart to the crowd's departure. Where many turned back, Peter clings to Christ — not because he understands everything but because he has tasted the words of eternal life and found them to be true. The order is significant: 'we have believed and have come to know' — faith precedes knowledge. Understanding follows commitment, not the reverse. This is the pattern of all true faith: 'I believe in order that I may understand' (credo ut intelligam).

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered them, Have not I chosen you twelve, and one of you is a devil?
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς Οὐκ ἐγὼ ὑμᾶς τοὺς δώδεκα ἐξελεξάμην καὶ ἐξ ὑμῶν εἷς διάβολός ἐστιν
verses.transliteration: apekrithē autois ho Iēsous Ouk egō hymas tous dōdeka exelexamēn kai ex hymōn heis diabolos estin
verses.study_note: 'Have not I chosen you twelve?' (ouk egō hymas exelexamēn — did I not choose you?). 'One of you is a devil' (heis diabolos estin — one is a devil/slanderer). Jesus responds to Peter's confession not with commendation (as in Matthew 16:17) but with a sobering warning. Even among the chosen Twelve, one is a betrayer. 'Devil' (diabolos — accuser, slanderer; not that Judas is Satan but that he serves Satan's purposes). Jesus chose Judas knowing what he would become (cf. 13:18: 'I know whom I have chosen').

verses.kjv_text: He spake of Judas Iscariot the son of Simon: for he it was that should betray him, being one of the twelve.
