verses.original_text: ἔλεγεν δὲ τὸν Ἰούδαν Σίμωνος Ἰσκαριώτην οὗτος γὰρ ἔμελλεν αὐτὸν παραδιδόναι εἷς ὢν ἐκ τῶν δώδεκα
verses.transliteration: elegen de ton Ioudan Simōnos Iskariōtēn houtos gar emellen auton paradidonai heis ōn ek tōn dōdeka
verses.study_note: John identifies the 'devil' as Judas Iscariot, son of Simon. 'Should betray him' (emellen auton paradidonai — was about to hand him over; mellō indicates inevitability). 'Being one of the twelve' (heis ōn ek tōn dōdeka — though he was one of the Twelve). The pathos is in the phrase: the betrayer was not an outsider but an insider, not an enemy but a chosen companion. This closing note casts a shadow over the entire chapter: even proximity to Jesus does not guarantee faith. External discipleship without internal transformation leads to betrayal.

meta.overview: John 7 takes place at the Feast of Tabernacles (Sukkot), six months after the events of chapter 6. The chapter is a study in division: the crowd is divided about Jesus, the authorities are divided over whether to arrest him, and even his own brothers do not believe in him. Jesus initially refuses to go up to the feast publicly — 'My time is not yet come' (v.6) — but then goes 'not openly, but as it were in secret' (v.10). Once there, he teaches in the temple and the people are amazed: 'How knoweth this man letters, having never learned?' (v.15). Jesus responds that his doctrine comes from the Father: 'My doctrine is not mine, but his that sent me' (v.16). He challenges his critics: 'Did not Moses give you the law, and yet none of you keepeth the law? Why go ye about to kill me?' (v.19). The crowd is sharply divided: 'Some said, He is a good man: others said, Nay; but he deceiveth the people' (v.12). Some recognise him as 'the Prophet' or 'the Christ,' while others object that 'the Christ cometh not out of Galilee' (vv.40-42). On the last great day of the feast, Jesus stands and cries with a loud voice: 'If any man thirst, let him come unto me, and drink. He that believeth on me, as the scripture hath said, out of his belly shall flow rivers of living water' (vv.37-38). John explains that this refers to the Holy Spirit, 'which they that believe on him should receive' (v.39). The Pharisees send officers to arrest Jesus, but the officers return empty-handed: 'Never man spake like this man' (v.46). Nicodemus, who had come to Jesus by night (ch.3), speaks cautiously in Jesus' defence: 'Doth our law judge any man, before it hear him?' (v.51). The chapter ends with the authorities' dismissive retort and everyone going home.
meta.theme: The Feast of Tabernacles — Division over the Christ
meta.keyWordMeaning: To divide, separate / a split, division, tear. John 7 is the chapter of schisma — the people are divided (v.12, 43), the authorities are divided (vv.45-52), and even Jesus' family is divided (vv.3-5). The feast of Tabernacles celebrated Israel's wilderness wandering (Leviticus 23:33-43) with water-pouring ceremonies and light illumination — both of which Jesus claims to fulfil: 'If any man thirst, let him come unto me' (7:37) and 'I am the light of the world' (8:12). Every person who encounters Jesus must take a position — neutrality is impossible.
outline: Jesus and His Brothers (vv.1-9): After these things Jesus walked in Galilee for he would not walk in Jewry because the Jews sought to kill him. Now the Jews' feast of tabernacles was at hand. His brethren therefore said unto him Depart hence and go into Judaea that thy disciples also may see the works that thou doest. For there is no man that doeth any thing in secret and he himself seeketh to be known openly. If thou do these things shew thyself to the world. For neither did his brethren believe in him. Then Jesus said unto them My time is not yet come but your time is alway ready. The world cannot hate you but me it hateth because I testify of it that the works thereof are evil. Go ye up unto this feast I go not up yet unto this feast for my time is not yet full come. When he had said these words he abode still in Galilee

Jesus at the Feast — Secret Arrival and Public Teaching (vv.10-24): But when his brethren were gone up then went he also up unto the feast not openly but as it were in secret. Then the Jews sought him at the feast and said Where is he? And there was much murmuring among the people concerning him for some said He is a good man others said Nay but he deceiveth the people. Howbeit no man spake openly of him for fear of the Jews. Now about the midst of the feast Jesus went up into the temple and taught. And the Jews marvelled saying How knoweth this man letters having never learned? Jesus answered them and said My doctrine is not mine but his that sent me. If any man will do his will he shall know of the doctrine whether it be of God or whether I speak of myself. He that speaketh of himself seeketh his own glory but he that seeketh his glory that sent him the same is true and no unrighteousness is in him. Did not Moses give you the law and yet none of you keepeth the law? Why go ye about to kill me? The people answered and said Thou hast a devil who goeth about to kill thee? Jesus answered and said unto them I have done one work and ye all marvel. Moses therefore gave unto you circumcision — not because it is of Moses but of the fathers — and ye on the sabbath day circumcise a man. If a man on the sabbath day receive circumcision that the law of Moses should not be broken are ye angry at me because I have made a man every whit whole on the sabbath day? Judge not according to the appearance but judge righteous judgment

Is This the Christ? Division Among the People (vv.25-36): Then said some of them of Jerusalem Is not this he whom they seek to kill? But lo he speaketh boldly and they say nothing unto him. Do the rulers know indeed that this is the very Christ? Howbeit we know this man whence he is but when Christ cometh no man knoweth whence he is. Then cried Jesus in the temple as he taught saying Ye both know me and ye know whence I am and I am not come of myself but he that sent me is true whom ye know not. But I know him for I am from him and he hath sent me. Then they sought to take him but no man laid hands on him because his hour was not yet come. And many of the people believed on him and said When Christ cometh will he do more miracles than these which this man hath done? The Pharisees heard that the people murmured such things concerning him and the Pharisees and the chief priests sent officers to take him. Then said Jesus Yet a little while am I with you and then I go unto him that sent me. Ye shall seek me and shall not find me and where I am thither ye cannot come. Then said the Jews among themselves Whither will he go that we shall not find him? will he go unto the dispersed among the Gentiles and teach the Gentiles? What manner of saying is this that he said Ye shall seek me and shall not find me and where I am thither ye cannot come?

Rivers of Living Water (vv.37-39): In the last day that great day of the feast Jesus stood and cried saying If any man thirst let him come unto me and drink. He that believeth on me as the scripture hath said out of his belly shall flow rivers of living water. But this spake he of the Spirit which they that believe on him should receive for the Holy Ghost was not yet given because that Jesus was not yet glorified

Division and Failed Arrest (vv.40-53): Many of the people therefore when they heard this saying said Of a truth this is the Prophet. Others said This is the Christ. But some said Shall Christ come out of Galilee? Hath not the scripture said That Christ cometh of the seed of David and out of the town of Bethlehem where David was? So there was a division among the people because of him. And some of them would have taken him but no man laid hands on him. Then came the officers to the chief priests and Pharisees and they said unto them Why have ye not brought him? The officers answered Never man spake like this man. Then answered them the Pharisees Are ye also deceived? Have any of the rulers or of the Pharisees believed on him? But this people who knoweth not the law are cursed. Nicodemus saith unto them — he that came to Jesus by night being one of them — Doth our law judge any man before it hear him and know what he doeth? They answered and said unto him Art thou also of Galilee? Search and look for out of Galilee ariseth no prophet. And every man went unto his own house

verses.kjv_text: After these things Jesus walked in Galilee: for he would not walk in Jewry, because the Jews sought to kill him.
verses.original_text: καὶ μετὰ ταῦτα περιεπάτει ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἐν τῇ Γαλιλαίᾳ οὐ γὰρ ἤθελεν ἐν τῇ Ἰουδαίᾳ περιπατεῖν ὅτι ἐζήτουν αὐτὸν οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι ἀποκτεῖναι
verses.transliteration: kai meta tauta periepatei ho Iēsous en tē Galilaia ou gar ēthelen en tē Ioudaia peripatein hoti ezētoun auton hoi Ioudaioi apokteinai
verses.study_note: 'After these things' — a gap of approximately six months between chapters 6 (near Passover, spring) and 7 (Feast of Tabernacles, autumn). 'Would not walk in Jewry' (ouk ēthelen en tē Ioudaia peripatein — he did not wish to travel in Judea). Not fear but strategic timing: Jesus controls his own schedule (cf. v.6, 8, 30). 'The Jews sought to kill him' — the plot from 5:18 continues.

verses.kjv_text: Now the Jews' feast of tabernacles was at hand.
verses.original_text: ἦν δὲ ἐγγὺς ἡ ἑορτὴ τῶν Ἰουδαίων ἡ σκηνοπηγία
verses.transliteration: ēn de engys hē heortē tōn Ioudaiōn hē skēnopēgia
verses.study_note: 'Feast of tabernacles' (hē skēnopēgia — the feast of tent-pitching, booth-building). Sukkot was one of three pilgrim feasts (Deuteronomy 16:16) celebrating the completed harvest and remembering Israel's 40-year wilderness wandering. It lasted seven days (Leviticus 23:34-36) with an eighth day of solemn assembly. Key ceremonies included water-pouring (from the Pool of Siloam onto the altar) and lighting of enormous candelabra in the Court of Women. Both ceremonies provide the backdrop for Jesus' claims in 7:37-38 and 8:12.

verses.kjv_text: His brethren therefore said unto him, Depart hence, and go into Judaea, that thy disciples also may see the works that thou doest.
verses.original_text: εἶπον οὖν πρὸς αὐτὸν οἱ ἀδελφοὶ αὐτοῦ Μετάβηθι ἐντεῦθεν καὶ ὕπαγε εἰς τὴν Ἰουδαίαν ἵνα καὶ οἱ μαθηταί σου θεωρήσωσιν τὰ ἔργα σου ἃ ποιεῖς
verses.transliteration: eipon oun pros auton hoi adelphoi autou Metabēthi enteuthen kai hypage eis tēn Ioudaian hina kai hoi mathētai sou theōrēsousin ta erga sou ha poieis
verses.study_note: 'His brethren' (hoi adelphoi autou — his brothers). These are Jesus' biological half-brothers: James, Joses, Simon, and Judas (Matthew 13:55). Their advice is worldly wisdom: go to Jerusalem where the crowds are, display your powers, build your platform. They think in terms of publicity and political strategy — the opposite of Jesus' approach.

verses.kjv_text: For there is no man that doeth any thing in secret, and he himself seeketh to be known openly. If thou do these things, shew thyself to the world.
verses.original_text: οὐδεὶς γὰρ ἐν κρυπτῷ τι ποιεῖ καὶ ζητεῖ αὐτὸς ἐν παρρησίᾳ εἶναι εἰ ταῦτα ποιεῖς φανέρωσον σεαυτὸν τῷ κόσμῳ
verses.transliteration: oudeis gar en kryptō ti poiei kai zētei autos en parrēsia einai ei tauta poieis phanerōson seauton tō kosmō
verses.study_note: 'In secret... known openly' (en kryptō... en parrēsia — in hiding vs. in public boldness). 'Show thyself to the world' (phanerōson seauton tō kosmō — manifest, reveal yourself to the world). The brothers' logic is worldly: if you have power, use it for maximum publicity. This echoes Satan's temptation (Matthew 4:5-6: throw yourself from the temple). Jesus will indeed manifest himself — but on the cross, not on a stage.

verses.kjv_text: For neither did his brethren believe in him.
verses.original_text: οὐδὲ γὰρ οἱ ἀδελφοὶ αὐτοῦ ἐπίστευον εἰς αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: oude gar hoi adelphoi autou episteuon eis auton
verses.study_note: 'Neither did his brethren believe in him' (oude hoi adelphoi autou episteuon eis auton — not even his brothers believed in him; imperfect tense: they were not believing, a sustained state of unbelief). This is remarkable: those who grew up with Jesus did not believe. Familiarity bred scepticism rather than faith. Their conversion came later — after the resurrection (Acts 1:14; 1 Corinthians 15:7). James became the leader of the Jerusalem church (Acts 15:13; Galatians 1:19) and Jude wrote the epistle.

verses.kjv_text: Then Jesus said unto them, My time is not yet come: but your time is alway ready.
verses.original_text: λέγει οὖν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ὁ καιρὸς ὁ ἐμὸς οὔπω πάρεστιν ὁ δὲ καιρὸς ὁ ὑμέτερος πάντοτέ ἐστιν ἕτοιμος
verses.transliteration: legei oun autois ho Iēsous Ho kairos ho emos oupō parestin ho de kairos ho hymeteros pantote estin hetoimos
verses.study_note: 'My time' (ho kairos ho emos — my appointed season, my opportune moment). 'Not yet come' (oupō parestin — has not yet arrived). Jesus operates on a divine timetable, not a human one. 'Your time is alway ready' (ho kairos ho hymeteros pantote estin hetoimos — your time is always ready) — for the unbelieving brothers, any moment is as good as any other because they have no divine commission with a set schedule. Jesus lives under the Father's sovereign timing; they live under their own.

verses.kjv_text: The world cannot hate you; but me it hateth, because I testify of it, that the works thereof are evil.
verses.original_text: οὐ δύναται ὁ κόσμος μισεῖν ὑμᾶς ἐμὲ δὲ μισεῖ ὅτι ἐγὼ μαρτυρῶ περὶ αὐτοῦ ὅτι τὰ ἔργα αὐτοῦ πονηρά ἐστιν
verses.transliteration: ou dynatai ho kosmos misein hymas eme de misei hoti egō martyrō peri autou hoti ta erga autou ponēra estin
verses.study_note: 'The world cannot hate you' — because the brothers belong to the world's value system, the world has no reason to oppose them. 'Me it hateth' (eme de misei — but me it hates). 'Because I testify of it, that the works thereof are evil' — Jesus' testimony exposes the world's evil, and the world hates the light that reveals its darkness (cf. 3:19-20). The world's hatred of Jesus is not random but moral: it is the reaction of evil exposed by good.

verses.kjv_text: Go ye up unto this feast: I go not up yet unto this feast; for my time is not yet full come.
verses.original_text: ὑμεῖς ἀνάβητε εἰς τὴν ἑορτὴν ταύτην ἐγὼ οὔπω ἀναβαίνω εἰς τὴν ἑορτὴν ταύτην ὅτι ὁ καιρὸς ὁ ἐμὸς οὔπω πεπλήρωται
verses.transliteration: hymeis anabēte eis tēn heortēn tautēn egō oupō anabainō eis tēn heortēn tautēn hoti ho kairos ho emos oupō peplērōtai
verses.study_note: 'I go not up yet' (egō oupō anabainō — I am not yet going up). The key word is 'yet' (oupō) — he will go, but not yet, and not publicly. 'My time is not yet full come' (oupō peplērōtai — has not yet been fulfilled). Jesus is not being deceptive but deliberate: he will attend the feast on his own terms, in the Father's timing, not as a public spectacle per his brothers' plan.

verses.kjv_text: When he had said these words unto them, he abode still in Galilee.
verses.original_text: ταῦτα δὲ εἰπὼν αὐτοῖς ἔμεινεν ἐν τῇ Γαλιλαίᾳ
verses.transliteration: tauta de eipōn autois emeinen en tē Galilaia
verses.study_note: Jesus remains in Galilee while his brothers depart. He waits — a pattern of divine restraint. The right action at the wrong time is the wrong action. Jesus waits for the Father's moment, then acts decisively.

verses.kjv_text: But when his brethren were gone up, then went he also up unto the feast, not openly, but as it were in secret.
verses.original_text: ὡς δὲ ἀνέβησαν οἱ ἀδελφοὶ αὐτοῦ τότε καὶ αὐτὸς ἀνέβη εἰς τὴν ἑορτήν οὐ φανερῶς ἀλλ᾽ ὡς ἐν κρυπτῷ
verses.transliteration: hōs de anebēsan hoi adelphoi autou tote kai autos anebē eis tēn heortēn ou phanerōs all hōs en kryptō
verses.study_note: 'Not openly, but as it were in secret' (ou phanerōs all hōs en kryptō — not publicly but as if in secret). Jesus goes to the feast privately, avoiding the public spectacle his brothers wanted. This is not stealth but strategic timing: he will teach publicly (v.14) but on his own schedule, not theirs. 'As it were' (hōs) — he was not truly hiding but simply not making a public entrance.

verses.kjv_text: Then the Jews sought him at the feast, and said, Where is he?
verses.original_text: οἱ οὖν Ἰουδαῖοι ἐζήτουν αὐτὸν ἐν τῇ ἑορτῇ καὶ ἔλεγον Ποῦ ἐστιν ἐκεῖνος
verses.transliteration: hoi oun Ioudaioi ezētoun auton en tē heortē kai elegon Pou estin ekeinos
verses.study_note: 'The Jews' (hoi Ioudaioi — the religious authorities, as John typically uses the term). 'Where is he?' (pou estin ekeinos — where is that one?). The use of 'that one' (ekeinos) without naming Jesus may express contempt or caution. Jesus has become the talk of the festival — his absence makes him more prominent, not less.

verses.kjv_text: And there was much murmuring among the people concerning him: for some said, He is a good man: others said, Nay; but he deceiveth the people.
verses.original_text: καὶ γογγυσμὸς πολὺς περὶ αὐτοῦ ἦν ἐν τοῖς ὄχλοις οἱ μὲν ἔλεγον ὅτι Ἀγαθός ἐστιν ἄλλοι δὲ ἔλεγον Οὔ ἀλλὰ πλανᾷ τὸν ὄχλον
verses.transliteration: kai gongysmos polys peri autou ēn en tois ochlois hoi men elegon hoti Agathos estin alloi de elegon Ou alla plana ton ochlon
verses.study_note: 'Much murmuring' (gongysmos polys — much grumbling, whispering debate). The crowd is divided into two camps: 'He is a good man' (agathos estin) vs. 'He deceiveth the people' (plana ton ochlon — he leads the crowd astray). The charge of being a 'deceiver' (planos) was later formalised in Jewish polemic against Jesus (cf. Matthew 27:63). There is no neutral position — Jesus forces a decision. C.S. Lewis's famous trilemma echoes this: Jesus is either Lord, liar, or lunatic.

verses.kjv_text: Howbeit no man spake openly of him for fear of the Jews.
verses.original_text: οὐδεὶς μέντοι παρρησίᾳ ἐλάλει περὶ αὐτοῦ διὰ τὸν φόβον τῶν Ἰουδαίων
verses.transliteration: oudeis mentoi parrēsia elalei peri autou dia ton phobon tōn Ioudaiōn
verses.study_note: 'No man spake openly' (oudeis parrēsia elalei — no one spoke with boldness/freedom). 'For fear of the Jews' (dia ton phobon tōn Ioudaiōn — because of the fear of the Jewish authorities). The religious leaders had created an atmosphere of intimidation. People whispered about Jesus but dared not speak publicly. This fear will intensify (cf. 9:22; 12:42; 19:38; 20:19). Fear of man silences truth — a recurring biblical theme (Proverbs 29:25).

verses.kjv_text: Now about the midst of the feast Jesus went up into the temple, and taught.
verses.original_text: ἤδη δὲ τῆς ἑορτῆς μεσούσης ἀνέβη ὁ Ἰησοῦς εἰς τὸ ἱερόν καὶ ἐδίδασκεν
verses.transliteration: ēdē de tēs heortēs mesousēs anebē ho Iēsous eis to hieron kai edidasken
verses.study_note: 'About the midst of the feast' (tēs heortēs mesousēs — the feast being at its midpoint, around day 3 or 4 of the 7-day festival). Jesus now appears publicly — not at the beginning (when everyone expected) but in the middle (when he chooses). He teaches in the temple (to hieron — the temple complex, likely in one of the porticoes or courts). His teaching provokes the astonishment of v.15.

verses.kjv_text: And the Jews marvelled, saying, How knoweth this man letters, having never learned?
verses.original_text: ἐθαύμαζον οὖν οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι λέγοντες Πῶς οὗτος γράμματα οἶδεν μὴ μεμαθηκώς
verses.transliteration: ethaumazon oun hoi Ioudaioi legontes Pōs houtos grammata oiden mē memathēkōs
verses.study_note: 'How knoweth this man letters?' (pōs houtos grammata oiden — how does this one know writings/learning?). 'Having never learned' (mē memathēkōs — not having been a student; he never studied under a recognised rabbi). Jesus had no formal rabbinic education — he was not a disciple of Gamaliel, Hillel, or Shammai. Yet his scriptural knowledge and teaching ability surpassed the trained scholars. The source of his learning is explained in v.16.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered them, and said, My doctrine is not mine, but his that sent me.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη οὖν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ εἶπεν Ἡ ἐμὴ διδαχὴ οὐκ ἔστιν ἐμὴ ἀλλὰ τοῦ πέμψαντός με
verses.transliteration: apekrithē oun autois ho Iēsous kai eipen Hē emē didachē ouk estin emē alla tou pempsantos me
verses.study_note: 'My doctrine is not mine' (hē emē didachē ouk estin emē — my teaching is not my own). 'But his that sent me' (alla tou pempsantos me — but belongs to the one who sent me). Jesus' teaching comes directly from the Father — not from rabbinic training. This is both a claim to divine authority (his teaching is God's teaching) and a model of humility (he does not speak independently). The pattern is consistent with 5:19: 'The Son can do nothing of himself.'

verses.kjv_text: If any man will do his will, he shall know of the doctrine, whether it be of God, or whether I speak of myself.
verses.original_text: ἐάν τις θέλῃ τὸ θέλημα αὐτοῦ ποιεῖν γνώσεται περὶ τῆς διδαχῆς πότερον ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ ἐστιν ἢ ἐγὼ ἀπ᾽ ἐμαυτοῦ λαλῶ
verses.transliteration: ean tis thelē to thelēma autou poiein gnōsetai peri tēs didachēs poteron ek tou theou estin ē egō ap emautou lalō
verses.study_note: 'If any man will do his will' (ean tis thelē to thelēma autou poiein — if anyone desires/wills to do God's will). 'He shall know of the doctrine' (gnōsetai peri tēs didachēs — he will come to know about the teaching). The test of divine doctrine is not intellectual analysis but moral obedience. The person who sincerely desires to do God's will receives spiritual discernment to recognise God's truth. Unbelief is not primarily an intellectual problem but a moral one — those who resist God's will cannot perceive God's truth.

verses.kjv_text: He that speaketh of himself seeketh his own glory: but he that seeketh his glory that sent him, the same is true, and no unrighteousness is in him.
verses.original_text: ὁ ἀφ᾽ ἑαυτοῦ λαλῶν τὴν δόξαν τὴν ἰδίαν ζητεῖ ὁ δὲ ζητῶν τὴν δόξαν τοῦ πέμψαντος αὐτόν οὗτος ἀληθής ἐστιν καὶ ἀδικία ἐν αὐτῷ οὐκ ἔστιν
verses.transliteration: ho aph heautou lalōn tēn doxan tēn idian zētei ho de zētōn tēn doxan tou pempsantos auton houtos alēthēs estin kai adikia en autō ouk estin
verses.study_note: A principle for evaluating teachers: self-promoters seek their own glory (tēn doxan tēn idian) and are unreliable. The one who seeks the glory of God who sent him is true (alēthēs) and without unrighteousness (adikia ouk estin). Jesus applies this to himself: since he seeks the Father's glory, not his own, his testimony is trustworthy. This criterion also applies to all ministers of the gospel — the test of authenticity is whether they seek God's glory or their own.

verses.kjv_text: Did not Moses give you the law, and yet none of you keepeth the law? Why go ye about to kill me?
verses.original_text: οὐ Μωϋσῆς δέδωκεν ὑμῖν τὸν νόμον καὶ οὐδεὶς ἐξ ὑμῶν ποιεῖ τὸν νόμον τί με ζητεῖτε ἀποκτεῖναι
verses.transliteration: ou Mōysēs dedōken hymin ton nomon kai oudeis ex hymōn poiei ton nomon ti me zēteite apokteinai
verses.study_note: Jesus turns the tables: they accuse him of breaking the Sabbath law, but none of them keeps the entire law. 'Why go ye about to kill me?' (ti me zēteite apokteinai — why are you seeking to kill me?). Murder is a far greater violation of the law than Sabbath healing. Their zeal for law-keeping is selective and hypocritical — they enforce minor points while planning to violate the sixth commandment.

verses.kjv_text: The people answered and said, Thou hast a devil: who goeth about to kill thee?
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη ὁ ὄχλος καὶ εἶπεν Δαιμόνιον ἔχεις τίς σε ζητεῖ ἀποκτεῖναι
verses.transliteration: apekrithē ho ochlos kai eipen Daimonion echeis tis se zētei apokteinai
verses.study_note: 'Thou hast a devil' (daimonion echeis — you have a demon; meaning: you are mad, deranged). The crowd (ochlos, not the Jewish leaders) is unaware of the plot against Jesus' life. Their denial ('Who goeth about to kill thee?') is innocent but ironic — the leaders are indeed planning murder (5:18; 7:1, 25). The charge of demon-possession will recur (8:48, 52; 10:20).

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered and said unto them, I have done one work, and ye all marvel.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη Ἰησοῦς καὶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς Ἓν ἔργον ἐποίησα καὶ πάντες θαυμάζετε
verses.transliteration: apekrithē Iēsous kai eipen autois Hen ergon epoiēsa kai pantes thaumazete
verses.study_note: 'One work' (hen ergon — one deed). This refers back to the healing at Bethesda (5:1-16) — the single act that triggered the murder plot. Despite the many miracles Jesus has performed, this one work on the Sabbath has consumed them. They marvel (thaumazete — are amazed, disturbed) at it still, months later.

verses.kjv_text: Moses therefore gave unto you circumcision; (not because it is of Moses, but of the fathers;) and ye on the sabbath day circumcise a man.
verses.original_text: διὰ τοῦτο Μωϋσῆς δέδωκεν ὑμῖν τὴν περιτομήν οὐχ ὅτι ἐκ τοῦ Μωϋσέως ἐστὶν ἀλλ᾽ ἐκ τῶν πατέρων καὶ ἐν σαββάτῳ περιτέμνετε ἄνθρωπον
verses.transliteration: dia touto Mōysēs dedōken hymin tēn peritomēn ouch hoti ek tou Mōyseōs estin all ek tōn paterōn kai en sabbatō peritemnete anthrōpon
verses.study_note: Jesus uses a rabbinic argument from the lesser to the greater (qal wahomer). Circumcision originated with the patriarchs (Abraham, Genesis 17:10-12) and was incorporated into the Mosaic law (Leviticus 12:3). When the eighth day falls on the Sabbath, circumcision is still performed — the Jews themselves override the Sabbath rest for one part of one body. The argument is irrefutable within their own legal framework.

verses.kjv_text: If a man on the sabbath day receive circumcision, that the law of Moses should not be broken; are ye angry at me, because I have made a man every whit whole on the sabbath day?
verses.original_text: εἰ περιτομὴν λαμβάνει ἄνθρωπος ἐν σαββάτῳ ἵνα μὴ λυθῇ ὁ νόμος Μωϋσέως ἐμοὶ χολᾶτε ὅτι ὅλον ἄνθρωπον ὑγιῆ ἐποίησα ἐν σαββάτῳ
verses.transliteration: ei peritomēn lambanei anthrōpos en sabbatō hina mē lythē ho nomos Mōyseōs emoi cholate hoti holon anthrōpon hygiē epoiēsa en sabbatō
verses.study_note: 'Every whit whole' (holon anthrōpon hygiē — a whole man well; the entire person made healthy). The argument: if you circumcise one member on the Sabbath to keep the law, why are you angry that I made an entire man whole? Circumcision perfects one part; healing perfects the whole. If the lesser act is permitted, the greater act should certainly be permitted. 'Are ye angry at me?' (emoi cholate — are you bitter toward me? From cholaō: to be full of bile, to be furious).

verses.kjv_text: Judge not according to the appearance, but judge righteous judgment.
verses.original_text: μὴ κρίνετε κατ᾽ ὄψιν ἀλλὰ τὴν δικαίαν κρίσιν κρίνατε
verses.transliteration: mē krinete kat opsin alla tēn dikaian krisin krinate
verses.study_note: 'Judge not according to the appearance' (mē krinete kat opsin — do not judge by surface, by what meets the eye). 'Judge righteous judgment' (tēn dikaian krisin krinate — judge with just/fair judgment). Jesus does not prohibit all judging but demands righteous judging. They judged superficially: a man carried his bed on the Sabbath — guilty! But righteous judgment considers the whole picture: a man was healed after 38 years of suffering. Appearance says 'Sabbath violation'; righteousness says 'God is at work.'

verses.kjv_text: Then said some of them of Jerusalem, Is not this he whom they seek to kill?
verses.original_text: ἔλεγον οὖν τινες ἐκ τῶν Ἱεροσολυμιτῶν Οὐχ οὗτός ἐστιν ὃν ζητοῦσιν ἀποκτεῖναι
verses.transliteration: elegon oun tines ek tōn Hierosolymitōn Ouch houtos estin hon zētousin apokteinai
verses.study_note: The Jerusalem residents (Hierosolymitōn — distinguished from the pilgrim crowds in v.20) know about the murder plot. Their question implies: this is indeed the man the authorities want to kill. Their awareness vindicates Jesus' statement in v.19.

verses.kjv_text: But, lo, he speaketh boldly, and they say nothing unto him. Do the rulers know indeed that this is the very Christ?
verses.original_text: καὶ ἴδε παρρησίᾳ λαλεῖ καὶ οὐδὲν αὐτῷ λέγουσιν μήποτε ἀληθῶς ἔγνωσαν οἱ ἄρχοντες ὅτι οὗτός ἐστιν ἀληθῶς ὁ Χριστός
verses.transliteration: kai ide parrēsia lalei kai ouden autō legousin mēpote alēthōs egnōsan hoi archontes hoti houtos estin alēthōs ho Christos
verses.study_note: 'He speaketh boldly' (parrēsia lalei — with freedom, openly, without restraint). The crowd reasons: if the authorities want him dead, why do they let him speak publicly? Perhaps they have secretly concluded he is the Christ? The question reveals growing messianic expectation — the people are open to the possibility, even if the leaders are not.

verses.kjv_text: Howbeit we know this man whence he is: but when Christ cometh, no man knoweth whence he is.
verses.original_text: ἀλλὰ τοῦτον οἴδαμεν πόθεν ἐστίν ὁ δὲ Χριστὸς ὅταν ἔρχηται οὐδεὶς γινώσκει πόθεν ἐστίν
verses.transliteration: alla touton oidamen pothen estin ho de Christos hotan erchētai oudeis ginōskei pothen estin
verses.study_note: 'We know this man whence he is' — they know Jesus is from Nazareth in Galilee. 'When Christ cometh, no man knoweth whence he is' — a popular Jewish belief that the Messiah would appear suddenly, mysteriously (cf. Malachi 3:1; the 'hidden Messiah' tradition). Their reasoning: we know where Jesus comes from, therefore he cannot be the Messiah. The irony is devastating: they think they know his origin (Nazareth) but they do not know his true origin (heaven, from the Father).

verses.kjv_text: Then cried Jesus in the temple as he taught, saying, Ye both know me, and ye know whence I am: and I am not come of myself, but he that sent me is true, whom ye know not.
verses.original_text: ἔκραξεν οὖν ἐν τῷ ἱερῷ διδάσκων ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ λέγων Κἀμὲ οἴδατε καὶ οἴδατε πόθεν εἰμί καὶ ἀπ᾽ ἐμαυτοῦ οὐκ ἐλήλυθα ἀλλ᾽ ἔστιν ἀληθινὸς ὁ πέμψας με ὃν ὑμεῖς οὐκ οἴδατε
verses.transliteration: ekraxen oun en tō hierō didaskōn ho Iēsous kai legōn Kame oidate kai oidate pothen eimi kai ap emautou ouk elēlytha all estin alēthinos ho pempsas me hon hymeis ouk oidate
verses.study_note: 'Cried' (ekraxen — shouted, cried out; a loud, public declaration). 'Ye both know me, and ye know whence I am' — this may be ironic (you think you know but you do not), or concessive (yes, you know my earthly origin, but there is more). 'I am not come of myself' (ap emautou ouk elēlytha — I have not come on my own initiative). 'He that sent me is true' (estin alēthinos ho pempsas me — the one who sent me is genuine, real). 'Whom ye know not' (hon hymeis ouk oidate — whom you do not know). The devastating charge: they claim to know God but do not know the one who sent Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: But I know him: for I am from him, and he hath sent me.
verses.original_text: ἐγὼ δὲ οἶδα αὐτόν ὅτι παρ᾽ αὐτοῦ εἰμι κἀκεῖνός με ἀπέστειλεν
verses.transliteration: egō de oida auton hoti par autou eimi kakeinos me apesteilen
verses.study_note: 'I know him' (egō oida auton — I, I know him; emphatic pronoun). 'I am from him' (par autou eimi — I am from his presence, from his side). 'He hath sent me' (kakeinos me apesteilen — and that one sent me). Two claims: (1) Jesus originates from the Father — pre-existence. (2) The Father has commissioned/sent him — divine mission. Together they assert unique intimacy with and authority from God.

verses.kjv_text: Then they sought to take him: but no man laid hands on him, because his hour was not yet come.
verses.original_text: ἐζήτουν οὖν αὐτὸν πιάσαι καὶ οὐδεὶς ἐπέβαλεν ἐπ᾽ αὐτὸν τὴν χεῖρα ὅτι οὔπω ἐληλύθει ἡ ὥρα αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: ezētoun oun auton piasai kai oudeis epebalen ep auton tēn cheira hoti oupō elēlythei hē hōra autou
verses.study_note: 'No man laid hands on him' (oudeis epebalen tēn cheira — no one put a hand on him). 'His hour was not yet come' (oupō elēlythei hē hōra autou — his hour had not yet arrived). John's 'hour' (hōra) refers to the appointed time of Jesus' death and glorification (cf. 2:4; 7:30; 8:20; 12:23, 27; 13:1; 17:1). No human scheme can accelerate or prevent God's timetable. Jesus is untouchable until the Father's hour arrives.

verses.kjv_text: And many of the people believed on him, and said, When Christ cometh, will he do more miracles than these which this man hath done?
verses.original_text: πολλοὶ δὲ ἐκ τοῦ ὄχλου ἐπίστευσαν εἰς αὐτὸν καὶ ἔλεγον Ὁ Χριστὸς ὅταν ἔλθῃ μήτι πλείονα σημεῖα τούτων ποιήσει ὧν οὗτος ἐποίησεν
verses.transliteration: polloi de ek tou ochlou episteusan eis auton kai elegon Ho Christos hotan elthē mēti pleiona sēmeia toutōn poiēsei hōn houtos epoiēsen
verses.study_note: 'Many of the people believed' (polloi episteusan — many believed). Their reasoning: even when the Messiah comes, can he surpass Jesus' signs? The implied answer is no — therefore Jesus must be the Messiah. This is sign-based faith, better than rejection but not yet mature faith (cf. 2:23-25). The question with mēti expects a negative answer: 'Surely the Christ won't do more signs than this man?'

verses.kjv_text: The Pharisees heard that the people murmured such things concerning him; and the Pharisees and the chief priests sent officers to take him.
verses.original_text: ἤκουσαν οἱ Φαρισαῖοι τοῦ ὄχλου γογγύζοντος περὶ αὐτοῦ ταῦτα καὶ ἀπέστειλαν οἱ Φαρισαῖοι καὶ οἱ ἀρχιερεῖς ὑπηρέτας ἵνα πιάσωσιν αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: ēkousan hoi Pharisaioi tou ochlou gongyzontos peri autou tauta kai apesteilan hoi Pharisaioi kai hoi archiereis hypēretas hina piasōsin auton
verses.study_note: 'The Pharisees and the chief priests' — a rare joint action between Pharisees and Sadducean chief priests who normally disagreed. Opposition to Jesus unites theological enemies (cf. Luke 23:12: Herod and Pilate became friends). 'Sent officers' (apesteilan hypēretas — dispatched temple police/attendants; hypēretēs: an under-rower, a subordinate servant). The officers are sent to arrest Jesus but will return empty-handed (v.45-46).

verses.kjv_text: Then said Jesus unto them, Yet a little while am I with you, and then I go unto him that sent me.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν οὖν ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ἔτι μικρὸν χρόνον μεθ᾽ ὑμῶν εἰμι καὶ ὑπάγω πρὸς τὸν πέμψαντά με
verses.transliteration: eipen oun ho Iēsous Eti mikron chronon meth hymōn eimi kai hypagō pros ton pempsanta me
verses.study_note: 'Yet a little while' (eti mikron chronon — still a small time). 'I go unto him that sent me' — referring to his death, resurrection, and ascension (the 'going to the Father,' cf. 13:1; 14:28; 16:5, 28). Jesus speaks of his death not as defeat but as departure — a going home to the Father. The brevity of his remaining time adds urgency.

verses.kjv_text: Ye shall seek me, and shall not find me: and where I am, thither ye cannot come.
verses.original_text: ζητήσετέ με καὶ οὐχ εὑρήσετέ με καὶ ὅπου εἰμὶ ἐγὼ ὑμεῖς οὐ δύνασθε ἐλθεῖν
verses.transliteration: zētēsete me kai ouch heurēsete me kai hopou eimi egō hymeis ou dynasthe elthein
verses.study_note: 'Ye shall seek me, and shall not find me' — this is a warning, not a promise. After Jesus departs, those who rejected him will seek him in vain. 'Where I am, thither ye cannot come' (hopou eimi egō hymeis ou dynasthe elthein — where I am you cannot come). Jesus is going to the Father; unbelievers cannot follow. The saying is enigmatic to the audience (v.35-36) but clear to John's readers: Jesus speaks of his exaltation to the Father's right hand.

verses.kjv_text: Then said the Jews among themselves, Whither will he go, that we shall not find him? will he go unto the dispersed among the Gentiles, and teach the Gentiles?
verses.original_text: εἶπον οὖν οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι πρὸς ἑαυτούς Ποῦ οὗτος μέλλει πορεύεσθαι ὅτι ἡμεῖς οὐχ εὑρήσομεν αὐτόν μὴ εἰς τὴν διασπορὰν τῶν Ἑλλήνων μέλλει πορεύεσθαι καὶ διδάσκειν τοὺς Ἕλληνας
verses.transliteration: eipon oun hoi Ioudaioi pros heautous Pou houtos mellei poreuesthai hoti hēmeis ouch heurēsomen auton mē eis tēn diasporan tōn Hellēnōn mellei poreuesthai kai didaskein tous Hellēnas
verses.study_note: 'The dispersed among the Gentiles' (tēn diasporan tōn Hellēnōn — the Diaspora among the Greeks; Jewish communities scattered throughout the Greco-Roman world). They wonder if Jesus will leave Palestine to teach among Diaspora Jews and Greeks. The suggestion is dismissive but ironically prophetic: through his apostles and the church, Jesus' teaching will indeed reach the Gentile world (cf. 12:20-23 where Greeks come seeking Jesus).

verses.kjv_text: What manner of saying is this that he said, Ye shall seek me, and shall not find me: and where I am, thither ye cannot come?
verses.original_text: τίς ἐστιν οὗτος ὁ λόγος ὃν εἶπεν Ζητήσετέ με καὶ οὐχ εὑρήσετέ με καὶ ὅπου εἰμὶ ἐγὼ ὑμεῖς οὐ δύνασθε ἐλθεῖν
verses.transliteration: tis estin houtos ho logos hon eipen Zētēsete me kai ouch heurēsete me kai hopou eimi egō hymeis ou dynasthe elthein
verses.study_note: They are baffled by Jesus' enigmatic saying. Their confusion is part of John's pattern: Jesus speaks on a heavenly plane; his listeners interpret on an earthly plane. The saying will be repeated in 8:21 with greater urgency: 'Ye shall seek me, and shall die in your sins.'

verses.kjv_text: In the last day, that great day of the feast, Jesus stood and cried, saying, If any man thirst, let him come unto me, and drink.
verses.original_text: ἐν δὲ τῇ ἐσχάτῃ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ μεγάλῃ τῆς ἑορτῆς εἱστήκει ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ ἔκραξεν λέγων Ἐάν τις διψᾷ ἐρχέσθω πρός με καὶ πινέτω
verses.transliteration: en de tē eschatē hēmera tē megalē tēs heortēs heistēkei ho Iēsous kai ekraxen legōn Ean tis dipsa erchesthō pros me kai pinetō
verses.study_note: 'The last day, that great day' (tē eschatē hēmera tē megalē — the last day, the great one). This is either the seventh day (the climax of the water-pouring ceremony) or the eighth day (the solemn assembly, Leviticus 23:36). During Tabernacles, a priest drew water from the Pool of Siloam in a golden pitcher and poured it on the altar while the people sang Isaiah 12:3: 'With joy shall ye draw water out of the wells of salvation.' At this precise moment, Jesus stands (heistēkei — stood up, rose to his feet; a dramatic gesture) and cries out with a loud voice: 'If any man thirst, let him come unto me, and drink.' He claims to be the fulfilment of everything the water ceremony symbolised.
verses.doctrinal_note: This is one of the great christological claims of the Gospel. The Feast of Tabernacles commemorated God's provision of water from the rock in the wilderness (Exodus 17:6; Numbers 20:11). The water-pouring ceremony expressed Israel's dependence on God for rain and life. Into this liturgical moment, Jesus inserts himself as the ultimate source: not the rock, not the ceremony, not the pool — but 'come unto me, and drink.' Paul identifies the rock as Christ (1 Corinthians 10:4). Isaiah promised rivers in the desert (Isaiah 35:6-7; 43:19-20; 44:3). Jesus claims to fulfil all these promises in his own person. The invitation is universal ('any man'), the condition is need ('thirst'), and the response is personal ('come unto me').

verses.kjv_text: He that believeth on me, as the scripture hath said, out of his belly shall flow rivers of living water.
verses.original_text: ὁ πιστεύων εἰς ἐμέ καθὼς εἶπεν ἡ γραφή ποταμοὶ ἐκ τῆς κοιλίας αὐτοῦ ῥεύσουσιν ὕδατος ζῶντος
verses.transliteration: ho pisteuōn eis eme kathōs eipen hē graphē potamoi ek tēs koilias autou rheusousin hydatos zōntos
verses.study_note: 'Rivers of living water' (potamoi hydatos zōntos — rivers of water that is alive, flowing, fresh). 'Out of his belly' (ek tēs koilias autou — from his innermost being). The 'scripture' Jesus cites may be Isaiah 58:11, Ezekiel 47:1-12, Zechariah 14:8, or a combination. The promise is extraordinary: the believer not only receives living water but becomes a source — rivers flowing outward to others. The one who drinks from Christ overflows to bless the world.

verses.kjv_text: (But this spake he of the Spirit, which they that believe on him should receive: for the Holy Ghost was not yet given; because that Jesus was not yet glorified.)
verses.original_text: τοῦτο δὲ εἶπεν περὶ τοῦ πνεύματος οὗ ἔμελλον λαμβάνειν οἱ πιστεύοντες εἰς αὐτόν οὔπω γὰρ ἦν πνεῦμα ἅγιον ὅτι Ἰησοῦς οὐδέπω ἐδοξάσθη
verses.transliteration: touto de eipen peri tou pneumatos hou emellon lambanein hoi pisteuontes eis auton oupō gar ēn pneuma hagion hoti Iēsous oudepō edoxasthē
verses.study_note: John's editorial comment provides the definitive interpretation: the 'living water' is the Holy Spirit. 'The Holy Ghost was not yet given' (oupō ēn pneuma hagion — the Spirit was not yet, meaning not yet poured out in fullness). The Spirit existed and was active in the OT, but the fullness of the Spirit's indwelling ministry awaited Jesus' glorification (his death, resurrection, and ascension). Pentecost (Acts 2) fulfils this promise. The sequence is: cross → resurrection → ascension → Spirit's outpouring.
verses.doctrinal_note: The connection between Jesus' glorification and the Spirit's coming is fundamental to NT theology. The Spirit could not be given in fullness until Jesus completed his atoning work: 'It is expedient for you that I go away: for if I go not away, the Comforter will not come unto you; but if I depart, I will send him unto you' (John 16:7). The Spirit's ministry is to apply the benefits of Christ's finished work to believers. The 'rivers of living water' are the Spirit producing the fruit of the Spirit (Galatians 5:22-23) and empowering witness (Acts 1:8) in and through believers.

verses.kjv_text: Many of the people therefore, when they heard this saying, said, Of a truth this is the Prophet.
verses.original_text: πολλοὶ οὖν ἐκ τοῦ ὄχλου ἀκούσαντες τὸν λόγον ἔλεγον Οὗτός ἐστιν ἀληθῶς ὁ προφήτης
verses.transliteration: polloi oun ek tou ochlou akousantes ton logon elegon Houtos estin alēthōs ho prophētēs
verses.study_note: 'The Prophet' (ho prophētēs — the definite article: the Prophet, the specific one foretold by Moses in Deuteronomy 18:15). Some identify Jesus as this Prophet — a correct identification, but incomplete if it excludes his identity as the Christ. In first-century Judaism, 'the Prophet' and 'the Christ' were sometimes distinguished as separate figures (cf. 1:20-21).

verses.kjv_text: Others said, This is the Christ. But some said, Shall Christ come out of Galilee?
verses.original_text: ἄλλοι ἔλεγον Οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ Χριστός ἄλλοι δὲ ἔλεγον Μὴ γὰρ ἐκ τῆς Γαλιλαίας ὁ Χριστὸς ἔρχεται
verses.transliteration: alloi elegon Houtos estin ho Christos alloi de elegon Mē gar ek tēs Galilaias ho Christos erchetai
verses.study_note: 'This is the Christ' — others go further, identifying Jesus as the Messiah. But objectors ask: 'Shall Christ come out of Galilee?' The question assumes a negative answer: the Messiah cannot be Galilean. The irony is layered: (1) Jesus was born in Bethlehem (Matthew 2:1; Luke 2:4-7), fulfilling the Micah 5:2 prophecy. (2) The crowd does not know this — their objection is based on incomplete information. (3) John does not correct them directly, trusting his readers to see the irony.

verses.kjv_text: Hath not the scripture said, That Christ cometh of the seed of David, and out of the town of Bethlehem, where David was?
verses.original_text: οὐχὶ ἡ γραφὴ εἶπεν ὅτι ἐκ τοῦ σπέρματος Δαυὶδ καὶ ἀπὸ Βηθλέεμ τῆς κώμης ὅπου ἦν Δαυὶδ ὁ Χριστὸς ἔρχεται
verses.transliteration: ouchi hē graphē eipen hoti ek tou spermatos Dauid kai apo Bēthleem tēs kōmēs hopou ēn Dauid ho Christos erchetai
verses.study_note: The crowd correctly cites Scripture: the Messiah comes from David's line (2 Samuel 7:12; Isaiah 11:1) and from Bethlehem (Micah 5:2). Their theology is correct; their application is wrong. They assume Jesus does not meet these criteria because they know him only as 'Jesus of Nazareth.' John's irony is profound: the very Scriptures they cite to dismiss Jesus actually confirm him — he is of David's line and was born in Bethlehem.

verses.kjv_text: So there was a division among the people because of him.
verses.original_text: σχίσμα οὖν ἐν τῷ ὄχλῳ ἐγένετο δι᾽ αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: schisma oun en tō ochlō egeneto di auton
verses.study_note: 'Division' (schisma — a split, tear, division; from schizō: to tear, split). Jesus causes division wherever he goes (cf. 9:16; 10:19; Matthew 10:34-36: 'I came not to send peace, but a sword'). This is not a failure of his ministry but its inevitable result: light divides from darkness, truth divides from error. Neutrality toward Christ is impossible.

verses.kjv_text: And some of them would have taken him; but no man laid hands on him.
verses.original_text: τινὲς δὲ ἤθελον ἐξ αὐτῶν πιάσαι αὐτόν ἀλλ᾽ οὐδεὶς ἐπέβαλεν ἐπ᾽ αὐτὸν τὰς χεῖρας
verses.transliteration: tines de ēthelon ex autōn piasai auton all oudeis epebalen ep auton tas cheiras
verses.study_note: Again, the attempt to seize Jesus fails (cf. v.30). Human hands cannot touch him until the Father's appointed hour (cf. 10:39; 18:4-6). Jesus is sovereign even over his own arrest — he cannot be taken, only given (10:17-18).

verses.kjv_text: Then came the officers to the chief priests and Pharisees; and they said unto them, Why have ye not brought him?
verses.original_text: ἦλθον οὖν οἱ ὑπηρέται πρὸς τοὺς ἀρχιερεῖς καὶ Φαρισαίους καὶ εἶπον αὐτοῖς ἐκεῖνοι Διατί οὐκ ἠγάγετε αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: ēlthon oun hoi hypēretai pros tous archiereis kai Pharisaious kai eipon autois ekeinoi Diati ouk ēgagete auton
verses.study_note: The temple officers return to the Sanhedrin without Jesus. 'Why have ye not brought him?' (diati ouk ēgagete auton — why did you not bring him?). They were sent to arrest him (v.32) but failed — or rather, refused. Their explanation in v.46 is remarkable.

verses.kjv_text: The officers answered, Never man spake like this man.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθησαν οἱ ὑπηρέται Οὐδέποτε οὕτως ἐλάλησεν ἄνθρωπος ὡς οὗτος ὁ ἄνθρωπος
verses.transliteration: apekrithēsan hoi hypēretai Oudepote houtōs elalēsen anthrōpos hōs houtos ho anthrōpos
verses.study_note: 'Never man spake like this man' (oudepote houtōs elalēsen anthrōpos — never has a man spoken like this). The temple police — trained, professional, accustomed to religious discourse — were so overcome by Jesus' teaching that they could not arrest him. They do not say 'He escaped' or 'We could not find him' — they confess they were disarmed by his words. This is an involuntary testimony from hostile witnesses: Jesus' speech is without parallel in human history.

verses.kjv_text: Then answered them the Pharisees, Are ye also deceived?
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθησαν οὖν αὐτοῖς οἱ Φαρισαῖοι Μὴ καὶ ὑμεῖς πεπλάνησθε
verses.transliteration: apekrithēsan oun autois hoi Pharisaioi Mē kai hymeis peplanēsthe
verses.study_note: 'Are ye also deceived?' (mē kai hymeis peplanēsthe — surely you also have not been led astray? Perfect passive: have you been deceived and remain so?). The Pharisees respond with contempt and social pressure: you too? Even you, our own officers? Their argument is not exegetical but sociological: the right people don't believe in Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: Have any of the rulers or of the Pharisees believed on him?
verses.original_text: μή τις ἐκ τῶν ἀρχόντων ἐπίστευσεν εἰς αὐτὸν ἢ ἐκ τῶν Φαρισαίων
verses.transliteration: mē tis ek tōn archontōn episteusen eis auton ē ek tōn Pharisaiōn
verses.study_note: 'Have any of the rulers or Pharisees believed?' The implied answer is 'no' — and the conclusion is: therefore he is not the Messiah. Their criterion for truth is peer approval, not evidence. This is the exact error Jesus identified in 5:44: 'How can ye believe, which receive honour one of another?' The irony: at least one ruler has come to Jesus — Nicodemus, standing right there (v.50). And later, Joseph of Arimathea will confess faith (19:38).

verses.kjv_text: But this people who knoweth not the law are cursed.
verses.original_text: ἀλλ᾽ ὁ ὄχλος οὗτος ὁ μὴ γινώσκων τὸν νόμον ἐπικατάρατοί εἰσιν
verses.transliteration: all ho ochlos houtos ho mē ginōskōn ton nomon epikatáratoi eisin
verses.study_note: 'This people who knoweth not the law are cursed' — the Pharisees dismiss the common people as 'am ha-aretz' (people of the land) — ignorant, unlearned in Torah, and therefore cursed. This is religious elitism at its worst: the unlearned cannot discern truth, so their opinions are worthless. The irony is that the 'unlearned' people correctly discern Jesus while the 'learned' Pharisees reject him. God reveals truth to 'babes' and hides it from the 'wise' (Matthew 11:25).

verses.kjv_text: Nicodemus saith unto them, (he that came to Jesus by night, being one of them,)
verses.original_text: λέγει Νικόδημος πρὸς αὐτούς ὁ ἐλθὼν νυκτὸς πρὸς αὐτὸν εἷς ὢν ἐξ αὐτῶν
verses.transliteration: legei Nikodēmos pros autous ho elthōn nyktos pros auton heis ōn ex autōn
verses.study_note: Nicodemus reappears — 'he that came to Jesus by night' (ho elthōn nyktos, cf. 3:1-2), 'being one of them' (heis ōn ex autōn — one of the Pharisees/Sanhedrin). He does not openly defend Jesus but raises a procedural objection — a cautious step. His faith is growing but still timid (he will appear again at the burial, 19:39, openly identifying with Jesus). Nicodemus represents the journey from secret belief to public confession.

verses.kjv_text: Doth our law judge any man, before it hear him, and know what he doeth?
verses.original_text: Μὴ ὁ νόμος ἡμῶν κρίνει τὸν ἄνθρωπον ἐὰν μὴ ἀκούσῃ παρ᾽ αὐτοῦ πρότερον καὶ γνῷ τί ποιεῖ
verses.transliteration: Mē ho nomos hēmōn krinei ton anthrōpon ean mē akousē par autou proteron kai gnō ti poiei
verses.study_note: 'Doth our law judge any man before it hear him?' — Nicodemus appeals to legal procedure: Deuteronomy 1:16-17 and 17:2-6 require hearing a case before rendering judgment. The Pharisees are condemning Jesus without a proper trial. Nicodemus does not declare faith but insists on fairness — a principled defence that exposes their hypocrisy. They are violating the very law they claim Jesus is breaking.

verses.kjv_text: They answered and said unto him, Art thou also of Galilee? Search, and look: for out of Galilee ariseth no prophet.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθησαν καὶ εἶπον αὐτῷ Μὴ καὶ σὺ ἐκ τῆς Γαλιλαίας εἶ ἐρεύνησον καὶ ἴδε ὅτι προφήτης ἐκ τῆς Γαλιλαίας οὐκ ἐγήγερται
verses.transliteration: apekrithēsan kai eipon autō Mē kai sy ek tēs Galilaias ei ereunēson kai ide hoti prophētēs ek tēs Galilaias ouk egēgertai
verses.study_note: 'Art thou also of Galilee?' — a sneer: are you too a Galilean sympathiser? 'Search, and look: for out of Galilee ariseth no prophet' — this is factually incorrect. Jonah was from Gath-hepher in Galilee (2 Kings 14:25). Nahum was possibly from Galilee (Elkosh). Elijah may have been from Galilee. The Pharisees' dismissive certainty is based on prejudice, not scholarship. They tell Nicodemus to 'search' (ereunēson) the Scriptures — the same verb Jesus used in 5:39.

verses.kjv_text: And every man went unto his own house.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐπορεύθη ἕκαστος εἰς τὸν οἶκον αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai eporeuthē hekastos eis ton oikon autou
verses.study_note: 'Every man went unto his own house' — the meeting breaks up inconclusively. No arrest, no resolution, no consensus. The chapter ends as it began: with division. Everyone goes home, but the question of Jesus' identity remains unanswered — for them. John's readers know the answer. Note: 7:53-8:11 (the woman caught in adultery) is absent from the earliest manuscripts and is likely a later insertion, though the story itself may be historically authentic.

meta.overview: John 8 contains some of the most dramatic confrontations and exalted christological claims in the entire Gospel. The chapter opens with the Pericope Adulterae (vv.1-11), the story of the woman taken in adultery — 'He that is without sin among you, let him first cast a stone at her' (v.7). Though absent from the earliest Greek manuscripts, the passage has been treasured by the church as authentic tradition. Beginning at v.12, Jesus continues teaching at the Feast of Tabernacles, now making his second great 'I AM' declaration: 'I am the light of the world: he that followeth me shall not walk in darkness, but shall have the light of life' (v.12). This claim, made during the festival's lamplight ceremony, is staggering — Jesus claims to be what the great candelabra in the temple court merely symbolised. The Pharisees challenge his self-testimony (v.13), and Jesus responds by asserting dual witness: 'I am one that bear witness of myself, and the Father that sent me beareth witness of me' (v.18). The discourse escalates through a series of increasingly confrontational exchanges. Jesus warns: 'I go my way, and ye shall seek me, and shall die in your sins: whither I go, ye cannot come' (v.21). 'If ye believe not that I am he, ye shall die in your sins' (v.24). He promises genuine freedom to those who believe: 'If ye continue in my word, then are ye my disciples indeed; And ye shall know the truth, and the truth shall make you free' (vv.31-32). The Jews protest that they are Abraham's children; Jesus counters that their works prove otherwise: 'Ye are of your father the devil, and the lusts of your father ye will do' (v.44). The confrontation reaches its climax with the supreme christological claim: 'Verily, verily, I say unto you, Before Abraham was, I am' (v.58). This absolute 'I AM' (egō eimi) is a direct claim to the divine name revealed at the burning bush (Exodus 3:14). The Jews understand perfectly and take up stones to kill him for blasphemy (v.59).
meta.theme: The Light of the World — Freedom, Truth, and the Divine I AM
meta.keyWordMeaning: I AM — the absolute self-designation of Jesus that echoes the divine name revealed to Moses at the burning bush: 'I AM THAT I AM' (Exodus 3:14, Hebrew: ehyeh asher ehyeh, LXX: egō eimi ho ōn). In John 8, egō eimi is used in its most absolute and confrontational form. In v.24, 'if ye believe not that I am (egō eimi), ye shall die in your sins.' In v.28, 'when ye have lifted up the Son of man, then shall ye know that I am (egō eimi).' In v.58, 'Before Abraham was, I am (egō eimi)' — not 'I was' (past tense) but 'I am' (present tense), claiming eternal, timeless existence. The Jews' immediate reaction — picking up stones to stone him (v.59) — proves they understood this as a claim to deity, not merely a claim to pre-existence.
outline: The Woman Taken in Adultery (vv.1-11): Jesus went unto the mount of Olives. Early in the morning he came again into the temple and all the people came unto him and he sat down and taught them. The scribes and Pharisees brought unto him a woman taken in adultery and set her in the midst. They say unto him Master this woman was taken in adultery in the very act. Now Moses in the law commanded us that such should be stoned but what sayest thou? This they said tempting him that they might have to accuse him. But Jesus stooped down and with his finger wrote on the ground. When they continued asking him he lifted up himself and said He that is without sin among you let him first cast a stone at her. And again he stooped down and wrote on the ground. They which heard it being convicted by their own conscience went out one by one beginning at the eldest even unto the last and Jesus was left alone and the woman standing in the midst. Jesus said unto her Woman where are those thine accusers? Hath no man condemned thee? She said No man Lord. And Jesus said Neither do I condemn thee go and sin no more

I Am the Light of the World (vv.12-20): Then spake Jesus again unto them saying I am the light of the world he that followeth me shall not walk in darkness but shall have the light of life. The Pharisees therefore said unto him Thou bearest record of thyself thy record is not true. Jesus answered and said unto them Though I bear record of myself yet my record is true for I know whence I came and whither I go but ye cannot tell whence I come and whither I go. Ye judge after the flesh I judge no man. And yet if I judge my judgment is true for I am not alone but I and the Father that sent me. It is also written in your law that the testimony of two men is true. I am one that bear witness of myself and the Father that sent me beareth witness of me. Then said they unto him Where is thy Father? Jesus answered Ye neither know me nor my Father — if ye had known me ye should have known my Father also. These words spake Jesus in the treasury as he taught in the temple and no man laid hands on him for his hour was not yet come

Ye Shall Die in Your Sins (vv.21-30): Then said Jesus again unto them I go my way and ye shall seek me and shall die in your sins whither I go ye cannot come. Then said the Jews Will he kill himself? because he saith Whither I go ye cannot come. And he said unto them Ye are from beneath I am from above ye are of this world I am not of this world. I said therefore unto you that ye shall die in your sins for if ye believe not that I am he ye shall die in your sins. Then said they unto him Who art thou? And Jesus saith unto them Even the same that I said unto you from the beginning. I have many things to say and to judge of you but he that sent me is true and I speak to the world those things which I have heard of him. They understood not that he spake to them of the Father. Then said Jesus unto them When ye have lifted up the Son of man then shall ye know that I am he and that I do nothing of myself but as my Father hath taught me I speak these things. And he that sent me is with me the Father hath not left me alone for I do always those things that please him. As he spake these words many believed on him

The Truth Shall Make You Free (vv.31-38): Then said Jesus to those Jews which believed on him If ye continue in my word then are ye my disciples indeed And ye shall know the truth and the truth shall make you free. They answered him We be Abraham's seed and were never in bondage to any man how sayest thou Ye shall be made free? Jesus answered them Verily verily I say unto you Whosoever committeth sin is the servant of sin. And the servant abideth not in the house for ever but the Son abideth ever. If the Son therefore shall make you free ye shall be free indeed. I know that ye are Abraham's seed but ye seek to kill me because my word hath no place in you. I speak that which I have seen with my Father and ye do that which ye have seen with your father

Children of Abraham or Children of the Devil (vv.39-47): They answered and said unto him Abraham is our father. Jesus saith unto them If ye were Abraham's children ye would do the works of Abraham. But now ye seek to kill me a man that hath told you the truth which I have heard of God this did not Abraham. Ye do the deeds of your father. Then said they to him We be not born of fornication we have one Father even God. Jesus said unto them If God were your Father ye would love me for I proceeded forth and came from God neither came I of myself but he sent me. Why do ye not understand my speech? even because ye cannot hear my word. Ye are of your father the devil and the lusts of your father ye will do. He was a murderer from the beginning and abode not in the truth because there is no truth in him. When he speaketh a lie he speaketh of his own for he is a liar and the father of it. And because I tell you the truth ye believe me not. Which of you convinceth me of sin? And if I say the truth why do ye not believe me? He that is of God heareth God's words ye therefore hear them not because ye are not of God

Before Abraham Was I AM (vv.48-59): Then answered the Jews and said unto him Say we not well that thou art a Samaritan and hast a devil? Jesus answered I have not a devil but I honour my Father and ye do dishonour me. And I seek not mine own glory there is one that seeketh and judgeth. Verily verily I say unto you If a man keep my saying he shall never see death. Then said the Jews unto him Now we know that thou hast a devil. Abraham is dead and the prophets and thou sayest If a man keep my saying he shall never taste of death. Art thou greater than our father Abraham which is dead? and the prophets are dead whom makest thou thyself? Jesus answered If I honour myself my honour is nothing it is my Father that honoureth me of whom ye say that he is your God. Yet ye have not known him but I know him and if I should say I know him not I shall be a liar like unto you but I know him and keep his saying. Your father Abraham rejoiced to see my day and he saw it and was glad. Then said the Jews unto him Thou art not yet fifty years old and hast thou seen Abraham? Jesus said unto them Verily verily I say unto you Before Abraham was I am. Then took they up stones to cast at him but Jesus hid himself and went out of the temple going through the midst of them and so passed by

verses.kjv_text: Jesus went unto the mount of Olives.
verses.original_text: Ἰησοῦς δὲ ἐπορεύθη εἰς τὸ ὄρος τῶν ἐλαιῶν
verses.transliteration: Iēsous de eporeuthē eis to oros tōn elaiōn
verses.study_note: The Pericope Adulterae (7:53-8:11) is absent from the earliest and best Greek manuscripts (P66, P75, Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) and likely was not part of John's original text. However, the story is widely considered historically authentic — it bears the marks of genuine tradition and was preserved by the church. 'The mount of Olives' (to oros tōn elaiōn) — Jesus often retreated there (Luke 21:37; 22:39). While 'every man went unto his own house' (7:53), Jesus had no permanent home (Matthew 8:20).

verses.kjv_text: And early in the morning he came again into the temple, and all the people came unto him; and he sat down, and taught them.
verses.original_text: ὄρθρου δὲ πάλιν παρεγένετο εἰς τὸ ἱερὸν καὶ πᾶς ὁ λαὸς ἤρχετο πρὸς αὐτόν καὶ καθίσας ἐδίδασκεν αὐτούς
verses.transliteration: orthrou de palin paregeneto eis to hieron kai pas ho laos ērcheto pros auton kai kathisas edidasken autous
verses.study_note: 'Early in the morning' (orthrou — at dawn). 'Sat down, and taught' — the seated posture of authoritative teaching. 'All the people came' — Jesus' popularity with the common people is persistent despite the authorities' opposition.

verses.kjv_text: And the scribes and Pharisees brought unto him a woman taken in adultery; and when they had set her in the midst,
verses.original_text: ἄγουσιν δὲ οἱ γραμματεῖς καὶ οἱ Φαρισαῖοι πρὸς αὐτὸν γυναῖκα ἐν μοιχείᾳ κατειλημμένην καὶ στήσαντες αὐτὴν ἐν μέσῳ
verses.transliteration: agousin de hoi grammateis kai hoi Pharisaioi pros auton gynaika en moicheia kateilēmmenēn kai stēsantes autēn en mesō
verses.study_note: 'Taken in adultery' (en moicheia kateilēmmenēn — caught in the act of adultery). 'Set her in the midst' — publicly exposed, humiliated. The absence of the man (who was equally guilty, Leviticus 20:10) reveals the trap's true nature: they do not care about justice but about ensnaring Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: They say unto him, Master, this woman was taken in adultery, in the very act.
verses.original_text: λέγουσιν αὐτῷ Διδάσκαλε αὕτη ἡ γυνὴ κατελήφθη ἐπαυτοφώρῳ μοιχευομένη
verses.transliteration: legousin autō Didaskale hautē hē gynē katelēphthē epautophōrō moicheuomenē
verses.study_note: 'In the very act' (epautophōrō — caught in the act itself, red-handed). The evidence is indisputable. But the situation is manufactured: for the woman to be 'caught in the very act,' witnesses had to be watching, which suggests a set-up. The Pharisees use a real sinner as a pawn in their game against Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: Now Moses in the law commanded us, that such should be stoned: but what sayest thou?
verses.original_text: ἐν δὲ τῷ νόμῳ Μωϋσῆς ἡμῖν ἐνετείλατο τὰς τοιαύτας λιθοβολεῖσθαι σὺ οὖν τί λέγεις
verses.transliteration: en de tō nomō Mōysēs hēmin eneteilato tas toiautas lithobolesthai sy oun ti legeis
verses.study_note: The reference is to Leviticus 20:10 and Deuteronomy 22:22-24 (stoning for adultery). The trap is a dilemma: if Jesus says 'Stone her,' he contradicts his message of mercy and potentially violates Roman law (which reserved capital punishment for Rome). If he says 'Don't stone her,' he contradicts Moses and can be accused of disregarding the Law.

verses.kjv_text: This they said, tempting him, that they might have to accuse him. But Jesus stooped down, and with his finger wrote on the ground, as though he heard them not.
verses.original_text: τοῦτο δὲ ἔλεγον πειράζοντες αὐτόν ἵνα ἔχωσιν κατηγορεῖν αὐτοῦ ὁ δὲ Ἰησοῦς κάτω κύψας τῷ δακτύλῳ ἔγραφεν εἰς τὴν γῆν
verses.transliteration: touto de elegon peirazontes auton hina echōsin katēgorein autou ho de Iēsous katō kypsas tō daktylō egraphen eis tēn gēn
verses.study_note: 'Tempting him' (peirazontes auton — testing him). 'Wrote on the ground' (tō daktylō egraphen eis tēn gēn — with his finger wrote on the ground). What Jesus wrote is unknown — speculations include: their sins, the names of accusers, OT texts (Jeremiah 17:13: 'they that depart from me shall be written in the earth'). The act itself is significant: Jesus refuses to engage with their trap on their terms. The 'finger of God' wrote the law on stone tablets (Exodus 31:18); now the finger of God writes on earth.

verses.kjv_text: So when they continued asking him, he lifted up himself, and said unto them, He that is without sin among you, let him first cast a stone at her.
verses.original_text: ὡς δὲ ἐπέμενον ἐρωτῶντες αὐτόν ἀνακύψας εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτούς Ὁ ἀναμάρτητος ὑμῶν πρῶτος τὸν λίθον ἐπ᾽ αὐτῇ βαλέτω
verses.transliteration: hōs de epemenon erōtōntes auton anakupsas eipen pros autous Ho anamartētos hymōn prōtos ton lithon ep autē baletō
verses.study_note: 'He that is without sin' (ho anamartētos — the sinless one, the one without sin). 'Let him first cast a stone' — Jesus does not deny the law's validity but turns it into a mirror. Deuteronomy 17:7 required the witnesses to cast the first stones. By adding 'without sin,' Jesus shifts the focus from the woman's guilt (which is undeniable) to the accusers' standing. They came to judge her; they end up judged. The response is devastating in its simplicity: it upholds the law, exposes hypocrisy, and extends mercy simultaneously.

verses.kjv_text: And again he stooped down, and wrote on the ground.
verses.original_text: καὶ πάλιν κάτω κύψας ἔγραφεν εἰς τὴν γῆν
verses.transliteration: kai palin katō kupsas egraphen eis tēn gēn
verses.study_note: Jesus bends down again and writes — giving the accusers time and space to process his words without the pressure of his gaze. He does not watch them leave; he does not gloat. The silence and the writing are acts of mercy toward the accusers as well as toward the woman.

verses.kjv_text: And they which heard it, being convicted by their own conscience, went out one by one, beginning at the eldest, even unto the last: and Jesus was left alone, and the woman standing in the midst.
verses.original_text: οἱ δὲ ἀκούσαντες καὶ ὑπὸ τῆς συνειδήσεως ἐλεγχόμενοι ἐξήρχοντο εἷς καθ᾽ εἷς ἀρξάμενοι ἀπὸ τῶν πρεσβυτέρων ἕως τῶν ἐσχάτων καὶ κατελείφθη μόνος ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ ἡ γυνὴ ἐν μέσῳ ἑστῶσα
verses.transliteration: hoi de akousantes kai hypo tēs syneidēseōs elenchomenoi exērchonto heis kath heis arxamenoi apo tōn presbyterōn heōs tōn eschatōn kai kateleiphthē monos ho Iēsous kai hē gynē en mesō hestōsa
verses.study_note: 'Convicted by their own conscience' (hypo tēs syneidēseōs elenchomenoi — being convicted/reproved by conscience). 'Beginning at the eldest' (arxamenoi apo tōn presbyterōn — starting from the oldest) — the oldest, with the most sins to remember, leave first. 'Jesus was left alone, and the woman' — the only one without sin and the one caught in sin remain together. Augustine: 'Two remained: misery and mercy (miseria et misericordia).'

verses.kjv_text: When Jesus had lifted up himself, and saw none but the woman, he said unto her, Woman, where are those thine accusers? hath no man condemned thee?
verses.original_text: ἀνακύψας δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ μηδένα θεασάμενος πλὴν τῆς γυναικός εἶπεν αὐτῇ Γύναι ποῦ εἰσιν ἐκεῖνοι οἱ κατήγοροί σου οὐδείς σε κατέκρινεν
verses.transliteration: anakupsas de ho Iēsous kai mēdena theasamenos plēn tēs gynaikos eipen autē Gynai pou eisin ekeinoi hoi katēgoroi sou oudeis se katekrinen
verses.study_note: 'Woman' (gynai — a respectful address, cf. 2:4; 19:26). 'Where are those thine accusers?' — the accusers have gone; without witnesses, the legal case collapses. 'Hath no man condemned thee?' (oudeis se katekrinen — has no one passed sentence on you?). Jesus creates space for the woman to see the mercy that has been extended.

verses.kjv_text: She said, No man, Lord. And Jesus said unto her, Neither do I condemn thee: go, and sin no more.
verses.original_text: ἡ δὲ εἶπεν Οὐδείς κύριε εἶπεν δὲ αὐτῇ ὁ Ἰησοῦς Οὐδὲ ἐγώ σε κατακρίνω πορεύου καὶ μηκέτι ἁμάρτανε
verses.transliteration: hē de eipen Oudeis kyrie eipen de autē ho Iēsous Oude egō se katakrinō poreuou kai mēketi hamartane
verses.study_note: 'Neither do I condemn thee' (oude egō se katakrinō — nor do I pass sentence on you). 'Go, and sin no more' (poreuou kai mēketi hamartane — go, and from now on stop sinning). Jesus neither condones the sin nor condemns the sinner. He extends mercy ('neither do I condemn') while demanding transformation ('sin no more'). This is grace: not the absence of moral standards but the presence of transforming love. Jesus is the only one qualified to cast the stone — and he chooses not to.
verses.doctrinal_note: This story illustrates the perfect balance of grace and truth (John 1:14, 17). Jesus does not lower the moral standard (sin is still sin); he does not excuse ('go and sin no more'); he does not condemn ('neither do I condemn thee'). The story shows how God deals with sinners: with compassion, without compromise, toward transformation. The woman is free from condemnation but not free to continue sinning. Mercy received must produce a changed life.

verses.kjv_text: Then spake Jesus again unto them, saying, I am the light of the world: he that followeth me shall not walk in darkness, but shall have the light of life.
verses.original_text: πάλιν οὖν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἐλάλησεν λέγων Ἐγώ εἰμι τὸ φῶς τοῦ κόσμου ὁ ἀκολουθῶν ἐμοὶ οὐ μὴ περιπατήσῃ ἐν τῇ σκοτίᾳ ἀλλ᾽ ἕξει τὸ φῶς τῆς ζωῆς
verses.transliteration: palin oun autois ho Iēsous elalēsen legōn Egō eimi to phōs tou kosmou ho akolouthōn emoi ou mē peripatēsē en tē skotia all hexei to phōs tēs zōēs
verses.study_note: 'I am the light of the world' (Egō eimi to phōs tou kosmou — the second great 'I AM' predicate statement). During Tabernacles, four enormous candelabra were lit in the Court of Women, illuminating all of Jerusalem. Jesus claims to be the reality these lights merely symbolised. 'He that followeth me' (ho akolouthōn emoi — the one who follows me; present participle: keeps following). 'Shall not walk in darkness' (ou mē peripatēsē en tē skotia — emphatic: will absolutely never walk in darkness). 'The light of life' (to phōs tēs zōēs — the light that gives life, the light that is life). Light and life are paired: as physical light enables physical life, so Christ the spiritual light enables spiritual life.
verses.doctrinal_note: This is the second of seven 'I AM' predicate statements in John. The OT repeatedly describes God as light: 'The LORD is my light' (Psalm 27:1). 'In thy light shall we see light' (Psalm 36:9). 'The LORD shall be unto thee an everlasting light' (Isaiah 60:19-20). Jesus claims this divine attribute for himself. The Tabernacles light ceremony celebrated the pillar of fire that guided Israel (Exodus 13:21); Jesus claims to be the true pillar of fire — the permanent divine presence that guides, illuminates, and gives life. Darkness in John is not merely absence of light but the realm of sin, ignorance, and death (1:5; 3:19; 12:35, 46).

verses.kjv_text: The Pharisees therefore said unto him, Thou bearest record of thyself; thy record is not true.
verses.original_text: εἶπον οὖν αὐτῷ οἱ Φαρισαῖοι Σὺ περὶ σεαυτοῦ μαρτυρεῖς ἡ μαρτυρία σου οὐκ ἔστιν ἀληθής
verses.transliteration: eipon oun autō hoi Pharisaioi Sy peri seautou martyreis hē martyria sou ouk estin alēthēs
verses.study_note: The Pharisees invoke the legal principle that self-testimony is insufficient (cf. Deuteronomy 19:15). This echoes 5:31, where Jesus himself said 'If I bear witness of myself, my witness is not true' (in the sense of legally valid). They use his own statement against him — but Jesus now elevates the discussion to a different plane (v.14).

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered and said unto them, Though I bear record of myself, yet my record is true: for I know whence I came, and whither I go; but ye cannot tell whence I come, and whither I go.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη Ἰησοῦς καὶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς Κἂν ἐγὼ μαρτυρῶ περὶ ἐμαυτοῦ ἀληθής ἐστιν ἡ μαρτυρία μου ὅτι οἶδα πόθεν ἦλθον καὶ ποῦ ὑπάγω ὑμεῖς δὲ οὐκ οἴδατε πόθεν ἔρχομαι ἢ ποῦ ὑπάγω
verses.transliteration: apekrithē Iēsous kai eipen autois Kan egō martyrō peri emautou alēthēs estin hē martyria mou hoti oida pothen ēlthon kai pou hypagō hymeis de ouk oidate pothen erchomai ē pou hypagō
verses.study_note: 'Though I bear record of myself, yet my record is true' — not a contradiction of 5:31 but a deeper claim. In 5:31, Jesus acknowledged the legal convention; here he asserts that his testimony is inherently true regardless of convention because he alone knows his origin and destiny. 'I know whence I came and whither I go' — from the Father and back to the Father (cf. 16:28). Their ignorance of his origin disqualifies their judgment.

verses.kjv_text: Ye judge after the flesh; I judge no man.
verses.original_text: ὑμεῖς κατὰ τὴν σάρκα κρίνετε ἐγὼ οὐ κρίνω οὐδένα
verses.transliteration: hymeis kata tēn sarka krinete egō ou krinō oudena
verses.study_note: 'Ye judge after the flesh' (kata tēn sarka krinete — you judge according to the flesh, by human/external standards). 'I judge no man' — not a denial of his judicial authority (5:22, 27) but a statement that he does not judge in the manner they do: by appearances, by prejudice, by fleshly criteria. His judgment, when exercised, is true (v.16).

verses.kjv_text: And yet if I judge, my judgment is true: for I am not alone, but I and the Father that sent me.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐὰν κρίνω δὲ ἐγώ ἡ κρίσις ἡ ἐμὴ ἀληθής ἐστιν ὅτι μόνος οὐκ εἰμί ἀλλ᾽ ἐγὼ καὶ ὁ πέμψας με πατήρ
verses.transliteration: kai ean krinō de egō hē krisis hē emē alēthēs estin hoti monos ouk eimi all egō kai ho pempsas me patēr
verses.study_note: 'I am not alone, but I and the Father' — Jesus' judgment is reliable because it is never solitary: the Father and Son judge together. This satisfies the 'two witnesses' requirement (v.17-18) at the deepest level: the two witnesses are the Father and the Son.

verses.kjv_text: It is also written in your law, that the testimony of two men is true.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐν τῷ νόμῳ δὲ τῷ ὑμετέρῳ γέγραπται ὅτι δύο ἀνθρώπων ἡ μαρτυρία ἀληθής ἐστιν
verses.transliteration: kai en tō nomō de tō hymeterō gegraptai hoti duo anthrōpōn hē martyria alēthēs estin
verses.study_note: 'Your law' (tō hymeterō nomō — your law; not distancing himself from the law but emphasising their own legal standard). The reference is to Deuteronomy 17:6; 19:15 — two or three witnesses required. Jesus will provide the two witnesses in v.18.

verses.kjv_text: I am one that bear witness of myself, and the Father that sent me beareth witness of me.
verses.original_text: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ μαρτυρῶν περὶ ἐμαυτοῦ καὶ μαρτυρεῖ περὶ ἐμοῦ ὁ πέμψας με πατήρ
verses.transliteration: egō eimi ho martyrōn peri emautou kai martyrei peri emou ho pempsas me patēr
verses.study_note: Two witnesses: (1) Jesus himself (egō eimi ho martyrōn — I AM the one witnessing). (2) The Father who sent him (ho pempsas me patēr — the Father who sent me). Note that 'I am' (egō eimi) opens the statement — in context, this may carry divine name overtones. The Father's testimony comes through Jesus' works (5:36), the Scriptures (5:39), and the inner witness of the Spirit.

verses.kjv_text: Then said they unto him, Where is thy Father? Jesus answered, Ye neither know me, nor my Father: if ye had known me, ye should have known my Father also.
verses.original_text: ἔλεγον οὖν αὐτῷ Ποῦ ἐστιν ὁ πατήρ σου ἀπεκρίθη Ἰησοῦς Οὔτε ἐμὲ οἴδατε οὔτε τὸν πατέρα μου εἰ ἐμὲ ᾔδειτε καὶ τὸν πατέρα μου ἂν ᾔδειτε
verses.transliteration: elegon oun autō Pou estin ho patēr sou apekrithē Iēsous Oute eme oidate oute ton patera mou ei eme ēdeite kai ton patera mou an ēdeite
verses.study_note: 'Where is thy Father?' — they may mean it sarcastically (a slur on his birth) or literally (produce this witness). 'Ye neither know me, nor my Father' — devastating: they are the religious experts, yet they know neither the Son nor the Father. 'If ye had known me, ye should have known my Father also' — knowledge of the Father comes exclusively through the Son (cf. 14:7, 9: 'He that hath seen me hath seen the Father'). To reject the Son is to forfeit knowledge of the Father.

verses.kjv_text: These words spake Jesus in the treasury, as he taught in the temple: and no man laid hands on him; for his hour was not yet come.
verses.original_text: ταῦτα τὰ ῥήματα ἐλάλησεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἐν τῷ γαζοφυλακίῳ διδάσκων ἐν τῷ ἱερῷ καὶ οὐδεὶς ἐπίασεν αὐτόν ὅτι οὔπω ἐληλύθει ἡ ὥρα αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: tauta ta rhēmata elalēsen ho Iēsous en tō gazophylakiō didaskōn en tō hierō kai oudeis epiasen auton hoti oupō elēlythei hē hōra autou
verses.study_note: 'In the treasury' (en tō gazophylakiō — in the area of the treasury, in the Court of Women where the offering boxes were and where the great candelabra of Tabernacles were lit). Jesus makes his 'Light of the world' claim in the very place where the festival lights burned. 'No man laid hands on him; for his hour was not yet come' — the refrain of divine sovereignty (cf. 7:30).

verses.kjv_text: Then said Jesus again unto them, I go my way, and ye shall seek me, and shall die in your sins: whither I go, ye cannot come.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν οὖν πάλιν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ἐγὼ ὑπάγω καὶ ζητήσετέ με καὶ ἐν τῇ ἁμαρτίᾳ ὑμῶν ἀποθανεῖσθε ὅπου ἐγὼ ὑπάγω ὑμεῖς οὐ δύνασθε ἐλθεῖν
verses.transliteration: eipen oun palin autois ho Iēsous Egō hypagō kai zētēsete me kai en tē hamartia hymōn apothaneisthe hopou egō hypagō hymeis ou dynasthe elthein
verses.study_note: 'Ye shall die in your sins' (en tē hamartia hymōn apothaneisthe — in your sin you will die). This is more severe than 7:34 (which omitted 'in your sins'). To die in one's sins means to face death with guilt unremoved, unpardoned. 'Whither I go, ye cannot come' — Jesus goes to the Father; those who reject him cannot follow. The warning is urgent: the opportunity to believe will not last forever.

verses.kjv_text: Then said the Jews, Will he kill himself? because he saith, Whither I go, ye cannot come.
verses.original_text: ἔλεγον οὖν οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι Μήτι ἀποκτενεῖ ἑαυτόν ὅτι λέγει Ὅπου ἐγὼ ὑπάγω ὑμεῖς οὐ δύνασθε ἐλθεῖν
verses.transliteration: elegon oun hoi Ioudaioi Mēti apoktenei heauton hoti legei Hopou egō hypagō hymeis ou dynasthe elthein
verses.study_note: 'Will he kill himself?' — in 7:35 they wondered if he would go to the Diaspora; here they suggest suicide. Jewish tradition placed suicides in the lowest part of Hades — so 'where I go ye cannot come' would mean Jesus goes to hell. The irony: Jesus will indeed lay down his life voluntarily (10:17-18), but this is not suicide — it is sacrifice.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, Ye are from beneath; I am from above: ye are of this world; I am not of this world.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς Ὑμεῖς ἐκ τῶν κάτω ἐστέ ἐγὼ ἐκ τῶν ἄνω εἰμί ὑμεῖς ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου τούτου ἐστέ ἐγὼ οὐκ εἰμὶ ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου τούτου
verses.transliteration: kai eipen autois Hymeis ek tōn katō este egō ek tōn anō eimi hymeis ek tou kosmou toutou este egō ouk eimi ek tou kosmou toutou
