verses.study_note: 'Ye are from beneath; I am from above' (hymeis ek tōn katō — you are from the things below; egō ek tōn anō — I am from the things above). 'Ye are of this world; I am not of this world.' The contrast is absolute: their origin is earthly, his is heavenly. 'This world' (tou kosmou toutou) in John means the fallen world-system in rebellion against God (cf. 1:10; 15:18-19; 17:14-16). Jesus entered this world (1:14) but does not belong to it.

verses.kjv_text: I said therefore unto you, that ye shall die in your sins: for if ye believe not that I am he, ye shall die in your sins.
verses.original_text: εἶπον οὖν ὑμῖν ὅτι ἀποθανεῖσθε ἐν ταῖς ἁμαρτίαις ὑμῶν ἐὰν γὰρ μὴ πιστεύσητε ὅτι ἐγώ εἰμι ἀποθανεῖσθε ἐν ταῖς ἁμαρτίαις ὑμῶν
verses.transliteration: eipon oun hymin hoti apothaneisthe en tais hamartiais hymōn ean gar mē pisteusēte hoti egō eimi apothaneisthe en tais hamartiais hymōn
verses.study_note: 'If ye believe not that I am' (ean mē pisteusēte hoti egō eimi — unless you believe that I AM). Note: 'he' is italicised in the KJV because it is not in the Greek — the text reads simply egō eimi (I AM), the absolute divine self-designation. Believing that Jesus is the I AM — God incarnate — is the condition for escaping death in sin. Without faith in the deity of Christ, there is no salvation.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse makes belief in Jesus' divine identity the dividing line between salvation and damnation. 'If ye believe not that I AM' — the absolute egō eimi without predicate. This is not merely 'I am the Messiah' but 'I AM' — the divine name (Exodus 3:14). The consequence of unbelief is dying 'in your sins' (en tais hamartiais hymōn — plural, emphasising the accumulated weight of unforgiven sin). There is no alternative path to forgiveness: either one believes in the I AM and is freed from sin, or one rejects him and dies under sin's condemnation.

verses.kjv_text: Then said they unto him, Who art thou? And Jesus saith unto them, Even the same that I said unto you from the beginning.
verses.original_text: ἔλεγον οὖν αὐτῷ Σὺ τίς εἶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς Τὴν ἀρχὴν ὅ τι καὶ λαλῶ ὑμῖν
verses.transliteration: elegon oun autō Sy tis ei eipen autois ho Iēsous Tēn archēn ho ti kai lalō hymin
verses.study_note: 'Who art thou?' (sy tis ei — who are you?). Jesus' answer is notoriously difficult to translate. 'Even the same that I said unto you from the beginning' is one rendering. Other translations: 'Why do I speak to you at all?' or 'Exactly what I have been telling you from the start.' The ambiguity may be intentional: Jesus has been declaring his identity throughout, and they still ask. His identity has been stated clearly — their problem is not lack of information but lack of faith.

verses.kjv_text: I have many things to say and to judge of you: but he that sent me is true; and I speak to the world those things which I have heard of him.
verses.original_text: πολλὰ ἔχω περὶ ὑμῶν λαλεῖν καὶ κρίνειν ἀλλ᾽ ὁ πέμψας με ἀληθής ἐστιν κἀγὼ ἃ ἤκουσα παρ᾽ αὐτοῦ ταῦτα λέγω εἰς τὸν κόσμον
verses.transliteration: polla echō peri hymōn lalein kai krinein all ho pempsas me alēthēs estin kagō ha ēkousa par autou tauta legō eis ton kosmon
verses.study_note: 'I have many things to say and to judge' — Jesus restrains himself; he could say much more in judgment. 'He that sent me is true' — the Father's truthfulness guarantees the Son's message. 'I speak to the world those things which I have heard of him' — Jesus' words originate in the Father's communication to the Son.

verses.kjv_text: They understood not that he spake to them of the Father.
verses.original_text: οὐκ ἔγνωσαν ὅτι τὸν πατέρα αὐτοῖς ἔλεγεν
verses.transliteration: ouk egnōsan hoti ton patera autois elegen
verses.study_note: They did not understand that 'he that sent me' referred to the Father. Their spiritual blindness is remarkable — Jesus has spoken of the Father repeatedly, yet they cannot perceive it. This incomprehension underscores the spiritual darkness Jesus came to illuminate.

verses.kjv_text: Then said Jesus unto them, When ye have lifted up the Son of man, then shall ye know that I am he, and that I do nothing of myself; but as my Father hath taught me, I speak these things.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν οὖν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ὅταν ὑψώσητε τὸν υἱὸν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου τότε γνώσεσθε ὅτι ἐγώ εἰμι καὶ ἀπ᾽ ἐμαυτοῦ ποιῶ οὐδέν ἀλλὰ καθὼς ἐδίδαξέν με ὁ πατήρ ταῦτα λαλῶ
verses.transliteration: eipen oun autois ho Iēsous Hotan hypsōsēte ton huion tou anthrōpou tote gnōsesthe hoti egō eimi kai ap emautou poiō ouden alla kathōs edidaxen me ho patēr tauta lalō
verses.study_note: 'When ye have lifted up the Son of man' (hotan hypsōsēte ton huion tou anthrōpou — when you lift up/exalt the Son of man). 'Lifted up' (hypsōsēte — from hypsoō: to lift up, to exalt) has a double meaning in John: physically lifting on the cross and spiritually exalting in glory (cf. 3:14; 12:32-34). 'Then shall ye know that I am' (tote gnōsesthe hoti egō eimi — then you will come to know that I AM). The cross will reveal Jesus' identity: the centurion's confession (Mark 15:39), the darkened sky, the torn veil, the resurrection — all confirm the I AM claim.

verses.kjv_text: And he that sent me is with me: the Father hath not left me alone; for I do always those things that please him.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὁ πέμψας με μετ᾽ ἐμοῦ ἐστιν οὐκ ἀφῆκέν με μόνον ὁ πατήρ ὅτι ἐγὼ τὰ ἀρεστὰ αὐτῷ ποιῶ πάντοτε
verses.transliteration: kai ho pempsas me met emou estin ouk aphēken me monon ho patēr hoti egō ta aresta autō poiō pantote
verses.study_note: 'The Father hath not left me alone' — in contrast to the woman whose accusers left (v.9), the Father never leaves the Son. 'I do always those things that please him' (ta aresta autō poiō pantote — the things pleasing to him I do always). This is an extraordinary claim to sinless, unbroken obedience — always, without exception, pleasing the Father. No mere human could truthfully say this.

verses.kjv_text: As he spake these words, many believed on him.
verses.original_text: ταῦτα αὐτοῦ λαλοῦντος πολλοὶ ἐπίστευσαν εἰς αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: tauta autou lalountos polloi episteusan eis auton
verses.study_note: 'Many believed on him' (polloi episteusan eis auton). This sets the stage for vv.31-59, where Jesus addresses 'those Jews which believed on him' (v.31). However, as the discourse progresses, these 'believers' turn hostile (vv.48, 59), suggesting their initial belief was superficial — a pattern John has shown before (2:23-25; 6:60-66).

verses.kjv_text: Then said Jesus to those Jews which believed on him, If ye continue in my word, then are ye my disciples indeed;
verses.original_text: ἔλεγεν οὖν ὁ Ἰησοῦς πρὸς τοὺς πεπιστευκότας αὐτῷ Ἰουδαίους Ἐὰν ὑμεῖς μείνητε ἐν τῷ λόγῳ τῷ ἐμῷ ἀληθῶς μαθηταί μού ἐστε
verses.transliteration: elegen oun ho Iēsous pros tous pepisteukotus autō Ioudaious Ean hymeis meinēte en tō logō tō emō alēthōs mathētai mou este
verses.study_note: 'If ye continue in my word' (ean hymeis meinēte en tō logō tō emō — if you remain/abide in my word). 'Then are ye my disciples indeed' (alēthōs mathētai mou este — you are truly my disciples). True discipleship is not initial belief but continuing abidence. The test of genuine faith is perseverance — remaining in Christ's word over time. Those who depart prove their faith was never real (cf. 1 John 2:19).

verses.kjv_text: And ye shall know the truth, and the truth shall make you free.
verses.original_text: καὶ γνώσεσθε τὴν ἀλήθειαν καὶ ἡ ἀλήθεια ἐλευθερώσει ὑμᾶς
verses.transliteration: kai gnōsesthe tēn alētheian kai hē alētheia eleutherōsei hymas
verses.study_note: 'Ye shall know the truth' (gnōsesthe tēn alētheian — you will come to know the truth; future: a progressive, experiential knowledge that comes through continuing in his word). 'The truth shall make you free' (hē alētheia eleutherōsei hymas — the truth will set you free). The 'truth' is not abstract philosophical truth but Jesus himself (14:6: 'I am the truth') and his word (17:17: 'Thy word is truth'). The freedom is from sin (v.34), death (v.51), and the devil (v.44) — not political freedom, as they misunderstand in v.33.
verses.doctrinal_note: This is one of the most quoted verses in Scripture, often lifted from context. The freedom Jesus offers is specifically spiritual: freedom from the bondage of sin (v.34: 'Whosoever committeth sin is the servant of sin'). The sequence is: (1) continue in the word → (2) become true disciples → (3) know the truth → (4) experience freedom. Knowledge that liberates is not mere information but personal, experiential acquaintance with Jesus, who is the Truth (14:6). This freedom is real freedom (v.36: 'free indeed'), unlike all human freedom which is constrained by sin.

verses.kjv_text: They answered him, We be Abraham's seed, and were never in bondage to any man: how sayest thou, Ye shall be made free?
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθησαν αὐτῷ Σπέρμα Ἀβραάμ ἐσμεν καὶ οὐδενὶ δεδουλεύκαμεν πώποτε πῶς σὺ λέγεις ὅτι Ἐλεύθεροι γενήσεσθε
verses.transliteration: apekrithēsan autō Sperma Abraam esmen kai oudeni dedouleukamen pōpote pōs sy legeis hoti Eleutheroi genēsesthe
verses.study_note: 'We be Abraham's seed and were never in bondage' — historically absurd: Egypt (400 years), Babylon (70 years), Persia, Greece, and now Rome. They are currently under Roman occupation. Their claim reveals self-deception on multiple levels: politically they deny their subjugation; spiritually they deny their bondage to sin. Abraham's lineage provides ethnic identity but not spiritual freedom.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered them, Verily, verily, I say unto you, Whosoever committeth sin is the servant of sin.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι πᾶς ὁ ποιῶν τὴν ἁμαρτίαν δοῦλός ἐστιν τῆς ἁμαρτίας
verses.transliteration: apekrithē autois ho Iēsous Amēn amēn legō hymin hoti pas ho poiōn tēn hamartian doulos estin tēs hamartias
verses.study_note: 'Whosoever committeth sin is the servant of sin' (pas ho poiōn tēn hamartian doulos estin tēs hamartias — everyone who practices sin is a slave of sin). 'Committeth' (ho poiōn — present participle: the one who keeps doing, practices habitually). Sin is not just an act but a master. The person who sins is not free but enslaved — addiction, compulsion, inability to stop. This slavery is universal: 'all have sinned' (Romans 3:23). Only the Son can set free (v.36).

verses.kjv_text: And the servant abideth not in the house for ever: but the Son abideth ever.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ δοῦλος οὐ μένει ἐν τῇ οἰκίᾳ εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα ὁ υἱὸς μένει εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα
verses.transliteration: ho de doulos ou menei en tē oikia eis ton aiōna ho huios menei eis ton aiōna
verses.study_note: 'The servant abideth not... but the Son abideth ever.' A slave has no permanent place in the household — he can be sold or expelled. The Son belongs permanently. Those enslaved to sin have no secure standing before God; the Son has eternal standing. The contrast sets up v.36: the Son's permanent status enables him to grant permanent freedom to slaves.

verses.kjv_text: If the Son therefore shall make you free, ye shall be free indeed.
verses.original_text: ἐὰν οὖν ὁ υἱὸς ὑμᾶς ἐλευθερώσῃ ὄντως ἐλεύθεροι ἔσεσθε
verses.transliteration: ean oun ho huios hymas eleutherōsē ontōs eleutheroi esesthe
verses.study_note: 'If the Son... shall make you free' (ean ho huios hymas eleutherōsē — if the Son sets you free). 'Free indeed' (ontōs eleutheroi — truly, really free). Not the partial freedom of political liberation or philosophical enlightenment but real, ontological freedom — freedom from sin's power, guilt, and penalty. Only the Son can do this because only the Son has authority over sin and death. The word 'indeed' (ontōs — in reality, genuinely) contrasts with all false freedoms.

verses.kjv_text: I know that ye are Abraham's seed; but ye seek to kill me, because my word hath no place in you.
verses.original_text: οἶδα ὅτι σπέρμα Ἀβραάμ ἐστε ἀλλὰ ζητεῖτέ με ἀποκτεῖναι ὅτι ὁ λόγος ὁ ἐμὸς οὐ χωρεῖ ἐν ὑμῖν
verses.transliteration: oida hoti sperma Abraam este alla zēteite me apokteinai hoti ho logos ho emos ou chōrei en hymin
verses.study_note: 'I know that ye are Abraham's seed' — Jesus concedes their physical descent from Abraham. 'But ye seek to kill me' — their actions contradict their heritage. 'My word hath no place in you' (ho logos ho emos ou chōrei en hymin — my word does not advance/find room in you). Chōreō means to make room, to progress — Jesus' word cannot penetrate their hearts. They are physically Abraham's children but spiritually they prove to have a different father (v.44).

verses.kjv_text: I speak that which I have seen with my Father: and ye do that which ye have seen with your father.
verses.original_text: ἐγὼ ὃ ἑώρακα παρὰ τῷ πατρί μου λαλῶ καὶ ὑμεῖς οὖν ὃ ἑωράκατε παρὰ τῷ πατρὶ ὑμῶν ποιεῖτε
verses.transliteration: egō ho heōraka para tō patri mou lalō kai hymeis oun ho heōrakate para tō patri hymōn poieite
verses.study_note: Two fathers, two behaviours: Jesus speaks what he has seen with his Father (God); they do what they have seen with their father (whom Jesus will identify as the devil in v.44). Children imitate their parents. Behaviour reveals parentage — not biology but spiritual allegiance.

verses.kjv_text: They answered and said unto him, Abraham is our father. Jesus saith unto them, If ye were Abraham's children, ye would do the works of Abraham.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθησαν καὶ εἶπον αὐτῷ Ὁ πατὴρ ἡμῶν Ἀβραάμ ἐστιν λέγει αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς Εἰ τέκνα τοῦ Ἀβραὰμ ἦτε τὰ ἔργα τοῦ Ἀβραὰμ ἐποιεῖτε ἂν
verses.transliteration: apekrithēsan kai eipon autō Ho patēr hēmōn Abraam estin legei autois ho Iēsous Ei tekna tou Abraam ēte ta erga tou Abraam epoieite an
verses.study_note: 'If ye were Abraham's children' — Jesus shifts from 'seed' (sperma, physical descent, v.37) to 'children' (tekna, spiritual character). Abraham's true children share Abraham's faith and works. Abraham believed God (Genesis 15:6); they disbelieve. Abraham welcomed divine messengers (Genesis 18:1-8); they seek to kill the divine messenger.

verses.kjv_text: But now ye seek to kill me, a man that hath told you the truth, which I have heard of God: this did not Abraham.
verses.original_text: νῦν δὲ ζητεῖτέ με ἀποκτεῖναι ἄνθρωπον ὃς τὴν ἀλήθειαν ὑμῖν λελάληκα ἣν ἤκουσα παρὰ τοῦ θεοῦ τοῦτο Ἀβραὰμ οὐκ ἐποίησεν
verses.transliteration: nyn de zēteite me apokteinai anthrōpon hos tēn alētheian hymin lelalēka hēn ēkousa para tou theou touto Abraam ouk epoiēsen
verses.study_note: 'A man that hath told you the truth' — Jesus describes himself as one who speaks truth heard from God. 'This did not Abraham' (touto Abraam ouk epoiēsen — Abraham did not do this). Abraham did not attempt to murder God's messenger; he received God's word with faith (Genesis 15:6). Their murderous intent proves they are not Abraham's spiritual children.

verses.kjv_text: Ye do the deeds of your father. Then said they to him, We be not born of fornication; we have one Father, even God.
verses.original_text: ὑμεῖς ποιεῖτε τὰ ἔργα τοῦ πατρὸς ὑμῶν εἶπον οὖν αὐτῷ Ἡμεῖς ἐκ πορνείας οὐ γεγεννήμεθα ἕνα πατέρα ἔχομεν τὸν θεόν
verses.transliteration: hymeis poieite ta erga tou patros hymōn eipon oun autō Hēmeis ek porneias ou gegennēmetha hena patera echomen ton theon
verses.study_note: 'We be not born of fornication' (hēmeis ek porneias ou gegennēmetha — we were not born of sexual immorality). This may be: (1) a slur against Jesus' birth (implying illegitimacy), or (2) a claim that they are not spiritual idolaters (the OT uses 'fornication' as a metaphor for idolatry, Hosea 1:2; 2:4). 'We have one Father, even God' — they escalate from Abraham to God himself. Jesus will demolish this claim in vv.42-44.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus said unto them, If God were your Father, ye would love me: for I proceeded forth and came from God; neither came I of myself, but he sent me.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς Εἰ ὁ θεὸς πατὴρ ὑμῶν ἦν ἠγαπᾶτε ἂν ἐμέ ἐγὼ γὰρ ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ ἐξῆλθον καὶ ἥκω οὐδὲ γὰρ ἀπ᾽ ἐμαυτοῦ ἐλήλυθα ἀλλ᾽ ἐκεῖνός με ἀπέστειλεν
verses.transliteration: eipen autois ho Iēsous Ei ho theos patēr hymōn ēn ēgapate an eme egō gar ek tou theou exēlthon kai hēkō oude gar ap emautou elēlytha all ekeinos me apesteilen
verses.study_note: 'If God were your Father, ye would love me' — the test is simple: do you love Jesus? If God is truly your Father, you will love the Son he sent. Their hatred of Jesus proves God is not their Father. 'I proceeded forth and came from God' (ek tou theou exēlthon kai hēkō — I came out from God and have arrived). Two verbs: exēlthon (went out, denoting origin) and hēkō (have come, denoting present arrival). Jesus' origin is divine, his mission is divinely commissioned.

verses.kjv_text: Why do ye not understand my speech? even because ye cannot hear my word.
verses.original_text: διατί τὴν λαλιὰν τὴν ἐμὴν οὐ γινώσκετε ὅτι οὐ δύνασθε ἀκούειν τὸν λόγον τὸν ἐμόν
verses.transliteration: diati tēn lalian tēn emēn ou ginōskete hoti ou dynasthe akouein ton logon ton emon
verses.study_note: 'Why do ye not understand my speech?' (diati tēn lalian tēn emēn ou ginōskete — why do you not recognise my mode of speaking?). 'Because ye cannot hear my word' (hoti ou dynasthe akouein ton logon ton emon — because you are not able to hear my word). Jesus distinguishes between lalian (speech, manner of speaking) and logon (word, message, content). They cannot understand his speech because they cannot receive his message. The inability is moral, not intellectual — their hearts are closed to divine truth.

verses.kjv_text: Ye are of your father the devil, and the lusts of your father ye will do. He was a murderer from the beginning, and abode not in the truth, because there is no truth in him. When he speaketh a lie, he speaketh of his own: for he is a liar, and the father of it.
verses.original_text: ὑμεῖς ἐκ τοῦ πατρὸς τοῦ διαβόλου ἐστὲ καὶ τὰς ἐπιθυμίας τοῦ πατρὸς ὑμῶν θέλετε ποιεῖν ἐκεῖνος ἀνθρωποκτόνος ἦν ἀπ᾽ ἀρχῆς καὶ ἐν τῇ ἀληθείᾳ οὐχ ἕστηκεν ὅτι οὐκ ἔστιν ἀλήθεια ἐν αὐτῷ ὅταν λαλῇ τὸ ψεῦδος ἐκ τῶν ἰδίων λαλεῖ ὅτι ψεύστης ἐστὶν καὶ ὁ πατὴρ αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: hymeis ek tou patros tou diabolou este kai tas epithymias tou patros hymōn thelete poiein ekeinos anthrōpoktonos ēn ap archēs kai en tē alētheia ouch hestēken hoti ouk estin alētheia en autō hotan lalē to pseudos ek tōn idiōn lalei hoti pseustēs estin kai ho patēr autou
verses.study_note: 'Ye are of your father the devil' (hymeis ek tou patros tou diabolou este — you are from the father, the devil). 'The lusts of your father ye will do' (tas epithymias tou patros hymōn thelete poiein — the desires of your father you wish to carry out). 'He was a murderer from the beginning' (anthrōpoktonos ēn ap archēs — a man-killer from the beginning; referring to Satan's role in the fall, which brought death to humanity, Genesis 3; and possibly to Cain, 1 John 3:12). 'There is no truth in him' — truth and the devil are fundamentally incompatible. 'He is a liar, and the father of it' (pseustēs estin kai ho patēr autou — he is a liar and the father of lies). Two characteristics of the devil: murder and lies. Those who seek to murder Jesus and reject his truth prove their spiritual paternity.
verses.doctrinal_note: This is the most explicit statement of satanic paternity in Scripture. Jesus does not accuse them of being demon-possessed but of belonging to Satan's family — sharing his character (lies) and his agenda (murder). The identification is based on behaviour: children resemble their father. Since the devil is characterised by murder and lies, those who seek to kill Jesus (murder) and reject his truth (lies) demonstrate that they are the devil's children, not God's. This does not mean they are beyond redemption — Paul, who once persecuted the church, was transformed by grace. But without repentance and faith, they remain in the devil's family.

verses.kjv_text: And because I tell you the truth, ye believe me not.
verses.original_text: ἐγὼ δὲ ὅτι τὴν ἀλήθειαν λέγω οὐ πιστεύετέ μοι
verses.transliteration: egō de hoti tēn alētheian legō ou pisteuete moi
verses.study_note: The paradox of unbelief: they do not believe because Jesus tells the truth. If he told them comfortable lies, they would believe. Truth offends because it exposes — and those who love darkness hate exposure (3:20). The more truthful the message, the more hostile the reception from those committed to falsehood.

verses.kjv_text: Which of you convinceth me of sin? And if I say the truth, why do ye not believe me?
verses.original_text: τίς ἐξ ὑμῶν ἐλέγχει με περὶ ἁμαρτίας εἰ δὲ ἀλήθειαν λέγω διατί ὑμεῖς οὐ πιστεύετέ μοι
verses.transliteration: tis ex hymōn elenchei me peri hamartias ei de alētheian legō diati hymeis ou pisteuete moi
verses.study_note: 'Which of you convinceth me of sin?' (tis ex hymōn elenchei me peri hamartias — who among you convicts me of sin?). An extraordinary challenge: Jesus invites his enemies to identify a single sin in him. No one responds. This is not arrogance but a unique claim to sinlessness — confirmed by the NT witness (2 Corinthians 5:21; Hebrews 4:15; 1 Peter 2:22; 1 John 3:5). If he is sinless, his testimony is trustworthy. If he speaks truth, why not believe?

verses.kjv_text: He that is of God heareth God's words: ye therefore hear them not, because ye are not of God.
verses.original_text: ὁ ὢν ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ τὰ ῥήματα τοῦ θεοῦ ἀκούει διὰ τοῦτο ὑμεῖς οὐκ ἀκούετε ὅτι ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ οὐκ ἐστέ
verses.transliteration: ho ōn ek tou theou ta rhēmata tou theou akouei dia touto hymeis ouk akouete hoti ek tou theou ouk este
verses.study_note: 'He that is of God heareth God's words' — the principle from 10:27 ('My sheep hear my voice'). 'Ye hear them not, because ye are not of God' — their inability to hear is diagnostic: it reveals their spiritual origin. Being 'of God' (ek tou theou) means being born of God, belonging to God. Those who belong to God recognise God's voice in Jesus; those who do not belong cannot hear.

verses.kjv_text: Then answered the Jews, and said unto him, Say we not well that thou art a Samaritan, and hast a devil?
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθησαν οὖν οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι καὶ εἶπον αὐτῷ Οὐ καλῶς λέγομεν ἡμεῖς ὅτι Σαμαρείτης εἶ σὺ καὶ δαιμόνιον ἔχεις
verses.transliteration: apekrithēsan oun hoi Ioudaioi kai eipon autō Ou kalōs legomen hēmeis hoti Samareitēs ei sy kai daimonion echeis
verses.study_note: 'A Samaritan' — the worst ethnic insult: Samaritans were despised as half-breeds and heretics. 'Hast a devil' (daimonion echeis — you have a demon; cf. 7:20; 10:20). Unable to refute his arguments, they resort to personal attacks. This is the pattern of defeated debate: when you cannot answer the argument, attack the person.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered, I have not a devil; but I honour my Father, and ye do dishonour me.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη Ἰησοῦς Ἐγὼ δαιμόνιον οὐκ ἔχω ἀλλὰ τιμῶ τὸν πατέρα μου καὶ ὑμεῖς ἀτιμάζετέ με
verses.transliteration: apekrithē Iēsous Egō daimonion ouk echō alla timō ton patera mou kai hymeis atimazete me
verses.study_note: Jesus ignores the 'Samaritan' slur and addresses only the demon charge: 'I have not a devil.' He contrasts his purpose (honouring the Father) with their action (dishonouring him). To dishonour the Son is to dishonour the Father (5:23).

verses.kjv_text: And I seek not mine own glory: there is one that seeketh and judgeth.
verses.original_text: ἐγὼ δὲ οὐ ζητῶ τὴν δόξαν μου ἔστιν ὁ ζητῶν καὶ κρίνων
verses.transliteration: egō de ou zētō tēn doxan mou estin ho zētōn kai krinōn
verses.study_note: 'I seek not mine own glory' — echoing 5:41 and 7:18. 'There is one that seeketh and judgeth' — the Father seeks the Son's glory and judges those who dishonour him. Jesus does not need to defend his own honour; the Father will vindicate him.

verses.kjv_text: Verily, verily, I say unto you, If a man keep my saying, he shall never see death.
verses.original_text: ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ἐάν τις τὸν ἐμὸν λόγον τηρήσῃ θάνατον οὐ μὴ θεωρήσῃ εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα
verses.transliteration: amēn amēn legō hymin ean tis ton emon logon tērēsē thanaton ou mē theōrēsē eis ton aiōna
verses.study_note: 'If a man keep my saying' (ean tis ton emon logon tērēsē — if anyone guards/keeps my word). 'He shall never see death' (thanaton ou mē theōrēsē eis ton aiōna — he will absolutely never see death, ever). 'See' (theōrēsē — contemplate, experience). Jesus does not promise immunity from physical death but freedom from the ultimate reality of death — eternal separation from God. The believer experiences physical death as a passage, not a terminus (cf. 11:25-26).

verses.kjv_text: Then said the Jews unto him, Now we know that thou hast a devil. Abraham is dead, and the prophets; and thou sayest, If a man keep my saying, he shall never taste of death.
verses.original_text: εἶπον οὖν αὐτῷ οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι Νῦν ἐγνώκαμεν ὅτι δαιμόνιον ἔχεις Ἀβραὰμ ἀπέθανεν καὶ οἱ προφῆται καὶ σὺ λέγεις Ἐάν τις τὸν λόγον μου τηρήσῃ οὐ μὴ γεύσηται θανάτου εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα
verses.transliteration: eipon oun autō hoi Ioudaioi Nyn egnōkamen hoti daimonion echeis Abraam apethanen kai hoi prophētai kai sy legeis Ean tis ton logon mou tērēsē ou mē geusētai thanatou eis ton aiōna
verses.study_note: 'Abraham is dead, and the prophets' — their objection: Abraham and the prophets kept God's word, yet they died. You claim your followers will never die — you must be mad. 'Never taste of death' (ou mē geusētai thanatou — will never experience death). They take Jesus literally; he speaks spiritually. They look at Abraham's grave; he looks at Abraham's soul.

verses.kjv_text: Art thou greater than our father Abraham, which is dead? and the prophets are dead: whom makest thou thyself?
verses.original_text: μὴ σὺ μείζων εἶ τοῦ πατρὸς ἡμῶν Ἀβραὰμ ὅστις ἀπέθανεν καὶ οἱ προφῆται ἀπέθανον τίνα σεαυτὸν σὺ ποιεῖς
verses.transliteration: mē sy meizōn ei tou patros hēmōn Abraam hostis apethanen kai hoi prophētai apethanon tina seauton sy poieis
verses.study_note: 'Art thou greater than our father Abraham?' (mē sy meizōn ei — surely you are not greater?). 'Whom makest thou thyself?' (tina seauton sy poieis — who do you make yourself out to be?). The ironic question: the implied answer is 'no,' but the true answer is 'infinitely greater.' Jesus will answer this question definitively in v.58.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered, If I honour myself, my honour is nothing: it is my Father that honoureth me; of whom ye say, that he is your God:
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη Ἰησοῦς Ἐὰν ἐγὼ δοξάζω ἐμαυτόν ἡ δόξα μου οὐδέν ἐστιν ἔστιν ὁ πατήρ μου ὁ δοξάζων με ὃν ὑμεῖς λέγετε ὅτι θεὸς ὑμῶν ἐστιν
verses.transliteration: apekrithē Iēsous Ean egō doxazō emauton hē doxa mou ouden estin estin ho patēr mou ho doxazōn me hon hymeis legete hoti theos hymōn estin
verses.study_note: 'If I honour myself, my honour is nothing' — self-glorification is empty. 'It is my Father that honoureth me' — Jesus' glory comes from the Father. 'Of whom ye say, that he is your God' — they claim God as their God, but if they truly knew God they would honour the one God honours. The disconnect is total: they claim God but reject God's Son.

verses.kjv_text: Yet ye have not known him; but I know him: and if I should say, I know him not, I shall be a liar like unto you: but I know him, and keep his saying.
verses.original_text: καὶ οὐκ ἐγνώκατε αὐτόν ἐγὼ δὲ οἶδα αὐτόν κἂν εἴπω ὅτι οὐκ οἶδα αὐτόν ἔσομαι ὅμοιος ὑμῶν ψεύστης ἀλλ᾽ οἶδα αὐτὸν καὶ τὸν λόγον αὐτοῦ τηρῶ
verses.transliteration: kai ouk egnōkate auton egō de oida auton kan eipō hoti ouk oida auton esomai homoios hymōn pseustēs all oida auton kai ton logon autou tērō
verses.study_note: 'Ye have not known him' (ouk egnōkate auton — you have not come to know him; perfect tense: you still do not know him). 'I know him' (oida auton — I know him with settled, certain, immediate knowledge). 'If I should say, I know him not, I shall be a liar like unto you' — the word 'like unto you' (homoios hymōn) is devastating: you are liars. The only way for Jesus to fit into their paradigm would be for him to lie about knowing God — and he refuses.

verses.kjv_text: Your father Abraham rejoiced to see my day: and he saw it, and was glad.
verses.original_text: Ἀβραὰμ ὁ πατὴρ ὑμῶν ἠγαλλιάσατο ἵνα ἴδῃ τὴν ἡμέραν τὴν ἐμήν καὶ εἶδεν καὶ ἐχάρη
verses.transliteration: Abraam ho patēr hymōn ēgalliasato hina idē tēn hēmeran tēn emēn kai eiden kai echarē
verses.study_note: 'Abraham rejoiced to see my day' (ēgalliasato hina idē tēn hēmeran tēn emēn — leaped for joy at the prospect of seeing my day). 'He saw it, and was glad' (eiden kai echarē — he saw and rejoiced). When did Abraham see Jesus' day? Possibilities: (1) The promise of the seed (Genesis 12:3; 22:18). (2) The birth of Isaac, the son of promise (Genesis 21:1-6; Hebrews 11:17-19). (3) A direct prophetic revelation. (4) From Paradise/heaven, Abraham sees the fulfilment. The point: Abraham, whom they claim as father, saw Jesus' day and rejoiced. They see Jesus in person and want to kill him.

verses.kjv_text: Then said the Jews unto him, Thou art not yet fifty years old, and hast thou seen Abraham?
verses.original_text: εἶπον οὖν οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι πρὸς αὐτόν Πεντήκοντα ἔτη οὔπω ἔχεις καὶ Ἀβραὰμ ἑώρακας
verses.transliteration: eipon oun hoi Ioudaioi pros auton Pentēkonta etē oupō echeis kai Abraam heōrakas
verses.study_note: 'Not yet fifty years old' (pentēkonta etē oupō echeis — you do not yet have fifty years). Jesus was about 33; they round up to an obviously implausible maximum. 'Hast thou seen Abraham?' — they reverse Jesus' statement: he said Abraham saw 'my day'; they ask if Jesus has seen Abraham. Both misunderstanding and scorn.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus said unto them, Verily, verily, I say unto you, Before Abraham was, I am.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν πρὶν Ἀβραὰμ γενέσθαι ἐγὼ εἰμί
verses.transliteration: eipen autois ho Iēsous Amēn amēn legō hymin prin Abraam genesthai egō eimi
verses.study_note: 'Before Abraham was' (prin Abraam genesthai — before Abraham came into being; genesthai: aorist of ginomai — to come into existence, to be born). 'I am' (egō eimi — I AM; present tense, not 'I was'). The contrast is deliberate: Abraham 'came into being' (genesthai — a point in time) but Jesus 'I am' (eimi — continuous, timeless existence). Jesus does not say 'Before Abraham was, I was' (which would claim merely pre-existence) but 'I am' (which claims eternal, timeless being — the divine name from Exodus 3:14). This is the supreme christological claim in the Gospel of John.
verses.doctrinal_note: This is the climactic 'I AM' of John's Gospel — the absolute, unqualified use of the divine name. The Jews understand perfectly: (1) Jesus claims to have existed before Abraham (c. 2000 BC). (2) He uses the present tense 'I am,' claiming not just pre-existence but eternal, timeless self-existence. (3) He deliberately echoes the divine name (Exodus 3:14: 'I AM THAT I AM'). Their immediate response — picking up stones to kill him (v.59) — confirms they understood this as a claim to deity, which they considered blasphemy (Leviticus 24:16). If Jesus is not God, this is the most outrageous claim in human history. If he is God, it is the most important truth ever spoken.

verses.kjv_text: Then took they up stones to cast at him: but Jesus hid himself, and went out of the temple, going through the midst of them, and so passed by.
verses.original_text: ἦραν οὖν λίθους ἵνα βάλωσιν ἐπ᾽ αὐτόν Ἰησοῦς δὲ ἐκρύβη καὶ ἐξῆλθεν ἐκ τοῦ ἱεροῦ διελθὼν διὰ μέσου αὐτῶν καὶ παρῆγεν οὕτως
verses.transliteration: ēran oun lithous hina balōsin ep auton Iēsous de ekrybē kai exēlthen ek tou hierou dielthōn dia mesou autōn kai parēgen houtōs
verses.study_note: 'Took they up stones' (ēran lithous — they picked up stones). In the temple courts, construction materials for ongoing renovation of Herod's temple were available. Stoning was the penalty for blasphemy (Leviticus 24:16). 'Jesus hid himself' (Iēsous ekrybē — Jesus was hidden; the passive may suggest divine concealment). 'Going through the midst of them' — a miraculous departure, as in Luke 4:30. His hour had not yet come (cf. 7:30; 8:20). He cannot be killed until the appointed time.

meta.overview: John 9 is one of the masterfully constructed narratives in the Gospel — a dramatic story of physical healing and spiritual awakening set against the backdrop of religious blindness. Jesus encounters a man blind from birth and declares: 'Neither hath this man sinned, nor his parents: but that the works of God should be made manifest in him' (v.3). He anoints the man's eyes with clay and sends him to wash in the Pool of Siloam: 'He went his way therefore, and washed, and came seeing' (v.7). The miracle provokes a series of interrogations — by neighbours (vv.8-12), by the Pharisees (vv.13-17), by the Jewish authorities who summon his parents (vv.18-23), and by the Pharisees again (vv.24-34). Through each interrogation, the healed man grows bolder in his testimony: first calling Jesus 'a man called Jesus' (v.11), then 'a prophet' (v.17), then defending him as one 'of God' (v.33). Meanwhile, the Pharisees grow increasingly hostile, excommunicating the man for testifying (v.34). Jesus seeks out the expelled man and reveals himself: 'Dost thou believe on the Son of God?... Thou hast both seen him, and it is he that talketh with thee' (vv.35-37). The man responds with worship: 'Lord, I believe. And he worshipped him' (v.38). The chapter closes with Jesus' pronouncement of judgment: 'For judgment I am come into this world, that they which see not might see; and that they which see might be made blind' (v.39). The Pharisees ask, 'Are we blind also?' Jesus replies: 'If ye were blind, ye should have no sin: but now ye say, We see; therefore your sin remaineth' (v.41). The supreme irony: the blind man sees; the seeing are blind.
meta.theme: Sight and Blindness — Physical Healing and Spiritual Awakening
meta.keyWordMeaning: Blind, unable to see, without sight — both physically and metaphorically. In John 9, typhlos operates on two levels: the man is physically blind from birth and receives physical sight; the Pharisees are spiritually blind and refuse to see. The physical miracle illustrates the spiritual reality: Jesus opens blind eyes — both literal and metaphorical. The healed man progressively 'sees' who Jesus is (man → prophet → from God → Son of God → Lord); the Pharisees progressively 'blind' themselves (investigating → denying → excommunicating → claiming to see while remaining blind). Typhlos in the NT: Matthew 15:14 ('blind leaders of the blind'), 2 Corinthians 4:4 ('the god of this world hath blinded the minds'), Revelation 3:17 ('thou art blind, and seest not').
outline: The Healing (vv.1-7): As Jesus passed by he saw a man which was blind from his birth. His disciples asked saying Master who did sin this man or his parents that he was born blind? Jesus answered Neither hath this man sinned nor his parents but that the works of God should be made manifest in him. I must work the works of him that sent me while it is day the night cometh when no man can work. As long as I am in the world I am the light of the world. When he had thus spoken he spat on the ground and made clay of the spittle and he anointed the eyes of the blind man with the clay And said unto him Go wash in the pool of Siloam which is by interpretation Sent. He went his way therefore and washed and came seeing

The Neighbours' Investigation (vv.8-12): The neighbours therefore and they which before had seen him that he was blind said Is not this he that sat and begged? Some said This is he others said He is like him but he said I am he. Therefore said they unto him How were thine eyes opened? He answered and said A man that is called Jesus made clay and anointed mine eyes and said unto me Go to the pool of Siloam and wash and I went and washed and I received sight. Then said they unto him Where is he? He said I know not

The First Pharisee Interrogation (vv.13-17): They brought to the Pharisees him that aforetime was blind. And it was the sabbath day when Jesus made the clay and opened his eyes. Then again the Pharisees also asked him how he had received his sight. He said unto them He put clay upon mine eyes and I washed and do see. Therefore said some of the Pharisees This man is not of God because he keepeth not the sabbath day. Others said How can a man that is a sinner do such miracles? And there was a division among them. They say unto the blind man again What sayest thou of him that he hath opened thine eyes? He said He is a prophet

The Parents' Interrogation (vv.18-23): But the Jews did not believe concerning him that he had been blind and received his sight until they called the parents of him that had received his sight. And they asked them saying Is this your son who ye say was born blind? how then doth he now see? His parents answered and said We know that this is our son and that he was born blind. But by what means he now seeth we know not or who hath opened his eyes we know not he is of age ask him he shall speak for himself. These words spake his parents because they feared the Jews for the Jews had agreed already that if any man did confess that he was Christ he should be put out of the synagogue. Therefore said his parents He is of age ask him

The Second Pharisee Interrogation and Expulsion (vv.24-34): Then again called they the man that was blind and said unto him Give God the praise we know that this man is a sinner. He answered and said Whether he be a sinner or no I know not one thing I know that whereas I was blind now I see. Then said they to him again What did he to thee? how opened he thine eyes? He answered them I have told you already and ye did not hear wherefore would ye hear it again? will ye also be his disciples? Then they reviled him and said Thou art his disciple but we are Moses' disciples. We know that God spake unto Moses as for this fellow we know not from whence he is. The man answered and said unto them Why herein is a marvellous thing that ye know not from whence he is and yet he hath opened mine eyes. Now we know that God heareth not sinners but if any man be a worshipper of God and doeth his will him he heareth. Since the world began was it not heard that any man opened the eyes of one that was born blind. If this man were not of God he could do nothing. They answered and said unto him Thou wast altogether born in sins and dost thou teach us? And they cast him out

Jesus Reveals Himself (vv.35-38): Jesus heard that they had cast him out and when he had found him he said unto him Dost thou believe on the Son of God? He answered and said Who is he Lord that I might believe on him? And Jesus said unto him Thou hast both seen him and it is he that talketh with thee. And he said Lord I believe. And he worshipped him

Spiritual Blindness Judged (vv.39-41): And Jesus said For judgment I am come into this world that they which see not might see and that they which see might be made blind. And some of the Pharisees which were with him heard these words and said unto him Are we blind also? Jesus said unto them If ye were blind ye should have no sin but now ye say We see therefore your sin remaineth

verses.kjv_text: And as Jesus passed by, he saw a man which was blind from his birth.
verses.original_text: καὶ παράγων εἶδεν ἄνθρωπον τυφλὸν ἐκ γενετῆς
verses.transliteration: kai paragōn eiden anthrōpon typhlon ek genetēs
verses.study_note: 'As Jesus passed by' (paragōn — passing along; the narrative continues from 8:59 where Jesus left the temple). 'Blind from his birth' (typhlon ek genetēs — blind from birth). This is significant: the man was not merely ill but had never seen — no deterioration to reverse, no memory of sight to restore. He needed entirely new sight, creation rather than repair. This is the sixth sign in John and the most medically extraordinary — no case of congenital blindness being cured was known in the ancient world.

verses.kjv_text: And his disciples asked him, saying, Master, who did sin, this man, or his parents, that he was born blind?
verses.original_text: καὶ ἠρώτησαν αὐτὸν οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ λέγοντες Ῥαββί τίς ἥμαρτεν οὗτος ἢ οἱ γονεῖς αὐτοῦ ἵνα τυφλὸς γεννηθῇ
verses.transliteration: kai ērōtēsan auton hoi mathētai autou legontes Rhabbi tis hēmarten houtos ē hoi goneis autou hina typhlos gennēthē
verses.study_note: The disciples assume a direct link between sin and suffering — a common ancient view. 'Who did sin, this man, or his parents?' The question presents a false dilemma: if the man sinned (perhaps in the womb — some Jewish teachers held this was possible), or if his parents sinned and he bears the consequence. Both options assume suffering must be punishment for specific sin.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered, Neither hath this man sinned, nor his parents: but that the works of God should be made manifest in him.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη ὁ Ἰησοῦς Οὔτε οὗτος ἥμαρτεν οὔτε οἱ γονεῖς αὐτοῦ ἀλλ᾽ ἵνα φανερωθῇ τὰ ἔργα τοῦ θεοῦ ἐν αὐτῷ
verses.transliteration: apekrithē ho Iēsous Oute houtos hēmarten oute hoi goneis autou all hina phanerōthē ta erga tou theou en autō
verses.study_note: 'Neither hath this man sinned, nor his parents' — Jesus rejects both options. This does not mean they were sinless (all have sinned, Romans 3:23) but that this specific blindness was not punishment for a specific sin. 'That the works of God should be made manifest in him' (hina phanerōthē ta erga tou theou — so that the works of God might be displayed). The purpose of the suffering is not retribution but revelation. God's glory can be displayed in and through human suffering — not because suffering is good but because God is sovereign over it.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse overturns simplistic theology that equates all suffering with divine punishment. While some suffering results from sin (cf. 5:14), much suffering serves God's redemptive purposes. Job suffered without personal cause; Paul's thorn was for his benefit (2 Corinthians 12:7-10). The blind man's lifelong darkness was not wasted — it became the canvas on which God painted his glory. This does not trivialise suffering but transforms its meaning: suffering in God's hands becomes an occasion for divine revelation.

verses.kjv_text: I must work the works of him that sent me, while it is day: the night cometh, when no man can work.
verses.original_text: ἐμὲ δεῖ ἐργάζεσθαι τὰ ἔργα τοῦ πέμψαντός με ἕως ἡμέρα ἐστίν ἔρχεται νὺξ ὅτε οὐδεὶς δύναται ἐργάζεσθαι
verses.transliteration: eme dei ergazesthai ta erga tou pempsantos me heōs hēmera estin erchetai nyx hote oudeis dynatai ergazesthai
verses.study_note: 'I must work' (eme dei — it is necessary for me; dei: divine necessity). 'While it is day' — Jesus' earthly ministry is the 'day' for his work; the 'night' is his departure through death. There is urgency: the time for work is limited. 'No man can work' when the night comes — not that believers will be unable to serve, but that Jesus' personal, incarnate ministry has a bounded timeframe.

verses.kjv_text: As long as I am in the world, I am the light of the world.
verses.original_text: ὅταν ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ ὦ φῶς εἰμι τοῦ κόσμου
verses.transliteration: hotan en tō kosmō ō phōs eimi tou kosmou
verses.study_note: 'I am the light of the world' — repeated from 8:12. Here, the claim is immediately demonstrated by giving light to a man born in darkness. The one who claims to be Light proves it by creating sight where none existed. The miracle is a visual parable of the spiritual reality: Jesus opens blind eyes, both physical and spiritual.

verses.kjv_text: When he had thus spoken, he spat on the ground, and made clay of the spittle, and he anointed the eyes of the blind man with the clay,
verses.original_text: ταῦτα εἰπὼν ἔπτυσεν χαμαί καὶ ἐποίησεν πηλὸν ἐκ τοῦ πτύσματος καὶ ἐπέχρισεν τὸν πηλὸν ἐπὶ τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς τοῦ τυφλοῦ
verses.transliteration: tauta eipōn eptusen chamai kai epoiēsen pēlon ek tou ptysmatos kai epechrisen ton pēlon epi tous ophthalmous tou typhlou
verses.study_note: 'Spat on the ground, and made clay' (eptusen chamai kai epoiēsen pēlon — he spat on the earth and made mud). The use of clay (pēlon) evokes Genesis 2:7 — God formed man from the dust of the ground. Jesus, the Creator (1:3), uses the same medium to create sight. The act is deliberately provocative: making clay on the Sabbath was explicitly prohibited by rabbinic law (Shabbat 24:3). Jesus intentionally violates their tradition to demonstrate divine authority over the Sabbath.

verses.kjv_text: And said unto him, Go, wash in the pool of Siloam, (which is by interpretation, Sent.) He went his way therefore, and washed, and came seeing.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ Ὕπαγε νίψαι εἰς τὴν κολυμβήθραν τοῦ Σιλωάμ ὃ ἑρμηνεύεται Ἀπεσταλμένος ἀπῆλθεν οὖν καὶ ἐνίψατο καὶ ἦλθεν βλέπων
verses.transliteration: kai eipen autō Hypage nipsai eis tēn kolymbēthran tou Silōam ho hermēneuetai Apestalmenos apēlthen oun kai enipsato kai ēlthen blepōn
verses.study_note: 'The pool of Siloam' (tēn kolymbēthran tou Silōam) — archaeologically confirmed; discovered in 2004, a large stepped pool at the southern end of the City of David. 'Which is by interpretation, Sent' (Apestalmenos) — John provides the translation because it is theologically significant: Siloam means 'Sent.' Jesus is the One sent by the Father (3:17, 34; 5:36; 6:29; 7:29). The man washes in the pool of the Sent One and receives sight. 'He went... washed... came seeing' — three decisive actions of obedient faith. He obeyed before seeing the result, like the man at Bethesda (5:8-9).

verses.kjv_text: The neighbours therefore, and they which before had seen him that he was blind, said, Is not this he that sat and begged?
verses.original_text: οἱ οὖν γείτονες καὶ οἱ θεωροῦντες αὐτὸν τὸ πρότερον ὅτι τυφλὸς ἦν ἔλεγον Οὐχ οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ καθήμενος καὶ προσαιτῶν
verses.transliteration: hoi oun geitones kai hoi theōrountes auton to proteron hoti typhlos ēn elegon Ouch houtos estin ho kathēmenos kai prosaitōn
verses.study_note: The neighbours recognise the man but are unsure — the transformation is so dramatic that some question his identity. The fact that he was a well-known beggar confirms the miracle's public nature and the man's long-standing blindness. The healing is too radical to ignore.

verses.kjv_text: Some said, This is he: others said, He is like him: but he said, I am he.
verses.original_text: ἄλλοι ἔλεγον ὅτι Οὗτός ἐστιν ἄλλοι δὲ ὅτι Ὅμοιος αὐτῷ ἐστιν ἐκεῖνος ἔλεγεν ὅτι Ἐγώ εἰμι
verses.transliteration: alloi elegon hoti Houtos estin alloi de hoti Homoios autō estin ekeinos elegen hoti Egō eimi
verses.study_note: 'I am he' (egō eimi — I am). The healed man's self-identification uses the same phrase Jesus uses throughout the Gospel. The former blind man bears witness to his own transformation — 'I am the one; I am changed.' His testimony begins here and will grow in boldness through the chapter.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore said they unto him, How were thine eyes opened?
verses.original_text: ἔλεγον οὖν αὐτῷ Πῶς ἀνεῴχθησάν σου οἱ ὀφθαλμοί
verses.transliteration: elegon oun autō Pōs aneōchthēsan sou hoi ophthalmoi
verses.study_note: 'How were thine eyes opened?' (pōs aneōchthēsan sou hoi ophthalmoi — how were your eyes opened?). The question of 'how' will dominate the chapter. The neighbours ask how (v.10), the Pharisees ask how (v.15, 26), the parents are asked how (v.19). Everyone wants to know the mechanism; Jesus is interested in the meaning.

verses.kjv_text: He answered and said, A man that is called Jesus made clay, and anointed mine eyes, and said unto me, Go to the pool of Siloam, and wash: and I went and washed, and I received sight.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη ἐκεῖνος καὶ εἶπεν Ἄνθρωπος λεγόμενος Ἰησοῦς πηλὸν ἐποίησεν καὶ ἐπέχρισέν μου τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς καὶ εἶπέν μοι Ὕπαγε εἰς τὴν κολυμβήθραν τοῦ Σιλωὰμ καὶ νίψαι ἀπελθὼν δὲ καὶ νιψάμενος ἀνέβλεψα
verses.transliteration: apekrithē ekeinos kai eipen Anthrōpos legomenos Iēsous pēlon epoiēsen kai epechrise mou tous ophthalmous kai eipen moi Hypage eis tēn kolymbēthran tou Silōam kai nipsai apelthōn de kai nipsamenos aneblepsa
verses.study_note: 'A man that is called Jesus' (anthrōpos legomenos Iēsous — a man called Jesus). The healed man's first description of Jesus: 'a man.' He does not yet know who Jesus truly is — his understanding will grow progressively: a man (v.11) → a prophet (v.17) → from God (v.33) → Son of God / Lord (vv.35-38). This progression from minimal knowledge to full faith mirrors the journey of every believer.

verses.kjv_text: Then said they unto him, Where is he? He said, I know not.
verses.original_text: εἶπον οὖν αὐτῷ Ποῦ ἐστιν ἐκεῖνος λέγει Οὐκ οἶδα
verses.transliteration: eipon oun autō Pou estin ekeinos legei Ouk oida
verses.study_note: 'Where is he? I know not' — the man cannot locate Jesus. Jesus healed him and sent him away; they have not met face to face with the man seeing. This absence of Jesus from the scene is important: the man must testify alone, without Jesus' visible support. His faith grows through testimony, not through Jesus' physical presence.

verses.kjv_text: They brought to the Pharisees him that aforetime was blind.
verses.original_text: ἄγουσιν αὐτὸν πρὸς τοὺς Φαρισαίους τόν ποτε τυφλόν
verses.transliteration: agousin auton pros tous Pharisaious ton pote typhlon
verses.study_note: 'Him that aforetime was blind' (ton pote typhlon — the formerly blind one). The neighbours bring the case to the Pharisees — the religious authorities — because a Sabbath controversy is involved. The investigation that follows (vv.13-34) is essentially a trial: the Pharisees serve as prosecutors, the man as the accused-turned-witness.

verses.kjv_text: And it was the sabbath day when Jesus made the clay, and opened his eyes.
verses.original_text: ἦν δὲ σάββατον ὅτε τὸν πηλὸν ἐποίησεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ ἀνέῳξεν αὐτοῦ τοὺς ὀφθαλμούς
verses.transliteration: ēn de sabbaton hote ton pēlon epoiēsen ho Iēsous kai aneōxen autou tous ophthalmous
verses.study_note: 'It was the sabbath day' — John places this detail strategically, as in 5:9. Jesus deliberately healed on the Sabbath — this is the third Sabbath confrontation in John (5:9-18; 7:21-24; 9:14-16). Making clay (kneading) was specifically listed among the 39 prohibited labours. Jesus is not accidentally breaking rules; he is purposefully challenging a system that values regulations over people.

verses.kjv_text: Then again the Pharisees also asked him how he had received his sight. He said unto them, He put clay upon mine eyes, and I washed, and do see.
verses.original_text: πάλιν οὖν ἠρώτων αὐτὸν καὶ οἱ Φαρισαῖοι πῶς ἀνέβλεψεν ὁ δὲ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς Πηλὸν ἐπέθηκέν μου ἐπὶ τοὺς ὀφθαλμούς καὶ ἐνιψάμην καὶ βλέπω
verses.transliteration: palin oun ērōtōn auton kai hoi Pharisaioi pōs aneblepsen ho de eipen autois Pēlon epethēken mou epi tous ophthalmous kai enipsamēn kai blepō
verses.study_note: The man's account becomes terser with each retelling: in v.11, a detailed narrative; here, a three-word summary: 'clay... washed... see.' He is growing impatient with repetitive questioning — a natural response that reveals his straightforward honesty.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore said some of the Pharisees, This man is not of God, because he keepeth not the sabbath day. Others said, How can a man that is a sinner do such miracles? And there was a division among them.
verses.original_text: ἔλεγον οὖν ἐκ τῶν Φαρισαίων τινές Οὗτος ὁ ἄνθρωπος οὐκ ἔστιν παρὰ θεοῦ ὅτι τὸ σάββατον οὐ τηρεῖ ἄλλοι ἔλεγον Πῶς δύναται ἄνθρωπος ἁμαρτωλὸς τοιαῦτα σημεῖα ποιεῖν καὶ σχίσμα ἦν ἐν αὐτοῖς
verses.transliteration: elegon oun ek tōn Pharisaiōn tines Houtos ho anthrōpos ouk estin para theou hoti to sabbaton ou tērei alloi elegon Pōs dynatai anthrōpos hamartōlos toiauta sēmeia poiein kai schisma ēn en autois
verses.study_note: 'This man is not of God' (ouk estin para theou — he is not from God) — because he breaks the Sabbath. 'How can a man that is a sinner do such miracles?' — the counter-argument: sinners cannot perform divine signs. A schisma (division) arises among the Pharisees themselves. The evidence (miracle) conflicts with their theology (Sabbath rules). Rather than adjusting their theology, most of them reject the evidence.

verses.kjv_text: They say unto the blind man again, What sayest thou of him, that he hath opened thine eyes? He said, He is a prophet.
verses.original_text: λέγουσιν τῷ τυφλῷ πάλιν Σὺ τί λέγεις περὶ αὐτοῦ ὅτι ἤνοιξέν σου τοὺς ὀφθαλμούς ὁ δὲ εἶπεν ὅτι Προφήτης ἐστίν
verses.transliteration: legousin tō typhlō palin Sy ti legeis peri autou hoti ēnoixen sou tous ophthalmous ho de eipen hoti Prophētēs estin
verses.study_note: 'He is a prophet' (prophētēs estin). The man's second identification of Jesus — an upgrade from 'a man' (v.11) to 'a prophet.' A prophet speaks and acts with God's authority; the healing proves Jesus has that authority. The man's understanding is growing through his experience and through the pressure of interrogation.

verses.kjv_text: But the Jews did not believe concerning him, that he had been blind, and received his sight, until they called the parents of him that had received his sight.
verses.original_text: οὐκ ἐπίστευσαν οὖν οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι περὶ αὐτοῦ ὅτι τυφλὸς ἦν καὶ ἀνέβλεψεν ἕως ὅτου ἐφώνησαν τοὺς γονεῖς αὐτοῦ τοῦ ἀναβλέψαντος
verses.transliteration: ouk episteusan oun hoi Ioudaioi peri autou hoti typhlos ēn kai aneblepsen heōs hotou ephōnēsan tous goneis autou tou anablepsantos
verses.study_note: 'Did not believe' (ouk episteusan — they refused to believe). Unable to deny the miracle, they attack its premise: perhaps he was never really blind. This is the last resort of determined unbelief — denial of facts. They summon the parents as witnesses to verify the original blindness.

verses.kjv_text: And they asked them, saying, Is this your son, who ye say was born blind? how then doth he now see?
verses.original_text: καὶ ἠρώτησαν αὐτοὺς λέγοντες Οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ υἱὸς ὑμῶν ὃν ὑμεῖς λέγετε ὅτι τυφλὸς ἐγεννήθη πῶς οὖν ἄρτι βλέπει
verses.transliteration: kai ērōtēsan autous legontes Houtos estin ho huios hymōn hon hymeis legete hoti typhlos egennēthē pōs oun arti blepei
verses.study_note: Three questions in one: (1) Is this your son? (2) Do you confirm he was born blind? (3) How does he now see? The phrasing 'who ye say was born blind' implies scepticism about the parents' claim. The authorities are looking for any way to invalidate the miracle.

verses.kjv_text: His parents answered them and said, We know that this is our son, and that he was born blind:
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθησαν αὐτοῖς οἱ γονεῖς αὐτοῦ καὶ εἶπον Οἴδαμεν ὅτι οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ υἱὸς ἡμῶν καὶ ὅτι τυφλὸς ἐγεννήθη
verses.transliteration: apekrithēsan autois hoi goneis autou kai eipon Oidamen hoti houtos estin ho huios hēmōn kai hoti typhlos egennēthē
verses.study_note: The parents confirm two facts: (1) he is their son, (2) he was born blind. These they know with certainty (oidamen — we know). But on the question of 'how' he now sees, they deflect.

verses.kjv_text: But by what means he now seeth, we know not; or who hath opened his eyes, we know not: he is of age; ask him: he shall speak for himself.
verses.original_text: πῶς δὲ νῦν βλέπει οὐκ οἴδαμεν ἢ τίς ἤνοιξεν αὐτοῦ τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς ἡμεῖς οὐκ οἴδαμεν αὐτὸν ἐρωτήσατε ἡλικίαν ἔχει αὐτὸς περὶ αὐτοῦ λαλήσει
verses.transliteration: pōs de nyn blepei ouk oidamen ē tis ēnoixen autou tous ophthalmous hēmeis ouk oidamen auton erōtēsate hēlikian echei autos peri autou lalēsei
verses.study_note: 'We know not... we know not' — the parents deliberately distance themselves from the miracle and from Jesus. 'He is of age; ask him' — a legal deflection: as an adult, he can testify for himself. Their evasion is explained in vv.22-23.

verses.kjv_text: These words spake his parents, because they feared the Jews: for the Jews had agreed already, that if any man did confess that he was Christ, he should be put out of the synagogue.
verses.original_text: ταῦτα εἶπον οἱ γονεῖς αὐτοῦ ὅτι ἐφοβοῦντο τοὺς Ἰουδαίους ἤδη γὰρ συνετέθειντο οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι ἵνα ἐάν τις αὐτὸν ὁμολογήσῃ Χριστόν ἀποσυνάγωγος γένηται
verses.transliteration: tauta eipon hoi goneis autou hoti ephobounto tous Ioudaious ēdē gar synetethento hoi Ioudaioi hina ean tis auton homologēsē Christon aposynagōgos genētai
verses.study_note: 'They feared the Jews' (ephobounto tous Ioudaious — they were afraid of the Jewish authorities). 'Put out of the synagogue' (aposynagōgos genētai — become expelled from the synagogue). Excommunication meant social, economic, and religious death in Jewish society — loss of worship, community, business relationships, and social standing. The threat was real and effective: fear silenced the parents' testimony. The cost of confessing Christ was (and still is) high.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore said his parents, He is of age; ask him.
verses.original_text: διὰ τοῦτο οἱ γονεῖς αὐτοῦ εἶπον ὅτι Ἡλικίαν ἔχει αὐτὸν ἐρωτήσατε
verses.transliteration: dia touto hoi goneis autou eipon hoti Hēlikian echei auton erōtēsate
verses.study_note: The parents' fear-driven deflection contrasts sharply with their son's growing boldness. The son has nothing to lose (he was a beggar); the parents have much to lose (their synagogue membership). Fear of man and faith in Christ are inversely proportional.

verses.kjv_text: Then again called they the man that was blind, and said unto him, Give God the praise: we know that this man is a sinner.
verses.original_text: ἐφώνησαν οὖν ἐκ δευτέρου τὸν ἄνθρωπον ὃς ἦν τυφλὸς καὶ εἶπον αὐτῷ Δὸς δόξαν τῷ θεῷ ἡμεῖς οἴδαμεν ὅτι ὁ ἄνθρωπος οὗτος ἁμαρτωλός ἐστιν
verses.transliteration: ephōnēsan oun ek deuterou ton anthrōpon hos ēn typhlos kai eipon autō Dos doxan tō theō hēmeis oidamen hoti ho anthrōpos houtos hamartōlos estin
verses.study_note: 'Give God the praise' (dos doxan tō theō — give glory to God). This is not an invitation to worship but a legal formula demanding truthful testimony under oath (cf. Joshua 7:19 where Joshua says to Achan: 'Give glory to God and tell the truth'). 'We know that this man is a sinner' — they have reached their verdict before hearing testimony. Their 'knowledge' is predetermined and evidence-proof.

verses.kjv_text: He answered and said, Whether he be a sinner or no, I know not: one thing I know, that, whereas I was blind, now I see.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη οὖν ἐκεῖνος καὶ εἶπεν Εἰ ἁμαρτωλός ἐστιν οὐκ οἶδα ἓν οἶδα ὅτι τυφλὸς ὢν ἄρτι βλέπω
verses.transliteration: apekrithē oun ekeinos kai eipen Ei hamartōlos estin ouk oida hen oida hoti typhlos ōn arti blepō
verses.study_note: 'One thing I know' (hen oida — one thing I know). 'Whereas I was blind, now I see' (typhlos ōn arti blepō — being blind, now I see). One of the most powerful personal testimonies in Scripture. The man does not argue theology — he states experience. He cannot be cross-examined out of his own transformation. This is the unanswerable argument for Christ: changed lives. The religious experts have theology; the healed man has transformation.

verses.kjv_text: Then said they to him again, What did he to thee? how opened he thine eyes?
verses.original_text: εἶπον δὲ αὐτῷ πάλιν Τί ἐποίησέν σοι πῶς ἤνοιξέν σου τοὺς ὀφθαλμούς
verses.transliteration: eipon de autō palin Ti epoiēsen soi pōs ēnoixen sou tous ophthalmous
verses.study_note: They ask again — hoping to find an inconsistency in his testimony. The repetition reveals their frustration: the evidence keeps pointing to the same conclusion they refuse to accept.

verses.kjv_text: He answered them, I have told you already, and ye did not hear: wherefore would ye hear it again? will ye also be his disciples?
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη αὐτοῖς Εἶπον ὑμῖν ἤδη καὶ οὐκ ἠκούσατε τί πάλιν θέλετε ἀκούειν μὴ καὶ ὑμεῖς θέλετε αὐτοῦ μαθηταὶ γενέσθαι
verses.transliteration: apekrithē autois Eipon hymin ēdē kai ouk ēkousate ti palin thelete akouein mē kai hymeis thelete autou mathētai genesthai
verses.study_note: 'I have told you already, and ye did not hear' — the man shows growing boldness and even sarcasm. 'Will ye also be his disciples?' (mē kai hymeis thelete autou mathētai genesthai — surely you don't want to become his disciples too?). The question with mē expects a negative answer, but the 'also' (kai) implies the man considers himself a disciple. Under pressure, his faith has solidified into identification with Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: Then they reviled him, and said, Thou art his disciple; but we are Moses' disciples.
verses.original_text: ἐλοιδόρησαν αὐτὸν καὶ εἶπον Σὺ εἶ μαθητὴς ἐκείνου ἡμεῖς δὲ τοῦ Μωϋσέως ἐσμὲν μαθηταί
verses.transliteration: eloidorēsan auton kai eipon Sy ei mathētēs ekeinou hēmeis de tou Mōyseōs esmen mathētai
verses.study_note: 'They reviled him' (eloidorēsan — they insulted, abused, mocked him). 'Thou art his disciple; but we are Moses' disciples' — they draw the line: you follow Jesus; we follow Moses. The irony: Jesus and Moses are not in opposition (5:46: 'Had ye believed Moses, ye would have believed me: for he wrote of me'). To truly follow Moses leads to following Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: We know that God spake unto Moses: as for this fellow, we know not from whence he is.
verses.original_text: ἡμεῖς οἴδαμεν ὅτι Μωϋσεῖ λελάληκεν ὁ θεός τοῦτον δὲ οὐκ οἴδαμεν πόθεν ἐστίν
verses.transliteration: hēmeis oidamen hoti Mōysei lelalēken ho theos touton de ouk oidamen pothen estin
verses.study_note: 'We know that God spake unto Moses' — this is true (Exodus 33:11). 'We know not from whence he is' (ouk oidamen pothen estin — we don't know where he comes from). This is the same objection as 7:27, but now deployed as a reason to reject the miracle. Their 'we know/we know not' is actually inverted: they think they know Moses but have missed his message; they claim not to know Jesus but should — his works testify to his origin.

verses.kjv_text: The man answered and said unto them, Why herein is a marvellous thing, that ye know not from whence he is, and yet he hath opened mine eyes.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη ὁ ἄνθρωπος καὶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς Ἐν γὰρ τούτῳ θαυμαστόν ἐστιν ὅτι ὑμεῖς οὐκ οἴδατε πόθεν ἐστίν καὶ ἀνέῳξέν μου τοὺς ὀφθαλμούς
verses.transliteration: apekrithē ho anthrōpos kai eipen autois En gar toutō thaumaston estin hoti hymeis ouk oidate pothen estin kai aneōxen mou tous ophthalmous
verses.study_note: 'Herein is a marvellous thing' (en toutō thaumaston estin — in this is an astonishing thing). The formerly blind beggar turns theologian: the truly marvellous thing is not the miracle but the Pharisees' ignorance. You, the religious experts, cannot determine the origin of a man who performs unprecedented miracles? The man's logic is devastating — and he is just getting started (vv.31-33).

verses.kjv_text: Now we know that God heareth not sinners: but if any man be a worshipper of God, and doeth his will, him he heareth.
verses.original_text: οἴδαμεν δὲ ὅτι ἁμαρτωλῶν ὁ θεὸς οὐκ ἀκούει ἀλλ᾽ ἐάν τις θεοσεβὴς ᾖ καὶ τὸ θέλημα αὐτοῦ ποιῇ τούτου ἀκούει
verses.transliteration: oidamen de hoti hamartōlōn ho theos ouk akouei all ean tis theosebēs ē kai to thelēma autou poiē toutou akouei
verses.study_note: 'God heareth not sinners' (hamartōlōn ho theos ouk akouei — God does not listen to sinners). This is a general OT principle (Psalm 66:18; Proverbs 15:29; Isaiah 1:15) — God does not honour the prayers of the unrepentant. 'If any man be a worshipper of God and doeth his will, him he heareth' — God responds to the devout who obey. The logic: if Jesus were a sinner, God would not have answered his prayer and performed this miracle. Since God clearly acted through Jesus, Jesus cannot be a sinner.

verses.kjv_text: Since the world began was it not heard that any man opened the eyes of one that was born blind.
verses.original_text: ἐκ τοῦ αἰῶνος οὐκ ἠκούσθη ὅτι ἤνοιξέν τις ὀφθαλμοὺς τυφλοῦ γεγεννημένου
verses.transliteration: ek tou aiōnos ouk ēkousthē hoti ēnoixen tis ophthalmous typhlou gegennēmenou
verses.study_note: 'Since the world began' (ek tou aiōnos — from the beginning of time). 'Was it not heard that any man opened the eyes of one that was born blind' — this is a factual statement. In the entire OT, there is no record of a congenitally blind person being healed. Elijah raised the dead, Elisha healed leprosy — but no prophet ever gave sight to the born blind. This miracle has no precedent. The man's argument is that this unprecedented miracle demands an unprecedented explanation: Jesus must be from God.

verses.kjv_text: If this man were not of God, he could do nothing.
verses.original_text: εἰ μὴ ἦν οὗτος παρὰ θεοῦ οὐκ ἠδύνατο ποιεῖν οὐδέν
verses.transliteration: ei mē ēn houtos para theou ouk ēdynato poiein ouden
verses.study_note: 'If this man were not of God, he could do nothing' (ei mē ēn houtos para theou — if this one were not from God). The man's third identification of Jesus: from God (para theou — from God's side). His theology has progressed: a man (v.11) → a prophet (v.17) → from God (v.33). His logic is simple and irrefutable: unprecedented miracle → divine origin. The Pharisees cannot answer this argument — so they attack the man instead (v.34).

verses.kjv_text: They answered and said unto him, Thou wast altogether born in sins, and dost thou teach us? And they cast him out.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθησαν καὶ εἶπον αὐτῷ Ἐν ἁμαρτίαις σὺ ἐγεννήθης ὅλος καὶ σὺ διδάσκεις ἡμᾶς καὶ ἐξέβαλον αὐτὸν ἔξω
verses.transliteration: apekrithēsan kai eipon autō En hamartiais sy egennēthēs holos kai sy didaskeis hēmas kai exebalon auton exō
verses.study_note: 'Thou wast altogether born in sins' (en hamartiais sy egennēthēs holos — in sins you were born entirely). They revert to the sin-suffering theology Jesus rejected in v.3. 'And dost thou teach us?' — outrage that an uneducated blind beggar dares to lecture the religious elite. 'They cast him out' (exebalon auton exō — they threw him out; expelled him from the synagogue, as threatened in v.22). The man who gained physical sight now loses social standing. But what he gains next (vv.35-38) infinitely exceeds what he lost.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus heard that they had cast him out; and when he had found him, he said unto him, Dost thou believe on the Son of God?
verses.original_text: ἤκουσεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς ὅτι ἐξέβαλον αὐτὸν ἔξω καὶ εὑρὼν αὐτὸν εἶπεν αὐτῷ Σὺ πιστεύεις εἰς τὸν υἱὸν τοῦ θεοῦ
verses.transliteration: ēkousen ho Iēsous hoti exebalon auton exō kai heurōn auton eipen autō Sy pisteueis eis ton huion tou theou
verses.study_note: 'Jesus heard... and when he had found him' (heurōn auton — having found him). Jesus seeks out the expelled man — the Good Shepherd goes after the rejected sheep. Those cast out by religious institutions are sought by Jesus (cf. 6:37: 'him that cometh to me I will in no wise cast out'). 'Dost thou believe on the Son of God?' (some manuscripts read 'Son of man' — either title is christological). This is the first time Jesus directly engages the healed man since the healing.

verses.kjv_text: He answered and said, Who is he, Lord, that I might believe on him?
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη ἐκεῖνος καὶ εἶπεν Καὶ τίς ἐστιν κύριε ἵνα πιστεύσω εἰς αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: apekrithē ekeinos kai eipen Kai tis estin kyrie hina pisteusō eis auton
verses.study_note: 'Who is he, Lord, that I might believe on him?' (tis estin kyrie — who is he, Sir/Lord?). The man does not yet know the identity of the Son of God — but he wants to believe. His heart is ready; he just needs to know who. 'That I might believe' (hina pisteusō — in order that I may believe) — his desire for faith is genuine and eager. He was healed before he believed; now belief will complete what healing began.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus said unto him, Thou hast both seen him, and it is he that talketh with thee.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς Καὶ ἑώρακας αὐτὸν καὶ ὁ λαλῶν μετὰ σοῦ ἐκεῖνός ἐστιν
verses.transliteration: eipen de autō ho Iēsous Kai heōrakas auton kai ho lalōn meta sou ekeinos estin
verses.study_note: 'Thou hast both seen him' (heōrakas auton — you have seen him). This is deeply poignant: the man who was born blind and has never seen before is now seeing the Son of God. His first meaningful sight is of Christ. 'It is he that talketh with thee' (ho lalōn meta sou ekeinos estin — the one speaking with you, that is he). Jesus reveals himself directly, as he did to the Samaritan woman (4:26). Revelation comes through personal encounter.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Lord, I believe. And he worshipped him.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ ἔφη Πιστεύω κύριε καὶ προσεκύνησεν αὐτῷ
verses.transliteration: ho de ephē Pisteuō kyrie kai prosekynēsen autō
verses.study_note: 'Lord, I believe' (pisteuō kyrie — I believe, Lord). 'And he worshipped him' (prosekynēsen autō — he fell down and worshipped him). Proskyneō is the word for religious worship — bowing down before deity. The man's journey is complete: from physical blindness to spiritual sight, from ignorance to worship, from 'a man called Jesus' (v.11) to 'Lord' (v.38). This is the full arc of faith: healing → testimony → persecution → revelation → worship. Jesus accepts the worship — something only God may rightfully receive.
verses.doctrinal_note: The man's progression is a model of genuine conversion: (1) He experiences Jesus' power (healing, v.7). (2) He testifies to what he knows (v.11, 15, 25). (3) His understanding grows under pressure (v.17, 30-33). (4) He suffers for his testimony (v.34, cast out). (5) Jesus finds him personally (v.35). (6) He believes and worships (v.38). Note that Jesus accepts proskyneō (worship) — an act that angels refuse (Revelation 22:8-9) and righteous men refuse (Acts 10:25-26). Only God may be worshipped; Jesus accepts worship because he is God.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus said, For judgment I am come into this world, that they which see not might see; and that they which see might be made blind.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς Εἰς κρίμα ἐγὼ εἰς τὸν κόσμον τοῦτον ἦλθον ἵνα οἱ μὴ βλέποντες βλέπωσιν καὶ οἱ βλέποντες τυφλοὶ γένωνται
verses.transliteration: kai eipen ho Iēsous Eis krima egō eis ton kosmon touton ēlthon hina hoi mē blepontes blepōsin kai hoi blepontes typhloi genōntai
verses.study_note: 'For judgment I am come' (eis krima ēlthon — for judgment I came). This does not contradict 3:17 ('God sent not his Son to condemn') — Jesus' purpose is salvation, but the effect of his coming is judgment by division. 'They which see not might see' — the blind man represents the spiritually blind who receive sight through Christ. 'They which see might be made blind' — the Pharisees represent the self-assured religious who, by rejecting Christ, are confirmed in their blindness. The light reveals and judges simultaneously.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse states the paradoxical effect of the incarnation: the coming of the Light simultaneously gives sight to the blind and blinds the seeing. This is not arbitrary but moral: those who know they are blind (the humble, the honest, the seeking) receive sight; those who claim to see (the proud, the self-righteous, the religious) are confirmed in blindness. The issue is not intelligence or education but honesty about one's condition. The prerequisite for spiritual sight is acknowledgment of spiritual blindness.

verses.kjv_text: And some of the Pharisees which were with him heard these words, and said unto him, Are we blind also?
verses.original_text: καὶ ἤκουσαν ἐκ τῶν Φαρισαίων ταῦτα οἱ μετ᾽ αὐτοῦ ὄντες καὶ εἶπον αὐτῷ Μὴ καὶ ἡμεῖς τυφλοί ἐσμεν
verses.transliteration: kai ēkousan ek tōn Pharisaiōn tauta hoi met autou ontes kai eipon autō Mē kai hēmeis typhloi esmen
verses.study_note: 'Are we blind also?' (mē kai hēmeis typhloi esmen — surely we are not blind too?). The question expects a negative answer: of course we are not blind — we are the teachers of Israel, the experts in the law. But Jesus' answer (v.41) turns their assumed negative into a devastating affirmative.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus said unto them, If ye were blind, ye should have no sin: but now ye say, We see; therefore your sin remaineth.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς Εἰ τυφλοὶ ἦτε οὐκ ἂν εἴχετε ἁμαρτίαν νῦν δὲ λέγετε ὅτι Βλέπομεν ἡ οὖν ἁμαρτία ὑμῶν μένει
verses.transliteration: eipen autois ho Iēsous Ei typhloi ēte ouk an eichete hamartian nyn de legete hoti Blepomen hē oun hamartia hymōn menei
verses.study_note: 'If ye were blind, ye should have no sin' — if they genuinely did not know, ignorance would mitigate guilt. 'But now ye say, We see' — their claim to knowledge removes the excuse of ignorance. 'Therefore your sin remaineth' (hē hamartia hymōn menei — your sin abides, stays, endures). The worst blindness is the blindness that claims to see. Those who admit blindness can be healed; those who insist they see remain blind. The Pharisees' sin is not ignorance but wilful refusal to acknowledge what they can see. This is the unforgivable posture: not inability but unwillingness.
verses.doctrinal_note: This closing statement is one of the most penetrating diagnoses of spiritual blindness in Scripture. Three categories emerge: (1) Those who know they are blind and seek sight — the healed man: they receive grace. (2) Those who are genuinely ignorant — they have diminished responsibility. (3) Those who claim to see but refuse the light — the Pharisees: their sin remains because they reject the cure while claiming they have no disease. The medical analogy is exact: the patient who denies being sick cannot be treated. Acknowledging spiritual blindness is the first step toward spiritual sight.

meta.overview: John 10 contains two of Jesus' most beloved self-revelations — 'I am the good shepherd' (v.11) and 'I and my Father are one' (v.30) — set within a discourse on sheep, shepherds, and the sheepfold. The chapter continues directly from the healing of the blind man (ch.9) and opens with the parable of the sheepfold: the true shepherd enters by the door, calls his sheep by name, and they follow because they know his voice (vv.1-5). Jesus then declares: 'I am the door of the sheep' (v.7) — the only legitimate entrance to safety and salvation. 'I am come that they might have life, and that they might have it more abundantly' (v.10). Then comes the central claim: 'I am the good shepherd: the good shepherd giveth his life for the sheep' (v.11). The hired servant flees when the wolf comes, but the good shepherd lays down his life voluntarily: 'No man taketh it from me, but I lay it down of myself. I have power to lay it down, and I have power to take it again' (v.18). The discourse provokes another division (schisma, v.19): some say 'He hath a devil'; others say 'Can a devil open the eyes of the blind?' (vv.20-21). The scene shifts to the Feast of Dedication (Hanukkah) in winter (v.22). The Jews demand a plain answer: 'If thou be the Christ, tell us plainly' (v.24). Jesus responds by pointing to his works and to the relationship between shepherd and sheep: 'My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me: And I give unto them eternal life; and they shall never perish, neither shall any man pluck them out of my hand' (vv.27-28). He then claims: 'My Father, which gave them me, is greater than all; and no man is able to pluck them out of my Father's hand. I and my Father are one' (vv.29-30). The Jews take up stones for blasphemy. Jesus defends himself from Psalm 82:6 and invites them to believe his works (vv.34-38). They seek to take him, but he escapes and withdraws beyond Jordan where John first baptised (vv.39-40). Many believe on him there (v.42).
meta.theme: The Good Shepherd — Life, Security, and Unity with the Father
meta.keyWordMeaning: Shepherd, one who tends and guards a flock — the most intimate biblical image for God's relationship to his people. In the OT, God is the shepherd of Israel: 'The LORD is my shepherd' (Psalm 23:1); 'He shall feed his flock like a shepherd' (Isaiah 40:11); 'I will set up one shepherd over them... even my servant David' (Ezekiel 34:23). Jesus claims to be this promised shepherd — the one who knows his sheep by name (v.3), leads them out (v.3), goes before them (v.4), gives his life for them (v.11, 15), and gathers them into one flock (v.16). The shepherd image combines authority (leadership), intimacy (knowing by name), provision (pasture), protection (laying down life), and unity (one flock, one shepherd).
outline: The Sheepfold Parable (vv.1-6): Verily verily I say unto you He that entereth not by the door into the sheepfold but climbeth up some other way the same is a thief and a robber. But he that entereth in by the door is the shepherd of the sheep. To him the porter openeth and the sheep hear his voice and he calleth his own sheep by name and leadeth them out. And when he putteth forth his own sheep he goeth before them and the sheep follow him for they know his voice. And a stranger will they not follow but will flee from him for they know not the voice of strangers. This parable spake Jesus unto them but they understood not what things they were which he spake unto them

I Am the Door — I Am the Good Shepherd (vv.7-18): Then said Jesus unto them again Verily verily I say unto you I am the door of the sheep. All that ever came before me are thieves and robbers but the sheep did not hear them. I am the door by me if any man enter in he shall be saved and shall go in and out and find pasture. The thief cometh not but for to steal and to kill and to destroy I am come that they might have life and that they might have it more abundantly. I am the good shepherd the good shepherd giveth his life for the sheep. But he that is an hireling and not the shepherd whose own the sheep are not seeth the wolf coming and leaveth the sheep and fleeth and the wolf catcheth them and scattereth the sheep. The hireling fleeth because he is an hireling and careth not for the sheep. I am the good shepherd and know my sheep and am known of mine. As the Father knoweth me even so know I the Father and I lay down my life for the sheep. And other sheep I have which are not of this fold them also I must bring and they shall hear my voice and there shall be one fold and one shepherd. Therefore doth my Father love me because I lay down my life that I might take it again. No man taketh it from me but I lay it down of myself. I have power to lay it down and I have power to take it again. This commandment have I received of my Father

Division Again (vv.19-21): There was a division therefore again among the Jews for these sayings. And many of them said He hath a devil and is mad why hear ye him? Others said These are not the words of him that hath a devil. Can a devil open the eyes of the blind?

The Feast of Dedication — I and My Father Are One (vv.22-39): And it was at Jerusalem the feast of the dedication and it was winter. And Jesus walked in the temple in Solomon's porch. Then came the Jews round about him and said unto him How long dost thou make us to doubt? If thou be the Christ tell us plainly. Jesus answered them I told you and ye believed not the works that I do in my Father's name they bear witness of me. But ye believe not because ye are not of my sheep as I said unto you. My sheep hear my voice and I know them and they follow me. And I give unto them eternal life and they shall never perish neither shall any man pluck them out of my hand. My Father which gave them me is greater than all and no man is able to pluck them out of my Father's hand. I and my Father are one. Then the Jews took up stones again to stone him. Jesus answered them Many good works have I shewed you from my Father for which of those works do ye stone me? The Jews answered him saying For a good work we stone thee not but for blasphemy and because that thou being a man makest thyself God.
