Jesus answered them Is it not written in your law I said Ye are gods? If he called them gods unto whom the word of God came and the scripture cannot be broken Say ye of him whom the Father hath sanctified and sent into the world Thou blasphemest because I said I am the Son of God? If I do not the works of my Father believe me not. But if I do though ye believe not me believe the works that ye may know and believe that the Father is in me and I in him. Therefore they sought again to take him but he escaped out of their hand

Beyond Jordan — Many Believed (vv.40-42): And went away again beyond Jordan into the place where John at first baptized and there he abode. And many resorted unto him and said John did no miracle but all things that John spake of this man were true. And many believed on him there

verses.kjv_text: Verily, verily, I say unto you, He that entereth not by the door into the sheepfold, but climbeth up some other way, the same is a thief and a robber.
verses.original_text: Ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ὁ μὴ εἰσερχόμενος διὰ τῆς θύρας εἰς τὴν αὐλὴν τῶν προβάτων ἀλλὰ ἀναβαίνων ἀλλαχόθεν ἐκεῖνος κλέπτης ἐστὶν καὶ λῃστής
verses.transliteration: Amēn amēn legō hymin ho mē eiserchomenos dia tēs thyras eis tēn aulēn tōn probatōn alla anabainōn allachothen ekeinos kleptēs estin kai lēstēs
verses.study_note: 'The sheepfold' (tēn aulēn tōn probatōn — the courtyard/enclosure of the sheep). Palestinian sheepfolds were stone-walled enclosures with a single entrance. At night, multiple flocks would share a communal fold with a doorkeeper. 'Thief and robber' (kleptēs kai lēstēs — kleptēs steals by stealth; lēstēs robs by violence). Anyone who bypasses the door is illegitimate — referring to false religious leaders who approach God's people through deception or force rather than through God's appointed way. The Pharisees who expelled the blind man (ch.9) are in view.

verses.kjv_text: But he that entereth in by the door is the shepherd of the sheep.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ εἰσερχόμενος διὰ τῆς θύρας ποιμήν ἐστιν τῶν προβάτων
verses.transliteration: ho de eiserchomenos dia tēs thyras poimēn estin tōn probatōn
verses.study_note: The legitimate shepherd enters through the proper entrance. He comes openly, with authority, through the recognised channel. Jesus enters through the proper 'door' — fulfilment of prophecy, authorisation by the Father, authenticated by signs.

verses.kjv_text: To him the porter openeth; and the sheep hear his voice: and he calleth his own sheep by name, and leadeth them out.
verses.original_text: τούτῳ ὁ θυρωρὸς ἀνοίγει καὶ τὰ πρόβατα τῆς φωνῆς αὐτοῦ ἀκούει καὶ τὰ ἴδια πρόβατα καλεῖ κατ᾽ ὄνομα καὶ ἐξάγει αὐτά
verses.transliteration: toutō ho thyrōros anoigei kai ta probata tēs phōnēs autou akouei kai ta idia probata kalei kat onoma kai exagei auta
verses.study_note: 'The porter openeth' (ho thyrōros anoigei — the doorkeeper opens for him). 'The sheep hear his voice' (ta probata tēs phōnēs autou akouei — the sheep listen to his voice). 'He calleth his own sheep by name' (ta idia probata kalei kat onoma — he calls his own particular sheep individually by name). In Palestine, shepherds named their sheep and the sheep recognised their shepherd's unique call. The intimacy is personal: Jesus does not manage a crowd but calls individuals by name (cf. Isaiah 43:1: 'I have called thee by thy name; thou art mine').

verses.kjv_text: And when he putteth forth his own sheep, he goeth before them, and the sheep follow him: for they know his voice.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὅταν τὰ ἴδια πρόβατα ἐκβάλῃ ἔμπροσθεν αὐτῶν πορεύεται καὶ τὰ πρόβατα αὐτῷ ἀκολουθεῖ ὅτι οἴδασιν τὴν φωνὴν αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai hotan ta idia probata ekbalē emprosthen autōn poreuetai kai ta probata autō akolouthei hoti oidasin tēn phōnēn autou
verses.study_note: 'He goeth before them' (emprosthen autōn poreuetai — he goes ahead of them). Eastern shepherds lead from the front; Western herding drives from behind. Jesus leads by example, going ahead into danger. 'They know his voice' (oidasin tēn phōnēn autou — they know, recognise his voice with settled certainty). Sheep follow the shepherd they know. This explains why the healed blind man (ch.9) followed Jesus while the Pharisees could not — the sheep know the Shepherd's voice.

verses.kjv_text: And a stranger will they not follow, but will flee from him: for they know not the voice of strangers.
verses.original_text: ἀλλοτρίῳ δὲ οὐ μὴ ἀκολουθήσωσιν ἀλλὰ φεύξονται ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ ὅτι οὐκ οἴδασιν τῶν ἀλλοτρίων τὴν φωνήν
verses.transliteration: allotriō de ou mē akolouthēsousin alla pheuxontai ap autou hoti ouk oidasin tōn allotriōn tēn phōnēn
verses.study_note: 'A stranger will they not follow' (allotriō ou mē akolouthēsousin — they will absolutely not follow a stranger). True sheep have built-in discernment: they flee (pheuxontai — will flee) from unfamiliar voices. The basis is relational knowledge — not theological education but experiential familiarity with the Shepherd's voice. False teachers may sound impressive, but Christ's sheep sense the difference.

verses.kjv_text: This parable spake Jesus unto them: but they understood not what things they were which he spake unto them.
verses.original_text: ταύτην τὴν παροιμίαν εἶπεν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἐκεῖνοι δὲ οὐκ ἔγνωσαν τίνα ἦν ἃ ἐλάλει αὐτοῖς
verses.transliteration: tautēn tēn paroimian eipen autois ho Iēsous ekeinoi de ouk egnōsan tina ēn ha elalei autois
verses.study_note: 'Parable' (paroimian — a figure of speech, a proverb, an extended metaphor; not the same word as parabolē used in the Synoptics). 'They understood not' (ouk egnōsan — they did not know, did not recognise). The Pharisees, who expelled the blind man, cannot understand the shepherd imagery because they are not sheep — they do not recognise the Shepherd's voice. Their incomprehension proves the point.

verses.kjv_text: Then said Jesus unto them again, Verily, verily, I say unto you, I am the door of the sheep.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν οὖν πάλιν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ θύρα τῶν προβάτων
verses.transliteration: eipen oun palin autois ho Iēsous Amēn amēn legō hymin hoti egō eimi hē thyra tōn probatōn
verses.study_note: 'I am the door of the sheep' (egō eimi hē thyra tōn probatōn — the third 'I AM' predicate in John). Jesus is not merely the shepherd who enters through the door — he is the door itself. In Palestine, the shepherd often lay across the entrance at night, literally becoming the door with his own body. No sheep could exit and no predator could enter without passing over the shepherd. Jesus is the only access point to God and to safety.
verses.doctrinal_note: This is the third of seven 'I AM' predicate statements. Jesus claims to be the exclusive point of access: 'by me if any man enter in, he shall be saved' (v.9). This claim to exclusivity is consistent with 14:6 ('no man cometh unto the Father, but by me') and Acts 4:12 ('neither is there salvation in any other'). The door metaphor emphasises both access (the way in) and security (protection once inside). Jesus is simultaneously the entrance and the boundary — the way of salvation and the wall of protection.

verses.kjv_text: All that ever came before me are thieves and robbers: but the sheep did not hear them.
verses.original_text: πάντες ὅσοι ἦλθον πρὸ ἐμοῦ κλέπται εἰσὶν καὶ λῃσταί ἀλλ᾽ οὐκ ἤκουσαν αὐτῶν τὰ πρόβατα
verses.transliteration: pantes hosoi ēlthon pro emou kleptai eisin kai lēstai all ouk ēkousan autōn ta probata
verses.study_note: 'All that ever came before me' — not the OT prophets (who came from God and pointed to Christ) but false messiahs, false leaders, and religious frauds who claimed authority apart from God's appointment. 'The sheep did not hear them' — true sheep have always discerned false voices. This retrospective judgment applies to the history of false leadership in Israel (cf. Ezekiel 34:1-10 where God condemns the shepherds of Israel).

verses.kjv_text: I am the door: by me if any man enter in, he shall be saved, and shall go in and out, and find pasture.
verses.original_text: ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ θύρα δι᾽ ἐμοῦ ἐάν τις εἰσέλθῃ σωθήσεται καὶ εἰσελεύσεται καὶ ἐξελεύσεται καὶ νομὴν εὑρήσει
verses.transliteration: egō eimi hē thyra di emou ean tis eiselthē sōthēsetai kai eiseleusetai kai exeleusetai kai nomēn heurēsei
verses.study_note: 'By me if any man enter in' (di emou ean tis eiselthē — through me, if anyone enters). 'He shall be saved' (sōthēsetai — will be saved, delivered, rescued). 'Go in and out' — a Hebrew idiom for living freely and securely (Numbers 27:17; Deuteronomy 28:6; Psalm 121:8). 'Find pasture' (nomēn heurēsei — will find grazing, nourishment). Three blessings through the Door: (1) salvation (rescue from danger), (2) freedom (going in and out without fear), (3) provision (finding pasture). The shepherd's fold provides everything the sheep need.

verses.kjv_text: The thief cometh not, but for to steal, and to kill, and to destroy: I am come that they might have life, and that they might have it more abundantly.
verses.original_text: ὁ κλέπτης οὐκ ἔρχεται εἰ μὴ ἵνα κλέψῃ καὶ θύσῃ καὶ ἀπολέσῃ ἐγὼ ἦλθον ἵνα ζωὴν ἔχωσιν καὶ περισσὸν ἔχωσιν
verses.transliteration: ho kleptēs ouk erchetai ei mē hina klepsē kai thysē kai apolesē egō ēlthon hina zōēn echōsin kai perisson echōsin
verses.study_note: 'To steal, kill, and destroy' (klepsē kai thysē kai apolesē) — the thief's threefold agenda: take what is not his, slaughter, and ruin. In contrast, Jesus' purpose is life: 'I am come that they might have life' (hina zōēn echōsin). 'More abundantly' (perisson — exceedingly, overflowing, beyond measure). Not merely survival but abundant, overflowing, superabundant life. The contrast is absolute: the thief takes life; Jesus gives life, and more than enough of it.

verses.kjv_text: I am the good shepherd: the good shepherd giveth his life for the sheep.
verses.original_text: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ποιμὴν ὁ καλός ὁ ποιμὴν ὁ καλὸς τὴν ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ τίθησιν ὑπὲρ τῶν προβάτων
verses.transliteration: egō eimi ho poimēn ho kalos ho poimēn ho kalos tēn psychēn autou tithēsin hyper tōn probatōn
verses.study_note: 'I am the good shepherd' (egō eimi ho poimēn ho kalos — the fourth 'I AM' predicate). 'Good' (kalos — not merely agathos/morally good but kalos: beautiful, noble, ideal, the model of excellence). 'Giveth his life for the sheep' (tēn psychēn autou tithēsin hyper tōn probatōn — lays down his life on behalf of the sheep). 'For' (hyper — on behalf of, in the place of) is the preposition of substitution. The shepherd dies so the sheep may live. This is the cross in shepherd imagery.
verses.doctrinal_note: The 'good shepherd' (poimēn kalos) stands in contrast to the hired servant (v.12-13) and the thief (v.10). Three categories of leadership: (1) The thief — exploits the sheep for personal gain. (2) The hireling — serves for wages but abandons when danger comes. (3) The good shepherd — owns the sheep, knows them personally, and dies for them. Jesus claims to be the fulfilment of Ezekiel 34:23: 'I will set up one shepherd over them, even my servant David; he shall feed them.' The 'laying down' of life (tithēsin — present tense: it is his constant disposition) points to the cross.

verses.kjv_text: But he that is an hireling, and not the shepherd, whose own the sheep are not, seeth the wolf coming, and leaveth the sheep, and fleeth: and the wolf catcheth them, and scattereth the sheep.
verses.original_text: ὁ μισθωτὸς δέ καὶ οὐκ ὢν ποιμήν οὗ οὐκ εἰσὶν τὰ πρόβατα ἴδια θεωρεῖ τὸν λύκον ἐρχόμενον καὶ ἀφίησιν τὰ πρόβατα καὶ φεύγει καὶ ὁ λύκος ἁρπάζει αὐτὰ καὶ σκορπίζει τὰ πρόβατα
verses.transliteration: ho misthōtos de kai ouk ōn poimēn hou ouk eisin ta probata idia theōrei ton lykon erchomenon kai aphiēsin ta probata kai pheugei kai ho lykos harpazei auta kai skorpizei ta probata
verses.study_note: 'Hireling' (misthōtos — a hired hand, one who works for wages). 'Whose own the sheep are not' (hou ouk eisin ta probata idia — the sheep do not belong to him). The hireling's fundamental problem: no ownership, therefore no commitment. 'The wolf' (ton lykon — false teachers, persecutors, spiritual predators; cf. Matthew 7:15; Acts 20:29). The hireling abandons at the first sign of danger because the sheep are not his. The good shepherd stays because they are.

verses.kjv_text: The hireling fleeth, because he is an hireling, and careth not for the sheep.
verses.original_text: ὁ μισθωτὸς φεύγει ὅτι μισθωτός ἐστιν καὶ οὐ μέλει αὐτῷ περὶ τῶν προβάτων
verses.transliteration: ho misthōtos pheugei hoti misthōtos estin kai ou melei autō peri tōn probatōn
verses.study_note: 'Careth not' (ou melei autō — it does not matter to him, he is unconcerned). The diagnosis is devastating: the hireling's problem is not cowardice but indifference. He does not care. His relationship to the sheep is commercial, not covenantal. Many religious leaders throughout history have proven to be hirelings — serving when convenient, fleeing when costly.

verses.kjv_text: I am the good shepherd, and know my sheep, and am known of mine.
verses.original_text: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ποιμὴν ὁ καλός καὶ γινώσκω τὰ ἐμὰ καὶ γινώσκομαι ὑπὸ τῶν ἐμῶν
verses.transliteration: egō eimi ho poimēn ho kalos kai ginōskō ta ema kai ginōskomai hypo tōn emōn
verses.study_note: 'I know my sheep, and am known of mine' (ginōskō ta ema kai ginōskomai hypo tōn emōn — I know mine and am known by mine). The knowledge is mutual and intimate — not mere awareness but deep, personal, covenantal knowing. This mutual knowledge is then compared to the ultimate standard of intimacy in v.15.

verses.kjv_text: As the Father knoweth me, even so know I the Father: and I lay down my life for the sheep.
verses.original_text: καθὼς γινώσκει με ὁ πατὴρ κἀγὼ γινώσκω τὸν πατέρα καὶ τὴν ψυχήν μου τίθημι ὑπὲρ τῶν προβάτων
verses.transliteration: kathōs ginōskei me ho patēr kagō ginōskō ton patera kai tēn psychēn mou tithēmi hyper tōn probatōn
verses.study_note: 'As the Father knoweth me, even so know I the Father' (kathōs ginōskei me ho patēr kagō ginōskō ton patera). The mutual knowledge between shepherd and sheep is patterned on the mutual knowledge between Father and Son — the most intimate relationship in the universe. 'I lay down my life for the sheep' — the cross is not forced upon Jesus but chosen: 'I lay down' (tithēmi — present tense: I am in the process of laying down). The atonement is voluntary, substitutionary, and motivated by knowledge and love.

verses.kjv_text: And other sheep I have, which are not of this fold: them also I must bring, and they shall hear my voice; and there shall be one fold, and one shepherd.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἄλλα πρόβατα ἔχω ἃ οὐκ ἔστιν ἐκ τῆς αὐλῆς ταύτης κἀκεῖνα δεῖ με ἀγαγεῖν καὶ τῆς φωνῆς μου ἀκούσουσιν καὶ γενήσεται μία ποίμνη εἷς ποιμήν
verses.transliteration: kai alla probata echō ha ouk estin ek tēs aulēs tautēs kakeina dei me agagein kai tēs phōnēs mou akousousin kai genēsetai mia poimnē heis poimēn
verses.study_note: 'Other sheep I have, which are not of this fold' (alla probata echō — other sheep I possess). 'This fold' (tēs aulēs tautēs) = the Jewish fold. The 'other sheep' are the Gentiles who will believe. 'I must bring' (dei me agagein — it is divinely necessary for me to bring them). 'They shall hear my voice' — the same voice, the same shepherd, the same call. 'One fold, one shepherd' (mia poimnē heis poimēn — one flock, one shepherd). Note: poimnē is 'flock' (not aulē/fold) — there will be one united flock under one shepherd, not one enclosure. Jews and Gentiles become one people in Christ.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse is the charter for the Gentile mission. Jesus' saving work extends beyond Israel to embrace 'other sheep' — people from every nation, tribe, and tongue (Revelation 7:9). The 'must' (dei) indicates divine necessity: the Gentile inclusion is not an afterthought but part of God's eternal plan. Ephesians 2:14-16 provides the commentary: Christ has 'broken down the middle wall of partition' and made 'one new man' of Jew and Gentile. The goal is not uniformity but unity: one flock, diverse members, one Shepherd.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore doth my Father love me, because I lay down my life, that I might take it again.
verses.original_text: διὰ τοῦτο ὁ πατήρ με ἀγαπᾷ ὅτι ἐγὼ τίθημι τὴν ψυχήν μου ἵνα πάλιν λάβω αὐτήν
verses.transliteration: dia touto ho patēr me agapa hoti egō tithēmi tēn psychēn mou hina palin labō autēn
verses.study_note: 'My Father love me, because I lay down my life' — the Father's love for the Son is connected to the Son's voluntary sacrifice. 'That I might take it again' (hina palin labō autēn — in order to take it up again). The laying down (death) is not the end — the taking up again (resurrection) is the goal. Death is the means; resurrection is the consummation. The entire sequence — death and resurrection — is the object of the Father's love.

verses.kjv_text: No man taketh it from me, but I lay it down of myself. I have power to lay it down, and I have power to take it again. This commandment have I received of my Father.
verses.original_text: οὐδεὶς αἴρει αὐτὴν ἀπ᾽ ἐμοῦ ἀλλ᾽ ἐγὼ τίθημι αὐτὴν ἀπ᾽ ἐμαυτοῦ ἐξουσίαν ἔχω θεῖναι αὐτήν καὶ ἐξουσίαν ἔχω πάλιν λαβεῖν αὐτήν ταύτην τὴν ἐντολὴν ἔλαβον παρὰ τοῦ πατρός μου
verses.transliteration: oudeis airei autēn ap emou all egō tithēmi autēn ap emautou exousian echō theinai autēn kai exousian echō palin labein autēn tautēn tēn entolēn elabon para tou patros mou
verses.study_note: 'No man taketh it from me' (oudeis airei autēn — no one takes it from me). Jesus' death is not a tragedy imposed upon him but a sacrifice he voluntarily offers. 'I lay it down of myself' (egō tithēmi autēn ap emautou — I lay it down from my own initiative). 'I have power' (exousian echō — I have authority/right/power). Two powers: power to die and power to rise. No mere human has authority over their own death and resurrection. 'This commandment have I received of my Father' — Jesus' voluntary death is simultaneously an act of free will and an act of obedience. Freedom and obedience are not contradictory but unified in the Son.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse establishes the voluntary nature of the atonement. The cross was not: (1) An accident — 'No man taketh it.' (2) A defeat — 'I lay it down of myself.' (3) An end — 'I have power to take it again.' (4) An independent act — 'This commandment have I received of my Father.' Jesus' death is a deliberate, authoritative, obedient, and reversible sacrifice. He possesses authority that no creature has — authority over death itself. This authority is both inherent (he has it) and received (the Father commanded it) — the perfect union of divine nature and divine mission.

verses.kjv_text: There was a division therefore again among the Jews for these sayings.
verses.original_text: σχίσμα οὖν πάλιν ἐγένετο ἐν τοῖς Ἰουδαίοις διὰ τοὺς λόγους τούτους
verses.transliteration: schisma oun palin egeneto en tois Ioudaiois dia tous logous toutous
verses.study_note: 'Division again' (schisma palin — another split; cf. 7:43; 9:16). Jesus consistently divides people. His words are too extraordinary to ignore — they demand a response, and the responses are polar opposites.

verses.kjv_text: And many of them said, He hath a devil, and is mad; why hear ye him?
verses.original_text: ἔλεγον δὲ πολλοὶ ἐξ αὐτῶν Δαιμόνιον ἔχει καὶ μαίνεται τί αὐτοῦ ἀκούετε
verses.transliteration: elegon de polloi ex autōn Daimonion echei kai mainetai ti autou akouete
verses.study_note: 'He hath a devil, and is mad' (daimonion echei kai mainetai — he has a demon and is insane). The charge of demonic possession recurs (7:20; 8:48, 52). 'Is mad' (mainetai — is raving, is out of his mind; cf. Acts 26:24 where Festus says to Paul 'thou art beside thyself'). Those who reject Jesus must explain him somehow — since they cannot accept his claims, they label him insane.

verses.kjv_text: Others said, These are not the words of him that hath a devil. Can a devil open the eyes of the blind?
verses.original_text: ἄλλοι ἔλεγον Ταῦτα τὰ ῥήματα οὐκ ἔστιν δαιμονιζομένου μὴ δαιμόνιον δύναται τυφλῶν ὀφθαλμοὺς ἀνοίγειν
verses.transliteration: alloi elegon Tauta ta rhēmata ouk estin daimonizomenou mē daimonion dynatai typhlōn ophthalmous anoigein
verses.study_note: 'Can a devil open the eyes of the blind?' — the healing of the man born blind (ch.9) provides irrefutable evidence. Demons destroy; they do not heal. The quality of Jesus' works contradicts the demon-accusation. The chapter 9 miracle is still reverberating — it cannot be explained away.

verses.kjv_text: And it was at Jerusalem the feast of the dedication, and it was winter.
verses.original_text: ἐγένετο δὲ τὰ ἐγκαίνια ἐν τοῖς Ἱεροσολύμοις καὶ χειμὼν ἦν
verses.transliteration: egeneto de ta enkainia en tois Hierosolymois kai cheimōn ēn
verses.study_note: 'The feast of the dedication' (ta enkainia — Hanukkah, the Festival of Lights/Dedication). This eight-day feast (late November/December) commemorated the rededication of the temple by Judas Maccabaeus in 164 BC after its desecration by Antiochus Epiphanes (1 Maccabees 4:36-59). 'It was winter' (cheimōn ēn) — both literal (cold, December) and atmospheric (hostility is chilling). About two months have passed since the Feast of Tabernacles (ch.7-9).

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus walked in the temple in Solomon's porch.
verses.original_text: καὶ περιεπάτει ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἐν τῷ ἱερῷ ἐν τῇ στοᾷ τοῦ Σολομῶντος
verses.transliteration: kai periepatei ho Iēsous en tō hierō en tē stoa tou Solomōntos
verses.study_note: 'Solomon's porch' (tē stoa tou Solomōntos — Solomon's colonnade/portico). A covered colonnade on the eastern side of the temple mount, believed to date from Solomon's original temple. In winter, it provided shelter from cold winds. The early church also gathered here (Acts 3:11; 5:12). Jesus teaches in the place where Israel's wisest king once walked.

verses.kjv_text: Then came the Jews round about him, and said unto him, How long dost thou make us to doubt? If thou be the Christ, tell us plainly.
verses.original_text: ἐκύκλωσαν οὖν αὐτὸν οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι καὶ ἔλεγον αὐτῷ Ἕως πότε τὴν ψυχὴν ἡμῶν αἴρεις εἰ σὺ εἶ ὁ Χριστός εἰπὲ ἡμῖν παρρησίᾳ
verses.transliteration: ekyklōsan oun auton hoi Ioudaioi kai elegon autō Heōs pote tēn psychēn hēmōn aireis ei sy ei ho Christos eipe hēmin parrēsia
verses.study_note: 'Came round about him' (ekyklōsan — encircled, surrounded; an aggressive, confrontational stance). 'How long dost thou make us to doubt?' (heōs pote tēn psychēn hēmōn aireis — how long do you keep us in suspense?). 'Tell us plainly' (eipe hēmin parrēsia — tell us openly, boldly). Their demand seems reasonable, but Jesus has been telling them plainly — in works (5:36; 10:25), in words (8:58; 10:11), in signs (ch.6, 9). The problem is not insufficient evidence but unwilling hearts.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered them, I told you, and ye believed not: the works that I do in my Father's name, they bear witness of me.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς Εἶπον ὑμῖν καὶ οὐ πιστεύετε τὰ ἔργα ἃ ἐγὼ ποιῶ ἐν τῷ ὀνόματι τοῦ πατρός μου ταῦτα μαρτυρεῖ περὶ ἐμοῦ
verses.transliteration: apekrithē autois ho Iēsous Eipon hymin kai ou pisteuete ta erga ha egō poiō en tō onomati tou patros mou tauta martyrei peri emou
verses.study_note: 'I told you, and ye believed not' — the evidence has been presented repeatedly. 'The works that I do... bear witness' (ta erga... martyrei peri emou — the works testify about me). Jesus' miracles are not just displays of power but testimonies to his identity. If they will not believe his words, they should believe his works (v.38). But they reject both.

verses.kjv_text: But ye believe not, because ye are not of my sheep, as I said unto you.
verses.original_text: ἀλλ᾽ ὑμεῖς οὐ πιστεύετε οὐ γάρ ἐστε ἐκ τῶν προβάτων τῶν ἐμῶν καθὼς εἶπον ὑμῖν
verses.transliteration: all hymeis ou pisteuete ou gar este ek tōn probatōn tōn emōn kathōs eipon hymin
verses.study_note: 'Ye believe not, because ye are not of my sheep' (ou pisteuete ou gar este ek tōn probatōn tōn emōn — you do not believe because you are not from among my sheep). Note the order: they are not sheep, therefore they do not believe. Jesus does not say 'You are not my sheep because you don't believe' but 'You don't believe because you are not my sheep.' Belonging precedes believing. This is consistent with 6:44 (the Father draws), 6:65 (given by the Father), and 8:47 ('he that is of God heareth God's words').

verses.kjv_text: My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me:
verses.original_text: τὰ πρόβατα τὰ ἐμὰ τῆς φωνῆς μου ἀκούει κἀγὼ γινώσκω αὐτὰ καὶ ἀκολουθοῦσίν μοι
verses.transliteration: ta probata ta ema tēs phōnēs mou akouei kagō ginōskō auta kai akolouthousin moi
verses.study_note: 'My sheep hear my voice' — hearing is the mark of belonging. 'I know them' (ginōskō auta — I know them personally). 'They follow me' (akolouthousin moi — they follow me, continuously). Three marks of Jesus' sheep: (1) they hear his voice (receptivity), (2) he knows them (divine knowledge), (3) they follow (obedient discipleship). The order is significant: hearing comes first, then following — but underneath both is the Shepherd's knowledge.

verses.kjv_text: And I give unto them eternal life; and they shall never perish, neither shall any man pluck them out of my hand.
verses.original_text: κἀγὼ ζωὴν αἰώνιον δίδωμι αὐτοῖς καὶ οὐ μὴ ἀπόλωνται εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα καὶ οὐχ ἁρπάσει τις αὐτὰ ἐκ τῆς χειρός μου
verses.transliteration: kagō zōēn aiōnion didōmi autois kai ou mē apolōntai eis ton aiōna kai ouch harpasei tis auta ek tēs cheiros mou
verses.study_note: 'I give unto them eternal life' (zōēn aiōnion didōmi autois — present tense: I am giving). 'They shall never perish' (ou mē apolōntai eis ton aiōna — they will absolutely not perish, ever). 'Neither shall any man pluck them out of my hand' (ouch harpasei tis auta ek tēs cheiros mou — no one will snatch them from my hand). Three guarantees: (1) eternal life as a present gift, (2) absolute impossibility of perishing, (3) no external force can remove them from Christ's protective hand.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse is one of the strongest statements of eternal security in Scripture. The double negative (ou mē — absolutely never) is the strongest negation in Greek. 'Perish' (apolōntai — be destroyed, be lost) is the same verb used in 3:16 ('should not perish'). The promise is unconditional: the sheep will never perish. 'No one' (tis — anyone, any force, any being) can snatch (harpazō — seize by force) them from Christ's hand. This includes Satan, demons, circumstances, persecution, and the believers themselves. The security is in the Shepherd's hand, not in the sheep's grip.

verses.kjv_text: My Father, which gave them me, is greater than all; and no man is able to pluck them out of my Father's hand.
verses.original_text: ὁ πατήρ μου ὃς δέδωκέν μοι μείζων πάντων ἐστίν καὶ οὐδεὶς δύναται ἁρπάζειν ἐκ τῆς χειρὸς τοῦ πατρός μου
verses.transliteration: ho patēr mou hos dedōken moi meizōn pantōn estin kai oudeis dynatai harpazein ek tēs cheiros tou patros mou
verses.study_note: 'My Father, which gave them me' — the sheep are the Father's gift to the Son (cf. 6:37, 39; 17:6). 'Is greater than all' (meizōn pantōn estin — is greater than everything/everyone). 'No man is able to pluck them out of my Father's hand' — double security: the sheep are in the Son's hand (v.28) and the Father's hand (v.29). Two divine hands — the entire Trinity protects the believer.

verses.kjv_text: I and my Father are one.
verses.original_text: ἐγὼ καὶ ὁ πατὴρ ἕν ἐσμεν
verses.transliteration: egō kai ho patēr hen esmen
verses.study_note: 'I and my Father are one' (egō kai ho patēr hen esmen). 'One' (hen — neuter: one thing, one in essence/nature; not heis/masculine: one person). Jesus claims unity of nature/essence with the Father while maintaining personal distinction ('I and my Father' — two persons). The context is security: the sheep are safe because the Father and Son are one in power, purpose, and nature. What the Son holds, the Father holds; what the Father protects, the Son protects.
verses.doctrinal_note: This is one of the most important christological statements in the NT. 'One' (hen, neuter) indicates unity of essence, not identity of person. Jesus does not say 'I am the Father' (Sabellianism) but 'I and the Father are one' (Trinitarian). Two persons (egō kai ho patēr — I and the Father), one essence (hen esmen — we are one thing). The Jews understand correctly: this is a claim to deity (v.33: 'thou, being a man, makest thyself God'). The entire Nicene Creed's affirmation that the Son is 'of one substance (homoousios) with the Father' is grounded in this verse.

verses.kjv_text: Then the Jews took up stones again to stone him.
verses.original_text: ἐβάστασαν οὖν πάλιν λίθους οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι ἵνα λιθάσωσιν αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: ebastasan oun palin lithous hoi Ioudaioi hina lithasōsin auton
verses.study_note: 'Took up stones again' (ebastasan palin lithous — carried/picked up stones again; 'again' recalls 8:59). This is the second attempt to stone Jesus for blasphemy. Their response confirms they understood his claim: he was claiming to be God. If 'I and my Father are one' meant merely unity of purpose, stoning would be an absurd overreaction.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered them, Many good works have I shewed you from my Father; for which of those works do ye stone me?
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς Πολλὰ καλὰ ἔργα ἔδειξα ὑμῖν ἐκ τοῦ πατρός μου διὰ ποῖον αὐτῶν ἔργον λιθάζετέ με
verses.transliteration: apekrithē autois ho Iēsous Polla kala erga edeixa hymin ek tou patros mou dia poion autōn ergon lithazete me
verses.study_note: 'Many good works' (polla kala erga — many beautiful/noble works). 'From my Father' — the works originate with the Father, performed through the Son. 'For which of those works do ye stone me?' — Jesus forces them to articulate their objection. They cannot say 'for healing' or 'for giving sight' — so they must admit it is for his claims (v.33).

verses.kjv_text: The Jews answered him, saying, For a good work we stone thee not; but for blasphemy; and because that thou, being a man, makest thyself God.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθησαν αὐτῷ οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι λέγοντες Περὶ καλοῦ ἔργου οὐ λιθάζομέν σε ἀλλὰ περὶ βλασφημίας καὶ ὅτι σὺ ἄνθρωπος ὢν ποιεῖς σεαυτὸν θεόν
verses.transliteration: apekrithēsan autō hoi Ioudaioi legontes Peri kalou ergou ou lithazomen se alla peri blasphēmias kai hoti sy anthrōpos ōn poieis seauton theon
verses.study_note: 'For blasphemy' (peri blasphēmias — concerning blasphemy). 'Thou, being a man, makest thyself God' (sy anthrōpos ōn poieis seauton theon — you, being a human, make yourself God). They state the charge precisely: Jesus claims to be God. This is exactly what Jesus has been claiming (5:18; 8:58; 10:30). If the claim is false, it is blasphemy; if true, it is the most important truth ever spoken. The Jews understand the claim correctly — their error is in rejecting it.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered them, Is it not written in your law, I said, Ye are gods?
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς Οὐκ ἔστιν γεγραμμένον ἐν τῷ νόμῳ ὑμῶν ὅτι Ἐγὼ εἶπα Θεοί ἐστε
verses.transliteration: apekrithē autois ho Iēsous Ouk estin gegrammenon en tō nomō hymōn hoti Egō eipa Theoi este
verses.study_note: Jesus quotes Psalm 82:6: 'I said, Ye are gods' (theoi este). In Psalm 82, God addresses unjust human judges who bear the title 'gods' (elohim) because they exercise God's judicial authority. Jesus' argument is a fortiori (from the lesser to the greater): if Scripture calls mere human judges 'gods' and that usage is not blasphemy, how much more appropriate is it for the one whom the Father sanctified and sent into the world to call himself the Son of God?

verses.kjv_text: If he called them gods, unto whom the word of God came, and the scripture cannot be broken;
verses.original_text: εἰ ἐκείνους εἶπεν θεοὺς πρὸς οὓς ὁ λόγος τοῦ θεοῦ ἐγένετο καὶ οὐ δύναται λυθῆναι ἡ γραφή
verses.transliteration: ei ekeinous eipen theous pros hous ho logos tou theou egeneto kai ou dynatai lythēnai hē graphē
verses.study_note: 'The scripture cannot be broken' (ou dynatai lythēnai hē graphē — the Scripture is not able to be dissolved/annulled/set aside). A foundational statement on biblical authority: Scripture is inviolable. If a term used in Scripture cannot be challenged, then Jesus' use of a much lesser title ('Son of God' rather than 'God') for a much greater person (himself, sanctified and sent by the Father) cannot be blasphemy. The argument is airtight within their own legal framework.

verses.kjv_text: Say ye of him, whom the Father hath sanctified, and sent into the world, Thou blasphemest; because I said, I am the Son of God?
verses.original_text: ὃν ὁ πατὴρ ἡγίασεν καὶ ἀπέστειλεν εἰς τὸν κόσμον ὑμεῖς λέγετε ὅτι Βλασφημεῖς ὅτι εἶπον Υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ εἰμι
verses.transliteration: hon ho patēr hēgiasen kai apesteilen eis ton kosmon hymeis legete hoti Blasphēmeis hoti eipon Huios tou theou eimi
verses.study_note: 'Whom the Father hath sanctified and sent' (hon ho patēr hēgiasen kai apesteilen — whom the Father set apart and sent). 'Sanctified' (hēgiasen — consecrated, set apart for a holy purpose). 'Sent into the world' (apesteilen eis ton kosmon). 'I am the Son of God' (huios tou theou eimi). Note that Jesus uses 'Son of God' here — less than the 'I and my Father are one' of v.30. He is not retreating from his claim but showing that even a lesser formulation should be acceptable. If mere human judges can be called 'gods,' the unique Son whom the Father sanctified and sent can certainly be called God's Son.

verses.kjv_text: If I do not the works of my Father, believe me not.
verses.original_text: εἰ οὐ ποιῶ τὰ ἔργα τοῦ πατρός μου μὴ πιστεύετέ μοι
verses.transliteration: ei ou poiō ta erga tou patros mou mē pisteuete moi
verses.study_note: 'If I do not the works of my Father, believe me not' — Jesus offers a falsifiable test: judge me by my works. If his works are not divine works, reject him. But if they are divine works, there is only one conclusion. This is remarkably fair: Jesus does not demand blind faith but offers evidence-based faith.

verses.kjv_text: But if I do, though ye believe not me, believe the works: that ye may know, and believe, that the Father is in me, and I in him.
verses.original_text: εἰ δὲ ποιῶ κἂν ἐμοὶ μὴ πιστεύητε τοῖς ἔργοις πιστεύσατε ἵνα γνῶτε καὶ πιστεύσητε ὅτι ἐν ἐμοὶ ὁ πατὴρ κἀγὼ ἐν αὐτῷ
verses.transliteration: ei de poiō kan emoi mē pisteuēte tois ergois pisteusate hina gnōte kai pisteusēte hoti en emoi ho patēr kagō en autō
verses.study_note: 'Believe the works' (tois ergois pisteusate — put your faith in the works/evidence). 'That the Father is in me, and I in him' (hoti en emoi ho patēr kagō en autō — that the Father is in me and I in him). Mutual indwelling — the Father dwells in the Son and the Son in the Father. This is a restatement of v.30 ('I and my Father are one') in relational rather than ontological terms. The works reveal this unity: what Jesus does, the Father does through him.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore they sought again to take him: but he escaped out of their hand,
verses.original_text: ἐζήτουν οὖν πάλιν αὐτὸν πιάσαι καὶ ἐξῆλθεν ἐκ τῆς χειρὸς αὐτῶν
verses.transliteration: ezētoun oun palin auton piasai kai exēlthen ek tēs cheiros autōn
verses.study_note: 'He escaped out of their hand' (exēlthen ek tēs cheiros autōn — he went out from their hand/grasp). Ironically, no one can snatch the sheep from Jesus' hand (v.28), and no one can seize Jesus from the Father's hand. He escapes not by running but by sovereign departure — his hour has not yet come.

verses.kjv_text: And went away again beyond Jordan into the place where John at first baptized; and there he abode.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀπῆλθεν πάλιν πέραν τοῦ Ἰορδάνου εἰς τὸν τόπον ὅπου ἦν Ἰωάννης τὸ πρῶτον βαπτίζων καὶ ἔμεινεν ἐκεῖ
verses.transliteration: kai apēlthen palin peran tou Iordanou eis ton topon hopou ēn Iōannēs to prōton baptizōn kai emeinen ekei
verses.study_note: Jesus withdraws to Bethany beyond Jordan (cf. 1:28) — where his public ministry began with John's testimony. The circle closes: from baptism to this retreat. He 'abode there' (emeinen ekei — remained, stayed; menō: the Johannine word for abiding). A period of rest before the final journey to Jerusalem and the cross.

verses.kjv_text: And many resorted unto him, and said, John did no miracle: but all things that John spake of this man were true.
verses.original_text: καὶ πολλοὶ ἦλθον πρὸς αὐτὸν καὶ ἔλεγον ὅτι Ἰωάννης μὲν σημεῖον ἐποίησεν οὐδέν πάντα δὲ ὅσα εἶπεν Ἰωάννης περὶ τούτου ἀληθῆ ἦν
verses.transliteration: kai polloi ēlthon pros auton kai elegon hoti Iōannēs men sēmeion epoiēsen ouden panta de hosa eipen Iōannēs peri toutou alēthē ēn
verses.study_note: 'John did no miracle' (Iōannēs sēmeion epoiēsen ouden — John performed no sign). 'But all things that John spake of this man were true' (panta hosa eipen Iōannēs peri toutou alēthē ēn). John the Baptist's witness is vindicated: he did no miracles, but his testimony was completely accurate. John was the witness; Jesus is the one witnessed to. The contrast: John's authority was in his word; Jesus' authority is in both word and works.

verses.kjv_text: And many believed on him there.
verses.original_text: καὶ πολλοὶ ἐπίστευσαν εἰς αὐτὸν ἐκεῖ
verses.transliteration: kai polloi episteusan eis auton ekei
verses.study_note: 'Many believed on him there' (polloi episteusan eis auton ekei). The chapter that began with hostility in Jerusalem ends with faith beyond Jordan. Where John's preparatory ministry laid the groundwork, Jesus' presence brings the harvest. The simple statement is a quiet triumph: in the place of beginnings, many come to faith.

meta.overview: John 11 records the climactic sign in the Book of Signs: the raising of Lazarus from the dead. This miracle is the seventh and greatest of Jesus' signs in John, and it precipitates the decision to put him to death — the very act that will accomplish the resurrection he has just demonstrated. The chapter opens with the illness of Lazarus of Bethany, brother of Mary and Martha (vv.1-3). Upon hearing the news, Jesus deliberately delays two days (v.6), declaring, 'This sickness is not unto death, but for the glory of God, that the Son of God might be glorified thereby' (v.4). He tells the disciples plainly, 'Lazarus is dead. And I am glad for your sakes that I was not there, to the intent ye may believe' (vv.14-15). Thomas responds with grim loyalty: 'Let us also go, that we may die with him' (v.16). Upon arrival, Jesus finds Lazarus has been in the tomb four days (v.17). Martha meets him first and confesses, 'Lord, if thou hadst been here, my brother had not died. But I know, that even now, whatsoever thou wilt ask of God, God will give it thee' (vv.21-22). Jesus declares: 'I am the resurrection, and the life: he that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live: And whosoever liveth and believeth in me shall never die' (vv.25-26) — the fifth great 'I AM' statement in John. Martha responds with the model confession: 'Yea, Lord: I believe that thou art the Christ, the Son of God, which should come into the world' (v.27). Mary comes weeping to Jesus, the Jews follow weeping, and then: 'Jesus wept' (v.35) — the shortest verse in the Bible, yet one of the most profound, revealing the full humanity and compassion of the incarnate Son. At the tomb, Jesus commands the stone removed. Martha protests — 'Lord, by this time he stinketh: for he hath been dead four days' (v.39). Jesus prays publicly to the Father, then cries with a loud voice, 'Lazarus, come forth' (v.43). The dead man emerges, bound in grave clothes, and Jesus says, 'Loose him, and let him go' (v.44). Many believe, but some report to the Pharisees. The Sanhedrin convenes and Caiaphas prophesies unwittingly: 'It is expedient for us, that one man should die for the people, and that the whole nation perish not' (v.50). John notes that Caiaphas spoke not of himself but prophesied that Jesus would die 'for that nation; and not for that nation only, but that also he should gather together in one the children of God that were scattered abroad' (vv.51-52). From that day they plot to kill him (v.53).
meta.theme: The Resurrection and the Life — Death Conquered, Death Decreed
meta.keyWordMeaning: Resurrection, rising up, standing again — from ana (up, again) + histēmi (to stand). In John 11, Jesus declares himself to be the anastasis: not merely one who performs resurrection but one who IS the resurrection (v.25). This is the definitive claim: life and resurrection are not events Jesus causes externally but realities he embodies personally. 'He that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live' (v.25). The raising of Lazarus demonstrates this claim visibly — the dead man rises because the Resurrection himself stands at the tomb and commands it. The irony of the chapter is that this demonstration of resurrection power leads directly to the decree of Jesus' own death (v.53), which will itself lead to the ultimate resurrection (ch.20).
outline: The Sickness of Lazarus (vv.1-6): Now a certain man was sick named Lazarus of Bethany the town of Mary and her sister Martha. It was that Mary which anointed the Lord with ointment and wiped his feet with her hair whose brother Lazarus was sick. Therefore his sisters sent unto him saying Lord behold he whom thou lovest is sick. When Jesus heard that he said This sickness is not unto death but for the glory of God that the Son of God might be glorified thereby. Now Jesus loved Martha and her sister and Lazarus. When he had heard therefore that he was sick he abode two days still in the same place where he was

Jesus Goes to Judaea (vv.7-16): Then after that saith he to his disciples Let us go into Judaea again. His disciples say unto him Master the Jews of late sought to stone thee and goest thou thither again? Jesus answered Are there not twelve hours in the day? If any man walk in the day he stumbleth not because he seeth the light of this world. But if a man walk in the night he stumbleth because there is no light in him. These things said he and after that he saith unto them Our friend Lazarus sleepeth but I go that I may awake him out of sleep. Then said his disciples Lord if he sleep he shall do well. Howbeit Jesus spake of his death but they thought that he had spoken of taking of rest in sleep. Then said Jesus unto them plainly Lazarus is dead. And I am glad for your sakes that I was not there to the intent ye may believe. Nevertheless let us go unto him. Then said Thomas which is called Didymus unto his fellowdisciples Let us also go that we may die with him

I Am the Resurrection and the Life (vv.17-27): Then when Jesus came he found that he had lain in the grave four days already. Now Bethany was nigh unto Jerusalem about fifteen furlongs off. And many of the Jews came to Martha and Mary to comfort them concerning their brother. Then Martha as soon as she heard that Jesus was coming went and met him but Mary sat still in the house. Then said Martha unto Jesus Lord if thou hadst been here my brother had not died. But I know that even now whatsoever thou wilt ask of God God will give it thee. Jesus saith unto her Thy brother shall rise again. Martha saith unto him I know that he shall rise again in the resurrection at the last day. Jesus said unto her I am the resurrection and the life he that believeth in me though he were dead yet shall he live. And whosoever liveth and believeth in me shall never die. Believest thou this? She saith unto him Yea Lord I believe that thou art the Christ the Son of God which should come into the world

Jesus Weeps (vv.28-37): And when she had so said she went her way and called Mary her sister secretly saying The Master is come and calleth for thee. As soon as she heard that she arose quickly and came unto him. Now Jesus was not yet come into the town but was in that place where Martha met him. The Jews then which were with her in the house and comforted her when they saw Mary that she rose up hastily and went out followed her saying She goeth unto the grave to weep there. Then when Mary was come where Jesus was and saw him she fell down at his feet saying unto him Lord if thou hadst been here my brother had not died. When Jesus therefore saw her weeping and the Jews also weeping which came with her he groaned in the spirit and was troubled. And said Where have ye laid him? They said unto him Lord come and see. Jesus wept. Then said the Jews Behold how he loved him! And some of them said Could not this man which opened the eyes of the blind have caused that even this man should not have died?

Lazarus Come Forth (vv.38-44): Jesus therefore again groaning in himself cometh to the grave. It was a cave and a stone lay upon it. Jesus said Take ye away the stone. Martha the sister of him that was dead saith unto him Lord by this time he stinketh for he hath been dead four days. Jesus saith unto her Said I not unto thee that if thou wouldest believe thou shouldest see the glory of God? Then they took away the stone from the place where the dead was laid. And Jesus lifted up his eyes and said Father I thank thee that thou hast heard me. And I knew that thou hearest me always but because of the people which stand by I said it that they may believe that thou hast sent me. And when he thus had spoken he cried with a loud voice Lazarus come forth. And he that was dead came forth bound hand and foot with graveclothes and his face was bound about with a napkin. Jesus saith unto them Loose him and let him go

The Plot to Kill Jesus (vv.45-57): Then many of the Jews which came to Mary and had seen the things which Jesus did believed on him. But some of them went their ways to the Pharisees and told them what things Jesus had done. Then gathered the chief priests and the Pharisees a council and said What do we? For this man doeth many miracles. If we let him thus alone all men will believe on him and the Romans shall come and take away both our place and nation. And one of them named Caiaphas being the high priest that same year said unto them Ye know nothing at all nor consider that it is expedient for us that one man should die for the people and that the whole nation perish not. And this spake he not of himself but being high priest that year he prophesied that Jesus should die for that nation and not for that nation only but that also he should gather together in one the children of God that were scattered abroad. Then from that day forth they took counsel together for to put him to death. Jesus therefore walked no more openly among the Jews but went thence unto a country near to the wilderness into a city called Ephraim and there continued with his disciples. And the Jews' passover was nigh at hand and many went out of the country up to Jerusalem before the passover to purify themselves. Then sought they for Jesus and spake among themselves as they stood in the temple What think ye that he will not come to the feast? Now both the chief priests and the Pharisees had given a commandment that if any man knew where he were he should shew it that they might take him

verses.kjv_text: Now a certain man was sick, named Lazarus, of Bethany, the town of Mary and her sister Martha.
verses.original_text: ἦν δέ τις ἀσθενῶν Λάζαρος ἀπὸ Βηθανίας ἐκ τῆς κώμης Μαρίας καὶ Μάρθας τῆς ἀδελφῆς αὐτῆς
verses.transliteration: ēn de tis asthenōn Lazaros apo Bēthanias ek tēs kōmēs Marias kai Marthas tēs adelphēs autēs
verses.study_note: 'Lazarus' (Lazaros — Greek form of the Hebrew El'azar, meaning 'God has helped'). The name is theologically fitting: God will indeed help, but in a way far beyond human expectation. 'Bethany' (Bēthania — 'House of Affliction' or 'House of Dates'), about two miles east of Jerusalem on the Mount of Olives (v.18). John identifies the town by its association with Mary and Martha, assuming his readers know them. This Lazarus is distinct from the beggar in Luke 16:19-31.

verses.kjv_text: It was that Mary which anointed the Lord with ointment, and wiped his feet with her hair, whose brother Lazarus was sick.
verses.original_text: ἦν δὲ Μαριὰμ ἡ ἀλείψασα τὸν κύριον μύρῳ καὶ ἐκμάξασα τοὺς πόδας αὐτοῦ ταῖς θριξὶν αὐτῆς ἧς ὁ ἀδελφὸς Λάζαρος ἠσθένει
verses.transliteration: ēn de Mariam hē aleipsasa ton kyrion myrō kai ekmaxasa tous podas autou tais thrixin autēs hēs ho adelphos Lazaros ēsthenei
verses.study_note: John identifies Mary by an event he has not yet narrated (the anointing occurs in 12:1-8), suggesting his readers already know the story from oral tradition or the Synoptic Gospels (cf. Mark 14:3-9). 'Anointed the Lord with ointment' (aleipsasa ton kyrion myrō) — the use of 'Lord' (kyrios) here is significant: Mary anointed the one she recognised as Lord. The anticipatory reference creates narrative tension: the one whose sister anoints Jesus for burial is about to die and be raised.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore his sisters sent unto him, saying, Lord, behold, he whom thou lovest is sick.
verses.original_text: ἀπέστειλαν οὖν αἱ ἀδελφαὶ πρὸς αὐτὸν λέγουσαι Κύριε ἴδε ὃν φιλεῖς ἀσθενεῖ
verses.transliteration: apesteilan oun hai adelphai pros auton legousai Kyrie ide hon phileis asthenei
verses.study_note: 'He whom thou lovest' (hon phileis — the one you love with affection, phileō). The sisters do not ask for healing; they simply inform Jesus and trust his love. This is a model prayer: stating the need before the Lord without dictating the response. 'Is sick' (asthenei — is weak, ill) — the same root as asthenōn in v.1. The message conveys both urgency and trust: surely the one who loves Lazarus will come.

verses.kjv_text: When Jesus heard that, he said, This sickness is not unto death, but for the glory of God, that the Son of God might be glorified thereby.
verses.original_text: ἀκούσας δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς εἶπεν Αὕτη ἡ ἀσθένεια οὐκ ἔστιν πρὸς θάνατον ἀλλ᾽ ὑπὲρ τῆς δόξης τοῦ θεοῦ ἵνα δοξασθῇ ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ δι᾽ αὐτῆς
verses.transliteration: akousas de ho Iēsous eipen Hautē hē astheneia ouk estin pros thanaton all hyper tēs doxēs tou theou hina doxasthē ho huios tou theou di autēs
verses.study_note: 'Not unto death' (ouk estin pros thanaton — not directed toward death as its final outcome). Lazarus will die, but death will not have the last word. 'For the glory of God' (hyper tēs doxēs tou theou — on behalf of God's glory). The purpose of suffering here is explicitly theological: it serves the manifestation of divine glory through the Son. 'That the Son of God might be glorified' — the raising of Lazarus glorifies both Father and Son, revealing Jesus' sovereign power over death.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse establishes a principle that applies beyond Lazarus: suffering and even death can serve the glory of God. The sickness was real, the death was real, but neither was the final word. God's glory is revealed not in the prevention of suffering but in the demonstration of power through and beyond suffering. This is the pattern of the cross itself.

verses.kjv_text: Now Jesus loved Martha, and her sister, and Lazarus.
verses.original_text: ἠγάπα δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς τὴν Μάρθαν καὶ τὴν ἀδελφὴν αὐτῆς καὶ τὸν Λάζαρον
verses.transliteration: ēgapa de ho Iēsous tēn Marthan kai tēn adelphēn autēs kai ton Lazaron
verses.study_note: John uses agapaō (ēgapa — he loved) here, the verb of deliberate, self-giving love, while the sisters used phileō (v.3, personal affection). John alternates these verbs throughout the Gospel (cf. 21:15-17). The statement is placed strategically before the delay in v.6: Jesus loved them — therefore he waited. The delay was not indifference but sovereign love acting according to a higher purpose. Martha is named first, consistent with her role as the elder sister and household manager (cf. Luke 10:38-42).

verses.kjv_text: When he had heard therefore that he was sick, he abode two days still in the same place where he was.
verses.original_text: ὡς οὖν ἤκουσεν ὅτι ἀσθενεῖ τότε μὲν ἔμεινεν ἐν ᾧ ἦν τόπῳ δύο ἡμέρας
verses.transliteration: hōs oun ēkousen hoti asthenei tote men emeinen en hō ēn topō dyo hēmeras
verses.study_note: 'He abode two days still' (emeinen... dyo hēmeras — he remained two days). The 'therefore' (oun) is startling: because he loved them, he stayed. Jesus' delay ensured Lazarus would be dead four days by the time he arrived (v.17), removing any possibility of claiming a premature burial or a resuscitation of someone not truly dead. Rabbinic tradition held that the soul lingered near the body for three days hoping to re-enter; after four days, death was considered irreversible. Jesus waited to make the miracle undeniable.

verses.kjv_text: Then after that saith he to his disciples, Let us go into Judaea again.
verses.original_text: ἔπειτα μετὰ τοῦτο λέγει τοῖς μαθηταῖς Ἄγωμεν εἰς τὴν Ἰουδαίαν πάλιν
verses.transliteration: epeita meta touto legei tois mathētais Agōmen eis tēn Ioudaian palin
verses.study_note: 'Let us go into Judaea again' (agōmen eis tēn Ioudaian palin — the subjunctive agōmen means 'let us go together'). 'Again' (palin) recalls the danger: Jesus had withdrawn from Judaea because of threats (10:39-40). Now he deliberately returns, knowing the danger. The disciples are alarmed (v.8). Jesus walks toward death — not his friend's, but his own — for the raising of Lazarus will set in motion the events leading to Calvary (vv.47-53).

verses.kjv_text: His disciples say unto him, Master, the Jews of late sought to stone thee; and goest thou thither again?
verses.original_text: λέγουσιν αὐτῷ οἱ μαθηταί Ῥαββί νῦν ἐζήτουν σε λιθάσαι οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι καὶ πάλιν ὑπάγεις ἐκεῖ
verses.transliteration: legousin autō hoi mathētai Rhabbi nyn ezētoun se lithasai hoi Ioudaioi kai palin hypageis ekei
verses.study_note: 'Master' (Rhabbi — Rabbi, Teacher). 'Of late sought to stone thee' (nyn ezētoun se lithasai — just recently they were seeking to stone you, referring to 10:31-33). The disciples' fear is reasonable: the last visit to Jerusalem nearly ended in death. 'Goest thou thither again?' expresses incredulity. The disciples think in terms of self-preservation; Jesus thinks in terms of the Father's will and the appointed 'hour.'

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered, Are there not twelve hours in the day? If any man walk in the day, he stumbleth not, because he seeth the light of this world.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη Ἰησοῦς Οὐχὶ δώδεκα ὧραί εἰσιν τῆς ἡμέρας ἐάν τις περιπατῇ ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ οὐ προσκόπτει ὅτι τὸ φῶς τοῦ κόσμου τούτου βλέπει
verses.transliteration: apekrithē Iēsous Ouchi dōdeka hōrai eisin tēs hēmeras ean tis peripatē en tē hēmera ou proskoptei hoti to phōs tou kosmou toutou blepei
verses.study_note: 'Twelve hours in the day' — Jesus uses the fixed daylight hours as a metaphor for his divinely appointed time of ministry. As long as he walks within the Father's will ('the day'), he cannot be harmed prematurely. 'He stumbleth not' (ou proskoptei — does not stumble, trip, or fall). The 'light of this world' here refers to the natural sun, but the double meaning is unmistakable: Jesus himself is the light of the world (8:12; 9:5). Walking in his light means walking safely within God's plan.

verses.kjv_text: But if a man walk in the night, he stumbleth, because there is no light in him.
verses.original_text: ἐὰν δέ τις περιπατῇ ἐν τῇ νυκτί προσκόπτει ὅτι τὸ φῶς οὐκ ἔστιν ἐν αὐτῷ
verses.transliteration: ean de tis peripatē en tē nykti proskoptei hoti to phōs ouk estin en autō
verses.study_note: 'In the night' (en tē nykti) — walking outside God's will, acting in spiritual darkness. 'There is no light in him' (to phōs ouk estin en autō — the light is not in him). John's Gospel contrasts light and darkness throughout (1:5; 3:19-21; 8:12; 12:35-36). Judas will go out 'and it was night' (13:30). The one who acts apart from God's timing and purposes stumbles because he lacks internal illumination — the guidance that comes from walking with God.

verses.kjv_text: These things said he: and after that he saith unto them, Our friend Lazarus sleepeth; but I go, that I may awake him out of sleep.
verses.original_text: ταῦτα εἶπεν καὶ μετὰ τοῦτο λέγει αὐτοῖς Λάζαρος ὁ φίλος ἡμῶν κεκοίμηται ἀλλὰ πορεύομαι ἵνα ἐξυπνίσω αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: tauta eipen kai meta touto legei autois Lazaros ho philos hēmōn kekoimētai alla poreuomai hina exypnisō auton
verses.study_note: 'Our friend' (ho philos hēmōn — our beloved friend; philos, from phileō). 'Sleepeth' (kekoimētai — has fallen asleep; perfect tense of koimaomai, to sleep). Jesus uses sleep as a euphemism for death — a usage common in both the OT (Daniel 12:2; Psalm 13:3) and the NT (1 Thessalonians 4:13-14; 1 Corinthians 15:51). For Jesus, death is merely sleep because he has the power to 'awake' (exypnisō — rouse from sleep) the dead. The metaphor implies that death for the believer is temporary and reversible.

verses.kjv_text: Then said his disciples, Lord, if he sleep, he shall do well.
verses.original_text: εἶπον οὖν οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ Κύριε εἰ κεκοίμηται σωθήσεται
verses.transliteration: eipon oun hoi mathētai autou Kyrie ei kekoimētai sōthēsetai
verses.study_note: 'If he sleep, he shall do well' (ei kekoimētai sōthēsetai — if he has fallen asleep, he will be saved/recover). The disciples take Jesus literally: natural sleep in illness is a sign of recovery. 'Shall do well' (sōthēsetai — will be saved, healed, restored; from sōzō). The misunderstanding is characteristic of Johannine irony: the disciples think the crisis is past, when in fact the greatest crisis — and the greatest miracle — lies ahead.

verses.kjv_text: Howbeit Jesus spake of his death: but they thought that he had spoken of taking of rest in sleep.
verses.original_text: εἰρήκει δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς περὶ τοῦ θανάτου αὐτοῦ ἐκεῖνοι δὲ ἔδοξαν ὅτι περὶ τῆς κοιμήσεως τοῦ ὕπνου λέγει
verses.transliteration: eirēkei de ho Iēsous peri tou thanatou autou ekeinoi de edoxan hoti peri tēs koimēseōs tou hypnou legei
verses.study_note: John's editorial clarification. 'Death' (thanatos) versus 'rest in sleep' (koimēsis tou hypnou — the repose of natural sleep). The disciples' misunderstanding highlights the difference between human and divine perspective: they see illness; Jesus sees death. They hope for recovery; Jesus plans resurrection. This is the Johannine pattern of earthly versus heavenly understanding (cf. 3:3-4 with Nicodemus; 4:10-11 with the Samaritan woman).

verses.kjv_text: Then said Jesus unto them plainly, Lazarus is dead.
verses.original_text: τότε οὖν εἶπεν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς παρρησίᾳ Λάζαρος ἀπέθανεν
verses.transliteration: tote oun eipen autois ho Iēsous parrēsia Lazaros apethanen
verses.study_note: 'Plainly' (parrēsia — openly, with boldness, without metaphor). 'Lazarus is dead' (Lazaros apethanen — Lazarus died; aorist tense: a definite past event). Jesus removes all ambiguity. The blunt declaration contrasts with the gentle euphemism of v.11. There is no sentimentality: Jesus states the fact because the fact is the foundation of the miracle. Death must be real before resurrection can be real. The verb apethanen (he died) is simple and final — and yet, for Jesus, it is not the last word.

verses.kjv_text: And I am glad for your sakes that I was not there, to the intent ye may believe; nevertheless let us go unto him.
verses.original_text: καὶ χαίρω δι᾽ ὑμᾶς ἵνα πιστεύσητε ὅτι οὐκ ἤμην ἐκεῖ ἀλλ᾽ ἄγωμεν πρὸς αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: kai chairō di hymas hina pisteusēte hoti ouk ēmēn ekei all agōmen pros auton
verses.study_note: 'I am glad' (chairō — I rejoice). Jesus rejoices not in death but in the opportunity for faith: 'that ye may believe' (hina pisteusēte — purpose clause: so that you might come to believe). Had Jesus been present, he would have healed Lazarus (as the sisters note, vv.21, 32), and the disciples would have witnessed a healing — impressive but not unprecedented. By allowing death, Jesus creates the occasion for resurrection, a far greater revelation of his glory and identity. 'Let us go unto him' (agōmen pros auton — let us go to him) — Jesus goes to the dead man, just as the Good Shepherd goes to the sheep.

verses.kjv_text: Then said Thomas, which is called Didymus, unto his fellowdisciples, Let us also go, that we may die with him.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν οὖν Θωμᾶς ὁ λεγόμενος Δίδυμος τοῖς συμμαθηταῖς Ἄγωμεν καὶ ἡμεῖς ἵνα ἀποθάνωμεν μετ᾽ αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: eipen oun Thōmas ho legomenos Didymos tois symmathētais Agōmen kai hēmeis hina apothanōmen met autou
verses.study_note: 'Thomas, which is called Didymus' (Thōmas... Didymos — both words mean 'twin,' the first Aramaic, the second Greek). 'Let us also go, that we may die with him' — a statement of courageous loyalty, not despair. Thomas expects death in Judaea (cf. v.8) but is willing to share it. This is the same Thomas who later doubts the resurrection (20:25) — John portrays him with psychological complexity. His loyalty is genuine even when his faith is incomplete. 'With him' (met autou) probably refers to Jesus, not Lazarus.

verses.kjv_text: Then when Jesus came, he found that he had lain in the grave four days already.
verses.original_text: ἐλθὼν οὖν ὁ Ἰησοῦς εὗρεν αὐτὸν τέσσαρας ἤδη ἡμέρας ἔχοντα ἐν τῷ μνημείῳ
verses.transliteration: elthōn oun ho Iēsous heuren auton tessaras ēdē hēmeras echonta en tō mnēmeiō
verses.study_note: 'Four days already' (tessaras ēdē hēmeras — four days already in the tomb). The chronology: one day for the message to reach Jesus, two days of delay (v.6), one day for the journey = four days. The four-day detail is crucial: by Jewish reckoning, after three days decomposition was advanced and the soul was considered permanently departed. Four days placed the death beyond any natural possibility of recovery. This is not a coma, not a premature burial — it is verified, irreversible death. Jesus meets death at its most definitive.

verses.kjv_text: Now Bethany was nigh unto Jerusalem, about fifteen furlongs off:
verses.original_text: ἦν δὲ ἡ Βηθανία ἐγγὺς τῶν Ἱεροσολύμων ὡς ἀπὸ σταδίων δεκαπέντε
verses.transliteration: ēn de hē Bēthania engys tōn Hierosolymōn hōs apo stadiōn dekapente
verses.study_note: 'Fifteen furlongs' (stadiōn dekapente — about 15 stadia, roughly 1.7 miles or 2.8 km). John notes the proximity to Jerusalem to explain why many Jerusalem Jews have come to mourn (v.19) and why news of the miracle will quickly reach the Pharisees (v.46). The nearness also foreshadows Jesus' final entry into Jerusalem (12:1, 12). Archaeological identification places Bethany at modern el-Azariyeh ('Place of Lazarus') on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives.

verses.kjv_text: And many of the Jews came to Martha and Mary, to comfort them concerning their brother.
verses.original_text: πολλοὶ δὲ ἐκ τῶν Ἰουδαίων ἐληλύθεισαν πρὸς τὴν Μάρθαν καὶ Μαριὰμ ἵνα παραμυθήσωνται αὐτὰς περὶ τοῦ ἀδελφοῦ
verses.transliteration: polloi de ek tōn Ioudaiōn elēlytheisan pros tēn Marthan kai Mariam hina paramythēsōntai autas peri tou adelphou
verses.study_note: 'Many of the Jews' (polloi... ek tōn Ioudaiōn) — Jerusalem Jews who knew the family, likely of some social standing. 'To comfort' (paramythēsōntai — to console, encourage alongside; from para + mythos). Jewish mourning customs included a seven-day period (shiv'ah) of intense grieving, during which neighbours and friends visited to comfort the bereaved. The visitors become eyewitnesses of the miracle (v.45) and potential witnesses in Jerusalem. Their presence serves God's purpose in making the sign public.

verses.kjv_text: Then Martha, as soon as she heard that Jesus was coming, went and met him: but Mary sat still in the house.
verses.original_text: ἡ οὖν Μάρθα ὡς ἤκουσεν ὅτι Ἰησοῦς ἔρχεται ὑπήντησεν αὐτῷ Μαριὰμ δὲ ἐν τῷ οἴκῳ ἐκαθέζετο
verses.transliteration: hē oun Martha hōs ēkousen hoti Iēsous erchetai hypēntēsen autō Mariam de en tō oikō ekathezeto
verses.study_note: Martha's active response contrasts with Mary's stillness — consistent with their characterisation in Luke 10:38-42. Martha is the doer, the initiator; Mary is the contemplative. 'Went and met him' (hypēntēsen autō — went to meet him, went out to encounter him). 'Sat still in the house' (en tō oikō ekathezeto — was sitting in the house), perhaps immobilised by grief, or receiving mourners. Both sisters will say the same words to Jesus (vv.21, 32), but their manner of approach differs.

verses.kjv_text: Then said Martha unto Jesus, Lord, if thou hadst been here, my brother had not died.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν οὖν ἡ Μάρθα πρὸς τὸν Ἰησοῦν Κύριε εἰ ἦς ὧδε οὐκ ἂν ἀπέθανεν ὁ ἀδελφός μου
verses.transliteration: eipen oun hē Martha pros ton Iēsoun Kyrie ei ēs hōde ouk an apethanen ho adelphos mou
verses.study_note: 'Lord, if thou hadst been here' (Kyrie ei ēs hōde — Lord, if you had been here). This is a statement of faith, not reproach: Martha believes in Jesus' power to heal. 'My brother had not died' (ouk an apethanen ho adelphos mou — my brother would not have died; contrary-to-fact condition). Martha expresses what she knows: Jesus' presence prevents death. What she does not yet know is that Jesus' power extends beyond prevention to reversal — he can not only prevent death but conquer it.

verses.kjv_text: But I know, that even now, whatsoever thou wilt ask of God, God will give it thee.
verses.original_text: ἀλλὰ καὶ νῦν οἶδα ὅτι ὅσα ἂν αἰτήσῃ τὸν θεόν δώσει σοι ὁ θεός
verses.transliteration: alla kai nyn oida hoti hosa an aitēsē ton theon dōsei soi ho theos
verses.study_note: 'Even now' (kai nyn — even at this point, even after death). Martha's faith reaches beyond the moment: she believes Jesus can still do something, though she may not know exactly what. 'Whatsoever thou wilt ask of God' (hosa an aitēsē ton theon — whatever you might ask God). Some interpret this as tentative hope for resurrection; others as a general confession of Jesus' relationship with the Father. Jesus will correct her theology slightly: he does not ask the Father for resurrection power — he possesses it inherently as the Son (v.25).

verses.kjv_text: Jesus saith unto her, Thy brother shall rise again.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῇ ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ἀναστήσεται ὁ ἀδελφός σου
verses.transliteration: legei autē ho Iēsous Anastēsetai ho adelphos sou
verses.study_note: 'Shall rise again' (anastēsetai — will rise up, will stand again; future middle of anistēmi). Jesus' statement is deliberately ambiguous: it could mean the final resurrection (as Martha takes it, v.24) or an immediate raising. Jesus uses the ambiguity to draw out Martha's theology and then transcend it. This is the Johannine pattern: begin with a statement the hearer interprets on one level, then elevate it to a higher plane (cf. 2:19-21; 3:3-5; 4:10-14).

verses.kjv_text: Martha saith unto him, I know that he shall rise again in the resurrection at the last day.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ Μάρθα Οἶδα ὅτι ἀναστήσεται ἐν τῇ ἀναστάσει ἐν τῇ ἐσχάτῃ ἡμέρᾳ
verses.transliteration: legei autō Martha Oida hoti anastēsetai en tē anastasei en tē eschatē hēmera
verses.study_note: 'In the resurrection at the last day' (en tē anastasei en tē eschatē hēmera — in the resurrection on the last day). Martha confesses the standard Pharisaic belief in future bodily resurrection (cf. Daniel 12:2; Acts 23:8). This is orthodox and correct — but it is comfort deferred. Martha knows the doctrine but needs the person. Jesus will now make the stunning claim that future resurrection is not merely a distant event but a present reality embodied in himself.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus said unto her, I am the resurrection, and the life: he that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live:
verses.original_text: εἶπεν αὐτῇ ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ ἀνάστασις καὶ ἡ ζωή ὁ πιστεύων εἰς ἐμὲ κἂν ἀποθάνῃ ζήσεται
verses.transliteration: eipen autē ho Iēsous Egō eimi hē anastasis kai hē zōē ho pisteuōn eis eme kan apothanē zēsetai
verses.study_note: 'I am the resurrection, and the life' (Egō eimi hē anastasis kai hē zōē) — the fifth 'I AM' declaration in John (following 6:35; 8:12; 10:7, 11). Jesus does not say 'I perform resurrection' or 'I will cause the resurrection' but 'I AM the resurrection.' Resurrection is not merely an event at the last day; it is a person standing before Martha. 'He that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live' — physical death does not end the life of the believer because the source of life is the living Christ. This is the heart of Christian hope.
verses.doctrinal_note: THE RESURRECTION AND THE LIFE: This is one of the most important christological declarations in Scripture. (1) 'I AM' (egō eimi) echoes the divine name (Exodus 3:14). (2) Jesus claims to BE resurrection, not merely to cause it — making resurrection a personal, relational reality. (3) 'Though he were dead, yet shall he live' promises that physical death is not final for the believer. (4) The claim is immediately demonstrated in the raising of Lazarus (vv.43-44). (5) It will be ultimately vindicated in Jesus' own resurrection (ch.20). This verse is the theological centre of John 11 and one of the great christological pillars of the NT.

verses.kjv_text: And whosoever liveth and believeth in me shall never die. Believest thou this?
verses.original_text: καὶ πᾶς ὁ ζῶν καὶ πιστεύων εἰς ἐμὲ οὐ μὴ ἀποθάνῃ εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα πιστεύεις τοῦτο
verses.transliteration: kai pas ho zōn kai pisteuōn eis eme ou mē apothanē eis ton aiōna pisteueis touto
verses.study_note: 'Shall never die' (ou mē apothanē eis ton aiōna — shall by no means die unto the age; double negative for emphatic denial). Two complementary promises: (1) v.25: even the physically dead believer will live; (2) v.26: the living believer will never truly die. Together they cover all cases: death cannot separate the believer from life in Christ. 'Believest thou this?' (pisteueis touto — do you believe this?) — Jesus presses for personal faith, not mere doctrinal assent. The question is the pivot of the narrative: everything depends on the answer.

verses.kjv_text: She saith unto him, Yea, Lord: I believe that thou art the Christ, the Son of God, which should come into the world.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ Ναί κύριε ἐγὼ πεπίστευκα ὅτι σὺ εἶ ὁ χριστός ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ ὁ εἰς τὸν κόσμον ἐρχόμενος
verses.transliteration: legei autō Nai kyrie egō pepisteuка hoti sy ei ho christos ho huios tou theou ho eis ton kosmon erchomenos
verses.study_note: 'Yea, Lord: I believe' (Nai kyrie egō pepisteuка — Yes, Lord, I have believed and continue to believe; perfect tense indicating settled conviction). Martha's confession is the Johannine equivalent of Peter's confession (Matthew 16:16). Three titles: (1) 'the Christ' (ho christos — the Messiah, the anointed one); (2) 'the Son of God' (ho huios tou theou — not merely messianic title but divine relationship); (3) 'which should come into the world' (ho eis ton kosmon erchomenos — the Coming One, cf. Malachi 3:1; Psalm 118:26). This is the model confession in John's Gospel, the response the entire book aims to produce (20:31).
verses.doctrinal_note: Martha's confession parallels and matches the stated purpose of John's Gospel: 'that ye might believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God; and that believing ye might have life through his name' (20:31). Her faith is exemplary not because it is complete in understanding — she still protests at the tomb (v.39) — but because it rests on the right person.

verses.kjv_text: And when she had so said, she went her way, and called Mary her sister secretly, saying, The Master is come, and calleth for thee.
verses.original_text: καὶ ταῦτα εἰποῦσα ἀπῆλθεν καὶ ἐφώνησεν Μαριὰμ τὴν ἀδελφὴν αὐτῆς λάθρᾳ εἰποῦσα Ὁ διδάσκαλος πάρεστιν καὶ φωνεῖ σε
verses.transliteration: kai tauta eipousa apēlthen kai ephōnēsen Mariam tēn adelphēn autēs lathra eipousa Ho didaskalos parestin kai phōnei se
verses.study_note: 'Secretly' (lathra — in secret, privately) — Martha calls Mary quietly, perhaps to avoid alerting the mourners or the hostile authorities. 'The Master' (ho didaskalos — the Teacher) — the title the sisters use for Jesus in private, suggesting an established relationship. 'Is come, and calleth for thee' (parestin kai phōnei se — is present and is calling you) — Jesus actively summons Mary. The Good Shepherd calls his own by name (10:3).

verses.kjv_text: As soon as she heard that, she arose quickly, and came unto him.
verses.original_text: ἐκείνη ὡς ἤκουσεν ἠγέρθη ταχὺ καὶ ἤρχετο πρὸς αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: ekeinē hōs ēkousen ēgerthē tachy kai ērcheto pros auton
verses.study_note: 'Arose quickly' (ēgerthē tachy — she rose up rapidly). Mary's response is immediate and wholehearted. The verb ēgerthē (was raised/rose up, passive of egeirō) is the same root used for resurrection. 'Came unto him' (ērcheto pros auton — was coming toward him; imperfect tense showing continuous action). Mary's quick response to Jesus' call mirrors the response of faith throughout John: when Jesus calls, those who know him come.

verses.kjv_text: Now Jesus was not yet come into the town, but was in that place where Martha met him.
verses.original_text: οὔπω δὲ ἐληλύθει ὁ Ἰησοῦς εἰς τὴν κώμην ἀλλ᾽ ἦν ἔτι ἐν τῷ τόπῳ ὅπου ὑπήντησεν αὐτῷ ἡ Μάρθα
verses.transliteration: oupō de elēlythei ho Iēsous eis tēn kōmēn all ēn eti en tō topō hopou hypēntēsen autō hē Martha
verses.study_note: Jesus remains outside the village at the place where Martha met him. This geographical note explains why Mary must go out to him and why the mourners follow (v.31). It also ensures that the encounter with Mary and the scene at the tomb happen in sequence, each building the emotional and theological intensity of the narrative.

verses.kjv_text: The Jews then which were with her in the house, and comforted her, when they saw Mary, that she rose up hastily and went out, followed her, saying, She goeth unto the grave to weep there.
verses.original_text: οἱ οὖν Ἰουδαῖοι οἱ ὄντες μετ᾽ αὐτῆς ἐν τῇ οἰκίᾳ καὶ παραμυθούμενοι αὐτήν ἰδόντες τὴν Μαριὰμ ὅτι ταχέως ἀνέστη καὶ ἐξῆλθεν ἠκολούθησαν αὐτῇ εἰπόντες ὅτι Ὑπάγει εἰς τὸ μνημεῖον ἵνα κλαύσῃ ἐκεῖ
verses.transliteration: hoi oun Ioudaioi hoi ontes met autēs en tē oikia kai paramythoumenoi autēn idontes tēn Mariam hoti tacheōs anestē kai exēlthen ēkolouthēsan autē eipontes hoti Hypagei eis to mnēmeion hina klausē ekei
verses.study_note: The mourners assume Mary is going to the tomb to weep (klausē — to weep loudly, wail). Their assumption is natural but wrong — she is going to Jesus. Their following her is providential: they become witnesses of the miracle. 'Rose up hastily' (tacheōs anestē — quickly stood up) — the same haste noted in v.29. 'Followed her' (ēkolouthēsan — they followed) uses the verb for discipleship (akoloutheō), though here the sense is physical following.

verses.kjv_text: Then when Mary was come where Jesus was, and saw him, she fell down at his feet, saying unto him, Lord, if thou hadst been here, my brother had not died.
verses.original_text: ἡ οὖν Μαριὰμ ὡς ἦλθεν ὅπου ἦν Ἰησοῦς ἰδοῦσα αὐτὸν ἔπεσεν αὐτοῦ πρὸς τοὺς πόδας λέγουσα αὐτῷ Κύριε εἰ ἦς ὧδε οὐκ ἂν μου ἀπέθανεν ὁ ἀδελφός
verses.transliteration: hē oun Mariam hōs ēlthen hopou ēn Iēsous idousa auton epesen autou pros tous podas legousa autō Kyrie ei ēs hōde ouk an mou apethanen ho adelphos
verses.study_note: Mary says the same words as Martha (v.21), but her manner is different: she 'fell down at his feet' (epesen autou pros tous podas — fell at his feet in worship/grief). Mary is consistently at Jesus' feet in the Gospels (Luke 10:39; John 12:3). She does not add Martha's 'even now' expression of hope (v.22) — her grief is more overwhelming, less theological, more emotional. Yet the posture of falling at Jesus' feet is itself an act of faith: she brings her grief to the right person.

verses.kjv_text: When Jesus therefore saw her weeping, and the Jews also weeping which came with her, he groaned in the spirit, and was troubled,
verses.original_text: Ἰησοῦς οὖν ὡς εἶδεν αὐτὴν κλαίουσαν καὶ τοὺς συνελθόντας αὐτῇ Ἰουδαίους κλαίοντας ἐνεβριμήσατο τῷ πνεύματι καὶ ἐτάραξεν ἑαυτόν
verses.transliteration: Iēsous oun hōs eiden autēn klaiousan kai tous synelthontas autē Ioudaious klaiontas enebrimēsato tō pneumati kai etaraxen heauton
verses.study_note: 'Groaned in the spirit' (enebrimēsato tō pneumati — was deeply moved with indignation/emotion in his spirit). Embrimaomai is a strong word: it can mean to snort with anger (used of horses), to be indignant, to be deeply agitated. Some scholars suggest Jesus is angered by death itself — its unnaturalness, its destructive power over those he loves. Others suggest anger at the unbelief surrounding him. 'Was troubled' (etaraxen heauton — he troubled himself; the active voice suggests Jesus allowed the emotion rather than being overwhelmed by it). The verse reveals the full humanity of Christ confronting the reality of death.

verses.kjv_text: And said, Where have ye laid him? They said unto him, Lord, come and see.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν Ποῦ τεθείκατε αὐτόν λέγουσιν αὐτῷ Κύριε ἔρχου καὶ ἴδε
verses.transliteration: kai eipen Pou tetheikate auton legousin autō Kyrie erchou kai ide
verses.study_note: 'Where have ye laid him?' (Pou tetheikate auton — where have you placed him?). Jesus asks not because he does not know (cf. 2:25; 6:6) but because he enters fully into the human experience of loss. 'Come and see' (erchou kai ide — come and see) — the same invitation used by Jesus to his first disciples (1:39, 46). Now the mourners invite Jesus to come and see death. He who said 'come and see life' is invited to come and see a grave.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus wept.
verses.original_text: ἐδάκρυσεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς
verses.transliteration: edakrysen ho Iēsous
verses.study_note: The shortest verse in the English Bible, but one of the most theologically significant. 'Wept' (edakrysen — shed tears; aorist of dakryō, to weep silently, with tears). This is a different word from klaō (to wail loudly) used of Mary and the Jews. Jesus does not wail; he weeps quietly, with deep feeling. The incarnate God weeps at a human grave. This is not theatrical — it is genuine grief. Yet Jesus weeps knowing he is about to raise Lazarus. His tears reveal that even when resurrection is certain, the present pain of death is real and worthy of compassion. The verse refutes both Docetism (Jesus was truly human, truly feeling) and Stoicism (emotion is not weakness but an expression of love).
verses.doctrinal_note: JESUS WEPT — THE HUMANITY OF THE SON: This verse is the supreme NT demonstration that the incarnation involved genuine human emotion. Jesus weeps not from helplessness but from love. He has the power to raise Lazarus and he knows he will — yet he still weeps. His tears affirm that grief is not lack of faith and that compassion is a divine attribute. 'In all their affliction he was afflicted' (Isaiah 63:9). 'We have not an high priest which cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities' (Hebrews 4:15).

verses.kjv_text: Then said the Jews, Behold how he loved him!
verses.original_text: ἔλεγον οὖν οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι Ἴδε πῶς ἐφίλει αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: elegon oun hoi Ioudaioi Ide pōs ephilei auton
verses.study_note: 'Behold how he loved him!' (Ide pōs ephilei auton — See how he was loving him!). The Jews use phileō (personal, affectionate love) — they recognise genuine human friendship. Their observation is correct but incomplete: Jesus' love extends far beyond what they can see. He loves Lazarus not only with human affection but with the eternal love of God, a love that will now express itself not merely in tears but in the conquest of death.

verses.kjv_text: And some of them said, Could not this man, which opened the eyes of the blind, have caused that even this man should not have died?
