verses.original_text: τινὲς δὲ ἐξ αὐτῶν εἶπον Οὐκ ἐδύνατο οὗτος ὁ ἀνοίξας τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς τοῦ τυφλοῦ ποιῆσαι ἵνα καὶ οὗτος μὴ ἀποθάνῃ
verses.transliteration: tines de ex autōn eipon Ouk edynato houtos ho anoixas tous ophthalmous tou typhlou poiēsai hina kai houtos mē apothanē
verses.study_note: 'Could not this man?' (Ouk edynato houtos — was not this man able?). They reference the healing of the blind man (ch.9), showing that miracle was widely known. Their question is posed negatively, implying doubt or criticism: if he could heal blindness, why not prevent death? The question is ironic: they wonder if Jesus could have prevented death, not imagining he can reverse it. They limit his power to prevention; Jesus will demonstrate power over death itself.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus therefore again groaning in himself cometh to the grave. It was a cave, and a stone lay upon it.
verses.original_text: Ἰησοῦς οὖν πάλιν ἐμβριμώμενος ἐν ἑαυτῷ ἔρχεται εἰς τὸ μνημεῖον ἦν δὲ σπήλαιον καὶ λίθος ἐπέκειτο ἐπ᾽ αὐτῷ
verses.transliteration: Iēsous oun palin embrimōmenos en heautō erchetai eis to mnēmeion ēn de spēlaion kai lithos epekeito ep autō
verses.study_note: 'Again groaning in himself' (palin embrimōmenos en heautō — again deeply moved/indignant within himself). The repetition of embrimaomai (cf. v.33) underscores the intensity of Jesus' emotion. 'A cave' (spēlaion — a cave, grotto) — typical Jewish burial: a cave or rock-cut tomb, natural or hewn. 'A stone lay upon it' (lithos epekeito ep autō — a stone was placed upon it). The stone sealed the tomb against animals and marked the finality of death. This stone must be removed before life can come forth — foreshadowing 20:1, where another stone is removed from another tomb.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus said, Take ye away the stone. Martha, the sister of him that was dead, saith unto him, Lord, by this time he stinketh: for he hath been dead four days.
verses.original_text: λέγει ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ἄρατε τὸν λίθον λέγει αὐτῷ ἡ ἀδελφὴ τοῦ τετελευτηκότος Μάρθα Κύριε ἤδη ὄζει τεταρταῖος γάρ ἐστιν
verses.transliteration: legei ho Iēsous Arate ton lithon legei autō hē adelphē tou teteleutēkotos Martha Kyrie ēdē ozei tetartaios gar estin
verses.study_note: 'Take ye away the stone' (Arate ton lithon — lift up the stone). Jesus could have spoken through the stone, but he involves human action — a pattern throughout his ministry (fill the waterpots, John 2:7; cast the net, John 21:6). 'He stinketh' (ēdē ozei — he already smells; present tense: the odour is already present). 'Four days' (tetartaios — a fourth-day one). Martha's protest reveals that her confession (v.27) has not yet fully grasped what Jesus is about to do. Faith in Jesus as the Christ does not automatically mean understanding every implication. Martha believes in who Jesus is but struggles with what he is about to do.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus saith unto her, Said I not unto thee, that, if thou wouldest believe, thou shouldest see the glory of God?
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῇ ὁ Ἰησοῦς Οὐκ εἶπόν σοι ὅτι ἐὰν πιστεύσῃς ὄψῃ τὴν δόξαν τοῦ θεοῦ
verses.transliteration: legei autē ho Iēsous Ouk eipon soi hoti ean pisteusēs opsē tēn doxan tou theou
verses.study_note: 'Said I not unto thee?' (Ouk eipon soi — Did I not tell you?). Jesus recalls v.4 ('for the glory of God') and vv.25-26 ('I am the resurrection'). 'If thou wouldest believe, thou shouldest see the glory of God' (ean pisteusēs opsē tēn doxan tou theou — if you believe, you will see God's glory). The order is significant: belief precedes sight. This is the opposite of the world's demand to see before believing. Glory (doxa) will be manifested in the resurrection of Lazarus — a visible display of divine power that rewards faith and invites it.

verses.kjv_text: Then they took away the stone from the place where the dead was laid. And Jesus lifted up his eyes, and said, Father, I thank thee that thou hast heard me.
verses.original_text: ἦραν οὖν τὸν λίθον ὁ δὲ Ἰησοῦς ἦρεν τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς ἄνω καὶ εἶπεν Πάτερ εὐχαριστῶ σοι ὅτι ἤκουσάς μου
verses.transliteration: ēran oun ton lithon ho de Iēsous ēren tous ophthalmous anō kai eipen Pater eucharistō soi hoti ēkousas mou
verses.study_note: 'Lifted up his eyes' (ēren tous ophthalmous anō — raised his eyes upward), the posture of prayer (cf. 17:1). 'Father, I thank thee' (Pater eucharistō soi — Father, I give thanks to you). Jesus prays not to obtain power but to acknowledge the source of power and to demonstrate his relationship with the Father. 'That thou hast heard me' (hoti ēkousas mou — that you heard me; aorist: already heard) — the answer is already given before the prayer is spoken publicly. Jesus' prayer life with the Father is continuous (v.42).

verses.kjv_text: And I knew that thou hearest me always: but because of the people which stand by I said it, that they may believe that thou hast sent me.
verses.original_text: ἐγὼ δὲ ᾔδειν ὅτι πάντοτέ μου ἀκούεις ἀλλὰ διὰ τὸν ὄχλον τὸν περιεστῶτα εἶπον ἵνα πιστεύσωσιν ὅτι σύ με ἀπέστειλας
verses.transliteration: egō de ēdein hoti pantote mou akoueis alla dia ton ochlon ton periestōta eipon hina pisteusōsin hoti sy me apesteilas
verses.study_note: 'Thou hearest me always' (pantote mou akoueis — you always hear me). Jesus enjoys unbroken communion with the Father — his prayer is never unheard. 'Because of the people which stand by' (dia ton ochlon ton periestōta — for the sake of the crowd standing around). The public prayer is for the benefit of the witnesses, not for Jesus' need. 'That they may believe that thou hast sent me' (hina pisteusōsin hoti sy me apesteilas) — the purpose of the sign is faith in Jesus' divine mission. Every miracle in John serves this evangelistic purpose (20:31).

verses.kjv_text: And when he thus had spoken, he cried with a loud voice, Lazarus, come forth.
verses.original_text: καὶ ταῦτα εἰπὼν φωνῇ μεγάλῃ ἐκραύγασεν Λάζαρε δεῦρο ἔξω
verses.transliteration: kai tauta eipōn phōnē megalē ekraugasen Lazare deuro exō
verses.study_note: 'Cried with a loud voice' (phōnē megalē ekraugasen — shouted with a great voice). The loud cry is not necessity but authority: Jesus speaks as the sovereign Lord of life and death. 'Lazarus, come forth' (Lazare deuro exō — Lazarus, come out here). The command is personal — Jesus calls Lazarus by name, as the Good Shepherd calls his sheep (10:3). Augustine noted: had Jesus not specified 'Lazarus,' all the dead would have come forth. The voice that created the world (1:3) now commands the dead to live. 'The dead shall hear the voice of the Son of God: and they that hear shall live' (5:25) is fulfilled before their eyes.
verses.doctrinal_note: THE VOICE OF THE SON OF GOD: This moment fulfils Jesus' own prophecy in 5:25, 28-29: 'The hour is coming, and now is, when the dead shall hear the voice of the Son of God: and they that hear shall live.' The raising of Lazarus is a preview of the general resurrection — the same voice, the same authority, the same power. What happens at this tomb will happen at every tomb on the last day. The personal summons ('Lazarus') reflects the intimate, individual nature of resurrection: God knows each by name.

verses.kjv_text: And he that was dead came forth, bound hand and foot with graveclothes: and his face was bound about with a napkin. Jesus saith unto them, Loose him, and let him go.
verses.original_text: ἐξῆλθεν ὁ τεθνηκὼς δεδεμένος τοὺς πόδας καὶ τὰς χεῖρας κειρίαις καὶ ἡ ὄψις αὐτοῦ σουδαρίῳ περιεδέδετο λέγει αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς Λύσατε αὐτὸν καὶ ἄφετε αὐτὸν ὑπάγειν
verses.transliteration: exēlthen ho tethnēkōs dedemenos tous podas kai tas cheiras keiriais kai hē opsis autou soudariō periedeto legei autois ho Iēsous Lysate auton kai aphete auton hypagein
verses.study_note: 'He that was dead' (ho tethnēkōs — the one having died; perfect participle: the one who was and had been dead). 'Came forth' (exēlthen — came out). 'Bound hand and foot with graveclothes' (dedemenos tous podas kai tas cheiras keiriais — bound at feet and hands with linen strips). 'Face bound about with a napkin' (soudariō periedeto — wrapped around with a burial cloth). Lazarus emerges still in his grave bindings — unlike Jesus at his resurrection, whose grave clothes will be found neatly folded (20:6-7). 'Loose him, and let him go' (Lysate auton kai aphete auton hypagein — unbind him and let him go). Again Jesus involves human action. The spiritual application is clear: Christ gives life; the community removes the remnants of death.

verses.kjv_text: Then many of the Jews which came to Mary, and had seen the things which Jesus did, believed on him.
verses.original_text: πολλοὶ οὖν ἐκ τῶν Ἰουδαίων οἱ ἐλθόντες πρὸς τὴν Μαριὰμ καὶ θεασάμενοι ἃ ἐποίησεν ἐπίστευσαν εἰς αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: polloi oun ek tōn Ioudaiōn hoi elthontes pros tēn Mariam kai theasamenoi ha epoiēsen episteusan eis auton
verses.study_note: 'Many... believed on him' (polloi... episteusan eis auton — many believed into him). The sign achieves its intended purpose: faith. 'Had seen the things which Jesus did' (theasamenoi ha epoiēsen — having beheld what he did). The verb theaomai implies careful, attentive observation — not a casual glance but witnessing. Yet 'many' is not 'all' — some respond with faith, others with hostility (v.46). The same evidence produces opposite responses, a recurring Johannine theme.

verses.kjv_text: But some of them went their ways to the Pharisees, and told them what things Jesus had done.
verses.original_text: τινὲς δὲ ἐξ αὐτῶν ἀπῆλθον πρὸς τοὺς Φαρισαίους καὶ εἶπαν αὐτοῖς ἃ ἐποίησεν Ἰησοῦς
verses.transliteration: tines de ex autōn apēlthon pros tous Pharisaious kai eipan autois ha epoiēsen Iēsous
verses.study_note: 'Some of them went... to the Pharisees' — not necessarily hostile witnesses; they may have been reporting in awe or seeking guidance. But the result is the same: the authorities are informed. 'Told them what things Jesus had done' — they do not deny the miracle; they report it. The Pharisees' problem is not lack of evidence but the theological and political implications of the evidence. John's irony: the miracle that should have produced universal faith instead triggers the plot to kill Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: Then gathered the chief priests and the Pharisees a council, and said, What do we? for this man doeth many miracles.
verses.original_text: συνήγαγον οὖν οἱ ἀρχιερεῖς καὶ οἱ Φαρισαῖοι συνέδριον καὶ ἔλεγον Τί ποιοῦμεν ὅτι οὗτος ὁ ἄνθρωπος πολλὰ ποιεῖ σημεῖα
verses.transliteration: synēgagon oun hoi archiereis kai hoi Pharisaioi synedrion kai elegon Ti poioumen hoti houtos ho anthrōpos polla poiei sēmeia
verses.study_note: 'Gathered a council' (synēgagon... synedrion — convened a Sanhedrin meeting; the supreme Jewish court). 'What do we?' (Ti poioumen — what are we doing? What shall we do?). 'This man doeth many miracles' (houtos ho anthrōpos polla poiei sēmeia — this man performs many signs). They do not deny the signs — they acknowledge them openly. Their concern is not theological ('is he the Messiah?') but political ('what will happen to us?'). They refer to Jesus dismissively as 'this man' (houtos ho anthrōpos). The supreme irony: religious leaders acknowledge undeniable signs and respond with a murder plot.

verses.kjv_text: If we let him thus alone, all men will believe on him: and the Romans shall come and take away both our place and nation.
verses.original_text: ἐὰν ἀφῶμεν αὐτὸν οὕτως πάντες πιστεύσουσιν εἰς αὐτόν καὶ ἐλεύσονται οἱ Ῥωμαῖοι καὶ ἀροῦσιν ἡμῶν καὶ τὸν τόπον καὶ τὸ ἔθνος
verses.transliteration: ean aphōmen auton houtōs pantes pisteusousin eis auton kai eleusontai hoi Rhōmaioi kai arousin hēmōn kai ton topon kai to ethnos
verses.study_note: 'All men will believe on him' (pantes pisteusousin eis auton) — an exaggerated fear, yet ironically pointing to truth: Jesus will indeed draw all men to himself (12:32). 'The Romans shall come' — they fear Roman reprisal against a messianic movement. 'Our place' (ton topon — the Temple, their centre of power) 'and nation' (to ethnos). The tragic irony: the very thing they sought to prevent by killing Jesus — Roman destruction of Temple and nation — happened in AD 70 precisely because they rejected him. Their political calculation proved catastrophically wrong.

verses.kjv_text: And one of them, named Caiaphas, being the high priest that same year, said unto them, Ye know nothing at all,
verses.original_text: εἷς δέ τις ἐξ αὐτῶν Καϊάφας ἀρχιερεὺς ὢν τοῦ ἐνιαυτοῦ ἐκείνου εἶπεν αὐτοῖς Ὑμεῖς οὐκ οἴδατε οὐδέν
verses.transliteration: heis de tis ex autōn Kaiaphas archiereus ōn tou eniautou ekeinou eipen autois Hymeis ouk oidate ouden
verses.study_note: 'Caiaphas' (Kaiaphas — Joseph Caiaphas, son-in-law of Annas, served as high priest c. AD 18-36). 'Being the high priest that same year' — John does not imply annual rotation of high priests (the office was for life); he emphasises that Caiaphas held office 'that year' — the year of Jesus' death, the most consequential year in history. 'Ye know nothing at all' (Hymeis ouk oidate ouden — you know nothing whatsoever) — arrogant dismissal of his fellow council members, typical of political pragmatism.

verses.kjv_text: Nor consider that it is expedient for us, that one man should die for the people, and that the whole nation perish not.
verses.original_text: οὐδὲ λογίζεσθε ὅτι συμφέρει ὑμῖν ἵνα εἷς ἄνθρωπος ἀποθάνῃ ὑπὲρ τοῦ λαοῦ καὶ μὴ ὅλον τὸ ἔθνος ἀπόληται
verses.transliteration: oude logizesthe hoti sympherei hymin hina heis anthrōpos apothanē hyper tou laou kai mē holon to ethnos apolētai
verses.study_note: 'It is expedient' (sympherei — it is profitable, advantageous, beneficial). 'One man should die for the people' (heis anthrōpos apothanē hyper tou laou — one man die on behalf of the people). Caiaphas speaks as a cold political calculator: better one man dead than Roman intervention. But his words carry a meaning far deeper than he intends: Jesus will indeed die 'for the people' — not as a political sacrifice but as the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world (1:29). The high priest unwittingly pronounces the theology of substitutionary atonement.
verses.doctrinal_note: UNWITTING PROPHECY OF SUBSTITUTIONARY ATONEMENT: Caiaphas' words — 'one man should die for the people' — express the core of atonement theology. The preposition hyper (on behalf of, in place of) is the language of substitution (cf. Mark 10:45; 2 Corinthians 5:21; Galatians 3:13; 1 Peter 3:18). Caiaphas means political expediency; God means redemptive substitution. The high priest, whose office was to offer sacrifice for sin, unknowingly announces the ultimate sacrifice.

verses.kjv_text: And this spake he not of himself: but being high priest that year, he prophesied that Jesus should die for that nation;
verses.original_text: τοῦτο δὲ ἀφ᾽ ἑαυτοῦ οὐκ εἶπεν ἀλλὰ ἀρχιερεὺς ὢν τοῦ ἐνιαυτοῦ ἐκείνου ἐπροφήτευσεν ὅτι ἔμελλεν Ἰησοῦς ἀποθνῄσκειν ὑπὲρ τοῦ ἔθνους
verses.transliteration: touto de aph heautou ouk eipen alla archiereus ōn tou eniautou ekeinou eprophēteusen hoti emellen Iēsous apothnēskein hyper tou ethnous
verses.study_note: 'Not of himself' (aph heautou ouk eipen — he did not speak from himself). 'He prophesied' (eprophēteusen — he prophesied, spoke by divine inspiration). John makes an extraordinary claim: even the wicked high priest, acting in his official capacity, could be the instrument of divine prophecy. This is consistent with OT precedent (Balaam, Numbers 22-24; Saul, 1 Samuel 19:23-24). God's sovereignty overrules human intention: Caiaphas means murder; God means salvation. The office speaks truth even when the officeholder acts in malice.

verses.kjv_text: And not for that nation only, but that also he should gather together in one the children of God that were scattered abroad.
verses.original_text: καὶ οὐχ ὑπὲρ τοῦ ἔθνους μόνον ἀλλ᾽ ἵνα καὶ τὰ τέκνα τοῦ θεοῦ τὰ διεσκορπισμένα συναγάγῃ εἰς ἕν
verses.transliteration: kai ouch hyper tou ethnous monon all hina kai ta tekna tou theou ta dieskorpismena synagagē eis hen
verses.study_note: 'Not for that nation only' — John expands the scope of Jesus' death beyond Israel. 'The children of God that were scattered abroad' (ta tekna tou theou ta dieskorpismena — the scattered children of God). This refers to Gentile believers who will be gathered into one people of God through the cross (cf. 10:16: 'other sheep I have, which are not of this fold... one flock, one shepherd'). 'Gather together in one' (synagagē eis hen — gather into unity) — the cross is the instrument of universal reconciliation (Ephesians 2:14-16). Jesus' death is not a national tragedy but a cosmic redemption.

verses.kjv_text: Then from that day forth they took counsel together for to put him to death.
verses.original_text: ἀπ᾽ ἐκείνης οὖν τῆς ἡμέρας ἐβουλεύσαντο ἵνα ἀποκτείνωσιν αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: ap ekeinēs oun tēs hēmeras ebouleusanto hina apokteinōsin auton
verses.study_note: 'From that day forth' (ap ekeinēs tēs hēmeras — from that day). 'They took counsel together to put him to death' (ebouleusanto hina apokteinōsin auton — they deliberated in order to kill him). The decision is now formal and corporate — the Sanhedrin has resolved to execute Jesus. The supreme irony of John 11: the one who raised the dead is condemned to death because he raised the dead. The miracle that most clearly demonstrated Jesus' divine authority becomes the immediate cause of his crucifixion. Life conquers death, and death responds by plotting against life.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus therefore walked no more openly among the Jews; but went thence unto a country near to the wilderness, into a city called Ephraim, and there continued with his disciples.
verses.original_text: ὁ οὖν Ἰησοῦς οὐκέτι παρρησίᾳ περιεπάτει ἐν τοῖς Ἰουδαίοις ἀλλὰ ἀπῆλθεν ἐκεῖθεν εἰς τὴν χώραν ἐγγὺς τῆς ἐρήμου εἰς Ἐφραὶμ λεγομένην πόλιν κἀκεῖ ἔμεινεν μετὰ τῶν μαθητῶν
verses.transliteration: ho oun Iēsous ouketi parrēsia periepatei en tois Ioudaiois alla apēlthen ekeithen eis tēn chōran engys tēs erēmou eis Ephraim legomenēn polin kakei emeinen meta tōn mathētōn
verses.study_note: 'No more openly' (ouketi parrēsia — no longer with openness/boldness). Jesus' withdrawal is not fear but timing: his 'hour' is near but has not yet come. 'Ephraim' (Ephraim — likely modern et-Taiyibeh, about 15 miles northeast of Jerusalem, near the wilderness). 'Continued with his disciples' (emeinen meta tōn mathētōn — he remained with his disciples). This quiet retreat before the Passover is a period of preparation, like the calm before the storm. The next time Jesus comes to Jerusalem, it will be for the triumphal entry and the cross.

verses.kjv_text: And the Jews' passover was nigh at hand: and many went out of the country up to Jerusalem before the passover, to purify themselves.
verses.original_text: ἦν δὲ ἐγγὺς τὸ πάσχα τῶν Ἰουδαίων καὶ ἀνέβησαν πολλοὶ εἰς Ἱεροσόλυμα ἐκ τῆς χώρας πρὸ τοῦ πάσχα ἵνα ἁγνίσωσιν ἑαυτούς
verses.transliteration: ēn de engys to pascha tōn Ioudaiōn kai anebēsan polloi eis Hierosolyma ek tēs chōras pro tou pascha hina hagnisōsin heautous
verses.study_note: 'The Jews' Passover' — this is the third Passover mentioned in John (2:13; 6:4; 11:55), the one at which Jesus will die. 'To purify themselves' (hagnisōsin heautous — to purify themselves ceremonially). Ritual purification was required before Passover participation (cf. Numbers 9:6-10; 2 Chronicles 30:17-18). Many arrived early to complete the process. The irony: while pilgrims purify themselves for Passover, the true Paschal Lamb is preparing for slaughter.

verses.kjv_text: Then sought they for Jesus, and spake among themselves, as they stood in the temple, What think ye, that he will not come to the feast?
verses.original_text: ἐζήτουν οὖν τὸν Ἰησοῦν καὶ ἔλεγον μετ᾽ ἀλλήλων ἐν τῷ ἱερῷ ἑστηκότες Τί δοκεῖ ὑμῖν ὅτι οὐ μὴ ἔλθῃ εἰς τὴν ἑορτήν
verses.transliteration: ezētoun oun ton Iēsoun kai elegon met allēlōn en tō hierō hestēkotes Ti dokei hymin hoti ou mē elthē eis tēn heortēn
verses.study_note: 'Sought they for Jesus' (ezētoun ton Iēsoun — they were seeking Jesus). The crowds look for Jesus in the Temple courts with a mixture of curiosity and expectation. 'What think ye?' (Ti dokei hymin — what does it seem to you?) — the question circulates through the crowd. 'That he will not come to the feast?' — they suspect he will avoid the danger. But Jesus will come — the Lamb must be at the Passover. Their speculation creates dramatic tension: the reader knows Jesus will come, and what will happen when he does.

verses.kjv_text: Now both the chief priests and the Pharisees had given a commandment, that, if any man knew where he were, he should shew it, that they might take him.
verses.original_text: δεδώκεισαν δὲ οἱ ἀρχιερεῖς καὶ οἱ Φαρισαῖοι ἐντολὰς ἵνα ἐάν τις γνῷ ποῦ ἐστιν μηνύσῃ ὅπως πιάσωσιν αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: dedōkeisan de hoi archiereis kai hoi Pharisaioi entolas hina ean tis gnō pou estin mēnysē hopōs piasōsin auton
verses.study_note: 'Had given a commandment' (dedōkeisan... entolas — had given orders; pluperfect: the orders were already in effect). 'If any man knew where he were, he should shew it' — an informant directive, effectively making Jesus a wanted man. 'That they might take him' (hopōs piasōsin auton — so that they might arrest him). The chapter ends with Jesus as a fugitive — the giver of life is hunted by those who deal in death. The net is closing. The next chapter opens with Jesus returning to Bethany (12:1), setting the final sequence in motion.

meta.overview: John 12 marks the great turning point in the Gospel: the close of Jesus' public ministry and the opening of the Passion narrative. It falls on the sixth day before Passover (v.1), placing it in the week of his death. The chapter opens with an act of extravagant devotion — Mary anoints Jesus' feet with a pound of pure spikenard worth 300 denarii and wipes them with her hair (vv.1-8). Judas objects on grounds of charity, but Jesus defends her: 'Against the day of my burying hath she kept this' (v.7). The chief priests plot to kill Lazarus also, since his resurrection is causing many to believe (vv.9-11). The Triumphal Entry follows (vv.12-19): a great multitude spreads palm branches and cries 'Hosanna: Blessed is the King of Israel that cometh in the name of the Lord' (v.13). Jesus rides on a young ass, fulfilling Zechariah 9:9. The disciples do not understand this until after his glorification (v.16). The Pharisees despair: 'Behold, the world is gone after him' (v.19). Greeks approach Philip, seeking to see Jesus — a sign that his saving work reaches beyond Israel (vv.20-22). Jesus responds with the great paradox of the kingdom: 'Except a corn of wheat fall into the ground and die, it abideth alone: but if it die, it bringeth forth much fruit' (v.24). He speaks of being 'lifted up' to draw all men to himself (v.32). The crowd is confused — the law says Christ abideth for ever, yet Jesus speaks of the Son of man being lifted up (v.34). Jesus gives a final urgent appeal: 'Walk while ye have the light, lest darkness come upon you' (v.35). John then appends a reflection on Jewish unbelief, citing Isaiah 53:1 and Isaiah 6:9-10 as both predicted and divinely ordered (vv.37-41). Even many rulers believed secretly but would not confess him for fear of the Pharisees (v.42). The chapter closes with Jesus' final public declaration: 'He that rejecteth me, and receiveth not my words, hath one that judgeth him: the word that I have spoken, the same shall judge him in the last day' (v.48).
meta.theme: The Hour Has Come — Glory Through Death
meta.keyWordMeaning: Glory, honour, splendour — from dokeō (to seem, to appear). In John, doxa is the visible manifestation of divine nature. Jesus' doxa is paradoxically revealed through his death: 'The hour is come, that the Son of man should be glorified' (v.23). The grain of wheat that dies brings forth much fruit — glory through humiliation. Isaiah 'saw his glory' (v.41) in the vision of the enthroned Lord (Isaiah 6:1-3), which John identifies as the pre-incarnate Christ. Glorification in John is always cross-shaped: the lifting up on the cross is simultaneously the lifting up into glory. The word and its cognates appear 6 times in this chapter (vv.16, 23, 28[x2], 41, 43).
outline: Mary Anoints Jesus at Bethany (vv.1-8): Six days before the passover Jesus came to Bethany where Lazarus was whom he raised from the dead. There they made him a supper and Martha served and Lazarus was one of them that sat at the table. Then took Mary a pound of ointment of spikenard very costly and anointed the feet of Jesus and wiped his feet with her hair and the house was filled with the odour of the ointment. Then saith Judas Iscariot Simon's son which should betray him Why was not this ointment sold for three hundred pence and given to the poor? This he said not that he cared for the poor but because he was a thief and had the bag and bare what was put therein. Then said Jesus Let her alone against the day of my burying hath she kept this. For the poor always ye have with you but me ye have not always

The Plot Against Lazarus (vv.9-11): Much people of the Jews therefore knew that he was there and they came not for Jesus' sake only but that they might see Lazarus also whom he had raised from the dead. But the chief priests consulted that they might put Lazarus also to death because that by reason of him many of the Jews went away and believed on Jesus

The Triumphal Entry (vv.12-19): On the next day much people that were come to the feast when they heard that Jesus was coming to Jerusalem took branches of palm trees and went forth to meet him and cried Hosanna Blessed is the King of Israel that cometh in the name of the Lord. And Jesus when he had found a young ass sat thereon as it is written Fear not daughter of Sion behold thy King cometh sitting on an ass's colt. These things understood not his disciples at the first but when Jesus was glorified then remembered they that these things were written of him. The Pharisees therefore said among themselves Perceive ye how ye prevail nothing? behold the world is gone after him

The Greeks Seek Jesus (vv.20-26): And there were certain Greeks among them that came up to worship at the feast and they came to Philip and desired him saying Sir we would see Jesus. Philip cometh and telleth Andrew and again Andrew and Philip tell Jesus. And Jesus answered them saying The hour is come that the Son of man should be glorified. Verily verily I say unto you Except a corn of wheat fall into the ground and die it abideth alone but if it die it bringeth forth much fruit. He that loveth his life shall lose it and he that hateth his life in this world shall keep it unto life eternal. If any man serve me let him follow me and where I am there shall also my servant be

The Voice from Heaven (vv.27-36): Now is my soul troubled and what shall I say? Father save me from this hour but for this cause came I unto this hour. Father glorify thy name. Then came there a voice from heaven saying I have both glorified it and will glorify it again. The people therefore that stood by and heard it said that it thundered others said An angel spake to him. Jesus answered This voice came not because of me but for your sakes. Now is the judgment of this world now shall the prince of this world be cast out. And I if I be lifted up from the earth will draw all men unto me. This he said signifying what death he should die. The people answered We have heard out of the law that Christ abideth for ever and how sayest thou The Son of man must be lifted up? who is this Son of man? Then Jesus said unto them Yet a little while is the light with you. Walk while ye have the light lest darkness come upon you

Jewish Unbelief and Isaiah's Witness (vv.37-43): But though he had done so many miracles before them yet they believed not on him. That the saying of Esaias the prophet might be fulfilled Lord who hath believed our report? and to whom hath the arm of the Lord been revealed? Therefore they could not believe because that Esaias said again He hath blinded their eyes and hardened their heart. These things said Esaias when he saw his glory and spake of him. Nevertheless among the chief rulers also many believed on him but because of the Pharisees they did not confess him lest they should be put out of the synagogue. For they loved the praise of men more than the praise of God

Jesus' Final Public Appeal (vv.44-50): Jesus cried and said He that believeth on me believeth not on me but on him that sent me. And he that seeth me seeth him that sent me. I am come a light into the world that whosoever believeth on me should not abide in darkness. And if any man hear my words and believe not I judge him not for I came not to judge the world but to save the world. He that rejecteth me and receiveth not my words hath one that judgeth him the word that I have spoken the same shall judge him in the last day. For I have not spoken of myself but the Father which sent me he gave me a commandment what I should say and what I should speak. And I know that his commandment is life everlasting

verses.kjv_text: Then Jesus six days before the passover came to Bethany, where Lazarus was which had been dead, whom he raised from the dead.
verses.original_text: Ὁ οὖν Ἰησοῦς πρὸ ἓξ ἡμερῶν τοῦ πάσχα ἦλθεν εἰς Βηθανίαν ὅπου ἦν Λάζαρος ὁ τεθνηκώς ὅν ἤγειρεν ἐκ νεκρῶν Ἰησοῦς
verses.transliteration: ho oun Iēsous pro hex hēmerōn tou pascha ēlthen eis Bēthanían hopou ēn Lazaros ho tethnēkōs hon ēgeiren ek nekrōn Iēsous
verses.study_note: 'Six days before the passover' (pro hex hēmerōn tou pascha) — this places the event on the Saturday before Palm Sunday; John's chronology is precise and intentional. Bethany is about two miles east of Jerusalem on the Mount of Olives (11:18). 'Which had been dead' (ho tethnēkōs — the perfect participle: who had died and remained dead) is deliberately juxtaposed with 'whom he raised' (hon ēgeiren — aorist: whom he raised up). Lazarus is introduced as living proof of the resurrection power that is now provoking the final crisis.

verses.kjv_text: There they made him a supper; and Martha served: but Lazarus was one of them that sat at the table with him.
verses.original_text: ἐποίησαν οὖν αὐτῷ δεῖπνον ἐκεῖ καὶ ἡ Μάρθα διηκόνει ὁ δὲ Λάζαρος εἷς ἦν ἐκ τῶν ἀνακειμένων σὺν αὐτῷ
verses.transliteration: epoiēsan oun autō deipnon ekei kai hē Martha diēkonei ho de Lazaros heis ēn ek tōn anakeimenōn syn autō
verses.study_note: 'Made him a supper' (epoiēsan deipnon) — the evening meal, the principal meal of the day. John does not say who gave the supper; the parallel accounts (Matthew 26:6-13; Mark 14:3-9) locate it at the house of Simon the Leper, likely a healed disciple. Martha is characteristically serving (diēkonei — imperfect: was serving, continuously). Lazarus reclines at table with Jesus — his presence is itself a testimony. The man who was dead now eats with the one who gave him life.

verses.kjv_text: Then took Mary a pound of ointment of spikenard, very costly, and anointed the feet of Jesus, and wiped his feet with her hair: and the house was filled with the odour of the ointment.
verses.original_text: ἡ οὖν Μαρία λαβοῦσα λίτραν μύρου νάρδου πιστικῆς πολυτίμου ἤλειψεν τοὺς πόδας τοῦ Ἰησοῦ καὶ ἐξέμαξεν ταῖς θριξὶν αὐτῆς τοὺς πόδας αὐτοῦ ἡ δὲ οἰκία ἐπληρώθη ἐκ τῆς ὀσμῆς τοῦ μύρου
verses.transliteration: hē oun Maria labousa litran myrou nardou pistikēs polytimou ēleipsen tous podas tou Iēsou kai exemaxen tais thrixin autēs tous podas autou hē de oikia eplērōthē ek tēs osmēs tou myrou
verses.study_note: 'A pound of ointment of spikenard' (litran myrou nardou pistikēs — a Roman pound [327g] of pure nard ointment). Nard (nardos) is an aromatic oil extracted from the spikenard plant (Nardostachys jatamansi), imported from the Himalayan foothills of India. 'Pistikēs' (pure, genuine, trustworthy — from pistos: faithful) indicates undiluted, authentic oil. 'Very costly' (polytimou — of great value; v.5 gives the price: 300 denarii, nearly a year's wages). Mary anoints Jesus' feet — an act of humility exceeding anointing the head (cf. Mark 14:3). Wiping with her hair is an act of extraordinary self-abasement; a Jewish woman's hair was her glory and was worn up in public. The 'fragrance filling the house' is a detail only John records, evocative of the incense of sacrifice.

verses.kjv_text: Then saith one of his disciples, Judas Iscariot, Simon's son, which should betray him,
verses.original_text: λέγει δὲ Ἰούδας ὁ Ἰσκαριώτης εἷς ἐκ τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ ὁ μέλλων αὐτὸν παραδιδόναι
verses.transliteration: legei de Ioudas ho Iskariōtēs heis ek tōn mathētōn autou ho mellōn auton paradidounai
verses.study_note: 'Which should betray him' (ho mellōn auton paradidounai — the one who was about to hand him over). John introduces Judas with this identifying epithet from the beginning (cf. 6:71). The contrast is stark: Mary's extravagance of love versus Judas's miserly calculation. John names Judas specifically where Matthew (26:8) says 'his disciples' objected — John focuses the opposition precisely.

verses.kjv_text: Why was not this ointment sold for three hundred pence, and given to the poor?
verses.original_text: Διὰ τί τοῦτο τὸ μύρον οὐκ ἐπράθη τριακοσίων δηναρίων καὶ ἐδόθη πτωχοῖς
verses.transliteration: Dia ti touto to myron ouk eprathē triakosīōn dēnariōn kai edothē ptōchois
verses.study_note: 'Three hundred pence' (triakosión dēnariōn — 300 denarii; a denarius was a day's wage for a labourer [Matthew 20:2], so this equals about a full year's wages). Judas frames his objection as concern for the poor — a plausible, even commendable-sounding complaint. But John unmasks the real motive in v.6.

verses.kjv_text: This he said, not that he cared for the poor; but because he was a thief, and had the bag, and bare what was put therein.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ τοῦτο οὐχ ὅτι περὶ τῶν πτωχῶν ἔμελεν αὐτῷ ἀλλ᾽ ὅτι κλέπτης ἦν καὶ τὸ γλωσσόκομον εἶχεν καὶ τὰ βαλλόμενα ἐβάσταζεν
verses.transliteration: eipen de touto ouch hoti peri tōn ptōchōn emelen autō all hoti kleptēs ēn kai to glōssokomon eichen kai ta ballomena ebastazen
verses.study_note: 'He was a thief' (kleptēs ēn — a thief by nature; ēn is the imperfect: habitually was). 'The bag' (to glōssokomon) — originally a case for the mouthpiece of a wind instrument, then used for a money box or cash bag. 'Bare what was put therein' (ta ballomena ebastazen) — 'bastazen' means to carry, but also idiomatically to pilfer; John may be saying Judas 'carried off' what was deposited (so RSV margin: 'used to take what was put into it'). The treasurer of the apostolic band was systematically embezzling. His piety is performance; his objection is greed disguised as charity.

verses.kjv_text: Then said Jesus, Let her alone: against the day of my burying hath she kept this.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν οὖν ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ἄφες αὐτήν ἵνα εἰς τὴν ἡμέραν τοῦ ἐνταφιασμοῦ μου τηρήσῃ αὐτό
verses.transliteration: eipen oun ho Iēsous Aphes autēn hina eis tēn hēmeran tou entaphiasmou mou tērēsē auto
verses.study_note: 'Let her alone' (aphes autēn — leave her, stop criticising her). 'Against the day of my burying' (eis tēn hēmeran tou entaphiasmou mou — for the day of my preparation for burial). 'Entaphiasmos' means burial preparation, embalming. Jesus interprets Mary's act as prophetically anointing him for burial. Whether Mary consciously intended this is uncertain, but Jesus accepts it as such. The beauty of the passage is that love's instinct perceives what the intellect cannot calculate: Mary's extravagant gift was exactly right, at exactly the right moment.

verses.kjv_text: For the poor always ye have with you; but me ye have not always.
verses.original_text: τοὺς πτωχοὺς γὰρ πάντοτε ἔχετε μεθ᾽ ἑαυτῶν ἐμὲ δὲ οὐ πάντοτε ἔχετε
verses.transliteration: tous ptōchous gar pantote echete meth heautōn eme de ou pantote echete
verses.study_note: 'The poor always ye have with you' — quoting Deuteronomy 15:11 ('the poor shall never cease out of the land'), which in context commands generosity to the poor. Jesus is not dismissing care for the poor; the disciples can serve the poor at any time. But the incarnate Son of God is present with them for a brief window, and this is that window. The logic is not 'poor vs. Jesus' but 'ordinary time vs. this extraordinary moment.' Mary seized the moment; Judas counted the cost in the wrong currency.

verses.kjv_text: Much people of the Jews therefore knew that he was there: and they came not for Jesus' sake only, but that they might see Lazarus also, whom he had raised from the dead.
verses.original_text: ἔγνω οὖν ὄχλος πολὺς ἐκ τῶν Ἰουδαίων ὅτι ἐκεῖ ἐστιν καὶ ἦλθον οὐ διὰ τὸν Ἰησοῦν μόνον ἀλλ᾽ ἵνα καὶ τὸν Λάζαρον ἴδωσιν ὃν ἤγειρεν ἐκ νεκρῶν
verses.transliteration: egnō oun ochlos polys ek tōn Ioudaiōn hoti ekei estin kai ēlthon ou dia ton Iēsoun monon all hina kai ton Lazaron idōsin hon ēgeiren ek nekrōn
verses.study_note: Lazarus is a public sensation — a living exhibit of resurrection power. People come on a dual motive: to see Jesus and to see the man who had been dead. This is not condemned; it is simply observed. The resurrection of Lazarus is both a sign (sēmeion) pointing to Jesus and an immediate catalyst for the Passion — it is simultaneously the greatest public miracle and the trigger for the crucifixion plot (11:47-53).

verses.kjv_text: But the chief priests consulted that they might put Lazarus also to death;
verses.original_text: ἐβουλεύσαντο δὲ οἱ ἀρχιερεῖς ἵνα καὶ τὸν Λάζαρον ἀποκτείνωσιν
verses.transliteration: ebouleusanto de hoi archiereis hina kai ton Lazaron apokteinōsin
verses.study_note: 'Consulted' (ebouleusanto — deliberated, took counsel). The supreme irony: the priests who should celebrate resurrection plot against the man who embodies it. To destroy the evidence of the miracle, they plan to destroy the miracle. This is the logical end of suppressed truth — when one cannot deny the fact, one attempts to eliminate the witness.

verses.kjv_text: Because that by reason of him many of the Jews went away, and believed on Jesus.
verses.original_text: ὅτι πολλοὶ δι᾽ αὐτὸν ὑπῆγον τῶν Ἰουδαίων καὶ ἐπίστευον εἰς τὸν Ἰησοῦν
verses.transliteration: hoti polloi di auton hypēgon tōn Ioudaiōn kai episteuon eis ton Iēsoun
verses.study_note: 'Went away' (hypēgon — were going away, departing from the establishment) and 'believed on Jesus' (episteuon eis ton Iēsoun — kept believing). The irony deepens: the more the religious leaders plot, the more people believe. Their effort to contain the movement accelerates it. Lazarus, by simply being alive, is a more powerful preacher than any speech.

verses.kjv_text: On the next day much people that were come to the feast, when they heard that Jesus was coming to Jerusalem,
verses.original_text: Τῇ ἐπαύριον ὁ ὄχλος πολὺς ὁ ἐλθὼν εἰς τὴν ἑορτήν ἀκούσαντες ὅτι ἔρχεται ὁ Ἰησοῦς εἰς Ἱεροσόλυμα
verses.transliteration: tē epaurion ho ochlos polys ho elthōn eis tēn heortēn akousantes hoti erchetai ho Iēsous eis Hierosolyma
verses.study_note: 'On the next day' (tē epaurion) — Palm Sunday, five days before Passover. Jerusalem would have been packed with pilgrims; estimates suggest 100,000-300,000 visitors for Passover. 'That were come to the feast' (ho elthōn eis tēn heortēn) — these are not Galileans who accompanied Jesus; they are Jerusalem pilgrims who have heard of him.

verses.kjv_text: Took branches of palm trees, and went forth to meet him, and cried, Hosanna: Blessed is the King of Israel that cometh in the name of the Lord.
verses.original_text: ἔλαβον τὰ βαΐα τῶν φοινίκων καὶ ἐξῆλθον εἰς ὑπάντησιν αὐτῷ καὶ ἐκραύγαζον Ὡσαννά εὐλογημένος ὁ ἐρχόμενος ἐν ὀνόματι κυρίου καὶ ὁ βασιλεὺς τοῦ Ἰσραήλ
verses.transliteration: elabon ta baia tōn phoinikōn kai exēlthon eis hypantēsin autō kai ekraugazon Hōsanna eulogēmenos ho erchomenos en onomati kyriou kai ho basileus tou Israēl
verses.study_note: 'Palm branches' (ta baia tōn phoinikōn) — palm fronds were a symbol of Jewish national identity and victory (1 Maccabees 13:51; Revelation 7:9). Only John uses the word 'baia' (palm branches); the Synoptics mention branches cut from trees. 'Hosanna' (hōsanna — from Hebrew hōshī'ā nā: 'Save now!') is from Psalm 118:25-26, the great Hallel psalm sung at Passover and Tabernacles. It has become an acclamation of praise. John uniquely adds 'the King of Israel' — explicitly messianic, and the phrase Nathanael used in 1:49.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus, when he had found a young ass, sat thereon; as it is written,
verses.original_text: εὑρὼν δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς ὀνάριον ἐκάθισεν ἐπ᾽ αὐτό καθώς ἐστιν γεγραμμένον
verses.transliteration: heurōn de ho Iēsous onarion ekathisen ep auto kathōs estin gegrammenon
verses.study_note: 'Found a young ass' (heurōn onarion — having found a little donkey). John abbreviates the Synoptic accounts of the disciples' procurement (Matthew 21:1-7; Mark 11:1-7; Luke 19:28-35). The Synoptics describe the colt as 'one whereon never man sat' — a detail stressing its sacred fitness for holy use (Numbers 19:2; Deuteronomy 21:3). John emphasises the fulfilment of prophecy immediately.

verses.kjv_text: Fear not, daughter of Sion: behold, thy King cometh, sitting on an ass's colt.
verses.original_text: μὴ φοβοῦ θυγάτηρ Σιών ἰδοὺ ὁ βασιλεύς σου ἔρχεται καθήμενος ἐπὶ πῶλον ὄνου
verses.transliteration: mē phobou thygater Siōn idou ho basileus sou erchetai kathēmenos epi pōlon onou
verses.study_note: Quoting Zechariah 9:9: 'Rejoice greatly, O daughter of Zion; shout, O daughter of Jerusalem: behold, thy King cometh unto thee: he is just, and having salvation; lowly, and riding upon an ass, and upon a colt the foal of an ass.' John compresses the quote, substituting 'Fear not' (mē phobou) for Zechariah's 'Rejoice' — perhaps drawing on Isaiah 40:9 or Zephaniah 3:16. The key interpretive point: the King comes on a donkey, not a war horse. It is a deliberate choice of meekness over military conquest. The Messiah's kingdom is not of this world (18:36).

verses.kjv_text: These things understood not his disciples at the first: but when Jesus was glorified, then remembered they that these things were written of him, and that they had done these things unto him.
verses.original_text: ταῦτα δὲ οὐκ ἔγνωσαν αὐτοῦ οἱ μαθηταὶ τὸ πρῶτον ἀλλ᾽ ὅτε ἐδοξάσθη Ἰησοῦς τότε ἐμνήσθησαν ὅτι ταῦτα ἦν ἐπ᾽ αὐτῷ γεγραμμένα καὶ ταῦτα ἐποίησαν αὐτῷ
verses.transliteration: tauta de ouk egnōsan autou hoi mathētai to prōton all hote edoxasthē Iēsous tote emnēsthēsan hoti tauta ēn ep autō gegrammena kai tauta epoiēsan autō
verses.study_note: 'Understood not his disciples at the first' (ouk egnōsan to prōton — did not know/recognise at first). This candid admission is historically honest and theologically profound. The disciples participated in the event without fully understanding it. 'When Jesus was glorified' (hote edoxasthē — when he was glorified; aorist: after the resurrection and ascension) 'then remembered they' (tote emnēsthēsan). The glorification of Jesus — cross, resurrection, Pentecost — is the hermeneutical key that unlocks the meaning of all that precedes it. The Spirit guides into all truth (16:13) by causing retrospective understanding.

verses.kjv_text: The people therefore that was with him when he called Lazarus out of his grave, and raised him from the dead, bare record.
verses.original_text: ἐμαρτύρει οὖν ὁ ὄχλος ὁ ὢν μετ᾽ αὐτοῦ ὅτε τὸν Λάζαρον ἐφώνησεν ἐκ τοῦ μνημείου καὶ ἤγειρεν αὐτὸν ἐκ νεκρῶν
verses.transliteration: emartyrei oun ho ochlos ho ōn met autou hote ton Lazaron ephōnesen ek tou mnēmeiou kai ēgeiren auton ek nekrōn
verses.study_note: 'Bare record' (emartyrei — imperfect: kept bearing witness). The eyewitnesses of the Lazarus miracle are actively spreading the report. 'Called Lazarus out of his grave' (ephōnesen ek tou mnēmeiou — called out from the tomb). John returns to the Lazarus event as the driver of the popular enthusiasm. The whole Passion week, in John's account, unfolds in the shadow — and the light — of that resurrection.

verses.kjv_text: For this cause the people also met him, for that they heard that he had done this miracle.
verses.original_text: διὰ τοῦτο καὶ ὑπήντησεν αὐτῷ ὁ ὄχλος ὅτι ἤκουσαν τοῦτο αὐτὸν πεποιηκέναι τὸ σημεῖον
verses.transliteration: dia touto kai hypēntēsen autō ho ochlos hoti ēkousan touto auton pepoiēkenai to sēmeion
verses.study_note: Two crowds converge on Jesus: those who came from Bethany having witnessed the miracle, and those from Jerusalem who have heard the report. The Lazarus miracle is the proximate cause of the Triumphal Entry's scale. 'Miracle' (to sēmeion — the sign) — John uses sēmeion consistently, emphasising the revelatory dimension of the miracle.

verses.kjv_text: The Pharisees therefore said among themselves, Perceive ye how ye prevail nothing? behold, the world is gone after him.
verses.original_text: οἱ οὖν Φαρισαῖοι εἶπαν πρὸς ἑαυτούς Θεωρεῖτε ὅτι οὐκ ὠφελεῖτε οὐδέν ἴδε ὁ κόσμος ὀπίσω αὐτοῦ ἀπῆλθεν
verses.transliteration: hoi oun Pharisaioi eipan pros heautous Theōreite hoti ouk ōpheleite ouden ide ho kosmos opisō autou apēlthen
verses.study_note: 'The world is gone after him' (ho kosmos opisō autou apēlthen — the world has gone after him; kosmos here = the whole world, everyone). The Pharisees' frustrated exclamation is ironically prophetic. They mean it as hyperbole of despair; John intends it as literal truth. The next verses (vv.20-23) confirm it: Greeks — representing the Gentile world — now come seeking Jesus. The 'world' will indeed go after him, drawn by his being lifted up (v.32).

verses.kjv_text: And there were certain Greeks among them that came up to worship at the feast:
verses.original_text: Ἦσαν δέ τινες Ἕλληνες ἐκ τῶν ἀναβαινόντων ἵνα προσκυνήσωσιν ἐν τῇ ἑορτῇ
verses.transliteration: Ēsan de tines Hellēnes ek tōn anabainontōn hina proskynēsōsin en tē heortē
verses.study_note: 'Certain Greeks' (tines Hellēnes) — Greek-speaking Gentiles, likely 'God-fearers' (proselytes or sympathisers who worshipped the God of Israel without full conversion; cf. Acts 10:2; 17:4). They 'came up to worship' (anabainontōn... proskynēsōsin — going up to worship) at the Passover feast, permitted to enter the Court of the Gentiles in the Temple. Their arrival immediately after the Pharisees' complaint that 'the world is gone after him' is a dramatic confirmation. The Gentiles are literally coming.

verses.kjv_text: The same came therefore to Philip, which was of Bethsaida of Galilee, and desired him, saying, Sir, we would see Jesus.
verses.original_text: οὗτοι οὖν προσῆλθον Φιλίππῳ τῷ ἀπὸ Βηθσαϊδὰ τῆς Γαλιλαίας καὶ ἠρώτων αὐτὸν λέγοντες Κύριε θέλομεν τὸν Ἰησοῦν ἰδεῖν
verses.transliteration: houtoi oun prosēlthon Philippō tō apo Bēthsaida tēs Galilaias kai ērōtōn auton legontes Kyrie thelomen ton Iēsoun idein
verses.study_note: 'Philip, which was of Bethsaida of Galilee' — Bethsaida was a mixed Jewish-Gentile area near the northern shore of the Sea of Galilee; Philip's Greek name (Philippos) and his frontier background may explain why the Greeks approach him. 'Sir, we would see Jesus' (Kyrie, thelomen ton Iēsoun idein) — kyrie here is simply a respectful address ('Sir'), not necessarily a theological title. Their request — 'we would see Jesus' — has become one of the most famous phrases in Christian homiletics, representing every seeker's ultimate desire.

verses.kjv_text: Philip cometh and telleth Andrew: and again Andrew and Philip tell Jesus.
verses.original_text: ἔρχεται ὁ Φίλιππος καὶ λέγει τῷ Ἀνδρέᾳ καὶ πάλιν Ἀνδρέας καὶ Φίλιππος λέγουσιν τῷ Ἰησοῦ
verses.transliteration: erchetai ho Philippos kai legei tō Andrea kai palin Andreas kai Philippos legousin tō Iēsou
verses.study_note: Philip consults Andrew before bringing the request to Jesus — perhaps uncertain whether Jesus would receive Gentiles. Andrew is always the connector who brings people to Jesus (1:41-42; 6:8-9). Together they present the request. Jesus' response is not a direct answer to their query but a proclamation of the significance of the moment: the arrival of the Greeks signals that 'the hour is come' (v.23).

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus answered them, saying, The hour is come, that the Son of man should be glorified.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ Ἰησοῦς ἀπεκρίνατο αὐτοῖς λέγων Ἐλήλυθεν ἡ ὥρα ἵνα δοξασθῇ ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου
verses.transliteration: ho de Iēsous apekrinatp autois legōn Elēlythen hē hōra hina doxasthē ho hyios tou anthrōpou
verses.study_note: 'The hour is come' (Elēlythen hē hōra — the hour has come; perfect tense: has arrived and is now here). Throughout John, Jesus has said his hour was 'not yet come' (2:4; 7:6,8,30; 8:20). Now, with the arrival of the Greeks — representing the Gentile world — the eschatological hour has arrived. The hour of 'glorification' (doxasthē — be glorified) is simultaneously the hour of crucifixion. The cross is the throne; the death is the glorification. This redefinition of glory is the central paradox of the Fourth Gospel.

verses.kjv_text: Verily, verily, I say unto you, Except a corn of wheat fall into the ground and die, it abideth alone: but if it die, it bringeth forth much fruit.
verses.original_text: ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ἐὰν μὴ ὁ κόκκος τοῦ σίτου πεσὼν εἰς τὴν γῆν ἀποθάνῃ αὐτὸς μόνος μένει ἐὰν δὲ ἀποθάνῃ πολὺν καρπὸν φέρει
verses.transliteration: amēn amēn legō hymin ean mē ho kokkos tou sitou pesōn eis tēn gēn apothanē autos monos menei ean de apothanē polun karpon pherei
verses.study_note: 'A corn of wheat' (ho kokkos tou sitou — a grain of wheat). Jesus uses an agricultural metaphor for the necessity and fruitfulness of his death. 'Abideth alone' (autos monos menei — it remains alone, single). A grain that is never planted remains one grain; it produces nothing. 'It bringeth forth much fruit' (polun karpon pherei — it bears much fruit). The death is not the end but the beginning of multiplication. This principle applies first to Christ — his death produces the harvest of redeemed souls from every nation (cf. vv.32; Revelation 7:9) — and second to every disciple (v.25).

verses.kjv_text: He that loveth his life shall lose it; and he that hateth his life in this world shall keep it unto life eternal.
verses.original_text: ὁ φιλῶν τὴν ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ ἀπολέσει αὐτήν καὶ ὁ μισῶν τὴν ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ τούτῳ εἰς ζωὴν αἰώνιον φυλάξει αὐτήν
verses.transliteration: ho philōn tēn psychēn autou apolesei autēn kai ho misōn tēn psychēn autou en tō kosmō toutō eis zōēn aiōnion phylaxei autēn
verses.study_note: 'He that loveth his life' (ho philōn tēn psychēn — the one loving his soul/life) 'shall lose it' (apolesei — will destroy/lose it). 'He that hateth his life in this world' (ho misōn tēn psychēn en tō kosmō toutō) — 'misōn' (hating) is Semitic hyperbole for 'loving less' (cf. Luke 14:26). The contrast is between self-preservation and self-surrender. This saying appears in all four Gospels (Matthew 10:39; 16:25; Mark 8:35; Luke 9:24; 17:33) — it is bedrock discipleship teaching. The 'life' (psychē — soul, self) that clings to itself contracts; the life surrendered expands into eternal life.

verses.kjv_text: If any man serve me, let him follow me; and where I am, there shall also my servant be: if any man serve me, him will my Father honour.
verses.original_text: ἐάν τις ἐμοὶ διακονῇ ἐμοὶ ἀκολουθείτω καὶ ὅπου εἰμὶ ἐγὼ ἐκεῖ καὶ ὁ διάκονος ὁ ἐμὸς ἔσται ἐάν τις ἐμοὶ διακονῇ τιμήσει αὐτὸν ὁ πατήρ
verses.transliteration: ean tis emoi diakonē emoi akoloutheitō kai hopou eimi egō ekei kai ho diakonos ho emos estai ean tis emoi diakonē timēsei auton ho patēr
verses.study_note: 'Serve me... follow me' (diakonē... akoloutheitō — serve... follow). Service to Christ requires following him — into his death, through his resurrection. 'Where I am, there shall also my servant be' — the servant shares the master's destiny: his death and his glory. 'Him will my Father honour' (timēsei auton ho patēr) — the Father honours those who follow the Son. This is the reversal of the world's honour system: worldly honour is gained by self-promotion; true honour comes from dying-to-self service.

verses.kjv_text: Now is my soul troubled; and what shall I say? Father, save me from this hour: but for this cause came I unto this hour.
verses.original_text: νῦν ἡ ψυχή μου τετάρακται καὶ τί εἴπω πάτερ σῶσόν με ἐκ τῆς ὥρας ταύτης ἀλλὰ διὰ τοῦτο ἦλθον εἰς τὴν ὥρα ταύτην
verses.transliteration: nyn hē psychē mou tetaraktai kai ti eipō pater sōson me ek tēs hōras tautēs alla dia touto ēlthon eis tēn hōran tautēn
verses.study_note: 'Now is my soul troubled' (nyn hē psychē mou tetaraktai — now my soul has been shaken/troubled; perfect passive: has been thrown into agitation). 'Tetaraktai' (from tarassō — to disturb, agitate) is the same word used of the disciples' fear (14:1,27) and of the pool of Bethesda being stirred (5:7). This is John's Gethsemane — the Fourth Gospel does not record the Gethsemane agonising of Matthew 26:36-46, but this moment is its theological equivalent. 'Father, save me from this hour' — Jesus voices the human recoil from death, then immediately rejects it: 'but for this cause came I unto this hour.' The resolution is instantaneous. The will of the Son submits to the purpose of the Father.

verses.kjv_text: Father, glorify thy name. Then came there a voice from heaven, saying, I have both glorified it, and will glorify it again.
verses.original_text: πάτερ δόξασόν σου τὸ ὄνομα ἦλθεν οὖν φωνὴ ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ Καὶ ἐδόξασα καὶ πάλιν δοξάσω
verses.transliteration: pater doxason sou to onoma ēlthen oun phōnē ek tou ouranou Kai edoxasa kai palin doxasō
verses.study_note: 'Father, glorify thy name' (pater, doxason sou to onoma) — this is the true Gethsemane prayer in John: not 'save me' but 'glorify thy name.' The submission is complete. 'Then came there a voice from heaven' (ēlthen phōnē ek tou ouranou) — this is the third divine voice in the Gospels: at Jesus' baptism (Matthew 3:17), at the Transfiguration (Matthew 17:5), and here. 'I have both glorified it' (kai edoxasa — aorist: I glorified it, in the life and works of Jesus) 'and will glorify it again' (kai palin doxasō — future: I will glorify it, in the death, resurrection, and Pentecost). Past glorification and future glorification bracket the present moment of the cross.

verses.kjv_text: The people therefore, that stood by, and heard it, said that it thundered: others said, An angel spake to him.
verses.original_text: ὁ οὖν ὄχλος ὁ ἑστὼς καὶ ἀκούσας ἔλεγεν βροντὴν γεγονέναι ἄλλοι ἔλεγον Ἄγγελος αὐτῷ λελάληκεν
verses.transliteration: ho oun ochlos ho hestōs kai akousas elegen brontēn gegonenai alloi elegon Angelos autō lelalēken
verses.study_note: The crowd hears two things: some hear formless thunder, others distinguish an angelic voice. The divine word is objectively spoken but subjectively received according to the hearer's spiritual capacity. This is a recurring theme in John: the same word or sign produces belief in some and incomprehension or hostility in others. The voice was for the crowd's benefit (v.30), yet many could not decode it. Spiritual hearing requires spiritual ears (cf. John 8:43,47; Matthew 13:13-17).

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered and said, This voice came not because of me, but for your sakes.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη Ἰησοῦς καὶ εἶπεν Οὐ δι᾽ ἐμὲ αὕτη ἡ φωνὴ γέγονεν ἀλλὰ δι᾽ ὑμᾶς
verses.transliteration: apekrithē Iēsous kai eipen Ou di eme hautē hē phōnē gegonen alla di hymas
verses.study_note: 'Not because of me, but for your sakes' (ou di eme... alla di hymas). Jesus did not need the audible reassurance — his communion with the Father is unbroken. The voice was a public sign for the crowd, an offer of confirmation. That many still did not believe (v.37) demonstrates that external signs, however miraculous, cannot compel faith where the heart is closed (cf. Luke 16:31).

verses.kjv_text: Now is the judgment of this world: now shall the prince of this world be cast out.
verses.original_text: νῦν κρίσις ἐστὶν τοῦ κόσμου τούτου νῦν ὁ ἄρχων τοῦ κόσμου τούτου ἐκβληθήσεται ἔξω
verses.transliteration: nyn krisis estin tou kosmou toutou nyn ho archōn tou kosmou toutou ekblēthēsetai exō
verses.study_note: 'Now is the judgment of this world' (nyn krisis estin tou kosmou toutou) — krisis (judgment, decisive turning point) arrives at the cross. The world system organised in opposition to God is judged by the death of the Son. 'The prince of this world' (ho archōn tou kosmou toutou — the ruler of this world) is Satan (cf. 14:30; 16:11; 2 Corinthians 4:4; Ephesians 2:2). 'Shall be cast out' (ekblēthēsetai exō — will be thrown out). The cross is the decisive defeat of Satan: 'he has no claim on me' (14:30). Colossians 2:15 describes it as a public disarming and triumphal procession. The 'casting out' is the reversal of the original rebellion (Isaiah 14:12-15; Revelation 12:9).

verses.kjv_text: And I, if I be lifted up from the earth, will draw all men unto me.
verses.original_text: κἀγὼ ἐὰν ὑψωθῶ ἐκ τῆς γῆς πάντας ἑλκύσω πρὸς ἐμαυτόν
verses.transliteration: kagō ean hypsōthō ek tēs gēs pantas helkysō pros emauton
verses.study_note: 'If I be lifted up' (ean hypsōthō — when/if I am lifted up; hypsōo is used in John for both crucifixion [3:14; 8:28] and exaltation — the ambiguity is intentional: the lifting on the cross is the lifting into glory). 'Will draw all men unto me' (pantas helkysō pros emauton — I will draw all people to myself). 'Helkō' (to draw, drag, pull) conveys the powerful, irresistible attraction of Christ crucified. 'All men' (pantas) does not mean every individual without exception necessarily, but all without distinction — Jew and Gentile, the 'world' of v.19 and the Greeks of v.20. The cross has universal drawing power.

verses.kjv_text: This he said, signifying what death he should die.
verses.original_text: τοῦτο δὲ ἔλεγεν σημαίνων ποίῳ θανάτῳ ἤμελλεν ἀποθνῄσκειν
verses.transliteration: touto de elegen sēmainōn poiō thanatō ēmellen apothnēiskein
verses.study_note: John provides the editorial gloss: 'lifted up from the earth' refers to crucifixion, the Roman death by suspension on a cross above the ground. Crucifixion was the death 'from the earth' — elevated between heaven and earth, between God and humanity, the perfect position for the mediator (1 Timothy 2:5).

verses.kjv_text: The people answered him, We have heard out of the law that Christ abideth for ever: and how sayest thou, The Son of man must be lifted up? who is this Son of man?
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη οὖν αὐτῷ ὁ ὄχλος Ἡμεῖς ἠκούσαμεν ἐκ τοῦ νόμου ὅτι ὁ Χριστὸς μένει εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα καὶ πῶς σὺ λέγεις ὅτι δεῖ ὑψωθῆναι τὸν υἱὸν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου τίς ἐστιν οὗτος ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου
verses.transliteration: apekrithē oun autō ho ochlos Hēmeis ēkousamen ek tou nomou hoti ho Christos menei eis ton aiōna kai pōs sy legeis hoti dei hypsōthēnai ton huion tou anthrōpou tis estin houtos ho huios tou anthrōpou
verses.study_note: The crowd cites Jewish messianic expectation: 'Christ abideth for ever' (ho Christos menei eis ton aiōna — the Messiah remains into the age). This may draw on texts like Psalm 110:4; Isaiah 9:7; Ezekiel 37:25; Psalm 89:36-37. If the Messiah is eternal, how can he be 'lifted up' in death? The crowd has correctly heard 'Son of man' as a messianic title (Daniel 7:13-14) but cannot reconcile a dying Messiah with their expectation of a triumphant one. This is the fundamental messianic misunderstanding that the cross resolves — the Messiah must die to abide forever.

verses.kjv_text: Then Jesus said unto them, Yet a little while is the light with you. Walk while ye have the light, lest darkness come upon you: for he that walketh in darkness knoweth not whither he goeth.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν οὖν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ἔτι μικρὸν χρόνον τὸ φῶς ἐν ὑμῖν ἐστιν περιπατεῖτε ὡς τὸ φῶς ἔχετε ἵνα μὴ σκοτία ὑμᾶς καταλάβῃ καὶ ὁ περιπατῶν ἐν τῇ σκοτίᾳ οὐκ οἶδεν ποῦ ὑπάγει
verses.transliteration: eipen oun autois ho Iēsous Eti mikron chronon to phōs en hymin estin peripateite hōs to phōs echete hina mē skotia hymas katalabē kai ho peripatōn en tē skotia ouk oiden pou hypagei
verses.study_note: 'Yet a little while is the light with you' (Eti mikron chronon to phōs en hymin estin — still a little time the light is among you). Jesus does not answer the question about the Son of man directly but issues an urgent appeal based on the limited window of opportunity. 'Walk while ye have the light' (peripateite hōs to phōs echete — walk while you have the light; present imperative: keep walking). 'Lest darkness come upon you' (hina mē skotia hymas katalabē — so that darkness may not overtake/seize you). 'Katalabē' (from katalambanō — to seize, overtake, apprehend). The darkness overtakes the unprepared traveller. The time for decision is now, while the light is present.

verses.kjv_text: While ye have light, believe in the light, that ye may be the children of light. These things spake Jesus, and departed, and did hide himself from them.
verses.original_text: ὡς τὸ φῶς ἔχετε πιστεύετε εἰς τὸ φῶς ἵνα υἱοὶ φωτὸς γένησθε Ταῦτα ἐλάλησεν Ἰησοῦς καὶ ἀπελθὼν ἐκρύβη ἀπ᾽ αὐτῶν
verses.transliteration: hōs to phōs echete pisteuete eis to phōs hina huioi phōtos genēsthe Tauta elalēsen Iēsous kai apelthōn ekrybē ap autōn
verses.study_note: 'Believe in the light, that ye may be the children of light' (pisteuete eis to phōs hina huioi phōtos genēsthe — believe into the light so that you may become sons of light). 'Sons of light' (huioi phōtos) is a Hebraic idiom for those characterised by light, belonging to light (cf. 1 Thessalonians 5:5; Ephesians 5:8; Luke 16:8). 'Departed, and did hide himself' (apelthōn ekrybē ap autōn) — Jesus withdraws from the public for the last time. The Passion narrative now begins. The public ministry is over.

verses.kjv_text: But though he had done so many miracles before them, yet they believed not on him:
verses.original_text: Τοσαῦτα δὲ αὐτοῦ σημεῖα πεποιηκότος ἔμπροσθεν αὐτῶν οὐκ ἐπίστευον εἰς αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: Tosauta de autou sēmeia pepoiēkotos emprosthen autōn ouk episteuon eis auton
verses.study_note: 'Though he had done so many miracles' (tosauta sēmeia pepoiēkotos — having done so many signs). John pauses to reflect on the mystery of unbelief. The sheer volume and quality of the signs — seven major ones recorded — did not produce faith in the majority. This mirrors the wilderness generation: 'How many times did they provoke him in the wilderness, and grieve him in the desert!' (Psalm 78:40). Signs can be witnessed without eliciting faith.

verses.kjv_text: That the saying of Esaias the prophet might be fulfilled, which he spake, Lord, who hath believed our report? and to whom hath the arm of the Lord been revealed?
verses.original_text: ἵνα ὁ λόγος Ἠσαΐου τοῦ προφήτου πληρωθῇ ὃν εἶπεν Κύριε τίς ἐπίστευσεν τῇ ἀκοῇ ἡμῶν καὶ ὁ βραχίων κυρίου τίνι ἀπεκαλύφθη
verses.transliteration: hina ho logos Ēsaiou tou prophētou plērōthē hon eipen Kyrie tis episteusen tē akoē hēmōn kai ho brachiōn kyriou tini apekalyphthē
verses.study_note: Quoting Isaiah 53:1 — the opening of the great Servant Song. 'Who hath believed our report?' (tis episteusen tē akoē hēmōn — who has believed what they heard from us?). 'The arm of the Lord' (ho brachiōn kyriou — the powerful saving action of God) is a Hebraic phrase for God's mighty intervention (Exodus 6:6; Psalm 98:1; Isaiah 52:10). Isaiah predicted that the Servant's ministry would be met with pervasive unbelief — a prediction fulfilled precisely in the ministry of Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore they could not believe, because that Esaias said again,
verses.original_text: διὰ τοῦτο οὐκ ἠδύναντο πιστεύειν ὅτι πάλιν εἶπεν Ἠσαΐας
verses.transliteration: dia touto ouk ēdynanto pisteuein hoti palin eipen Ēsaias
verses.study_note: 'They could not believe' (ouk ēdynanto pisteuein — they were not able to believe). John makes a strong statement: their inability to believe is not merely an unwillingness but a divine judicial hardening. The theological tension between human responsibility (v.37: 'they believed not') and divine sovereignty (v.39: 'they could not believe') is not resolved by John but held together. Both are true. Isaiah's prophecy is given as the explanation — God's foreknowledge and human rebellion interlock.

verses.kjv_text: He hath blinded their eyes, and hardened their heart; that they should not see with their eyes, nor understand with their heart, and be converted, and I should heal them.
verses.original_text: Τετύφλωκεν αὐτῶν τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς καὶ ἐπώρωσεν αὐτῶν τὴν καρδίαν ἵνα μὴ ἴδωσιν τοῖς ὀφθαλμοῖς καὶ νοήσωσιν τῇ καρδίᾳ καὶ ἐπιστραφῶσιν καὶ ἰάσομαι αὐτούς
verses.transliteration: Tetyphlōken autōn tous ophthalmous kai epōrōsen autōn tēn kardian hina mē idōsin tois ophthalmois kai noēsōsin tē kardia kai epistraphōsin kai iasomai autous
verses.study_note: Quoting Isaiah 6:9-10 — God's commission to Isaiah at his call. 'He hath blinded their eyes' (tetyphlōken — perfect: has blinded, with ongoing effect) and 'hardened their heart' (epōrōsen — aorist: hardened). This is cited by all four Gospels and Paul (Matthew 13:14-15; Mark 4:12; Luke 8:10; Acts 28:26-27; Romans 11:8). The hardening is not arbitrary cruelty but the divine confirmation of freely chosen rejection. God gives people over to what they have persistently chosen (Romans 1:24,26,28). 'And I should heal them' — the purpose of the blinding is remedial in Isaiah's context: Israel will be exiled and then restored. The door of mercy is not finally shut.

verses.kjv_text: These things said Esaias, when he saw his glory, and spake of him.
verses.original_text: ταῦτα εἶπεν Ἠσαΐας ὅτε εἶδεν τὴν δόξαν αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐλάλησεν περὶ αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: tauta eipen Ēsaias hote eiden tēn doxan autou kai elalēsen peri autou
verses.study_note: 'When he saw his glory' (hote eiden tēn doxan autou). John makes an astonishing claim: Isaiah's vision of the enthroned Lord in Isaiah 6:1-3 ('I saw also the Lord sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up, and his train filled the temple... Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of hosts') was a vision of the pre-incarnate Christ. 'His glory' (tēn doxan autou) refers to Jesus. 'Spake of him' (elalēsen peri autou) — Isaiah's prophecies are Christological. This is one of the clearest NT affirmations of Jesus' pre-existence and divine identity: the LORD of Hosts whom Isaiah saw is Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: Nevertheless among the chief rulers also many believed on him; but because of the Pharisees they did not confess him, lest they should be put out of the synagogue:
verses.original_text: ὅμως μέντοι καὶ ἐκ τῶν ἀρχόντων πολλοὶ ἐπίστευσαν εἰς αὐτόν ἀλλὰ διὰ τοὺς Φαρισαίους οὐχ ὡμολόγουν ἵνα μὴ ἀποσυνάγωγοι γένωνται
verses.transliteration: homōs mentoi kai ek tōn archontōn polloi episteusan eis auton alla dia tous Pharisaious ouch hōmologoun hina mē aposynagōgoi genōntai
verses.study_note: 'Among the chief rulers also many believed' (ek tōn archontōn polloi episteusan) — members of the Sanhedrin, the ruling council. Nicodemus (3:1-2; 7:50-51) and Joseph of Arimathaea (19:38) are named later. 'They did not confess him' (ouch hōmologoun — imperfect: were not confessing him; continued silence). 'Lest they should be put out of the synagogue' (aposynagōgoi genōntai — lest they become excommunicated from the synagogue). This is the social death: loss of community, commerce, family ties. Secret faith under social pressure — a perennial temptation.

verses.kjv_text: For they loved the praise of men more than the praise of God.
verses.original_text: ἠγάπησαν γὰρ τὴν δόξαν τῶν ἀνθρώπων μᾶλλον ἤπερ τὴν δόξαν τοῦ θεοῦ
verses.transliteration: ēgapēsan gar tēn doxan tōn anthrōpōn mallon ēper tēn doxan tou theou
verses.study_note: 'They loved the praise of men more than the praise of God' (tēn doxan tōn anthrōpōn mallon ēper tēn doxan tou theou). 'Praise' (doxan — glory, honour). The same word used throughout ch.12 for divine glory is here applied to human approval. The choice is between God's doxa (eternal, true) and human doxa (temporary, false). This is the fundamental disorder of fallen human nature: preferring creature-honour over Creator-honour. John 5:44 asks the same question: 'How can ye believe, which receive honour one of another, and seek not the honour that cometh from God only?'

verses.kjv_text: Jesus cried and said, He that believeth on me, believeth not on me, but on him that sent me.
verses.original_text: Ἰησοῦς δὲ ἔκραξεν καὶ εἶπεν Ὁ πιστεύων εἰς ἐμὲ οὐ πιστεύει εἰς ἐμὲ ἀλλ᾽ εἰς τὸν πέμψαντά με
verses.transliteration: Iēsous de ekraxen kai eipen Ho pisteuōn eis eme ou pisteuei eis eme all eis ton pempsanta me
verses.study_note: 'Jesus cried' (ekraxen — shouted aloud, cried out; a loud, urgent proclamation). This is Jesus' final public cry before the Passion. 'He that believeth on me, believeth not on me, but on him that sent me' — this is not a contradiction but a statement of the Trinitarian unity: believing in Jesus is believing in the Father. To receive the Son is to receive the Father (Matthew 10:40; Luke 10:16; John 13:20). To reject one is to reject the other.

verses.kjv_text: And he that seeth me seeth him that sent me.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὁ θεωρῶν ἐμὲ θεωρεῖ τὸν πέμψαντά με
verses.transliteration: kai ho theōrōn eme theōrei ton pempsanta me
verses.study_note: 'He that seeth me seeth him that sent me' — the same principle applied to sight/perception. Jesus is the visible image of the invisible God (Colossians 1:15; Hebrews 1:3). Philip will ask the same question in 14:8, and Jesus will give the same answer: 'He that hath seen me hath seen the Father' (14:9). The incarnation makes God visible; the Son is the definitive revelation of the Father.

verses.kjv_text: I am come a light into the world, that whosoever believeth on me should not abide in darkness.
verses.original_text: ἐγὼ φῶς εἰς τὸν κόσμον ἐλήλυθα ἵνα πᾶς ὁ πιστεύων εἰς ἐμὲ ἐν τῇ σκοτίᾳ μὴ μείνῃ
verses.transliteration: egō phōs eis ton kosmon elēlytha hina pas ho pisteuōn eis eme en tē skotia mē meinē
verses.study_note: 'I am come a light into the world' (egō phōs eis ton kosmon elēlytha) — echoing 1:4-5,9; 8:12. The purpose statement: 'that whosoever believeth on me should not abide in darkness' (hina pas ho pisteuōn eis eme en tē skotia mē meinē — so that everyone believing into me may not remain in darkness). 'Meinē' (remain, abide) — the believer is delivered from darkness as a permanent state, not merely as an occasional experience. The light transforms and keeps.

verses.kjv_text: And if any man hear my words, and believe not, I judge him not: for I came not to judge the world, but to save the world.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐάν τίς μου ἀκούσῃ τῶν ῥημάτων καὶ μὴ φυλάξῃ ἐγὼ οὐ κρίνω αὐτόν οὐ γὰρ ἦλθον ἵνα κρίνω τὸν κόσμον ἀλλ᾽ ἵνα σώσω τὸν κόσμον
verses.transliteration: kai ean tis mou akousē tōn rhēmatōn kai mē phylaxē egō ou krinō auton ou gar ēlthon hina krinō ton kosmon all hina sōsō ton kosmon
verses.study_note: 'I judge him not' (egō ou krinō auton) — during the first advent, Jesus came in saving mission, not judicial. 'I came not to judge the world, but to save the world' (ou gar ēlthon hina krinō ton kosmon all hina sōsō ton kosmon). This does not mean there is no judgment; v.48 shows the word itself will judge. But judgment is not the present purpose — salvation is. The first advent is the day of grace; the second advent is the day of judgment (cf. John 5:22,27; Acts 17:31).

verses.kjv_text: He that rejecteth me, and receiveth not my words, hath one that judgeth him: the word that I have spoken, the same shall judge him in the last day.
verses.original_text: ὁ ἀθετῶν ἐμὲ καὶ μὴ λαμβάνων τὰ ῥήματά μου ἔχει τὸν κρίνοντα αὐτόν ὁ λόγος ὃν ἐλάλησα ἐκεῖνος κρινεῖ αὐτὸν ἐν τῇ ἐσχάτῃ ἡμέρᾳ
verses.transliteration: ho athetōn eme kai mē lambanōn ta rhēmata mou echei ton krinonta auton ho logos hon elalēsa ekeinos krinei auton en tē eschatē hēmera
verses.study_note: 'He that rejecteth me' (ho athetōn eme — the one setting me aside, nullifying me; athetō = to set aside as invalid). 'The word that I have spoken, the same shall judge him in the last day' (ho logos hon elalēsa ekeinos krinei auton en tē eschatē hēmera). The 'last day' (hē eschatē hēmera) — a phrase John uses repeatedly (6:39,40,44,54; 11:24). The logos — the very word of life offered and rejected — becomes the standard of judgment. Those who have heard the gospel and rejected it are judged by what they have spurned. The measure of privilege is the measure of accountability.

verses.kjv_text: For I have not spoken of myself; but the Father which sent me, he gave me a commandment, what I should say, and what I should speak.
verses.original_text: ὅτι ἐγὼ ἐξ ἐμαυτοῦ οὐκ ἐλάλησα ἀλλ᾽ ὁ πέμψας με πατὴρ αὐτός μοι ἐντολὴν δέδωκεν τί εἴπω καὶ τί λαλήσω
verses.transliteration: hoti egō ex emautou ouk elalēsa all ho pempsas me patēr autos moi entolēn dedōken ti eipō kai ti lalēsō
verses.study_note: 'I have not spoken of myself' (egō ex emautou ouk elalēsa — I did not speak from myself). The authority of Jesus' words is grounded in the Father's commission: 'the Father which sent me, he gave me a commandment' (entolēn dedōken — has given me a commandment; perfect: a standing directive). Every word Jesus has spoken in the Gospel of John carries the authority of the Father's instruction. To reject his words is to reject the Father's commandment.

verses.kjv_text: And I know that his commandment is life everlasting: whatsoever I speak therefore, even as the Father said unto me, so I speak.
verses.original_text: καὶ οἶδα ὅτι ἡ ἐντολὴ αὐτοῦ ζωὴ αἰώνιός ἐστιν ἃ οὖν λαλῶ ἐγώ καθὼς εἴρηκέν μοι ὁ πατήρ οὕτως λαλῶ
verses.transliteration: kai oida hoti hē entolē autou zōē aiōnios estin ha oun lalō egō kathōs eirēken moi ho patēr houtōs lalō
verses.study_note: 'His commandment is life everlasting' (hē entolē autou zōē aiōnios estin — his commandment is eternal life). The Father's commandment is not merely about eternal life — it IS eternal life in content and effect. 'Whatsoever I speak therefore, even as the Father said unto me, so I speak' (ha oun lalō egō kathōs eirēken moi ho patēr houtōs lalō). Jesus closes his public ministry with this declaration of perfect fidelity: every word faithfully transmitted from the Father. His public discourse is complete; henceforth he speaks privately to his disciples (chapters 13-17).

1.meaning: Six days before the Passover — on the Saturday before Passion Week. John alone fixes the Bethany supper at this moment (cf. Mark 14:3). The countdown to the cross has begun; the Lamb is inspected before sacrifice (Exod 12:3-6).

3.meaning: A pound of ointment of spikenard — pistikē = pure, unadulterated; nardos = aromatic oil from the Himalayan nard root, imported at great cost. A litra ≈ 12 ounces, a year's wages liquefied into one extravagant act.
Very costly — polytimos = of great price (same word Jesus uses of the pearl of great price, Matt 13:46). Mary's treasure is poured out on Jesus' feet.

Anointed the feet — aleiphō = to anoint (distinct from chriō, cultic anointing). Mary chooses the humblest part of the body; the action of a servant, not a priest. The feet will soon walk to Golgotha.

Wiped with her hair — unbound hair in public was scandalous for a Jewish woman; she surrenders dignity as well as wealth. The whole house is filled with the fragrance (osmē) — worship perfumes the whole community.

5.meaning: Three hundred pence — 300 denarii ≈ one year's wages for a labourer (a denarius = a day's wage, Matt 20:2). Judas prices devotion in coin; Mary prices it in love.

6.meaning: Thief — kleptēs = one who pilfers stealthily (vs. lēstēs, a violent robber). John's retrospective verdict on Judas: his concern for the poor was a cover for private theft.
The bag — originally a case for the tongues/reeds of flutes, then a money-box. Judas held the common purse; proximity to Jesus did not cure him.
Bare — imperfect: habitually carried away (pilfered). John uses the same verb in a different sense (he who "took" what was put in) — double meaning: he carried the box and carried off its contents.

7.original_word: εἰς τὴν ἡμέραν τοῦ ἐνταφιασμοῦ μου
7.transliteration: eis tēn hēmeran tou entaphiasmou mou
7.meaning: For the day of my burying — entaphiasmos = preparation for burial. Mary's anointing interprets Jesus' death before it happens; she alone among his followers has grasped the passion prediction.

10.meaning: Took counsel — aorist middle: deliberated among themselves. The chief priests plot to kill Lazarus (whose resurrection is evidence too dangerous to leave living); murder compounds murder.

13.meaning: Branches of palm trees — baion = young palm frond. Palm branches had become a national-messianic symbol after the Maccabees (1 Macc 13:51; 2 Macc 10:7). The crowd hails Jesus as warrior-king — a role he will not play.

Hosanna — transliterated Hebrew hôšîʿâ-nāʾ (Ps 118:25): "Save now!" By the first century hardened into a cry of acclamation as well as petition. The pilgrim psalm (Ps 113-118, the Hallel) is sung at Passover.

13.original_word: Εὐλογημένος ὁ ἐρχόμενος ἐν ὀνόματι Κυρίου
13.transliteration: Eulogēmenos ho erchomenos en onomati Kyriou
13.meaning: Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord — direct quotation of Ps 118:26, the pilgrim greeting. John adds "even the King of Israel" — the crowd (and John) read the psalm as coronation.

14.meaning: A young ass — diminutive of onos, a foal. Jesus deliberately selects the mount of peace (Zech 9:9) rather than the war-horse; his kingship confounds expectations.

15.meaning: Daughter of Sion — personification of Jerusalem and its people. Zech 9:9 quoted: "Fear not... thy King cometh, sitting on an ass's colt." Jesus enacts prophecy consciously.

16.meaning: Was glorified — aorist passive: only in retrospect (after Resurrection) did the disciples understand that Scripture had predicted this scene. John repeatedly acknowledges post-Easter illumination (2:22; 20:9).

21.meaning: Greeks — Gentile God-fearers (not Hellenistic Jews = Hellēnistai). Their approach signals the world-wide reach of the gospel; Jesus takes it as the sign that his hour has come.

23.meaning: The hour is come that the Son of man should be glorified — perfect tense (elēlythen): has arrived and abides. In John the hour is the cross as glory; crucifixion IS glorification (cf. 2:4; 7:30; 8:20; 13:1; 17:1).

24.meaning: A corn of wheat — kokkos = single grain. The parable of the seed: apparent burial is the precondition of abundant life. Applied first to Jesus' own death-resurrection, then to all discipleship.
Die... bear fruit — aorist subjunctive + present indicative: unless death occurs, no fruit. The logic of the kingdom: multiplication through death, never apart from it.

25.meaning: He that loveth his life — philōn = has affection for. psychē here = earthly life, one's natural self-preservation. Cling to it and lose it; John's version of the synoptic cross-saying.
Hateth — Semitic idiom of comparative preference (Luke 14:26). Not literal hostility to one's life, but a decisive preference for eternal life over temporal self-interest.

26.meaning: Servant — diakonos = table-waiter, one who renders lowly service. The Messiah is served by serving him; Jesus' followers share his posture, and his Father's honour (timēsei ho patēr).

27.meaning: Is troubled — perfect passive: has been and remains agitated. John's brief Gethsemane: the same inward tumult Matthew/Mark locate in the garden (Matt 26:38), compressed here.

27.original_word: σῶσόν με ἐκ τῆς ὥρας ταύτης
27.transliteration: sōson me ek tēs hōras tautēs
27.meaning: Save me from this hour — the unspoken prayer Jesus rejects. "For this cause came I unto this hour": the hour is not avoided but embraced, because it is the purpose of the incarnation.
