28.meaning: I have both glorified it and will glorify it again — the Father's audible voice. Past glorifications (the signs) and future (the cross-resurrection) join in one testimony. The third Father-voice in the Gospels (baptism, transfiguration, here — on the eve of the passion).

31.meaning: The judgment of this world — krisis = decisive verdict. At the Cross the world judges Jesus, but in fact Jesus judges the world; the verdict reverses.
The prince of this world — Satan, the usurping ruler. His eviction (ekblēthēsetai exō — shall be cast out) is accomplished at Golgotha; the Cross dethrones the dragon.

32.meaning: I be lifted up from the earth — hypsoō = to raise up. John's triple use (3:14; 8:28; 12:32) exploits the pun: physical lifting on the cross IS exaltation. Verse 33 explains: "signifying what death he should die."
I will draw — helkyō = to draw, attract (used of the Father's drawing in 6:44). Not coerce, but attract. "All" = people of every kind, now that the Gentile Greeks have come asking.

34.original_word: ὁ Χριστὸς μένει εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα
34.transliteration: ho Christos menei eis ton aiōna
34.meaning: Christ abideth for ever — the crowd cites popular messianic expectation (Ps 89:36-37; Isa 9:7; Dan 7:14). A dying Messiah contradicts their theology; yet a dying Messiah is what Scripture prophesied (Isa 53).

35.meaning: A little while — the limited window of Jesus' public ministry. Walk in the light while the light is with you; tomorrow the darkness arrives.

38.meaning: The saying of Isaiah — John names the prophet. The quotation is Isa 53:1 LXX verbatim: "Lord, who hath believed our report?" Unbelief was foretold; the rejection of the Servant is scripted.
Report — akoē = that which is heard, the preached message. Double sense: the auditory proclamation and its reception; both failed among most hearers.
The arm of the Lord — Old Testament idiom for God's saving power (Exod 6:6; Isa 52:10). Jesus IS the arm of the Lord revealed, and few believed the revelation.

40.meaning: Blinded... hardened — perfect actives from Isa 6:9-10. Pōroō = to petrify, harden like a callus. A divine-judicial hardening upon persistent unbelief: sin becomes its own punishment.

41.meaning: Isaiah saw his glory — the vision of Isa 6 (the Lord high and lifted up) was a vision of Christ. John identifies the pre-incarnate Christ as the one enthroned in Isaiah's temple theophany.

42.meaning: Put out of the synagogue — aposynagōgos, a Johannine word (9:22; 12:42; 16:2). Synagogue excommunication meant social, economic, and religious ostracism; a costly confession indeed.

43.meaning: Loved the praise of men — two doxai in competition: human honour and divine honour. The rulers chose the lesser; the tragic diagnosis of every cowardly faith.

46.meaning: A light into the world — perfect tense elēlytha (I have come and remain). The mission statement, echoing 1:9; 8:12; 9:5. Whoever believes does not abide in darkness.

47.meaning: I came not to judge — the first advent is salvation-oriented; judgment is secondary and eschatological. Those who reject the word are judged by the word itself on the last day.

48.meaning: The word that I have spoken — Jesus' words ARE the agent of the last-day verdict (v.48). Scripture and the word of Christ are one court of judgment.

49.meaning: Gave commandment — perfect tense: a standing directive. Jesus' words are neither private opinion nor prophetic borrowing; they are the Father's authoritative entolē lodged in the Son.

50.original_word: ἡ ἐντολὴ αὐτοῦ ζωὴ αἰώνιός ἐστιν
50.transliteration: hē entolē autou zōē aiōnios estin
50.meaning: His commandment is life everlasting — the Father's entolē IS eternal life, not merely its precondition. The word of Jesus carries the life it promises.

meta.overview: John 13 opens the second half of the Gospel — the Book of Glory (chapters 13-21), set entirely in the context of the Passion and its aftermath. The chapter is set at the Last Supper (not the Passover meal in John's chronology, but the evening before — John places the crucifixion on Nisan 14, the day the Passover lambs were slaughtered). It is the longest single evening in the Gospels, introducing five chapters of intimate teaching to the Twelve (chapters 13-17). The chapter opens with a majestic prologue: 'Jesus knowing that his hour was come that he should depart out of this world unto the Father, having loved his own which were in the world, he loved them unto the end' (v.1). 'Unto the end' (eis telos — to the uttermost, completely, finally). The love that sent Jesus into the world now drives him through death out of it. The foot-washing (vv.4-11) is the enacted parable of this love: the Lord and Master removes his garments, girds himself with a towel, and washes his disciples' feet — the work of the lowest slave. Peter's horrified protest (v.6) gives Jesus occasion to teach that such washing is necessary for fellowship with him (v.8) and that it is a model for all his disciples (vv.14-17). Jesus then reveals to the disciples that one of them will betray him (vv.18-30). The beloved disciple reclines at Jesus' bosom; Peter asks him to inquire of Jesus the identity of the betrayer. Jesus identifies Judas by the dipped morsel; 'Satan entered into him' (v.27); Judas goes out into the night (v.30). The chapter closes with the New Commandment: 'A new commandment I give unto you, That ye love one another; as I have loved you, that ye also love one another' (v.34), and Peter's confident claim that he will lay down his life, followed by Jesus' prediction that Peter will deny him three times before the cock crows (v.38).
meta.theme: Love to the End — Servant Love as the Mark of Discipleship
meta.keyWordMeaning: To love, to cherish, to regard with a self-giving, unconditional love — distinguished from phileō (warm affection) and erōs (romantic love). Agapaō in John 13 is love as action, not feeling: Jesus loved his own 'unto the end' (v.1) and demonstrated it by washing their feet. The New Commandment (v.34) is to agapaō one another 'as I have loved you' — the standard of love is the cross-shaped, servant love of Christ himself. The noun agapē and verb agapaō form the backbone of the Upper Room Discourse (chs.13-17). This is not a generalised sentiment but a specific orientation: willing the good of another at cost to oneself.
outline: Prologue: The Hour of Love (vv.1-3): Before the feast of the passover when Jesus knew that his hour was come that he should depart out of this world unto the Father having loved his own which were in the world he loved them unto the end. And supper being ended the devil having now put into the heart of Judas Iscariot Simon's son to betray him — Jesus knowing that the Father had given all things into his hands and that he was come from God and went to God

The Foot-washing (vv.4-11): He riseth from supper and laid aside his garments and took a towel and girded himself. After that he poureth water into a bason and began to wash the disciples' feet and to wipe them with the towel wherewith he was girded. Then cometh he to Simon Peter and Peter saith unto him Lord dost thou wash my feet? Jesus answered and said unto him What I do thou knowest not now but thou shalt know hereafter. Peter saith unto him Thou shalt never wash my feet. Jesus answered him If I wash thee not thou hast no part with me. Simon Peter saith unto him Lord not my feet only but also my hands and my head. Jesus saith to him He that is washed needeth not save to wash his feet but is clean every whit and ye are clean but not all. For he knew who should betray him therefore said he Ye are not all clean

The Example Explained (vv.12-17): So after he had washed their feet and had taken his garments and was set down again he said unto them Know ye what I have done to you? Ye call me Master and Lord and ye say well for so I am. If I then your Lord and Master have washed your feet ye also ought to wash one another's feet. For I have given you an example that ye should do as I have done to you. Verily verily I say unto you The servant is not greater than his lord neither he that is sent greater than he that sent him. If ye know these things happy are ye if ye do them

The Betrayer Foretold (vv.18-30): I speak not of you all I know whom I have chosen but that the scripture may be fulfilled He that eateth bread with me hath lifted up his heel against me. Now I tell you before it come that when it is come to pass ye may believe that I am he. Verily verily I say unto you He that receiveth whomsoever I send receiveth me and he that receiveth me receiveth him that sent me. When Jesus had thus said he was troubled in spirit and testified and said Verily verily I say unto you that one of you shall betray me. Then the disciples looked one on another doubting of whom he spake. Now there was leaning on Jesus' bosom one of his disciples whom Jesus loved. Simon Peter therefore beckoned to him that he should ask who it should be of whom he spake. He then lying on Jesus' breast saith unto him Lord who is it? Jesus answered He it is to whom I shall give a sop when I have dipped it. And when he had dipped the sop he gave it to Judas Iscariot the son of Simon. And after the sop Satan entered into him. Then said Jesus unto him That thou doest do quickly. Now no man at the table knew for what intent he spake this unto him. For some of them thought because Judas had the bag that Jesus had said Buy those things that we have need of against the feast or that he should give something to the poor. He then having received the sop went immediately out and it was night

The New Commandment and Peter's Denial Foretold (vv.31-38): Therefore when he was gone out Jesus said Now is the Son of man glorified and God is glorified in him. If God be glorified in him God shall also glorify him in himself and shall straightway glorify him. Little children yet a little while I am with you. Ye shall seek me and as I said unto the Jews Whither I go ye cannot come so now I say to you. A new commandment I give unto you That ye love one another as I have loved you that ye also love one another. By this shall all men know that ye are my disciples if ye have love one to another. Simon Peter said unto him Lord whither goest thou? Jesus answered him Whither I go thou canst not follow me now but thou shalt follow me afterwards. Peter said unto him Lord why cannot I follow thee now? I will lay down my life for thy sake. Jesus answered him Wilt thou lay down thy life for my sake? Verily verily I say unto thee The cock shall not crow till thou hast denied me thrice

verses.kjv_text: Now before the feast of the passover, when Jesus knew that his hour was come that he should depart out of this world unto the Father, having loved his own which were in the world, he loved them unto the end.
verses.original_text: Πρὸ δὲ τῆς ἑορτῆς τοῦ πάσχα εἰδὼς ὁ Ἰησοῦς ὅτι ἦλθεν αὐτοῦ ἡ ὥρα ἵνα μεταβῇ ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου τούτου πρὸς τὸν πατέρα ἀγαπήσας τοὺς ἰδίους τοὺς ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ εἰς τέλος ἠγάπησεν αὐτούς
verses.transliteration: Pro de tēs heortēs tou pascha eidōs ho Iēsous hoti ēlthen autou hē hōra hina metabē ek tou kosmou toutou pros ton patera agapēsas tous idious tous en tō kosmō eis telos ēgapēsen autous
verses.study_note: 'Before the feast of the passover' (pro tēs heortēs tou pascha) — John places this supper the evening before the Passover, making the crucifixion occur on Nisan 14 when the lambs were being slaughtered — Jesus as the true Passover Lamb (19:14; 1 Corinthians 5:7). 'Having loved his own' (agapēsas tous idious — having loved those who were his own; the participle sets the background). 'Unto the end' (eis telos) — this phrase has a double meaning: (1) to the end, until the last moment; (2) completely, to the uttermost. Both meanings apply. The cross is both the final act and the fullest expression of his love. John opens the Passion narrative not with betrayal or arrest but with love.

verses.kjv_text: And supper being ended, the devil having now put into the heart of Judas Iscariot, Simon's son, to betray him;
verses.original_text: καὶ δείπνου γινομένου τοῦ διαβόλου ἤδη βεβληκότος εἰς τὴν καρδίαν Ἰούδα Σίμωνος Ἰσκαριώτου ἵνα αὐτὸν παραδοῖ
verses.transliteration: kai deipnou ginomenou tou diabolou ēdē beblēkotos eis tēn kardian Iouda Simōnos Iskariōtou hina auton paradoi
verses.study_note: 'The devil having now put into the heart of Judas... to betray him' (tou diabolou ēdē beblēkotos eis tēn kardian Iouda — the devil having already thrown into the heart of Judas). 'Beblēkotos' (perfect participle from ballō: to throw, cast) — the devil had already cast the intention into Judas's heart before the supper began. 'Diabolos' (devil, from diaballō: to throw across, slander). The betrayal is presented as a satanic instigation, but Judas is not merely a puppet — he acts as one responsible (v.27; Acts 1:18-19). The mystery of human agency and satanic influence is maintained.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus knowing that the Father had given all things into his hands, and that he was come from God, and went to God;
verses.original_text: εἰδὼς ὁ Ἰησοῦς ὅτι πάντα ἔδωκεν αὐτῷ ὁ πατὴρ εἰς τὰς χεῖρας καὶ ὅτι ἀπὸ θεοῦ ἐξῆλθεν καὶ πρὸς τὸν θεὸν ὑπάγει
verses.transliteration: eidōs ho Iēsous hoti panta edōken autō ho patēr eis tas cheiras kai hoti apo theou exēlthen kai pros ton theon hypagei
verses.study_note: 'Knowing that the Father had given all things into his hands' (eidōs hoti panta edōken autō ho patēr eis tas cheiras) — full knowledge, full authority. 'He was come from God, and went to God' — the eternal arc of the incarnation: pre-existence → incarnation → passion → glorification. It is precisely because Jesus knows all this — his origin, his destination, his authority — that he is free to perform the servant's act. True greatness can afford to stoop. The one who has everything can give everything.

verses.kjv_text: He riseth from supper, and laid aside his garments; and took a towel, and girded himself.
verses.original_text: ἐγείρεται ἐκ τοῦ δείπνου καὶ τίθησιν τὰ ἱμάτια καὶ λαβὼν λέντιον διέζωσεν ἑαυτόν
verses.transliteration: egeiretai ek tou deipnou kai tithēsin ta himatia kai labōn lention diezōsen heauton
verses.study_note: 'He riseth from supper' (egeiretai — present tense: rises; vivid, dramatic). 'Laid aside his garments' (tithēsin ta himatia — lays aside his garments; the same verb used in 10:11,17,18 for 'laying down' his life). The connection is intentional: laying aside his garments is a parable of laying down his life. He takes a linen towel (lention — a Latin loanword: linteum, a servant's cloth) and wraps himself with it (diezōsen — girded himself). A slave's apron. The Lord of glory dresses as a household servant.

verses.kjv_text: After that he poureth water into a bason, and began to wash the disciples' feet, and to wipe them with the towel wherewith he was girded.
verses.original_text: εἶτα βάλλει ὕδωρ εἰς τὸν νιπτῆρα καὶ ἤρξατο νίπτειν τοὺς πόδας τῶν μαθητῶν καὶ ἐκμάσσειν τῷ λεντίῳ ᾧ ἦν διεζωσμένος
verses.transliteration: eita ballei hydōr eis ton niptēra kai ērxato niptein tous podas tōn mathētōn kai ekmassein tō lentiō hō ēn diezōsmenos
verses.study_note: 'Poureth water into a bason' (ballei hydōr eis ton niptēra — pours water into the basin; niptēr = washing basin, from niptō: to wash). The roads of first-century Palestine were dusty and unpaved; guests removed sandals at the door, and foot-washing was performed by the lowest household slave or by a devoted wife. Jewish law explicitly stated that disciples should not wash their master's feet. Jesus inverts the entire hierarchy of honour. This act was so shocking that a Rabbinic text (Mekhilta on Exodus) states that a disciple should do everything for his teacher that a slave does, except remove his sandals.

verses.kjv_text: Then cometh he to Simon Peter: and Peter saith unto him, Lord, dost thou wash my feet?
verses.original_text: ἔρχεται οὖν πρὸς Σίμωνα Πέτρον λέγει αὐτῷ Κύριε σύ μου νίπτεις τοὺς πόδας
verses.transliteration: erchetai oun pros Simōna Petron legei autō Kyrie sy mou nipteis tous podas
verses.study_note: 'Lord, dost thou wash my feet?' (Kyrie, sy mou nipteis tous podas?) — the Greek word order is emphatic: 'Lord — YOU — of me — wash — the feet?' The astonishment is in the reversal: the Lord performing the slave's task. Peter's protest is entirely comprehensible and, within the normal framework of honour, entirely appropriate. The scandal of the cross has the same shape: that the holy One should take the place of the guilty is equally incomprehensible to natural reason.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered and said unto him, What I do thou knowest not now; but thou shalt know hereafter.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη Ἰησοῦς καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ Ὃ ἐγὼ ποιῶ σὺ οὐκ οἶδας ἄρτι γνώσῃ δὲ μετὰ ταῦτα
verses.transliteration: apekrithē Iēsous kai eipen autō Ho egō poiō sy ouk oidas arti gnōsē de meta tauta
verses.study_note: 'Thou knowest not now; but thou shalt know hereafter' (ouk oidas arti, gnōsē de meta tauta — you do not know now, but you will know after these things). Understanding comes after the event — after the cross, resurrection, and Pentecost. This is the pattern of discipleship in John: events are lived before they are understood; the Spirit then 'brings to remembrance' (14:26). Faith walks ahead of full comprehension.

verses.kjv_text: Peter saith unto him, Thou shalt never wash my feet. Jesus answered him, If I wash thee not, thou hast no part with me.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ Πέτρος Οὐ μὴ νίψῃς μου τοὺς πόδας εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα ἀπεκρίθη Ἰησοῦς αὐτῷ Ἐὰν μὴ νίψω σε οὐκ ἔχεις μέρος μετ᾽ ἐμοῦ
verses.transliteration: legei autō Petros Ou mē nipsēs mou tous podas eis ton aiōna apekrithē Iēsous autō Ean mē nipsō se ouk echeis meros met emou
verses.study_note: 'Thou shalt never wash my feet' (ou mē nipsēs mou tous podas eis ton aiōna — you shall certainly not wash my feet into the age). 'Ou mē' with the subjunctive is the strongest negation in Greek: absolutely not, under no circumstances. Peter's refusal is vigorous and loyal-sounding but misses the point. 'If I wash thee not, thou hast no part with me' (ean mē nipsō se, ouk echeis meros met emou — if I do not wash you, you have no share with me). The foot-washing represents cleansing that only Jesus can give; to refuse it is to forfeit fellowship. The application extends to the whole redemptive work: one cannot have 'part' with Christ while refusing his atoning service.

verses.kjv_text: Simon Peter saith unto him, Lord, not my feet only, but also my hands and my head.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ Σίμων Πέτρος Κύριε μὴ τοὺς πόδας μου μόνον ἀλλὰ καὶ τὰς χεῖρας καὶ τὴν κεφαλήν
verses.transliteration: legei autō Simōn Petros Kyrie mē tous podas mou monon alla kai tas cheiras kai tēn kephalēn
verses.study_note: Peter swings from absolute refusal to lavish demand — typical of his impulsive character. 'Not my feet only, but also my hands and my head' — an all-or-nothing response once he grasps the stakes. The irony is charming: Peter is so eager for complete fellowship that he wants to be washed from head to foot. Jesus' response distinguishes between bathing (the once-for-all washing of regeneration) and foot-washing (ongoing cleansing of daily defilement).

verses.kjv_text: Jesus saith to him, He that is washed needeth not save to wash his feet, but is clean every whit: and ye are clean, but not all.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ὁ λελουμένος οὐχὶ χρείαν ἔχει εἰ μὴ τοὺς πόδας νίψασθαι ἀλλ᾽ ἔστιν καθαρὸς ὅλος καὶ ὑμεῖς καθαροί ἐστε ἀλλ᾽ οὐχὶ πάντες
verses.transliteration: legei autō ho Iēsous Ho leloumenos ouchi chreian echei ei mē tous podas nipsasthai all estin katharos holos kai hymeis katharoi este all ouchi pantes
verses.study_note: 'He that is washed' (ho leloumenos — the one who has bathed; perfect participle from louō: to bathe the whole body) 'needeth not save to wash his feet' (nipsasthai — niptō: to wash a part of the body). The distinction is between full bathing (regeneration, once-for-all) and foot-washing (ongoing cleansing from daily defilement, 1 John 1:9). 'Ye are clean' (katharoi este — clean, pure) 'but not all' — a reference to Judas. The word 'clean' (katharos) will reappear in 15:3: 'Ye are clean through the word which I have spoken unto you.'

verses.kjv_text: For he knew who should betray him; therefore said he, Ye are not all clean.
verses.original_text: ᾔδει γὰρ τὸν παραδιδόντα αὐτόν διὰ τοῦτο εἶπεν Οὐχὶ πάντες καθαροί ἐστε
verses.transliteration: ēdei gar ton paradidonta auton dia touto eipen Ouchi pantes katharoi este
verses.study_note: John provides the editorial explanation: the exception in 'not all clean' refers to Judas (ton paradidonta auton — the one betraying him; present participle: the one in the process of betraying). Judas had participated in the foot-washing — Jesus had washed his feet too — but the physical washing could not cleanse the one who had not received the inward reality. External religious participation does not guarantee inward renewal.

verses.kjv_text: So after he had washed their feet, and had taken his garments, and was set down again, he said unto them, Know ye what I have done to you?
verses.original_text: ὅτε οὖν ἔνιψεν τοὺς πόδας αὐτῶν καὶ ἔλαβεν τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ ἀναπεσὼν πάλιν εἶπεν αὐτοῖς Γινώσκετε τί πεποίηκα ὑμῖν
verses.transliteration: hote oun enipsen tous podas autōn kai elaben ta himatia autou anapesōn palin eipen autois Ginōskete ti pepoiēka hymin
verses.study_note: 'Taken his garments' (elaben ta himatia — took back his garments; the same verb as 'laid aside' in v.4 in reverse). The enacted parable is complete; now comes the explanation. 'Know ye what I have done to you?' (Ginōskete ti pepoiēka hymin? — do you understand what I have done to you?) The question is rhetorical — they know the action but not the full meaning. Jesus is about to unfold it.

verses.kjv_text: Ye call me Master and Lord: and ye say well; for so I am.
verses.original_text: ὑμεῖς φωνεῖτέ με ὁ διδάσκαλος καὶ ὁ κύριος καὶ καλῶς λέγετε εἰμὶ γάρ
verses.transliteration: hymeis phōneite me ho didaskalos kai ho kyrios kai kalōs legete eimi gar
verses.study_note: 'Ye call me Master and Lord' (ho didaskalos kai ho kyrios — the Teacher and the Lord). Both titles are used with the definite article, indicating they are official, recognised titles. 'And ye say well' (kalōs legete — you say rightly) 'for so I am' (eimi gar — for I am). Jesus affirms the titles — he does not deflect the honour. The foot-washing is not a denial of his lordship but an expression of it: the Lord's lordship is servant-shaped. True authority serves; it does not merely command.

verses.kjv_text: If I then, your Lord and Master, have washed your feet; ye also ought to wash one another's feet.
verses.original_text: εἰ οὖν ἐγὼ ἔνιψα ὑμῶν τοὺς πόδας ὁ κύριος καὶ ὁ διδάσκαλος καὶ ὑμεῖς ὀφείλετε ἀλλήλων νίπτειν τοὺς πόδας
verses.transliteration: ei oun egō enipsa hymōn tous podas ho kyrios kai ho didaskalos kai hymeis opheilete allēlōn niptein tous podas
verses.study_note: 'Ye also ought to wash one another's feet' (kai hymeis opheilete allēlōn niptein tous podas — you also are obligated to wash one another's feet; opheilō = to owe a debt, be obligated). The argument moves from example to obligation: if the greater serves the lesser, the lesser must serve one another. Some traditions (Mennonites, some Catholic and Anglican orders) practice literal foot-washing as a sacramental rite; the broader application is mutual humble service in the community of faith.

verses.kjv_text: For I have given you an example, that ye should do as I have done to you.
verses.original_text: ὑπόδειγμα γὰρ ἔδωκα ὑμῖν ἵνα καθὼς ἐγὼ ἐποίησα ὑμῖν καὶ ὑμεῖς ποιῆτε
verses.transliteration: hypodeigma gar edōka hymin hina kathōs egō epoiēsa hymin kai hymeis poiēte
verses.study_note: 'An example' (hypodeigma — an example, a pattern set for imitation; from hypo: under + deiknymi: to show). The same word is used in James 5:10 ('example of suffering') and Hebrews 4:11 ('example of unbelief'). 'As I have done to you' (kathōs egō epoiēsa — just as I did) — the standard is not 'be helpful' or 'be nice' but 'exactly as I, the Lord, served you.' The measure is the cross-shaped service of the Son of God.

verses.kjv_text: Verily, verily, I say unto you, The servant is not greater than his lord; neither he that is sent greater than he that sent him.
verses.original_text: ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν οὐκ ἔστιν δοῦλος μείζων τοῦ κυρίου αὐτοῦ οὐδὲ ἀπόστολος μείζων τοῦ πέμψαντος αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: amēn amēn legō hymin ouk estin doulos meizōn tou kyriou autou oude apostolos meizōn tou pempsantos auton
verses.study_note: 'The servant is not greater than his lord' (ouk estin doulos meizōn tou kyriou autou). This proverb appears also in Matthew 10:24 and John 15:20. Here the application is to humility in service: if the Lord served, the servant must serve. 'Neither he that is sent greater than he that sent him' (oude apostolos meizōn tou pempsantos auton — nor an apostle greater than the one who sent him). 'Apostolos' literally means 'one sent' — its only use in John. The apostle derives authority from the Sender; the Sender is the Lord who washed feet.

verses.kjv_text: If ye know these things, happy are ye if ye do them.
verses.original_text: εἰ ταῦτα οἴδατε μακάριοί ἐστε ἐὰν ποιῆτε αὐτά
verses.transliteration: ei tauta oidate makarioi este ean poiēte auta
verses.study_note: 'Happy are ye if ye do them' (makarioi este ean poiēte auta — blessed are you if you do them). 'Makarios' (blessed, happy) — the same word used in the Beatitudes (Matthew 5:3-12). Knowledge without action produces guilt rather than blessing. The blessing is in the doing (James 1:25). John's Gospel, often regarded as purely contemplative, here insists that understanding must be lived out. Orthodoxy without orthopraxy is incomplete.

verses.kjv_text: I speak not of you all: I know whom I have chosen: but that the scripture may be fulfilled, He that eateth bread with me hath lifted up his heel against me.
verses.original_text: οὐ περὶ πάντων ὑμῶν λέγω ἐγὼ οἶδα τίνας ἐξελεξάμην ἀλλ᾽ ἵνα ἡ γραφὴ πληρωθῇ Ὁ τρώγων μου τὸν ἄρτον ἐπῆρεν ἐπ᾽ ἐμὲ τὴν πτέρναν αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: ou peri pantōn hymōn legō egō oida tinas exelexamēn all hina hē graphē plērōthē Ho trōgōn mou ton arton epēren ep eme tēn pternan autou
verses.study_note: 'I know whom I have chosen' (oida tinas exelexamēn — I know the ones I selected; aorist: the selection is complete). The election of the Twelve includes Judas — not accidentally but purposefully, 'that the scripture may be fulfilled.' Quoting Psalm 41:9: 'He that eateth bread with me hath lifted up his heel against me.' 'Lifted up his heel' (epēren tēn pternan — raised his heel) — a metaphor of betrayal; a trusted companion turns and kicks. David wrote of Ahithophel; the Holy Spirit intended Jesus and Judas. The betrayal is not a divine accident but a divinely anticipated and overruled event.

verses.kjv_text: Now I tell you before it come, that, when it is come to pass, ye may believe that I am he.
verses.original_text: ἀπ᾽ ἄρτι λέγω ὑμῖν πρὸ τοῦ γενέσθαι ἵνα πιστεύσητε ὅταν γένηται ὅτι ἐγώ εἰμι
verses.transliteration: ap arti legō hymin pro tou genesthai hina pisteusēte hotan genētai hoti egō eimi
verses.study_note: 'That when it is come to pass, ye may believe that I am he' (hoti egō eimi — that I AM). The foreknowledge and forewarning of the betrayal is itself a proof of divine identity. 'Egō eimi' (I AM) echoes the divine name (Exodus 3:14; Isaiah 43:10). Jesus predicts in order to strengthen faith when the prediction is fulfilled. The disciples will remember his words (16:4) and the fulfilment will confirm rather than destroy their faith.

verses.kjv_text: Verily, verily, I say unto you, He that receiveth whomsoever I send receiveth me; and he that receiveth me receiveth him that sent me.
verses.original_text: ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ὁ λαμβάνων ἄν τινα πέμψω ἐμὲ λαμβάνει ὁ δὲ ἐμὲ λαμβάνων λαμβάνει τὸν πέμψαντά με
verses.transliteration: amēn amēn legō hymin ho lambanōn an tina pempsō eme lambanei ho de eme lambanōn lambanei ton pempsanta me
verses.study_note: This principle of agency (the envoy carries the authority of the sender) is inserted here perhaps as a counterweight to the news of betrayal: despite Judas's failure, the mission of the Twelve continues. Those sent in Jesus' name carry his authority; receiving them is receiving Christ, and receiving Christ is receiving the Father. The chain of representation extends from the Father through the Son through the apostles to all who receive the message.

verses.kjv_text: When Jesus had thus said, he was troubled in spirit, and testified, and said, Verily, verily, I say unto you, that one of you shall betray me.
verses.original_text: Ταῦτα εἰπὼν ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἐταράχθη τῷ πνεύματι καὶ ἐμαρτύρησεν καὶ εἶπεν Ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι εἷς ἐξ ὑμῶν παραδώσει με
verses.transliteration: Tauta eipōn ho Iēsous etarachthē tō pneumati kai emartyrésen kai eipen Amēn amēn legō hymin hoti heis ex hymōn paradōsei me
verses.study_note: 'He was troubled in spirit' (etarachthē tō pneumati — was agitated/shaken in the spirit; the same verb as 11:33, where he was 'troubled' at Lazarus's tomb, and 12:27, where his soul was 'troubled'). Jesus is not unmoved by the betrayal. 'Testified' (emartyrésen — bore witness, made a solemn declaration). The announcement 'one of you shall betray me' is a formal testimony, a solemn statement of terrible truth. The use of 'Verily, verily' marks it as a pronouncement of the highest gravity.

verses.kjv_text: Then the disciples looked one on another, doubting of whom he spake.
verses.original_text: ἔβλεπον οὖν εἰς ἀλλήλους οἱ μαθηταί ἀπορούμενοι περὶ τίνος λέγει
verses.transliteration: eblepon oun eis allēlous hoi mathētai aporoumenoi peri tinos legei
verses.study_note: 'Doubting of whom he spake' (aporoumenoi peri tinos legei — perplexed, at a loss as to whom he was speaking). 'Aporoumenoi' (from aporeo: to be at a loss, without a way through; a + poros: no passage). The disciples are genuinely baffled — they cannot identify the traitor by reading his face or manner. Judas had concealed his treachery perfectly. Their exchanged glances reveal innocent incomprehension. (The Synoptics record each asking, 'Is it I?' — Matthew 26:22; Mark 14:19.)

verses.kjv_text: Now there was leaning on Jesus' bosom one of his disciples, whom Jesus loved.
verses.original_text: ἦν δὲ ἀνακείμενος εἷς ἐκ τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ ἐν τῷ κόλπῳ τοῦ Ἰησοῦ ὃν ἠγάπα ὁ Ἰησοῦς
verses.transliteration: ēn de anakeimenos heis ek tōn mathētōn autou en tō kolpō tou Iēsou hon ēgapa ho Iēsous
verses.study_note: 'Leaning on Jesus' bosom' (anakeimenos en tō kolpō tou Iēsou — reclining in the bosom of Jesus). At ancient banquets, diners reclined on their left side on couches arranged in a U-shape, leaning on the left elbow; the person to one's right would have their head at one's breast level. 'The disciple whom Jesus loved' (hon ēgapa ho Iēsous — whom Jesus kept loving; imperfect: ongoing, habitual love) — the Beloved Disciple, the author of this Gospel, traditionally identified as John son of Zebedee. He is introduced here for the first time in this role. The phrase 'in the bosom' (en tō kolpō) echoes 1:18: 'the only begotten Son, which is in the bosom of the Father' — the Beloved Disciple's intimacy with Jesus mirrors Jesus' intimacy with the Father.

verses.kjv_text: Simon Peter therefore beckoned to him, that he should ask who it should be of whom he spake.
verses.original_text: νεύει οὖν τούτῳ Σίμων Πέτρος πυθέσθαι τίς ἂν εἴη περὶ οὗ λέγει
verses.transliteration: neuei oun toutō Simōn Petros pythesthai tis an eiē peri hou legei
verses.study_note: 'Beckoned' (neuei — nodded, gestured; from neuō: to nod). Peter signals across the table to the Beloved Disciple to ask on his behalf. This detail reflects the seating: the Beloved Disciple is next to Jesus (at his right), Peter is further away. Peter's action shows his characteristic directness: he wants to know who the traitor is. The inquiry proceeds through the one closest to Jesus — a pattern: access to truth comes through proximity to Christ.

verses.kjv_text: He then lying on Jesus' breast saith unto him, Lord, who is it?
verses.original_text: ἀναπεσὼν δὲ ἐκεῖνος οὕτως ἐπὶ τὸ στῆθος τοῦ Ἰησοῦ λέγει αὐτῷ Κύριε τίς ἐστιν
verses.transliteration: anapesōn de ekeinos houtōs epi to stēthos tou Iēsou legei autō Kyrie tis estin
verses.study_note: 'Lying on Jesus' breast' (anapesōn epi to stēthos tou Iēsou — having leaned back upon the chest of Jesus). The physical intimacy is striking. 'Lord, who is it?' (Kyrie, tis estin?) — a simple, urgent question. The question is asked privately (or at least quietly); the answer is given privately. The rest of the disciples do not hear or understand (v.28).

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered, He it is, to whom I shall give a sop, when I have dipped it. And when he had dipped the sop, he gave it to Judas Iscariot, the son of Simon.
verses.original_text: ἀποκρίνεται ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ἐκεῖνός ἐστιν ᾧ ἐγὼ βάψω τὸ ψωμίον καὶ δώσω αὐτῷ βάψας οὖν τὸ ψωμίον λαμβάνει καὶ δίδωσιν Ἰούδᾳ Σίμωνος Ἰσκαριώτῃ
verses.transliteration: apokrinetai ho Iēsous Ekeinos estin hō egō bapsō to psōmion kai dōsō autō bapsas oun to psōmion lambanei kai didōsin Iouda Simōnos Iskariōtē
verses.study_note: 'A sop' (to psōmion — a morsel of bread, a small piece; diminutive of psōmos). The host at a banquet would dip a piece of bread in sauce and hand it to a guest as a mark of special honour. Jesus gives the sop to Judas — an act of extraordinary, final grace. Even in the moment of identification, the gesture is one of hospitality and honour. This is love to 'the uttermost' — love extended to the betrayer himself. Judas receives the sop and with it, his final opportunity.

verses.kjv_text: And after the sop Satan entered into him. Then said Jesus unto him, That thou doest, do quickly.
verses.original_text: καὶ μετὰ τὸ ψωμίον τότε εἰσῆλθεν εἰς ἐκεῖνον ὁ Σατανᾶς λέγει οὖν αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ὃ ποιεῖς ποίησον τάχιον
verses.transliteration: kai meta to psōmion tote eisēlthen eis ekeinon ho Satanas legei oun autō ho Iēsous Ho poieis poiēson tachion
verses.study_note: 'After the sop Satan entered into him' (meta to psōmion tote eisēlthen eis ekeinon ho Satanas) — the final, full Satanic possession occurs at this moment. The sop received without repentance marks the point of no return for Judas. Grace refused becomes the occasion of hardening. 'That thou doest, do quickly' (Ho poieis, poiēson tachion — what you are doing, do it more quickly). Jesus commands the betrayal — not as approval but as sovereign authority. He is not a victim carried away by events but the master who permits and times his own arrest. Even the betrayal is under his control.

verses.kjv_text: Now no man at the table knew for what intent he spake this unto him.
verses.original_text: τοῦτο δὲ οὐδεὶς ἔγνω τῶν ἀνακειμένων πρὸς τί εἶπεν αὐτῷ
verses.transliteration: touto de oudeis egnō tōn anakeimenōn pros ti eipen autō
verses.study_note: The exchange between Jesus and Judas is not understood by the other disciples. Even the Beloved Disciple (who knew the sign) apparently does not communicate this to the others, or the significance is not grasped. The darkness that is Judas's spiritual state is also an epistemic darkness: no one sees what is happening. The moment of history's greatest treachery is invisible to those present.

verses.kjv_text: For some of them thought, because Judas had the bag, that Jesus had said unto him, Buy those things that we have need of against the feast; or, that he should give something to the poor.
verses.original_text: τινὲς γὰρ ἐδόκουν ἐπεὶ τὸ γλωσσόκομον εἶχεν Ἰούδας ὅτι λέγει αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ἀγόρασον ὧν χρείαν ἔχομεν εἰς τὴν ἑορτήν ἢ τοῖς πτωχοῖς ἵνα τι δῷ
verses.transliteration: tines gar edokoun epei to glōssokomon eichen Ioudas hoti legei autō ho Iēsous Agorazon hōn chreian echomen eis tēn heortēn ē tois ptōchois hina ti dō
verses.study_note: The disciples rationalise the departure of Judas in the most innocent terms: he is on an errand. 'Buy those things' or 'give to the poor' — the very justifications Judas himself had used in 12:5 ('give to the poor') now echo ironically. The trusted treasurer is trusted to the last — even by those who should suspect. Evil often works under the cover of the plausible.

verses.kjv_text: He then having received the sop went immediately out: and it was night.
verses.original_text: λαβὼν οὖν τὸ ψωμίον ἐκεῖνος ἐξῆλθεν εὐθύς ἦν δὲ νύξ
verses.transliteration: labōn oun to psōmion ekeinos exēlthen euthys ēn de nyx
verses.study_note: 'He then having received the sop went immediately out' (exēlthen euthys — went out immediately; immediately without hesitation). 'And it was night' (ēn de nyx) — three words in Greek, one of the most evocative in the entire Gospel. Night is the realm of Judas, of the prince of this world, of those who will not come to the light (3:19-20). In John's symbolic world, 'night' is not merely a time of day but a spiritual state. Judas steps out of the light of Christ's presence into the darkness he has chosen.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore, when he was gone out, Jesus said, Now is the Son of man glorified, and God is glorified in him.
verses.original_text: ὅτε οὖν ἐξῆλθεν λέγει Ἰησοῦς Νῦν ἐδοξάσθη ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου καὶ ὁ θεὸς ἐδοξάσθη ἐν αὐτῷ
verses.transliteration: hote oun exēlthen legei Iēsous Nyn edoxasthē ho huios tou anthrōpou kai ho theos edoxasthē en autō
verses.study_note: 'Now is the Son of man glorified' (nyn edoxasthē — now has been glorified; aorist, speaking of the future passion as already accomplished from the divine perspective). The departure of Judas into the night triggers Jesus' farewell discourse. With the betrayer gone, he speaks freely to the Eleven. 'God is glorified in him' — the mutual glorification of Father and Son through the Passion is the culmination of the Gospel's theme. Glory is not in triumph over enemies but in obedient self-giving love.

verses.kjv_text: If God be glorified in him, God shall also glorify him in himself, and shall straightway glorify him.
verses.original_text: εἰ ὁ θεὸς ἐδοξάσθη ἐν αὐτῷ καὶ ὁ θεὸς δοξάσει αὐτὸν ἐν αὐτῷ καὶ εὐθὺς δοξάσει αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: ei ho theos edoxasthē en autō kai ho theos doxasei auton en autō kai euthys doxasei auton
verses.study_note: 'God shall also glorify him in himself' (ho theos doxasei auton en autō — God will glorify him in himself; the Father glorifies the Son by the resurrection, ascension, and Pentecost). 'Straightway' (euthys — immediately, without delay). The Father's glorification of the Son follows immediately upon the Son's glorification of the Father through the cross. There is no long delay between crucifixion and resurrection — three days is 'straightway' in God's economy.

verses.kjv_text: Little children, yet a little while I am with you. Ye shall seek me: and as I said unto the Jews, Whither I go, ye cannot come; so now I say to you.
verses.original_text: τεκνία ἔτι μικρὸν μεθ᾽ ὑμῶν εἰμι ζητήσετέ με καὶ καθὼς εἶπον τοῖς Ἰουδαίοις ὅτι ὅπου ἐγὼ ὑπάγω ὑμεῖς οὐ δύνασθε ἐλθεῖν καὶ ὑμῖν λέγω ἄρτι
verses.transliteration: teknia eti mikron meth hymōn eimi zētēsete me kai kathōs eipon tois Ioudaiois hoti hopou egō hypagō hymeis ou dynasthe elthein kai hymin legō arti
verses.study_note: 'Little children' (teknia — dear little children; a tender diminutive; in John only here; in 1 John seven times). This is the only time in the Fourth Gospel that Jesus uses this term of address to the disciples — it marks a new intimacy now that Judas is gone. 'Yet a little while I am with you' — the Passion is imminent; hours remain. 'Ye cannot come' — temporarily, not permanently; for now they cannot follow him through death, but they will (13:36; 14:3; 17:24).

verses.kjv_text: A new commandment I give unto you, That ye love one another; as I have loved you, that ye also love one another.
verses.original_text: ἐντολὴν καινὴν δίδωμι ὑμῖν ἵνα ἀγαπᾶτε ἀλλήλους καθὼς ἠγάπησα ὑμᾶς ἵνα καὶ ὑμεῖς ἀγαπᾶτε ἀλλήλους
verses.transliteration: entolēn kainēn didōmi hymin hina agapate allēlous kathōs ēgapēsa hymas hina kai hymeis agapate allēlous
verses.study_note: 'A new commandment' (entolēn kainēn — a new commandment; kainēn: new in quality, not merely in time; kainos vs. neos). The command to love is not new in itself — Leviticus 19:18 commands love of neighbour. The newness is in the standard: 'as I have loved you' (kathōs ēgapēsa hymas — just as I loved you; the love of the cross is the measure). No previous standard had been set this high. And the scope: 'one another' — the covenant community, those united in Christ. This love is distinctively Christian: cross-shaped, self-giving, sacrificial.

verses.kjv_text: By this shall all men know that ye are my disciples, if ye have love one to another.
verses.original_text: ἐν τούτῳ γνώσονται πάντες ὅτι ἐμοὶ μαθηταί ἐστε ἐὰν ἀγάπην ἔχητε ἐν ἀλλήλοις
verses.transliteration: en toutō gnōsontai pantes hoti emoi mathētai este ean agapēn echēte en allēlois
verses.study_note: 'By this shall all men know' (en toutō gnōsontai pantes — by this all people will know). The distinguishing mark of discipleship, the badge of Christianity, is not orthodoxy of belief alone, not correct ritual, not ecclesiastical belonging, but agapē love in the community. Tertullian (c.200 AD) reports pagans observing: 'See how they love one another!' This was the great witness of the early church — a community of mutual love crossing ethnic, social, and economic barriers in the Roman world.

verses.kjv_text: Simon Peter said unto him, Lord, whither goest thou? Jesus answered him, Whither I go, thou canst not follow me now; but thou shalt follow me afterwards.
verses.original_text: Λέγει αὐτῷ Σίμων Πέτρος Κύριε ποῦ ὑπάγεις ἀπεκρίθη αὐτῷ Ἰησοῦς Ὅπου ὑπάγω οὐ δύνασαί μοι νῦν ἀκολουθῆσαι ἀκολουθήσεις δὲ ὕστερον
verses.transliteration: Legei autō Simōn Petros Kyrie pou hypageis apekrithē autō Iēsous Hopou hypagō ou dynasai moi nyn akolouthēsai akolouthēseis de hysteron
verses.study_note: 'Lord, whither goest thou?' Peter, characteristically, focuses on the departure rather than the love commandment. He cannot bear the thought of Jesus leaving. 'Thou canst not follow me now' (ou dynasai moi nyn akolouthēsai — you are not able to follow me now). The 'now' is important: the prohibition is temporary. 'Thou shalt follow me afterwards' (akolouthēseis de hysteron — you will follow later). Jesus is speaking of his death; Peter will follow him in martyrdom — traditionally crucifixion, upside-down (21:18-19). Peter's road to the cross runs through denial and restoration first.

verses.kjv_text: Peter said unto him, Lord, why cannot I follow thee now? I will lay down my life for thy sake.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ ὁ Πέτρος Κύριε διὰ τί οὐ δύναμαί σοι ἀκολουθῆσαι ἄρτι τὴν ψυχήν μου ὑπὲρ σοῦ θήσω
verses.transliteration: legei autō ho Petros Kyrie dia ti ou dynamai soi akolouthēsai arti tēn psychēn mou hyper sou thēsō
verses.study_note: 'I will lay down my life for thy sake' (tēn psychēn mou hyper sou thēsō — I will lay down my soul/life on your behalf). Peter uses the exact language Jesus has used in 10:11,17,18 for the Good Shepherd laying down his life. He means it sincerely — Peter is genuinely courageous (he will draw his sword in Gethsemane, 18:10). But his courage is natural courage, unaided by grace; it will fail. The self-confidence is real; the self-knowledge is absent. True courage in discipleship is the gift of the Spirit, not the achievement of the will (Acts 4:13).

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered him, Wilt thou lay down thy life for my sake? Verily, verily, I say unto thee, The cock shall not crow, till thou hast denied me thrice.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς Τὴν ψυχήν σου ὑπὲρ ἐμοῦ θήσεις ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω σοι οὐ μὴ ἀλέκτωρ φωνήσῃ ἕως οὗ ἀρνήσῃ με τρίς
verses.transliteration: apekrithē autō ho Iēsous Tēn psychēn sou hyper emou thēseis amēn amēn legō soi ou mē alektōr phōnēsē heōs hou arnēsē me tris
verses.study_note: 'Wilt thou lay down thy life for my sake?' The question is not a rebuke but a gentle, knowing challenge. Jesus accepts the intention and predicts the failure. 'The cock shall not crow, till thou hast denied me thrice' (ou mē alektōr phōnēsē heōs hou arnēsē me tris) — 'alektōr' (cockerel; the crowing between midnight and 3am, or at dawn). 'Arnēsē' (you will deny; from arneomai — to disown, disavow). The prediction is precise: three denials, one crowing, one night. It will be fulfilled exactly in 18:15-27. But Jesus' foreknowledge of the failure does not mean he has abandoned Peter — 21:15-19 shows the restoration.

1.original_word: πρὸ δὲ τῆς ἑορτῆς τοῦ πάσχα
1.transliteration: pro de tēs heortēs tou pascha
1.meaning: Before the feast of the Passover — John's signal that what follows occurs on the eve of the great festival. He aligns Jesus' death with the slaughter of the paschal lambs (19:14, 31); the foot-washing opens the Passover discourse.
His hour was come — perfect tense: has arrived and abides. The long-anticipated 'hour' of passion and glory (2:4; 7:30; 8:20; 12:23) has dawned.
He loved them unto the end — telos = both 'to the final limit' and 'to perfection/completeness'. Double meaning: to the end of his earthly life, and to the uttermost depth of love.

2.meaning: Devil having now put — perfect participle: the devil has lodged the betrayal-thought in Judas's heart and it abides. Satan and Judas's own avarice collaborate; human freedom and demonic influence are not mutually exclusive.

3.original_word: πάντα δέδωκεν αὐτῷ ὁ πατὴρ εἰς τὰς χεῖρας
3.transliteration: panta dedōken autō ho patēr eis tas cheiras
3.meaning: The Father had given all things into his hands — perfect: the plenary transfer abides. John stages the foot-washing as an act of the Universal Lord: awareness of total sovereignty is the very backdrop of humility.

4.meaning: Laid aside his garments — tithēmi = to lay down. The same verb Jesus uses of laying down his life (10:11, 15, 17-18). The foot-washing is a prophetic sign of the greater laying-aside about to happen at the cross.
A towel — Latin loanword (linteum); the attire of the lowest household slave. Jesus dons servant's uniform; the Master becomes diakonos in enacted parable.

5.meaning: To wash the feet — niptō = to wash a body part (vs. louō, whole body bath). Gentile slaves washed feet; Jewish slaves were exempt from this task as too demeaning. Jesus crosses the line.

Wipe them with the towel — ekmassō = to wipe off (also 11:2; 12:3 of Mary). A literary echo: Mary wiped Jesus' feet with her hair; Jesus wipes disciples' feet with the slave's towel. Love reciprocates love.

7.original_word: οὐκ οἶδας ἄρτι... γνώσῃ δὲ μετὰ ταῦτα
7.transliteration: ouk oidas arti... gnōsē de meta tauta
7.meaning: Thou knowest not now but shalt know hereafter — present ignorance / future comprehension. The typical Johannine pattern: meaning revealed post-Resurrection (2:22; 12:16; 16:4).

8.meaning: Thou hast no part with me — meros = share, portion (used of inheritance). To refuse Christ's cleansing is to forfeit every share in him; salvation requires the humiliating gift of being served.

10.meaning: Bathed... wash — two distinct verbs contrasted. Those bathed (leloumenos, perfect passive — the one decisive bath of regeneration) need only ongoing foot-washing (post-conversion cleansing), not re-bathing. Distinction between justification and sanctification in germinal form.
Clean, but not all — Judas present, already inwardly corrupted. Outward association with Jesus is no substitute for inward cleansing.

13.meaning: Master and Lord — didaskalos (Rabbi/Teacher) + kyrios (Lord, owner). Jesus accepts both titles, then shows what they mean: the true Master serves his slaves.

14.meaning: Ye ought — opheilō = to owe as a debt. Moral obligation derives from the Master's example: if the Greater has stooped, the lesser has no ground to refuse.

15.meaning: An example — hypodeigma = pattern, copy, paradigm to be reproduced. Not merely inspiration but imitative template. Foot-washing is prescriptive in spirit, not simply anecdotal.

16.meaning: Verily, verily — the double amēn, unique to John (25x). Solemn oath formula introducing divine pronouncement; intensified attention required.

18.original_word: ὁ τρώγων μετ᾽ ἐμοῦ τὸν ἄρτον
18.transliteration: ho trōgōn met' emou ton arton
18.meaning: He that eateth bread with me — direct quotation of Ps 41:9 LXX (with variation). trōgō = to gnaw, chew (vivid, corporeal). Table fellowship was covenantal; betrayal by a table-companion is the deepest treachery.
Lifted up his heel against me — pterna = heel. Idiom of violent rejection. The verse echoes Gen 3:15 (the serpent's bruising of the heel); Judas is the serpent's instrument.
I know whom I have chosen — perfect knowledge, aorist election. Jesus' choice of the Twelve, Judas included, was not miscalculation. Judas's betrayal fulfils, not frustrates, divine purpose.

20.meaning: He that receiveth whomsoever I send — the apostolic chain of reception: reject the apostle and you reject Jesus and the Father. Antidote to the coming scandal of Judas: one false apostle does not discredit the institution.

21.meaning: Troubled in spirit — aorist passive. The third of Jesus' inward tarassō disturbances in John (11:33 at Lazarus's tomb; 12:27 at the hour's approach; here at treachery in the inner circle). Christ's humanity feels every wound.

23.meaning: In Jesus' bosom — kolpos = the fold of the garment over the chest; the place of intimacy. Echoes 1:18 (the Son in the Father's bosom). The beloved disciple's position mirrors the Son's filial intimacy.

26.meaning: A sop — diminutive of psōmos, a morsel. Dipping a sop and handing it to a guest was a gesture of honour. Jesus extends dignity to his betrayer to the very last instant; Judas spurns grace.

27.meaning: Satan entered into him — aorist: at the moment Judas received the sop and rejected the gesture, the adversary took full possession. Demonic indwelling follows the final hardening against grace.
What thou doest, do quickly — tachion = more quickly. Jesus commands his own betrayal; he is not passively overtaken by events but actively orchestrates his hour.

29.meaning: The bag — money-box (12:6). The disciples assume Judas is on benevolence business; the depth of his deceit is that he has never raised suspicion.

30.meaning: It was night — John's spare, loaded narrative note. Literal twilight; theological darkness. Judas goes out from the light of the world into his chosen dark (cf. 3:19; 9:4; 11:10).

31.original_word: νῦν ἐδοξάσθη ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου
31.transliteration: nyn edoxasthē ho huios tou anthrōpou
31.meaning: Now is the Son of man glorified — aorist: the glorification is begun the moment the betrayer exits to set the cross in motion. John's doxa = the Cross itself is the throne of glory.

32.original_word: εἰ ὁ Θεὸς ἐδοξάσθη ἐν αὐτῷ
32.transliteration: ei ho Theos edoxasthē en autō
32.meaning: God shall glorify him in himself — reciprocal glorification between Father and Son; and the Father will glorify the Son 'straightway' (euthys) — the resurrection-exaltation follows immediately on the cross.

33.meaning: Little children — diminutive of teknon, a term of tender affection. First use in John; standard in 1 John. The tone shifts from public discourse to fatherly farewell.

34.meaning: A new commandment — kainos = new in quality (not neos, merely recent in time). The love-command is 'new' in that its norm is now 'as I have loved you' (the cross-pattern), and it is the new-covenant law written on the heart.
As I have loved you — kathōs = in the same manner and on the same basis. The measure of Christian love is the self-giving of Christ himself, not common human affection.

35.meaning: By this shall all men know — love within the community is the apologetic to the world. The world does not first read our doctrine; it reads our mutual love.

36.meaning: Whither goest thou? — Peter's question (also Thomas in 14:5). hypagō = to depart, withdraw. The disciples cannot yet grasp the cross-destination; Jesus must cushion the revelation.

37.original_word: τὴν ψυχήν μου ὑπὲρ σοῦ θήσω
37.transliteration: tēn psychēn mou hyper sou thēsō
37.meaning: Lay down my life for thee — Peter unconsciously borrows Jesus' own shepherd-language (10:11, 15). Fine intention, insufficient grace; Peter will discover the gap tonight.

38.meaning: The cock — alektōr = rooster. In Roman reckoning the alektorophōnia (cock-crow) named the third night watch (12 midnight – 3 a.m.). The prediction has a precise timetable; Peter's fall is already booked.
Deny me thrice — arneomai = to disown, repudiate. Three denials to match three affirmations of love in 21:15-17. The narrative architecture is already plotted.

meta.overview: John 14 is one of the most beloved chapters in the Bible — the opening of the Upper Room Discourse (chapters 14-16) that Jesus delivers on the last night of his earthly life. It is spoken to eleven frightened, confused disciples who face the imminent departure of their Lord. The chapter opens with the great pastoral command: 'Let not your heart be troubled' (v.1) — a command grounded in faith: 'ye believe in God, believe also in me.' Jesus speaks of going to prepare a place for the disciples in the Father's house, and promises to return and receive them (vv.1-3). Thomas's question — 'Lord, we know not whither thou goest; and how can we know the way?' — draws the most famous reply in John: 'I am the way, the truth, and the life: no man cometh unto the Father, but by me' (v.6). Philip's request — 'shew us the Father, and it sufficeth us' — receives the profound response: 'He that hath seen me hath seen the Father' (v.9). Jesus promises that the disciples will do 'greater works' because he goes to the Father (v.12). He commits to answering prayer in his name (vv.13-14). He introduces the Paraclete — the Holy Spirit, the Comforter, the Spirit of truth — who will be 'another Comforter' (allon Paraklēton — another of the same kind) to be with them forever (vv.15-17). He promises the mutual indwelling of Father, Son, and believer (vv.18-23). Judas (not Iscariot) asks why Jesus manifests himself to the disciples and not to the world — Jesus replies that love and obedience are the condition of divine manifestation (vv.22-24). The chapter closes with the gift of peace — 'my peace I give unto you: not as the world giveth, give I unto you' (v.27) — and the final call not to be troubled or afraid, because Jesus goes to the Father and the victory is already won (vv.28-31).
meta.theme: I Am the Way — The Father Revealed in the Son by the Spirit
meta.keyWordMeaning: Advocate, Helper, Comforter, Counsellor — from para (beside) + kaleō (to call). One called alongside to help, an advocate in court, a counsellor in time of need. The word appears 5 times in John (14:16,26; 15:26; 16:7 — for the Spirit; and 1 John 2:1 — for Christ as our advocate with the Father). Jesus calls the Spirit 'another Comforter' (allon Paraklēton — another of the same kind), implying that Jesus himself is the first Paraclete. The Spirit continues Christ's ministry: teaching, reminding, testifying, convicting, guiding. He is not an impersonal force but a person who can be grieved (Ephesians 4:30), who speaks, who prays, who wills (1 Corinthians 12:11). The promise of the Paraclete is the answer to the disciples' anxiety about Jesus's departure.
outline: The Father's House (vv.1-4): Let not your heart be troubled ye believe in God believe also in me. In my Father's house are many mansions if it were not so I would have told you. I go to prepare a place for you. And if I go and prepare a place for you I will come again and receive you unto myself that where I am there ye may be also. And whither I go ye know and the way ye know

I Am the Way, the Truth, and the Life (vv.5-7): Thomas saith unto him Lord we know not whither thou goest and how can we know the way? Jesus saith unto him I am the way the truth and the life no man cometh unto the Father but by me. If ye had known me ye should have known my Father also and from henceforth ye know him and have seen him

Philip's Request (vv.8-14): Philip saith unto him Lord shew us the Father and it sufficeth us. Jesus saith unto him Have I been so long time with you and yet hast thou not known me Philip? he that hath seen me hath seen the Father and how sayest thou then Shew us the Father? Believest thou not that I am in the Father and the Father in me? the words that I speak unto you I speak not of myself but the Father that dwelleth in me he doeth the works. Believe me that I am in the Father and the Father in me or else believe me for the very works' sake. Verily verily I say unto you He that believeth on me the works that I do shall he do also and greater works than these shall he do because I go unto my Father. And whatsoever ye shall ask in my name that will I do that the Father may be glorified in the Son. If ye shall ask any thing in my name I will do it

The Promise of the Paraclete (vv.15-17): If ye love me keep my commandments. And I will pray the Father and he shall give you another Comforter that he may abide with you for ever even the Spirit of truth whom the world cannot receive because it seeth him not neither knoweth him but ye know him for he dwelleth with you and shall be in you

The Mutual Indwelling (vv.18-24): I will not leave you comfortless I will come to you. Yet a little while and the world seeth me no more but ye see me because I live ye shall live also. At that day ye shall know that I am in my Father and ye in me and I in you. He that hath my commandments and keepeth them he it is that loveth me and he that loveth me shall be loved of my Father and I will love him and will manifest myself to him. Judas saith unto him not Iscariot Lord how is it that thou wilt manifest thyself unto us and not unto the world? Jesus answered and said unto him If a man love me he will keep my words and my Father will love him and we will come unto him and make our abode with him

Peace and the Return (vv.25-31): These things have I spoken unto you being yet present with you. But the Comforter which is the Holy Ghost whom the Father will send in my name he shall teach you all things and bring all things to your remembrance whatsoever I have said unto you. Peace I leave with you my peace I give unto you not as the world giveth give I unto you. Let not your heart be troubled neither let it be afraid. Ye have heard how I said unto you I go away and come again unto you. If ye loved me ye would rejoice because I said I go unto the Father for my Father is greater than I. And now I have told you before it come to pass that when it is come to pass ye might believe. Hereafter I will not talk much with you for the prince of this world cometh and hath nothing in me. But that the world may know that I love the Father and as the Father gave me commandment even so I do Arise let us go hence

verses.kjv_text: Let not your heart be troubled: ye believe in God, believe also in me.
verses.original_text: Μὴ ταρασσέσθω ὑμῶν ἡ καρδία πιστεύετε εἰς τὸν θεὸν καὶ εἰς ἐμὲ πιστεύετε
verses.transliteration: Mē tarassesthō hymōn hē kardia pisteuete eis ton theon kai eis eme pisteuete
verses.study_note: 'Let not your heart be troubled' (mē tarassesthō hymōn hē kardia — do not let your heart be disturbed/shaken; tarassō: to agitate, throw into confusion). The disciples are in anguish: Jesus has announced his departure (13:33), his death, and Peter's denial. The command is grounded in faith — 'ye believe in God, believe also in me' (pisteuete eis ton theon kai eis eme pisteuete). Both 'believe's may be indicative ('you believe in God, you believe also in me') or both imperatives, or the first indicative and the second imperative. In any reading, faith in God and faith in Christ are co-extensive. Anxiety is conquered not by circumstances but by trust in the one who controls circumstances.

verses.kjv_text: In my Father's house are many mansions: if it were not so, I would have told you. I go to prepare a place for you.
verses.original_text: ἐν τῇ οἰκίᾳ τοῦ πατρός μου μοναὶ πολλαί εἰσιν εἰ δὲ μή εἶπον ἂν ὑμῖν ὅτι πορεύομαι ἑτοιμάσαι τόπον ὑμῖν
verses.transliteration: en tē oikia tou patros mou monai pollai eisin ei de mē eipon an hymin hoti poreuomai hetoimasai topon hymin
verses.study_note: 'In my Father's house are many mansions' (en tē oikia tou patros mou monai pollai eisin — in my Father's house there are many dwelling places). 'Monai' (plural of monē — from menō: to abide, remain; a staying-place, a dwelling). The KJV's 'mansions' (from Latin mansiones) can mislead: it is not palatial grandeur but permanent, settled dwelling — a home, not a hotel. 'Many' (pollai — many, ample room for all). 'I go to prepare a place for you' (poreuomai hetoimasai topon hymin) — Jesus' departure is purposeful: he goes as the forerunner (Hebrews 6:20) to prepare the dwelling for those who follow.

verses.kjv_text: And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again, and receive you unto myself; that where I am, there ye may be also.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐὰν πορευθῶ καὶ ἑτοιμάσω τόπον ὑμῖν πάλιν ἔρχομαι καὶ παραλήμψομαι ὑμᾶς πρὸς ἐμαυτόν ἵνα ὅπου εἰμὶ ἐγὼ καὶ ὑμεῖς ἦτε
verses.transliteration: kai ean poreuthō kai hetoimasō topon hymin palin erchomai kai paralēmpsomai hymas pros emauton hina hopou eimi egō kai hymeis ēte
verses.study_note: 'I will come again' (palin erchomai — I am coming again; the present tense expresses the certainty of the future event). This is one of the clearest promises of the Second Coming in John (cf. 21:22-23; Acts 1:11). 'Receive you unto myself' (paralēmpsomai hymas pros emauton — will take you alongside to myself; paralambano: to take alongside, receive to oneself). 'That where I am, there ye may be also' — the goal of salvation is not just escape from hell but eternal fellowship with Christ in the Father's presence.

verses.kjv_text: And whither I go ye know, and the way ye know.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὅπου ἐγὼ ὑπάγω οἴδατε τὴν ὁδὸν οἴδατε
verses.transliteration: kai hopou egō hypagō oidate tēn hodon oidate
verses.study_note: Jesus assumes the disciples know both the destination (the Father) and the way (himself). But Thomas will immediately reveal they do not understand. Jesus's statement is not an oversight — it is a prompt to draw out the question that leads to the great 'I AM the way' declaration.

verses.kjv_text: Thomas saith unto him, Lord, we know not whither thou goest; and how can we know the way?
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ Θωμᾶς Κύριε οὐκ οἴδαμεν ποῦ ὑπάγεις καὶ πῶς δυνάμεθα τὴν ὁδὸν εἰδέναι
verses.transliteration: legei autō Thōmas Kyrie ouk oidamen pou hypageis kai pōs dynametha tēn hodon eidenai
verses.study_note: Thomas (Thōmas — Aramaic: twin; his Greek name is Didymus, 11:16; 20:24; 21:2) is consistently the disciple who raises honest, searching doubts (11:16; 20:24-25). His question is literal and confused: 'we do not know where you are going, so how can we know the way?' It is precisely this honest incomprehension that draws from Jesus the most explicit statement of his exclusive mediatorship. Good questions receive great answers.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus saith unto him, I am the way, the truth, and the life: no man cometh unto the Father, but by me.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ ὁδὸς καὶ ἡ ἀλήθεια καὶ ἡ ζωή οὐδεὶς ἔρχεται πρὸς τὸν πατέρα εἰ μὴ δι᾽ ἐμοῦ
verses.transliteration: legei autō ho Iēsous Egō eimi hē hodos kai hē alētheia kai hē zōē oudeis erchetai pros ton patera ei mē di emou
verses.study_note: 'I am the way, the truth, and the life' (Egō eimi hē hodos kai hē alētheia kai hē zōē) — the sixth of the seven great 'I AM' declarations in John (6:35; 8:12; 10:9; 10:11; 11:25; 14:6; 15:1). All three nouns have the definite article: not 'a' way, 'a' truth, 'a' life, but THE way, THE truth, THE life — exclusive and definitive. The three terms are not three separate things but one threefold reality: Jesus is the way because he is the truth; he is the truth because he is the life; he is the life because he is the way. 'No man cometh unto the Father, but by me' (oudeis erchetai pros ton patera ei mē di emou) — the most explicit statement of exclusive salvation in the New Testament. Not arrogance but ontological necessity: only the Son can bring us to the Father because only the Son knows the Father (v.7; Matthew 11:27).

verses.kjv_text: If ye had known me, ye should have known my Father also: and from henceforth ye know him, and have seen him.
verses.original_text: εἰ ἐγνώκατέ με καὶ τὸν πατέρα μου γνώσεσθε καὶ ἀπ᾽ ἄρτι γινώσκετε αὐτὸν καὶ ἑωράκατε αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: ei egnōkate me kai ton patera mou gnōsesthe kai ap arti ginōskete auton kai heōrakate auton
verses.study_note: 'If ye had known me, ye should have known my Father also' — the knowledge of Christ is the knowledge of God. The disciples have been with Jesus for three years; they have seen the Father through him, though they have not realised it. 'From henceforth ye know him, and have seen him' (ap arti ginōskete auton kai heōrakate auton — from now on you know him and have seen him). The disclosure is now complete: the departure of Jesus and the coming of the Spirit will make this knowledge conscious and articulate.

verses.kjv_text: Philip saith unto him, Lord, shew us the Father, and it sufficeth us.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ Φίλιππος Κύριε δεῖξον ἡμῖν τὸν πατέρα καὶ ἀρκεῖ ἡμῖν
verses.transliteration: legei autō Philippos Kyrie deixon hēmin ton patera kai arkei hēmin
verses.study_note: Philip (like Thomas) raises an earnest question: 'Shew us the Father, and it sufficeth us' (deixon hēmin ton patera kai arkei hēmin — show us the Father, and this is enough for us). Philip wants a theophany — a direct vision of God like Moses at Sinai or Isaiah in the temple. His request is not unreasonable; it is simply that he does not recognise what has already been given. He has had three years of theophany — the Father has been visible in the Son throughout.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus saith unto him, Have I been so long time with you, and yet hast thou not known me, Philip? he that hath seen me hath seen the Father; and how sayest thou then, Shew us the Father?
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς Τοσοῦτον χρόνον μεθ᾽ ὑμῶν εἰμι καὶ οὐκ ἔγνωκάς με Φίλιππε ὁ ἑωρακὼς ἐμὲ ἑώρακεν τὸν πατέρα καὶ πῶς σὺ λέγεις Δεῖξον ἡμῖν τὸν πατέρα
verses.transliteration: legei autō ho Iēsous Tosouton chronon meth hymōn eimi kai ouk egnōkas me Philippe ho heōrakōs eme heōraken ton patera kai pōs sy legeis Deixon hēmin ton patera
verses.study_note: 'He that hath seen me hath seen the Father' (ho heōrakōs eme heōraken ton patera — the one who has seen me has seen the Father; perfect tenses: the seeing has occurred and the result stands). This is the summit of Johannine Christology: Jesus is not merely a messenger from the Father or a window through which the Father can be partly glimpsed; he is the full and perfect image of the Father (Colossians 1:15; Hebrews 1:3). To have seen Jesus is to have seen God. The incarnation is the definitive theophany.

verses.kjv_text: Believest thou not that I am in the Father, and the Father in me? the words that I speak unto you I speak not of myself: but the Father that dwelleth in me, he doeth the works.
verses.original_text: οὐ πιστεύεις ὅτι ἐγὼ ἐν τῷ πατρὶ καὶ ὁ πατὴρ ἐν ἐμοί ἐστιν τὰ ῥήματα ἃ ἐγὼ λαλῶ ὑμῖν ἀπ᾽ ἐμαυτοῦ οὐ λαλῶ ὁ δὲ πατὴρ ἐν ἐμοὶ μένων αὐτὸς ποιεῖ τὰ ἔργα
verses.transliteration: ou pisteueis hoti egō en tō patri kai ho patēr en emoi estin ta rhēmata ha egō lalō hymin ap emautou ou lalō ho de patēr en emoi menōn autos poiei ta erga
verses.study_note: 'I am in the Father, and the Father in me' (egō en tō patri kai ho patēr en emoi) — mutual indwelling, perichoresis (the theological term for the mutual interpenetration of the Trinity). This is not identity (the Father and Son are distinct persons) but perfect union of will, knowledge, and being. 'The Father that dwelleth in me, he doeth the works' (ho patēr en emoi menōn autos poiei ta erga) — the miracles of Jesus are the Father's works performed through the Son. The Son acts, the Father acts in him; they are inseparable in operation.

verses.kjv_text: Believe me that I am in the Father, and the Father in me: or else believe me for the very works' sake.
verses.original_text: πιστεύετέ μοι ὅτι ἐγὼ ἐν τῷ πατρὶ καὶ ὁ πατὴρ ἐν ἐμοί εἰ δὲ μή διὰ τὰ ἔργα αὐτὰ πιστεύετε
verses.transliteration: pisteuete moi hoti egō en tō patri kai ho patēr en emoi ei de mē dia ta erga auta pisteuete
verses.study_note: 'Believe me for the very works' sake' (dia ta erga auta pisteuete — believe because of the works themselves). Jesus offers two grounds for faith: his word and his works. Ideally faith accepts the word of Christ as sufficient; if not, the miracles provide secondary, visible evidence. The argument mirrors 5:36: 'the works which the Father hath given me to finish, the same works that I do, bear witness of me.' Faith based on miracles is accepted, though not as highly commended as faith based on the word alone (20:29).

verses.kjv_text: Verily, verily, I say unto you, He that believeth on me, the works that I do shall he do also; and greater works than these shall he do; because I go unto my Father.
verses.original_text: ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ὁ πιστεύων εἰς ἐμὲ τὰ ἔργα ἃ ἐγὼ ποιῶ κἀκεῖνος ποιήσει καὶ μείζονα τούτων ποιήσει ὅτι ἐγὼ πρὸς τὸν πατέρα πορεύομαι
verses.transliteration: amēn amēn legō hymin ho pisteuōn eis eme ta erga ha egō poiō kakeinos poiēsei kai meizona toutōn poiēsei hoti egō pros ton patera poreuomai
verses.study_note: 'Greater works than these shall he do' (meizona toutōn poiēsei — greater things than these he will do). The 'greater works' are not superior miracles (as if healings better than Jesus's) but greater in scope and scale: the mission of the church through the Spirit would reach the whole world (Acts 1:8), bringing thousands to salvation (Acts 2:41; 4:4). Jesus's ministry was geographically confined to Israel; the disciples' ministry, empowered by the Spirit, would be universal. 'Because I go unto my Father' — the departure of Jesus and the coming of the Spirit is the enabling condition of the greater works.

verses.kjv_text: And whatsoever ye shall ask in my name, that will I do, that the Father may be glorified in the Son.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὅ τι ἂν αἰτήσητε ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί μου τοῦτο ποιήσω ἵνα δοξασθῇ ὁ πατὴρ ἐν τῷ υἱῷ
verses.transliteration: kai ho ti an aitēsēte en tō onomati mou touto poiēsō hina doxasthē ho patēr en tō huiō
verses.study_note: 'Whatsoever ye shall ask in my name' (ho ti an aitēsēte en tō onomati mou — whatever you ask in my name). 'In my name' (en tō onomati mou) is not a formula appended to prayer but praying in accordance with Christ's character, will, and authority — as his representatives, on his behalf, for his purposes. The promise is expansive but not blank: 'in my name' excludes selfish, faithless, or contrary-to-God's-will requests (James 4:3; 1 John 5:14). The purpose: 'that the Father may be glorified in the Son' — answered prayer is doxological; it brings glory to God.

verses.kjv_text: If ye shall ask any thing in my name, I will do it.
verses.original_text: ἐάν τι αἰτήσητέ με ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί μου ἐγὼ ποιήσω
verses.transliteration: ean ti aitēsēte me en tō onomati mou egō poiēsō
verses.study_note: The promise is repeated with slight variation — 'ask me' (aitēsēte me — ask me directly; Jesus himself as the answerer of prayer). This confirms that prayer to Jesus is prayer to God; he is not merely an intermediary but the one addressed. The repetition (vv.13-14) emphasises the certainty and the personal involvement of Christ in prayer.

verses.kjv_text: If ye love me, keep my commandments.
verses.original_text: Ἐὰν ἀγαπᾶτέ με τὰς ἐντολὰς τὰς ἐμὰς τηρήσετε
verses.transliteration: Ean agapate me tas entolas tas emas tērēsete
verses.study_note: 'If ye love me, keep my commandments' (ean agapate me, tas entolas tas emas tērēsete — if you love me, you will keep my commandments). Love is not primarily an emotion but an orientation of the will that produces obedience. 'Keep' (tērēsete — you will keep; future indicative, not imperative: this is a prediction arising from love, not merely a command). If love is genuine, obedience follows naturally. The commandments are not burdens imposed from without but the natural expression of love for Christ (1 John 5:3). This is the fulfilment of the New Covenant promise: the law written on the heart (Jeremiah 31:33).

verses.kjv_text: And I will pray the Father, and he shall give you another Comforter, that he may abide with you for ever;
verses.original_text: κἀγὼ ἐρωτήσω τὸν πατέρα καὶ ἄλλον παράκλητον δώσει ὑμῖν ἵνα μένῃ μεθ᾽ ὑμῶν εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα
verses.transliteration: kagō erōtēsō ton patera kai allon paraklēton dōsei hymin hina menē meth hymōn eis ton aiōna
verses.study_note: 'Another Comforter' (allon paraklēton — another of the same kind of Paraclete; allos: another of the same kind, vs. heteros: another of a different kind). The Spirit is another Paraclete because Jesus is the first — 1 John 2:1 calls Christ our Paraklētos with the Father. The Spirit continues and internalises what Christ did externally. 'That he may abide with you for ever' (hina menē meth hymōn eis ton aiōna — so that he may remain with you into the age). Jesus' bodily presence was temporary; the Spirit's presence is permanent. No disciples have lost the Spirit; he abides with them forever.

verses.kjv_text: Even the Spirit of truth; whom the world cannot receive, because it seeth him not, neither knoweth him: but ye know him; for he dwelleth with you, and shall be in you.
verses.original_text: τὸ πνεῦμα τῆς ἀληθείας ὃ ὁ κόσμος οὐ δύναται λαβεῖν ὅτι οὐ θεωρεῖ αὐτὸ οὐδὲ γινώσκει αὐτό ὑμεῖς δὲ γινώσκετε αὐτό ὅτι παρ᾽ ὑμῖν μένει καὶ ἐν ὑμῖν ἔσται
verses.transliteration: to pneuma tēs alētheias ho ho kosmos ou dynatai labein hoti ou theōrei auto oude ginōskei auto hymeis de ginōskete auto hoti par hymin menei kai en hymin estai
verses.study_note: 'The Spirit of truth' (to pneuma tēs alētheias) — the Spirit's defining characteristic is truth; he guides into all truth (16:13), witnesses to the truth (15:26), is identified with truth (1 John 5:6). 'The world cannot receive' (ho kosmos ou dynatai labein) — the world is constitutionally incapable of receiving the Spirit of truth, because it does not see or know him. The Spirit is spiritually discerned (1 Corinthians 2:14). 'He dwelleth with you, and shall be in you' — a progressive movement: with (para: alongside, the Spirit's presence with Jesus and the disciples during his ministry) → in (en: within, after Pentecost).

verses.kjv_text: I will not leave you comfortless: I will come to you.
verses.original_text: Οὐκ ἀφήσω ὑμᾶς ὀρφανούς ἔρχομαι πρὸς ὑμᾶς
verses.transliteration: Ouk aphēsō hymas orphanous erchomai pros hymas
verses.study_note: 'I will not leave you comfortless' (ouk aphēsō hymas orphanous — I will not leave you as orphans). 'Orphanous' (orphans — from orphanos: bereaved of a parent or teacher; used for disciples bereft of their rabbi). Jesus will not abandon the disciples as helpless orphans. 'I will come to you' (erchomai pros hymas — I am coming to you; present tense of certainty). This coming may refer to: (1) the resurrection appearances; (2) the coming of the Spirit (vv.16-17, 23); (3) the Second Coming. All three are likely in view as a telescoping fulfilment.

verses.kjv_text: Yet a little while, and the world seeth me no more; but ye see me: because I live, ye shall live also.
verses.original_text: ἔτι μικρὸν καὶ ὁ κόσμος με οὐκέτι θεωρεῖ ὑμεῖς δὲ θεωρεῖτέ με ὅτι ἐγὼ ζῶ καὶ ὑμεῖς ζήσετε
verses.transliteration: eti mikron kai ho kosmos me ouketi theōrei hymeis de theōreite me hoti egō zō kai hymeis zēsete
verses.study_note: 'The world seeth me no more' (ho kosmos me ouketi theōrei — the world no longer beholds me). After the resurrection, Jesus does not appear to the world at large — only to the disciples (Acts 10:40-41). 'But ye see me' (hymeis de theōreite me — you see/behold me; theōreō: a sustained, perceptive beholding, not a glance). 'Because I live, ye shall live also' (hoti egō zō kai hymeis zēsete) — the resurrection life of Christ is the foundation of the disciples' resurrection life. His life is the source of theirs; they live because he lives (cf. Galatians 2:20; Colossians 3:4).

verses.kjv_text: At that day ye shall know that I am in my Father, and ye in me, and I in you.
verses.original_text: ἐν ἐκείνῃ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ γνώσεσθε ὑμεῖς ὅτι ἐγὼ ἐν τῷ πατρί μου καὶ ὑμεῖς ἐν ἐμοὶ κἀγὼ ἐν ὑμῖν
verses.transliteration: en ekeinē tē hēmera gnōsesthe hymeis hoti egō en tō patri mou kai hymeis en emoi kagō en hymin
verses.study_note: 'At that day' (en ekeinē tē hēmera — in that day; the day of resurrection, Pentecost, and the ongoing life in the Spirit). The Trinitarian indwelling extends to the believer: Father → Son → believer; and believer → Son → Father. 'Ye in me, and I in you' — the mutual indwelling of Christ and the believer (cf. Galatians 2:20; Colossians 1:27; 1 John 4:13). This is the goal of salvation: union with God through union with Christ in the Spirit.

verses.kjv_text: He that hath my commandments, and keepeth them, he it is that loveth me: and he that loveth me shall be loved of my Father, and I will love him, and will manifest myself to him.
verses.original_text: ὁ ἔχων τὰς ἐντολάς μου καὶ τηρῶν αὐτὰς ἐκεῖνός ἐστιν ὁ ἀγαπῶν με ὁ δὲ ἀγαπῶν με ἀγαπηθήσεται ὑπὸ τοῦ πατρός μου καὶ ἐγὼ ἀγαπήσω αὐτὸν καὶ ἐμφανίσω αὐτῷ ἐμαυτόν
verses.transliteration: ho echōn tas entolas mou kai tērōn autas ekeinos estin ho agapōn me ho de agapōn me agapēthēsetai hypo tou patros mou kai egō agapēsō auton kai emphanisō autō emauton
verses.study_note: 'He that hath my commandments, and keepeth them, he it is that loveth me' — love is defined by obedience, not merely by feeling. 'Having' (echōn — possessing, knowing) and 'keeping' (tērōn — guarding, obeying) are two dimensions: understanding and doing. 'I will manifest myself to him' (emphanisō autō emauton — I will make myself visible/known to him). 'Emphanizō' (to make manifest, to disclose). Jesus promises a personal, experiential disclosure of himself to the obedient lover — an interior manifestation through the Spirit (cf. v.23).

verses.kjv_text: Judas saith unto him, not Iscariot, Lord, how is it that thou wilt manifest thyself unto us, and not unto the world?
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ Ἰούδας οὐχ ὁ Ἰσκαριώτης Κύριε καὶ τί γέγονεν ὅτι ἡμῖν μέλλεις ἐμφανίζειν σεαυτὸν καὶ οὐχὶ τῷ κόσμῳ
verses.transliteration: legei autō Ioudas ouch ho Iskariōtēs Kyrie kai ti gegonen hoti hēmin melleis emphanizein seauton kai ouchi tō kosmō
verses.study_note: Judas (not Iscariot) — distinguished from the betrayer; probably Judas son of James (Luke 6:16; Acts 1:13; also called Thaddaeus in Matthew/Mark). He voices the disciples' continuing Jewish messianic expectation: the Messiah should manifest himself publicly, triumphantly, to the whole world. Why a private disclosure? The question reveals an expectation of a political, visible kingdom. Jesus' answer reframes 'manifestation': the presence of God is not given as an external spectacle to the world but as an internal indwelling to the obedient.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered and said unto him, If a man love me, he will keep my words: and my Father will love him, and we will come unto him, and make our abode with him.
