verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη Ἰησοῦς καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ Ἐάν τις ἀγαπᾷ με τὸν λόγον μου τηρήσει καὶ ὁ πατήρ μου ἀγαπήσει αὐτὸν καὶ πρὸς αὐτὸν ἐλευσόμεθα καὶ μονὴν παρ᾽ αὐτῷ ποιησόμεθα
verses.transliteration: apekrithē Iēsous kai eipen autō Ean tis agapā me ton logon mou tērēsei kai ho patēr mou agapēsei auton kai pros auton eleusometha kai monēn par autō poiēsometha
verses.study_note: 'We will come unto him, and make our abode with him' (pros auton eleusometha kai monēn par autō poiēsometha). 'Monēn' — dwelling place (the same word as 'mansions' in v.2). The Father's house has many dwelling places; and the obedient believer's heart is a dwelling place for the Father and Son. The indwelling of the Trinity in the believer is the deepest mystery of Christian experience. This is not merely forensic justification but ontological union — God making his home in the human heart. The condition: love expressed in keeping the word.

verses.kjv_text: He that loveth me not keepeth not my sayings: and the word which ye hear is not mine, but the Father's which sent me.
verses.original_text: ὁ μὴ ἀγαπῶν με τοὺς λόγους μου οὐ τηρεῖ καὶ ὁ λόγος ὃν ἀκούετε οὐκ ἔστιν ἐμὸς ἀλλὰ τοῦ πέμψαντός με πατρός
verses.transliteration: ho mē agapōn me tous logous mou ou tērei kai ho logos hon akouete ouk estin emos alla tou pempsantos me patros
verses.study_note: The negative corollary: absence of love produces disobedience. 'The word which ye hear is not mine, but the Father's' — a reminder (as in 12:49) that Jesus speaks entirely as the Father's agent. To reject Jesus's words is to reject the Father's word. Disobedience to Christ is not merely a human failing but a rejection of God himself.

verses.kjv_text: These things have I spoken unto you, being yet present with you.
verses.original_text: Ταῦτα λελάληκα ὑμῖν παρ᾽ ὑμῖν μένων
verses.transliteration: Tauta lelalēka hymin par hymin menōn
verses.study_note: 'Being yet present with you' (par hymin menōn — while remaining alongside you). The discourse is spoken while Jesus is physically present. He is preparing them for the time when he will no longer be physically present but the Comforter will take his place.

verses.kjv_text: But the Comforter, which is the Holy Ghost, whom the Father will send in my name, he shall teach you all things, and bring all things to your remembrance, whatsoever I have said unto you.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ παράκλητος τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ ἅγιον ὃ πέμψει ὁ πατὴρ ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί μου ἐκεῖνος ὑμᾶς διδάξει πάντα καὶ ὑπομνήσει ὑμᾶς πάντα ἃ εἶπον ὑμῖν ἐγώ
verses.transliteration: ho de paraklētos to pneuma to hagion ho pempsει ho patēr en tō onomati mou ekeinos hymas didaxei panta kai hypomnēsei hymas panta ha eipon hymin egō
verses.study_note: 'The Holy Ghost' (to pneuma to hagion — the Holy Spirit; the name 'Holy Spirit' appears here for the first time in John). 'Sent in my name' (en tō onomati mou — in my name, as my representative and continuation). 'He shall teach you all things' (ekeinos hymas didaxei panta — that one will teach you all things). 'Bring all things to your remembrance' (hypomnēsei hymas panta — will remind you of all things). The two functions: (1) teaching new understanding of what has already been said; (2) reminding, calling to mind specific words of Jesus. This is the inspiration of the apostolic writings: the Spirit both reminded them of Jesus's exact words and gave them the understanding to interpret those words rightly.

verses.kjv_text: Peace I leave with you, my peace I give unto you: not as the world giveth, give I unto you. Let not your heart be troubled, neither let it be afraid.
verses.original_text: Εἰρήνην ἀφίημι ὑμῖν εἰρήνην τὴν ἐμὴν δίδωμι ὑμῖν οὐ καθὼς ὁ κόσμος δίδωσιν ἐγὼ δίδωμι ὑμῖν μὴ ταρασσέσθω ὑμῶν ἡ καρδία μηδὲ δειλιάτω
verses.transliteration: Eirēnēn aphiēmi hymin eirēnēn tēn emēn didōmi hymin ou kathōs ho kosmos didōsin egō didōmi hymin mē tarassesthō hymōn hē kardia mēde deiliatō
verses.study_note: 'Peace I leave with you; my peace I give unto you' (eirēnēn aphiēmi hymin; eirēnēn tēn emēn didōmi hymin). Departing guests would say 'I leave you peace' — a conventional farewell. Jesus transforms the convention: 'my peace' (tēn emēn — the definite, emphatic 'mine') is qualitatively different from the world's peace. The world's peace is the absence of conflict; Christ's peace is the shalom of the Hebrew Bible — completeness, wholeness, wellbeing in God. It is the peace he demonstrated in the storm ('Peace, be still'), in Gethsemane, on the cross. 'Not as the world giveth' — the world's peace depends on circumstances; Christ's peace transcends them (Philippians 4:7). 'Neither let it be afraid' (mēde deiliatō — nor let it be cowardly; deiliaō: to be timid, fearful; a word of moral cowardice).

verses.kjv_text: Ye have heard how I said unto you, I go away, and come again unto you. If ye loved me, ye would rejoice, because I said, I go unto the Father: for my Father is greater than I.
verses.original_text: ἠκούσατε ὅτι ἐγὼ εἶπον ὑμῖν Ὑπάγω καὶ ἔρχομαι πρὸς ὑμᾶς εἰ ἠγαπᾶτέ με ἐχάρητε ἄν ὅτι πορεύομαι πρὸς τὸν πατέρα ὅτι ὁ πατήρ μου μείζων μού ἐστιν
verses.transliteration: ēkousate hoti egō eipon hymin Hypagō kai erchomai pros hymas ei ēgapate me echarēte an hoti poreuomai pros ton patera hoti ho patēr mou meizōn mou estin
verses.study_note: 'My Father is greater than I' (ho patēr mou meizōn mou estin) — this statement has been much debated. In context, Jesus is speaking from the perspective of his incarnate, earthly state: the Father is greater in terms of position and role within the economy of salvation. The Son, having voluntarily limited himself in the incarnation, goes to the Father who is greater in terms of the divine order of operations (1 Corinthians 15:28). This is an economic Trinitarian statement, not an ontological one — it does not deny the co-equality of Father and Son in essence (cf. 10:30: 'I and the Father are one').

verses.kjv_text: And now I have told you before it come to pass, that, when it is come to pass, ye might believe.
verses.original_text: καὶ νῦν εἴρηκα ὑμῖν πρὶν γενέσθαι ἵνα ὅταν γένηται πιστεύσητε
verses.transliteration: kai nyn eirēka hymin prin genesthai hina hotan genētai pisteusēte
verses.study_note: The purpose of prophecy: forewarning strengthens faith when the foretold event arrives. The disciples will recall these words after the resurrection and their faith will be confirmed rather than destroyed by the very events (arrest, crucifixion, apparent defeat) that seemed to contradict everything Jesus had taught.

verses.kjv_text: Hereafter I will not talk much with you: for the prince of this world cometh, and hath nothing in me.
verses.original_text: οὐκέτι πολλὰ λαλήσω μεθ᾽ ὑμῶν ἔρχεται γὰρ ὁ τοῦ κόσμου τούτου ἄρχων καὶ ἐν ἐμοὶ οὐκ ἔχει οὐδέν
verses.transliteration: ouketi polla lalēsō meth hymōn erchetai gar ho tou kosmou toutou archōn kai en emoi ouk echei ouden
verses.study_note: 'The prince of this world cometh' (ho tou kosmou toutou archōn — the ruler of this world; Satan, cf. 12:31; 16:11). 'Hath nothing in me' (en emoi ouk echei ouden — in me he has nothing). Satan has no claim on Jesus, no foothold, no point of access. Jesus is perfectly sinless; there is nothing in him that corresponds to Satan's temptations or accusations. This is the ground of his victory on the cross: Satan has no legal or moral claim on the sinless one, yet the sinless one dies — and by that death defeats the one who held the power of death (Hebrews 2:14).

verses.kjv_text: But that the world may know that I love the Father; and as the Father gave me commandment, even so I do. Arise, let us go hence.
verses.original_text: ἀλλ᾽ ἵνα γνῷ ὁ κόσμος ὅτι ἀγαπῶ τὸν πατέρα καὶ καθὼς ἐντολὴν ἔδωκέν μοι ὁ πατήρ οὕτως ποιῶ ἐγείρεσθε ἄγωμεν ἐντεῦθεν
verses.transliteration: all hina gnō ho kosmos hoti agapō ton patera kai kathōs entolēn edōken moi ho patēr houtōs poiō egeiresthe agōmen enteuthen
verses.study_note: 'That the world may know that I love the Father' (hina gnō ho kosmos hoti agapō ton patera) — the cross is not merely Jesus's submission to death but his public demonstration of love for the Father. The Passion is the supreme act of filial obedience. 'As the Father gave me commandment, even so I do' (kathōs entolēn edōken moi ho patēr, houtōs poiō) — perfect obedience. 'Arise, let us go hence' (egeiresthe, agōmen enteuthen — rise up, let us go from here). Jesus rises to go to Gethsemane and the arrest. He goes willingly, commandingly. The discourse of chapters 15-17 may have been spoken as they walked, or before they left the room.

1.meaning: Let not your heart be troubled — present passive imperative with mē: stop being troubled (they already are). tarassō = inward agitation, the same word used of Jesus himself (11:33; 12:27; 13:21). The disciples are permitted feelings, not commanded ease.

1.original_word: πιστεύετε εἰς τὸν Θεόν καὶ εἰς ἐμὲ πιστεύετε
1.transliteration: pisteuete eis ton Theon kai eis eme pisteuete
1.meaning: Ye believe in God, believe also in me — ambiguous grammar (both indicatives, both imperatives, or mixed). Most naturally: 'go on believing in God; go on believing in me also'. Jesus sets trust in himself on the same plane as trust in God — implicit deity claim.

2.original_word: ἐν τῇ οἰκίᾳ τοῦ πατρός μου μοναὶ πολλαί
2.transliteration: en tē oikia tou patros mou monai pollai
2.meaning: In my Father's house are many mansions — monē = abiding-place, dwelling (from menō). Not 'mansions' in the grand modern sense (that is KJV-era English) but 'abodes'. Heaven has abundant room; no disciple need fear exclusion for lack of space.
To prepare a place for you — topos = specific reserved location. The ascension is purposive: Jesus goes as forerunner-preparer. Echoes the priestly entrance into the holy of holies (Heb 6:20; 9:24).

3.meaning: I will come again — present tense with future force: 'I am coming again'. The certainty of the parousia; the first explicit return-promise in John's gospel.
Receive you unto myself — paralambanō = to take alongside (as a bridegroom takes a bride, or master takes a disciple). Intimacy of the reception; not merely location transfer, but personal embrace.

4.meaning: The way — anticipates v.6. The departure 'whither' and the 'how' are both known: the Father's house via the cross-resurrection. Thomas's confusion in v.5 sets up the climactic ego eimi.

6.meaning: I am the way — the fifth egō eimi 'I AM' predication in John. hodos = road, path. Not a way-shower but the Way himself; access to the Father is a person, not a method.
The truth — alētheia = reality, unveiling. Jesus does not merely teach truth; he IS the embodiment of divine reality (1:14, 17).
The life — zōē = the quality of life eternal, divine life (vs. bios, biological life). The Logos has life in himself (1:4; 5:26) and gives it.

6.original_word: οὐδεὶς ἔρχεται πρὸς τὸν πατέρα εἰ μὴ δι᾽ ἐμοῦ
6.transliteration: oudeis erchetai pros ton patera ei mē di' emou
6.meaning: No man cometh unto the Father but by me — exclusive soteriological claim. Christ alone is the mediator (1 Tim 2:5); every other access-claim is false.

9.original_word: ὁ ἑωρακὼς ἐμὲ ἑώρακεν τὸν πατέρα
9.transliteration: ho heōrakōs eme heōraken ton patera
9.meaning: He that hath seen me hath seen the Father — perfect tense twice: abiding vision. The Son is the exact representation of the Father (Heb 1:3); to see Christ is to see God.

10.original_word: ὁ πατὴρ ἐν ἐμοὶ μένων ποιεῖ τὰ ἔργα
10.transliteration: ho patēr en emoi menōn poiei ta erga
10.meaning: The Father that dwelleth in me, he doeth the works — mutual indwelling of Father and Son; the Son's works are the Father's works, not imitative but ontologically shared activity.

11.meaning: For the very works' sake — Jesus admits the possibility of weaker faith resting on miraculous evidence (cf. 10:38). The works witness to the Worker when the Word alone does not yet persuade.

12.meaning: Greater works than these shall he do — not greater in miraculous intensity but in scope and fruit: post-Pentecost mission, global in reach; greater because accomplished through the ascended Christ by Spirit-empowered witnesses.

13.original_word: ὅ τι ἂν αἰτήσητε ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί μου
13.transliteration: ho ti an aitēsēte en tō onomati mou
13.meaning: Whatsoever ye shall ask in my name — en tō onomati = on the basis of who I am and what I have done. Prayer in Jesus' name is prayer aligned with his character and mission, not a magical tagline.

15.original_word: ἐὰν ἀγαπᾶτέ με τὰς ἐντολὰς τὰς ἐμὰς τηρήσετε
15.transliteration: ean agapate me tas entolas tas emas tērēsete
15.meaning: If ye love me, keep my commandments — tēreō = to guard, watch over. Love is not sentimental affection but obedient guarding of Jesus' entolai. Love and obedience are not rivals but inseparable.

16.meaning: Another Comforter — allos = another of the same kind (vs. heteros, another of a different kind). The Spirit is Christ's counterpart — same divine office, different personal mission. paraklētos = one called alongside: advocate, helper, counsellor.
Forever — unlike Jesus' temporary bodily absence, the Spirit's indwelling is permanent. The new-covenant Spirit does not depart for disobedience (cf. 1 Sam 16:14) but abides in the believer always.

17.meaning: The Spirit of truth — threefold in John (14:17; 15:26; 16:13). The Spirit is characterised by alētheia: he unveils reality, reveals the Father and Son, opposes the 'spirit of error' (1 John 4:6).

17.original_word: παρ᾽ ὑμῖν μένει καὶ ἐν ὑμῖν ἔσται
17.transliteration: par hymin menei kai en hymin estai
17.meaning: Dwelleth with you, and shall be in you — shift from para (alongside, external) to en (inside, internal). Anticipating Pentecost: the Old Testament Spirit who came upon prophets now indwells every believer.

The world cannot receive — three negations: cannot receive (dynatai labein), does not see (theōrei), does not know (ginōskei). The unbelieving world is spiritually incapable until the Spirit himself opens the eyes (16:8-11).

18.meaning: Not leave you comfortless — orphanos = orphaned, fatherless. Rabbinic disciples were called children of their teacher; Jesus' departure threatens to orphan them, but the Spirit maintains the Father's presence.

19.original_word: ὅτι ἐγὼ ζῶ καὶ ὑμεῖς ζήσετε
19.transliteration: hoti egō zō kai hymeis zēsete
19.meaning: Because I live, ye shall live also — the resurrection of Christ is the guarantee of believers' life. His indestructible life (Heb 7:16) communicates itself to his own.

21.meaning: Will manifest myself to him — emphanizō = to make visible, disclose. Post-Resurrection self-revelation to disciples, not to the world (v.22 follows). The obedient believer receives ongoing spiritual manifestations of Christ.

22.meaning: Judas not Iscariot — likely Jude/Thaddaeus, son of James (Luke 6:16; Acts 1:13). John pointedly distinguishes him; Iscariot has already exited (13:30).

23.meaning: Make our abode with him — monē (as in v.2) reused: believers are not only destined for monai in the Father's house; the Father and Son come and make monē in the believer now. Heaven and earth meet in the indwelt saint.

24.original_word: ὁ λόγος ὃν ἀκούετε οὐκ ἔστιν ἐμὸς
24.transliteration: ho logos hon akouete ouk estin emos
24.meaning: The word which ye hear is not mine — the message originates from the Father; Jesus is the faithful transmitter. Rejection of Christ's word is rejection of the Father's word — the gravest kind of unbelief.

26.original_word: ὁ παράκλητος τὸ Πνεῦμα τὸ ἅγιον
26.transliteration: ho paraklētos to Pneuma to hagion
26.meaning: The Comforter, the Holy Ghost — first explicit identification of the Paraclete as the Holy Spirit. Sent by the Father 'in my name' — on Jesus' commission and authority.
Bring all things to your remembrance — hypomimnēskō = to remind. The Spirit-taught recollection underwrites the apostolic gospel records; John claims divine anamnēsis for the tradition he hands on.

27.meaning: My peace I give unto you — eirēnē = Hebrew shalom: wholeness, right-ordering. Not the Pax Romana (superficial imperial absence of war) but Christ's own inward tranquillity bequeathed as a farewell gift.
Not as the world giveth — the world's peace is conditional, shallow, revocable; Christ's peace is rooted in reconciliation with God and endures through storms.

28.meaning: The Father is greater than I — meizōn refers to functional/economic ordering within the Trinity during the incarnation, not ontological inequality. The Son voluntarily submits in the economy of redemption (cf. Phil 2:6-7); he is fully equal in essence (10:30; 14:9).

29.meaning: Before it come to pass — Jesus tells them now so that when the events occur, faith may be confirmed (not created ab novo). Prophetic prediction is apologetic scaffolding for future belief (cf. 13:19; 16:4).

30.meaning: The prince of this world — Satan (cf. 12:31; 16:11). He 'cometh' through Judas and the arresting soldiers; yet he has 'nothing in me' (en emoi ouk echei ouden) — no legitimate claim, no foothold of sin.

31.original_word: ἵνα γνῷ ὁ κόσμος ὅτι ἀγαπῶ τὸν πατέρα
31.transliteration: hina gnō ho kosmos hoti agapō ton patera
31.meaning: That the world may know I love the Father — the cross is Jesus' public declaration of love for the Father, answered by perfect obedience to the entolē. Love and command are inseparable in the Son as they will be in disciples.

Arise, let us go hence — present middle imperative + hortatory subjunctive. Jesus rises decisively; the cross is embraced, not stumbled into. Whether 15-17 was spoken en route or before departing is debated; the willingness is unambiguous.

meta.overview: John 15 continues the Upper Room Discourse, possibly spoken as Jesus and the disciples walked toward Gethsemane (14:31). It opens with the seventh and final 'I AM' declaration: 'I am the true vine, and my Father is the husbandman' (v.1). The vine was a central symbol of Israel in the Old Testament (Psalm 80:8-16; Isaiah 5:1-7; Jeremiah 2:21; Ezekiel 15; Hosea 10:1) — Israel was God's vine, called to bear fruit but consistently failing. Jesus now declares himself the true (alēthinos — genuine, authentic) vine, the fulfilment of all that Israel was meant to be. The disciples are the branches: apart from Christ they can do nothing (v.5); connected to him, they bear much fruit (v.5). The key word of the passage is 'abide' (menō — to remain, dwell, continue) — appearing 11 times in vv.1-11. Branches that do not bear fruit are removed; those that bear fruit are pruned to bear more (vv.2-3). The Father is glorified by much fruit (v.8). Jesus then deepens the relationship: no longer servants but friends, chosen by him, appointed to bear fruit that remains (vv.15-16). The second half of the chapter (vv.18-27) warns about the world's hatred: as the world hated Christ, it will hate his disciples (v.18). The disciples are not of the world (v.19); the servant is not greater than the master (v.20). This hatred fulfils Scripture: 'They hated me without a cause' (Psalm 35:19; 69:4). The Paraclete (the Spirit of truth) will testify about Christ, and so will the disciples (vv.26-27).
meta.theme: Abide in Me — Union with Christ as the Source of Fruitfulness
meta.keyWordMeaning: To remain, abide, dwell, stay — the central verb of John 15, appearing 11 times in vv.1-11. It describes the continuous, settled, vital union between the branch and the vine, the disciple and Christ. 'Menō' in John consistently denotes a permanent, relational, life-giving connection: the Son abides in the Father's love (v.10), the disciples are to abide in the Son (v.4), the Son abides in the disciples (v.5), the word abides in them (v.7), they abide in his love (v.9). Abiding is not passive but active: it involves keeping his commandments (v.10), being cleansed by his word (v.3), and bearing fruit. The alternative to abiding is withering — the branch cut off dries up (v.6). Christian life is not an event but an abiding relationship.
outline: The True Vine (vv.1-3): I am the true vine and my Father is the husbandman. Every branch in me that beareth not fruit he taketh away and every branch that beareth fruit he purgeth it that it may bring forth more fruit. Now ye are clean through the word which I have spoken unto you

Abide in Me (vv.4-8): Abide in me and I in you. As the branch cannot bear fruit of itself except it abide in the vine no more can ye except ye abide in me. I am the vine ye are the branches He that abideth in me and I in him the same bringeth forth much fruit for without me ye can do nothing. If a man abide not in me he is cast forth as a branch and is withered and men gather them and cast them into the fire and they are burned. If ye abide in me and my words abide in you ye shall ask what ye will and it shall be done unto you. Herein is my Father glorified that ye bear much fruit so shall ye be my disciples

Love One Another (vv.9-17): As the Father hath loved me so have I loved you continue ye in my love. If ye keep my commandments ye shall abide in my love even as I have kept my Father's commandments and abide in his love. These things have I spoken unto you that my joy might remain in you and that your joy might be full. This is my commandment That ye love one another as I have loved you. Greater love hath no man than this that a man lay down his life for his friends. Ye are my friends if ye do whatsoever I command you. Henceforth I call you not servants for the servant knoweth not what his lord doeth but I have called you friends for all things that I have heard of my Father I have made known unto you. Ye have not chosen me but I have chosen you and ordained you that ye should go and bring forth fruit and that your fruit should remain that whatsoever ye shall ask of the Father in my name he may give it you. These things I command you that ye love one another

The World's Hatred (vv.18-27): If the world hate you ye know that it hated me before it hated you. If ye were of the world the world would love his own but because ye are not of the world but I have chosen you out of the world therefore the world hateth you. Remember the word that I said unto you The servant is not greater than his lord. If they have persecuted me they will also persecute you if they have kept my saying they will keep yours also. But all these things will they do unto you for my name's sake because they know not him that sent me. If I had not come and spoken unto them they had not had sin but now they have no cloke for their sin. He that hateth me hateth my Father also. If I had not done among them the works which none other man did they had not had sin but now have they both seen and hated both me and my Father. But this cometh to pass that the word might be fulfilled that is written in their law They hated me without a cause. But when the Comforter is come whom I will send unto you from the Father even the Spirit of truth which proceedeth from the Father he shall testify of me and ye also shall bear witness because ye have been with me from the beginning

verses.kjv_text: I am the true vine, and my Father is the husbandman.
verses.original_text: Ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ ἄμπελος ἡ ἀληθινή καὶ ὁ πατήρ μου ὁ γεωργός ἐστιν
verses.transliteration: Egō eimi hē ampelos hē alēthinē kai ho patēr mou ho geōrgos estin
verses.study_note: 'I am the true vine' (Egō eimi hē ampelos hē alēthinē) — the seventh and final of John's great 'I AM' declarations. 'True' (alēthinē — genuine, authentic, real, as opposed to shadow or type). The vine was the pre-eminent symbol of Israel: Psalm 80:8 ('Thou hast brought a vine out of Egypt'); Isaiah 5:1-7 (the song of the vineyard); Jeremiah 2:21 ('I had planted thee a noble vine, wholly a right seed: how then art thou turned into the degenerate plant of a strange vine?'). Israel was God's vine but failed to bear fruit. Jesus is the true vine — what Israel was called to be, now perfectly embodied. 'The husbandman' (ho geōrgos — the farmer, vinedresser, one who works the earth). God the Father tends the vine with the same care and purpose as a devoted vinedresser.

verses.kjv_text: Every branch in me that beareth not fruit he taketh away: and every branch that beareth fruit, he purgeth it, that it may bring forth more fruit.
verses.original_text: πᾶν κλῆμα ἐν ἐμοὶ μὴ φέρον καρπόν αἴρει αὐτό καὶ πᾶν τὸ καρπὸν φέρον καθαίρει αὐτό ἵνα καρπὸν πλείονα φέρῃ
verses.transliteration: pan klēma en emoi mē pheron karpon airei auto kai pan to karpon pheron kathairei auto hina karpon pleiona pherē
verses.study_note: 'Every branch in me that beareth not fruit he taketh away' (airei auto — lifts it away/removes it). 'Airei' can mean 'lifts up' (as a vinedresser lifts trailing branches off the ground to expose them to sun, a common viticulture practice) or 'takes away/removes.' In context, the contrast with the fruitful branch that is 'purged' (kept but cleaned) suggests 'removes' is the primary meaning. The fruitless branch faces removal; the fruitful branch faces pruning (kathairo — to cleanse, purify, prune). The same Greek root as 'clean' (katharos, v.3; 13:10). Pruning is a sign of being in the vine, not of rejection.

verses.kjv_text: Now ye are clean through the word which I have spoken unto you.
verses.original_text: ἤδη ὑμεῖς καθαροί ἐστε διὰ τὸν λόγον ὃν λελάληκα ὑμῖν
verses.transliteration: ēdē hymeis katharoi este dia ton logon hon lelalēka hymin
verses.study_note: 'Ye are clean' (katharoi este — you are already clean; the same root as 'purgeth/prunes': kathairo). Jesus is playing on the double meaning: the disciples are already pruned/cleansed through the word. The word of Christ is the pruning agent — it cuts away false ideas, sinful patterns, misplaced loyalties. 'Through the word which I have spoken unto you' (dia ton logon hon lelalēka hymin — through the word I have spoken; perfect tense: word already spoken with ongoing effect). The scripture, the teaching of Jesus, cleanses (Ephesians 5:26; Psalm 119:9).

verses.kjv_text: Abide in me, and I in you. As the branch cannot bear fruit of itself, except it abide in the vine; no more can ye, except ye abide in me.
verses.original_text: μείνατε ἐν ἐμοί κἀγὼ ἐν ὑμῖν καθὼς τὸ κλῆμα οὐ δύναται καρπὸν φέρειν ἀφ᾽ ἑαυτοῦ ἐὰν μὴ μένῃ ἐν τῇ ἀμπέλῳ οὕτως οὐδὲ ὑμεῖς ἐὰν μὴ ἐν ἐμοὶ μένητε
verses.transliteration: meinate en emoi kagō en hymin kathōs to klēma ou dynatai karpon pherein aph heautou ean mē menē en tē ampelō houtōs oude hymeis ean mē en emoi menēte
verses.study_note: 'Abide in me' (meinate en emoi — remain in me; aorist imperative: a decisive act of settling into union with Christ). The branch does not remain in the vine by striving; it remains by not separating itself. The metaphor describes organic union: the branch's life-sap comes entirely from the vine. Severed, it cannot function. So the disciple's spiritual fruitfulness comes entirely from union with Christ — not from human effort, education, or willpower. 'No more can ye, except ye abide in me' — the impossibility is absolute. Fruitfulness without connection to Christ is as impossible as a branch bearing grapes without the vine.

verses.kjv_text: I am the vine, ye are the branches: He that abideth in me, and I in him, the same bringeth forth much fruit: for without me ye can do nothing.
verses.original_text: ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ ἄμπελος ὑμεῖς τὰ κλήματα ὁ μένων ἐν ἐμοὶ κἀγὼ ἐν αὐτῷ οὗτος φέρει καρπὸν πολύν ὅτι χωρὶς ἐμοῦ οὐ δύνασθε ποιεῖν οὐδέν
verses.transliteration: egō eimi hē ampelos hymeis ta klēmata ho menōn en emoi kagō en autō houtos pherei karpon polun hoti chōris emou ou dynasthe poiein ouden
verses.study_note: 'Without me ye can do nothing' (chōris emou ou dynasthe poiein ouden — apart from me you are able to do nothing). 'Chōris' (apart from, separate from) — not 'without my help' but 'severed from me, disconnected from me.' 'Nothing' (ouden) — absolute zero. This is one of the most radical statements of spiritual dependence in all of Scripture. Paul echoes it: 'I can do all things through Christ which strengtheneth me' (Philippians 4:13) — the other side of the same coin. Human effort without vital union with Christ produces, spiritually speaking, nothing of eternal value (1 Corinthians 3:12-15).

verses.kjv_text: If a man abide not in me, he is cast forth as a branch, and is withered; and men gather them, and cast them into the fire, and they are burned.
verses.original_text: ἐὰν μή τις μένῃ ἐν ἐμοί ἐβλήθη ἔξω ὡς τὸ κλῆμα καὶ ἐξηράνθη καὶ συνάγουσιν αὐτὰ καὶ εἰς τὸ πῦρ βάλλουσιν καὶ καίεται
verses.transliteration: ean mē tis menē en emoi eblēthē exō hōs to klēma kai exēranthē kai synagousin auta kai eis to pyr ballousin kai kainetai
verses.study_note: 'Cast forth as a branch, and is withered' (eblēthē exō hōs to klēma kai exēranthē — was cast outside as the branch and withered; the aorist 'was cast' treats the future event as already accomplished). 'Cast into the fire' (eis to pyr ballousin) — the image of judgment echoes Matthew 3:10; 7:19 ('cut down and cast into the fire'). The withered branch is useless for any purpose except burning. The warning is solemn: persistent non-abiding leads to judgment. The passage raises difficult questions about whether 'branches in me' can ultimately be lost — the pastoral context is a warning to persevere, not a theological treatise on perseverance.

verses.kjv_text: If ye abide in me, and my words abide in you, ye shall ask what ye will, and it shall be done unto you.
verses.original_text: ἐὰν μείνητε ἐν ἐμοὶ καὶ τὰ ῥήματά μου ἐν ὑμῖν μείνῃ ὃ ἐὰν θέλητε αἰτήσασθε καὶ γενήσεται ὑμῖν
verses.transliteration: ean meinēte en emoi kai ta rhēmata mou en hymin meinē ho ean thelēte aitēsasthe kai genēsetai hymin
verses.study_note: 'Ask what ye will, and it shall be done unto you' (ho ean thelēte aitēsasthe kai genēsetai hymin — whatever you wish, ask and it will happen for you). The promise of answered prayer here has two conditions: abiding in Christ, and his words abiding in us. When the words of Christ dwell richly in us (Colossians 3:16), our desires are shaped by his desires; what we 'will' aligns with what he wills. This is not a blank cheque for selfish prayer but the promise of effective prayer for those deeply formed by Christ's word.

verses.kjv_text: Herein is my Father glorified, that ye bear much fruit; so shall ye be my disciples.
verses.original_text: ἐν τούτῳ ἐδοξάσθη ὁ πατήρ μου ἵνα καρπὸν πολὺν φέρητε καὶ γενήσεσθε ἐμοὶ μαθηταί
verses.transliteration: en toutō edoxasthē ho patēr mou hina karpon polun phērēte kai genēsesthe emoi mathētai
verses.study_note: 'My Father is glorified that ye bear much fruit' (edoxasthē ho patēr mou — my Father was/is glorified; aorist of certainty). God's glory — his visible splendour — is displayed in fruitful disciples. The fruitful life is a theophany: it shows the world what God is like. 'So shall ye be my disciples' (genēsesthe emoi mathētai — you will become/prove to be my disciples). Fruit is not the condition of discipleship but its evidence and proof. As in 13:35 (love is the mark), here fruit is the mark.

verses.kjv_text: As the Father hath loved me, so have I loved you: continue ye in my love.
verses.original_text: καθὼς ἠγάπησέν με ὁ πατήρ κἀγὼ ὑμᾶς ἠγάπησα μείνατε ἐν τῇ ἀγάπῃ τῇ ἐμῇ
verses.transliteration: kathōs ēgapēsen me ho patēr kagō hymas ēgapēsa meinate en tē agapē tē emē
verses.study_note: 'As the Father hath loved me, so have I loved you' (kathōs ēgapēsen me ho patēr, kagō hymas ēgapēsa) — the love between Father and Son is the measure and model of Christ's love for the disciples. 'Continue ye in my love' (meinate en tē agapē tē emē — remain in my love; the imperative form of menō). The disciple does not generate love for Christ from their own resources but remains in the love already extended to them by Christ. Love is the atmosphere of abiding.

verses.kjv_text: If ye keep my commandments, ye shall abide in my love; even as I have kept my Father's commandments, and abide in his love.
verses.original_text: ἐὰν τὰς ἐντολάς μου τηρήσητε μενεῖτε ἐν τῇ ἀγάπῃ μου καθὼς ἐγὼ τοῦ πατρός μου τὰς ἐντολὰς τετήρηκα καὶ μένω αὐτοῦ ἐν τῇ ἀγάπῃ
verses.transliteration: ean tas entolas mou tērēsēte meneite en tē agapē mou kathōs egō tou patros mou tas entolas tetērēka kai menō autou en tē agapē
verses.study_note: 'Even as I have kept my Father's commandments, and abide in his love' — Jesus's own obedience to the Father is the pattern for the disciples' obedience to him. The Son modelled perfect filial obedience; the disciples are called to the same. The connection between commandment-keeping and abiding in love is not legalistic but relational: obedience is the expression of love, and love is the context of obedience. The Father's love for the Son did not depend on the Son's obedience but found its expression through it.

verses.kjv_text: These things have I spoken unto you, that my joy might remain in you, and that your joy might be full.
verses.original_text: ταῦτα λελάληκα ὑμῖν ἵνα ἡ χαρὰ ἡ ἐμὴ ἐν ὑμῖν μείνῃ καὶ ἡ χαρὰ ὑμῶν πληρωθῇ
verses.transliteration: tauta lelalēka hymin hina hē chara hē emē en hymin meinē kai hē chara hymōn plērōthē
verses.study_note: 'That my joy might remain in you' (hē chara hē emē en hymin meinē — my joy may remain in you). Jesus speaks of his joy (hē chara hē emē — the definite, emphatic 'my' joy) — the joy of the Son in his relationship with the Father, the joy of obedient love, the joy of doing the Father's will even through suffering (Hebrews 12:2: 'who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross'). 'That your joy might be full' (plērōthē — might be filled up completely). Christ's joy transmitted into the disciples produces joy that is full — not partial, not occasional, but complete (cf. 16:24; 1 John 1:4; 2 John 12).

verses.kjv_text: This is my commandment, That ye love one another, as I have loved you.
verses.original_text: αὕτη ἐστὶν ἡ ἐντολὴ ἡ ἐμή ἵνα ἀγαπᾶτε ἀλλήλους καθὼς ἠγάπησα ὑμᾶς
verses.transliteration: hautē estin hē entolē hē emē hina agapate allēlous kathōs ēgapēsa hymas
verses.study_note: 'This is my commandment' (hautē estin hē entolē hē emē — this is the commandment, my commandment; the definite article and possessive make it singular and supreme). The one commandment that encompasses all others is mutual love after the pattern of Christ's love. Repeated from 13:34 but now deepened: there the standard was 'as I have loved you'; here in context the standard is immediately defined as the greatest love — laying down one's life (v.13).

verses.kjv_text: Greater love hath no man than this, that a man lay down his life for his friends.
verses.original_text: μείζονα ταύτης ἀγάπην οὐδεὶς ἔχει ἵνα τις τὴν ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ θῇ ὑπὲρ τῶν φίλων αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: meizona tautēs agapēn oudeis echei hina tis tēn psychēn autou thē hyper tōn philōn autou
verses.study_note: 'Greater love hath no man than this, that a man lay down his life for his friends' (meizona tautēs agapēn oudeis echei — no one has greater love than this). 'Lay down his life' (tēn psychēn autou thē — put down his soul/life; the same verb as 10:11,17,18: the Good Shepherd laying down his life). The superlative of love is self-sacrifice for another. Jesus is both defining the ultimate love and announcing what he is about to do — he is the one who lays down his life for his friends. Paul extends the wonder: 'God commendeth his love toward us, in that, while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us' (Romans 5:8) — not even for friends, but for enemies.

verses.kjv_text: Ye are my friends, if ye do whatsoever I command you.
verses.original_text: ὑμεῖς φίλοι μού ἐστε ἐὰν ποιῆτε ἃ ἐγὼ ἐντέλλομαι ὑμῖν
verses.transliteration: hymeis philoi mou este ean poiēte ha egō entellomai hymin
verses.study_note: 'Ye are my friends' (hymeis philoi mou este — you are my friends). Abraham was called 'the Friend of God' (Isaiah 41:8; James 2:23) — now the disciples are given that same title. 'If ye do whatsoever I command you' — the condition is not the cause of friendship (Jesus has already chosen them, v.16) but the expression and evidence of it. Friends of Jesus are distinguished from servants (v.15) not by absence of obedience but by having been taken into his confidence.

verses.kjv_text: Henceforth I call you not servants; for the servant knoweth not what his lord doeth: but I have called you friends; for all things that I have heard of my Father I have made known unto you.
verses.original_text: οὐκέτι λέγω ὑμᾶς δούλους ὅτι ὁ δοῦλος οὐκ οἶδεν τί ποιεῖ αὐτοῦ ὁ κύριος ὑμᾶς δὲ εἴρηκα φίλους ὅτι πάντα ἃ ἤκουσα παρὰ τοῦ πατρός μου ἐγνώρισα ὑμῖν
verses.transliteration: ouketi legō hymas doulous hoti ho doulos ouk oiden ti poiei autou ho kyrios hymas de eirēka philous hoti panta ha ēkousa para tou patros mou egnōrisa hymin
verses.study_note: 'Henceforth I call you not servants' (ouketi legō hymas doulous — no longer do I call you slaves). A slave obeys without knowing the master's plans; a friend is taken into confidence. 'I have made known unto you' (egnōrisa hymin — I made known to you; aorist: the disclosure is complete). The disciples have been given access to the Father's purposes — the mystery hidden through ages is now revealed (Ephesians 3:3-6; Colossians 1:26-27). This is unprecedented intimacy: the eternal Son sharing the Father's counsels with mortal friends.

verses.kjv_text: Ye have not chosen me, but I have chosen you, and ordained you, that ye should go and bring forth fruit, and that your fruit should remain: that whatsoever ye shall ask of the Father in my name, he may give it you.
verses.original_text: οὐχ ὑμεῖς με ἐξελέξασθε ἀλλ᾽ ἐγὼ ἐξελεξάμην ὑμᾶς καὶ ἔθηκα ὑμᾶς ἵνα ὑμεῖς ὑπάγητε καὶ καρπὸν φέρητε καὶ ὁ καρπὸς ὑμῶν μένῃ ἵνα ὅ τι ἂν αἰτήσητε τὸν πατέρα ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί μου δῷ ὑμῖν
verses.transliteration: ouch hymeis me exelexasthe all egō exelexamēn hymas kai ethēka hymas hina hymeis hypagēte kai karpon phērēte kai ho karpos hymōn menē hina ho ti an aitēsēte ton patera en tō onomati mou dō hymin
verses.study_note: 'Ye have not chosen me, but I have chosen you' (ouch hymeis me exelexasthe, all egō exelexamēn hymas) — the priority of divine election. In the Jewish world, disciples chose their rabbi; here the Rabbi chose his disciples. 'Ordained you' (ethēka hymas — appointed/placed you; ethēka: I placed, set). The purpose: 'that your fruit should remain' (ho karpos hymōn menē — your fruit may remain; eternal fruit, fruit of lasting significance). The word menē (remain) ties back to the abiding theme of vv.4-11: fruit that remains is fruit produced by abiding.

verses.kjv_text: These things I command you, that ye love one another.
verses.original_text: ταῦτα ἐντέλλομαι ὑμῖν ἵνα ἀγαπᾶτε ἀλλήλους
verses.transliteration: tauta entellomai hymin hina agapate allēlous
verses.study_note: The love commandment is repeated for the third time in the Upper Room Discourse (13:34; 15:12; 15:17) — marking it as the central, defining obligation of the Christian community. Three repetitions bracket the section on friendship (vv.14-16): love is the context in which friendship with Christ is expressed and maintained. The repetition is not redundancy but emphasis — this is the foundational command from which all other aspects of Christian community life flow.

verses.kjv_text: If the world hate you, ye know that it hated me before it hated you.
verses.original_text: Εἰ ὁ κόσμος ὑμᾶς μισεῖ γινώσκετε ὅτι ἐμὲ πρῶτον ὑμῶν μεμίσηκεν
verses.transliteration: Ei ho kosmos hymas misei ginōskete hoti eme prōton hymōn memisēken
verses.study_note: 'If the world hate you' (ei ho kosmos hymas misei — if the world hates you; and it does). The abrupt shift from love within the community to hatred from the world marks the beginning of the second section (vv.18-27). 'It hated me before it hated you' (eme prōton hymōn memisēken — it has hated me before [hating] you; perfect tense: the hatred of Jesus is an established fact that precedes the disciples' experience). The disciples' experience of hostility is not surprising or anomalous — it is the continuation of the world's response to Christ.

verses.kjv_text: If ye were of the world, the world would love his own: but because ye are not of the world, but I have chosen you out of the world, therefore the world hateth you.
verses.original_text: εἰ ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου ἦτε ὁ κόσμος ἂν τὸ ἴδιον ἐφίλει ὅτι δὲ ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου οὐκ ἐστέ ἀλλ᾽ ἐγὼ ἐξελεξάμην ὑμᾶς ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου διὰ τοῦτο μισεῖ ὑμᾶς ὁ κόσμος
verses.transliteration: ei ek tou kosmou ēte ho kosmos an to idion ephilei hoti de ek tou kosmou ouk este all egō exelexamēn hymas ek tou kosmou dia touto misei hymas ho kosmos
verses.study_note: 'The world would love his own' (ho kosmos an to idion ephilei — the world would love its own; to idion: its own people, its own kind). The world system is self-referential — it loves those who belong to it and reflect its values. The disciples have been 'chosen out of the world' (exelexamēn hymas ek tou kosmou) — extracted from the world-system by divine election. The hostility of the world is therefore structural: the disciples are, by their transformed nature and allegiance, a standing rebuke to the world's values.

verses.kjv_text: Remember the word that I said unto you, The servant is not greater than his lord. If they have persecuted me, they will also persecute you; if they have kept my saying, they will keep yours also.
verses.original_text: μνημονεύετε τοῦ λόγου οὗ ἐγὼ εἶπον ὑμῖν Οὐκ ἔστιν δοῦλος μείζων τοῦ κυρίου αὐτοῦ εἰ ἐμὲ ἐδίωξαν καὶ ὑμᾶς διώξουσιν εἰ τὸν λόγον μου ἐτήρησαν καὶ τὸν ὑμέτερον τηρήσουσιν
verses.transliteration: mnēmoneuete tou logou hou egō eipon hymin Ouk estin doulos meizōn tou kyriou autou ei eme ediōxan kai hymas diōxousin ei ton logon mou etērēsan kai ton hymeteron tērēsousin
verses.study_note: 'If they have persecuted me, they will also persecute you' (ei eme ediōxan, kai hymas diōxousin — if they persecuted me, they will also persecute you). The logic of persecution: those who hate the master will hate the servant (cf. Matthew 10:24-25; Luke 6:40). The converse is also true: 'if they have kept my saying, they will keep yours also' — but this is the minority response. Jesus prepares the disciples not for ease but for hardship, not for popularity but for rejection, explaining it so they are not caught off guard (16:1-4).

verses.kjv_text: But all these things will they do unto you for my name's sake, because they know not him that sent me.
verses.original_text: ἀλλὰ ταῦτα πάντα ποιήσουσιν εἰς ὑμᾶς διὰ τὸ ὄνομά μου ὅτι οὐκ οἴδασιν τὸν πέμψαντά με
verses.transliteration: alla tauta panta poiēsousin eis hymas dia to onoma mou hoti ouk oidasin ton pempsanta me
verses.study_note: 'For my name's sake' (dia to onoma mou — on account of my name). The disciples suffer not for their own sins or provocations but because of their identification with Christ. 'Because they know not him that sent me' (ouk oidasin ton pempsanta me) — the root of all rejection of Jesus and his followers is ignorance of the Father. Those who truly know God recognise the Son. Rejection of Christ is simultaneously ignorance of and rejection of God (cf. 8:19,42,55; 16:3).

verses.kjv_text: If I had not come and spoken unto them, they had not had sin: but now they have no cloke for their sin.
verses.original_text: εἰ μὴ ἦλθον καὶ ἐλάλησα αὐτοῖς ἁμαρτίαν οὐκ εἴχοσαν νῦν δὲ πρόφασιν οὐκ ἔχουσιν περὶ τῆς ἁμαρτίας αὐτῶν
verses.transliteration: ei mē ēlthon kai elalēsa autois hamartian ouk eichosan nyn de prophasisin ouk echousin peri tēs hamartias autōn
verses.study_note: 'They had not had sin' (hamartian ouk eichosan — they would not have had sin; not absolute sinlessness, but specifically the sin of rejecting the light). The coming of Jesus created a new category of accountability. Before his coming, the Jews sinned in ignorance; now they have heard his words and seen his works, and yet reject him. Their rejection is therefore 'without cloke' (prophasisin ouk echousin — without excuse; prophasis: a pretext, cover, excuse). Greater light creates greater accountability (cf. Luke 12:47-48; Romans 2:12-16; Hebrews 10:26-31).

verses.kjv_text: He that hateth me hateth my Father also.
verses.original_text: ὁ ἐμὲ μισῶν καὶ τὸν πατέρα μου μισεῖ
verses.transliteration: ho eme misōn kai ton patera mou misei
verses.study_note: The unity of Father and Son means that hatred of one is hatred of the other — just as love of one is love of the other (14:9; Matthew 10:40). There is no path to the Father that bypasses the Son (14:6); correspondingly, there is no rejection of the Son that does not implicate rejection of the Father. Anti-Christian hostility is, at its root, enmity against God.

verses.kjv_text: If I had not done among them the works which none other man did, they had not had sin: but now have they both seen and hated both me and my Father.
verses.original_text: εἰ τὰ ἔργα μὴ ἐποίησα ἐν αὐτοῖς ἃ οὐδεὶς ἄλλος πεποίηκεν ἁμαρτίαν οὐκ εἴχοσαν νῦν δὲ καὶ ἑωράκασιν καὶ μεμισήκασιν καὶ ἐμὲ καὶ τὸν πατέρα μου
verses.transliteration: ei ta erga mē epoiēsa en autois ha oudeis allos pepoiēken hamartian ouk eichosan nyn de kai heōrakusin kai memisēkasin kai eme kai ton patera mou
verses.study_note: 'The works which none other man did' (ha oudeis allos pepoiēken — which no one else has done; perfect tense: unique and without parallel). The miracles of Jesus are a second ground of accountability (alongside his words, v.22). They are unprecedented in scale, consistency, compassion, and divine authority. To have seen these works and still reject the one who performed them is to be 'without excuse.' 'Seen and hated' — the hatred is inexplicable except by the category of sin: irrational, willful, heart-rooted rejection of the light.

verses.kjv_text: But this cometh to pass, that the word might be fulfilled that is written in their law, They hated me without a cause.
verses.original_text: ἀλλ᾽ ἵνα πληρωθῇ ὁ λόγος ὁ γεγραμμένος ἐν τῷ νόμῳ αὐτῶν ὅτι Ἐμίσησάν με δωρεάν
verses.transliteration: all hina plērōthē ho logos ho gegrammenos en tō nomō autōn hoti Emisēsan me dōrean
verses.study_note: 'They hated me without a cause' (Emisēsan me dōrean — they hated me freely/without reason). Quoting Psalm 35:19 and 69:4. 'Dōrean' (freely, as a gift, without cause) — the hatred has no moral justification, no legitimate grounds. David wrote of his persecutors; the Holy Spirit intended this as a prophecy of Christ. 'Their law' (tō nomō autōn) — Jesus refers to the Psalms as 'their law' (Torah in its broader sense), ironically noting that their own scriptures predict this irrational hatred.

verses.kjv_text: But when the Comforter is come, whom I will send unto you from the Father, even the Spirit of truth, which proceedeth from the Father, he shall testify of me:
verses.original_text: ὅταν δὲ ἔλθῃ ὁ παράκλητος ὃν ἐγὼ πέμψω ὑμῖν παρὰ τοῦ πατρός τὸ πνεῦμα τῆς ἀληθείας ὃ παρὰ τοῦ πατρὸς ἐκπορεύεται ἐκεῖνος μαρτυρήσει περὶ ἐμοῦ
verses.transliteration: hotan de elthē ho paraklētos hon egō pempsō hymin para tou patros to pneuma tēs alētheias ho para tou patros ekporeuetai ekeinos martyrēsei peri emou
verses.study_note: 'Which proceedeth from the Father' (ho para tou patros ekporeuetai — who goes out/proceeds from alongside the Father). 'Ekporeuetai' (present tense: continually proceeds, goes forth). This verse is the key text in the historic filioque controversy: Western Christianity added to the Nicene Creed that the Spirit proceeds from the Father 'and the Son' (filioque); Eastern Orthodoxy insists he proceeds from the Father alone. Here, Jesus says the Spirit 'proceeds from the Father' but also that Jesus 'will send' him — both are true. The Spirit's eternal procession is from the Father; his temporal mission is by the Son. 'He shall testify of me' (martyrēsei peri emou) — the Spirit's primary ministry is bearing witness to Christ, not to himself.

verses.kjv_text: And ye also shall bear witness, because ye have been with me from the beginning.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὑμεῖς δὲ μαρτυρεῖτε ὅτι ἀπ᾽ ἀρχῆς μετ᾽ ἐμοῦ ἐστε
verses.transliteration: kai hymeis de martyreite hoti ap archēs met emou este
verses.study_note: 'Ye also shall bear witness' (kai hymeis de martyreite — and you also are witnessing/bear witness; present tense: ongoing). The apostolic witness runs alongside the Spirit's witness — they are distinct but inseparable. The Spirit testifies through and with the apostles. 'Because ye have been with me from the beginning' (oti ap archēs met emou este — because from the beginning you have been with me; the beginning of Jesus's ministry). This is the apostolic qualification: eyewitness presence throughout Jesus's ministry (Acts 1:21-22; 1 John 1:1-3). The disciples' witness is rooted in direct experience; the Spirit's witness is divine authentication.

1.original_word: ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ ἄμπελος ἡ ἀληθινή
1.transliteration: egō eimi hē ampelos hē alēthinē
1.meaning: I am the true vine — the sixth (of seven) egō eimi + predicate in John. alēthinos = genuine, real (as opposed to substitute). Israel was a vine that failed (Isa 5:1-7; Ps 80:8-18; Jer 2:21); Jesus is the Vine that does not fail.
The husbandman — geōrgos = farmer, vinedresser. The Father tends the vine: pruning, lifting, cleansing. Care and discipline are twin expressions of love.

2.meaning: Taketh away... purgeth — wordplay (airō/kathairō). Fruitless branches are removed; fruitful ones are pruned (cleansed of excess foliage) for greater fruit. Clean ≠ exempt from the pruning knife.

Beareth not fruit — some exegetes render airei 'lifts up' (trellis a fallen branch) rather than 'removes'; viticultural practice supports both. In either case, fruit-bearing is the test of genuine branch-life.

3.original_word: ἤδη καθαροί ἐστε διὰ τὸν λόγον
3.transliteration: ēdē katharoi este dia ton logon
3.meaning: Now ye are clean through the word — katharoi picks up kathairō in v.2: they are 'pruned/cleansed' by Jesus' teaching. The word of Christ is the sanctifying agent (cf. Eph 5:26; John 17:17).

4.original_word: μείνατε ἐν ἐμοί κἀγὼ ἐν ὑμῖν
4.transliteration: meinate en emoi kagō en hymin
4.meaning: Abide in me, and I in you — meinate (aorist imperative) calls for decisive abiding; mutual indwelling is the central vine-branch reality. menō is the keyword of the chapter (11x).
Branch — klēma = a branch specifically of a vine (not generic klados). It has no independent life-source; separated, it is firewood (v.6).

5.original_word: χωρὶς ἐμοῦ οὐ δύνασθε ποιεῖν οὐδέν
5.transliteration: chōris emou ou dynasthe poiein ouden
5.meaning: Without me ye can do nothing — chōris = apart from, severed from. Absolute spiritual sterility apart from Christ; no spiritual fruit originates in the branch itself.

6.original_word: ὡς τὸ κλῆμα... συνάγουσιν... εἰς τὸ πῦρ
6.transliteration: hōs to klēma... synagousin... eis to pyr
6.meaning: Gathered... cast into fire — the destiny of the unfruitful branch is the burn-pile. A warning about apparent affiliation without living union; severity of judgment balanced against promise of abundant fruit.

7.original_word: τὰ ῥήματά μου ἐν ὑμῖν μείνῃ
7.transliteration: ta rēmata mou en hymin meinē
7.meaning: My words abide in you — rēma = uttered saying, specific word. Abiding in Christ and having his rēmata abide in us are mutually defining.
Ye shall ask what ye will — prayer-desires shaped by Christ's abiding word will necessarily align with his will. The 'blank cheque' is signed only by the abiding branch.

8.meaning: Herein is my Father glorified — the Father's glory is the telos of fruit-bearing. Disciples exist for the Father's doxa, not their own.
So shall ye be my disciples — aorist subjunctive: come to be / prove yourselves. Discipleship is evidenced by abiding fruit, not merely initial following.

9.original_word: μείνατε ἐν τῇ ἀγάπῃ τῇ ἐμῇ
9.transliteration: meinate en tē agapē tē emē
9.meaning: Abide in my love — the locus of abiding: agapē. Love is the atmosphere of union, not an occasional emotion but a continuous sphere of existence.

10.meaning: If ye keep my commandments — tēreō = to watch, guard. Obedience is the evidence and condition of abiding love; the Son's own pattern of keeping the Father's commandments is normative.

11.original_word: ἡ χαρὰ ἡ ἐμὴ ἐν ὑμῖν ᾖ
11.transliteration: hē chara hē emē en hymin ē
11.meaning: My joy might remain in you — chara = joy. Christ's own joy (26 hours before Gethsemane!) is communicable; it is deeper than circumstance, rooted in the Father's love and mission accomplished.

12.meaning: Love one another as — the new commandment of 13:34 re-stated. kathōs = according to the pattern of. Cruciform love is the measure.

13.meaning: Lay down his life — tithēmi (as in 10:11, 15, 17-18; 13:37-38). The maximum expression of love. Jesus will demonstrate it within hours.

For his friends — Paul will say 'for the ungodly' (Rom 5:6) and 'while we were enemies' (Rom 5:10): Christ's love exceeds the max love Jesus here names. He dies not merely for friends but to make enemies friends.

14.meaning: Ye are my friends — philos = friend, one in intimate relation. Abraham was called 'friend of God' (Jas 2:23); the disciples join that noble company, but the condition remains obedience.

15.meaning: No more servants — doulos = slave, one who does without knowing the master's purposes. Jesus has unveiled the Father's counsel; intimacy replaces mere obedience.

16.meaning: I have chosen you — aorist middle: divine election precedes and enables human response. The disciples' relationship to Jesus originates in his initiative, not theirs.
Ordained you to bear fruit — tithēmi = appointed. The mission of the apostles is expressly fruit-bearing, fruit that abides (menē) — lasting results, not ephemeral gains.
Your fruit should remain — not the pyrotechnics of novelty but the enduring fruit that outlasts the harvester. Eternal, not seasonal.

18.meaning: If the world hate you — first-class condition: assume it does. miseō = active antagonism. The disciples now share the world's hatred of Jesus, a mark of authentic fellowship.

19.meaning: Ye are not of the world — disciples are in the world geographically, not of it ontologically. Their origin, loyalty, and destiny belong to another order.

20.original_word: οὐκ ἔστιν δοῦλος μείζων τοῦ κυρίου αὐτοῦ
20.transliteration: ouk estin doulos meizōn tou kyriou autou
20.meaning: Servant not greater than his lord — the disciple's treatment will match the Master's (13:16 applied to persecution). Persecution is credential, not anomaly.

22.meaning: No cloak for their sin — prophasis = pretext, excuse. Christ's arrival strips away all ignorance-pleas; his word turns latent unbelief into manifest sin.

24.meaning: Works no other man did — Jesus' signs are sui generis; no comparable miracle-worker in history. To witness them and reject him compounds guilt exponentially.

25.meaning: Hated me without a cause — quotation of Ps 35:19 / 69:4 LXX. dōrean = gratuitously, without just occasion. The rejection of Jesus is causeless in its object and lawless in its motive; Scripture foresaw both.

26.meaning: The Comforter — one called alongside (para + kaleō). Second of the Paraclete sayings (14:16, 26; 15:26; 16:7, 13). Here emphasised: sent by the Son, proceeding from the Father, bearing witness to the Son.
Proceedeth — present tense: timeless procession. The patristic filioque debate hangs partly on this verb: the Spirit proceeds from the Father (and, Western tradition adds, from the Son).
He shall testify — martyreō = bear witness. The Spirit's role is forensic: he testifies to the truth of Christ both in the disciples' proclamation and in the world's conviction.

27.meaning: From the beginning ye have been with me — the apostolic qualification (cf. Acts 1:21-22; 1 John 1:1). Eyewitness continuity from the ministry's beginning grounds apostolic authority.

meta.overview: John 16 continues and concludes the Upper Room Discourse before the High Priestly Prayer of chapter 17. Jesus begins by explaining why he has told the disciples about the world's hatred (15:18-25): so that they would not stumble when persecution comes (v.1). He predicts that they will be expelled from synagogues and even killed by those who think they are serving God (vv.1-4). He explains that his departure is to their advantage because only through his departure can the Paraclete come (v.7). The Spirit's ministry is then described in the most extensive pneumatological passage in the Upper Room Discourse: he will 'reprove the world of sin, and of righteousness, and of judgment' (v.8) — three aspects of the Spirit's convicting work. 'Of sin, because they believe not on me; of righteousness, because I go to my Father; of judgment, because the prince of this world is judged' (vv.9-11). Jesus then speaks of the Spirit of truth who 'will guide you into all truth' (v.13) and will 'shew you things to come' (v.13). The Spirit glorifies Christ by taking what is Christ's and declaring it to the disciples (vv.14-15). The disciples are puzzled by the 'little while' sayings (vv.16-19). Jesus explains with the parable of the woman in childbirth: present sorrow will become irreplaceable joy — a joy that no one will take from them (vv.20-22). He promises that prayer in his name will be answered by the Father directly — no longer through an intermediary (vv.23-27). The disciples confess their belief, but Jesus warns that they will scatter, leaving him alone — yet he is never alone because the Father is with him (vv.28-32). The chapter closes with the most triumphant verse in the Discourse: 'In the world ye shall have tribulation: but be of good cheer; I have overcome the world' (v.33).
meta.theme: The Spirit Convicts, Guides, and Glorifies — Be of Good Cheer
meta.keyWordMeaning: To convict, convince, rebuke, expose — from elegchos (proof, conviction). The Spirit's work toward the world is elegchō: he makes the case, presses the argument, brings to light what is hidden. In legal usage, elegchō is to cross-examine and refute; in moral usage, to convict of wrongdoing and bring to shame. The three dimensions of v.8 — sin, righteousness, judgment — are three courtroom verdicts the Spirit brings: (1) the world is guilty of the sin of unbelief; (2) Christ is vindicated as righteous by his resurrection/exaltation; (3) the prince of this world stands condemned. This work happens through the preaching of the gospel; the Spirit uses the proclaimed word to produce conviction leading to repentance (Acts 2:37: 'they were pricked in their heart').
outline: Warning About Persecution (vv.1-4): These things have I spoken unto you that ye should not be offended. They shall put you out of the synagogues yea the time cometh that whosoever killeth you will think that he doeth God service. And these things will they do unto you because they have not known the Father nor me. But these things have I told you that when the time shall come ye may remember that I told you of them

The Spirit's Coming (vv.5-11): But now I go my way to him that sent me and none of you asketh me Whither goest thou? But because I have said these things unto you sorrow hath filled your heart. Nevertheless I tell you the truth It is expedient for you that I go away for if I go not away the Comforter will not come unto you but if I depart I will send him unto you. And when he is come he will reprove the world of sin and of righteousness and of judgment of sin because they believe not on me of righteousness because I go to my Father and ye see me no more of judgment because the prince of this world is judged

The Spirit Guides into All Truth (vv.12-15): I have yet many things to say unto you but ye cannot bear them now. Howbeit when he the Spirit of truth is come he will guide you into all truth for he shall not speak of himself but whatsoever he shall hear that shall he speak and he will shew you things to come. He shall glorify me for he shall receive of mine and shall shew it unto you. All things that the Father hath are mine therefore said I that he shall take of mine and shall shew it unto you

Sorrow Turned to Joy (vv.16-24): A little while and ye shall not see me and again a little while and ye shall see me because I go to the Father. Then said some of his disciples among themselves What is this that he saith unto us A little while and ye shall not see me and again a little while and ye shall see me? Jesus knew that they were desirous to ask him and said unto them Do ye enquire among yourselves of that I said A little while and ye shall not see me and again a little while and ye shall see me? Verily verily I say unto you That ye shall weep and lament but the world shall rejoice and ye shall be sorrowful but your sorrow shall be turned into joy. A woman when she is in travail hath sorrow because her hour is come but as soon as she is delivered of the child she remembereth no more the anguish for joy that a man is born into the world. And ye now therefore have sorrow but I will see you again and your heart shall rejoice and your joy no man taketh from you. And in that day ye shall ask me nothing. Verily verily I say unto you Whatsoever ye shall ask the Father in my name he will give it you. Hitherto have ye asked nothing in my name ask and ye shall receive that your joy may be full

The Father's Love (vv.25-28): These things have I spoken unto you in proverbs but the time cometh when I shall no more speak unto you in proverbs but I shall shew you plainly of the Father. At that day ye shall ask in my name and I say not unto you that I will pray the Father for you for the Father himself loveth you because ye have loved me and have believed that I came out from God. I came forth from the Father and am come into the world again I leave the world and go to the Father

Victory over the World (vv.29-33): His disciples said unto him Lo now speakest thou plainly and speakest no proverb. Now are we sure that thou knowest all things and needest not that any man should ask thee by this we believe that thou camest forth from God. Jesus answered them Do ye now believe? Behold the hour cometh yea is now come that ye shall be scattered every man to his own and shall leave me alone and yet I am not alone because the Father is with me. These things I have spoken unto you that in me ye might have peace. In the world ye shall have tribulation but be of good cheer I have overcome the world

verses.kjv_text: These things have I spoken unto you, that ye should not be offended.
verses.original_text: Ταῦτα λελάληκα ὑμῖν ἵνα μὴ σκανδαλισθῆτε
verses.transliteration: Tauta lelalēka hymin hina mē skandalisthēte
verses.study_note: 'That ye should not be offended' (hina mē skandalisthēte — so that you may not be caused to stumble; skandalizō: to cause to fall, to be a stumbling block). 'Skandalon' (stumbling block, trap) gives us 'scandal.' Jesus's purpose in foretelling persecution is prophylactic: when it comes, they will remember and stand firm rather than be caught off guard and fall away. The teaching serves as an inoculation against faith-destroying shock.

verses.kjv_text: They shall put you out of the synagogues: yea, the time cometh, that whosoever killeth you will think that he doeth God service.
verses.original_text: ἀποσυναγώγους ποιήσουσιν ὑμᾶς ἀλλ᾽ ἔρχεται ὥρα ἵνα πᾶς ὁ ἀποκτείνας ὑμᾶς δόξῃ λατρείαν προσφέρειν τῷ θεῷ
verses.transliteration: aposynagōgous poiēsousin hymas all erchetai hōra hina pas ho apokteinōn hymas doxē latreian prospherein tō theō
verses.study_note: 'Whosoever killeth you will think that he doeth God service' (ho apokteinōn hymas doxē latreian prospherein tō theō — the one killing you will suppose he is offering a service/act of worship to God; latreian: religious service, worship). This was the mentality of the early persecutors — Saul of Tarsus believed he was serving God by destroying the church (Acts 8:1-3; 26:9-11; Philippians 3:6). Religious sincerity without truth is the most dangerous combination. The Pharisees' framework defined blasphemy as a capital offence deserving death (Leviticus 24:16); by their definition, Jesus and his followers were blasphemers to be eliminated.

verses.kjv_text: And these things will they do unto you, because they have not known the Father, nor me.
verses.original_text: καὶ ταῦτα ποιήσουσιν ὅτι οὐκ ἔγνωσαν τὸν πατέρα οὐδὲ ἐμέ
verses.transliteration: kai tauta poiēsousin hoti ouk egnōsan ton patera oude eme
verses.study_note: The root of religious persecution is theological ignorance: 'they have not known the Father, nor me.' True knowledge of God produces mercy, not murder; compassion, not condemnation. The persecutors do not lack religious knowledge — they have centuries of Torah study — but they lack the knowledge of God that only comes through the Son (14:6-9). This is the same diagnosis as 15:21. Zeal without knowledge is dangerous (Romans 10:2).

verses.kjv_text: But these things have I told you, that when the time shall come, ye may remember that I told you of them. And these things I said not unto you at the beginning, because I was with you.
verses.original_text: ἀλλὰ ταῦτα λελάληκα ὑμῖν ἵνα ὅταν ἔλθῃ ἡ ὥρα αὐτῶν μνημονεύητε αὐτῶν ὅτι ἐγὼ εἶπον ὑμῖν ταῦτα δὲ ὑμῖν ἐξ ἀρχῆς οὐκ εἶπον ὅτι μεθ᾽ ὑμῶν ἤμην
verses.transliteration: alla tauta lelalēka hymin hina hotan elthē hē hōra autōn mnēmoneuēte autōn hoti egō eipon hymin tauta de hymin ex archēs ouk eipon hoti meth hymōn ēmēn
verses.study_note: 'Because I was with you' (hoti meth hymōn ēmēn — because I was with you). While Jesus was physically present, his presence was their protection; the authorities focused their opposition on him rather than the disciples. Now that he is leaving, they need to be forewarned. The warnings of persecution were not given earlier because they were not yet needed — timing in teaching is as important as content.

verses.kjv_text: But now I go my way to him that sent me; and none of you asketh me, Whither goest thou?
verses.original_text: νῦν δὲ ὑπάγω πρὸς τὸν πέμψαντά με καὶ οὐδεὶς ἐξ ὑμῶν ἐρωτᾷ με Ποῦ ὑπάγεις
verses.transliteration: nyn de hypagō pros ton pempsanta me kai oudeis ex hymōn erōtā me Pou hypageis
verses.study_note: 'None of you asketh me, Whither goest thou?' Thomas asked this in 14:5 and Peter in 13:36, but those questions were self-centred (what about us?). Now, absorbed in their own sorrow (v.6), they have stopped asking about his destination. Jesus is gently pointing to their inward focus: they are grieving about themselves rather than rejoicing for him (cf. 14:28: 'If ye loved me, ye would rejoice, because I said, I go unto the Father').

verses.kjv_text: But because I have said these things unto you, sorrow hath filled your heart.
verses.original_text: ἀλλ᾽ ὅτι ταῦτα λελάληκα ὑμῖν ἡ λύπη πεπλήρωκεν ὑμῶν τὴν καρδίαν
verses.transliteration: all hoti tauta lelalēka hymin hē lypē peplērōken hymōn tēn kardian
verses.study_note: 'Sorrow hath filled your heart' (hē lypē peplērōken hymōn tēn kardian — sorrow has filled your heart; perfect: has filled and remains filled). The disciples are overwhelmed with grief. They cannot see beyond the immediate loss to the gain that will follow. This is the human limitation that Jesus is about to address: sorrow is real and valid, but it is partial sight. The full picture is much better.

verses.kjv_text: Nevertheless I tell you the truth; It is expedient for you that I go away: for if I go not away, the Comforter will not come unto you; but if I depart, I will send him unto you.
verses.original_text: ἀλλ᾽ ἐγὼ τὴν ἀλήθειαν λέγω ὑμῖν συμφέρει ὑμῖν ἵνα ἐγὼ ἀπέλθω ἐὰν γὰρ μὴ ἀπέλθω ὁ παράκλητος οὐκ ἐλεύσεται πρὸς ὑμᾶς ἐὰν δὲ πορευθῶ πέμψω αὐτὸν πρὸς ὑμᾶς
verses.transliteration: all egō tēn alētheian legō hymin sympherei hymin hina egō apelthō ean gar mē apelthō ho paraklētos ouk eleusetai pros hymas ean de poreuthō pempsō auton pros hymas
verses.study_note: 'It is expedient for you that I go away' (sympherei hymin hina egō apelthō — it is profitable/advantageous for you that I go away). This is a stunning claim: the disciples are better off with the Spirit than with the bodily-present Jesus. The Spirit is omnipresent (Jesus was locally present); the Spirit indwells (Jesus was externally with them); the Spirit is the agent of new birth (Jesus performed it but the Spirit effects it). The departure of the Incarnate Son is the condition for the universal presence of the Spirit.

verses.kjv_text: And when he is come, he will reprove the world of sin, and of righteousness, and of judgment:
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐλθὼν ἐκεῖνος ἐλέγξει τὸν κόσμον περὶ ἁμαρτίας καὶ περὶ δικαιοσύνης καὶ περὶ κρίσεως
verses.transliteration: kai elthōn ekeinos elengxei ton kosmon peri hamartias kai peri dikaiosynēs kai peri kriseōs
verses.study_note: 'He will reprove the world' (ekeinos elengxei ton kosmon — that one will convict the world; elengchō: to convict by evidence, to expose). The Spirit's work toward the world (through the disciples' witness) is prosecutorial: he brings three verdicts against the world system. This is the courtroom of history: the Spirit is both witness and advocate, presenting the case against the world's rejection of Christ. 'The world' (ton kosmon) — the world-system organised in opposition to God.

verses.kjv_text: Of sin, because they believe not on me;
verses.original_text: περὶ ἁμαρτίας μέν ὅτι οὐ πιστεύουσιν εἰς ἐμέ
verses.transliteration: peri hamartias men hoti ou pisteuousin eis eme
verses.study_note: 'Of sin, because they believe not on me' (peri hamartias hoti ou pisteuousin eis eme — concerning sin, because they do not believe into me). The supreme sin in John is not murder, adultery, or idolatry — it is unbelief in Christ. All other sins are subsumed in this fundamental refusal of the light (3:19-20). The Spirit convicts of this specific sin: not merely of moral failure but of the categorical rejection of the Son of God. This is the sin that leads to death (1 John 5:16) because it cuts off the only source of forgiveness.

verses.kjv_text: Of righteousness, because I go to my Father, and ye see me no more;
verses.original_text: περὶ δικαιοσύνης δέ ὅτι πρὸς τὸν πατέρα ὑπάγω καὶ οὐκέτι θεωρεῖτέ με
verses.transliteration: peri dikaiosynēs de hoti pros ton patera hypagō kai ouketi theōreite me
verses.study_note: 'Of righteousness, because I go to my Father' (peri dikaiosynēs hoti pros ton patera hypagō). The Spirit convicts the world concerning righteousness — specifically by the fact of Jesus's resurrection and exaltation. The world said Jesus was a blasphemer and lawbreaker; the resurrection reversed that verdict: God declared him righteous by raising him (Romans 1:4; Acts 2:32-36). The ascension to the Father is the Father's vindication of the Son. 'Ye see me no more' — though no longer visibly present, the Spirit testifies to his risen, vindicated status.

verses.kjv_text: Of judgment, because the prince of this world is judged.
verses.original_text: περὶ δὲ κρίσεως ὅτι ὁ ἄρχων τοῦ κόσμου τούτου κέκριται
verses.transliteration: peri de kriseōs hoti ho archōn tou kosmou toutou kekritai
verses.study_note: 'Because the prince of this world is judged' (hoti ho archōn tou kosmou toutou kekritai — because the ruler of this world has been judged; perfect tense: a past act with present standing). At the cross, Satan was definitively judged and disarmed (12:31; Colossians 2:15; Hebrews 2:14). The Spirit convicts of coming judgment by pointing to the already-accomplished judgment of the world's ruler. If the ruler is judged, all who remain in his domain face the same judgment. The cross is the anticipation and guarantee of the final judgment.

verses.kjv_text: I have yet many things to say unto you, but ye cannot bear them now.
verses.original_text: ἔτι πολλὰ ἔχω ὑμῖν λέγειν ἀλλ᾽ οὐ δύνασθε βαστάζειν ἄρτι
verses.transliteration: eti polla echō hymin legein all ou dynasthe bastazein arti
verses.study_note: 'Ye cannot bear them now' (ou dynasthe bastazein arti — you are not able to carry/bear them now; bastazō: to carry a weight, to endure). The disciples are spiritually incapable of receiving more revelation in their current state — grief, confusion, immaturity. The Holy Spirit will complete the disclosure. This verse is important for understanding the relationship between Jesus's teaching and the apostolic writings: the Spirit continued the teaching Jesus had begun, bringing deeper understanding of everything Jesus had said and done.

verses.kjv_text: Howbeit when he, the Spirit of truth, is come, he will guide you into all truth: for he shall not speak of himself; but whatsoever he shall hear, that shall he speak: and he will shew you things to come.
verses.original_text: ὅταν δὲ ἔλθῃ ἐκεῖνος τὸ πνεῦμα τῆς ἀληθείας ὁδηγήσει ὑμᾶς εἰς πᾶσαν τὴν ἀλήθειαν οὐ γὰρ λαλήσει ἀφ᾽ ἑαυτοῦ ἀλλ᾽ ὅσα ἂν ἀκούσῃ λαλήσει καὶ τὰ ἐρχόμενα ἀναγγελεῖ ὑμῖν
verses.transliteration: hotan de elthē ekeinos to pneuma tēs alētheias hodēgēsei hymas eis pasan tēn alētheian ou gar lalēsei aph heautou all hosa an akousē lalēsei kai ta erchomena ananggelei hymin
verses.study_note: 'He will guide you into all truth' (hodēgēsei hymas eis pasan tēn alētheian — will guide you into all the truth; hodēgeō: to lead as a guide on a journey, from hodos: road + agō: to lead). The Spirit is the Guide who leads into truth — not by replacing the disciples' reasoning but by illuminating and directing it. 'He shall not speak of himself' (ou lalēsei aph heautou — will not speak from himself) — the Spirit subordinates himself to Christ just as Christ subordinated himself to the Father (7:16-17; 14:10). 'Things to come' (ta erchomena — the coming things) — prophetic revelation, particularly fulfilled in the book of Revelation.

verses.kjv_text: He shall glorify me: for he shall receive of mine, and shall shew it unto you.
verses.original_text: ἐκεῖνος ἐμὲ δοξάσει ὅτι ἐκ τοῦ ἐμοῦ λήψεται καὶ ἀναγγελεῖ ὑμῖν
verses.transliteration: ekeinos eme doxasei hoti ek tou emou lēpsetai kai ananggelei hymin
verses.study_note: 'He shall glorify me' (ekeinos eme doxasei — that one will glorify me). The Spirit's ministry is Christocentric: he glorifies Christ, not himself. Any spirit that glorifies itself or draws attention away from Christ is not the Holy Spirit (1 John 4:2-3). 'He shall receive of mine' (ek tou emou lēpsetai — will receive from what is mine; taking from the treasure of Christ's person and work) 'and shall shew it unto you' (ananggelei hymin — will declare/announce it to you). The Spirit unpacks and applies the inexhaustible riches of Christ.

verses.kjv_text: All things that the Father hath are mine: therefore said I, that he shall take of mine, and shall shew it unto you.
verses.original_text: πάντα ὅσα ἔχει ὁ πατὴρ ἐμά ἐστιν διὰ τοῦτο εἶπον ὅτι ἐκ τοῦ ἐμοῦ λαμβάνει καὶ ἀναγγελεῖ ὑμῖν
verses.transliteration: panta hosa echei ho patēr ema estin dia touto eipon hoti ek tou emou lambanei kai ananggelei hymin
verses.study_note: 'All things that the Father hath are mine' (panta hosa echei ho patēr ema estin — all things that the Father has are mine). The complete co-equality of Father and Son: what belongs to one belongs to the other. Therefore when the Spirit 'takes of mine,' he is also taking of the Father's. The Trinitarian unity is complete: the Father's riches are the Son's; the Son's riches are what the Spirit reveals to the disciples. The believer receives the full inheritance of the triune God.

verses.kjv_text: A little while, and ye shall not see me: and again, a little while, and ye shall see me, because I go to the Father.
verses.original_text: μικρὸν καὶ οὐκέτι θεωρεῖτέ με καὶ πάλιν μικρὸν καὶ ὄψεσθέ με ὅτι ὑπάγω πρὸς τὸν πατέρα
verses.transliteration: mikron kai ouketi theōreite me kai palin mikron kai opsesthe me hoti hypagō pros ton patera
verses.study_note: 'A little while' (mikron — a short time, a little; used twice). The first 'little while' is the time between the Discourse and the crucifixion (hours). 'Ye shall not see me' — the period of the burial and disciples' absence from the cross. The second 'little while' and 'ye shall see me' likely refers to the resurrection appearances (the third day). The deeper meaning may also extend to the present age: believers do not see Jesus bodily, but will see him at his return (1 John 3:2).

verses.kjv_text: Then said some of his disciples among themselves, What is this that he saith unto us, A little while, and ye shall not see me: and again, a little while, and ye shall see me: and, Because I go to the Father?
verses.original_text: εἶπον οὖν ἐκ τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ πρὸς ἀλλήλους Τί ἐστιν τοῦτο ὃ λέγει ἡμῖν Μικρὸν καὶ οὐ θεωρεῖτέ με καὶ πάλιν μικρὸν καὶ ὄψεσθέ με καὶ ὅτι Ὑπάγω πρὸς τὸν πατέρα
verses.transliteration: eipon oun ek tōn mathētōn autou pros allēlous Ti estin touto ho legei hēmin Mikron kai ou theōreite me kai palin mikron kai opsesthe me kai hoti Hypagō pros ton patera
verses.study_note: The disciples are genuinely bewildered — they discuss it among themselves rather than asking Jesus directly (perhaps fearing another answer they won't understand). Jesus perceives their desire to ask and addresses it proactively (v.19). The repetition of his words in their question (verbatim) shows they have heard correctly but cannot interpret. Understanding comes from the Spirit (v.13), not merely from hearing.

verses.kjv_text: They said therefore, What is this that he saith, A little while? we cannot tell what he saith.
verses.original_text: ἔλεγον οὖν Τί ἐστιν τοῦτο ὃ λέγει τὸ μικρόν οὐκ οἴδαμεν τί λαλεῖ
verses.transliteration: elegon oun Ti estin touto ho legei to mikron ouk oidamen ti lalei
verses.study_note: 'We cannot tell what he saith' (ouk oidamen ti lalei — we do not know what he is saying). Honest acknowledgement of incomprehension. The disciples' confusion at this point (the night of the Last Supper) contrasts sharply with their post-resurrection understanding (Acts 2; the Epistles). The Spirit's coming makes all the difference.

verses.kjv_text: Now Jesus knew that they were desirous to ask him, and said unto them, Do ye enquire among yourselves of that I said, A little while, and ye shall not see me: and again, a little while, and ye shall see me?
verses.original_text: ἔγνω ὁ Ἰησοῦς ὅτι ἤθελον αὐτὸν ἐρωτᾶν καὶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς Περὶ τούτου ζητεῖτε μετ᾽ ἀλλήλων ὅτι εἶπον Μικρὸν καὶ οὐ θεωρεῖτέ με καὶ πάλιν μικρὸν καὶ ὄψεσθέ με
verses.transliteration: egnō ho Iēsous hoti ēthelon auton erōtan kai eipen autois Peri toutou zēteite met allēlōn hoti eipon Mikron kai ou theōreite me kai palin mikron kai opsesthe me
verses.study_note: 'Jesus knew that they were desirous to ask him' (egnō ho Iēsous hoti ēthelon auton erōtan — Jesus knew that they wanted to ask him). Another demonstration of Jesus's omniscience (cf. 1:47-48; 2:24-25; 6:64). He meets the question before it is fully formed. The pastoral care shown here — actively addressing the disciples' confusion — is characteristic of the Good Shepherd.

verses.kjv_text: Verily, verily, I say unto you, That ye shall weep and lament, but the world shall rejoice: and ye shall be sorrowful, but your sorrow shall be turned into joy.
verses.original_text: ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι κλαύσετε καὶ θρηνήσετε ὑμεῖς ὁ δὲ κόσμος χαρήσεται ὑμεῖς δὲ λυπηθήσεσθε ἀλλ᾽ ἡ λύπη ὑμῶν εἰς χαρὰν γενήσεται
verses.transliteration: amēn amēn legō hymin hoti klausete kai thrēnēsete hymeis ho de kosmos charēsetai hymeis de lypēthēsesthe all hē lypē hymōn eis charan genēsetai
verses.study_note: 'Ye shall weep and lament' (klausete kai thrēnēsete — you will weep and wail; klaiō: to weep; thrēneō: to lament loudly, wail). 'The world shall rejoice' — the crucifixion appeared to the world as victory over the troublemaker. 'Your sorrow shall be turned into joy' (hē lypē hymōn eis charan genēsetai — your grief will become joy).
