Not merely that joy will follow sorrow, but that the very sorrow will be transformed into joy — the crucifixion becomes the resurrection, the worst thing becomes the best thing.

verses.kjv_text: A woman when she is in travail hath sorrow, because her hour is come: but as soon as she is delivered of the child, she remembereth no more the anguish, for joy that a man is born into the world.
verses.original_text: ἡ γυνὴ ὅταν τίκτῃ λύπην ἔχει ὅτι ἦλθεν ἡ ὥρα αὐτῆς ὅταν δὲ γεννήσῃ τὸ παιδίον οὐκέτι μνημονεύει τῆς θλίψεως διὰ τὴν χαρὰν ὅτι ἐγεννήθη ἄνθρωπος εἰς τὸν κόσμον
verses.transliteration: hē gynē hotan tiktē lypēn echei hoti ēlthen hē hōra autēs hotan de gennēsē to paidion ouketi mnēmoneuei tēs thlipseōs dia tēn charan hoti egennēthē anthrōpos eis ton kosmon
verses.study_note: 'She remembereth no more the anguish' (ouketi mnēmoneuei tēs thlipseōs — she no longer remembers the tribulation). The birth analogy is profound: (1) the sorrow is real and intense; (2) the outcome transforms the experience retrospectively; (3) the new life that emerges is of infinitely greater value than the pain endured. The resurrection completely transforms the meaning of the crucifixion. Isaiah 66:7-8 and Micah 4:9-10 use the same imagery for the messianic deliverance.

verses.kjv_text: And ye now therefore have sorrow: but I will see you again, and your heart shall rejoice, and your joy no man taketh from you.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὑμεῖς οὖν νῦν μὲν λύπην ἔχετε πάλιν δὲ ὄψομαι ὑμᾶς καὶ χαρήσεται ὑμῶν ἡ καρδία καὶ τὴν χαρὰν ὑμῶν οὐδεὶς αἴρει ἀφ᾽ ὑμῶν
verses.transliteration: kai hymeis oun nyn men lypēn echete palin de opsomai hymas kai charēsetai hymōn hē kardia kai tēn charan hymōn oudeis airei aph hymōn
verses.study_note: 'Your joy no man taketh from you' (tēn charan hymōn oudeis airei aph hymōn — your joy no one takes from you; present tense: no one is able to take it). The resurrection joy is permanent and unassailable. No persecution, bereavement, or tribulation can remove it because it is grounded not in circumstances but in the risen, living Christ (1 Peter 1:8: 'joy unspeakable and full of glory'). The world can take many things from the believer but not the inward joy of knowing the risen Lord.

verses.kjv_text: And in that day ye shall ask me nothing. Verily, verily, I say unto you, Whatsoever ye shall ask the Father in my name, he will give it you.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐν ἐκείνῃ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ἐμὲ οὐκ ἐρωτήσετε οὐδέν ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι ὅσα ἂν αἰτήσητε τὸν πατέρα ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί μου δώσει ὑμῖν
verses.transliteration: kai en ekeinē tē hēmera eme ouk erōtēsete ouden amēn amēn legō hymin hoti hosa an aitēsēte ton patera en tō onomati mou dōsei hymin
verses.study_note: 'In that day ye shall ask me nothing' (eme ouk erōtēsete ouden — you will ask me nothing; erōtaō: to ask a question, to enquire). In that day (after the resurrection, in the age of the Spirit) the disciples will no longer need to ask clarifying questions — the Spirit will guide them (v.13). The second clause introduces a different word for 'ask': 'ask the Father' (aitēsēte — ask, request, petition). Prayer to the Father in Christ's name is the new mode of access in the post-resurrection age.

verses.kjv_text: Hitherto have ye asked nothing in my name: ask, and ye shall receive, that your joy may be full.
verses.original_text: ἕως ἄρτι οὐκ ᾐτήσατε οὐδὲν ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί μου αἰτεῖτε καὶ λήψεσθε ἵνα ἡ χαρὰ ὑμῶν ᾖ πεπληρωμένη
verses.transliteration: heōs arti ouk ētēsate ouden en tō onomati mou aiteite kai lēpsesthe hina hē chara hymōn ē peplērōmenē
verses.study_note: 'Hitherto have ye asked nothing in my name' — prayer 'in the name of Jesus' was not practiced during his earthly ministry; direct access through his name becomes available after his death, resurrection, and ascension. 'Ask, and ye shall receive, that your joy may be full' (hina hē chara hymōn ē peplērōmenē — so that your joy may be filled up; perfect passive participle: completely, permanently filled). Answered prayer and fullness of joy are inseparably connected: prayer that is in alignment with Christ's will and name will be answered, and answered prayer produces joy.

verses.kjv_text: These things have I spoken unto you in proverbs: but the time cometh, when I shall no more speak unto you in proverbs, but I shall shew you plainly of the Father.
verses.original_text: Ταῦτα ἐν παροιμίαις λελάληκα ὑμῖν ἔρχεται ὥρα ὅτε οὐκέτι ἐν παροιμίαις λαλήσω ὑμῖν ἀλλὰ παρρησίᾳ περὶ τοῦ πατρὸς ἀναγγελῶ ὑμῖν
verses.transliteration: Tauta en paroimiais lelalēka hymin erchetai hōra hote ouketi en paroimiais lalēsō hymin alla parrēsia peri tou patros ananggelō hymin
verses.study_note: 'In proverbs' (en paroimiais — in figures, allegories, proverbial sayings; paroimia: a saying at the side of the road, a dark saying). The Upper Room Discourse has used many figurative expressions: 'going to the Father,' 'the vine and branches,' 'a little while.' 'I shall shew you plainly' (parrēsia — openly, boldly, without concealment; parrēsia: free-spokenness, frankness). After Pentecost, the apostles' preaching and writings speak plainly about the Father's nature, will, and purposes.

verses.kjv_text: At that day ye shall ask in my name: and I say not unto you, that I will pray the Father for you:
verses.original_text: ἐν ἐκείνῃ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί μου αἰτήσεσθε καὶ οὐ λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι ἐγὼ ἐρωτήσω τὸν πατέρα περὶ ὑμῶν
verses.transliteration: en ekeinē tē hēmera en tō onomati mou aitēsesthe kai ou legō hymin hoti egō erōtēsō ton patera peri hymōn
verses.study_note: 'I say not unto you, that I will pray the Father for you' — this is not a denial of Christ's intercession (cf. Romans 8:34; Hebrews 7:25; 1 John 2:1) but an emphasis on the directness of access believers now have to the Father. Christ's intercession is not a separate prayer line that bypasses the Father; rather, prayer in Christ's name brings the disciples directly into the Father's presence. The mediation of Christ does not make the Father distant but accessible.

verses.kjv_text: For the Father himself loveth you, because ye have loved me, and have believed that I came out from God.
verses.original_text: αὐτὸς γὰρ ὁ πατὴρ φιλεῖ ὑμᾶς ὅτι ὑμεῖς ἐμὲ πεφιλήκατε καὶ πεπιστεύκατε ὅτι ἐγὼ παρὰ τοῦ θεοῦ ἐξῆλθον
verses.transliteration: autos gar ho patēr philei hymas hoti hymeis eme pephilēkate kai pepisteukate hoti egō para tou theou exēlthon
verses.study_note: 'The Father himself loveth you' (autos ho patēr philei hymas — the Father himself loves you; philei from phileō: warm, personal affection). The disciples need not fear that God the Father is a cold, remote deity who must be appeased by the Son's intercession. The Father himself is disposed toward the disciples in personal, warm love — the same 'friendly' love that Jesus has for Lazarus (11:3,36). This love is because they have loved the Son and believed in his divine origin.

verses.kjv_text: I came forth from the Father, and am come into the world: again, I leave the world, and go to the Father.
verses.original_text: ἐξῆλθον παρὰ τοῦ πατρὸς καὶ ἐλήλυθα εἰς τὸν κόσμον πάλιν ἀφίημι τὸν κόσμον καὶ πορεύομαι πρὸς τὸν πατέρα
verses.transliteration: exēlthon para tou patros kai elēlytha eis ton kosmon palin aphiēmi ton kosmon kai poreuomai pros ton patera
verses.study_note: Four movements in one verse: (1) 'I came forth from the Father' — pre-existence and eternal origin; (2) 'am come into the world' — the incarnation; (3) 'I leave the world' — the ascension/departure; (4) 'go to the Father' — return to the pre-incarnate glory. This is the entire arc of the Son's mission in four clauses. It is perhaps the most compact summary of Johannine Christology in the Gospel. The disciples hear this and immediately understand — the plain speech Jesus promised (v.25) is already beginning.

verses.kjv_text: His disciples said unto him, Lo, now speakest thou plainly, and speakest no proverb.
verses.original_text: λέγουσιν αὐτῷ οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ Ἴδε νῦν ἐν παρρησίᾳ λαλεῖς καὶ παροιμίαν οὐδεμίαν λέγεις
verses.transliteration: legousin autō hoi mathētai autou Ide nyn en parrēsia laleis kai paroimian oudemian legeis
verses.study_note: 'Lo, now speakest thou plainly' (Ide, nyn en parrēsia laleis — look, now you speak openly). The disciples feel they have finally understood — and they have grasped more than before. But the test of understanding will come in Gethsemane and at the cross, where their apparent comprehension will be overwhelmed by events. Understanding Christ's words about the cross is always partial until you have been through it.

verses.kjv_text: Now are we sure that thou knowest all things, and needest not that any man should ask thee: by this we believe that thou camest forth from God.
verses.original_text: νῦν οἴδαμεν ὅτι οἶδας πάντα καὶ οὐ χρείαν ἔχεις ἵνα τίς σε ἐρωτᾷ ἐν τούτῳ πιστεύομεν ὅτι ἀπὸ θεοῦ ἐξῆλθες
verses.transliteration: nyn oidamen hoti oidas panta kai ou chreian echeis hina tis se erōtā en toutō pisteuomen hoti apo theou exēlthes
verses.study_note: 'Thou knowest all things' (oidas panta — you know all things). They base their confidence on his foreknowledge — he knew their questions before they asked (v.19). 'By this we believe that thou camest forth from God' — the confession is genuine but premature in its confidence. Jesus immediately tests it (v.31).

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered them, Do ye now believe?
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη αὐτοῖς Ἰησοῦς Ἄρτι πιστεύετε
verses.transliteration: apekrithē autois Iēsous Arti pisteuete
verses.study_note: 'Do ye now believe?' (Arti pisteuete — Do you believe now?). This can be read as a gentle question or an affirmation with an implied contrast: 'you believe now — but watch what is about to happen to your faith.' The disciples' faith is real but shallow-rooted; the storm that is coming will prove its depth. Jesus does not dismiss their faith but warns that it will be severely tested.

verses.kjv_text: Behold, the hour cometh, yea, is now come, that ye shall be scattered, every man to his own, and shall leave me alone: and yet I am not alone, because the Father is with me.
verses.original_text: ἰδοὺ ἔρχεται ὥρα καὶ νῦν ἐλήλυθεν ἵνα σκορπισθῆτε ἕκαστος εἰς τὰ ἴδια κἀμὲ μόνον ἀφῆτε καὶ οὐκ εἰμὶ μόνος ὅτι ὁ πατὴρ μετ᾽ ἐμοῦ ἐστιν
verses.transliteration: idou erchetai hōra kai nyn elēlythen hina skorpisthēte hekastos eis ta idia kame monon aphēte kai ouk eimi monos hoti ho patēr met emou estin
verses.study_note: 'Scattered every man to his own' (skorpisthēte hekastos eis ta idia — you will be scattered, each one to his own things; fulfilling Zechariah 13:7: 'smite the shepherd, and the sheep shall be scattered'). 'Leave me alone' (kame monon aphēte — and leave me alone). The disciples' scattering is predicted with perfect accuracy — fulfilled in Gethsemane (Matthew 26:56; Mark 14:50). But: 'I am not alone, because the Father is with me' (ouk eimi monos hoti ho patēr met emou estin). Even in his worst moment of human abandonment, the Father is present. The cry of dereliction (Matthew 27:46) is the one moment of abandonment — even from the Father — that Jesus will bear for us.

verses.kjv_text: These things I have spoken unto you, that in me ye might have peace. In the world ye shall have tribulation: but be of good cheer; I have overcome the world.
verses.original_text: ταῦτα λελάληκα ὑμῖν ἵνα ἐν ἐμοὶ εἰρήνην ἔχητε ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ θλῖψιν ἔξετε ἀλλὰ θαρσεῖτε ἐγὼ νενίκηκα τὸν κόσμον
verses.transliteration: tauta lelalēka hymin hina en emoi eirēnēn echēte en tō kosmō thlipsin exete alla tharseite egō nenikēka ton kosmon
verses.study_note: 'In the world ye shall have tribulation' (en tō kosmō thlipsin exete — in the world you will have tribulation; thlipsis: pressure, distress, affliction — from thlibō: to press, squeeze). This is not a warning but a statement of guaranteed fact. 'But be of good cheer' (alla tharseite — but take courage, be bold; tharseo: to be courageous). The courage is grounded in a completed act: 'I have overcome the world' (egō nenikēka ton kosmon — I have overcome the world; perfect tense: a past victory with permanent standing). The cross was the victory, though it appeared defeat. 'Nenikēka' (from nikaō: to conquer, overcome). The same root as the 'overcomers' of Revelation (2:7,11,17,26; 3:5,12,21; 21:7). Believers overcome because Christ has already overcome (1 John 5:4-5; Romans 8:37).

1.meaning: That ye should not be offended — skandalizō = to cause to stumble, trap. Jesus forewarns so that coming persecution does not shipwreck faith; prophecy is pastoral prophylaxis.

2.meaning: Put you out of the synagogues — aposynagōgos (John's word: 9:22; 12:42). Excommunication cut off social, economic, and religious standing. The new community will face the old community's ban.

Doeth God service — latreia = cultic/priestly service. Persecutors will kill Christians thinking they offer worship to God — a chilling anticipation of Saul of Tarsus and every religiously-rationalised martyrdom.

4.meaning: From the beginning — Jesus has withheld these warnings until now because, while present, he absorbed the persecution himself. Now that he departs, the disciples step into the crosshairs; preparation becomes urgent.

5.meaning: None of you asketh me — despite Peter's 'whither' in 13:36 and Thomas's in 14:5, they have stopped pursuing the question. Grief has paralysed curiosity; they should be asking about destination, not only departure.

6.meaning: Sorrow hath filled your heart — lypē = grief. The disciples focus on loss rather than gain; Jesus will turn the grief inside out in v.20-22.

7.meaning: Expedient for you that I go away — sympherei = it is profitable/to advantage. The incarnation's localised presence yields, in exchange for the Spirit's universal indwelling. A staggering theology of ascension: Jesus withdrawn to the throne is more accessible than Jesus confined to Galilee.
I will send him unto you — the Son sends the Spirit (cf. 15:26). Trinitarian missio: Father and Son co-sending.

8.meaning: Reprove the world — elegchō = to convict, expose, bring to conviction (both forensic and moral). The Spirit's convicting work is prosecutorial: he brings the world into court and exposes its case as false.

8.original_word: περὶ ἁμαρτίας καὶ περὶ δικαιοσύνης καὶ περὶ κρίσεως
8.transliteration: peri hamartias kai peri dikaiosynēs kai peri kriseōs
8.meaning: Of sin, righteousness, judgment — the threefold conviction: sin defined by unbelief in Christ (v.9); righteousness defined by Christ's vindicating return to the Father (v.10); judgment defined by Satan's defeat (v.11). A Christocentric apologetic by the Spirit.

9.meaning: Of sin, because they believe not — the definitive sin is unbelief in Christ. All other sins stem from this root rupture with the sent Son.

10.meaning: Ye see me no more — theōreō = to observe with the eyes. The ascension removes physical sight; the next seeing is eschatological (1 John 3:2).

10.original_word: περὶ δικαιοσύνης ὅτι πρὸς τὸν πατέρα ὑπάγω
10.transliteration: peri dikaiosynēs hoti pros ton patera hypagō
10.meaning: Of righteousness, because I go to my Father — Christ's ascension is the Father's vindicating verdict. The one the world condemned, the Father enthroned.

11.original_word: ὁ ἄρχων τοῦ κόσμου τούτου κέκριται
11.transliteration: ho archōn tou kosmou toutou kekritai
11.meaning: Prince of this world is judged — perfect passive: has been judged and stands condemned. At the cross, Satan's verdict was entered; his ongoing activity is appeal-without-reversal.

12.meaning: Ye cannot bear them now — bastazō = to carry, endure. Pedagogical restraint: revelation is paced to capacity. Post-Pentecost they will bear more.

13.meaning: Spirit of truth... will guide — hodēgeō = to lead the way (literally 'way-lead', cf. hodos). The Spirit guides into all truth — comprehensive but Christ-centred, not novel revelation detached from Jesus.
Show you things to come — anangellō = to announce. Covers both apostolic prophecy and the eschatological content of the New Testament (esp. Revelation).

14.meaning: He shall glorify me — the Spirit's mission is Christ-glorifying, not self-promoting. Any 'spirit' that deflects from Jesus is not the Holy Spirit.
Shall take of mine — the Spirit mediates the riches of Christ to the church. Nothing the Spirit reveals originates apart from Christ; everything belongs to him and is passed on to us (v.15).

16.meaning: A little while and ye shall not see me — the two mikra: first absence (cross/burial), second presence (resurrection), with further sense of ascension-until-Parousia. Deliberate ambiguity layered with multiple horizons.

20.meaning: Ye shall weep and lament... the world shall rejoice — thrēneō = to wail a dirge. At the cross the lamentation and rejoicing invert; at the resurrection they invert again, permanently.

20.original_word: ἡ λύπη ὑμῶν εἰς χαρὰν γενήσεται
20.transliteration: hē lypē hymōn eis charan genēsetai
20.meaning: Your sorrow shall be turned into joy — not replaced, but transformed. The very wound becomes the source of joy (the scars are present yet no longer painful, 20:20, 27).

21.meaning: Woman in travail — childbirth analogy: severe pain giving way to joy that eclipses the pain. Old Testament background of Zion's birth-pangs for the messianic age (Isa 26:17; 66:7-9; Mic 5:3).

23.meaning: In that day — the post-resurrection era, opened by Easter and confirmed at Pentecost. Direct access to the Father without the earthly Jesus as intermediary.

23.original_word: αἰτήσητε τὸν πατέρα ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί μου
23.transliteration: aitēsēte ton patera en tō onomati mou
23.meaning: Ask the Father in my name — prayer addressed to the Father through the Son's mediation. New-covenant access formula, made operational by the finished work of Christ.

24.original_word: ἵνα ἡ χαρὰ ὑμῶν ᾖ πεπληρωμένη
24.transliteration: hina hē chara hymōn ē peplērōmenē
24.meaning: That your joy may be full — perfect passive participle: joy brought to completed fulness. Prayer in Jesus' name is a divinely ordained channel of saturated joy.

25.meaning: In proverbs... plainly — paroimia = figurative speech, dark saying (cf. parabolē). parrēsia = frank, bold, open speech. The post-Pentecost era will know unambiguous revelation; the current veiling gives way to clarity.

27.meaning: The Father himself loveth you — phileō (warm, affectionate love). The disciples' love for Jesus meets with the Father's own warm reciprocity; the circuit of love completes.

28.meaning: I came forth from the Father — the four-fold career: from the Father, into the world, leaving the world, returning to the Father. A compact Johannine Christology of incarnation and exaltation.

29.meaning: Now speakest thou plainly — the disciples perceive a shift to clarity, though Jesus rebuts their confidence in v.31-32: their grasp is partial; full comprehension awaits Pentecost.

32.meaning: Ye shall be scattered — fulfilment of Zech 13:7 ('smite the shepherd and the sheep shall be scattered'), quoted in Matt 26:31. Each flees to 'his own' (personal safety); the ecclesial body disintegrates in Gethsemane.

The Father is with me — the conscious filial intimacy sustaining Jesus through abandonment by disciples. Not inconsistent with the dereliction cry (Matt 27:46); that later moment reflects the uniquely borne wrath, not a rupture of essential union.

33.meaning: In me ye might have peace — the locus of eirēnē is en emoi, in union with Christ. Outside him is the world's thlipsis; inside him, the Father's peace.
Ye shall have tribulation — thlipsis = pressure, squeezing (from thlibō, to crush grapes). Persecution is promised, not possible. The Christian life includes guaranteed pressure in the world system.

I have overcome the world — perfect active: definitive, permanent victory. nikaō = to conquer (root of the Nicene 'overcomer' theology, Rev 2-3; 1 John 5:4-5). Spoken before the cross; the victory is certain in the Father's counsel.

meta.overview: John 17 is the longest recorded prayer of Jesus and one of the most profound theological documents in Scripture. Often called the 'High Priestly Prayer' (a title used by David Chytraeus in 1583), it is Jesus's prayer of consecration before the Passion — the eternal Son presenting himself and his people to the Father on the eve of the cross. It has been described as 'the most important chapter in the Bible' and 'the Holy of Holies of the New Testament.' The prayer falls into three distinct sections: (1) Jesus prays for himself (vv.1-5), asking the Father to glorify the Son so that the Son may glorify the Father, and to restore the pre-incarnate glory he had before the world was; (2) Jesus prays for the eleven disciples (vv.6-19), asking that they be kept in the Father's name, protected from the evil one, sanctified through the truth, and unified; (3) Jesus prays for all future believers (vv.20-26), asking that all who believe through the apostolic word may be one as the Father and Son are one, so that the world may believe, and that they may ultimately behold Christ's glory in eternity. The dominant themes are glory (doxa/doxazō — 8 times), sanctification/truth, unity/oneness, and the love between Father, Son, and believers. This prayer bridges heaven and earth: Jesus speaks to the Father but in the disciples' hearing ('these things I speak in the world', v.13), so that his joy may be fulfilled in them. Every believer is named in this prayer — 'neither pray I for these alone, but for them also which shall believe on me through their word' (v.20).
meta.theme: The High Priestly Prayer — Glory, Unity, and Sanctification
meta.keyWordMeaning: To sanctify, consecrate, set apart as holy — from hagios (holy). Sanctification in John 17 has both a positional and a moral dimension. Jesus consecrates himself ('I sanctify myself' v.19) — not because he needs purification but as a sacrificial offering, setting himself apart for the work of atonement. He prays that the disciples be sanctified through the truth (v.17) — set apart from the world by the transforming power of the word of God. Sanctification is the process by which believers are made holy in character (conformity to Christ) as well as position (set apart for God). The instrument is 'thy word' (ho logos — the written and living word of God). This distinguishes Christian holiness from asceticism or ritualism: it is truth-shaped, not merely practice-shaped.
outline: Jesus Prays for Himself (vv.1-5): These words spake Jesus and lifted up his eyes to heaven and said Father the hour is come glorify thy Son that thy Son also may glorify thee as thou hast given him power over all flesh that he should give eternal life to as many as thou hast given him. And this is life eternal that they might know thee the only true God and Jesus Christ whom thou hast sent. I have glorified thee on the earth I have finished the work which thou gavest me to do. And now O Father glorify thou me with thine own self with the glory which I had with thee before the world was

Jesus Prays for the Disciples (vv.6-19): I have manifested thy name unto the men which thou gavest me out of the world thine they were and thou gavest them me and they have kept thy word. Now they have known that all things whatsoever thou hast given me are of thee. For I have given unto them the words which thou gavest me and they have received them and have known surely that I came out from thee and they have believed that thou didst send me. I pray for them I pray not for the world but for them which thou hast given me for they are thine. And all mine are thine and thine are mine and I am glorified in them. And now I am no more in the world but these are in the world and I come to thee. Holy Father keep through thine own name those whom thou hast given me that they may be one as we are. While I was with them in the world I kept them in thy name those that thou gavest me I have kept and none of them is lost but the son of perdition that the scripture might be fulfilled. And now come I to thee and these things I speak in the world that they might have my joy fulfilled in themselves. I have given them thy word and the world hath hated them because they are not of the world even as I am not of the world. I pray not that thou shouldest take them out of the world but that thou shouldest keep them from the evil. They are not of the world even as I am not of the world. Sanctify them through thy truth thy word is truth. As thou hast sent me into the world even so have I also sent them into the world. And for their sakes I sanctify myself that they also might be sanctified through the truth

Jesus Prays for All Believers (vv.20-26): Neither pray I for these alone but for them also which shall believe on me through their word That they all may be one as thou Father art in me and I in thee that they also may be one in us that the world may believe that thou hast sent me. And the glory which thou gavest me I have given them that they may be one even as we are one I in them and thou in me that they may be made perfect in one and that the world may know that thou hast sent me and hast loved them as thou hast loved me. Father I will that they also whom thou hast given me be with me where I am that they may behold my glory which thou hast given me for thou lovedst me before the foundation of the world. O righteous Father the world hath not known thee but I have known thee and these have known that thou hast sent me. And I have declared unto them thy name and will declare it that the love wherewith thou hast loved me may be in them and I in them

verses.kjv_text: These words spake Jesus, and lifted up his eyes to heaven, and said, Father, the hour is come; glorify thy Son, that thy Son also may glorify thee:
verses.original_text: Ταῦτα ἐλάλησεν Ἰησοῦς καὶ ἐπάρας τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς αὐτοῦ εἰς τὸν οὐρανὸν εἶπεν Πάτερ ἐλήλυθεν ἡ ὥρα δόξασόν σου τὸν υἱόν ἵνα καὶ ὁ υἱὸς δοξάσῃ σέ
verses.transliteration: Tauta elalēsen Iēsous kai eparas tous ophthalmous autou eis ton ouranon eipen Pater elēlythen hē hōra doxason sou ton huion hina kai ho huios doxasē se
verses.study_note: 'Lifted up his eyes to heaven' (eparas tous ophthalmous autou eis ton ouranon — lifting up his eyes to heaven). This is the posture of ancient prayer — eyes open, face upward, not bowed (cf. 11:41; Luke 18:13). The high priest on the Day of Atonement lifted his voice to God on behalf of the people; Jesus now lifts his eyes on behalf of his people. 'The hour is come' (elēlythen hē hōra — the hour has come; perfect: has arrived). The hour that has been approaching throughout the Gospel (2:4; 7:30; 8:20; 12:23,27; 13:1) has now arrived. 'Glorify thy Son, that thy Son also may glorify thee' — the mutual glorification of Father and Son through the cross: the Father glorifies the Son by raising him from the dead; the Son glorifies the Father by his obedient self-offering. Glory comes through death.

verses.kjv_text: As thou hast given him power over all flesh, that he should give eternal life to as many as thou hast given him.
verses.original_text: καθὼς ἔδωκας αὐτῷ ἐξουσίαν πάσης σαρκός ἵνα πᾶν ὃ δέδωκας αὐτῷ δώσῃ αὐτοῖς ζωὴν αἰώνιον
verses.transliteration: kathōs edōkas autō exousian pasēs sarkos hina pan ho dedōkas autō dōsē autois zōēn aiōnion
verses.study_note: 'Power over all flesh' (exousian pasēs sarkos — authority over all flesh). 'Exousia' (authority, right, delegated power). The scope is universal — 'all flesh' (all humanity), yet the gift of eternal life is particular: 'as many as thou hast given him' (pan ho dedōkas autō — all [the whole] that you have given him). The universal authority serves a particular mission: giving eternal life to the elect given to the Son by the Father. Both the universal scope of Christ's authority and the particular gift of salvation to those given to him are affirmed.

verses.kjv_text: And this is life eternal, that they might know thee the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom thou hast sent.
verses.original_text: αὕτη δέ ἐστιν ἡ αἰώνιος ζωή ἵνα γινώσκωσίν σε τὸν μόνον ἀληθινὸν θεὸν καὶ ὃν ἀπέστειλας Ἰησοῦν Χριστόν
verses.transliteration: hautē de estin hē aiōnios zōē hina ginōskōsin se ton monon alēthinon theon kai hon apesteiles Iēsoun Christon
verses.study_note: 'This is life eternal, that they might know thee' (hautē estin hē aiōnios zōē hina ginōskōsin se — this is eternal life: that they know you). Eternal life is not primarily duration (living forever) but relationship (knowing God). 'Ginōskōsin' (present subjunctive: that they may keep knowing — an ongoing, progressive, deepening knowledge). This is knowledge in the Hebrew sense (yada'): intimate, experiential, relational knowing. 'The only true God' (ton monon alēthinon theon — the only genuine/authentic God) — monotheistic and exclusive. 'And Jesus Christ, whom thou hast sent' — the only way to know the only true God is through Jesus Christ. The combination of these two clauses is crucial: eternal life requires knowledge of both the Father and the Son — they cannot be separated.

verses.kjv_text: I have glorified thee on the earth: I have finished the work which thou gavest me to do.
verses.original_text: ἐγώ σε ἐδόξασα ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς τὸ ἔργον τελειώσας ὃ δέδωκάς μοι ἵνα ποιήσω
verses.transliteration: egō se edoxasa epi tēs gēs to ergon teleiōsas ho dedōkas moi hina poiēsō
verses.study_note: 'I have finished the work' (to ergon teleiōsas — having completed the work; teleiōsas from teleioō: to bring to completion, to perfect). The same root as 'tetelestai' ('It is finished', 19:30). Jesus speaks on the eve of the cross as if the work is already complete — such is the certainty of his will and the Father's plan. The three years of public ministry are complete; only the cross remains, and its completion is treated as a foregone conclusion. 'Which thou gavest me to do' — the work is the Father's assignment, faithfully executed.

verses.kjv_text: And now, O Father, glorify thou me with thine own self with the glory which I had with thee before the world was.
verses.original_text: καὶ νῦν δόξασόν με σύ πάτερ παρὰ σεαυτῷ τῇ δόξῃ ᾗ εἶχον πρὸ τοῦ τὸν κόσμον εἶναι παρὰ σοί
verses.transliteration: kai nyn doxason me sy pater para seautō tē doxē hē eichon pro tou ton kosmon einai para soi
verses.study_note: 'The glory which I had with thee before the world was' (tē doxē hē eichon pro tou ton kosmon einai para soi — the glory which I had alongside you before the world existed). One of the clearest statements of Christ's pre-existence and pre-incarnate glory in Scripture. The incarnation involved a voluntary laying aside of the visible glory ('made himself of no reputation', Philippians 2:7; 'became flesh', 1:14). Now Jesus prays for the restoration of that glory — fulfilled in the ascension and the glorified state of the risen Christ (Acts 3:13; 7:55-56; Revelation 1:13-16).

verses.kjv_text: I have manifested thy name unto the men which thou gavest me out of the world: thine they were, and thou gavest them me; and they have kept thy word.
verses.original_text: Ἐφανέρωσά σου τὸ ὄνομα τοῖς ἀνθρώποις οὓς ἔδωκάς μοι ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου σοὶ ἦσαν καὶ ἐμοὶ αὐτοὺς ἔδωκας καὶ τὸν λόγον σου τετήρηκαν
verses.transliteration: Ephanerōsa sou to onoma tois anthrōpois hous edōkas moi ek tou kosmou soi ēsan kai emoi autous edōkas kai ton logon sou tetērēkan
verses.study_note: 'I have manifested thy name' (ephanerōsa sou to onoma — I revealed/made visible your name). 'Name' (onoma) — not merely a verbal label but the character and nature of God as expressed through Jesus's life, teaching, and miracles. 'Thine they were, and thou gavest them me' (soi ēsan kai emoi autous edōkas — they were yours and you gave them to me) — the sovereignty of the Father's gift: the disciples belong first to the Father, then are given to the Son. Election is the Father's act; discipleship is the Son's keeping.

verses.kjv_text: Now they have known that all things whatsoever thou hast given me are of thee.
verses.original_text: νῦν ἐγνώκασιν ὅτι πάντα ὅσα δέδωκάς μοι παρὰ σοῦ ἐστιν
verses.transliteration: nyn egnōkasin hoti panta hosa dedōkas moi para sou estin
verses.study_note: 'Now they have known' (nyn egnōkasin — now they have come to know; perfect: the knowledge has arrived and stands). The disciples' understanding has progressively deepened through three years with Jesus. By the eve of the Passion, they have come to know that everything Jesus has — his words, his works, his authority, his nature — all comes from the Father. This is the Trinitarian insight at the heart of discipleship.

verses.kjv_text: For I have given unto them the words which thou gavest me; and they have received them, and have known surely that I came out from thee, and they have believed that thou didst send me.
verses.original_text: ὅτι τὰ ῥήματα ἃ ἔδωκάς μοι δέδωκα αὐτοῖς καὶ αὐτοὶ ἔλαβον καὶ ἔγνωσαν ἀληθῶς ὅτι παρὰ σοῦ ἐξῆλθον καὶ ἐπίστευσαν ὅτι σύ με ἀπέστειλας
verses.transliteration: hoti ta rhēmata ha edōkas moi dedōka autois kai autoi elabon kai egnōsan alēthōs hoti para sou exēlthon kai episteusan hoti sy me apesteiles
verses.study_note: 'I have given unto them the words which thou gavest me' (ta rhēmata ha edōkas moi dedōka autois — the words which you gave me I have given them). The chain of revelation: Father → Son → disciples. 'They have received them' (autoi elabon — they themselves received them; emphatic: as distinct from those who rejected them). 'Known surely' (egnōsan alēthōs — knew truly, genuinely). 'Believed that thou didst send me' — faith in the divine mission of the Son is the mark of true discipleship.

verses.kjv_text: I pray for them: I pray not for the world, but for them which thou hast given me; for they are thine.
verses.original_text: ἐγὼ περὶ αὐτῶν ἐρωτῶ οὐ περὶ τοῦ κόσμου ἐρωτῶ ἀλλὰ περὶ ὧν δέδωκάς μοι ὅτι σοί εἰσιν
verses.transliteration: egō peri autōn erōtō ou peri tou kosmou erōtō alla peri hōn dedōkas moi hoti soi eisin
verses.study_note: 'I pray not for the world' (ou peri tou kosmou erōtō — I do not pray for the world). This is not indifference to the world (3:16 shows God's love for the world) but a focused intercession: in this prayer, Jesus prays specifically for his own. He prays elsewhere for the salvation of the world implicitly (v.21: 'that the world may believe'); here the immediate concern is for those already given to him. The High Priest's intercession is particularly for his people.

verses.kjv_text: And all mine are thine, and thine are mine; and I am glorified in them.
verses.original_text: καὶ τὰ ἐμὰ πάντα σά ἐστιν καὶ τὰ σὰ ἐμά καὶ δεδόξασμαι ἐν αὐτοῖς
verses.transliteration: kai ta ema panta sa estin kai ta sa ema kai dedoxasmai en autois
verses.study_note: 'All mine are thine, and thine are mine' (ta ema panta sa estin kai ta sa ema) — the complete mutual possession of Father and Son. Nothing the Son has is his alone; nothing the Father has excludes the Son. The disciples are thus simultaneously the Father's possession and the Son's. 'I am glorified in them' (dedoxasmai en autois — I have been glorified in them; perfect: the glorification is a present, standing reality). The disciples, imperfect as they are, are the medium through which Christ's glory is expressed in the world.

verses.kjv_text: And now I am no more in the world, but these are in the world, and I come to thee. Holy Father, keep through thine own name those whom thou hast given me, that they may be one, as we are.
verses.original_text: καὶ οὐκέτι εἰμὶ ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ καὶ οὗτοι ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ εἰσίν καὶ ἐγὼ πρὸς σὲ ἔρχομαι Πάτερ ἅγιε τήρησον αὐτοὺς ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί σου ᾧ δέδωκάς μοι ἵνα ὦσιν ἓν καθὼς ἡμεῖς
verses.transliteration: kai ouketi eimi en tō kosmō kai houtoi en tō kosmō eisin kai egō pros se erchomai Pater hagie tērēson autous en tō onomati sou hō dedōkas moi hina ōsin hen kathōs hēmeis
verses.study_note: 'Holy Father' (Pater hagie — Holy Father) — a unique address in the NT; 'Father' expresses intimacy, 'Holy' expresses the divine character that is the ground of the keeping. 'Keep through thine own name' (tērēson autous en tō onomati sou — keep them in your name). The keeping is by the power and character of God himself — his name is his nature. 'That they may be one, as we are' (hina ōsin hen kathōs hēmeis — so that they may be one just as we are). The unity of the disciples is modelled on the unity of the Trinity — not uniformity but profound oneness of will, love, and purpose.

verses.kjv_text: While I was with them in the world, I kept them in thy name: those that thou gavest me I have kept, and none of them is lost, but the son of perdition; that the scripture might be fulfilled.
verses.original_text: ὅτε ἤμην μετ᾽ αὐτῶν ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ ἐγὼ ἐτήρουν αὐτοὺς ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί σου οὓς δέδωκάς μοι ἐφύλαξα καὶ οὐδεὶς ἐξ αὐτῶν ἀπώλετο εἰ μὴ ὁ υἱὸς τῆς ἀπωλείας ἵνα ἡ γραφὴ πληρωθῇ
verses.transliteration: hote ēmēn met autōn en tō kosmō egō etēroun autous en tō onomati sou hous dedōkas moi ephylaxa kai oudeis ex autōn apōleto ei mē ho huios tēs apōleias hina hē graphē plērōthē
verses.study_note: 'None of them is lost, but the son of perdition' (oudeis ex autōn apōleto ei mē ho huios tēs apōleias — none of them perished except the son of perdition). 'Son of perdition' (ho huios tēs apōleias — the son of destruction/ruin; the same phrase is used of the Antichrist in 2 Thessalonians 2:3). 'Apōleia' (destruction, perdition) is from apollymi — the same verb used throughout John for 'perishing' (3:16; 6:39; 10:28; 17:12). The exception of Judas is stated plainly but 'that the scripture might be fulfilled' (hina hē graphē plērōthē) — his perdition was predicted, not caused, by divine foreknowledge (Psalm 41:9; 109:8).

verses.kjv_text: And now come I to thee; and these things I speak in the world, that they might have my joy fulfilled in themselves.
verses.original_text: νῦν δὲ πρὸς σὲ ἔρχομαι καὶ ταῦτα λαλῶ ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ ἵνα ἔχωσιν τὴν χαρὰν τὴν ἐμὴν πεπληρωμένην ἐν ἑαυτοῖς
verses.transliteration: nyn de pros se erchomai kai tauta lalō en tō kosmō hina echōsin tēn charan tēn emēn peplērōmenēn en heautois
verses.study_note: 'I speak in the world' (lalō en tō kosmō) — Jesus prays audibly, in the presence of the disciples. He could have prayed silently; he speaks aloud so that the disciples overhear and are strengthened. 'My joy fulfilled in themselves' (tēn charan tēn emēn peplērōmenēn — my joy, having been filled up in them; the perfect passive participle indicates completeness). The purpose of the prayer is the disciples' joy — the Son's prayer for the disciples is pastoral as well as priestly.

verses.kjv_text: I have given them thy word; and the world hath hated them, because they are not of the world, even as I am not of the world.
verses.original_text: ἐγὼ δέδωκα αὐτοῖς τὸν λόγον σου καὶ ὁ κόσμος ἐμίσησεν αὐτούς ὅτι οὐκ εἰσὶν ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου καθὼς ἐγὼ οὐκ εἰμὶ ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου
verses.transliteration: egō dedōka autois ton logon sou kai ho kosmos emisēsen autous hoti ouk eisin ek tou kosmou kathōs egō ouk eimi ek tou kosmou
verses.study_note: 'I have given them thy word' (dedōka autois ton logon sou — I have given them your word; perfect: the giving is complete and the word remains with them). The word of God is the source of the disciples' non-conformity to the world, and therefore the cause of the world's hatred. 'They are not of the world, even as I am not of the world' — the disciples' 'otherness' is not their own achievement but a reflection of their union with Christ.

verses.kjv_text: I pray not that thou shouldest take them out of the world, but that thou shouldest keep them from the evil.
verses.original_text: οὐκ ἐρωτῶ ἵνα ἄρῃς αὐτοὺς ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου ἀλλ᾽ ἵνα τηρήσῃς αὐτοὺς ἐκ τοῦ πονηροῦ
verses.transliteration: ouk erōtō hina arēs autous ek tou kosmou all hina tērēsēs autous ek tou ponērou
verses.study_note: 'Not that thou shouldest take them out of the world' (ouk erōtō hina arēs autous ek tou kosmou — I do not ask that you take them out of the world). The Christian calling is not withdrawal from the world (cf. 1 Corinthians 5:10) but engagement with it. The world is the mission field. 'But that thou shouldest keep them from the evil' (alla hina tērēsēs autous ek tou ponērou — but that you keep them from the evil one; ho ponēros: the evil one = Satan, cf. 1 John 2:13-14; 3:12; 5:18-19; Matthew 6:13). Protection from the evil one is the content of the petition — not immunity from suffering but preservation from Satanic assault on their faith.

verses.kjv_text: They are not of the world, even as I am not of the world.
verses.original_text: ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου οὐκ εἰσὶν καθὼς ἐγὼ ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου οὐκ εἰμί
verses.transliteration: ek tou kosmou ouk eisin kathōs egō ek tou kosmou ouk eimi
verses.study_note: Repetition of v.14b — emphasis by reiteration. The disciples' non-worldliness is not a matter of temperament or choice but of nature: they are born from above (3:3), children of God (1:12-13), citizens of heaven (Philippians 3:20). Their primary identity is not in any earthly category but in Christ.

verses.kjv_text: Sanctify them through thy truth: thy word is truth.
verses.original_text: ἁγίασον αὐτοὺς ἐν τῇ ἀληθείᾳ ὁ λόγος ὁ σὸς ἀλήθειά ἐστιν
verses.transliteration: hagiason autous en tē alētheia ho logos ho sos alētheia estin
verses.study_note: 'Sanctify them through thy truth' (hagiason autous en tē alētheia — consecrate/set apart them in the truth). 'Hagiazō' (to make holy, set apart): the disciples are to be consecrated for mission, set apart from the world by the truth. 'Thy word is truth' (ho logos ho sos alētheia estin — your word is truth; not merely contains truth but IS truth). This is a profound epistemological claim: the word of God is the standard and source of all truth. Sanctification is truth-mediated: the word of God renews the mind (Romans 12:2), cleanses (15:3; Ephesians 5:26), and sets apart. The instrument of sanctification is Scripture.

verses.kjv_text: As thou hast sent me into the world, even so have I also sent them into the world.
verses.original_text: καθὼς ἐμὲ ἀπέστειλας εἰς τὸν κόσμον κἀγὼ ἀπέστειλα αὐτοὺς εἰς τὸν κόσμον
verses.transliteration: kathōs eme apesteiles eis ton kosmon kagō apesteila autous eis ton kosmon
verses.study_note: 'As thou hast sent me... even so have I also sent them' (kathōs eme apesteiles... kagō apesteila autous — just as you sent me... I also sent them). The mission of the church is modelled on and grounded in the mission of the Son. The disciples are 'apostles' (sent ones) as Jesus is the supreme Apostle (Hebrews 3:1). This verse is the missional basis for John's version of the Great Commission (20:21). The sending-by-the-Father authorises the sending-by-the-Son.

verses.kjv_text: And for their sakes I sanctify myself, that they also might be sanctified through the truth.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὑπὲρ αὐτῶν ἐγὼ ἁγιάζω ἐμαυτόν ἵνα ὦσιν καὶ αὐτοὶ ἡγιασμένοι ἐν ἀληθείᾳ
verses.transliteration: kai hyper autōn egō hagiazō emauton hina ōsin kai autoi hēgiasmenoi en alētheia
verses.study_note: 'I sanctify myself' (egō hagiazō emauton — I consecrate/set apart myself). Jesus does not sanctify himself because he is unholy — he is absolutely holy. He consecrates himself as a sacrificial offering: as the priest set apart the sacrifice for the altar, Jesus sets himself apart for the cross. 'For their sakes' (hyper autōn — on their behalf). 'That they also might be sanctified through the truth' (hina ōsin kai autoi hēgiasmenoi en alētheia — so that they also may be sanctified in truth; perfect passive participle: completely and permanently set apart). The cross of Christ is the foundation of the sanctification of his people.

verses.kjv_text: Neither pray I for these alone, but for them also which shall believe on me through their word;
verses.original_text: Οὐ περὶ τούτων δὲ ἐρωτῶ μόνον ἀλλὰ καὶ περὶ τῶν πιστευόντων διὰ τοῦ λόγου αὐτῶν εἰς ἐμέ
verses.transliteration: Ou peri toutōn de erōtō monon alla kai peri tōn pisteuontōn dia tou logou autōn eis eme
verses.study_note: 'For them also which shall believe on me through their word' (peri tōn pisteuontōn dia tou logou autōn eis eme — concerning those believing through their word into me; the present participle in a future sense: those who will believe). This verse is the most personal verse in the Bible for every Christian: Jesus names you in his prayer. The prayer of John 17:20-26 is prayed for every person who will ever come to faith through the apostolic gospel. Jesus prayed for you before you existed.

verses.kjv_text: That they all may be one; as thou, Father, art in me, and I in thee, that they also may be one in us: that the world may believe that thou hast sent me.
verses.original_text: ἵνα πάντες ἓν ὦσιν καθὼς σύ πάτερ ἐν ἐμοὶ κἀγὼ ἐν σοί ἵνα καὶ αὐτοὶ ἐν ἡμῖν ἓν ὦσιν ἵνα ὁ κόσμος πιστεύῃ ὅτι σύ με ἀπέστειλας
verses.transliteration: hina pantes hen ōsin kathōs sy pater en emoi kagō en soi hina kai autoi en hēmin hen ōsin hina ho kosmos pisteuē hoti sy me apesteiles
verses.study_note: 'That they all may be one' (hina pantes hen ōsin — so that all may be one). The scope is universal: all believers of all times and places. 'One' (hen — neuter: one thing, one reality; not heis/mia: one person). The unity is not organisational uniformity but spiritual oneness — the same kind of oneness that exists between Father and Son. 'That the world may believe' (hina ho kosmos pisteuē) — the unity of the church is the apologetic argument for the truth of the gospel. A divided, unloving church denies what it proclaims; a unified, loving church validates it.

verses.kjv_text: And the glory which thou gavest me I have given them; that they may be one, even as we are one:
verses.original_text: κἀγὼ τὴν δόξαν ἣν δέδωκάς μοι δέδωκα αὐτοῖς ἵνα ὦσιν ἓν καθὼς ἡμεῖς ἕν ἐσμεν
verses.transliteration: kagō tēn doxan hēn dedōkas moi dedōka autois hina ōsin hen kathōs hēmeis hen esmen
verses.study_note: 'The glory which thou gavest me I have given them' (tēn doxan hēn dedōkas moi dedōka autois — the glory which you have given me I have given them; both perfects: given and remain given). The doxa (glory) given to the disciples is the Spirit-given participation in the divine nature (2 Peter 1:4), the indwelling of Christ (Colossians 1:27: 'Christ in you, the hope of glory'), the transforming presence that makes them one. Glory is not a future possession only but a present reality that produces present unity.

verses.kjv_text: I in them, and thou in me, that they may be made perfect in one; and that the world may know that thou hast sent me, and hast loved them, as thou hast loved me.
verses.original_text: ἐγὼ ἐν αὐτοῖς καὶ σὺ ἐν ἐμοί ἵνα ὦσιν τετελειωμένοι εἰς ἕν καὶ ἵνα γινώσκῃ ὁ κόσμος ὅτι σύ με ἀπέστειλας καὶ ἠγάπησας αὐτοὺς καθὼς ἐμὲ ἠγάπησας
verses.transliteration: egō en autois kai sy en emoi hina ōsin teteleōmenoi eis hen kai hina ginōskē ho kosmos hoti sy me apesteiles kai ēgapēsas autous kathōs eme ēgapēsas
verses.study_note: 'Hast loved them, as thou hast loved me' (ēgapēsas autous kathōs eme ēgapēsas — you have loved them just as you loved me). The staggering claim: the Father's love for individual believers is the same in quality as his love for the eternal Son. This does not mean the Father's love for us equals his love for the Son in degree (the Son is the eternal image of the invisible God); but it means the same agapē that binds Father and Son in eternal communion is extended to us through union with the Son (Romans 8:38-39; Ephesians 1:4-6).

verses.kjv_text: Father, I will that they also, whom thou hast given me, be with me where I am; that they may behold my glory, which thou hast given me: for thou lovedst me before the foundation of the world.
verses.original_text: Πάτερ ὃ δέδωκάς μοι θέλω ἵνα ὅπου εἰμὶ ἐγὼ κἀκεῖνοι ὦσιν μετ᾽ ἐμοῦ ἵνα θεωρῶσιν τὴν δόξαν τὴν ἐμήν ἣν δέδωκάς μοι ὅτι ἠγάπησάς με πρὸ καταβολῆς κόσμου
verses.transliteration: Pater ho dedōkas moi thelō hina hopou eimi egō kakeinoi ōsin met emou hina theōrōsin tēn doxan tēn emēn hēn dedōkas moi hoti ēgapēsas me pro katabolēs kosmou
verses.study_note: 'Father, I will' (Pater, thelō — Father, I wish/I will; thelō expresses desire and will). Jesus does not say 'I ask' (erōtō) here but 'I will' — the language of co-equal will with the Father. 'That they may behold my glory' (hina theōrōsin tēn doxan tēn emēn — so that they may behold/gaze upon my glory; theōreō: sustained, contemplative seeing). The ultimate purpose of salvation is the beholding and participation in Christ's glory — not merely forgiveness but glorification (Romans 8:30; 1 John 3:2). 'Before the foundation of the world' (pro katabolēs kosmou — before the laying down/founding of the world) — the love of the Father for the Son precedes creation, is eternal.

verses.kjv_text: O righteous Father, the world hath not known thee: but I have known thee, and these have known that thou hast sent me.
verses.original_text: Πάτερ δίκαιε καὶ ὁ κόσμος σε οὐκ ἔγνω ἐγὼ δέ σε ἔγνων καὶ οὗτοι ἔγνωσαν ὅτι σύ με ἀπέστειλας
verses.transliteration: Pater dikaie kai ho kosmos se ouk egnō egō de se egnōn kai houtoi egnōsan hoti sy me apesteiles
verses.study_note: 'O righteous Father' (Pater dikaie — Righteous Father). The address appeals to the Father's righteousness/justice as the ground for what follows — it is right that those who know the Son should receive the Father's love. 'The world hath not known thee' (ho kosmos se ouk egnō — the world did not know you; aorist: at the time of Christ's coming, the world as a whole had not known God). The three-way distinction: the world (does not know the Father), Jesus (has known the Father perfectly), the disciples (have known that the Father sent the Son).

verses.kjv_text: And I have declared unto them thy name, and will declare it: that the love wherewith thou hast loved me may be in them, and I in them.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐγνώρισα αὐτοῖς τὸ ὄνομά σου καὶ γνωρίσω ἵνα ἡ ἀγάπη ἣν ἠγάπησάς με ἐν αὐτοῖς ᾖ κἀγὼ ἐν αὐτοῖς
verses.transliteration: kai egnōrisa autois to onoma sou kai gnōrisō hina hē agapē hēn ēgapēsas me en autois ē kagō en autois
verses.study_note: 'I have declared... and will declare' (egnōrisa... kai gnōrisō — I made known and will make known; past and future: the revelation of the Father's name is both complete in the incarnation and continuing through the Spirit). The final clause of the High Priestly Prayer — the goal of everything: 'that the love wherewith thou hast loved me may be in them, and I in them' (hina hē agapē hēn ēgapēsas me en autois ē kagō en autois). The destination of the prayer is the indwelling of divine love and the indwelling of Christ himself in every believer. The Trinity's eternal love becomes the interior life of the redeemed. This is the telos of salvation: not merely pardon but participation — the love of the Father for the Son becomes the love circulating in the community of believers through the indwelling Christ.

1.meaning: Hour — in John, the appointed hour of the passion, glorification, and return to the Father (cf. 2:4; 7:30; 12:23; 13:1). The whole Gospel has been driving toward this hour.
Glorify (aorist imperative of doxazō) — the prayer that opens the High Priestly Prayer; mutual glorification of Father and Son through the cross.
Son — the relational title central to the prayer: Son speaks to Father.

2.meaning: Authority — delegated sovereign right over all flesh (pasēs sarkos); echoes Daniel 7:14.
Eternal life — in John, qualitative life of the age to come, possessed now by the believer (cf. v.3 definition).

3.meaning: May know (present subjunctive) — ongoing, experiential, relational knowing; John's definition of eternal life.
Only true — exclusive monotheism; alēthinos = real/genuine as opposed to false or shadowy.
You sent (aorist of apostellō) — the sending formula that runs through John (4:34; 5:24; 20:21).

4.meaning: I glorified (aorist) — Jesus speaks of the earthly mission as already accomplished; proleptic perfect tense of completion.
Having finished/completed — cognate to tetelestai (19:30); the work (ergon) is brought to its telos.

5.meaning: Before the world existed — pre-existent glory with the Father; affirms eternal personal pre-existence (cf. 1:1; Phil 2:6).

6.meaning: I manifested/revealed — made the Father's name (character, nature) visible through incarnation and ministry.
Name — in Hebrew idiom, the self-revelation of God; Jesus reveals the Father's very identity.
They have kept (perfect) — abiding obedience; tēreō is the prayer's key verb (vv.11, 12, 15).

8.meaning: Words/sayings — the spoken utterances Jesus has given; the disciples have received them.

9.meaning: I ask/pray — the intercessory request; specifically for those given to him, not for the world at this point.

11.original_word: τήρησον αὐτοὺς ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί σου
11.transliteration: tērēson autous en tō onomati sou
11.meaning: Keep them in your name — guard, watch over, preserve within the sphere of the Father's revealed character.
One — the prayer for unity (hina ōsin hen, that they may be one) modeled on Father-Son oneness.

12.meaning: I was keeping (imperfect) — continuous guarding during the earthly ministry.
I guarded (aorist of phylassō) — watchful protection; paired with tēreō.
Son of perdition/destruction — Semitic idiom for one belonging to ruin; applied to Judas here and to antichrist in 2 Thess 2:3.

13.meaning: Joy — the fullness of messianic joy imparted to the disciples even as Jesus departs.

14.meaning: World — the fallen order in opposition to God; disciples are in it but not of it.

15.meaning: The evil one — personal evil, the devil (cf. Matt 6:13; 1 John 5:19); Jesus does not pray for removal but for protection.

17.meaning: Sanctify (aorist imperative of hagiazō) — set apart, consecrate; same root as hagios (holy).
Truth — the sphere and instrument of sanctification; in John, truth is personal (14:6) and verbal (your word is truth).
Word — the Father's revelation; the sanctifying agent.

18.meaning: I sent (aorist) — the apostolic commission prefigured; as the Father sent the Son, the Son sends them.

19.meaning: I sanctify myself — Jesus consecrates himself as the priestly sacrifice; OT cultic language applied to the cross.
Having been sanctified (perfect passive) — result state; disciples are set apart in truth through Christ's self-consecration.

20.meaning: Through their word — future believers will come to faith through the apostolic testimony; Jesus prays for the whole church.

21.meaning: May be one — subjunctive; the prayer's central petition for ecclesial unity grounded in Trinitarian unity.
In us — believers' oneness is participation in the Father-Son communion, not mere social unity.

22.meaning: The glory you have given me (perfect) — the glory of sonship shared with believers as enabling ground of unity.

23.meaning: Perfected into one (perfect passive) — brought to completion in unity; telic consummation of the church.
You loved (aorist) — the Father's love for believers is identical in kind with his love for the Son.

24.meaning: I will — not erōtō (ask) but thelō (will); co-equal volitional language of Son with Father.
May behold (present subjunctive of theōreō) — sustained, contemplative seeing; the beatific vision of glory.
Before the foundation of the world — eternal intra-Trinitarian love preceding creation (cf. 1 Pet 1:20; Eph 1:4).

25.meaning: Righteous Father — appeal to the Father's justice as ground for the final petition; unique in NT.

26.meaning: I made known — revealed, disclosed the Father's name through the incarnation.
I will make known (future) — continuing revelation of the Father through the Spirit and the church.
That the love — final purpose clause; Father's love for Son becomes interior life of believers.

meta.overview: John 18 opens the Passion narrative proper. After the High Priestly Prayer (ch.17), Jesus and the disciples cross the Kidron Valley to a garden (Gethsemane, named only in the Synoptics). There Judas comes with a cohort of Roman soldiers and Jewish officers to arrest him. Jesus steps forward — 'I am he' (egō eimi) — and the soldiers 'went backward, and fell to the ground' (v.6), a display of sovereign power that proves Jesus is not a victim but a willing sacrifice. He negotiates the release of the disciples ('that the saying might be fulfilled... of them which thou gavest me have I lost none,' vv.8-9). Peter draws his sword and cuts off Malchus's ear; Jesus rebukes him (vv.10-11). Jesus is led first to Annas (the former high priest and father-in-law of Caiaphas, vv.12-14), then to Caiaphas (mentioned briefly, v.24), then to the Roman Praetorium and Pilate (vv.28-40). John's account of the Jewish trial is abbreviated; his account of the Roman trial is the most detailed in any Gospel. Peter's three denials are interwoven with the Jewish trial (vv.15-18, 25-27) — three questions, three denials, one crowing cock, just as Jesus predicted. Before Pilate, the great exchange on the nature of kingship and truth takes place: 'My kingdom is not of this world' (v.36); 'To this end was I born, and for this cause came I into the world, that I should bear witness unto the truth' (v.37); 'What is truth?' (v.38). Pilate finds no fault in him but offers Barabbas and Jesus to the crowd — the crowd chooses Barabbas (v.40).
meta.theme: The Sovereign Prisoner — Jesus in Control of His Own Arrest and Trial
meta.keyWordMeaning: Kingdom, reign, royal dominion — from basileus (king). The central theme of the Pilate trial (vv.33-38) is the nature of Jesus's kingdom. 'Art thou the King of the Jews?' (v.33); 'My kingdom is not of this world' (v.36); 'Thou sayest that I am a king' (v.37). John's Passion narrative is pervasively royal: Jesus enters Jerusalem as king (12:13-15), is arrested like a sovereign granting terms (vv.4-9), is mocked with a crown of thorns and a purple robe (19:2-3), is crucified with the title 'The King of the Jews' (19:19-22), and reigns from the cross. The paradox of the Gospel is that the 'King of kings' is crucified — and in the crucifixion reveals the nature of his kingdom: not coercive power but self-giving truth and love.
outline: The Arrest in the Garden (vv.1-11): When Jesus had spoken these words he went forth with his disciples over the brook Cedron where was a garden into the which he entered and his disciples. And Judas also which betrayed him knew the place for Jesus ofttimes resorted thither with his disciples. Judas then having received a band of men and officers from the chief priests and Pharisees cometh thither with lanterns and torches and weapons. Jesus therefore knowing all things that should come upon him went forth and said unto them Whom seek ye? They answered him Jesus of Nazareth. Jesus saith unto them I am he and Judas also which betrayed him stood with them. As soon then as he had said unto them I am he they went backward and fell to the ground. Then asked he them again Whom seek ye? and they said Jesus of Nazareth. Jesus answered I have told you that I am he if therefore ye seek me let these go their way. That the saying might be fulfilled which he spake Of them which thou gavest me have I lost none. Then Simon Peter having a sword drew it and smote the high priest's servant and cut off his right ear. The servant's name was Malchus. Then said Jesus unto Peter Put up thy sword into the sheath the cup which my Father hath given me shall I not drink it?

Before Annas and Peter's Denial (vv.12-27): Then the band and the captain and officers of the Jews took Jesus and bound him and led him away to Annas first for he was father in law to Caiaphas which was the high priest that same year. Now Caiaphas was he which gave counsel to the Jews that it was expedient that one man should die for the people. And Simon Peter followed Jesus and so did another disciple that disciple was known unto the high priest and went in with Jesus into the palace of the high priest. But Peter stood at the door without. Then went out that other disciple which was known unto the high priest and spake unto her that kept the door and brought in Peter. Then saith the damsel that kept the door unto Peter Art not thou also one of this man's disciples? He saith I am not. And the servants and officers stood there who had made a fire of coals for it was cold and they warmed themselves and Peter stood with them and warmed himself. The high priest then asked Jesus of his disciples and of his doctrine. Jesus answered him I spake openly to the world I ever taught in the synagogue and in the temple whither the Jews always resort and in secret have I said nothing. Why askest thou me? ask them which heard me what I have said unto them behold they know what I said. And when he had thus spoken one of the officers which stood by struck Jesus with the palm of his hand saying Answerest thou the high priest so? Jesus answered him If I have spoken evil bear witness of the evil but if well why smitest thou me? Now Annas had sent him bound unto Caiaphas the high priest. And Simon Peter stood and warmed himself. They said therefore unto him Art not thou also one of his disciples? He denied it and said I am not. One of the servants of the high priest being his kinsman whose ear Peter cut off saith Did not I see thee in the garden with him? Peter then denied again and immediately the cock crew

The Trial Before Pilate (vv.28-40): Then led they Jesus from Caiaphas unto the hall of judgment and it was early and they themselves went not into the judgment hall lest they should be defiled but that they might eat the passover. Pilate then went out unto them and said What accusation bring ye against this man? They answered and said unto him If he were not a malefactor we would not have delivered him up unto thee. Then said Pilate unto them Take ye him and judge him according to your law. The Jews therefore said unto him It is not lawful for us to put any man to death that the saying of Jesus might be fulfilled which he spake signifying what death he should die. Then Pilate entered into the judgment hall again and called Jesus and said unto him Art thou the King of the Jews? Jesus answered him Sayest thou this thing of thyself or did others tell it thee of me? Pilate answered Am I a Jew? Thine own nation and the chief priests have delivered thee unto me what hast thou done? Jesus answered My kingdom is not of this world if my kingdom were of this world then would my servants fight that I should not be delivered to the Jews but now is my kingdom not from hence. Pilate therefore said unto him Art thou a king then? Jesus answered Thou sayest that I am a king. To this end was I born and for this cause came I into the world that I should bear witness unto the truth. Every one that is of the truth heareth my voice. Pilate saith unto him What is truth? And when he had said this he went out again unto the Jews and saith unto them I find in him no fault at all. But ye have a custom that I should release unto you one at the passover will ye therefore that I release unto you the King of the Jews? Then cried they all again saying Not this man but Barabbas. Now Barabbas was a robber

verses.kjv_text: When Jesus had spoken these words, he went forth with his disciples over the brook Cedron, where was a garden, into the which he entered, and his disciples.
verses.original_text: Ταῦτα εἰπὼν Ἰησοῦς ἐξῆλθεν σὺν τοῖς μαθηταῖς αὐτοῦ πέραν τοῦ χειμάρρου τῶν Κέδρων ὅπου ἦν κῆπος εἰς ὃν εἰσῆλθεν αὐτὸς καὶ οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: Tauta eipōn Iēsous exēlthen syn tois mathētais autou peran tou cheimarrou tōn Kedrōn hopou ēn kēpos eis hon eisēlthen autos kai hoi mathētai autou
verses.study_note: 'The brook Cedron' (tou cheimarrou tōn Kedrōn — the winter torrent of Cedron; cheimarrous: a seasonal stream, a wadi). The Kidron Valley runs between Jerusalem and the Mount of Olives. In spring, the brook flows with rainwater; in summer, it is dry. Jesus crosses from the city to the eastern garden — symbolically significant: David fled across the Kidron when Absalom conspired against him (2 Samuel 15:23). The Second David crosses the Kidron before his greater betrayal. 'A garden' (kēpos) — John uniquely uses this word for Gethsemane (the Synoptics say 'a place'), and will use it again for the tomb and resurrection garden (19:41; 20:15). The garden recalls Eden: the first Adam sinned in a garden; the second Adam conquers in a garden.

verses.kjv_text: And Judas also, which betrayed him, knew the place: for Jesus ofttimes resorted thither with his disciples.
verses.original_text: ᾔδει δὲ καὶ Ἰούδας ὁ παραδιδοὺς αὐτὸν τὸν τόπον ὅτι πολλάκις συνήχθη Ἰησοῦς ἐκεῖ μετὰ τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: ēdei de kai Ioudas ho paradidous auton ton topon hoti pollakis synēchthē Iēsous ekei meta tōn mathētōn autou
verses.study_note: 'Jesus ofttimes resorted thither' (pollakis synēchthē Iēsous ekei — Jesus often assembled/gathered there). Gethsemane was a regular meeting place — Jesus did not choose an obscure spot but his habitual retreat, known to all. There is nothing secretive about his movements; he is not hiding. Judas's knowledge of the location means Jesus could not have evaded him if he chose. He did not choose to evade — he went to his accustomed place and waited.

verses.kjv_text: Judas then, having received a band of men and officers from the chief priests and Pharisees, cometh thither with lanterns and torches and weapons.
verses.original_text: ὁ οὖν Ἰούδας λαβὼν τὴν σπεῖραν καὶ ἐκ τῶν ἀρχιερέων καὶ ἐκ τῶν Φαρισαίων ὑπηρέτας ἔρχεται ἐκεῖ μετὰ φανῶν καὶ λαμπάδων καὶ ὅπλων
verses.transliteration: ho oun Ioudas labōn tēn speiran kai ek tōn archiereon kai ek tōn Pharisaiōn hypēretas erchetai ekei meta phanōn kai lampadōn kai hoplōn
verses.study_note: 'A band of men' (tēn speiran — the cohort; a Roman military unit of 600 men, though sometimes as few as 200; a significant detachment). The word 'speiran' (Latin: cohors) is a Roman military term — Roman soldiers accompanied by Jewish temple officers (hypēretas). The size of the force reflects the authorities' expectation of resistance — Jesus has a following among the common people and has recently disrupted the Temple commerce. 'Lanterns and torches' (phanōn kai lampadōn) — it was spring (Passover, full moon), but they bring artificial light, perhaps to search in shadows. Ironically, they come with lights to arrest the Light of the World.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus therefore, knowing all things that should come upon him, went forth, and said unto them, Whom seek ye?
verses.original_text: Ἰησοῦς οὖν εἰδὼς πάντα τὰ ἐρχόμενα ἐπ᾽ αὐτόν ἐξῆλθεν καὶ λέγει αὐτοῖς Τίνα ζητεῖτε
verses.transliteration: Iēsous oun eidōs panta ta erchomena ep auton exēlthen kai legei autois Tina zēteite
verses.study_note: 'Knowing all things that should come upon him, went forth' (eidōs panta ta erchomena ep auton, exēlthen — knowing all things coming upon him, he went out). Jesus is not surprised; he is in control. He steps forward — not backward, not hiding — to meet the arresting party. This is the posture of a sovereign, not a victim. The question 'Whom seek ye?' (Tina zēteite — whom are you seeking?) echoes the first question Jesus asks in this Gospel (1:38: 'What seek ye?'). The entire Gospel is framed by this question of seeking.

verses.kjv_text: They answered him, Jesus of Nazareth. Jesus saith unto them, I am he. And Judas also, which betrayed him, stood with them.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθησαν αὐτῷ Ἰησοῦν τὸν Ναζωραῖον λέγει αὐτοῖς Ἰησοῦς Ἐγώ εἰμι καὶ εἱστήκει καὶ Ἰούδας ὁ παραδιδοὺς αὐτὸν μετ᾽ αὐτῶν
verses.transliteration: apekrithēsan autō Iēsoun ton Nazōraion legei autois Iēsous Egō eimi kai heistēkei kai Ioudas ho paradidous auton met autōn
verses.study_note: 'I am he' (Egō eimi — I AM; the Greek has no 'he'; it is the absolute 'I AM' of divine self-disclosure). This is John's most dramatic use of egō eimi. In the garden of darkness and arrest, Jesus speaks the divine name — the same words by which God identified himself to Moses (Exodus 3:14). The effect in v.6 is immediate and supernatural. 'And Judas also, which betrayed him, stood with them' (heistēkei kai Ioudas — Judas also stood; imperfect: was standing there). John places Judas on the opposite side — with them, not with Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: As soon then as he had said unto them, I am he, they went backward, and fell to the ground.
verses.original_text: ὡς οὖν εἶπεν αὐτοῖς ὅτι Ἐγώ εἰμι ἀπῆλθον εἰς τὰ ὀπίσω καὶ ἔπεσον χαμαί
verses.transliteration: hōs oun eipen autois hoti Egō eimi apēlthon eis ta opisō kai epesan chamai
verses.study_note: 'They went backward, and fell to the ground' (apēlthon eis ta opisō kai epesan chamai — they went back and fell to the ground). Six hundred armed soldiers are knocked off their feet by two words. This is a demonstration of divine power — the same power that will raise Jesus from the dead. Jesus allows himself to be arrested only because he chooses to. He is not overcome by superior force; he voluntarily gives himself. 'That no man could bind him' (cf. Psalm 78:65; the language of supernatural restraint) — yet he goes willingly. The cross is not defeat; it is sovereign choice.

verses.kjv_text: Then asked he them again, Whom seek ye? And they said, Jesus of Nazareth.
verses.original_text: πάλιν οὖν ἐπηρώτησεν αὐτούς Τίνα ζητεῖτε οἱ δὲ εἶπον Ἰησοῦν τὸν Ναζωραῖον
verses.transliteration: palin oun epērōtēsen autous Tina zēteite hoi de eipon Iēsoun ton Nazōraion
verses.study_note: After the display of power, Jesus asks the same question again — giving the soldiers a second chance, allowing them to regroup and answer deliberately. He is in no hurry; he controls the pace of events. The repetition of 'Jesus of Nazareth' is their identification — and Jesus will satisfy it precisely, while securing the freedom of his disciples.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered, I have told you that I am he: if therefore ye seek me, let these go their way:
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη Ἰησοῦς Εἶπον ὑμῖν ὅτι ἐγώ εἰμι εἰ οὖν ἐμὲ ζητεῖτε ἄφετε τούτους ὑπάγειν
verses.transliteration: apekrithē Iēsous Eipon hymin hoti egō eimi ei oun eme zēteite aphete toutous hypagein
verses.study_note: 'Let these go their way' (aphete toutous hypagein — let these go; release these). Jesus negotiates the release of the disciples as a condition of his surrender. The Good Shepherd gives himself for the sheep (10:11). He does not merely allow them to flee — he actively secures their departure. This is shepherd-love in action: interposing himself between the wolf and the flock.

verses.kjv_text: That the saying might be fulfilled, which he spake, Of them which thou gavest me have I lost none.
verses.original_text: ἵνα πληρωθῇ ὁ λόγος ὃν εἶπεν ὅτι Οὓς δέδωκάς μοι οὐκ ἀπώλεσα ἐξ αὐτῶν οὐδένα
verses.transliteration: hina plērōthē ho logos hon eipen hoti Hous dedōkas moi ouk apōlesa ex autōn oudena
verses.study_note: John uniquely applies the fulfilment of 17:12 ('none of them is lost') to the physical deliverance of the disciples in Gethsemane. The words about spiritual keeping (17:12) are here extended to physical protection. The divine word that promised preservation of those given to the Son is fulfilled at every level: spiritual, physical, and eschatological. Jesus's word does not fail.

verses.kjv_text: Then Simon Peter having a sword drew it, and smote the high priest's servant, and cut off his right ear. The servant's name was Malchus.
verses.original_text: Σίμων οὖν Πέτρος ἔχων μάχαιραν εἵλκυσεν αὐτὴν καὶ ἔπαισεν τὸν τοῦ ἀρχιερέως δοῦλον καὶ ἀπέκοψεν αὐτοῦ τὸ ὠτάριον τὸ δεξιόν ἦν δὲ ὄνομα τῷ δούλῳ Μάλχος
verses.transliteration: Simōn oun Petros echōn machairan heilkysen autēn kai epaisen ton tou archiereon doulon kai apekopsen autou to ōtarion to dexion ēn de onoma tō doulō Malchos
verses.study_note: 'Simon Peter having a sword' (echōn machairan — having a dagger/sword; machaira: a short sword). Peter acts with characteristic impulsive courage — he had promised to die for Jesus (13:37) and now attempts to start the fight. 'Malchus' — only John names the servant. Luke 22:51 records that Jesus healed the ear immediately. The servant's name may have been known to John's community through subsequent conversion (cf. Romans 16:11 mentions a Narcissus household). The injury is to the 'right ear' (to dexion) — a specific detail of an eyewitness.

verses.kjv_text: Then said Jesus unto Peter, Put up thy sword into the sheath: the cup which my Father hath given me, shall I not drink it?
verses.original_text: εἶπεν οὖν ὁ Ἰησοῦς τῷ Πέτρῳ Βάλε τὴν μάχαιραν εἰς τὴν θήκην τὸ ποτήριον ὃ δέδωκέν μοι ὁ πατήρ οὐ μὴ πίω αὐτό
verses.transliteration: eipen oun ho Iēsous tō Petrō Bale tēn machairan eis tēn thēkēn to potērion ho dedōken moi ho patēr ou mē piō auto
verses.study_note: 'The cup which my Father hath given me, shall I not drink it?' (to potērion ho dedōken moi ho patēr, ou mē piō auto?) — the cup of suffering (cf. Matthew 26:39: 'let this cup pass from me'). The Gethsemane prayer (not recorded in John) is resolved; the cup is accepted. 'Shall I not drink it?' (ou mē piō auto — shall I certainly not drink it? — emphatic double negative: I will certainly drink it). The arrest is the beginning of drinking the cup. Peter's sword would have replaced the cup with a violent rescue — which is not God's plan. The Kingdom does not advance by human violence.

verses.kjv_text: Then the band and the captain and officers of the Jews took Jesus, and bound him,
verses.original_text: Ἡ οὖν σπεῖρα καὶ ὁ χιλίαρχος καὶ οἱ ὑπηρέται τῶν Ἰουδαίων συνέλαβον τὸν Ἰησοῦν καὶ ἔδησαν αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: Hē oun speira kai ho chiliarchos kai hoi hypēretai tōn Ioudaiōn synelabon ton Iēsoun kai edēsan auton
verses.study_note: 'The band and the captain' (hē speira kai ho chiliarchos — the cohort and the commander of a thousand; chiliarchos: military tribune). Roman military authority is combined with Jewish religious authority in the arrest — the two powers that will be united in the execution. 'Bound him' (edēsan auton — they bound him). The binding of the Son of God — he who could have called twelve legions of angels (Matthew 26:53) — is the most astonishing condescension. 'He came unto his own, and his own received him not' (1:11).

verses.kjv_text: And led him away to Annas first; for he was father in law to Caiaphas, which was the high priest that same year.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἤγαγον αὐτὸν πρὸς Ἄνναν πρῶτον ἦν γὰρ πενθερὸς τοῦ Καϊάφα ὃς ἦν ἀρχιερεὺς τοῦ ἐνιαυτοῦ ἐκείνου
verses.transliteration: kai ēgagon auton pros Annan prōton ēn gar pentheros tou Kaiapa hos ēn archiereus tou eniautou ekeinou
verses.study_note: 'Led him away to Annas first' (pros Annan prōton — to Annas first). Annas (Hanan) had been high priest AD 6-15, deposed by the Romans; five of his sons and his son-in-law Caiaphas held the office after him. He remained the power behind the high priesthood. The preliminary hearing before Annas is unique to John; the Synoptics focus on Caiaphas. John shows that the real power-broker is the elder statesman Annas.

verses.kjv_text: Now Caiaphas was he, which gave counsel to the Jews, that it was expedient that one man should die for the people.
verses.original_text: ἦν δὲ Καϊάφας ὁ συμβουλεύσας τοῖς Ἰουδαίοις ὅτι συμφέρει ἕνα ἄνθρωπον ἀπολέσθαι ὑπὲρ τοῦ λαοῦ
verses.transliteration: ēn de Kaiaphas ho symbouleusos tois Ioudaiois hoti sympherei hena anthrōpon apolesthai hyper tou laou
verses.study_note: John reminds the reader of Caiaphas's earlier pronouncement (11:50-51) — the unknowing prophecy that one man should die for the people. The irony is supreme: the man who planned Jesus's death for cynical political reasons uttered the deepest truth of the atonement. God overrules the calculations of ungodly men to accomplish his saving purpose. 'Expedient that one man should die' — substitutionary atonement declared by the high priest, not the apostle.

verses.kjv_text: And Simon Peter followed Jesus, and so did another disciple: that disciple was known unto the high priest, and went in with Jesus into the palace of the high priest.
verses.original_text: Ἠκολούθει δὲ τῷ Ἰησοῦ Σίμων Πέτρος καὶ ἄλλος μαθητής ἐκεῖνος δὲ ὁ μαθητὴς ἦν γνωστὸς τῷ ἀρχιερεῖ καὶ συνεισῆλθεν τῷ Ἰησοῦ εἰς τὴν αὐλὴν τοῦ ἀρχιερέως
verses.transliteration: Ēkolouthei de tō Iēsou Simōn Petros kai allos mathētēs ekeinos de ho mathētēs ēn gnōstos tō archiersei kai syneisēlthen tō Iēsou eis tēn aulēn tou archiereos
verses.study_note: 'Another disciple... known unto the high priest' (allos mathētēs... gnōstos tō archiersei — another disciple known to the high priest). Traditionally identified as John (the Beloved Disciple), whose family (as fishermen supplying the Jerusalem market) may have had connections with the high priest's household. His access enables Peter to enter — and puts Peter in the dangerous position that produces the denial.

verses.kjv_text: But Peter stood at the door without. Then went out that other disciple, which was known unto the high priest, and spake unto her that kept the door, and brought in Peter.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ Πέτρος εἱστήκει πρὸς τῇ θύρᾳ ἔξω ἐξῆλθεν οὖν ὁ ἄλλος μαθητὴς ὁ γνωστὸς τοῦ ἀρχιερέως καὶ εἶπεν τῇ θυρωρῷ καὶ εἰσήγαγεν τὸν Πέτρον
verses.transliteration: ho de Petros heistēkei pros tē thyra exō exēlthen oun ho allos mathētēs ho gnōstos tou archiereos kai eipen tē thyrōrō kai eisēgagen ton Petron
verses.study_note: Peter stands outside — excluded by lack of connection, exposed in the cold. The other disciple's mediation brings Peter in. But being brought into the high priest's courtyard puts Peter in the most dangerous position: surrounded by the servants and officers of the men who want Jesus dead, a fire burning in the cold night, and his Galilean accent soon to give him away.

verses.kjv_text: Then saith the damsel that kept the door unto Peter, Art not thou also one of this man's disciples? He saith, I am not.
verses.original_text: λέγει οὖν τῷ Πέτρῳ ἡ παιδίσκη ἡ θυρωρός Μὴ καὶ σὺ ἐκ τῶν μαθητῶν εἶ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου τούτου λέγει ἐκεῖνος Οὐκ εἰμί
verses.transliteration: legei oun tō Petrō hē paidiskē hē thyrōros Mē kai sy ek tōn mathētōn ei tou anthrōpou toutou legei ekeinos Ouk eimi
verses.study_note: 'Art not thou also one of this man's disciples?' (Mē kai sy ek tōn mathētōn ei — you are not also one of the disciples, are you?). The question expects the answer 'no' (mē: the negative particle). The door-girl's question is almost casual; it is not an official interrogation. Peter's denial is therefore without immediate compulsion — he denies out of fear of what might happen, not under duress. 'I am not' (Ouk eimi — I am not) — the exact negation of 'I AM.' Peter's 'I am not' contrasts starkly with Jesus's 'I AM' in v.5.

verses.kjv_text: And the servants and officers stood there, who had made a fire of coals; for it was cold: and they warmed themselves: and Peter stood with them, and warmed himself.
verses.original_text: εἱστήκεισαν δὲ οἱ δοῦλοι καὶ οἱ ὑπηρέται ἀνθρακιὰν πεποιηκότες ὅτι ψῦχος ἦν καὶ ἐθερμαίνοντο ἦν δὲ καὶ ὁ Πέτρος μετ᾽ αὐτῶν ἑστὼς καὶ θερμαινόμενος
verses.transliteration: heistēkeisan de hoi douloi kai hoi hypēretai anthrakia pepoiēkotes hoti psychos ēn kai ethermainonto ēn de kai ho Petros met autōn estōs kai thermainomenos
verses.study_note: 'A fire of coals' (anthrakia — a charcoal fire; anthrax: coal). The word 'anthrakia' appears only twice in John's Gospel: here (18:18) and in 21:9, where Jesus makes a charcoal fire for the restoration breakfast with Peter. The two fires bracket Peter's denial and restoration — a deliberate and moving literary connection. 'Peter stood with them' (ēn kai ho Petros met autōn — Peter also was with them) — with the servants of the high priest, warming himself beside the enemy's fire. The progressive identification with the wrong group leads to the denial.

verses.kjv_text: The high priest then asked Jesus of his disciples, and of his doctrine.
