verses.original_text: ὁ οὖν ἀρχιερεὺς ἠρώτησεν τὸν Ἰησοῦν περὶ τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ καὶ περὶ τῆς διδαχῆς αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: ho oun archiereus ērōtēsen ton Iēsoun peri tōn mathētōn autou kai peri tēs didachēs autou
verses.study_note: Annas interrogates Jesus on two fronts: his followers (seeking to assess the movement's size and danger) and his teaching (seeking material for a charge). The questions are politically motivated: can the movement be contained, and is there blasphemy or sedition to work with? Jesus's response (vv.20-21) is a masterpiece of legal self-defence: he points to the public nature of his ministry, refusing to incriminate himself or his disciples.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered him, I spake openly to the world; I ever taught in the synagogue, and in the temple, whither the Jews always resort; and in secret have I said nothing.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη αὐτῷ Ἰησοῦς Ἐγὼ παρρησίᾳ λελάληκα τῷ κόσμῳ ἐγὼ πάντοτε ἐδίδαξα ἐν τῇ συναγωγῇ καὶ ἐν τῷ ἱερῷ ὅπου πάντοτε οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι συνέρχονται καὶ ἐν κρυπτῷ ἐλάλησα οὐδέν
verses.transliteration: apekrithē autō Iēsous Egō parrēsia lelalēka tō kosmō egō pantote edidaxa en tē synagōgē kai en tō hierō hopou pantote hoi Ioudaioi synerchontai kai en kryptō elalēsa ouden
verses.study_note: 'In secret have I said nothing' (en kryptō elalēsa ouden — in secret I said nothing). Jesus's ministry was entirely public and transparent. His teaching was in synagogues, in the Temple, in the open air — places of public assembly. No secret society, no hidden agenda, no esoteric inner doctrine. This makes the night arrest particularly conspicuous: why arrest in the night a teacher who speaks only in the day? (cf. Luke 22:52-53).

verses.kjv_text: Why askest thou me? ask them which heard me, what I have said unto them: behold, they know what I said.
verses.original_text: τί με ἐρωτᾷς ἐρώτησον τοὺς ἀκηκοότας τί ἐλάλησα αὐτοῖς ἴδε οὗτοι οἴδασιν ἃ εἶπον ἐγώ
verses.transliteration: ti me erōtas erōtēson tous akēkootas ti elalēsa autois ide houtoi oidasin ha eipon egō
verses.study_note: 'Ask them which heard me' (erōtēson tous akēkootas — ask those who have heard; perfect participle: those who have heard and the hearing remains). Jesus invokes the legal principle of due process: witnesses must be heard (Deuteronomy 17:6; 19:15). He refuses to be his own accuser. His boldness before the high priest contrasts with Peter's timidity before a servant girl — the sovereign bearing of the Son of God in the face of unjust power.

verses.kjv_text: And when he had thus spoken, one of the officers which stood by struck Jesus with the palm of his hand, saying, Answerest thou the high priest so?
verses.original_text: ταῦτα δὲ αὐτοῦ εἰπόντος εἷς τῶν ὑπηρετῶν παρεστηκὼς ἔδωκεν ῥάπισμα τῷ Ἰησοῦ εἰπών Οὕτως ἀποκρίνῃ τῷ ἀρχιερεῖ
verses.transliteration: tauta de autou eipontos heis tōn hypēretōn parestēkōs edōken rhapisma tō Iēsou eipōn Houtōs apoarinē tō archiersei
verses.study_note: 'Struck Jesus with the palm of his hand' (edōken rhapisma — gave a slap; rhapisma: a blow with a rod or the open hand). This is the first physical abuse of Jesus in John's Passion — an officer striking him for speaking too directly. The contrast with Peter's sword-stroke on Malchus's ear is pointed: the bold swordsman is now warming himself by a fire and denying his Lord, while the Lord himself receives blows in silence.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered him, If I have spoken evil, bear witness of the evil: but if well, why smitest thou me?
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη αὐτῷ Ἰησοῦς Εἰ κακῶς ἐλάλησα μαρτύρησον περὶ τοῦ κακοῦ εἰ δὲ καλῶς τί με δέρεις
verses.transliteration: apekrithē autō Iēsous Ei kakōs elalēsa martyrēson peri tou kakou ei de kalōs ti me dereis
verses.study_note: 'If I have spoken evil, bear witness of the evil' (ei kakōs elalēsa, martyrēson peri tou kakou — if I spoke wrongly, testify about the wrong). Jesus does not return evil for evil but insists on legal justice: prove the charge or acknowledge the injustice of the blow. This is consistent with his teaching ('turn the other cheek', Matthew 5:39) and Paul's principle (Romans 12:17-19). The challenge is not aggression but legal/moral accountability.

verses.kjv_text: Now Annas had sent him bound unto Caiaphas the high priest.
verses.original_text: ἀπέστειλεν οὖν αὐτὸν ὁ Ἄννας δεδεμένον πρὸς Καϊάφαν τὸν ἀρχιερέα
verses.transliteration: apesteilen oun auton ho Annas dedemenon pros Kaiphan ton archiersa
verses.study_note: The abbreviated note about Caiaphas — John does not describe the trial before Caiaphas (the Sanhedrin session with false witnesses and the high priest tearing his garments, recorded in Matthew 26:57-68 and Mark 14:53-65). John focuses on Annas and Pilate. The transfer from Annas to Caiaphas is noted briefly as a transitional step toward the Pilate trial.

verses.kjv_text: And Simon Peter stood and warmed himself. They said therefore unto him, Art not thou also one of his disciples? He denied it, and said, I am not.
verses.original_text: ἦν δὲ Σίμων Πέτρος ἑστὼς καὶ θερμαινόμενος εἶπον οὖν αὐτῷ Μὴ καὶ σὺ ἐκ τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ εἶ ἠρνήσατο ἐκεῖνος καὶ εἶπεν Οὐκ εἰμί
verses.transliteration: ēn de Simōn Petros hestōs kai thermainomenos eipon oun autō Mē kai sy ek tōn mathētōn autou ei ērnēsato ekeinos kai eipen Ouk eimi
verses.study_note: Peter's second denial — 'I am not' again. The question is from the group warming themselves by the fire. The setting is the same as vv.17-18: the charcoal fire, the cold night, Peter standing in the wrong company. His denial is escalating: the first was to the door-girl; the second to the group; the third (v.26) to a kinsman of the man whose ear he cut off — an increasingly dangerous questioner.

verses.kjv_text: One of the servants of the high priest, being his kinsman whose ear Peter cut off, saith, Did not I see thee in the garden with him?
verses.original_text: λέγει εἷς ἐκ τῶν δούλων τοῦ ἀρχιερέως συγγενὴς ὢν οὗ ἀπέκοψεν Πέτρος τὸ ὠτίον Οὐκ ἐγώ σε εἶδον ἐν τῷ κήπῳ μετ᾽ αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: legei heis ek tōn doulōn tou archiereos synggenēs ōn hou apekopsen Petros to ōtion Ouk egō se eidon en tō kēpō met autou
verses.study_note: 'Being his kinsman whose ear Peter cut off' (synggenēs ōn hou apekopsen Petros to ōtion — being a relative of the one whose ear Peter cut off). This questioner has a personal reason to press Peter: his cousin was wounded. 'Did not I see thee in the garden with him?' — an eyewitness identification. Peter cannot deny being at the garden (the man saw him) without the eyewitness testimony being put directly against him. The trap tightens.

verses.kjv_text: Peter then denied again: and immediately the cock crew.
verses.original_text: πάλιν οὖν ἠρνήσατο Πέτρος καὶ εὐθέως ἀλέκτωρ ἐφώνησεν
verses.transliteration: palin oun ērnēsato Petros kai eutheōs alektōr ephōnēsen
verses.study_note: 'Immediately the cock crew' (eutheōs alektōr ephōnēsen — immediately a cock crowed). Three denials, one crowing — exactly as Jesus predicted (13:38). Luke 22:61 records that at this moment 'the Lord turned, and looked upon Peter' — Jesus was in the courtyard, saw Peter's face, and the eye-contact broke Peter's heart. He 'went out, and wept bitterly' (Matthew 26:75; Luke 22:62). The prophecy fulfilled is both judgment and mercy: Peter had been warned; the warning comes to fruition with pastoral precision.

verses.kjv_text: Then led they Jesus from Caiaphas unto the hall of judgment: and it was early; and they themselves went not into the judgment hall, lest they should be defiled; but that they might eat the passover.
verses.original_text: Ἄγουσιν οὖν τὸν Ἰησοῦν ἀπὸ τοῦ Καϊάφα εἰς τὸ πραιτώριον ἦν δὲ πρωΐ καὶ αὐτοὶ οὐκ εἰσῆλθον εἰς τὸ πραιτώριον ἵνα μὴ μιανθῶσιν ἀλλ᾽ ἵνα φάγωσιν τὸ πάσχα
verses.transliteration: Agousin oun ton Iēsoun apo tou Kaipha eis to praitōrion ēn de prōi kai autoi ouk eisēlthon eis to praitōrion hina mē mianthōsin all hina phagōsin to pascha
verses.study_note: 'The hall of judgment' (to praitōrion — the Praetorium; the governor's official residence). 'It was early' (prōi — early morning; the sixth watch, 3-6 AM by Roman reckoning, or dawn). 'They themselves went not into the judgment hall, lest they should be defiled' (hina mē mianthōsin — lest they be polluted; contact with Gentile space on the eve of Passover would render them ritually unclean). The supreme irony: the men who are orchestrating an unjust execution are scrupulously careful about ritual purity. They strain out the gnat of Gentile defilement while swallowing the camel of judicial murder (Matthew 23:24).

verses.kjv_text: Pilate then went out unto them, and said, What accusation bring ye against this man?
verses.original_text: ἐξῆλθεν οὖν ὁ Πιλᾶτος πρὸς αὐτοὺς καὶ εἶπεν Τίνα κατηγορίαν φέρετε κατὰ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου τούτου
verses.transliteration: exēlthen oun ho Pilatos pros autous kai eipen Tina katēgorian pherete kata tou anthrōpou toutou
verses.study_note: Pilate accommodates their ritual scruple by coming out to them — the first of many such accommodations that end in a miscarriage of justice. 'What accusation bring ye?' (Tina katēgorian pherete — what charge do you bring?). Pilate the Roman judge follows proper legal procedure: a charge must be specified before a trial can begin. The Jewish authorities had conducted a religious trial; now they need a political conviction from the Romans (who alone had the authority to execute, v.31).

verses.kjv_text: They answered and said unto him, If he were not a malefactor, we would not have delivered him up unto thee.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθησαν καὶ εἶπαν αὐτῷ Εἰ μὴ ἦν οὗτος κακοποιός οὐκ ἄν σοι παρεδώκαμεν αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: apekrithēsan kai eipan autō Ei mē ēn houtos kakopoios ouk an soi paredōkamen auton
verses.study_note: 'If he were not a malefactor, we would not have delivered him up' — they do not specify a charge but appeal to their own authority and reputation: trust our verdict. This is an argument from institutional credibility, not evidence. Pilate sees through it immediately and tries to pass the case back to them (v.31). The circular argument reveals the weakness of their case.

verses.kjv_text: Then said Pilate unto them, Take ye him, and judge him according to your law. The Jews therefore said unto him, It is not lawful for us to put any man to death:
verses.original_text: εἶπεν οὖν αὐτοῖς ὁ Πιλᾶτος Λάβετε αὐτὸν ὑμεῖς καὶ κατὰ τὸν νόμον ὑμῶν κρίνατε αὐτόν εἶπον οὖν αὐτῷ οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι Ἡμῖν οὐκ ἔξεστιν ἀποκτεῖναι οὐδένα
verses.transliteration: eipen oun autois ho Pilatos Labete auton hymeis kai kata ton nomon hymōn krinate auton eipon oun autō hoi Ioudaioi Hēmin ouk exestin apokteinai oudena
verses.study_note: 'It is not lawful for us to put any man to death' (Hēmin ouk exestin apokteinai oudena — It is not lawful for us to kill anyone). By AD 30, the Romans had removed the Sanhedrin's authority to carry out capital punishment (ius gladii — the right of the sword). Jesus must be executed by Roman authority. This is providentially significant: Roman crucifixion fulfils the 'lifted up' prophecy (3:14; 12:32-33). Jewish stoning would not have lifted him up from the earth.

verses.kjv_text: That the saying of Jesus might be fulfilled, which he spake, signifying what death he should die.
verses.original_text: ἵνα ὁ λόγος τοῦ Ἰησοῦ πληρωθῇ ὃν εἶπεν σημαίνων ποίῳ θανάτῳ ἤμελλεν ἀποθνῄσκειν
verses.transliteration: hina ho logos tou Iēsou plērōthē hon eipen sēmainōn poiō thanatō ēmellen apothnēiskein
verses.study_note: John connects the legal/political circumstance with divine prophecy: the Roman legal limitation on Jewish capital punishment is the instrument by which Jesus's prediction of being 'lifted up' (12:32-33) is fulfilled. Behind the manoeuvres of priests and governors, the word of Jesus is being precisely fulfilled. Providence moves through human plans, even unjust ones.

verses.kjv_text: Then Pilate entered into the judgment hall again, and called Jesus, and said unto him, Art thou the King of the Jews?
verses.original_text: Εἰσῆλθεν οὖν εἰς τὸ πραιτώριον πάλιν ὁ Πιλᾶτος καὶ ἐφώνησεν τὸν Ἰησοῦν καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ Σὺ εἶ ὁ βασιλεὺς τῶν Ἰουδαίων
verses.transliteration: Eisēlthen oun eis to praitōrion palin ho Pilatos kai ephōnēsen ton Iēsoun kai eipen autō Sy ei ho basileus tōn Ioudaiōn
verses.study_note: 'Art thou the King of the Jews?' (Sy ei ho basileus tōn Ioudaiōn?) — the charge has apparently been brought in political terms: Jesus claims to be a king, which would be treason against Rome. Pilate's question is partly official (is this a political threat?) and partly curious (what kind of king is this?). The private interview that follows is one of the most remarkable conversations in history: the Roman governor and the Son of God, alone in the Praetorium, discussing the nature of kingship and truth.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered him, Sayest thou this thing of thyself, or did others tell it thee of me?
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ἀφ᾽ ἑαυτοῦ σὺ τοῦτο λέγεις ἢ ἄλλοι εἶπόν σοι περὶ ἐμοῦ
verses.transliteration: apekrithē autō ho Iēsous Aph heautou sy touto legeis ē alloi eipon soi peri emou
verses.study_note: Jesus's counter-question is diagnostic: is Pilate asking as a Roman official investigating a political charge, or as a man genuinely wondering about the identity of this prisoner? The two types of 'king' question require different answers. Pilate's answer (v.35: 'Am I a Jew?') reveals he is asking officially, as a Roman — which allows Jesus to explain the nature of his kingdom.

verses.kjv_text: Pilate answered, Am I a Jew? Thine own nation and the chief priests have delivered thee unto me: what hast thou done?
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη ὁ Πιλᾶτος Μήτι ἐγὼ Ἰουδαῖός εἰμι τὸ ἔθνος τὸ σὸν καὶ οἱ ἀρχιερεῖς παρέδωκάν σε ἐμοί τί ἐποίησας
verses.transliteration: apekrithē ho Pilatos Mēti egō Ioudaios eimi to ethnos to son kai hoi archiereis paredōkan se emoi ti epoiēsas
verses.study_note: 'Am I a Jew?' — with contempt for Jewish affairs. Pilate is not personally invested in the messianic question; he is a Roman administrator dealing with a Jewish religious dispute. 'What hast thou done?' — a practical question: what act has led to this arrest? Pilate is trying to assess whether there is a real Roman law issue here.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered, My kingdom is not of this world: if my kingdom were of this world, then would my servants fight, that I should not be delivered to the Jews: but now is my kingdom not from hence.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη Ἰησοῦς Ἡ βασιλεία ἡ ἐμὴ οὐκ ἔστιν ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου τούτου εἰ ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου τούτου ἦν ἡ βασιλεία ἡ ἐμή οἱ ὑπηρέται ἄν οἱ ἐμοὶ ἠγωνίζοντο ἵνα μὴ παραδοθῶ τοῖς Ἰουδαίοις νῦν δὲ ἡ βασιλεία ἡ ἐμὴ οὐκ ἔστιν ἐντεῦθεν
verses.transliteration: apekrithē Iēsous Hē basileia hē emē ouk estin ek tou kosmou toutou ei ek tou kosmou toutou ēn hē basileia hē emē hoi hypēretai an hoi emoi ēgōnizonto hina mē paradothō tois Ioudaiois nyn de hē basileia hē emē ouk estin enteuthen
verses.study_note: 'My kingdom is not of this world' (hē basileia hē emē ouk estin ek tou kosmou toutou — my kingdom is not from this world). Not 'not in this world' but 'not of/from this world' — its origin and nature are not of the world-order. Its power is not coercive, its borders are not military, its advancement is not by violence. 'Then would my servants fight' — the proof of the kingdom's nature: Jesus's followers are not fighting. Peter has been rebuked (v.11). The pacifism of the arrest is the evidence. The kingdom of God is not advanced by the sword but by the word and the Spirit.

verses.kjv_text: Pilate therefore said unto him, Art thou a king then? Jesus answered, Thou sayest that I am a king. To this end was I born, and for this cause came I into the world, that I should bear witness unto the truth. Every one that is of the truth heareth my voice.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν οὖν αὐτῷ ὁ Πιλᾶτος Οὐκοῦν βασιλεὺς εἶ σύ ἀπεκρίθη ὁ Ἰησοῦς Σὺ λέγεις ὅτι βασιλεύς εἰμι ἐγὼ εἰς τοῦτο γεγέννημαι καὶ εἰς τοῦτο ἐλήλυθα εἰς τὸν κόσμον ἵνα μαρτυρήσω τῇ ἀληθείᾳ πᾶς ὁ ὢν ἐκ τῆς ἀληθείας ἀκούει μου τῆς φωνῆς
verses.transliteration: eipen oun autō ho Pilatos Oukoun basileus ei sy apekrithē ho Iēsous Sy legeis hoti basileus eimi egō eis touto gegennēmai kai eis touto elēlytha eis ton kosmon hina martyrēsō tē alētheia pas ho ōn ek tēs alētheias akouei mou tēs phōnēs
verses.study_note: 'To this end was I born, and for this cause came I into the world, that I should bear witness unto the truth' (eis touto gegennēmai kai eis touto elēlytha eis ton kosmon hina martyrēsō tē alētheia — for this I have been born and for this I have come into the world: that I may bear witness to the truth). The purpose of the incarnation: testimony to truth. 'Every one that is of the truth heareth my voice' (pas ho ōn ek tēs alētheias akouei mou tēs phōnēs) — echoing 10:27 ('My sheep hear my voice'). Pilate is being tested: is he of the truth? His response (v.38) reveals he is not.

verses.kjv_text: Pilate saith unto him, What is truth? And when he had said this, he went out again unto the Jews, and saith unto them, I find in him no fault at all.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ ὁ Πιλᾶτος Τί ἐστιν ἀλήθεια καὶ τοῦτο εἰπὼν πάλιν ἐξῆλθεν πρὸς τοὺς Ἰουδαίους καὶ λέγει αὐτοῖς Ἐγὼ οὐδεμίαν αἰτίαν εὑρίσκω ἐν αὐτῷ
verses.transliteration: legei autō ho Pilatos Ti estin alētheia kai touto eipōn palin exēlthen pros tous Ioudaious kai legei autois Egō oudemian aitian heuriskō en autō
verses.study_note: 'What is truth?' (Ti estin alētheia?) — Pilate's question is immediately followed by his exit: he does not wait for an answer. The question is either cynical (the postmodern sceptic: there is no truth) or rhetorical (a politician's dismissal). The irony: Truth himself is standing before Pilate, and Pilate walks away. 'I find in him no fault at all' (oudemian aitian heuriskō en autō — I find no charge/cause in him at all). The Roman governor declares Jesus innocent — the first of three such declarations (19:4, 6). Justice demands release; politics will produce condemnation.

verses.kjv_text: But ye have a custom, that I should release unto you one at the passover: will ye therefore that I release unto you the King of the Jews?
verses.original_text: ἔστιν δὲ συνήθεια ὑμῖν ἵνα ἕνα ὑμῖν ἀπολύσω ἐν τῷ πάσχα βούλεσθε οὖν ἀπολύσω ὑμῖν τὸν βασιλέα τῶν Ἰουδαίων
verses.transliteration: estin de synētheia hymin hina hena hymin apolyso en tō pascha boulesthe oun apolyso hymin ton basilea tōn Ioudaiōn
verses.study_note: The Passover amnesty ('apolusis') is attested by the Gospels but not by other historical sources — though the practice of releasing prisoners at religious festivals is paralleled in the Roman world. Pilate offers 'the King of the Jews' — using the title he now knows they hate, perhaps to shame them into accepting Jesus's release. The plan backfires: they prefer Barabbas.

verses.kjv_text: Then cried they all again, saying, Not this man, but Barabbas. Now Barabbas was a robber.
verses.original_text: ἐκραύγασαν οὖν πάλιν πάντες λέγοντες Μὴ τοῦτον ἀλλὰ τὸν Βαραββᾶν ἦν δὲ ὁ Βαραββᾶς λῃστής
verses.transliteration: ekraugasan oun palin pantes legontes Mē touton alla ton Barabban ēn de ho Barabbas lēstēs
verses.study_note: 'Barabbas was a robber' (ho Barabbas lēstēs — a bandit, robber; lēstēs: a highway robber; the same word used in 10:1,8 for those who climb over the sheepfold wall). The Synoptics add that Barabbas was involved in insurrection and murder (Mark 15:7; Luke 23:18-19). 'Barabbas' (bar-Abba) in Aramaic means 'son of the father.' The crowd chooses the violent 'son of the father' over the true Son of the Father. And in choosing so, they unwittingly demonstrate the theology of substitution: the guilty man goes free, the innocent man dies.

1.meaning: Across/beyond — Jesus crosses the Kidron ravine, echoing David's flight from Absalom (2 Sam 15:23).
Winter-flowing brook/wadi — the Kidron, dry most of the year, a ravine east of Jerusalem.
Kidron — "the dark/turbid one"; the ravine blood from temple sacrifices drained into.
Garden — deliberate echo of Eden; the second Adam's obedience in a garden reverses Adam's disobedience.

3.meaning: Cohort — Roman military unit of 600 (or maniple of 200); a large force for a single arrest.
Officers/attendants — temple police dispatched by the chief priests.
Torches — they come with lights seeking the Light of the world in the dark (cf. 1:5).

4.meaning: Knowing all things — Jesus's sovereign foreknowledge; he steps forward voluntarily, not caught.

5.meaning: I AM — the divine name (Exod 3:14); the soldiers fall backward at the name.

6.meaning: Fell to the ground — involuntary prostration at the divine Name (cf. Rev 1:17; Dan 10:9).

10.meaning: Sword/short blade — a dagger, not a long sword; standard sidearm of the period.
Little ear/earlobe — diminutive form; John alone names Peter (Petros) and Malchus.

11.meaning: Sheath/scabbard — "put up your sword into the sheath"; Jesus rejects armed defense.
Cup — the cup of wrath/suffering the Father has given him to drink (Mark 14:36; Ps 75:8).

13.meaning: Father-in-law — Annas was father-in-law of Caiaphas; though deposed, still power-broker.

14.meaning: Having counseled/advised — backward reference to 11:49-52 (better one man die).

15.meaning: Another disciple — traditionally the Beloved Disciple (John); known to the high priest.

17.meaning: "I am not" — Peter's denial is the anti-egō-eimi; he refuses to identify himself as the Lord identifies himself.

18.meaning: Charcoal fire — only here and 21:9 in the NT; John uses it to link Peter's denial with his restoration.

20.meaning: Openly/boldly — Jesus's public ministry contrasts with secret plotting of his accusers.
Synagogue — Jewish assembly for teaching; Jesus taught there openly.

22.meaning: Slap/blow with the hand — the first physical abuse; fulfills Isa 50:6.

23.meaning: Wrongly/evilly — Jesus appeals to evidence: "if I spoke evil, bear witness to the evil."

26.meaning: Kinsman — relative of Malchus, witnessed the ear-cutting; Peter's lie unravels.

27.meaning: Cock/rooster — fulfilled prediction of 13:38; the bird of remembrance.

28.meaning: Praetorium — governor's residence, probably Herod's palace or Antonia fortress.
Be defiled — Gentile dwellings ceremonially unclean; tragic irony: they avoid ritual defilement while committing murder.

29.meaning: Accusation/formal charge — Pilate demands legal basis; Romans required formal cognitio.

31.meaning: It is not lawful — Sanhedrin had no jus gladii (right of capital punishment) under Roman rule.

32.meaning: By what kind of death — cross was Roman execution; Jesus predicted being "lifted up" (12:32-33).

33.meaning: King of the Jews — Gentile phrasing; Jesus is "King of Israel" in Jewish lips.

36.meaning: My kingdom — possessive emphatic; "mine, uniquely mine" — in contrast to any earthly realm.
Of this world — origin, not location; Christ's rule is from above though exercised in the world.
Servants — if an earthly kingdom, his servants would fight; but they do not, revealing its nature.

37.meaning: Truth — Jesus's mission is witness to the truth; all who are of the truth hear his voice.
I have been born (perfect) — the incarnation stated at the Roman tribunal.
I have come (perfect) — mission-language; come into the world for this purpose.

38.meaning: "What is truth?" — Pilate's cynical dismissal; stands face-to-face with Truth incarnate (14:6) and walks away.

40.meaning: Bandit/insurrectionist — more than common thief; possibly Zealot revolutionary.
Barabbas — Aramaic "son of the father"; ironic substitution: false son released, true Son condemned.

meta.overview: John 19 is the crucifixion chapter — the climax of the Gospel. It falls into three movements: (1) the final stages of the Roman trial (vv.1-16a), including the scourging, the mocking with crown of thorns and purple robe, Pilate's 'Ecce Homo' (Behold the Man, v.5), the Jewish accusation of blasphemy ('he made himself the Son of God', v.7), and the final condemnation; (2) the crucifixion itself (vv.16b-37), including the carrying of the cross, the titulus (the placard: 'Jesus of Nazareth the King of the Jews' in three languages, vv.19-22), the casting of lots for his garments (fulfilling Psalm 22:18), the care of his mother (the Beloved Disciple commissioned as her son, vv.25-27), the cry 'I thirst' (fulfilling Psalm 69:21, v.28), the completion of the work ('It is finished,' tetelestai, v.30), the death by voluntary giving up of the spirit, the soldiers' decision not to break his legs (fulfilling Psalm 34:20 and Zechariah 12:10), the blood and water from the pierced side (vv.31-37); (3) the burial (vv.38-42), by Joseph of Arimathaea and Nicodemus with a royal quantity of spices. John's crucifixion narrative is the most theologically interpreted of the four Gospels: every detail is understood as fulfilment of Scripture (Psalm 22; 34; 69; Zechariah 12:10; Numbers 9:12), and the cross is presented not as defeat but as enthronement — Jesus reigns from the cross as King of the Jews.
meta.theme: It Is Finished — The Cross as Throne and Victory
meta.keyWordMeaning: To complete, finish, bring to its end, fulfil, accomplish — from telos (end, goal, completion). The climactic word of the crucifixion narrative is 'Tetelestai' (v.30: 'It is finished') — perfect passive indicative: It has been completed and the completion stands. The same root appears in v.28 ('that the scripture might be fulfilled', hina teleiōthē hē graphē). Tetelestai was used in ancient papyri as a commercial term stamped on receipts: 'Paid in full.' The debt of sin is completely paid; nothing remains to be added. The cross is not the prelude to the real work; it IS the completed work. Jesus does not say 'I am dying' but 'It is finished' — the work is done, not the worker.
outline: The Scourging and Mocking (vv.1-7): Then Pilate therefore took Jesus and scourged him. And the soldiers platted a crown of thorns and put it on his head and they put on him a purple robe and said Hail King of the Jews and they smote him with their hands. Pilate therefore went forth again and saith unto them Behold I bring him forth to you that ye may know that I find no fault in him. Then came Jesus forth wearing the crown of thorns and the purple robe. And Pilate saith unto them Behold the man. When the chief priests therefore and officers saw him they cried out saying Crucify him crucify him. Pilate saith unto them Take ye him and crucify him for I find no fault in him. The Jews answered him We have a law and by our law he ought to die because he made himself the Son of God

The Final Verdict (vv.8-16): When Pilate therefore heard that saying he was the more afraid and went again into the judgment hall and saith unto Jesus Whence art thou? But Jesus gave him no answer. Then saith Pilate unto him Speakest thou not unto me? knowest thou not that I have power to crucify thee and have power to release thee? Jesus answered Thou couldest have no power at all against me except it were given thee from above therefore he that delivered me unto thee hath the greater sin. And from thenceforth Pilate sought to release him but the Jews cried out saying If thou let this man go thou art not Caesar's friend whosoever maketh himself a king speaketh against Caesar. When Pilate therefore heard that saying he brought Jesus forth and sat down in the judgment seat in a place that is called the Pavement but in the Hebrew Gabbatha. And it was the preparation of the passover and about the sixth hour and he saith unto the Jews Behold your King. But they cried out Away with him away with him crucify him. Pilate saith unto them Shall I crucify your King? The chief priests answered We have no king but Caesar. Then delivered he him therefore unto them to be crucified

The Crucifixion (vv.16-27): And they took Jesus and led him away. And he bearing his cross went forth into a place called the place of a skull which is called in the Hebrew Golgotha where they crucified him and two other with him on either side one and Jesus in the midst. And Pilate wrote a title and put it on the cross. And the writing was JESUS OF NAZARETH THE KING OF THE JEWS. This title then read many of the Jews for the place where Jesus was crucified was nigh to the city and it was written in Hebrew and Greek and Latin. Then said the chief priests of the Jews to Pilate Write not The King of the Jews but that he said I am King of the Jews. Pilate answered What I have written I have written. Then the soldiers when they had crucified Jesus took his garments and made four parts to every soldier a part and also his coat now the coat was without seam woven from the top throughout. They said therefore among themselves Let us not rend it but cast lots for it whose it shall be that the scripture might be fulfilled which saith They parted my raiment among them and for my vesture they did cast lots. These things therefore the soldiers did. Now there stood by the cross of Jesus his mother and his mother's sister Mary the wife of Cleophas and Mary Magdalene. When Jesus therefore saw his mother and the disciple standing by whom he loved he saith unto his mother Woman behold thy son. Then saith he to the disciple Behold thy mother. And from that hour that disciple took her unto his own home

The Death of Jesus (vv.28-37): After this Jesus knowing that all things were now accomplished that the scripture might be fulfilled saith I thirst. Now there was set a vessel full of vinegar and they filled a spunge with vinegar and put it upon hyssop and put it to his mouth. When Jesus therefore had received the vinegar he said It is finished and he bowed his head and gave up the ghost. The Jews therefore because it was the preparation that the bodies should not remain upon the cross on the sabbath day for that sabbath day was an high day besought Pilate that their legs might be broken and that they might be taken away. Then came the soldiers and brake the legs of the first and of the other which was crucified with him. But when they came to Jesus and saw that he was dead already they brake not his legs but one of the soldiers with a spear pierced his side and forthwith came there out blood and water. And he that saw it bare record and his record is true and he knoweth that he saith true that ye might believe. For these things were done that the scripture should be fulfilled A bone of him shall not be broken. And again another scripture saith They shall look on him whom they pierced

The Burial (vv.38-42): And after this Joseph of Arimathaea being a disciple of Jesus but secretly for fear of the Jews besought Pilate that he might take away the body of Jesus and Pilate gave him leave. He came therefore and took the body of Jesus. And there came also Nicodemus which at the first came to Jesus by night and brought a mixture of myrrh and aloes about an hundred pound weight. Then took they the body of Jesus and wound it in linen clothes with the spices as the manner of the Jews is to bury. Now in the place where he was crucified there was a garden and in the garden a new sepulchre wherein was never man yet laid. There laid they Jesus therefore because of the Jews' preparation day for the sepulchre was nigh at hand

verses.kjv_text: Then Pilate therefore took Jesus, and scourged him.
verses.original_text: τότε οὖν ἔλαβεν ὁ Πιλᾶτος τὸν Ἰησοῦν καὶ ἐμαστίγωσεν
verses.transliteration: tote oun elaben ho Pilatos ton Iēsoun kai emastigōsen
verses.study_note: 'Scourged him' (emastigōsen — flogged him; mastigōo: to flog with a whip; mastigoō refers to the Jewish scourging of 40 lashes minus one; phragelloō [used in Matthew 27:26] refers to Roman flogging, the flagellum). Roman scourging (verberatio) was severe: a leather whip embedded with bone or metal fragments, tearing flesh to the bone, sometimes fatal in itself. Pilate's intention seems to be to satisfy the crowd with a lesser punishment and release Jesus (cf. Luke 23:16,22). Instead, the scourging only escalates the demand for crucifixion.

verses.kjv_text: And the soldiers platted a crown of thorns, and put it on his head, and they put on him a purple robe,
verses.original_text: καὶ οἱ στρατιῶται πλέξαντες στέφανον ἐξ ἀκανθῶν ἐπέθηκαν αὐτοῦ τῇ κεφαλῇ καὶ ἱμάτιον πορφυροῦν περιέβαλον αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: kai hoi stratiōtai plexantes stephanon ex akanthōn epethēkan autou tē kephalē kai himation porphyroun periebalon auton
verses.study_note: 'A crown of thorns' (stephanon ex akanthōn — a wreath/crown woven from thorns). The crown (stephanon — a victor's wreath, not a royal diadem [diadēma]) is a mock-crown, but it is also unknowingly a true crown: the King of Glory crowned with the thorns of the curse (Genesis 3:18). 'Purple robe' (himation porphyroun — a garment of purple; porphyra: purple dye from the murex shellfish, the colour of royalty). The mockery is simultaneously a proclamation: they dress him as a king precisely when he is being enthroned on the cross.

verses.kjv_text: And said, Hail, King of the Jews! and they smote him with their hands.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἤρχοντο πρὸς αὐτὸν καὶ ἔλεγον Χαῖρε ὁ βασιλεὺς τῶν Ἰουδαίων καὶ ἐδίδουν αὐτῷ ῥαπίσματα
verses.transliteration: kai ērchonto pros auton kai elegon Chaire ho basileus tōn Ioudaiōn kai edidoun autō rhaismata
verses.study_note: 'Hail, King of the Jews!' (Chaire ho basileus tōn Ioudaiōn — Hail [greeting], the King of the Jews). 'Chaire' (from chairō: to rejoice) was the standard Greek greeting, equivalent to 'Ave' in Latin — ironically the same word used by the angel to Mary (Luke 1:28: 'Hail, thou that art highly favoured'). The soldiers parody the imperial salute ('Ave Caesar'). Yet their mockery is truer than they know: he is the King not just of the Jews but of the universe.

verses.kjv_text: Pilate therefore went forth again, and saith unto them, Behold, I bring him forth to you, that ye may know that I find no fault in him.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐξῆλθεν πάλιν ἔξω ὁ Πιλᾶτος καὶ λέγει αὐτοῖς Ἴδε ἄγω ὑμῖν αὐτὸν ἔξω ἵνα γνῶτε ὅτι ἐν αὐτῷ οὐδεμίαν αἰτίαν εὑρίσκω
verses.transliteration: kai exēlthen palin exō ho Pilatos kai legei autois Ide agō hymin auton exō hina gnōte hoti en autō oudemian aitian heuriskō
verses.study_note: Pilate's second declaration of innocence (cf. 18:38; 19:6). He brings the scourged, mocked Jesus out as a pathetic figure — perhaps hoping that the sight of his suffering will satisfy the crowd and allow release. 'That ye may know that I find no fault in him' — the public declaration is important: Rome officially declares Jesus innocent even as it prepares to execute him. The injustice is public, deliberate, and officially recorded.

verses.kjv_text: Then came Jesus forth, wearing the crown of thorns, and the purple robe. And Pilate saith unto them, Behold the man!
verses.original_text: ἐξῆλθεν οὖν ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἔξω φορῶν τὸν ἀκάνθινον στέφανον καὶ τὸ πορφυροῦν ἱμάτιον καὶ λέγει αὐτοῖς Ἰδοὺ ὁ ἄνθρωπος
verses.transliteration: exēlthen oun ho Iēsous exō phorōn ton akanthinon stephanon kai to porphyroun himation kai legei autois Idou ho anthrōpos
verses.study_note: 'Behold the man!' (Idou ho anthrōpos — Behold the man; 'Ecce Homo' in Latin). One of the most famous utterances in history. Pilate intends a display of pitiable humanity to move the crowd to mercy: look at this broken man, surely this is enough? But John's readers hear more: this is THE Man — the Son of Man (Daniel 7:13), the representative human, the second Adam, the one in whom humanity finds its destiny. 'Behold the man' is simultaneously Pilate's plea for mercy and John's theological declaration.

verses.kjv_text: When the chief priests therefore and officers saw him, they cried out, saying, Crucify him, crucify him. Pilate saith unto them, Take ye him, and crucify him: for I find no fault in him.
verses.original_text: ὅτε οὖν εἶδον αὐτὸν οἱ ἀρχιερεῖς καὶ οἱ ὑπηρέται ἐκραύγασαν λέγοντες Σταύρωσον σταύρωσον αὐτόν λέγει αὐτοῖς ὁ Πιλᾶτος Λάβετε αὐτὸν ὑμεῖς καὶ σταυρώσατε ἐγὼ γὰρ οὐχ εὑρίσκω ἐν αὐτῷ αἰτίαν
verses.transliteration: hote oun eidon auton hoi archiereis kai hoi hypēretai ekraugasan legontes Stauroson stauroson auton legei autois ho Pilatos Labete auton hymeis kai staurōsate egō gar ouch heuriskō en autō aitian
verses.study_note: 'Crucify him, crucify him' (Stauroson, stauroson auton — crucify, crucify him; the verb stauróō: to affix to a cross). The repetition is frenzy. Pilate's third declaration of innocence: 'I find no fault in him.' Three times 'no fault' (18:38; 19:4; 19:6) — a threefold legal acquittal that makes the subsequent condemnation the most unjust verdict in history. Yet it is simultaneously the fulfilment of God's saving purpose.

verses.kjv_text: The Jews answered him, We have a law, and by our law he ought to die, because he made himself the Son of God.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθησαν αὐτῷ οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι Ἡμεῖς νόμον ἔχομεν καὶ κατὰ τὸν νόμον ὀφείλει ἀποθανεῖν ὅτι ἑαυτὸν υἱὸν θεοῦ ἐποίησεν
verses.transliteration: apekrithēsan autō hoi Ioudaioi Hēmeis nomon echomen kai kata ton nomon opheilei apothanein hoti heauton huion theou epoiēsen
verses.study_note: 'He made himself the Son of God' (heauton huion theou epoiēsen — he made himself Son of God). The real charge is now disclosed: not political rebellion but blasphemy. Leviticus 24:16: 'He that blasphemeth the name of the LORD, he shall surely be put to death.' The Jewish authorities understood Jesus's claim to divine sonship as blasphemy because they did not believe it was true. The irony is complete: he was executed as a blasphemer precisely because his claim was TRUE — and because it was true, the death he died became the atoning sacrifice for the world.

verses.kjv_text: When Pilate therefore heard that saying, he was the more afraid;
verses.original_text: ὅτε οὖν ἤκουσεν ὁ Πιλᾶτος τοῦτον τὸν λόγον μᾶλλον ἐφοβήθη
verses.transliteration: hote oun ēkousen ho Pilatos touton ton logon mallon ephobēthē
verses.study_note: 'He was the more afraid' (mallon ephobēthē — he became more afraid; the comparative: his existing fear deepened). Pilate is already unsettled by this prisoner (his wife's dream, Matthew 27:19; Jesus's answers about kingship). Now the charge is 'Son of God' — in Roman polytheistic religion, the son of a god (divi filius) was a real category; the emperors claimed divine sonship. Pilate is afraid of what he might be doing if this is actually true.

verses.kjv_text: And went again into the judgment hall, and saith unto Jesus, Whence art thou? But Jesus gave him no answer.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἰσῆλθεν εἰς τὸ πραιτώριον πάλιν καὶ λέγει τῷ Ἰησοῦ Πόθεν εἶ σύ ὁ δὲ Ἰησοῦς ἀπόκρισιν οὐκ ἔδωκεν αὐτῷ
verses.transliteration: kai eisēlthen eis to praitōrion palin kai legei tō Iēsou Pothen ei sy ho de Iēsous apokrisin ouk edōken autō
verses.study_note: 'Whence art thou?' (Pothen ei sy? — Where are you from? What is your origin?). This is the deepest question in the Gospel of John — the question of Jesus's origin. It has been asked before (7:27-28; 8:14; 9:29-30). Pilate now asks it with genuine anxiety. 'But Jesus gave him no answer' (apokrisin ouk edōken autō — he gave him no answer). The silence fulfils Isaiah 53:7: 'He is brought as a lamb to the slaughter, and as a sheep before her shearers is dumb, so he openeth not his mouth.' The moment for dialogue with Pilate has passed; the Governor had his chance (18:37-38) and walked away from truth.

verses.kjv_text: Then saith Pilate unto him, Speakest thou not unto me? knowest thou not that I have power to crucify thee, and have power to release thee?
verses.original_text: λέγει οὖν αὐτῷ ὁ Πιλᾶτος Ἐμοὶ οὐ λαλεῖς οὐκ οἶδας ὅτι ἐξουσίαν ἔχω ἀπολῦσαί σε καὶ ἐξουσίαν ἔχω σταυρῶσαί σε
verses.transliteration: legei oun autō ho Pilatos Emoi ou laleis ouk oidas hoti exousian echō apolusai se kai exousian echō staurōsai se
verses.study_note: 'I have power to crucify thee, and have power to release thee' (exousian echō apolusai se kai exousian echō staurōsai se — I have authority to release you and authority to crucify you). Pilate's boast of power triggers Jesus's correction. The word 'exousia' (authority, power) is used throughout John for the authority given by the Father to the Son (1:12; 5:27; 17:2). Pilate thinks he holds the ultimate power; Jesus reveals who actually controls the situation.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus answered, Thou couldest have no power at all against me, except it were given thee from above: therefore he that delivered me unto thee hath the greater sin.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη αὐτῷ Ἰησοῦς Οὐκ εἶχες ἐξουσίαν οὐδεμίαν κατ᾽ ἐμοῦ εἰ μὴ ἦν δεδομένον σοι ἄνωθεν διὰ τοῦτο ὁ παραδιδούς μέ σοι μείζονα ἁμαρτίαν ἔχει
verses.transliteration: apekrithē autō Iēsous Ouk eiches exousian oudemian kat emou ei mē ēn dedomenon soi anōthen dia touto ho paradidous me soi meizona hamartian echei
verses.study_note: 'No power at all against me, except it were given thee from above' (ei mē ēn dedomenon soi anōthen — unless it had been given to you from above; anōthen: from above, the same word as 'born again' in 3:3). All human authority is derived authority — it comes from God (Romans 13:1). Pilate exercises a delegated authority over Jesus only because the Father has ordained this moment. 'He that delivered me' (ho paradidous me — the one handing me over) — probably referring primarily to Caiaphas or the Jewish leadership, though Judas is included. 'Greater sin' — comparative culpability: both are guilty, but the one who used religious authority to demand the execution has greater sin than the pagan who executes under political pressure.

verses.kjv_text: And from thenceforth Pilate sought to release him: but the Jews cried out, saying, If thou let this man go, thou art not Caesar's friend: whosoever maketh himself a king speaketh against Caesar.
verses.original_text: ἐκ τούτου ὁ Πιλᾶτος ἐζήτει ἀπολῦσαι αὐτόν οἱ δὲ Ἰουδαῖοι ἔκραζον λέγοντες Ἐὰν τοῦτον ἀπολύσῃς οὐκ εἶ φίλος τοῦ Καίσαρος πᾶς ὁ βασιλέα ἑαυτὸν ποιῶν ἀντιλέγει τῷ Καίσαρι
verses.transliteration: ek toutou ho Pilatos ezētei apolusai auton hoi de Ioudaioi ekrazon legontes Ean touton apolusēs ouk ei philos tou Kaisaros pas ho basilea heauton poiōn antilegei tō Kaisari
verses.study_note: 'Thou art not Caesar's friend' (ouk ei philos tou Kaisaros — you are not the friend of Caesar). 'Amicus Caesaris' (Friend of Caesar) was a specific honorific title in Rome — an official position of favour and trust. The threat is direct: release Jesus and we will report you to Rome as a man who released a rival king. Pilate is in a politically impossible position. His desire for justice is trumped by his desire for self-preservation. The politics of fear produces the greatest injustice.

verses.kjv_text: When Pilate therefore heard that saying, he brought Jesus forth, and sat down in the judgment seat in a place that is called the Pavement, but in the Hebrew, Gabbatha.
verses.original_text: ὁ οὖν Πιλᾶτος ἀκούσας τούτων τῶν λόγων ἤγαγεν ἔξω τὸν Ἰησοῦν καὶ ἐκάθισεν ἐπὶ βήματος εἰς τόπον λεγόμενον Λιθόστρωτον Ἑβραϊστὶ δὲ Γαββαθα
verses.transliteration: ho oun Pilatos akousōn toutōn tōn logōn ēgagen exō ton Iēsoun kai ekathisen epi bēmatos eis topon legomenon Lithostroton Hebraisti de Gabbatha
verses.study_note: 'The Pavement' (Lithostroton — the Stone Pavement; from lithos: stone + strōnnymi: to spread, pave). The Greek word describes an ornate stone mosaic floor; 'Gabbatha' in Aramaic means 'elevated place, raised platform.' The bēma (judgment seat) — the official raised platform from which judgments were pronounced. This is the moment of formal sentencing. John's detail about the location is historically significant — archaeological excavations have uncovered such pavement stones near the site of the Antonia Fortress or Herod's Palace.

verses.kjv_text: And it was the preparation of the passover, and about the sixth hour: and he saith unto the Jews, Behold your King!
verses.original_text: ἦν δὲ παρασκευὴ τοῦ πάσχα ὥρα δὲ ὡσεὶ ἕκτη καὶ λέγει τοῖς Ἰουδαίοις Ἴδε ὁ βασιλεὺς ὑμῶν
verses.transliteration: ēn de paraskeuē tou pascha hōra de hōsei hektē kai legei tois Ioudaiois Ide ho basileus hymōn
verses.study_note: 'The preparation of the passover' (paraskeuē tou pascha — the Preparation [Friday before the Passover meal]). John places the crucifixion on Nisan 14, the day the Passover lambs were slaughtered (12-3 PM), making Jesus the true Passover Lamb (1 Corinthians 5:7). 'About the sixth hour' — noon by Roman reckoning (though Mark 15:25 says 'the third hour,' 9 AM; the discrepancy has various explanations: different reckoning, rounded hours, or textual issues). 'Behold your King!' (Ide ho basileus hymōn) — Pilate's final, ironic, contemptuous offer: this pathetic scourged figure is your king.

verses.kjv_text: But they cried out, Away with him, away with him, crucify him. Pilate saith unto them, Shall I crucify your King? The chief priests answered, We have no king but Caesar.
verses.original_text: οἱ δὲ ἐκραύγασαν Ἆρον ἆρον σταύρωσον αὐτόν λέγει αὐτοῖς ὁ Πιλᾶτος Τὸν βασιλέα ὑμῶν σταυρώσω ἀπεκρίθησαν οἱ ἀρχιερεῖς Οὐκ ἔχομεν βασιλέα εἰ μὴ Καίσαρα
verses.transliteration: hoi de ekraugasan Aron aron staurōson auton legei autois ho Pilatos Ton basilea hymōn staurōsō apekrithēsan hoi archiereis Ouk echomen basilea ei mē Kaisara
verses.study_note: 'We have no king but Caesar' (Ouk echomen basilea ei mē Kaisara — We have no king except Caesar). The chief priests' blasphemous declaration. In Israel's theology, God alone was King (1 Samuel 8:7; Psalm 47:7; Isaiah 43:15). By declaring Caesar their only king, the high priests formally renounced the sovereignty of God over Israel — apostasy expressed in political terms. The nation that was to have no king but Yahweh now chooses the Roman Emperor. The irony is historically crushing: within 40 years, the city and Temple would be destroyed by this Caesar's armies.

verses.kjv_text: Then delivered he him therefore unto them to be crucified. And they took Jesus, and led him away.
verses.original_text: τότε οὖν παρέδωκεν αὐτὸν αὐτοῖς ἵνα σταυρωθῇ Παρέλαβον δὲ τὸν Ἰησοῦν καὶ ἤγαγον
verses.transliteration: tote oun paredōken auton autois hina staurōthē Parelabon de ton Iēsoun kai ēgagon
verses.study_note: 'Then delivered he him therefore unto them to be crucified' (paredōken auton autois hina staurōthē — he handed him over to them to be crucified). 'Paredōken' (handed over, delivered up) — the same verb used for Judas's betrayal (paradidōmi: 13:2,11; 18:2,5). Pilate, Judas, the chief priests — all hand Jesus over. Yet behind all of them is the Father, who 'spared not his own Son, but delivered him up for us all' (Romans 8:32). The chain of betrayal leads back to divine love.

verses.kjv_text: And he bearing his cross went forth into a place called the place of a skull, which is called in the Hebrew Golgotha:
verses.original_text: καὶ βαστάζων αὐτῷ τὸν σταυρὸν ἐξῆλθεν εἰς τὸν λεγόμενον Κρανίου Τόπον ὃ λέγεται Ἑβραϊστὶ Γολγοθᾶ
verses.transliteration: kai bastazōn autō ton stauron exēlthen eis ton legomenon Kraniou Topon ho legetai Hebraisti Golgotha
verses.study_note: 'He bearing his cross' (bastazōn auton ton stauron — bearing his cross). John omits the detail of Simon of Cyrene being compelled to carry the cross (Mark 15:21; Luke 23:26), emphasising Jesus's own bearing. In 10:18 Jesus said 'I lay down my life of myself' — he goes to the cross under his own power and will. 'Golgotha' (Aramaic: gulgoltha, skull) — probably named for its skull-like appearance, an elevated rocky outcrop outside the city wall (Hebrews 13:12). The traditional site is within the present Church of the Holy Sepulchre, inside what was then outside the city walls.

verses.kjv_text: Where they crucified him, and two other with him, on either side one, and Jesus in the midst.
verses.original_text: ὅπου αὐτὸν ἐσταύρωσαν καὶ μετ᾽ αὐτοῦ ἄλλους δύο ἐντεῦθεν καὶ ἐντεῦθεν μέσον δὲ τὸν Ἰησοῦν
verses.transliteration: hopou auton estaurōsan kai met autou allous dyo enteuthen kai enteuthen meson de ton Iēsoun
verses.study_note: 'Jesus in the midst' (meson de ton Iēsoun — Jesus in the middle). Between two criminals, Jesus occupies the central position — the place of supreme shame becomes the place of supreme honour. Isaiah 53:12: 'He was numbered with the transgressors.' The two criminals represent the division that Christ brings (Luke 23:39-43: one mocks, one believes) — the division that will continue throughout history as all men encounter the crucified Christ.

verses.kjv_text: And Pilate wrote a title, and put it on the cross. And the writing was, JESUS OF NAZARETH THE KING OF THE JEWS.
verses.original_text: ἔγραψεν δὲ καὶ τίτλον ὁ Πιλᾶτος καὶ ἔθηκεν ἐπὶ τοῦ σταυροῦ ἦν δὲ γεγραμμένον ΙΗΣΟΥΣ Ο ΝΑΖΩΡΑΙΟΣ Ο ΒΑΣΙΛΕΥΣ ΤΩΝ ΙΟΥΔΑΙΩΝ
verses.transliteration: egrapsen de kai titlon ho Pilatos kai ethēken epi tou staurou ēn de gegrammenon IĒSOUS HO NAZŌRAIOS HO BASILEUS TŌN IOUDAIŌN
verses.study_note: 'JESUS OF NAZARETH THE KING OF THE JEWS' (IĒSOUS HO NAZŌRAIOS HO BASILEUS TŌN IOUDAIŌN) — the titulus (Latin inscription giving the name and charge). In Roman crucifixion, the charge was written on a placard carried before the condemned or affixed to the cross. Pilate's inscription is the most theologically accurate statement in the Passion narrative — he who intended contempt proclaimed truth. 'The King of the Jews' is the title the Magi sought (Matthew 2:2), that Pilate wrote, that the soldiers mocked — and that is eternally true. The cross is the throne.

verses.kjv_text: This title then read many of the Jews: for the place where Jesus was crucified was nigh to the city: and it was written in Hebrew, and Greek, and Latin.
verses.original_text: τοῦτον οὖν τὸν τίτλον πολλοὶ ἀνέγνωσαν τῶν Ἰουδαίων ὅτι ἐγγὺς ἦν ὁ τόπος τῆς πόλεως ὅπου ἐσταυρώθη ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ ἦν γεγραμμένον Ἑβραϊστί Ῥωμαϊστί Ἑλληνιστί
verses.transliteration: touton oun ton titlon polloi anegnōsan tōn Ioudaiōn hoti engys ēn ho topos tēs poleōs hopou estaurōthē ho Iēsous kai ēn gegrammenon Hebraisti Rōmaisti Hellēnisti
verses.study_note: 'Written in Hebrew, and Greek, and Latin' — the three great languages of the ancient world: Hebrew (the language of religion), Latin (the language of law and empire), Greek (the language of commerce and culture). The universal proclamation of the King of kings in the three universal languages of the day. This is the original 'international' Gospel declaration, proclaimed from the cross before the first missionary journey.

verses.kjv_text: Then said the chief priests of the Jews to Pilate, Write not, The King of the Jews; but that he said, I am King of the Jews.
verses.original_text: ἔλεγον οὖν τῷ Πιλάτῳ οἱ ἀρχιερεῖς τῶν Ἰουδαίων Μὴ γράφε ὁ βασιλεὺς τῶν Ἰουδαίων ἀλλ᾽ ὅτι ἐκεῖνος εἶπεν Βασιλεύς εἰμι τῶν Ἰουδαίων
verses.transliteration: elegon oun tō Pilatō hoi archiereis tōn Ioudaiōn Mē graphe ho basileus tōn Ioudaiōn all hoti ekeinos eipen Basileus eimi tōn Ioudaiōn
verses.study_note: The chief priests want a correction: change 'The King' to 'He claimed to be king' — a difference between proclamation and allegation. They are uncomfortable with the title as a statement of fact. Their discomfort confirms that Pilate's inscription, however unintentionally, is a truthful declaration.

verses.kjv_text: Pilate answered, What I have written I have written.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη ὁ Πιλᾶτος Ὃ γέγραφα γέγραφα
verses.transliteration: apekrithē ho Pilatos Ho gegrafa gegrafa
verses.study_note: 'What I have written I have written' (Ho gegrafa, gegrafa — What I have written, I have written; perfect tense: a done thing that stands). Pilate's most famous and most stubborn statement. In all his weakness throughout the trial, here he is immovable. The words stand — permanently, in three languages, in all four Gospels — as the most widely distributed theological statement of the crucifixion. Divine providence used a stubborn Roman governor to write the eternal truth about the cross.

verses.kjv_text: Then the soldiers, when they had crucified Jesus, took his garments, and made four parts, to every soldier a part; and also his coat: now the coat was without seam, woven from the top throughout.
verses.original_text: οἱ οὖν στρατιῶται ὅτε ἐσταύρωσαν τὸν Ἰησοῦν ἔλαβον τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐποίησαν τέσσαρα μέρη ἑκάστῳ στρατιώτῃ μέρος καὶ τὸν χιτῶνα ἦν δὲ ὁ χιτὼν ἄραφος ἐκ τῶν ἄνωθεν ὑφαντὸς δι᾽ ὅλου
verses.transliteration: hoi oun stratiōtai hote estaurōsan ton Iēsoun elabon ta himatia autou kai epoiēsan tessara merē hekastō stratiōtē meros kai ton chitōna ēn de ho chitōn araphos ek tōn anōthen hyphantos di holou
verses.study_note: 'Without seam, woven from the top throughout' (araphos — seamless; a-: without + rhaptō: to sew; ek tōn anōthen hyphantos di holou: woven from the top all the way through). The undergarment (chitōn) is a single woven piece — a valuable, unusual garment. The Talmud (Yoma 71b-72a) describes the high priest's tunic as seamless. Jesus, the true High Priest, wears a seamless garment. Its integrity mirrors the unity of his body, the Church (17:21-23; 1 Corinthians 1:13: 'Is Christ divided?').

verses.kjv_text: They said therefore among themselves, Let us not rend it, but cast lots for it, whose it shall be: that the scripture might be fulfilled, which saith, They parted my raiment among them, and for my vesture they did cast lots. These things therefore the soldiers did.
verses.original_text: εἶπαν οὖν πρὸς ἀλλήλους Μὴ σχίσωμεν αὐτόν ἀλλὰ λάχωμεν περὶ αὐτοῦ τίνος ἔσται ἵνα ἡ γραφὴ πληρωθῇ ἡ λέγουσα Διεμερίσαντο τὰ ἱμάτιά μου ἑαυτοῖς καὶ ἐπὶ τὸν ἱματισμόν μου ἔβαλον κλῆρον οἱ μὲν οὖν στρατιῶται ταῦτα ἐποίησαν
verses.transliteration: eipon oun pros allēlous Mē schisōmen auton alla lachōmen peri autou tinos estai hina hē graphē plērōthē hē legousa Diemerisanto ta himatia mou heautois kai epi ton himatismon mou ebalon klēron hoi men oun stratiōtai tauta epoiēsan
verses.study_note: Quoting Psalm 22:18 — the great Psalm of the crucifixion. The soldiers' practical decision (don't tear the seamless garment; cast lots instead) fulfils Scripture written 1000 years earlier. The detail of the garments — four soldiers, four parts, one seamless robe — fits the pattern of a Roman execution squad of four (quaternion, Acts 12:4). The fulfilment of Scripture in the most mundane detail (who gets a piece of cloth?) demonstrates that the cross is under divine supervision at every level.

verses.kjv_text: Now there stood by the cross of Jesus his mother, and his mother's sister, Mary the wife of Cleophas, and Mary Magdalene.
verses.original_text: εἱστήκεισαν δὲ παρὰ τῷ σταυρῷ τοῦ Ἰησοῦ ἡ μήτηρ αὐτοῦ καὶ ἡ ἀδελφὴ τῆς μητρὸς αὐτοῦ Μαρία ἡ τοῦ Κλωπᾶ καὶ Μαρία ἡ Μαγδαληνή
verses.transliteration: heistēkeisan de para tō staurō tou Iēsou hē mētēr autou kai hē adelphē tēs mētros autou Maria hē tou Klōpa kai Maria hē Magdalēnē
verses.study_note: 'There stood by the cross of Jesus his mother' (heistēkeisan de para tō staurō tou Iēsou hē mētēr autou — they were standing beside the cross of Jesus, his mother). Four women at the cross: (1) his mother (Mary, though unnamed in John); (2) his mother's sister — possibly Salome, mother of James and John (Mark 15:40), which would make John a cousin of Jesus; (3) Mary wife of Cleophas — possibly mother of James the Less; (4) Mary Magdalene. The women of the Passion stand where the disciples (mostly) fled. Their courage and love are the primary testimony to the death.

verses.kjv_text: When Jesus therefore saw his mother, and the disciple standing by, whom he loved, he saith unto his mother, Woman, behold thy son!
verses.original_text: Ἰησοῦς οὖν ἰδὼν τὴν μητέρα καὶ τὸν μαθητὴν παρεστῶτα ὃν ἠγάπα λέγει τῇ μητρί Γύναι ἴδε ὁ υἱός σου
verses.transliteration: Iēsous oun idōn tēn mētera kai ton mathētēn parestōta hon ēgapa legei tē mētri Gynai ide ho huios sou
verses.study_note: 'Woman, behold thy son!' (Gynai, ide ho huios sou — Woman, behold your son). Even on the cross, Jesus fulfils his filial duty. 'Woman' (gynai) — not disrespectful but formal and solemn (cf. 2:4; 20:15). From the cross, with diminishing breath, Jesus makes legal provision for his mother's care. He commits her to the Beloved Disciple — the one he loves as a son — creating a new family relationship. This is one of the seven words from the cross.

verses.kjv_text: Then saith he to the disciple, Behold thy mother! And from that hour that disciple took her unto his own home.
verses.original_text: εἶτα λέγει τῷ μαθητῇ Ἴδε ἡ μήτηρ σου καὶ ἀπ᾽ ἐκείνης τῆς ὥρας ἔλαβεν ὁ μαθητὴς αὐτὴν εἰς τὰ ἴδια
verses.transliteration: eita legei tō mathētē Ide hē mētēr sou kai ap ekeinēs tēs hōras elaben ho mathētēs autēn eis ta idia
verses.study_note: 'From that hour that disciple took her unto his own home' (ap ekeinēs tēs hōras elaben ho mathētēs autēn eis ta idia — from that hour the disciple took her into his own). 'Eis ta idia' (into his own things/home) — the same phrase used in 1:11 ('he came unto his own'). The Beloved Disciple becomes the new 'beloved son' of Mary; Mary becomes his mother. The cross creates a new family — the family of faith. James (Jesus's brother) was not yet a believer (7:5); after the resurrection he became a pillar of the church (Acts 15:13; Galatians 1:19).

verses.kjv_text: After this, Jesus knowing that all things were now accomplished, that the scripture might be fulfilled, saith, I thirst.
verses.original_text: Μετὰ τοῦτο εἰδὼς ὁ Ἰησοῦς ὅτι πάντα ἤδη τετέλεσται ἵνα τελειωθῇ ἡ γραφή λέγει Διψῶ
verses.transliteration: Meta touto eidōs ho Iēsous hoti panta ēdē tetelestai hina teleiōthē hē graphē legei Dipsō
verses.study_note: 'I thirst' (Dipsō — I am thirsty; present: I thirst now, actively). 'Knowing that all things were now accomplished' (eidōs hoti panta ēdē tetelestai — knowing that everything had already been completed; perfect passive: was in a state of completion). Jesus speaks the thirst because he knows the work is complete — this is the final fulfilment of Scripture before the death. Fulfilling Psalm 69:21: 'In my thirst they gave me vinegar to drink.' The one who promised living water (4:10-14; 7:37-38) thirsts on the cross — bearing the full human condition.

verses.kjv_text: Now there was set a vessel full of vinegar: and they filled a spunge with vinegar, and put it upon hyssop, and put it to his mouth.
verses.original_text: σκεῦος οὖν ἔκειτο ὄξους μεστόν σπόγγον οὖν μεστὸν τοῦ ὄξους ὑσσώπῳ περιθέντες προσήνεγκαν αὐτοῦ τῷ στόματι
verses.transliteration: skeuos oun ekeito oxous meston spongon oun meston tou oxous hyssōpō perithentes prosēnenkan autou tō stomati
verses.study_note: 'Upon hyssop' (hyssōpō — hyssop; a bushy plant). John uniquely specifies hyssop — a plant used in the Passover ritual (Exodus 12:22: the hyssop dipped in the lamb's blood to mark the doorposts). The hyssop connects the dying Jesus to the Passover lamb. The sponge of vinegar (posca — the common sour wine drink of Roman soldiers) offered on hyssop is both practical relief and fulfilment of Psalm 69:21.

verses.kjv_text: When Jesus therefore had received the vinegar, he said, It is finished: and he bowed his head, and gave up the ghost.
verses.original_text: ὅτε οὖν ἔλαβεν τὸ ὄξος ὁ Ἰησοῦς εἶπεν Τετέλεσται καὶ κλίνας τὴν κεφαλὴν παρέδωκεν τὸ πνεῦμα
verses.transliteration: hote oun elaben to oxos ho Iēsous eipen Tetelestai kai klinas tēn kephalēn paredōken to pneuma
verses.study_note: 'It is finished' (Tetelestai — It has been completed and remains complete; perfect passive indicative of teleō). The single word in Greek that is the climax of the entire Gospel. 'Tetelestai' was written on papyri receipts in the ancient world to mean 'paid in full.' The debt of sin — every transgression, every rebellion, the entire weight of human guilt — is 'paid in full' by the atoning death of the Son of God. Nothing can be added to it; nothing needs to be added. 'He bowed his head, and gave up the ghost' (klinas tēn kephalēn, paredōken to pneuma — having bowed his head, he handed over the spirit). 'Gave up' (paredōken — handed over, delivered up; as a free, deliberate act). John uses the language of volition: Jesus does not have his life taken; he hands it over (10:18).

verses.kjv_text: The Jews therefore, because it was the preparation, that the bodies should not remain upon the cross on the sabbath day, (for that sabbath day was an high day,) besought Pilate that their legs might be broken, and that they might be taken away.
verses.original_text: οἱ οὖν Ἰουδαῖοι ἐπεὶ παρασκευὴ ἦν ἵνα μὴ μείνῃ ἐπὶ τοῦ σταυροῦ τὰ σώματα ἐν τῷ σαββάτῳ ἦν γὰρ μεγάλη ἡ ἡμέρα ἐκείνου τοῦ σαββάτου ἠρώτησαν τὸν Πιλᾶτον ἵνα κατεαγῶσιν αὐτῶν τὰ σκέλη καὶ ἀρθῶσιν
verses.transliteration: hoi oun Ioudaioi epei paraskeuē ēn hina mē meinē epi tou staurou ta sōmata en tō sabbatō ēn gar megalē hē hēmera ekeinou tou sabbatou ērōtēsan ton Pilaton hina kateagōsin autōn ta skelē kai arthōsin
verses.study_note: 'Legs might be broken' (kateagōsin ta skelē — that their legs might be broken; crurifragium). Breaking the legs (with a heavy mallet) accelerated death by preventing the crucified from pushing himself up to breathe. Roman law permitted bodies to remain on crosses; Jewish law required burial before sunset on the Sabbath (Deuteronomy 21:23). The fact that Jesus was dead before crurifragium was necessary preserves the fulfilment of Psalm 34:20 and Numbers 9:12.

verses.kjv_text: Then came the soldiers, and brake the legs of the first, and of the other which was crucified with him.
verses.original_text: ἦλθον οὖν οἱ στρατιῶται καὶ τοῦ μὲν πρώτου κατέαξαν τὰ σκέλη καὶ τοῦ ἄλλου τοῦ συσταυρωθέντος αὐτῷ
verses.transliteration: ēlthon oun hoi stratiōtai kai tou men prōtou kateaxan ta skelē kai tou allou tou systaurōthentos autō
verses.study_note: The two criminals' legs are broken — they are still alive, still struggling to breathe. Jesus is approached next (v.33). The order of events confirms the timeline: Jesus died before the others, earlier than expected. This is medically plausible: the severity of the scourging, the loss of blood, the physical agony — Jesus's body may have given out before the others who were perhaps less weakened before the crucifixion.

verses.kjv_text: But when they came to Jesus, and saw that he was dead already, they brake not his legs:
verses.original_text: ἐπὶ δὲ τὸν Ἰησοῦν ἐλθόντες ὡς εἶδον αὐτὸν ἤδη τεθνηκότα οὐ κατέαξαν αὐτοῦ τὰ σκέλη
verses.transliteration: epi de ton Iēsoun elthontes hōs eidon auton ēdē tethnēkota ou kateaxan autou ta skelē
verses.study_note: 'They saw that he was dead already' (eidon auton ēdē tethnēkota — they saw that he was already dead; perfect active participle: a dead man in a state of death). Professional executioners who have seen many deaths confirm that Jesus was dead. No crurifragium needed. The preservation of his bones is providentially secured by the soldiers' own professional assessment. Scripture is fulfilled not by miraculous intervention but by the natural outworking of events under divine oversight.

verses.kjv_text: But one of the soldiers with a spear pierced his side, and forthwith came there out blood and water.
verses.original_text: ἀλλ᾽ εἷς τῶν στρατιωτῶν λόγχῃ αὐτοῦ τὴν πλευρὰν ἔνυξεν καὶ εὐθέως ἐξῆλθεν αἷμα καὶ ὕδωρ
verses.transliteration: all heis tōn stratiōtōn lonchē autou tēn pleuran enuxen kai eutheōs exēlthen haima kai hydōr
verses.study_note: 'Blood and water' (haima kai hydōr — blood and water). The medical phenomenon: the separation of dark clots and clear serum from the pericardial sac or pleural cavity, a sign of death by cardiac failure or hemothorax. John emphasises this as an eyewitness testimony (v.35). Theologically, 1 John 5:6-8 uses the blood and water as testimony to the reality of the incarnation and death: 'This is he that came by water and blood.' The Fathers also saw sacramental significance: the blood of the Eucharist and the water of baptism flowing from the pierced side of Christ.

verses.kjv_text: And he that saw it bare record, and his record is true: and he knoweth that he saith true, that ye might believe.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὁ ἑωρακὼς μεμαρτύρηκεν καὶ ἀληθινὴ αὐτοῦ ἐστιν ἡ μαρτυρία καὶ ἐκεῖνος οἶδεν ὅτι ἀληθῆ λέγει ἵνα καὶ ὑμεῖς πιστεύσητε
verses.transliteration: kai ho heōrakōs memartyrēken kai alēthinē autou estin hē martyria kai ekeinos oiden hoti alēthē legei hina kai hymeis pisteusēte
verses.study_note: 'He that saw it bare record' (ho heōrakōs memartyrēken — the one who has seen has borne witness; both perfects: the seeing and the witnessing remain). The Beloved Disciple breaks through his own narrative anonymity to insist on his personal eyewitness testimony. 'His record is true' (alēthinē autou estin hē martyria — his testimony is genuine). The repetition of 'true' (alēthinē... alēthē) is emphatic: this is not pious legend but eyewitness fact. The historical reality of the death of Jesus is the foundation of the entire Christian gospel (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

verses.kjv_text: For these things were done, that the scripture should be fulfilled, A bone of him shall not be broken.
verses.original_text: ἐγένετο γὰρ ταῦτα ἵνα ἡ γραφὴ πληρωθῇ Ὀστοῦν οὐ συντριβήσεται αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: egeneto gar tauta hina hē graphē plērōthē Ostoun ou syntribēsetai autou
verses.study_note: 'A bone of him shall not be broken' — fulfilling Exodus 12:46 and Numbers 9:12 ('neither shall ye break a bone thereof' — of the Passover lamb); also Psalm 34:20 ('He keepeth all his bones: not one of them is broken'). Jesus is the true Passover Lamb (1 Corinthians 5:7; John 1:29). The Passover instructions about the lamb's bones, given to Moses 1,500 years earlier, are fulfilled precisely in the crucifixion of the Son of God.

verses.kjv_text: And again another scripture saith, They shall look on him whom they pierced.
verses.original_text: καὶ πάλιν ἑτέρα γραφὴ λέγει Ὄψονται εἰς ὃν ἐξεκέντησαν
verses.transliteration: kai palin hetera graphē legei Opsontai eis hon exekentēsan
verses.study_note: Quoting Zechariah 12:10: 'They shall look upon me whom they have pierced, and they shall mourn for him.' In Zechariah, it is Yahweh who is pierced. John quotes this of Jesus — another implicit identification of Jesus with Yahweh. The piercing is both historical (at the cross) and eschatological: Revelation 1:7 applies the same verse to the second coming: 'Behold, he cometh with clouds; and every eye shall see him, and they also which pierced him.' The wound that proved his death also becomes the sign by which he will be recognised at his return.

verses.kjv_text: And after this Joseph of Arimathaea, being a disciple of Jesus, but secretly for fear of the Jews, besought Pilate that he might take away the body of Jesus: and Pilate gave him leave. He came therefore, and took the body of Jesus.
verses.original_text: Μετὰ δὲ ταῦτα ἠρώτησεν τὸν Πιλᾶτον Ἰωσὴφ ἀπὸ Ἁριμαθαίας ὢν μαθητὴς τοῦ Ἰησοῦ κεκρυμμένος δὲ διὰ τὸν φόβον τῶν Ἰουδαίων ἵνα ἄρῃ τὸ σῶμα τοῦ Ἰησοῦ καὶ ἐπέτρεψεν ὁ Πιλᾶτος ἦλθεν οὖν καὶ ἦρεν τὸ σῶμα αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: Meta de tauta ērōtēsen ton Pilaton Iōsēph apo Harimathaias ōn mathētēs tou Iēsou kekrymenos de dia ton phobon tōn Ioudaiōn hina arē to sōma tou Iēsou kai epetrepsen ho Pilatos ēlthen oun kai ēren to sōma autou
verses.study_note: 'Joseph of Arimathaea, being a disciple of Jesus, but secretly' (ōn mathētēs tou Iēsou kekrymenos — being a disciple of Jesus having been hidden; perfect participle: a secret disciple up to this point). The cross brings out what life concealed: Joseph's secret faith becomes public courage at the moment of greatest danger. He goes to Pilate — a senior member of the Sanhedrin voluntarily identifying himself as a disciple of the executed man. This is bold. Isaiah 53:9: 'And he made his grave... with the rich in his death' — Joseph was a wealthy man who provided the tomb.

verses.kjv_text: And there came also Nicodemus, which at the first came to Jesus by night, and brought a mixture of myrrh and aloes, about an hundred pound weight.
verses.original_text: ἦλθεν δὲ καὶ Νικόδημος ὁ ἐλθὼν πρὸς τὸν Ἰησοῦν νυκτὸς τὸ πρῶτον φέρων μίγμα σμύρνης καὶ ἀλόης ὡσεὶ λίτρας ἑκατόν
verses.transliteration: ēlthen de kai Nikodēmos ho elthōn pros ton Iēsoun nyktos to prōton pherōn migma smyrnēs kai aloēs hōsei litras hekaton
verses.study_note: 'Nicodemus, which at the first came to Jesus by night' (ho elthōn pros ton Iēsoun nyktos to prōton — the one who had come to Jesus at night, at the first; cf. 3:1-2; 7:50-51). The arc of Nicodemus: from secret nighttime enquiry (ch.3) to cautious procedural defence (7:51) to public, costly burial service. 'About an hundred pound weight' (hōsei litras hekaton — about 100 Roman pounds, approximately 33 kg/75 lbs of spices). A royal quantity — kings were buried with enormous amounts of fragrant spices (cf. 2 Chronicles 16:14). Nicodemus anoints Jesus as a king in burial, unwittingly confirming Pilate's title on the cross.

verses.kjv_text: Then took they the body of Jesus, and wound it in linen clothes with the spices, as the manner of the Jews is to bury.
verses.original_text: ἔλαβον οὖν τὸ σῶμα τοῦ Ἰησοῦ καὶ ἔδησαν αὐτὸ ὀθονίοις μετὰ τῶν ἀρωμάτων καθὼς ἔθος ἐστὶν τοῖς Ἰουδαίοις ἐνταφιάζειν
verses.transliteration: elabon oun to sōma tou Iēsou kai edēsan auto othoniois meta tōn arōmatōn kathōs ethos estin tois Ioudaiois entaphiazein
verses.study_note: 'Wound it in linen clothes' (edēsan auto othoniois — they bound it in linen strips; othonion: a linen strip, bandage). Jewish burial practice involved wrapping the body in linen with spices placed in the wrappings. The Shroud of Turin is a linen cloth claimed to be the burial cloth of Jesus. Whatever one's view of the relic, the burial was real, physical, and careful — these details emphasise the reality of the death. The same linen wrappings will be found in the tomb on resurrection morning (20:5-7).

verses.kjv_text: Now in the place where he was crucified there was a garden; and in the garden a new sepulchre, wherein was never man yet laid.
verses.original_text: ἦν δὲ ἐν τῷ τόπῳ ὅπου ἐσταυρώθη κῆπος καὶ ἐν τῷ κήπῳ μνημεῖον καινόν ἐν ᾧ οὐδέπω οὐδεὶς ἐτέθη
verses.transliteration: ēn de en tō topō hopou estaurōthē kēpos kai en tō kēpō mnēmeion kainon en hō oudepō oudeis etethē
verses.study_note: 'A garden' (kēpos — a garden). John uniquely calls the area around Golgotha a garden — the same word used for the garden of the arrest (18:1) and of the resurrection (20:15). The death occurs in a garden; the resurrection will occur in a garden — echoing Eden: in the garden, death entered through sin; in the garden, life returns through the sacrifice of the sinless one. 'A new sepulchre, wherein was never man yet laid' (mnēmeion kainon en hō oudepō oudeis etethē — a new tomb in which no one had yet been placed). The unused tomb is significant: Jesus would be the first and last occupant — no confusion possible about whether the resurrection appearances were someone else.

verses.kjv_text: There laid they Jesus therefore because of the Jews' preparation day; for the sepulchre was nigh at hand.
verses.original_text: ἐκεῖ οὖν διὰ τὴν παρασκευὴν τῶν Ἰουδαίων ὅτι ἐγγὺς ἦν τὸ μνημεῖον ἔθηκαν τὸν Ἰησοῦν
verses.transliteration: ekei oun dia tēn paraskeuēn tōn Ioudaiōn hoti engys ēn to mnēmeion ethēkan ton Iēsoun
verses.study_note: 'There laid they Jesus' (ethēkan ton Iēsoun — they laid Jesus there). The body of the Lord is placed in the tomb as evening approaches, the Sabbath begins, and all is still. The Gospel of John's narrative of the life, death, and ministry of the Son of God comes to its apparent end in a garden tomb on the eve of the Jewish Sabbath. What seems like the end of the story is the beginning of the new creation. As God rested on the seventh day after the first creation (Genesis 2:2-3), the Son of God rests in the tomb on the Sabbath before the resurrection morning — the first day of the new creation.

1.meaning: Scourged (aorist) — Roman flagellation with the flagrum, lead-tipped leather thongs; intended to bring near death.

2.meaning: Crown of thorns — mockery of royal diadem; thorns first appear in Gen 3:18 as fruit of the curse Christ now bears.
Purple robe — color of royalty; the soldiers' mockery becomes unconscious testimony.

5.meaning: "Behold the Man" — Latin Ecce Homo; Pilate's words echo Zech 6:12 ("behold the man whose name is the Branch").

6.meaning: Crucify (aorist imperative) — repeated cry; the most shameful death (Deut 21:23; Gal 3:13).

7.meaning: Son of God — the Jewish charge under Lev 24:16; from his own lips testified deity.

9.meaning: "Where are you from?" — Pilate now asks the Johannine question (cf. 7:27-28; 8:14); Jesus is silent (Isa 53:7).

11.meaning: Authority — Pilate's authority is derivative, given "from above" (anōthen); divine providence governs even pagan power.
Greater sin — degrees of culpability; Caiaphas with greater knowledge bears greater guilt.

12.meaning: Friend of Caesar — formal title (amicus Caesaris); political threat that breaks Pilate's resistance.

13.meaning: Stone Pavement — paved tribunal area; possibly the Antonia fortress courtyard.
Gabbatha — Aramaic for "raised place"; the elevated judgment seat.

14.meaning: Preparation Day — Friday before Sabbath; here Preparation of Passover.
Sixth hour — John's Roman reckoning (early morning) vs Mark's Jewish (noon); time of trial.
"Your King" — Pilate ironically presents Jesus as Israel's king; theological truth in mocking lips.

15.original_word: οὐκ ἔχομεν βασιλέα εἰ μὴ Καίσαρα
15.transliteration: ouk echomen basilea ei mē Kaisara
15.meaning: "We have no king but Caesar" — at Passover (commemorating freedom from Egypt) the priests renounce Yahweh's kingship.

16.meaning: Handed over — the verb of betrayal; Pilate hands him over to be crucified, completing the chain of paradosis.

17.meaning: Bearing his own cross — the patibulum (crossbeam); Isaac typology (Gen 22:6).
Place of a Skull — Calvary (Latin); Aramaic Golgotha; possibly skull-shaped rock.
Golgotha — from Aramaic gulgolta; the execution site outside the city wall (Heb 13:12).

18.meaning: They crucified — the bare verb; John refuses to dramatize the brutality.

19.meaning: Titulus — placard stating the crime; Latin loan-word.

20.meaning: In Hebrew, Latin, Greek — three universal languages: religion, law, culture; the universal claim of Christ.

23.meaning: Seamless tunic — woven from top in one piece; high priest's ephod was similarly woven (Exod 28:32; Josephus).

24.meaning: They divided my garments — direct quotation of Ps 22:18 LXX; lot-casting fulfills detail.

25.meaning: By the cross — Mary stands; Stabat Mater; the sword of Simeon's prophecy (Luke 2:35) now pierces.

26.meaning: "Behold your son" — Jesus entrusts Mary to the beloved disciple, fulfilling fifth commandment from the cross.

28.meaning: "I thirst" — fulfilling Ps 22:15; 69:21; the Living Water (4:10) thirsts for our salvation.

29.meaning: Hyssop — same plant used to apply Passover blood (Exod 12:22); deliberate Paschal symbolism.
Sour wine — posca, the soldier's drink; not the drugged wine he refused earlier (Mark 15:23).

30.meaning: "It is finished" — perfect passive; complete and abiding completion. Found on Greco-Roman receipts: "paid in full".
Yielded up the spirit — voluntarily gave up; Jesus dies as he lived, in sovereign control (10:18).

31.meaning: Legs — crurifragium broke legs to hasten death by suffocation; Jesus already dead, so spared.

33.meaning: They did not break — fulfills Exod 12:46 (Passover lamb); Ps 34:20 (righteous sufferer).

34.meaning: Spear/lance — soldier's pilum; pierced side certifies death.
Pierced — same verb in LXX Zech 12:10 ("they shall look on me whom they pierced").
Blood and water — physical proof of death (pericardial fluid); theological symbol of atonement and cleansing (1 John 5:6-8).

35.meaning: Has borne witness (perfect) — eyewitness testimony certified; the basis of belief.

37.meaning: "They shall look on him whom they pierced" — Zech 12:10; eschatological recognition (Rev 1:7).

38.meaning: Secret because of fear — Joseph of Arimathea's hidden discipleship now openly declared at the cross.

39.meaning: Myrrh and aloes — burial spices; Nicodemus brings ~75 lbs, a king's burial.

40.meaning: Linen wrappings — strips of fine linen; same word recurs at the empty tomb (20:5-7).

41.meaning: Garden — death and resurrection in a garden, reversing Eden; same word as 18:1 and 20:15.
New tomb — kainos (qualitatively new); never used; no possible body-confusion.

meta.overview: John 20 is the resurrection chapter — the climax of the Gospel and the hinge of history. Four distinct encounters constitute the chapter: (1) the empty tomb discovered by Mary Magdalene, then Peter and the Beloved Disciple (vv.1-10); (2) Mary Magdalene's encounter with the risen Jesus in the garden (vv.11-18) — the first resurrection appearance in John; (3) the appearance to the ten disciples on Easter evening (vv.19-23), including the giving of the Spirit and the commission to forgive and retain sins; (4) Thomas's doubt and confession a week later (vv.24-29) — the great christological climax: 'My Lord and my God!' The chapter closes with the first statement of the Gospel's purpose (vv.30-31): 'these are written, that ye might believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God; and that believing ye might have life through his name.' John's resurrection narrative is characterised by personal, intimate encounters — each person meets the risen Christ differently: Mary weeping by the tomb; the disciples behind locked doors; Thomas in his doubt. The risen Jesus addresses each person by name, in their specific need. The resurrection is not a theological abstraction but a personal encounter. The transformation of the disciples from hiding in fear to bold witnesses (Acts 2) is explicable only by the actual, bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead.
meta.theme: I Have Seen the Lord — The Resurrection Appearances
meta.keyWordMeaning: To raise up, awaken, lift — used throughout the NT for resurrection. In the passive (ēgerthē — he was raised), it emphasises divine action: God the Father raised the Son from the dead (Romans 10:9; Galatians 1:1). In John, resurrection is also described as Jesus raising himself (10:17-18; 2:19: 'I will raise it up'). Both are true: the resurrection is the act of the triune God — the Father raises, the Son rises, the Spirit is the power of resurrection life (Romans 8:11). The perfect tense in the angel's announcement (ēgerthē — 'he is risen', Matthew 28:6) emphasises the abiding result: he was raised and remains risen. The risen life of Christ is permanent, glorified, unending (Romans 6:9: 'death hath no more dominion over him').
outline: The Empty Tomb (vv.1-10): The first day of the week cometh Mary Magdalene early when it was yet dark unto the sepulchre and seeth the stone taken away from the sepulchre. Then she runneth and cometh to Simon Peter and to the other disciple whom Jesus loved and saith unto them They have taken away the Lord out of the sepulchre and we know not where they have laid him. Peter therefore went forth and that other disciple and came to the sepulchre. So they ran both together and the other disciple did outrun Peter and came first to the sepulchre. And he stooping down and looking in saw the linen clothes lying yet went he not in. Then cometh Simon Peter following him and went into the sepulchre and seeth the linen clothes lie and the napkin that was about his head not lying with the linen clothes but wrapped together in a place by itself. Then went in also that other disciple which came first to the sepulchre and he saw and believed. For as yet they knew not the scripture that he must rise again from the dead. Then the disciples went away again unto their own home
