The Appearance to Mary Magdalene (vv.11-18): But Mary stood without at the sepulchre weeping and as she wept she stooped down and looked into the sepulchre and seeth two angels in white sitting the one at the head and the other at the feet where the body of Jesus had lain. And they say unto her Woman why weepest thou? She saith unto them Because they have taken away my Lord and I know not where they have laid him. And when she had thus said she turned herself back and saw Jesus standing and knew not that it was Jesus. Jesus saith unto her Woman why weepest thou? whom seekest thou? She supposing him to be the gardener saith unto him Sir if thou have borne him hence tell me where thou hast laid him and I will take him away. Jesus saith unto her Mary. She turned herself and saith unto him Rabboni which is to say Master. Jesus saith unto her Touch me not for I am not yet ascended to my Father but go to my brethren and say unto them I ascend unto my Father and your Father and to my God and your God. Mary Magdalene came and told the disciples that she had seen the Lord and that he had spoken these things unto her

The Appearance to the Disciples (vv.19-23): Then the same day at evening being the first day of the week when the doors were shut where the disciples were assembled for fear of the Jews came Jesus and stood in the midst and saith unto them Peace be unto you. And when he had so said he shewed unto them his hands and his side. Then were the disciples glad when they saw the Lord. Then said Jesus to them again Peace be unto you as my Father hath sent me even so send I you. And when he had said this he breathed on them and saith unto them Receive ye the Holy Ghost. Whose soever sins ye remit they are remitted unto them and whose soever sins ye retain they are retained

Thomas's Doubt and Confession (vv.24-29): But Thomas one of the twelve called Didymus was not with them when Jesus came. The other disciples therefore said unto him We have seen the Lord. But he said unto them Except I shall see in his hands the print of the nails and put my finger into the print of the nails and thrust my hand into his side I will not believe. And after eight days again his disciples were within and Thomas with them then came Jesus the doors being shut and stood in the midst and said Peace be unto you. Then saith he to Thomas Reach hither thy finger and behold my hands and reach hither thy hand and thrust it into my side and be not faithless but believing. And Thomas answered and said unto him My Lord and my God. Jesus saith unto him Thomas because thou hast seen me thou hast believed blessed are they that have not seen and yet have believed

The Purpose of the Gospel (vv.30-31): And many other signs truly did Jesus in the presence of his disciples which are not written in this book. But these are written that ye might believe that Jesus is the Christ the Son of God and that believing ye might have life through his name

verses.kjv_text: The first day of the week cometh Mary Magdalene early, when it was yet dark, unto the sepulchre, and seeth the stone taken away from the sepulchre.
verses.original_text: Τῇ δὲ μιᾷ τῶν σαββάτων Μαρία ἡ Μαγδαληνὴ ἔρχεται πρωῒ σκοτίας ἔτι οὔσης εἰς τὸ μνημεῖον καὶ βλέπει τὸν λίθον ἠρμένον ἐκ τοῦ μνημείου
verses.transliteration: Tē de mia tōn sabbatōn Maria hē Magdalēnē erchetai prōi skotias eti ousēs eis to mnēmeion kai blepei ton lithon ērmenon ek tou mnēmeiou
verses.study_note: 'The first day of the week' (tē mia tōn sabbatōn — on the first of the Sabbath-week). The resurrection occurs on the first day — Sunday. This is the beginning of the new creation: as God began creation on the first day (Genesis 1:3), the new creation begins on the first day with the resurrection of the Son of God. 'When it was yet dark' (skotias eti ousēs — while it was still dark). Mary comes in the dark — John's characteristic symbolism. She leaves in the dark; she will return and find the Light. 'The stone taken away' (ton lithon ērmenon — the stone having been lifted away; perfect passive participle: removed and remains removed). Matthew 28:2 records the angel moving the stone; the stone is removed not to let Jesus out (the resurrection body can pass through walls, v.19) but to let the witnesses in.

verses.kjv_text: Then she runneth, and cometh to Simon Peter, and to the other disciple, whom Jesus loved, and saith unto them, They have taken away the Lord out of the sepulchre, and we know not where they have laid him.
verses.original_text: τρέχει οὖν καὶ ἔρχεται πρὸς Σίμωνα Πέτρον καὶ πρὸς τὸν ἄλλον μαθητὴν ὃν ἐφίλει ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ λέγει αὐτοῖς Ἦραν τὸν κύριον ἐκ τοῦ μνημείου καὶ οὐκ οἴδαμεν ποῦ ἔθηκαν αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: trechei oun kai erchetai pros Simōna Petron kai pros ton allon mathētēn hon ephilei ho Iēsous kai legei autois Ēran ton kyrion ek tou mnēmeiou kai ouk oidamen pou ethēkan auton
verses.study_note: 'They have taken away the Lord' (Ēran ton kyrion — they have taken the Lord). Mary assumes grave-robbery. 'We know not' (ouk oidamen — we do not know; the 'we' suggests other women were present, cf. Luke 24:1,10: 'the other women'). Her grief is deep and her loss complete: she has lost him in death, and now even the body is gone. The pathos of Mary's situation — love with no object, grief with no resolution — is the darkness before the dawn. She calls him 'the Lord' (ton kyrion) — already the title of the risen Christ, though she does not yet know he is risen.

verses.kjv_text: Peter therefore went forth, and that other disciple, and came to the sepulchre.
verses.original_text: Ἐξῆλθεν οὖν ὁ Πέτρος καὶ ὁ ἄλλος μαθητής καὶ ἤρχοντο εἰς τὸ μνημεῖον
verses.transliteration: Exēlthen oun ho Petros kai ho allos mathētēs kai ērchonto eis to mnēmeion
verses.study_note: The two disciples run to the tomb — Peter and the Beloved Disciple. The race to the tomb is described with vivid, personal detail that marks this as an eyewitness account. The narrative slows deliberately to attend to small but significant physical details: the linen clothes, the napkin, who went in first, what each one saw. These details have the feel of remembered experience.

verses.kjv_text: So they ran both together: and the other disciple did outrun Peter, and came first to the sepulchre.
verses.original_text: ἔτρεχον δὲ οἱ δύο ὁμοῦ καὶ ὁ ἄλλος μαθητὴς προέδραμεν τάχιον τοῦ Πέτρου καὶ ἦλθεν πρῶτος εἰς τὸ μνημεῖον
verses.transliteration: etrechon de hoi dyo homou kai ho allos mathētēs proedrailen tachion tou Petrou kai ēlthen prōtos eis to mnēmeion
verses.study_note: 'The other disciple did outrun Peter' (proedrailen tachion tou Petrou — ran ahead faster than Peter). The detail of the race has charmed readers for centuries — the younger disciple (John) outrunning the older Peter. The Beloved Disciple arrives first but waits at the entrance; Peter arrives and goes straight in — character revealed in a footrace.

verses.kjv_text: And he stooping down, and looking in, saw the linen clothes lying; yet went he not in.
verses.original_text: καὶ παρακύψας βλέπει κείμενα τὰ ὀθόνια οὐ μέντοι εἰσῆλθεν
verses.transliteration: kai parakypas blepei keimena ta othōnia ou mentoi eisēlthen
verses.study_note: 'Stooping down, and looking in' (parakypas — having stooped/bent down to look; parakyptō: to stoop and look). The tomb is cut into rock with a low entrance; one must stoop to enter. 'Saw the linen clothes lying' (blepei keimena ta othōnia — sees the linen strips lying; blepō: a quick look, a casual seeing). The Beloved Disciple looks in and sees the grave clothes but hesitates to enter — whether from reverence, uncertainty, or waiting for Peter.

verses.kjv_text: Then cometh Simon Peter following him, and went into the sepulchre, and seeth the linen clothes lie,
verses.original_text: ἔρχεται οὖν Σίμων Πέτρος ἀκολουθῶν αὐτῷ καὶ εἰσῆλθεν εἰς τὸ μνημεῖον καὶ θεωρεῖ τὰ ὀθόνια κείμενα
verses.transliteration: erchetai oun Simōn Petros akolouthōn autō kai eisēlthen eis to mnēmeion kai theōrei ta othōnia keimena
verses.study_note: 'Seeth the linen clothes lie' (theōrei ta othōnia keimena — beholds/observes the linen strips lying; theōreō: sustained, careful observation, more than a glance). Peter goes in and examines more carefully. The use of theōreō (sustained seeing) vs. blepō (quick seeing) in vv.5-6 is a linguistic distinction John makes deliberately: the Beloved Disciple glances; Peter studies.

verses.kjv_text: And the napkin, that was about his head, not lying with the linen clothes, but wrapped together in a place by itself.
verses.original_text: καὶ τὸ σουδάριον ὃ ἦν ἐπὶ τῆς κεφαλῆς αὐτοῦ οὐ μετὰ τῶν ὀθονίων κείμενον ἀλλὰ χωρὶς ἐντετυλιγμένον εἰς ἕνα τόπον
verses.transliteration: kai to soudation ho ēn epi tēs kephalēs autou ou meta tōn othoniōn keimenon alla chōris entetyleimenon eis hena topon
verses.study_note: 'The napkin... wrapped together in a place by itself' (to soudation... entyleimenon eis hena topon — the face cloth... rolled up in one place by itself). 'Soudation' (from Latin: sudarium — a sweat cloth, face cloth). The precise arrangement is significant: grave-robbers would not fold the grave clothes; they would take the body with them. The ordered arrangement of the linen — body clothes lying flat (as if the body simply passed through them), face cloth separately rolled — speaks of deliberate, unhurried departure. This is the evidence that struck the Beloved Disciple (v.8).

verses.kjv_text: Then went in also that other disciple, which came first to the sepulchre, and he saw, and believed.
verses.original_text: τότε οὖν εἰσῆλθεν καὶ ὁ ἄλλος μαθητὴς ὁ ἐλθὼν πρῶτος εἰς τὸ μνημεῖον καὶ εἶδεν καὶ ἐπίστευσεν
verses.transliteration: tote oun eisēlthen kai ho allos mathētēs ho elthōn prōtos eis to mnēmeion kai eiden kai episteusen
verses.study_note: 'He saw, and believed' (eiden kai episteusen — he saw and believed). The third verb for seeing in this passage: eiden (from horaō — the deepest, most penetrating form of seeing, insight). 'He saw' — with full perception of the evidence — 'and believed' — the first Easter faith, based on the physical evidence of the empty tomb and the folded grave clothes. He does not yet have the full understanding (v.9) but responds to the evidence with faith.

verses.kjv_text: For as yet they knew not the scripture, that he must rise again from the dead.
verses.original_text: οὐδέπω γὰρ ᾔδεισαν τὴν γραφὴν ὅτι δεῖ αὐτὸν ἐκ νεκρῶν ἀναστῆναι
verses.transliteration: oudepō gar ēdeisan tēn graphēn hoti dei auton ek nekrōn anastēnai
verses.study_note: 'They knew not the scripture, that he must rise again' (oudepō gar ēdeisan tēn graphēn — for as yet they had not known the scripture). The disciples had not yet connected the resurrection prophecies (Psalm 16:10; Isaiah 53:10-12; Jonah; Hosea 6:2) to Jesus. Understanding comes later, through the Spirit (16:13; Luke 24:45-46). The Beloved Disciple believed on the basis of what he saw; full scriptural understanding would follow. Faith can precede full theological articulation.

verses.kjv_text: Then the disciples went away again unto their own home.
verses.original_text: ἀπῆλθον οὖν πάλιν πρὸς αὑτοὺς οἱ μαθηταί
verses.transliteration: apēlthon oun palin pros hautous hoi mathētai
verses.study_note: The two disciples leave. They have seen the evidence; one has believed. But they leave without encountering the risen Jesus — that privilege falls first to Mary. The disciples' departure leaves Mary alone at the tomb, still weeping — setting the stage for the most tender resurrection encounter in any of the Gospels.

verses.kjv_text: But Mary stood without at the sepulchre weeping: and as she wept, she stooped down, and looked into the sepulchre,
verses.original_text: Μαρία δὲ εἱστήκει πρὸς τῷ μνημείῳ κλαίουσα ἔξω ὡς οὖν ἔκλαιεν παρέκυψεν εἰς τὸ μνημεῖον
verses.transliteration: Maria de heistēkei pros tō mnēmeiō klaiousa exō hōs oun eklaien parekypsen eis to mnēmeion
verses.study_note: 'Mary stood without at the sepulchre weeping' (Maria de heistēkei pros tō mnēmeiō klaiousa exō — Mary was standing outside at the tomb, weeping). 'Klaiousa' (weeping; klaiō: to weep, wail; a word for audible weeping/crying). Mary's love is expressed in persistence: the disciples have gone home, but she remains. Her devotion outlasts the others' curiosity. It is love that keeps her at the empty tomb in the dark.

verses.kjv_text: And seeth two angels in white sitting, the one at the head, and the other at the feet, where the body of Jesus had lain.
verses.original_text: καὶ θεωρεῖ δύο ἀγγέλους ἐν λευκοῖς καθεζομένους ἕνα πρὸς τῇ κεφαλῇ καὶ ἕνα πρὸς τοῖς ποσίν ὅπου ἔκειτο τὸ σῶμα τοῦ Ἰησοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai theōrei dyo anggelous en leukois kathezomenous hena pros tē kephalē kai hena pros tois posin hopou ekeito to sōma tou Iēsou
verses.study_note: 'Two angels in white' (dyo anggelous en leukois — two angels in white). In John, only Mary sees the angels; the disciples presumably departed before the angel appearances. The two angels, one at the head and one at the feet — where the body had lain — recall the two cherubim on the ark of the covenant (Exodus 25:18-22), between which God's presence dwelled. The empty space between the angels is the mercy seat — the place where atonement is made, now empty because the sacrifice is complete.

verses.kjv_text: And they say unto her, Woman, why weepest thou? She saith unto them, Because they have taken away my Lord, and I know not where they have laid him.
verses.original_text: καὶ λέγουσιν αὐτῇ ἐκεῖνοι Γύναι τί κλαίεις λέγει αὐτοῖς Ὅτι ἦραν τὸν κύριόν μου καὶ οὐκ οἶδα ποῦ ἔθηκαν αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: kai legousin autē ekeinoi Gynai ti klaieis legei autois Hoti ēran ton kyrion mou kai ouk oida pou ethēkan auton
verses.study_note: 'They have taken away my Lord' (ēran ton kyrion mou — they have taken my Lord; the personal possessive: 'my Lord'). Even the sight of two angelic beings in a sealed tomb does not distract Mary from her grief — she wants the body of Jesus. Her love is so fixed on him that nothing can redirect it. 'Kyrios' (Lord) — the word that will become the central confession of the resurrection church (1 Corinthians 12:3; Romans 10:9; Philippians 2:11).

verses.kjv_text: And when she had thus said, she turned herself back, and saw Jesus standing, and knew not that it was Jesus.
verses.original_text: καὶ ταῦτα εἰποῦσα ἐστράφη εἰς τὰ ὀπίσω καὶ θεωρεῖ τὸν Ἰησοῦν ἑστῶτα καὶ οὐκ ᾔδει ὅτι Ἰησοῦς ἐστιν
verses.transliteration: kai tauta eipousa estraphē eis ta opisō kai theōrei ton Iēsoun hestōta kai ouk ēdei hoti Iēsous estin
verses.study_note: 'She turned herself back, and saw Jesus standing, and knew not that it was Jesus' (ouk ēdei hoti Iēsous estin — she did not know that it was Jesus). The risen Christ is not immediately recognisable — a pattern consistent across all the resurrection appearances (Luke 24:16; John 21:4; Mark 16:12). The resurrection body is a glorified body — continuous with but transformed from the pre-death body (1 Corinthians 15:42-44). Mary does not recognise him initially, perhaps because her eyes are blurred with tears, or because the resurrection form is different.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus saith unto her, Woman, why weepest thou? whom seekest thou? She, supposing him to be the gardener, saith unto him, Sir, if thou have borne him hence, tell me where thou hast laid him, and I will take him away.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῇ Ἰησοῦς Γύναι τί κλαίεις τίνα ζητεῖς ἐκείνη δοκοῦσα ὅτι ὁ κηπουρός ἐστιν λέγει αὐτῷ Κύριε εἰ σύ ἐβάστασας αὐτόν εἰπέ μοι ποῦ ἔθηκας αὐτόν κἀγὼ αὐτὸν ἀρῶ
verses.transliteration: legei autē Iēsous Gynai ti klaieis tina zēteis ekeinē dokousa hoti ho kēpouros estin legei autō Kyrie ei sy ebastasas auton eipe moi pou ethēkas auton kagō auton arō
verses.study_note: 'Whom seekest thou?' (tina zēteis — whom are you seeking?). The first question Jesus ever asked in this Gospel was 'What seek ye?' (1:38). Now, after his death and resurrection, he asks the seeking question again — but personalised: not 'what' but 'whom.' Mary supposed him to be 'the gardener' (ho kēpouros) — an understandable mistake: who else would be in a garden at dawn? The irony: Adam was the first gardener (Genesis 2:15), and Adam's fall brought death. The Second Adam, the true Gardener, restores life in a garden.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus saith unto her, Mary. She turned herself, and saith unto him, Rabboni; which is to say, Master.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῇ Ἰησοῦς Μαριάμ στραφεῖσα ἐκείνη λέγει αὐτῷ Ἑβραϊστί Ῥαββουνί ὃ λέγεται Διδάσκαλε
verses.transliteration: legei autē Iēsous Mariam strapheisa ekeinē legei autō Hebraisti Rabboyni ho legetai Didaskale
verses.study_note: 'Mary' (Mariam — her Aramaic name). One word. Her name spoken by the risen Lord. Jesus said in 10:3: 'he calleth his own sheep by name.' This is that moment: the Good Shepherd calls the sheep by name, and she recognises the voice instantly. 'Rabboni' (Hebraisti Rabbouni — in Hebrew/Aramaic, Rabboni; a heightened form of Rabbi: 'my great master/teacher'). The one word from Jesus produces instant recognition; the recognition produces the Aramaic title of deepest personal devotion. The resurrection is not a theological idea but a personal encounter.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus saith unto her, Touch me not; for I am not yet ascended to my Father: but go to my brethren, and say unto them, I ascend unto my Father, and your Father; and to my God, and your God.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῇ Ἰησοῦς Μή μου ἅπτου οὔπω γὰρ ἀναβέβηκα πρὸς τὸν πατέρα πορεύου δὲ πρὸς τοὺς ἀδελφούς μου καὶ εἰπὲ αὐτοῖς Ἀναβαίνω πρὸς τὸν πατέρα μου καὶ πατέρα ὑμῶν καὶ θεόν μου καὶ θεὸν ὑμῶν
verses.transliteration: legei autē Iēsous Mē mou haptou oupō gar anabebēka pros ton patera poreuou de pros tous adelphous mou kai eipe autois Anabainō pros ton patera mou kai patera hymōn kai theon mou kai theon hymōn
verses.study_note: 'Touch me not; for I am not yet ascended' (Mē mou haptou — Do not touch/cling to me; present imperative with mē: stop touching, or do not keep touching). The Latin Noli me tangere. The precise meaning is debated: is it a prohibition of all touch (impossible, since he invites Thomas to touch him v.27)? Or a prohibition of clinging/detaining him? The latter is more likely: Mary must not try to keep the risen Jesus in the resurrection form; his destination is the ascension, and she must not delay his departure by clinging. The commission replaces the clinging. 'My Father and your Father; my God and your God' — a careful, precise formulation: the relationship is analogous but not identical. He is Son by nature; they are sons and daughters by adoption (Romans 8:15-17). Both relationships are with the same God.

verses.kjv_text: Mary Magdalene came and told the disciples that she had seen the Lord, and that he had spoken these things unto her.
verses.original_text: ἔρχεται Μαρία ἡ Μαγδαληνὴ ἀγγέλλουσα τοῖς μαθηταῖς ὅτι ἑώρακα τὸν κύριον καὶ ταῦτα εἶπεν αὐτῇ
verses.transliteration: erchetai Maria hē Magdalēnē angellousa tois mathētais hoti heōraka ton kyrion kai tauta eipen autē
verses.study_note: 'I have seen the Lord' (heōraka ton kyrion — I have seen the Lord; perfect: I have seen and the seeing stands). Mary is the first apostle of the resurrection — the first human being to proclaim the risen Christ. 'Apostle' means 'one sent'; Jesus sent (v.17) her to the disciples with the resurrection message. The church's tradition has rightly called her 'apostola apostolorum' (apostle to the apostles). Her testimony is rejected by the disciples (Luke 24:11: 'their words seemed to them as idle tales') — but it is true.

verses.kjv_text: Then the same day at evening, being the first day of the week, when the doors were shut where the disciples were assembled for fear of the Jews, came Jesus and stood in the midst, and saith unto them, Peace be unto you.
verses.original_text: Οὔσης οὖν ὀψίας τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ἐκείνῃ τῇ μιᾷ σαββάτων καὶ τῶν θυρῶν κεκλεισμένων ὅπου ἦσαν οἱ μαθηταὶ διὰ τὸν φόβον τῶν Ἰουδαίων ἦλθεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ ἔστη εἰς τὸ μέσον καὶ λέγει αὐτοῖς Εἰρήνη ὑμῖν
verses.transliteration: Ousēs oun opsias tē hēmera ekeinē tē mia sabbatōn kai tōn thurōn kekleismenōn hopou ēsan hoi mathētai dia ton phobon tōn Ioudaiōn ēlthen ho Iēsous kai estē eis to meson kai legei autois Eirēnē hymin
verses.study_note: 'The doors were shut... came Jesus and stood in the midst' (tōn thurōn kekleismenōn... ēlthen ho Iēsous kai estē eis to meson — the doors having been locked... Jesus came and stood in the midst). The locked doors do not hinder the risen body of Christ. The resurrection body is not a ghost (Luke 24:39-43: he eats; 24:39: 'Handle me and see; for a spirit hath not flesh and bones') but it is not bound by the physical limitations of the pre-resurrection body. 'Peace be unto you' (Eirēnē hymin — Peace to you) — the standard Hebrew greeting 'Shalom' but transformed: this is the peace promised in 14:27, now delivered in the presence of the risen Christ.

verses.kjv_text: And when he had so said, he shewed unto them his hands and his side. Then were the disciples glad, when they saw the Lord.
verses.original_text: καὶ τοῦτο εἰπὼν ἔδειξεν τὰς χεῖρας καὶ τὴν πλευρὰν αὐτοῖς ἐχάρησαν οὖν οἱ μαθηταὶ ἰδόντες τὸν κύριον
verses.transliteration: kai touto eipōn edeixen tas cheiras kai tēn pleuran autois echarēsan oun hoi mathētai idontes ton kyrion
verses.study_note: 'He shewed unto them his hands and his side' (edeixen tas cheiras kai tēn pleuran autois — he showed them his hands and his side). The wounds of the crucifixion remain in the resurrection body — the nail prints, the spear wound. This is theologically significant: the risen Christ carries the marks of the cross into eternity. Revelation 5:6 describes the Lamb 'as it had been slain' in the heavenly throne room. The resurrection does not erase the cross but glorifies it. 'The disciples were glad' (echarēsan — they rejoiced; the fulfilment of 16:20-22).

verses.kjv_text: Then said Jesus to them again, Peace be unto you: as my Father hath sent me, even so send I you.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν οὖν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς πάλιν Εἰρήνη ὑμῖν καθὼς ἀπέσταλκέν με ὁ πατήρ κἀγὼ πέμπω ὑμᾶς
verses.transliteration: eipen oun autois ho Iēsous palin Eirēnē hymin kathōs apestalken me ho patēr kagō pempō hymas
verses.study_note: 'As my Father hath sent me, even so send I you' (kathōs apestalken me ho patēr, kagō pempō hymas — just as the Father has sent me, so I also send you). The Great Commission in John: the disciples' mission is modelled on and grounded in the Son's mission from the Father (17:18). 'Apestalken' (perfect: has sent and the sending stands) vs. 'pempō' (present: I am sending now). Both verbs for 'send' are used — apostello and pempō — the full authority of divine commission.

verses.kjv_text: And when he had said this, he breathed on them, and saith unto them, Receive ye the Holy Ghost:
verses.original_text: καὶ τοῦτο εἰπὼν ἐνεφύσησεν καὶ λέγει αὐτοῖς Λάβετε πνεῦμα ἅγιον
verses.transliteration: kai touto eipōn enephysēsen kai legei autois Labete pneuma hagion
verses.study_note: 'He breathed on them' (enephysēsen — he breathed into/upon them; emphysao: to breathe into; only here in the NT). The same verb used in Genesis 2:7 (LXX: enephysēsen — God breathed into Adam's nostrils the breath of life). The new creation corresponds to the first creation: as God breathed life into Adam, the risen Son breathes the life of the Spirit into the new humanity. 'Receive ye the Holy Ghost' (Labete pneuma hagion — receive Holy Spirit; without the article: receive [a gift of] Holy Spirit). This is a preliminary, anticipatory gift of the Spirit — a foretaste of Pentecost (Acts 2). The relationship between this gift and Pentecost is debated; most scholars see this as an earnest or a symbolic act of promise.

verses.kjv_text: Whose soever sins ye remit, they are remitted unto them; and whose soever sins ye retain, they are retained.
verses.original_text: ἄν τινων ἀφῆτε τὰς ἁμαρτίας ἀφίενται αὐτοῖς ἄν τινων κρατῆτε κεκράτηνται
verses.transliteration: an tinōn aphēte tas hamartias aphientai autois an tinōn kratēte kekratēntai
verses.study_note: 'Whose soever sins ye remit, they are remitted; whose soever sins ye retain, they are retained' — the power of the keys (Matthew 16:19; 18:18). This is the apostolic authority to proclaim forgiveness and judgment in accordance with the gospel. It is not an independent priestly authority to forgive sin (only God forgives sin, Mark 2:7) but the authoritative declaration of the gospel: those who believe the apostolic message of forgiveness receive forgiveness; those who reject it retain their sins. The church's mission is proclamation, and that proclamation has binding consequences.

verses.kjv_text: But Thomas, one of the twelve, called Didymus, was not with them when Jesus came.
verses.original_text: Θωμᾶς δὲ εἷς ἐκ τῶν δώδεκα ὁ λεγόμενος Δίδυμος οὐκ ἦν μετ᾽ αὐτῶν ὅτε ἦλθεν Ἰησοῦς
verses.transliteration: Thōmas de heis ek tōn dōdeka ho legomenos Didymos ouk ēn met autōn hote ēlthen Iēsous
verses.study_note: 'Thomas... was not with them' (ouk ēn met autōn — was not with them). Thomas's absence on Easter evening is not explained. Perhaps he was too grief-stricken to join the others; perhaps the meeting was held so privately he was not found. His absence means he misses the first appearance and must receive the testimony of the others secondhand. When people are absent from the community of faith, they miss what only that community can give.

verses.kjv_text: The other disciples therefore said unto him, We have seen the Lord. But he said unto them, Except I shall see in his hands the print of the nails, and put my finger into the print of the nails, and thrust my hand into his side, I will not believe.
verses.original_text: ἔλεγον οὖν αὐτῷ οἱ ἄλλοι μαθηταί Ἑωράκαμεν τὸν κύριον ὁ δὲ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς Ἐὰν μὴ ἴδω ἐν ταῖς χερσὶν αὐτοῦ τὸν τύπον τῶν ἥλων καὶ βάλω τὸν δάκτυλόν μου εἰς τὸν τύπον τῶν ἥλων καὶ βάλω μου τὴν χεῖρα εἰς τὴν πλευρὰν αὐτοῦ οὐ μὴ πιστεύσω
verses.transliteration: elegon oun autō hoi alloi mathētai Heōrakamen ton kyrion ho de eipen autois Ean mē idō en tais chersin autou ton typon tōn hēlōn kai balō ton daktylo mou eis ton typon tōn hēlōn kai balō mou tēn cheira eis tēn pleuran autou ou mē pisteusō
verses.study_note: 'Except I shall see... I will not believe' (Ean mē idō... ou mē pisteusō — unless I see... I will certainly not believe). Thomas demands empirical verification of the most specific kind: the wound-marks of the nails and the spear. His demand is remarkably precise — he specifies exactly what he needs. He refuses to believe on the testimony of the others. This is not unreasonable (the resurrection is the most extraordinary claim in history) but it is inadequate: the risen Christ is not a body to be empirically verified but a Lord to be personally encountered.

verses.kjv_text: And after eight days again his disciples were within, and Thomas with them: then came Jesus, the doors being shut, and stood in the midst, and said, Peace be unto you.
verses.original_text: καὶ μεθ᾽ ἡμέρας ὀκτὼ πάλιν ἦσαν ἔσω οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ καὶ Θωμᾶς μετ᾽ αὐτῶν ἔρχεται ὁ Ἰησοῦς τῶν θυρῶν κεκλεισμένων καὶ ἔστη εἰς τὸ μέσον καὶ εἶπεν Εἰρήνη ὑμῖν
verses.transliteration: kai meth hēmeras oktō palin ēsan esō hoi mathētai autou kai Thōmas met autōn erchetai ho Iēsous tōn thurōn kekleismenōn kai estē eis to meson kai eipen Eirēnē hymin
verses.study_note: 'After eight days' (meth hēmeras oktō — after eight days; the following Sunday). Jesus returns specifically for Thomas. His patience with the doubter is pastoral: he does not rebuke Thomas's absence, does not shame his demand, but meets him exactly where he is. Eight days — the eighth day is the day of new creation (circumcision on the eighth day; the octave of Easter; Sunday as the new Sabbath/eighth day). The assembly of disciples on the first/eighth day anticipates the pattern of Christian Sunday worship.

verses.kjv_text: Then saith he to Thomas, Reach hither thy finger, and behold my hands; and reach hither thy hand, and thrust it into my side: and be not faithless, but believing.
verses.original_text: εἶτα λέγει τῷ Θωμᾷ Φέρε τὸν δάκτυλόν σου ὧδε καὶ ἴδε τὰς χεῖράς μου καὶ φέρε τὴν χεῖρά σου καὶ βάλε εἰς τὴν πλευράν μου καὶ μὴ γίνου ἄπιστος ἀλλὰ πιστός
verses.transliteration: eita legei tō Thōma Phere ton daktylo sou hōde kai ide tas cheiras mou kai phere tēn cheira sou kai bale eis tēn pleuran mou kai mē ginou apistos alla pistos
verses.study_note: 'Jesus knew Thomas's exact words (v.25) and addresses them precisely — 'reach thy finger... thrust it into my side' — the identical language Thomas used in his demand. Jesus was absent from the room when Thomas spoke; he heard across time and space. He comes for Thomas with perfect knowledge of his doubt and perfect love for his person. 'Be not faithless, but believing' (mē ginou apistos alla pistos — do not become faithless but faithful/believing). The call is not to blind credulity but to the willing trust that responds to evidence.

verses.kjv_text: And Thomas answered and said unto him, My Lord and my God.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη Θωμᾶς καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ Ὁ κύριός μου καὶ ὁ θεός μου
verses.transliteration: apekrithē Thōmas kai eipen autō Ho kyrios mou kai ho theos mou
verses.study_note: 'My Lord and my God' (Ho kyrios mou kai ho theos mou). The christological climax of the Fourth Gospel. Thomas's confession is the highest in any of the Gospels: 'Lord' (kyrios — a title of the risen Christ, the Greek equivalent of Adonai) and 'God' (theos — God, the divine name). The confession is explicitly addressed to Jesus ('to him'). This is not an exclamation of surprise but a confession of faith. Thomas had demanded empirical proof; the encounter with the risen Christ produces something beyond empirical certainty — personal surrender: 'MY Lord and MY God' (the possessives are emphatic). This verse is one of the clearest affirmations of Christ's full deity in the NT (cf. 1:1,18; Romans 9:5; Titus 2:13; Hebrews 1:8; 2 Peter 1:1; 1 John 5:20).

verses.kjv_text: Jesus saith unto him, Thomas, because thou hast seen me, thou hast believed: blessed are they that have not seen, and yet have believed.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς ὅτι ἑώρακάς με πεπίστευκας μακάριοι οἱ μὴ ἰδόντες καὶ πιστεύσαντες
verses.transliteration: legei autō ho Iēsous hoti heōrakas me pepistekas makarioi hoi mē idontes kai pisteusantes
verses.study_note: 'Blessed are they that have not seen, and yet have believed' (makarioi hoi mē idontes kai pisteusantes — blessed are those not having seen and yet having believed). A beatitude for all post-resurrection believers. Thomas was blessed to see and believe; those who will come to faith without eyewitness encounter with the risen Christ are equally — perhaps specially — blessed. This verse is John's pastoral bridge between the Gospel narrative and the reader: 'you have not seen, but you can believe, and believing you will have life' (v.31). The entire purpose of the Gospel is to enable this non-eyewitness faith.

verses.kjv_text: And many other signs truly did Jesus in the presence of his disciples, which are not written in this book:
verses.original_text: Πολλὰ μὲν οὖν καὶ ἄλλα σημεῖα ἐποίησεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἐνώπιον τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ ἃ οὐκ ἔστιν γεγραμμένα ἐν τῷ βιβλίῳ τούτῳ
verses.transliteration: Polla men oun kai alla sēmeia epoiēsen ho Iēsous enōpion tōn mathētōn autou ha ouk estin gegrammena en tō bibliō toutō
verses.study_note: 'Many other signs truly did Jesus... which are not written in this book' (oun kai alla sēmeia epoiēsen... ha ouk estin gegrammena en tō bibliō toutō — and many other signs... which are not written in this book). The Gospel is a selection — a highly intentional, theologically purposeful selection — from a much larger body of events. John acknowledges the selection explicitly. 21:25 will expand this: 'the world itself could not contain the books that should be written.'

verses.kjv_text: But these are written, that ye might believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God; and that believing ye might have life through his name.
verses.original_text: ταῦτα δὲ γέγραπται ἵνα πιστεύσητε ὅτι Ἰησοῦς ἐστιν ὁ χριστὸς ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ ἵνα πιστεύοντες ζωὴν ἔχητε ἐν τῷ ὀνόματι αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: tauta de gegraptai hina pisteusēte hoti Iēsous estin ho christos ho huios tou theou kai hina pisteuontes zōēn echēte en tō onomati autou
verses.study_note: 'These are written, that ye might believe' (gegraptai hina pisteusēte — have been written so that you may believe). The stated purpose of the Gospel of John: to produce faith. 'That Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God' — the dual title: Christos (the anointed one, the Messiah — the human, royal title) and the Son of God (the divine title). The Gospel began by affirming both (1:1-18); it ends by restating them as the object of faith. 'That believing ye might have life through his name' (hina pisteusēte zōēn echēte en tō onomati autou — so that believing you may have life in his name). Life through his name: the name represents the person; to have life in his name is to have life through union with him. This is the Gospel in one verse: believe → life. 'Life' (zōēn echēte — may have/possess life; the same eternal life promised in 3:16; 6:40; 10:10; 17:3).

1.meaning: On the first of the week — Sunday; the new creation begins as Genesis 1:1 begins.
While it was still dark — Mary comes in literal and theological darkness; light dawns at the tomb (cf. 1:5).
Mary Magdalene — from Magdala on Sea of Galilee; first witness of resurrection (Mark 16:9).
The stone removed (perfect passive) — the rolled-back stone is the consummated act of God.

2.meaning: Whom Jesus loved — phileō here (cf. agapaō in 13:23); the Beloved Disciple = traditionally John.
"They have taken away the Lord" — Mary assumes grave-robbery; even her hopelessness uses the title Kyrios.

4.meaning: Outran more quickly — Beloved Disciple younger and faster; first to the tomb.

5.meaning: Stooping down to look — bent over to peer in; same verb in 1 Pet 1:12 of angels gazing at salvation.
Linen wrappings — strips of fine linen; lying flat, undisturbed; not a robbery scene.

6.meaning: Sees (theōreō) — careful, observant looking; Peter examines the evidence.

7.meaning: Face cloth — Latin sudarium; the head-cloth folded apart in its own place.
Rolled up/folded (perfect passive) — the orderly arrangement contradicts grave-robbery hypothesis.

8.meaning: He saw and believed — the Beloved Disciple's faith without an appearance; first Easter believer.

9.meaning: Scripture — they did not yet understand; the OT pattern of resurrection (Ps 16:10; Hos 6:2; Isa 53:10-11).

12.meaning: Angels — two; possibly echoing the cherubim on the mercy seat (Exod 25:18-22) flanking the body-place.

15.meaning: Gardener — only NT use; resurrection in a garden; the new Adam tilling the new Eden.

16.meaning: Mariam — the Aramaic form Jesus uses; recognition by name (cf. 10:3 "calls his sheep by name").
Rabbouni — Aramaic intensified form of Rabbi; "my dear teacher".

17.meaning: "Stop clinging to me" — present imperative of cessation; Mary holding on, must release for new mode of presence.
I have ascended — perfect; the ascension is the new state Mary must accept.

17.original_word: τὸν πατέρα μου καὶ πατέρα ὑμῶν
17.transliteration: ton patera mou kai patera hymōn
17.meaning: "My Father and your Father" — distinction preserved (not "our Father") yet adoptive sonship secured.
Brothers — first time Jesus calls disciples "brothers" (cf. Heb 2:11-12; Ps 22:22).

19.meaning: Doors having been shut (perfect passive) — emphatic; resurrection body transcends physical barriers.
"Peace be unto you" — shalom; the Easter greeting fulfilling 14:27; 16:33; spoken three times in this chapter.

20.meaning: Hands and side — visible wounds prove identity; the resurrection body bears the marks.

21.meaning: As the Father has sent me — Johannine Great Commission; mission of Son becomes mission of church.
I send you — pempō; commissioning into apostolic witness.

22.meaning: He breathed on them — only here in NT; LXX echo Gen 2:7 (creation) and Ezek 37:9 (re-creation).
"Receive Holy Spirit" — anarthrous; either anticipatory of Pentecost or proleptic enabling.

23.meaning: Are forgiven (perfect passive) — heaven's forgiveness already stands; church proclaims, not creates.
You retain/hold fast — judicial declaration of unrepentant's state; binding-loosing parallel (Matt 18:18).

25.meaning: Mark of the nails — typos: imprint, scar; tangible evidence demanded.
"I will never believe" — emphatic double negative; absolute refusal of secondhand testimony.

27.meaning: "Become believing" — present imperative; ongoing posture of faith, not single act.

28.original_word: ὁ κύριός μου καὶ ὁ θεός μου
28.transliteration: ho kyrios mou kai ho theos mou
28.meaning: "My Lord and my God" — climactic deity confession; bookends with prologue (1:1 "the Word was God").

29.original_word: μακάριοι οἱ μὴ ἰδόντες καὶ πιστεύσαντες
29.transliteration: makarioi hoi mē idontes kai pisteusantes
29.meaning: "Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed" — beatitude for all post-apostolic believers.

30.meaning: Signs — Johannine technical term for miracles as revelatory acts; the Gospel is a sign-selection.

31.meaning: Have been written that you may believe — purpose statement of the Fourth Gospel.

31.original_word: ζωὴν ἔχητε ἐν τῷ ὀνόματι αὐτοῦ
31.transliteration: zōēn echēte en tō onomati autou
31.meaning: Life in his name — the gospel summary; eternal life through union with the named Lord.

meta.overview: John 21 is an epilogue to the Gospel, set beside the Sea of Tiberias after the Resurrection. Seven disciples go fishing and toil all night without success. The risen Jesus appears on the shore unrecognised, directs a miraculous catch of 153 fish, and provides a breakfast of bread and fish at a charcoal fire — deliberately echoing the courtyard fire of Peter's denials. After the meal Jesus puts three questions of love to Peter, matching his three denials, and commissions him to shepherd the flock. Jesus foretells Peter's martyrdom and corrects a misunderstanding about the Beloved Disciple. The chapter closes with a testimony of the Beloved Disciple's authorship and the famous hyperbole that the world itself could not contain all the books that might be written about Jesus.
meta.theme: Restoration, commission, and the inexhaustible testimony of Jesus Christ. The risen Lord transforms failure into fruitfulness, replaces shame with love, and renews calling through gracious encounter. The epilogue grounds apostolic ministry in the experience of resurrection grace and anchors the Gospel in eyewitness testimony.
meta.keyWordMeaning: To love (unconditional/covenantal love vs. warm affection) — the two Greek words at the heart of Jesus's threefold question to Peter illuminate the depth, cost, and transforming power of love as the foundation of all pastoral ministry.
outline: 21:1–3 — Seven disciples return to fishing on the Sea of Tiberias
21:4–8 — Jesus unrecognised on the shore; the miraculous catch
21:9–14 — Breakfast at the charcoal fire; the third resurrection appearance
21:15–17 — Threefold restoration of Peter: 'Feed my sheep'
21:18–19 — Prophecy of Peter's martyrdom; 'Follow me'
21:20–23 — Peter asks about the Beloved Disciple; Jesus corrects the rumour
21:24–25 — Authorial testimony and the world-filling testimony of Jesus

verses.kjv_text: After these things Jesus shewed himself again to the disciples at the sea of Tiberias; and on this wise shewed he himself.
verses.original_text: Μετὰ ταῦτα ἐφανέρωσεν ἑαυτὸν πάλιν ὁ Ἰησοῦς τοῖς μαθηταῖς ἐπὶ τῆς θαλάσσης τῆς Τιβεριάδος· ἐφανέρωσεν δὲ οὕτως.
verses.transliteration: Meta tauta ephanerōsen heauton palin ho Iēsous tois mathētais epi tēs thalassēs tēs Tiberiados; ephanerōsen de houtōs.
verses.study_note: The Sea of Tiberias is another name for the Sea of Galilee (6:1). Ephanerōsen (he manifested) is the same verb used in 1:31; 2:11 — each post-resurrection appearance is an act of divine self-disclosure, not merely a coincidence. The disciples have returned to Galilee as instructed (Mark 16:7), yet in the waiting they have resumed their former trade.

verses.kjv_text: There were together Simon Peter, and Thomas called Didymus, and Nathanael of Cana in Galilee, and the sons of Zebedee, and two other of his disciples.
verses.original_text: ἦσαν ὁμοῦ Σίμων Πέτρος καὶ Θωμᾶς ὁ λεγόμενος Δίδυμος καὶ Ναθαναὴλ ὁ ἀπὸ Κανὰ τῆς Γαλιλαίας καὶ οἱ τοῦ Ζεβεδαίου καὶ ἄλλοι ἐκ τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ δύο.
verses.transliteration: ēsan homou Simōn Petros kai Thōmas ho legomenos Didymos kai Nathanaēl ho apo Kana tēs Galilaias kai hoi tou Zebedaiou kai alloi ek tōn mathētōn autou dyo.
verses.study_note: Seven disciples are named — a symbolically complete number. Thomas, who had confessed "My Lord and my God" (20:28), is present; his doubt has been replaced with faith. Nathanael, the man "without guile" (1:47) who first confessed Jesus as Son of God and King of Israel, appears only here and in chapter 1, forming a literary bracket around the Gospel.

verses.kjv_text: Simon Peter saith unto them, I go a fishing. They say unto him, We also go with thee. They went forth, and entered into a ship immediately; and that night they caught nothing.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτοῖς Σίμων Πέτρος· Ὑπάγω ἁλιεύειν. λέγουσιν αὐτῷ· Ἐρχόμεθα καὶ ἡμεῖς σύν σοι. ἐξῆλθον καὶ ἐνέβησαν εἰς τὸ πλοῖον, καὶ ἐν ἐκείνῃ τῇ νυκτὶ ἐπίασαν οὐδέν.
verses.transliteration: legei autois Simōn Petros: Hypagō halieuein. legousin autō: Erchometha kai hēmeis syn soi. exēlthon kai enebēsan eis to ploion, kai en ekeinē tē nykti epiasen ouden.
verses.study_note: The return to fishing may reflect uncertainty about their future, not apostasy. Fishing was the natural livelihood of Galilean disciples. The fruitless night (epiasen ouden — they caught nothing) mirrors Luke 5:5, where Peter's night of empty nets preceded his first miraculous call. The darkness of failure always precedes the dawn of resurrection grace.

verses.kjv_text: But when the morning was now come, Jesus stood on the shore: but the disciples knew not that it was Jesus.
verses.original_text: Πρωΐας δὲ ἤδη γενομένης ἔστη Ἰησοῦς εἰς τὸν αἰγιαλόν· οὐ μέντοι ᾔδεισαν οἱ μαθηταὶ ὅτι Ἰησοῦς ἐστιν.
verses.transliteration: Prōias de ēdē genomenēs estē Iēsous eis ton aigialon; ou mentoi ēdeisan hoi mathētai hoti Iēsous estin.
verses.study_note: Morning (prōias) — dawn light after a night of failure is a resurrection motif: Mary came "when it was yet dark" (20:1) and Jesus appeared at dawn. The disciples' failure to recognise Jesus is consistent with other resurrection accounts (Luke 24:16; Matt. 28:17) and may reflect the transformed glory of the resurrection body, distance, or providential withholding.

verses.kjv_text: Then Jesus saith unto them, Children, have ye any meat? They answered him, No.
verses.original_text: λέγει οὖν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς· Παιδία, μή τι προσφάγιον ἔχετε; ἀπεκρίθησαν αὐτῷ· Οὔ.
verses.transliteration: legei oun autois ho Iēsous: Paidia, mē ti prosphagion echete? apekrithēsan autō: Ou.
verses.study_note: Paidia (children/lads) is an affectionate address, used also in 1 John 2:14. Prosphagion (fish/meat — literally "something eaten with bread") is a colloquial fishing term. The one-word answer Ou (No) captures the blunt honesty of exhausted fishermen. Jesus asks knowing the answer, just as he asked Philip about bread before the feeding of the five thousand (6:5-6) — he tests to reveal.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, Cast the net on the right side of the ship, and ye shall find. They cast therefore, and now they were not able to draw it for the multitude of fishes.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς· Βάλετε εἰς τὰ δεξιὰ μέρη τοῦ πλοίου τὸ δίκτυον, καὶ εὑρήσετε. ἔβαλον οὖν, καὶ οὐκέτι αὐτὸ ἑλκύσαι ἴσχυον ἀπὸ τοῦ πλήθους τῶν ἰχθύων.
verses.transliteration: ho de eipen autois: Balete eis ta dexia merē tou ploiou to diktyon, kai heurēsete. ebalon oun, kai ouketi auto helkysai ischyon apo tou plēthous tōn ichthyōn.
verses.study_note: Obedience to the stranger's word produces miraculous abundance. The right side was conventionally the side of blessing in Jewish symbolism. Helkysai ischyon — they were not strong enough to haul it — the net full of great fish is a visible parable: apart from Christ, nothing; in obedience to his word, inexhaustible fruitfulness. Compare Luke 5:6 where the nets began to break — here the net holds (see v.11).

verses.kjv_text: Therefore that disciple whom Jesus loved saith unto Peter, It is the Lord. Now when Simon Peter heard that it was the Lord, he girt his fisher's coat unto him, (for he was naked,) and did cast himself into the sea.
verses.original_text: λέγει οὖν ὁ μαθητὴς ἐκεῖνος ὃν ἠγάπα ὁ Ἰησοῦς τῷ Πέτρῳ· Ὁ κύριός ἐστιν. Σίμων οὖν Πέτρος, ἀκούσας ὅτι ὁ κύριός ἐστιν, τὸν ἐπενδύτην διεζώσατο, ἦν γὰρ γυμνός, καὶ ἔβαλεν ἑαυτὸν εἰς τὴν θάλασσαν.
verses.transliteration: legei oun ho mathētēs ekeinos hon ēgapa ho Iēsous tō Petrō: Ho kyrios estin. Simōn oun Petros, akousas hoti ho kyrios estin, ton ependytēn diezōsato, ēn gar gymnos, kai ebalen heauton eis tēn thalassan.
verses.study_note: The Beloved Disciple perceives first (as at the empty tomb, 20:8); Peter acts first — the two men embody complementary gifts of insight and impulsive love. Peter's eagerness to reach Jesus at once — plunging into the sea rather than waiting for the boat — stands in poignant contrast to his denial. The man who once warmed himself at a fire while denying Jesus now cannot reach him fast enough.

verses.kjv_text: And the other disciples came in a little ship; (for they were not far from land, but as it were two hundred cubits,) dragging the net with fishes.
verses.original_text: οἱ δὲ ἄλλοι μαθηταὶ τῷ πλοιαρίῳ ἦλθον, οὐ γὰρ ἦσαν μακρὰν ἀπὸ τῆς γῆς ἀλλὰ ὡς ἀπὸ πηχῶν διακοσίων, σύροντες τὸ δίκτυον τῶν ἰχθύων.
verses.transliteration: hoi de alloi mathētai tō ploiariō ēlthon, ou gar ēsan makran apo tēs gēs alla hōs apo pēchōn diakosiōn, syrontes to diktyon tōn ichthyōn.
verses.study_note: Two hundred cubits is approximately 100 metres — close enough for the disciples to see Jesus clearly once Peter recognised him. Syrontes (dragging) emphasises the weight and abundance of the catch. The detail of the distance is the kind of eyewitness precision characteristic of the Beloved Disciple's testimony throughout the Gospel.

verses.kjv_text: As soon then as they were come to land, they saw a fire of coals there, and fish laid thereon, and bread.
verses.original_text: ὡς οὖν ἀπέβησαν εἰς τὴν γῆν, βλέπουσιν ἀνθρακιὰν κειμένην καὶ ὀψάριον ἐπικείμενον καὶ ἄρτον.
verses.transliteration: hōs oun apebēsan eis tēn gēn, blepousin anthrakia n keimenen kai opsarion epikelmenon kai arton.
verses.study_note: Anthrakian (a charcoal fire) — this precise word appears only twice in the NT: here and in 18:18, where Peter warmed himself at a charcoal fire while denying Jesus. The deliberate echo is unmistakable: the same kind of fire now becomes the setting not of denial but of restoration. Jesus the Good Shepherd has already provided the meal — he does not need the disciples' fish, but he graciously includes them.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus saith unto them, Bring of the fish which ye have now caught.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς· Ἐνέγκατε ἀπὸ τῶν ὀψαρίων ὧν ἐπιάσατε νῦν.
verses.transliteration: legei autois ho Iēsous:Enegkate apo tōn opsariōn hōn epiasthe nyn.
verses.study_note: Although Jesus already has fish on the fire, he invites the disciples to bring their catch — incorporating their labour into his provision. This is the pattern of divine-human co-operation: God's grace is not a substitute for human effort but its fulfilment and completion. The miracle of the full net and the meal at the fire together declare resurrection abundance.

verses.kjv_text: Simon Peter went up, and drew the net to land full of great fishes, an hundred and fifty and three: and for all there were so many, yet was not the net broken.
verses.original_text: ἀνέβη οὖν Σίμων Πέτρος καὶ εἵλκυσεν τὸ δίκτυον εἰς τὴν γῆν μεστὸν ἰχθύων μεγάλων ἑκατὸν πεντήκοντα τριῶν· καὶ τοσούτων ὄντων οὐκ ἐσχίσθη τὸ δίκτυον.
verses.transliteration: anebē oun Simōn Petros kai heilkysen to diktyon eis tēn gēn meston ichthyōn megalōn hekaton pentēkonta triōn; kai tosoutōn ontōn ouk eschisthē to diktyon.
verses.study_note: 153 fish — the precise number has generated much theological reflection. Jerome noted that ancient naturalists counted 153 species of fish, suggesting the universal scope of the Gospel catch. Others see gematria (the number of "the sons of God" in Hebrew). Most importantly: the specificity is the mark of eyewitness testimony, and the unbroken net (ouk eschisthē) contrasts with Luke 5:6 — the resurrection community will hold together all whom Christ draws (compare 6:39; 10:28).

verses.kjv_text: Jesus saith unto them, Come and dine. And none of the disciples durst ask him, Who art thou? knowing that it was the Lord.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς· Δεῦτε ἀριστήσατε. οὐδεὶς δὲ ἐτόλμα τῶν μαθητῶν ἐξετάσαι αὐτόν· Σὺ τίς εἶ; εἰδότες ὅτι ὁ κύριός ἐστιν.
verses.transliteration: legei autois ho Iēsous: Deute aristēsate. oudeis de etolma tōn mathētōn exetasai auton: Sy tis ei? eidotes hoti ho kyrios estin.
verses.study_note: Deute aristēsate — "Come, eat breakfast." The risen Lord hosts a meal: bread and fish by the sea, echoing the feeding of the multitude (6:11). Oudeis etolma — no one dared ask, "Who are you?" They knew, yet the knowledge defied articulation. The risen Christ inhabits a mode of being that is real and tangible (he has fish; he has bread) yet beyond full comprehension — known in the breaking of bread, as at Emmaus.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus then cometh, and taketh bread, and giveth them; and fish likewise.
verses.original_text: ἔρχεται Ἰησοῦς καὶ λαμβάνει τὸν ἄρτον καὶ δίδωσιν αὐτοῖς, καὶ τὸ ὀψάριον ὁμοίως.
verses.transliteration: erchetai Iēsous kai lambanei ton arton kai didōsin autois, kai to opsarion homoiōs.
verses.study_note: The action — taking bread and giving — mirrors 6:11 (the feeding of the five thousand) and the Last Supper. Every meal Jesus hosts is an acted parable of the Eucharist and of his role as the Bread of Life. The resurrection breakfast on the shore is both intimate and cosmic: the Lord of all creation serving breakfast to seven tired fishermen.

verses.kjv_text: This is now the third time that Jesus shewed himself to his disciples, after that he was risen from the dead.
verses.original_text: τοῦτο ἤδη τρίτον ἐφανερώθη Ἰησοῦς τοῖς μαθηταῖς ἐγερθεὶς ἐκ νεκρῶν.
verses.transliteration: touto ēdē triton ephanerōthē Iēsous tois mathētais egertheis ek nekrōn.
verses.study_note: The third appearance: to Mary (20:14-17), to the ten on the evening of Resurrection Day (20:19-23), and now to the seven at the sea. The number establishes the pattern and credibility of resurrection appearances. Egertheis ek nekrōn (having been raised from the dead) is the Pauline formula for resurrection, grounding the appearance in bodily resurrection.

verses.kjv_text: So when they had dined, Jesus saith to Simon Peter, Simon, son of Jonas, lovest thou me more than these? He saith unto him, Yea, Lord; thou knowest that I love thee. He saith unto him, Feed my lambs.
verses.original_text: Ὅτε οὖν ἠρίστησαν, λέγει τῷ Σίμωνι Πέτρῳ ὁ Ἰησοῦς· Σίμων Ἰωάννου, ἀγαπᾷς με πλέον τούτων; λέγει αὐτῷ· Ναί, κύριε, σὺ οἶδας ὅτι φιλῶ σε. λέγει αὐτῷ· Βόσκε τὰ ἀρνία μου.
verses.transliteration: Hote oun ēristēsan, legei tō Simōni Petrō ho Iēsous: Simōn Iōannou, agapas me pleon toutōn? legei autō: Nai, kyrie, sy oidas hoti philō se. legei autō: Boske ta arnia mou.
verses.study_note: Jesus reverts to Peter's pre-discipleship name Simon bar Jonah (Matt. 16:17) — the name of the man before the calling. Agapas me pleon toutōn — do you love me more than these? (more than the other disciples, or more than your nets/livelihood?) Peter had boasted superiority in love (13:37; Mark 14:29). Now he answers only with phileō — warm affection, not daring to claim the higher agapaō. Jesus accepts it and commissions: Boske ta arnia mou — Feed (tend, nourish) my lambs. The shepherd's first duty is to the young and vulnerable.

verses.kjv_text: He saith to him again the second time, Simon, son of Jonas, lovest thou me? He saith unto him, Yea, Lord; thou knowest that I love thee. He saith unto him, Feed my sheep.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ πάλιν δεύτερον· Σίμων Ἰωάννου, ἀγαπᾷς με; λέγει αὐτῷ· Ναί, κύριε, σὺ οἶδας ὅτι φιλῶ σε. λέγει αὐτῷ· Ποίμαινε τὰ πρόβατά μου.
verses.transliteration: legei autō palin deuteron: Simōn Iōannou, agapas me? legei autō: Nai, kyrie, sy oidas hoti philō se. legei autō: Poimaine ta probata mou.
verses.study_note: The second question drops "more than these" — the comparison is removed. Agapas me still asks for the higher love. Peter's answer is unchanged: phileō. The commission now is Poimaine ta probata — shepherd (govern, guide, protect) my sheep — a broader and more demanding role than simply feeding lambs. Each affirmation of love corresponds to a commission: pastoral leadership is grounded in love, not authority or achievement.

verses.kjv_text: He saith unto him the third time, Simon, son of Jonas, lovest thou me? Peter was grieved because he said unto him the third time, Lovest thou me? And he said unto him, Lord, thou knowest all things; thou knowest that I love thee. Jesus saith unto him, Feed my sheep.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ τὸ τρίτον· Σίμων Ἰωάννου, φιλεῖς με; ἐλυπήθη ὁ Πέτρος ὅτι εἶπεν αὐτῷ τὸ τρίτον· Φιλεῖς με; καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ· Κύριε, πάντα σὺ οἶδας, σὺ γινώσκεις ὅτι φιλῶ σε. λέγει αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς· Βόσκε τὰ πρόβατά μου.
verses.transliteration: legei autō to triton: Simōn Iōannou, phileis me? elypēthē ho Petros hoti eipen autō to triton: Phileis me? kai eipen autō: Kyrie, panta sy oidas, sy ginōskeis hoti philō se. legei autō ho Iēsous: Boske ta probata mou.
verses.study_note: The third question descends to Peter's own word phileō — do you even love me in this lesser sense? Three questions for three denials. Peter is grieved (elypēthē) — the grief is itself the healing, the same grief that led him to weep bitterly (Luke 22:62). His answer now is surrender not self-assertion: Kyrie, panta sy oidas — Lord, you know all things. He appeals to the omniscience of Christ (who knows the heart) rather than his own protestations. The triple commission — feed/shepherd/feed — restores Peter to apostolic leadership through the surgery of love.

verses.kjv_text: Verily, verily, I say unto thee, When thou wast young, thou girdedst thyself, and walkedst whither thou wouldest: but when thou shalt be old, thou shalt stretch forth thy hands, and another shall gird thee, and carry thee whither thou wouldest not.
verses.original_text: ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω σοι, ὅτε ἦς νεώτερος, ἐζώννυες σεαυτὸν καὶ περιεπάτεις ὅπου ἤθελες· ὅταν δὲ γηράσῃς, ἐκτενεῖς τὰς χεῖράς σου, καὶ ἄλλος σε ζώσει καὶ οἴσει ὅπου οὐ θέλεις.
verses.transliteration: amēn amēn legō soi, hote ēs neōteros, ezōnnyes seauton kai periepateis hopou ētheles; hotan de gērasēs, ekteneis tas cheiras sou, kai allos se zōsei kai oisei hopou ou theleis.
verses.study_note: The amen-amen formula introduces a solemn prophetic word. Young freedom versus old constraint is a proverb of life. But ekteneis tas cheiras sou — you will stretch out your hands — is the posture of crucifixion. Tradition uniformly records that Peter was crucified in Rome (under Nero, c. AD 64-68), reportedly upside down at his own request, to distinguish his death from his Lord's. The prophecy transforms Peter's martyrdom into the ultimate expression of the love he had just confessed.

verses.kjv_text: This spake he, signifying by what death he should glorify God. And when he had spoken this, he saith unto him, Follow me.
verses.original_text: τοῦτο δὲ εἶπεν σημαίνων ποίῳ θανάτῳ δοξάσει τὸν θεόν. καὶ τοῦτο εἰπὼν λέγει αὐτῷ· Ἀκολούθει μοι.
verses.transliteration: touto de eipen sēmainōn poiō thanatō doxasei ton theon. kai touto eipōn legei autō: Akolouthei moi.
verses.study_note: The narrator's comment sēmainōn (signifying) is the same verb used to interpret the manner of Jesus' own death (12:33; 18:32). Peter's martyrdom will be a doxological act — glorifying God. Then comes the simplest and most profound of all commands: Akolouthei moi — Follow me. These were Jesus' first words to Peter on the shore of the same sea (Mark 1:17; Matt. 4:19). Now they are his last. The entire arc of discipleship is contained in this call: follow me, even to the cross.

verses.kjv_text: Then Peter, turning about, seeth the disciple whom Jesus loved following; which also leaned on his breast at supper, and said, Lord, which is he that betrayeth thee?
verses.original_text: Ἐπιστραφεὶς δὲ ὁ Πέτρος βλέπει τὸν μαθητὴν ὃν ἠγάπα ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἀκολουθοῦντα, ὃς καὶ ἀνέπεσεν ἐν τῷ δείπνῳ ἐπὶ τὸ στῆθος αὐτοῦ καὶ εἶπεν· Κύριε, τίς ἐστιν ὁ παραδιδούς σε;
verses.transliteration: Epistrepheis de ho Petros blepei ton mathētēn hon ēgapa ho Iēsous akolouthounta, hos kai anepesen en tō deipnō epi to stēthos autou kai eipen: Kyrie, tis estin ho paradidous se?
verses.study_note: Peter turns and sees the Beloved Disciple — already following Jesus, without being commanded. The identification by reference to 13:23-25 (leaning on Jesus' breast at the supper, asking about the betrayer) is the evangelist's self-identification signature. The question Peter is about to ask arises from a perfectly human impulse: if my path leads to the cross, what about his?

verses.kjv_text: Peter seeing him saith to Jesus, Lord, and what shall this man do?
verses.original_text: τοῦτον οὖν ἰδὼν ὁ Πέτρος λέγει τῷ Ἰησοῦ· Κύριε, οὗτος δὲ τί;
verses.transliteration: touton oun idōn ho Petros legei tō Iēsou: Kyrie, houtos de ti?
verses.study_note: Kyrie, houtos de ti? — Lord, but what about this man? The question is entirely natural and entirely beside the point. Jesus had just given Peter his entire calling and destiny; Peter's first instinct is to look sideways at another disciple's path. The question represents the perennial human temptation to compare our calling and cost with another's rather than simply following.

verses.kjv_text: Jesus saith unto him, If I will that he tarry till I come, what is that to thee? follow thou me.
verses.original_text: λέγει αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς· Ἐὰν αὐτὸν θέλω μένειν ἕως ἔρχομαι, τί πρὸς σέ; σύ μοι ἀκολούθει.
verses.transliteration: legei autō ho Iēsous: Ean auton thelō menein heōs erchomai, ti pros se? sy moi akolouthei.
verses.study_note: Jesus' answer is gently decisive: "What is that to you?" (ti pros se — what is it to you?) Each disciple's calling is sovereign and particular. The conditional — if I will that he remain until I come — is not a promise but a hypothetical. The emphasis falls on the repeated command: sy moi akolouthei — you, follow me. The second person pronoun is emphatic: whatever others' paths, yours is simply to follow. This is the charter of individual Christian vocation.

verses.kjv_text: Then went this saying abroad among the brethren, that that disciple should not die: yet Jesus said not unto him, He shall not die; but, If I will that he tarry till I come, what is that to thee?
verses.original_text: ἐξῆλθεν οὖν οὗτος ὁ λόγος εἰς τοὺς ἀδελφοὺς ὅτι ὁ μαθητὴς ἐκεῖνος οὐκ ἀποθνῄσκει· οὐκ εἶπεν δὲ αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς ὅτι οὐκ ἀποθνῄσκει, ἀλλ'· Ἐὰν αὐτὸν θέλω μένειν ἕως ἔρχομαι, τί πρὸς σέ;
verses.transliteration: exēlthen oun houtos ho logos eis tous adelphous hoti ho mathētēs ekeinos ouk apothnēskei; ouk eipen de autō ho Iēsous hoti ouk apothnēskei, all': Ean auton thelō menein heōs erchomai, ti pros se?
verses.study_note: The narrator corrects a misunderstanding that had circulated in the early church. This pastoral clarification suggests that the Beloved Disciple had by the time of writing reached extreme old age (or had died), making the correction urgent. The narrator's care to distinguish what Jesus said from what was inferred demonstrates the Gospel's commitment to accurate testimony over comforting legend.

verses.kjv_text: This is the disciple which testifieth of these things, and wrote these things: and we know that his testimony is true.
verses.original_text: Οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ μαθητὴς ὁ μαρτυρῶν περὶ τούτων καὶ ὁ γράψας ταῦτα, καὶ οἴδαμεν ὅτι ἀληθὴς αὐτοῦ ἡ μαρτυρία ἐστίν.
verses.transliteration: Houtos estin ho mathētēs ho martyrōn peri toutōn kai ho grapsas tauta, kai oidamen hoti alēthēs autou hē martyria estin.
verses.study_note: The colophon of the Gospel. Ho martyrōn — the one who testifies — is present tense, suggesting ongoing living witness or the enduring authority of written testimony. Ho grapsas tauta — who wrote these things — identifies the Beloved Disciple as the author. The we (oidamen — we know) may be the Johannine community or elders who attest the testimony. Alēthēs hē martyria — the testimony is true — is the community's imprimatur, echoing Pilate's unwitting "What is truth?" (18:38) with the answer: this.

verses.kjv_text: And there are also many other things which Jesus did, the which, if they should be written every one, I suppose that even the world itself could not contain the books that should be written. Amen.
verses.original_text: Ἔστιν δὲ καὶ ἄλλα πολλὰ ἃ ἐποίησεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς, ἅτινα ἐὰν γράφηται καθ' ἕν, οὐδ' αὐτὸν οἶμαι τὸν κόσμον χωρῆσαι τὰ γραφόμενα βιβλία. ἀμήν.
verses.transliteration: Estin de kai alla polla ha epoiēsen ho Iēsous, hatina ean graphētai kath' hen, oud' auton oimai ton kosmon chōrēsai ta graphomena biblia. amēn.
verses.study_note: The Gospel's final word is an extravagant hyperbole worthy of its subject. Chōrēsai — contain, hold — the world itself could not hold the books. This is not bibliographic claim but theological doxology: the life of the eternal Word made flesh is inexhaustible, beyond all human capacity to record or comprehend. Every generation's meditation on Christ fills more volumes; yet he exceeds them all. The closing Amen is the reader's response — so let it be — to the testimony of the one who is the way, the truth, and the life.

1.meaning: Manifested himself — phaneroō: revealed/disclosed; resurrection appearance is self-disclosure.
Tiberias — alternate name for Sea of Galilee, after the Herodian city; characteristically Johannine usage.

2.meaning: Didymus — Twin; Greek translation of Aramaic Toma, here Thomas now joining the group.
Nathanael of Cana — only mentioned here outside chapter 1 (1:45-51); inclusio with first sign.

3.meaning: "I am going fishing" — Peter's return to old occupation; not necessarily apostasy but waiting/restless.
Nothing — empty net; recapitulates Luke 5:5; foil for the miraculous catch.

4.meaning: Day having already broken — dawn; resurrection time, second creation light.
They did not however know — recognition delay (cf. Luke 24:16; Mary at tomb 20:14).

5.meaning: Children/lads — affectionate vocative; informal as one might address young workers.
Anything to eat (with bread) — relish, side dish; idiomatic for fish caught.

6.meaning: On the right side — specific direction; the divine word brings success where human effort failed.
To haul — same verb of drawing to Christ in 6:44; 12:32; the catch foreshadows mission.

7.meaning: The disciple whom Jesus loved — agapaō here; the Beloved Disciple identifies; Peter acts.
Outer garment — tunic worn over for work; Peter dons it to greet the Lord properly.
Threw himself — Peter's impulsive plunge; characteristic Petrine eagerness.

9.meaning: Charcoal fire — only here and 18:18 in NT; deliberate echo of denial scene; restoration setting.
Fish (cooked, prepared as food) — Johannine word; appears in 6:9,11; 21:9,10,13.

11.meaning: 153 — exact count; Jerome relayed ancient view of 153 species of fish (universality of mission).
Was not torn — schizō: same verb of temple veil torn (Mark 15:38); the unity of the catch preserved.

12.meaning: Have breakfast (aorist imperative) — aristaō: to take the morning meal; eucharistic overtones.

13.meaning: Takes the bread — echoes feeding miracle (6:11) and Last Supper; same gesture.

15.meaning: Do you agapē-love me more than these? — first question; "more than these" likely the other disciples.
I phileō you — Peter's answer uses warmer-but-lesser term; refuses the comparative claim.
Feed my lambs — boskō: feed; arnia: lambs (diminutive); pastoral nourishment of the youngest.

16.meaning: Shepherd my sheep — poimainō: full shepherding (rule, lead, protect, feed); probata: mature sheep.

17.meaning: Do you phileō me? — third time Jesus descends to Peter's vocabulary; the painful condescension.
Was grieved — three questions echo three denials; cuts to the heart.
"You know all things" — Peter appeals to Jesus's omniscience as final witness to his love.
Feed my sheep — combines feeding (boskō) with mature sheep; fullness of pastoral charge.

18.meaning: Will gird you — passive girding by another; veiled prophecy of Peter's crucifixion.
Where you do not wish — martyrdom against natural inclination but for Christ's sake.

19.meaning: Signifying by what death — Johannine narrator-comment; tradition: Peter crucified upside-down (~AD 64).
Would glorify God — martyrdom as glorifying God (cf. 12:27-28; 17:1).
"Follow me" — present imperative; Christ's call to discipleship reissued post-restoration; the original call (1:43) renewed.

20.meaning: Having leaned on the breast — explicit identification with the Beloved Disciple from 13:25.

22.meaning: Until I come — parousia language; sovereign disposition of each disciple's lot.
"What is that to you?" — Peter must not compare; each follows uniquely.

23.meaning: That disciple does not die — corrected misinterpretation of Jesus's saying; suggests Beloved Disciple's death prompted clarification.

24.meaning: The testimony is true — eyewitness authentication formula; community attestation.
Who wrote these things — author identified as the Beloved Disciple; Johannine authorship claim.

25.original_word: οὐδ' αὐτὸν οἶμαι τὸν κόσμον χωρῆσαι
25.transliteration: oud' auton oimai ton kosmon chōrēsai
25.meaning: I suppose the world itself could not contain — hyperbolic doxology; chōreō: hold/contain.
Amen — Hebrew/Aramaic affirmation "so let it be"; Gospel's closing word.
